- Concrete deteriorates with time due to carbonation, chloride ingress, freeze-thaw, ASR, and corrosion of rebar.
- Preventive maintenance is proactive — it *extends* the design life and *reduces* the LCC by avoiding emergency repairs.
- Required by **IS 15971** and is a regulatory necessity for public/industrial structures.

**Objectives (2 marks):**
1. Maintain structural safety and serviceability.
2. Extend the useful life towards the design life.
3. Optimize the life-cycle cost.
4. Document the asset's health for legal/insurance purposes.
5. Reduce unplanned downtime and inconvenience to users.

**Activities and Frequency (3 marks):**

| # | Activity | Frequency |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Visual inspection of beams/slabs for cracks | 6-monthly |
| 2 | Rebound hammer test on critical members | Annually |
| 3 | UPV testing for concrete homogeneity | 3-yearly |
| 4 | Half-cell potential mapping for rebar corrosion | 3-yearly |
| 5 | Re-painting of exposed steel elements | 2-yearly |
| 6 | Waterproofing renewal of roof | 5-yearly |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Age $T_a = 20$ years
- Cover $c = 25$ mm
- Carbonation depth at 20 years = 8 mm
- $k_c = 2$ mm/√year
- $T_d = 50$ years

**Step 1 — Verify $k_c$ from observation (1 mark):**
Using $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$:

$$k_c^{\text{actual}} = \frac{x_c}{\sqrt{t}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{20}} = \frac{8}{4.472} \approx 1.789 \text{ mm/}\sqrt{\text{year}}$$

The *actual* carbonation coefficient is **lower** than the conservative estimate of 2 mm/√year. (This is favourable — the mix is more resistant than assumed.)

**Step 2 — Time to rebar corrosion initiation (2 marks):**

$$t_{\text{init}} = \left( \frac{c}{k_c^{\text{actual}}} \right)^{2} = \left( \frac{25}{1.789} \right)^{2} = (13.97)^{2} \approx 195.2 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — Remaining service life (1 mark):**

$$T_r = 195.2 - 20 = 175.2 \text{ years}$$

This is **vastly greater** than the remaining design life of 30 years.

**Step 4 — Safety Comment (1 mark):**
Under current exposure conditions, the slab is **structurally safe from carbonation-induced corrosion** for the rest of its design life. The 8 mm carbonation depth at 20 years is consistent with $k_c = 1.79$ mm/√year, indicating good-quality, dense cover concrete.

**Step 5 — Recommended Maintenance Plan (2 marks):**
1. Continue **routine annual inspection** for any new crack propagation.
2. Conduct **UPV and half-cell testing every 3 years** to detect any acceleration in deterioration.
3. Apply a **silane/siloxane based surface sealer** to the soffit to slow further carbonation.
4. Maintain a **logbook** of inspection results to enable trend analysis.
5. Re-evaluate at the **30-year mark** (i.e., after 10 more years) and revise the plan.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (10 bullets, KTU ESE-ready):**

- **Routine maintenance** = time-based, repetitive, low cost (1×). Examples: cleaning, sweeping, minor touch-up.
- **Preventive maintenance** = condition-based, inspection-driven, medium cost (2×–3×). Examples: NDT, epoxy injection, re-painting.
- The **"1 : 5 : 25" rule** justifies prevention economically.
- **MTBF ↑, MTTR ↓** → Higher **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$.
- **Asfahl's optimal interval:** $T^{*} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{2C_p/C_f}$ — for KTU numericals.
- **Carbonation model:** $x_c = k_c \sqrt{t}$ (Fickian); time-to-corrosion = $(c/k_c)^2$.
- **IS 15971** governs maintenance management of buildings; **IRC codes** govern bridges.
- **Inspection hierarchy:** Routine (visual) → Detailed (NDT) → Comprehensive (load test + audit).
- **RCC deterioration sequence:** Carbonation / chloride → rebar corrosion → cover cracking → spalling → structural distress.
- **Preventive > Corrective > Emergency** in both cost and safety; always prefer a *planned* maintenance policy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rehabilitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rehabilitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Rehabilitation** is the comprehensive engineering process of restoring a deteriorated, damaged, or underperforming structure to a desired level of **serviceability**, **strength**, **durability**, and **functional utility**, often upgrading it beyond its original design capacity. It is a holistic intervention strategy that goes beyond mere repair, encompassing **diagnosis, assessment, design, and execution** of corrective measures to extend the residual service life of a structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Rehabilitation = Repair + Restoration + Strengthening + Upgradation + Maintenance, executed as a single engineered lifecycle strategy.

The formal KTU definition also recognizes rehabilitation as the **fourth stage in the structural lifecycle**: Construction → Service Life → Deterioration → **Rehabilitation** → Extended Service Life → Demolition.

## 1.2 Classification of Structural Intervention (Hierarchical Framework)

According to the **ACI 562 Code (Code Requirements for Assessment, Repair, and Rehabilitation of Existing Concrete Structures)** and adopted by KTU 2024 scheme, structural interventions are classified into **four distinct categories** based on the depth of intervention:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Conceptual Hierarchy (Lowest to Highest Intervention Intensity)**
> 1. **Repair** — Restoring the structure to its original intended function by addressing localized defects (spalling, cracks, corrosion patches).
> 2. **Restoration** — Bringing the structure back to its original geometric, strength, and aesthetic condition.
> 3. **Strengthening** — Enhancing the load-carrying capacity beyond the original design intent.
> 4. **Rehabilitation** — The umbrella term combining all three above, applied as a systematic engineered program.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a 60-year-old Kerala-style ancestral house (traditional *nalukettu*) whose wooden rafters are decaying, the lime plaster is flaking, the Mangalore tile roof leaks, and the foundation stones have settled unevenly. A **repair** would mean just patching the cracked plaster and replacing broken tiles. A **restoration** would mean bringing the house back to how it looked in 1965. A **strengthening** would mean adding hidden steel bands so the house can withstand a future cyclone better than before. A **rehabilitation**, however, is the **complete engineered program** — engineers inspect every beam and joint, perform NDT tests, redesign the load paths, execute repairs, restore heritage aesthetics, add cyclone-resistant strengthening, and provide a future maintenance plan. The house doesn't just become "old but fixed" — it becomes **a better version of its former self, with a certified extended life**.

This analogy precisely captures why rehabilitation is not a "patch job" but a **systems-level engineering discipline**.

## 1.4 Why Rehabilitation? The Engineering Imperative

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **sustainability and lifecycle engineering** over the outdated "demolish and rebuild" approach. The three primary drivers are:

| **Driver** | **Engineering Rationale** |
|---|---|
| **Economic** | New construction costs **3 to 5 times** more than rehabilitation. A ₹100 Cr demolition-rebuild can be replaced by a ₹20–30 Cr rehabilitation program yielding equivalent performance. |
| **Environmental** | Cement production alone contributes **~8% of global CO₂ emissions**. Rehabilitation reduces embodied carbon by **40–60%**. |
| **Social & Heritage** | Many structures (bridges, heritage buildings, hospitals) **cannot be demolished** due to cultural, operational, or strategic reasons. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric (KTU Highlighted):** The **Life-Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA)** governs rehabilitation decisions. The structure is rehabilitated when **Net Present Value (NPV) of rehabilitation < NPV of reconstruction** over the design horizon.

## 1.5 Typical Performance Gaps Addressed by Rehabilitation

A structure demands rehabilitation when its **as-built capacity** falls below the **required performance level**. The performance gap is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{required}} - P_{\text{available}}
$$

where $\Delta P > 0$ triggers the rehabilitation workflow.

Common manifestations of $\Delta P$ include:
- Visible cracking, spalling, and deflection
- Excessive vibration under service loads
- Corrosion-induced rebar loss
- Seismic deficiency (post-Bhuj 2001, this became a national priority in India)
- Functional obsolescence (e.g., an old hospital ward unable to support modern MRI loads)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rehabilitation Workflow (5-Phase KTU Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts a **five-phase systematic workflow** for any rehabilitation project. Each phase has well-defined deliverables and gate-criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Gate Concept:** No phase may begin until the previous phase's deliverables are formally signed off by the **Project Structural Engineer of Record (SER)**.

### Phase 1 — Preliminary Investigation & Documentation
- Historical record review (original drawings, soil reports, material test records)
- Visual inspection and photographic documentation
- Identification of distress zones
- **Deliverable:** Preliminary Distress Report (PDR)

### Phase 2 — Detailed Condition Assessment (DCA)
- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Rebound Hammer, Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV), Cover Meter, Half-Cell Potential
- **Partially Destructive Testing (PDT):** Core extraction, pull-out tests
- **Destructive Testing:** Load testing (in extreme cases)
- **Deliverable:** Condition Assessment Report (CAR) with quantitative strength, durability, and serviceability indices.

### Phase 3 — Diagnosis & Root-Cause Analysis
- Mapping observed distress to **mechanisms** (carbonation, chloride ingress, ASR, sulfate attack, overload, fatigue, design deficiency)
- Distinguishing **active** vs. **passive** distress
- **Deliverable:** Diagnostic Report with prioritized root causes.

### Phase 4 — Rehabilitation Strategy & Detailed Design
- Selection of techniques (FRP, jacketing, grouting, post-tensioning, etc.)
- Structural redesign with upgraded load factors
- Material specification and compatibility check
- **Deliverable:** Detailed Project Report (DPR) with BoQ, drawings, and method statement.

### Phase 5 — Execution, Quality Assurance & Monitoring
- Material qualification tests
- On-site mock-up trials
- Real-time QA/QC (cube tests, pull-off adhesion, FRP bond tests)
- Long-term Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)
- **Deliverable:** As-built records and **Maintenance Manual** for the owner.

## 2.2 Performance-Based Rehabilitation Philosophy

Modern KTU-aligned rehabilitation is **performance-based**, not prescriptive. The engineer specifies **desired outcomes** rather than mandating a specific material. The target performance is governed by:

$$
\text{Performance Index (PI)} = \frac{\text{Actual Capacity}}{\text{Required Capacity}} \geq 1.0
$$

For seismic rehabilitation (governed by **IS 15988:2013** and **ASCE 41**), the two-tier objective is:

| **Performance Level** | **Target** | **Earthquake Return Period** |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate Occupancy (IO)** | Structure usable post-event, minor damage | Serviceability Earthquake (SE) |
| **Life Safety (LS)** | No collapse, life protected | Design Basis Earthquake (DBE) |
| **Collapse Prevention (CP)** | No global collapse | Maximum Considered Earthquake (MCE) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all **board-examination critical equations** for Module 1 — Rehabilitation. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| **Formula** | **Application** | **Units / Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta P = P_{\text{req}} - P_{\text{avail}}$ | Performance gap triggering rehabilitation | $P$ in kN, kNm, MPa, or dimensionless index |
| $\text{PI} = \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{required}}} \geq 1.0$ | Rehabilitation adequacy check | Dimensionless |
| $\sigma_{\text{permissible}} = \dfrac{0.67 \cdot f_{\text{ck}}}{1.0}$ (working stress) | Concrete strength check (old IS 456) | MPa |
| $f_{\text{c,reduced}} = f_{\text{ck}} \cdot (1 - k \cdot d^{n})$ | In-situ strength degradation model | $d$ = damage depth, $k,n$ are material constants |
| $\text{CL} = \text{CO}_2 \text{ depth} - \text{cover depth}$ | Carbonation-induced corrosion indicator | mm; if CL $\geq 0$, corrosion initiated |
| $C_{\text{crit}} = 0.4\% \text{ by mass of cement}$ | Chloride threshold for rebar depassivation | \% wt. of cement |
| $V = \dfrac{f_{\text{ct}}}{f_{\text{ct,std}}}$ (Leko's method) | Concrete quality grading by UPV | Dimensionless ratio |
| $\text{LCCA: NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Life-Cycle Cost Analysis | $i$ = discount rate, $T$ = design life |
| $f_{\text{bond,FRP}} = 0.427 \cdot \beta_p \cdot \beta_L \cdot \sqrt{f_c' \cdot E_{FRP} \cdot t_{FRP}}$ (ACI 440.2R) | FRP bond strength design | MPa, length in mm |
| $R = \dfrac{\Delta f_{y,\text{eff}}}{f_{y,\text{original}}}$ | Steel reinforcement efficiency ratio | Dimensionless |

> **Note on notation:** In the formula table, all absolute values or "less than/equal" are written with explicit words to avoid breaking markdown table syntax with vertical bars.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Rehabilitation Science

The rehabilitation discipline directly enables:

1. **Critical Infrastructure Continuity:** Hospitals, bridges, and nuclear facilities cannot be taken offline for reconstruction. Rehabilitation allows in-service upgrading.
2. **Disaster Resilience:** Post-earthquake (e.g., **2001 Bhuj, 2015 Nepal, 2023 Türkiye**) and post-cyclone (e.g., **2018 Kerala floods, 2013 Odisha cyclone**) rehabilitation is a national strategic priority.
3. **Heritage Conservation:** Restoration of *Vrindavan* temples, Charminar, Kerala backwater bridges, and colonial-era railway stations uses rehabilitation science blended with conservation engineering.
4. **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) Alignment:** Directly contributes to **SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure)** and **SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Rehabilitation Trigger Index (RTI)

The **Rehabilitation Trigger Index (RTI)** is an engineering decision metric that quantifies the urgency and extent of intervention. It is derived from first principles using the **Capacity-Demand Ratio (CDR)** approach adopted by **IS 15988:2013** and **FEMA 356**.

**Step 1 — Define the Demand (D) and Capacity (C)**
- Let $D$ be the demand imposed on the structural element (moment, shear, axial force, stress).
- Let $C$ be the available capacity of the element in its current deteriorated state.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Capacity-Demand Ratio**

$$
\text{CDR} = \frac{C_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{applied}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Threshold Bands**
- If CDR $\geq 1.0$: Structure is adequate; no rehabilitation required (only maintenance).
- If $0.85 \leq \text{CDR} < 1.0$: Minor rehabilitation (cosmetic + localized repair).
- If $0.65 \leq \text{CDR} < 0.85$: Moderate rehabilitation (strengthening required).
- If CDR $< 0.65$: Major rehabilitation or replacement consideration.

**Step 4 — Express CDR in Terms of Material Degradation**

The available capacity of a reinforced concrete column after $t$ years of service, accounting for rebar corrosion, is given by:

$$
C_{\text{available}}(t) = 0.85 \cdot f_c'(t) \cdot A_c(t) + f_y(t) \cdot A_{s,\text{eff}}(t)
$$

where:
- $f_c'(t) = f_{ck} \cdot (1 - \alpha_c \cdot t)$ is the time-dependent concrete strength (with $\alpha_c$ = annual degradation coefficient, typically $0.005$ to $0.015$ per year for marine exposure).
- $A_c(t) = A_g - A_{s,\text{eff}}(t)$ is the effective concrete area after rebar loss.
- $f_y(t) = f_y \cdot (1 - 0.02 \cdot X_{corr})$ is the residual steel yield strength, where $X_{corr}$ is the percentage corrosion.
- $A_{s,\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_b^2(t)$ is the effective bar cross-section with reduced diameter $d_b(t)$.

**Step 5 — Bar Diameter Loss Model (Faraday's Law of Corrosion)**

The bar diameter loss is computed from the **corrosion current density** $i_{corr}$ (μA/cm²) using Faraday's law:

$$
d_b(t) = d_{b,0} - 0.0232 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot t
$$

where:
- $d_{b,0}$ = initial bar diameter (mm)
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (μA/cm²)
- $t$ = time in years
- The constant $0.0232$ incorporates Faraday's constant (96487 C/mol), iron's molar mass (55.85 g/mol), and iron's density (7.85 g/cm³), with appropriate unit conversions.

**Step 6 — Derivation of the RTI**

The Rehabilitation Trigger Index is the **complement of CDR** scaled to a 0–10 scale:

$$
\text{RTI} = 10 \cdot (1 - \text{CDR})
$$

- RTI $= 0$: No action needed.
- RTI $= 2$: Cosmetic intervention.
- RTI $= 5$: Major strengthening.
- RTI $= 10$: Imminent failure, emergency action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "define when rehabilitation is triggered," the **RTI > 2** threshold is the most defensible textbook-grade answer for a 7-mark question.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Estimation of Residual Service Life

The residual service life of a deteriorated RC element is estimated using the **Tuutti Two-Stage Corrosion Model**, which is the **standard KTU 2024 framework** for chloride-induced deterioration.

**Step 1 — Initiation Period $t_i$**
The time for chloride ions to reach the rebar and initiate corrosion:

$$
t_i = \frac{x^2}{4 \cdot D_c} \cdot \left[\text{erf}^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{C_{crit}}{C_s}\right)\right]^{-2}
$$

where:
- $x$ = concrete cover (mm)
- $D_c$ = chloride diffusion coefficient (mm²/year), typically $30$ to $200$ for ordinary concrete in marine exposure
- $C_{crit}$ = critical chloride concentration (typically $0.4\%$ by mass of cement)
- $C_s$ = surface chloride concentration
- $\text{erf}^{-1}$ = inverse error function

**Step 2 — Propagation Period $t_p$**
The time from corrosion initiation to an unacceptable damage state (e.g., $10\%$ bar section loss or visible cracking):

$$
t_p = \frac{d_{cr}}{0.0116 \cdot i_{corr}}
$$

where:
- $d_{cr}$ = critical loss of bar diameter (mm) before unacceptable damage
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (μA/cm²)

**Step 3 — Total Service Life**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = t_i + t_p
$$

**Step 4 — Residual Service Life**

$$
t_{\text{residual}} = t_{\text{total}} - t_{\text{elapsed}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Decision**
If $t_{\text{residual}} < t_{\text{design horizon}}$ (typically 30 years for KTU 2024), **rehabilitation is mandatory**. If $t_{\text{residual}} > t_{\text{design horizon}}$, preventive maintenance suffices.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Rehabilitation Technique

The following decision matrix maps **distress type** to **recommended rehabilitation technique** (per **ACI 546R-14** and **IS 13311**):

| **Distress Type** | **Root Cause** | **Primary Technique** | **Secondary / Backup Technique** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hairline cracks (width $\leq 0.3$ mm) | Shrinkage, thermal | Surface sealing (acrylic, polyurethane) | None required |
| Active structural cracks | Overload, settlement | Epoxy injection (low pressure) | Pre-stressing or external post-tensioning |
| Spalling with shallow cover | Corrosion initiation | Patch repair with polymer-modified mortar | Anti-carbonation coating |
| Severe corrosion (10–25% section loss) | Chloride ingress | Jacketing (RC or steel) | Cathodic protection (impressed current) |
| Loss of $\geq 25\%$ section | Advanced corrosion | Section enlargement + FRP wrapping | Column replacement |
| Seismic deficiency | Inadequate ductility, lack of confinement | RC jacketing, steel jacketing, FRP confinement | Base isolation, supplemental damping |
| Carbonation (depth $>$ cover) | CO₂ penetration | Anti-carbonation coating + patch repair | Re-alkalization treatment |
| Honeycombing | Poor construction quality | Pressure grouting with non-shrink grout | Epoxy injection for structural zones |
| Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR) | Reactive aggregate | Lithium-silicate treatment + moisture control | Section replacement |

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Standard)

**Problem:** A coastal bridge pier in Kochi has $f_{ck,\text{original}} = 30$ MPa concrete, cover $= 50$ mm, $d_{b,0} = 20$ mm bars, $i_{corr} = 1.5$ μA/cm², and the chloride front has just reached the rebar. Determine:
- (a) The bar diameter after 10 years of propagation.
- (b) The residual capacity ratio if corrosion loss exceeds 20%.
- (c) The RTI classification.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using Faraday's law:
$$
d_b(10) = 20 - 0.0232 \times 1.5 \times 10
$$

$$
d_b(10) = 20 - 0.348 = 19.652 \text{ mm}
$$

Percentage loss:
$$
\Delta d = \frac{20 - 19.652}{20} \times 100 = 1.74\%
$$

**Part (b):** For a 20% corrosion loss, the bar diameter becomes:
$$
d_{b,20} = 20 \times (1 - 0.20) = 16 \text{ mm}
$$

Area ratio:
$$
\frac{A_{s,\text{eff}}}{A_{s,\text{original}}} = \left(\frac{16}{20}\right)^2 = 0.64
$$

Capacity ratio (assuming concrete unaffected, steel governs):
$$
\text{CDR} = \frac{A_{s,\text{eff}}}{A_{s,\text{original}}} = 0.64
$$

**Part (c):** RTI calculation:
$$
\text{RTI} = 10 \times (1 - 0.64) = 3.6
$$

**Interpretation:** RTI = 3.6 falls in the **Minor to Moderate Rehabilitation** band → **Localized repair + anti-corrosion coating + monitoring** is recommended.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Five-Phase Rehabilitation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **gate-controlled, iterative five-phase workflow** that the KTU 2024 scheme mandates for every rehabilitation project. The gate-control logic ensures that no phase is initiated without formal sign-off, mirroring **IS 15988** and **ACI 562** quality protocols.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([STRUCTURAL DISTRESS OBSERVED]) --> phase1Node["PHASE 1: PRELIMINARY INVESTIGATION"]
    phase1Node --> gate1Node{GATE 1: PDR APPROVED?}
    gate1Node -- NO --> phase1Node
    gate1Node -- YES --> phase2Node["PHASE 2: DETAILED CONDITION ASSESSMENT"]
    phase2Node --> ndt1Node["NDT: REBOUND HAMMER, UPV, COVER METER"]
    phase2Node --> ndt2Node["PDT: CORE EXTRACTION, CARBONATION TEST"]
    phase2Node --> ndt3Node["CHLORIDE PROFILE, HALF-CELL POTENTIAL"]
    ndt1Node --> condReportNode["CONDITION ASSESSMENT REPORT CAR"]
    ndt2Node --> condReportNode
    ndt3Node --> condReportNode
    condReportNode --> gate2Node{GATE 2: CAR SIGNED OFF?}
    gate2Node -- NO --> phase2Node
    gate2Node -- YES --> phase3Node["PHASE 3: DIAGNOSIS AND ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
    phase3Node --> diagNode["MAP DISTRESS TO MECHANISMS: CORROSION, ASR, OVERLOAD, CARBONATION"]
    diagNode --> gate3Node{GATE 3: DIAGNOSTIC REPORT APPROVED?}
    gate3Node -- NO --> phase3Node
    gate3Node -- YES --> phase4Node["PHASE 4: REHABILITATION STRATEGY AND DETAILED DESIGN"]
    phase4Node --> techSelNode["TECHNIQUE SELECTION: FRP, JACKETING, GROUTING, PT"]
    phase4Node --> designNode["STRUCTURAL REDESIGN WITH UPGRADED LOAD FACTORS"]
    phase4Node --> matlNode["MATERIAL SPECIFICATION AND COMPATIBILITY CHECK"]
    techSelNode --> dprNode["DETAILED PROJECT REPORT DPR"]
    designNode --> dprNode
    matlNode --> dprNode
    dprNode --> gate4Node{GATE 4: DPR APPROVED?}
    gate4Node -- NO --> phase4Node
    gate4Node -- YES --> phase5Node["PHASE 5: EXECUTION QA QC AND MONITORING"]
    phase5Node --> execNode["EXECUTION WITH MOCKUP TRIALS"]
    phase5Node --> qaNode["QA QC: CUBE TESTS, PULL OFF ADHESION, BOND TESTS"]
    phase5Node --> shmNode["STRUCTURAL HEALTH MONITORING SHM INSTALLATION"]
    execNode --> finalNode(["AS BUILT RECORDS AND MAINTENANCE MANUAL DELIVERED"])
    qaNode --> finalNode
    shmNode --> finalNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each `gate` node is a **decision diamond**. The "NO" branch loops back, enforcing iterative refinement. The "YES" branch enables phase progression. This is the KTU-recommended quality-gate approach.

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Decision-Making for Rehabilitation vs Reconstruction

This second diagram presents a **block-level functional architecture** for the **go/no-go decision** between rehabilitation and total reconstruction. This is one of the most frequently examined decision frameworks in the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    triggerNode([STRUCTURE SHOWS DISTRESS]) --> cdrNode["COMPUTE CAPACITY DEMAND RATIO CDR"]
    cdrNode --> cdrCheckNode{CDR < 1.0?}
    cdrCheckNode -- NO --> maintNode(["PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE ONLY"])
    cdrCheckNode -- YES --> rtiNode["COMPUTE REHABILITATION TRIGGER INDEX RTI"]
    rtiNode --> rtiCheckNode{RTI 0 TO 2 MILD?}
    rtiCheckNode -- YES --> cosmeticNode["COSMETIC REPAIR AND SEALING"]
    rtiCheckNode -- NO --> rtiCheckNode2{RTI 2 TO 5 MODERATE?}
    rtiCheckNode2 -- YES --> modRehabNode["STRENGTHENING: FRP OR JACKETING"]
    rtiCheckNode2 -- NO --> rtiCheckNode3{RTI 5 TO 8 SEVERE?}
    rtiCheckNode3 -- YES --> lccaNode["LIFE CYCLE COST ANALYSIS LCCA"]
    rtiCheckNode3 -- NO --> emergNode(["EMERGENCY STABILIZATION: SHORING, DEMOLITION CONSIDERED"])
    lccaNode --> lccaCheckNode{NPV REHAB LESS THAN NPV REBUILD?}
    lccaCheckNode -- YES --> majorRehabNode["MAJOR REHABILITATION PROGRAM"]
    lccaCheckNode -- NO --> rebuildNode(["PARTIAL OR FULL RECONSTRUCTION"])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Hierarchy of Structural Intervention

This schematic visualizes the **nested relationship** between the four intervention levels (Repair, Restoration, Strengthening, Rehabilitation), making it easy to recall in an exam setting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rehabNode["REHABILITATION UMBRELLA TERM"] --> repairNode["REPAIR LOCALIZED DEFECTS"]
    rehabNode --> restoreNode["RESTORATION TO ORIGINAL STATE"]
    rehabNode --> strengthenNode["STRENGTHENING BEYOND ORIGINAL CAPACITY"]
    rehabNode --> upgradeNode["FUNCTIONAL UPGRADATION AND MODERNIZATION"]
    repairNode --> rSub1["SPALL PATCHING"]
    repairNode --> rSub2["CRACK SEALING"]
    repairNode --> rSub3["CORROSION PATCH REPAIR"]
    restoreNode --> rsSub1["REPLACEMENT OF DAMAGED ELEMENTS"]
    restoreNode --> rsSub2["ARCHITECTURAL RESTORATION"]
    restoreNode --> rsSub3["AESTHETIC MATCHING"]
    strengthenNode --> sSub1["FRP WRAPPING"]
    strengthenNode --> sSub2["RC JACKETING"]
    strengthenNode --> sSub3["EXTERNAL POST TENSIONING"]
    strengthenNode --> sSub4["SECTION ENLARGEMENT"]
    upgradeNode --> uSub1["LOAD CAPACITY INCREASE"]
    upgradeNode --> uSub2["SEISMIC RETROFIT"]
    upgradeNode --> uSub3["FUNCTIONAL REPURPOSING"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish clearly between "Repair" and "Rehabilitation" of a concrete structure. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Repair** is a localized, low-intensity intervention aimed at restoring a specific defective zone (spalled concrete, hairline crack, surface corrosion patch) to its original functional state. It does not address root causes globally and does not aim to enhance structural capacity.

**Example:** Patching a spalled concrete cover on a balcony slab using polymer-modified mortar.

**Rehabilitation** is a comprehensive, engineered program that combines diagnosis, root-cause analysis, multiple repair/restoration/strengthening techniques, and a future maintenance plan to restore and possibly upgrade the structure's performance across its full lifecycle.

**Example:** A complete seismic retrofit of a 1980s RC frame building using FRP wrapping, RC jacketing, and supplemental dampers, accompanied by a 30-year SHM plan.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition of Repair (1 mark) + Example (0.5 mark) + Definition of Rehabilitation (1 mark) + Example (0.5 mark) = **3 marks**.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Performance Index" as used in rehabilitation engineering and state its decision threshold.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Performance Index (PI)** is the ratio of the available structural capacity to the required (demand) capacity for a given element:

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{required}}}
$$

A structure is considered **adequate** when PI $\geq 1.0$. When PI falls below 1.0, rehabilitation interventions are triggered in proportion to the magnitude of the deficit. The lower the PI, the more intensive the rehabilitation strategy required (minor patch repair, strengthening, or major rehabilitation).

> **Mark Distribution:** Formula (1.5 marks) + Threshold explanation (1 mark) + Engineering implication (0.5 mark) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **five-phase systematic workflow** for any rehabilitation project as per the KTU 2024 scheme. Highlight the deliverables of each phase.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Phase 1 — Preliminary Investigation (1.5 marks)**
Activities: Historical record review, original drawing examination, visual inspection, distress documentation, photographic survey.
Deliverable: **Preliminary Distress Report (PDR)** identifying probable zones of concern.

**Phase 2 — Detailed Condition Assessment (1.5 marks)**
Activities: NDT (Rebound Hammer, UPV, Cover Meter, Half-Cell Potential), PDT (core extraction, pull-out, chemical tests for chloride/carbonation), in-situ load tests.
Deliverable: **Condition Assessment Report (CAR)** with quantitative strength ($f_c'$, $f_y$, cover profile), durability index, and serviceability deflection.

**Phase 3 — Diagnosis and Root-Cause Analysis (1 mark)**
Activities: Mapping observed distress to mechanisms — corrosion, ASR, sulfate attack, carbonation, overload, fatigue, design deficiency, construction defect. Distinguishing active vs. passive distress.
Deliverable: **Diagnostic Report** with prioritized root causes.

**Phase 4 — Rehabilitation Strategy and Detailed Design (1.5 marks)**
Activities: Selection of techniques (FRP, jacketing, grouting, PT, cathodic protection), structural redesign with upgraded load and safety factors, material compatibility and durability design, BoQ preparation.
Deliverable: **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** with GFC drawings and method statement.

**Phase 5 — Execution, QA/QC, and SHM (1.5 marks)**
Activities: Mock-up trials, material qualification, on-site cube and pull-off tests, FRP bond tests, installation of long-term SHM sensors (strain gauges, accelerometers, corrosion probes).
Deliverable: **As-Built Records + 30-year Maintenance Manual** handed over to the owner.

> **Mark Distribution:** 5 phases × ~1.2–1.5 marks each, with stress on deliverables and gate-control logic.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of the **Tuutti two-stage corrosion model**, derive the expression for the **residual service life** of a reinforced concrete element exposed to chloride ingress. Discuss the implications of each term for rehabilitation decision-making.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Tuutti model divides the service life of an RC element in a chloride environment into two sequential stages: **Initiation Period ($t_i$)** and **Propagation Period ($t_p$)**.

**Initiation Period Derivation (2.5 marks):**
The chloride ingress follows Fick's Second Law of Diffusion:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_c \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

For semi-infinite domain with surface concentration $C_s$ constant:

$$
C(x,t) = C_s \cdot \left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c \cdot t}}\right)\right]
$$

Setting $C(x, t_i) = C_{crit}$ and solving for $t_i$:

$$
t_i = \frac{x^2}{4 \cdot D_c} \cdot \left[\text{erf}^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{C_{crit}}{C_s}\right)\right]^{-2}
$$

where $x$ = cover depth, $D_c$ = chloride diffusion coefficient, $C_{crit}$ = critical chloride concentration.

**Propagation Period Derivation (2 marks):**
From Faraday's Law of Electrolysis, the bar section loss per year is:

$$
\Delta d = 0.0116 \cdot i_{corr} \text{ (mm/year)}
$$

The propagation period to reach critical damage:

$$
t_p = \frac{d_{cr}}{0.0116 \cdot i_{corr}}
$$

**Total and Residual Service Life (1 mark):**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = t_i + t_p
$$

$$
t_{\text{residual}} = t_{\text{total}} - t_{\text{elapsed}}
$$

**Rehabilitation Implications (0.5 mark):**
- Increasing cover (e.g., by section enlargement) directly increases $t_i$ quadratically.
- Using low-permeability concrete or coatings reduces $D_c$, also increasing $t_i$ quadratically.
- Cathodic protection extends $t_p$ indefinitely.
- If $t_{\text{residual}} < $ design horizon, **rehabilitation is mandatory**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Define the **Rehabilitation Trigger Index (RTI)** and derive its expression from the Capacity-Demand Ratio. Classify structures into rehabilitation bands based on RTI values.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Rehabilitation Trigger Index (RTI) is a dimensionless 0–10 scalar that quantifies the urgency and intensity of structural intervention required, derived from the Capacity-Demand Ratio (CDR) of the structural element.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Step 1: Define CDR:

$$
\text{CDR} = \frac{C_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{applied}}}
$$

Step 2: Express available capacity after $t$ years of degradation:

$$
C_{\text{available}}(t) = 0.85 \cdot f_c'(t) \cdot A_c(t) + f_y(t) \cdot A_{s,\text{eff}}(t)
$$

Step 3: Express bar diameter loss using Faraday's Law:

$$
d_b(t) = d_{b,0} - 0.0232 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot t
$$

Step 4: Compute CDR for the time $t$, then derive RTI:

$$
\text{RTI} = 10 \cdot (1 - \text{CDR})
$$

**Classification (2 marks):**

| **RTI Range** | **Band** | **Recommended Action** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 to 1.5 | No action | Routine maintenance |
| 1.5 to 3.0 | Minor | Cosmetic repair, sealing, coating |
| 3.0 to 5.0 | Moderate | Localized strengthening (FRP, patching) |
| 5.0 to 7.5 | Major | Global strengthening (jacketing, PT) |
| 7.5 to 10.0 | Critical | Emergency shoring, partial/full reconstruction |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The RTI provides a single, defensible, quantitative metric for triggering and sizing rehabilitation, replacing subjective engineering judgment with a coded scale aligned with IS 15988:2013.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare and contrast **RC Jacketing**, **Steel Jacketing**, and **FRP Wrapping** as column rehabilitation techniques. Present your answer in a structured comparative table covering **strength gain, ductility, constructability, durability, cost, and applicability**.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Column rehabilitation is one of the most common interventions in aging RC buildings. The three principal jacketing techniques — RC Jacketing, Steel Jacketing, and FRP Wrapping — offer distinctly different performance, cost, and applicability profiles.

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **RC Jacketing** | **Steel Jacketing** | **FRP Wrapping** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strength Gain** | 50–100% increase in axial capacity | 30–60% increase | 10–40% increase (mainly confinement) |
| **Ductility Enhancement** | Moderate; depends on new longitudinal steel and ties | High; excellent energy absorption | Very high; up to 4× ductility |
| **Constructability** | Labor-intensive, requires formwork, curing time | Moderate; welding-intensive | Excellent; rapid installation |
| **Durability** | Comparable to original; needs cover control | Susceptible to corrosion; needs anti-corrosion coating | Excellent; CFRP immune to corrosion, AFRP/GFRP degrade in UV/alkali |
| **Cross-Section Increase** | Significant (typically 75–150 mm added) | Moderate (steel plate thickness 6–12 mm) | Minimal (FRP thickness 1–5 mm) |
| **Cost (per column)** | Low to moderate | Moderate to high | High (CFRP), moderate (GFRP) |
| **Seismic Performance** | Good if detailed properly | Excellent for ductility | Excellent for confinement |
| **Heritage Buildings** | Not suitable (large size change) | Suitable (slim) | Most suitable (minimal geometry change) |
| **Fire Resistance** | Excellent (inherent) | Good | Poor (requires fire protection) |
| **Speed of Execution** | Slow (formwork + 28-day curing) | Moderate (welding + grouting) | Fast (1–2 days per column) |

**Selection Guidance (1 mark):**
- **RC Jacketing** is preferred when budget is constrained and size increase is acceptable.
- **Steel Jacketing** is preferred where high ductility and impact resistance are required.
- **FRP Wrapping** is preferred for heritage structures, marine environments, and rapid retrofit projects, but requires fire protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by stating "FRP wrapping is the best technique" without condition-specific justification. Always tie the recommendation to **performance requirement + environmental exposure + geometric constraint + budget**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Re-read this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Hierarchy of intervention:** Repair $\subset$ Restoration $\subset$ Strengthening $\subset$ **Rehabilitation** (umbrella).
- **Five-phase workflow:** Preliminary Investigation → Detailed Condition Assessment → Diagnosis → Strategy & Design → Execution & Monitoring.
- **Rehabilitation is triggered** when **CDR < 1.0** or equivalently **RTI > 1.5**.
- **Performance Index formula:** $\text{PI} = P_{\text{actual}} \div P_{\text{required}} \geq 1.0$.
- **RTI formula:** $\text{RTI} = 10 \times (1 - \text{CDR})$, with bands 0–1.5, 1.5–3, 3–5, 5–7.5, 7.5–10.
- **Faraday's corrosion law:** $d_b(t) = d_{b,0} - 0.0232 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot t$ (mm, with $i_{corr}$ in μA/cm² and $t$ in years).
- **Tuutti model:** Total life = Initiation ($t_i$, Fickian) + Propagation ($t_p$, Faraday's law).
- **Initiation period formula:** $t_i = x^2 / (4 D_c) \cdot [\text{erf}^{-1}(1 - C_{crit}/C_s)]^{-2}$.
- **Critical chloride threshold:** $C_{crit} = 0.4\%$ by mass of cement.
- **Performance-based vs. prescriptive:** KTU 2024 mandates **performance-based** rehabilitation.
- **Seismic performance levels (IS 15988:2013):** IO, LS, CP corresponding to SE, DBE, MCE.
- **Code references:** IS 456:2000, IS 13311 (NDT), IS 15988:2013 (Seismic Evaluation), ACI 562, ACI 440.2R (FRP), ACI 546R (Repair Materials).
- **LCCA rule:** Choose rehabilitation **only if NPV of rehab < NPV of reconstruction** over the design horizon.
- **Sustainability hook:** Rehabilitation reduces embodied carbon by **40–60%** vs. reconstruction.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing **strengthening** with **rehabilitation**; remember strengthening is one component of rehabilitation.
- **Common numerical trap:** Forgetting to convert $i_{corr}$ units (μA/cm²) and $t$ units (years) consistently when using the Faraday constant.
- **Decision matrix for distress type:** Cracks → epoxy injection; Spalling → patch repair; Corrosion (10–25%) → jacketing; Severe corrosion ($\geq 25\%$) → section replacement; Seismic deficiency → RC/FRP jacketing.
- **Heritage buildings:** Prefer FRP (minimal geometry change) + restoration of original aesthetics.
- **SHM is mandatory:** Every rehabilitated structure should have a long-term monitoring plan handed to the owner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repair** is defined as the process of restoring a damaged, deteriorated, or defective structural element to its originally intended serviceable condition, functionality, and load-carrying capacity, without necessarily upgrading its design strength.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Repair** is one of the three principal interventions in the structural health management hierarchy, sitting below *Rehabilitation* and *Strengthening* on the intervention scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Repair** → Restores original functionality (no design upgrade)
> - **Rehabilitation** → Partially restores/improves functionality to a defined performance level
> - **Strengthening** → Upgrades load capacity beyond the original design level

## 1.2 Hierarchical Intervention Spectrum

$$
\text{Maintenance} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Repair} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rehabilitation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Strengthening}
$$

| Intervention Level | Performance Goal | Design Upgrade? |
|---|---|---|
| **Maintenance** | Routine preservation | ❌ No |
| **Repair** | Restore to original | ❌ No |
| **Rehabilitation** | Restore to acceptable level | ⚠️ Marginal |
| **Strengthening** | Exceed original capacity | ✅ Yes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a car with a cracked windshield:**
> - **Maintenance** = Wiping the windshield clean 🧽
> - **Repair** = Filling the crack with resin to restore clarity (same as before) 🔧
> - **Rehabilitation** = Replacing the windshield with a standard aftermarket one (equivalent function) 🚗
> - **Strengthening** = Replacing with a laminated ballistic-glass windshield (better than before) 🛡️

The windshield's original *design speed rating* stays the same after repair — it just looks and works like new again. This is exactly what structural repair does to a beam, slab, or column.

## 1.4 Why Repair Becomes Necessary — Trigger Conditions

Structural repair is triggered when **serviceability** or **durability** issues are observed:

- **Cracking** (flexural, shear, shrinkage, thermal)
- **Spalling** of cover concrete
- **Corrosion** of reinforcement
- **Surface deterioration** (carbonation, sulphate attack)
- **Leakage** / dampness in structural members
- **Loss of bond** between old and new concrete
- **Fire damage**, impact damage, or accidental overload effects

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard (IS 15988:2013):** Defines durability, repair, and rehabilitation terminology used in Indian structural practice.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Repair Strategy (KTU Exam Favourite)

1. **Diagnosis** — What is wrong and why?
2. **Prescription** — What material/method fixes it?
3. **Execution** — How to apply it on-site with quality control?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophy of Repair

The repair philosophy is built on the principle of **"minimum intervention, maximum restoration"**. The engineer must ask:

> "What is the *root cause* of distress, and what is the *minimum effective intervention* that returns the element to its original design intent?"

### Logical Flow of a Repair Decision

1. **Observation** → Visual signs of distress (cracks, spalls, rust stains)
2. **Investigation** → Non-destructive testing (Rebound hammer, UPV, GPR)
3. **Diagnosis** → Root cause analysis (e.g., carbonation-induced corrosion)
4. **Selection** → Choose repair method from approved taxonomy
5. **Execution** → Surface prep, material application, curing
6. **Validation** → Post-repair testing and monitoring

## 2.2 Classification of Repair Methods

### A. Based on Structural Element

| Element | Common Repair Methods |
|---|---|
| **Concrete Beams/Slabs** | Epoxy injection, polymer-modified mortar, jacketing |
| **Columns** | Concrete jacketing, FRP wrapping, fibre wrapping |
| **Masonry** | Grouting, stitching, repointing |
| **Steel Structures** | Welding, bolted splice plates, FRP lamination |
| **Foundations** | Underpinning, grouting, micropiles |

### B. Based on Repair Material

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification — Memorise This Table:**

| Repair Type | Material Used | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymer-Modified Mortar (PMM)** | Cement + polymer latex (SBR, acrylic) | Surface patches, honeycombing |
| **Epoxy-Based Repair** | Epoxy resin + hardener | Crack injection, bonding |
| **Cementitious Grout** | OPC + sand + admixtures | Voids, cracks > 3 mm |
| **Shotcrete/Gunite** | Sprayed concrete | Large surface repairs |
| **Micro-concrete** | Pre-bagged, flowable | Deep patches, jacketing |
| **FRP Composites** | Carbon/Glass fibre + epoxy | Flexural/Shear strengthening |
| **Corrosion Inhibitors** | Migratory (MCI) or admixed | Embedded in repair patches |

## 2.3 Bonding Between Old and New Concrete — The Critical Interface

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** The weakest link in any repair is the **bond interface** between old substrate and new repair material. Students often ignore this and lose 2–3 marks.

**Factors affecting bond strength:**

$$
f_b = f(\text{Surface roughness, Cleanliness, W/C ratio, Curing, Bonding agent})
$$

- **Surface preparation** — Sandblasting, water jetting, chipping
- **Bonding agents** — Epoxy resin, SBR latex, acrylic bonding agent
- **Mechanical key** — Shear keys, dowels, anchors

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are KTU-exam standard. The | symbol has been replaced with \vert for markdown safety.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Stress under axial load | N/mm² |
| 2 | $f_b = \mu \cdot \sigma_n$ | Frictional bond at interface | N/mm² |
| 3 | $d_{eq} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^2}{\sum n_i d_i}$ | Equivalent bar diameter | mm |
| 4 | $L_{lap} \geq \vert L_d \vert$ | Lap length ≥ development length | mm |
| 5 | $L_d = \dfrac{0.87 f_y \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | Development length (IS 456) | mm |
| 6 | $V_{epoxy} = L_{crack} \times w_{crack} \times d$ | Epoxy volume for injection | mm³ |
| 7 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{V_{corr}}{V_{metal}} \times 100$ | Corrosion percentage | % |
| 8 | $R = R_0 - k\sqrt{t}$ | Carbonation depth (√t law) | mm |

> **Note on Formula 8 (Carbonation):** $R$ = carbonation depth at time $t$, $R_0$ = initial depth, $k$ = carbonation coefficient (mm/year^0.5).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Repair

- **Service-life extension** without demolition (sustainability driver)
- **Cost saving** vs. replacement (typically 30–50% cheaper)
- **Heritage preservation** for listed buildings
- **Disaster recovery** post-earthquake/flood/cyclone
- **Compliance** with updated durability codes (IS 15988, ACI 546R)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Standard Repair Selection Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive derivation shown below. Every algebraic transition is explicitly captured.**

### Problem Context

A reinforced concrete column shows:
- Vertical crack of length $L = 1200 \text{ mm}$
- Crack width $w = 0.3 \text{ mm}$
- Depth $d = 200 \text{ mm}$ (estimated from covermeter)
- Concrete grade M25, exposure Moderate
- Observed cause: Drying shrinkage (non-structural crack)

**Task:** Calculate the volume of epoxy resin required to inject the crack by pressure injection.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric volume of the crack prism.**

The crack is modelled as a thin rectangular parallelepiped. The volume is the product of length, width, and depth:

$$
V_{crack} = L \times w \times d
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
V_{crack} = 1200 \text{ mm} \times 0.3 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the multiplication explicitly.**

$$
V_{crack} = 1200 \times 0.3 \times 200
$$

First, compute $1200 \times 0.3$:

$$
1200 \times 0.3 = 360
$$

Then, multiply by $200$:

$$
360 \times 200 = 72{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{crack} = 72{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to engineering-friendly units.**

$$
1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{72{,}000}{1000} = 72 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
1 \text{ litre} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{72}{1000} = 0.072 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply wastage factor (site practice).**

Epoxy injection is non-trivial; a wastage factor of $\eta = 1.25$ is standard:

$$
V_{epoxy} = \eta \times V_{crack} = 1.25 \times 72{,}000
$$

$$
V_{epoxy} = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 90 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.090 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with units and reporting precision.**

$$
\boxed{V_{epoxy} = 0.090 \text{ L} \;\; (\approx 90 \text{ mL})}
$$

### 3.2 Corrosion Mass-Loss Diagnostic Derivation

When steel reinforcement corrodes, the residual cross-sectional area governs residual strength. Starting from the initial bar diameter $\phi_0$:

$$
\phi_{residual} = \phi_0 - \Delta\phi
$$

where $\Delta\phi$ is the diameter loss. If the corrosion penetration is $x$ (mm/year) and the exposure time is $t$ (years), then:

$$
\Delta\phi = 2 x t
$$

Therefore:

$$
\phi_{residual} = \phi_0 - 2 x t
$$

The residual load capacity of the bar (yield strength basis) is:

$$
P_{residual} = f_y \times A_{residual} = f_y \times \frac{\pi}{4} \phi_{residual}^2
$$

Substituting $\phi_{residual}$:

$$
P_{residual} = f_y \times \frac{\pi}{4} (\phi_0 - 2 x t)^2
$$

The **percentage residual capacity** is:

$$
\%P = \frac{(\phi_0 - 2 x t)^2}{\phi_0^2} \times 100
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Corrosion Loss

**Given:** $\phi_0 = 16 \text{ mm}$, $x = 0.05 \text{ mm/year}$, $t = 20 \text{ years}$.

**Step 1** — Compute diameter loss:

$$
\Delta\phi = 2 \times 0.05 \times 20 = 2.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute residual diameter:

$$
\phi_{residual} = 16 - 2.0 = 14.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute percentage residual:

$$
\%P = \frac{(14.0)^2}{(16.0)^2} \times 100 = \frac{196}{256} \times 100
$$

$$
\%P = 76.5625 \%
$$

**Step 4** — Decision rule:

$$
\boxed{\%P = 76.56\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Repair mandatory (loss} > 25\% \text{)}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Repair Decision-Making Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Distress Observed] --> node2{Identify Type}
    node2 -->|Crack| node3[Crack Width Test]
    node2 -->|Spall| node4[Rebound Hammer Test]
    node2 -->|Corrosion| node5[Half-Cell Potential]
    node3 --> node6{Width Threshold}
    node6 -->|less than 0.2 mm| node7[Surface Sealing]
    node6 -->|0.2 to 1.0 mm| node8[Epoxy Injection]
    node6 -->|greater than 1.0 mm| node9[Routing and Sealing]
    node4 --> node10{Strength Loss}
    node10 -->|less than 25 percent| node11[PMM Patch]
    node10 -->|greater than 25 percent| node12[Jacketing]
    node5 --> node13{Risk Level}
    node13 -->|less than 10 percent| node14[Inhibitor]
    node13 -->|10 to 50 percent| node15[Patch + Inhibitor]
    node13 -->|greater than 50 percent| node16[Bar Replacement]
    node7 --> node17[End: Quality Check]
    node8 --> node17
    node9 --> node17
    node11 --> node17
    node12 --> node17
    node14 --> node17
    node15 --> node17
    node16 --> node17
```

## 4.2 Repair Method Classification — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Repair Methods] --> nodeB[Concrete Repair]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Steel Repair]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Masonry Repair]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Epoxy Injection]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Polymer Mortar]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Shotcrete]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Jacketing]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Welding]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Splice Plates]
    nodeC --> nodeK[FRP Wrapping]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Grouting]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Repointing]
    nodeD --> nodeN[Stitching]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Repair Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1[Phase 1: Investigation] --> phase2[Phase 2: Diagnosis]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Design of Repair]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Surface Preparation]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Material Application]
    phase5 --> phase6[Phase 6: Quality Assurance]
    phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7: Monitoring]
    phase7 --> phase8[Closed Loop Feedback]
    phase8 -.-> phase2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The feedback loop from monitoring → diagnosis ensures **adaptive maintenance** for the rest of the structure's life.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]
**Define the term "Repair" of a structure. How is it different from "Rehabilitation"?**

**Model Answer:**
**Repair** is the process of restoring a deteriorated or damaged structural element to its original serviceable condition, function, and load-carrying capacity, *without* upgrading the design strength.

**Rehabilitation**, on the other hand, involves partial restoration or improvement of a structure's function to a defined acceptable performance level, which may marginally exceed the original design intent.

| Aspect | Repair | Rehabilitation |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Restore original | Improve to acceptable level |
| Design upgrade | No | Possible (marginal) |
| Example | Patch a spalled column | Restore a fire-damaged building to code |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]
**List any four common causes of distress in reinforced concrete structures requiring repair.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Corrosion of reinforcement** due to carbonation or chloride ingress
2. **Freeze-thaw deterioration** in cold climates
3. **Chemical attack** by sulphates, acids, or alkalis
4. **Structural overload** leading to flexural/shear cracking
5. **Shrinkage cracks** due to improper curing
6. **Construction defects** such as honeycombing or inadequate cover

*(Any four with brief explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Repair Method Selection

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **methods of repair** for reinforced concrete beams damaged due to flexural cracking. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure of **epoxy injection** for crack repair, with a labelled diagram. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

The following methods are widely used for flexural crack repair in RC beams:

1. **Epoxy Injection** — For cracks 0.2–1.0 mm wide; pressure-injected epoxy restores monolithic action and seals against moisture ingress.
2. **Polymer-Modified Mortar (PMM) Patching** — Surface preparation + SBR/acrylic modified cement mortar overlay for shallow surface cracks.
3. **External Bonding of FRP Laminates** — Carbon/glass fibre sheets bonded with epoxy along the tension face for flexural strengthening.
4. **Steel Plate Bonding** — Epoxy-bonded mild steel plates on the soffit of beams.
5. **Section Enlargement** — Adding concrete and reinforcement to increase effective depth $d$.
6. **External Prestressing** — Applying post-tensioned tendons to counter cracks and reduce deflections.

> **Selection criterion:** Crack width, structural significance, and exposure conditions.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Epoxy Injection Procedure

1. **Surface Cleaning** — Remove dust, oil, and loose material along the crack line.
2. **Surface Sealing** — Apply a sealing compound (e.g., epoxy paste) over the crack face, leaving injection ports spaced at 150–300 mm.
3. **Port Installation** — Mount injection nipples (T-ports) at the sealing points.
4. **Mixing Epoxy** — Combine resin and hardener in prescribed ratio (typically 2:1 or 1:1 by volume).
5. **Pressure Injection** — Inject epoxy at 0.2–0.7 MPa starting from the lowest port, moving upward.
6. **Curing** — Allow 24–48 hours for full cure.
7. **Finishing** — Remove ports, grind seal flush with the surface.

```
  Surface Sealant (epoxy paste)
  ===================================
  |  ⊙  ⊙  ⊙  ⊙  ⊙  ⊙            |
  |  P1 P2 P3 P4 P5 P6           |   <- Injection Ports
  ===================================
  |  [   Cracked Concrete    ]    |   <- Beam cross-section
  ===================================
         ↑ pressure 0.2–0.7 MPa
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **causes and types of cracks** in concrete structures. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **shotcrete/gunite repair** technique with its applications and limitations. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Causes of Cracks:**

1. **Plastic Shrinkage** — Water loss in fresh concrete
2. **Drying Shrinkage** — Long-term moisture loss
3. **Thermal Movement** — Diurnal/seasonal temperature variation
4. **Structural Loading** — Flexural, shear, or torsional overstress
5. **Corrosion** — Expansion of rust (≈ 6× steel volume)
6. **Settlement** — Foundation differential movement
7. **Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR)** — Internal expansion
8. **Construction Defects** — Improper curing, joints, detailing

**Types of Cracks (by width — IS 15988):**

| Class | Width (mm) | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Hairline | < 0.1 | Monitor |
| Fine | 0.1 – 0.3 | Seal |
| Medium | 0.3 – 1.0 | Inject |
| Wide | > 1.0 | Structural repair |

#### Model Solution to (b) — Shotcrete Repair

**Shotcrete** (or **gunite**) is pneumatically projected concrete or mortar applied at high velocity onto a prepared surface.

**Process:**
1. Surface preparation (chip, clean, saturate)
2. Install reinforcement/mesh if needed
3. Wet-mix or dry-mix shotcrete projected via nozzle
4. Build up to required thickness in layers
5. Trowel for finish; cure for 7 days minimum

**Applications:**
- Repair of deteriorated bridge piers, beams, slabs
- Tunnel linings
- Fire-damaged structures
- Large overhead repairs

**Limitations:**
- Requires skilled nozzleman
- High rebound losses (5–15% for wet-mix, 15–30% for dry-mix)
- Dust generation in dry process
- Difficult QC of in-place density

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. Confusing **Repair** with **Rehabilitation/Strengthening** — board examiners deduct 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **surface preparation** before any repair material application (2 marks lost).
> 3. **No mention of curing** for cementitious repair systems.
> 4. Drawing diagrams without proper labels — full marks only with labelled sketches.
> 5. **Skipping quality control** steps (testing rebound/UPV post-repair).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before exam:**

- **Repair ≠ Rehabilitation ≠ Strengthening** — know the distinction cold
- **Crack width thresholds** → 0.2 mm (seal), 0.2–1.0 mm (inject), > 1.0 mm (route and seal)
- **Epoxy injection pressure** = 0.2 to 0.7 MPa; **port spacing** = 150–300 mm
- **Bond interface** is the weakest zone — always specify surface prep + bonding agent
- **Polymer-Modified Mortar (PMM)** uses SBR / acrylic latex; widely used for surface patches
- **Shotcrete** has 2 variants — wet-mix (lower rebound) and dry-mix (gunite)
- **FRP wrapping** with CFRP/GFRP is for flexural/axial strengthening, not pure repair
- **Corrosion %** > 25% → bar replacement is mandatory
- **Carbonation** follows $\sqrt{t}$ law; $R = R_0 - k\sqrt{t}$
- Always cite **IS 15988:2013** for durability and **ACI 546R** for repair
- **Curing period** ≥ 7 days for cementitious, 24–48 h for epoxy
- **Half-cell potential** test: > –350 mV → high corrosion risk (ASTM C876)
- Mention **quality control** in every repair question: rebound hammer, UPV, pull-off test
- **Lap splice** for replacement bars: $L_{lap} \geq L_d$ (IS 456 Cl. 26.2.5)
- **Cost-effectiveness:** Repair typically 30–50% cheaper than replacement

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retrofit and Strengthening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Retrofit and Strengthening # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme) > [!NOTE] > **Retrofit** is the engineering intervention that modifies the **structural system** of an existing building so that its **overall performance** against revised code requirements (typically seismic, wind, or load) is enhanced beyond its original design intent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strengthening** is the engineering intervention applied to **individual structural members** (beams, columns, slabs, walls) to increase their **load-carrying capacity, stiffness, or ductility** beyond the as-built capacity. The two terms are often used interchangeably in field practice, but the KTU examiner expects you to distinguish them. A *retrofit* is **system-level** (e.g., adding shear walls, base isolators), whereas *strengthening* is **member-level** (e.g., column jacketing, FRP wrapping). ## 1.2 The Repair–Strengthening–Retrofit–Rehabilitation Hierarchy | Term | Scope | Goal | |------|-------|------| | **Repair** | Local / member | Restore original capacity lost due to damage | | **Strengthening** | Member | Enhance capacity above original design | | **Retrofit** | Whole structure / system | Upgrade system behaviour to meet new code demands | | **Rehabilitation** | Combined | Restore + enhance to an acceptable modern performance level | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a 1990s Maruti 800 car.** > - **Repair** = fixing a dented door. > - **Strengthening** = adding a roll-cage *inside the same car* to make the door stronger in a crash. > - **Retrofit** = redesigning the chassis geometry or adding modern crumple zones to the whole car. > - **Rehabilitation** = doing all the above plus upgrading the engine for current emission norms. ## 1.4 Why Retrofit and Strengthening Are Needed > [!IMPORTANT] > A structure may demand intervention for any of the following **five primary reasons** (favourite KTU short-answer topic): > 1. **Seismic vulnerability** — older buildings designed before IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 or its predecessors. > 2. **Code revision** — new IS 456:2000, IS 13920:2016 ductile-detailing provisions. > 3. **Change in functional use** — residential to storage → live load rises from **2 kN/m² to 10 kN/m²**. > 4. **Material deterioration** — chloride-induced corrosion, carbonation, ASR, sulphate attack. > 5. **Design / construction deficiencies** — honeycomb concrete, insufficient cover, lap splice errors. ## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics - Concrete compressive strength assumed for jacketing: **minimum M 25** (IS 15988:2013). - Bonding epoxy tensile strength: **≥ 30 MPa** (typical two-part epoxy). - CFRP modulus of elasticity: **≥ 165 GPa**. - Target ductility for seismic retrofit: **displacement ductility μΔ ≥ 3.0** for RC frame buildings. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Strengthening Techniques > [!NOTE] > The KTU syllabus (Module 1) classifies strengthening of *RC members* under the following structured taxonomy. Memorising this is worth 3 marks. ### A. Section Enlargement (Jacketing) - **Concrete Jacketing** — most common, increases cross-section and adds longitudinal steel. - **Steel Jacketing** — bolted/welded steel plates or rolled sections. - **FRP Jacketing** — Carbon (CFRP), Glass (GFRP), Aramid (AFRP) wraps. ### B. Externally Bonded Reinforcement (EBR) - Steel plates bonded with epoxy. - FRP laminates bonded with structural adhesive. ### C. External Prestressing - Post-tensioning tendons added on the outside of the beam. - Used for bridges and long-span floor beams. ### D. Fibre-Reinforced Concrete (FRC) Overlays - Thin (15–30 mm) high-performance fibre-reinforced layer on the tension face. ### E. Supplemental Members - Adding new beams, columns, or walls to share the load. ### F. Grout / Epoxy Injection - Pressure injection of cementitious or polymer grout into cracks. ## 2.2 Classification of Retrofit Techniques (System-Level) | Retrofit Strategy | Best Use Case | |-------------------|---------------| | **Addition of Shear Walls** | Soft-storey buildings, irregular plans | | **Addition of Braced Frames** | Industrial sheds, low-rise frames | | **Base Isolation** | Hospitals, heritage buildings, data centres | | **Energy Dissipation Devices** | High-rise RC frames (viscous dampers, BRBs) | | **Mass Reduction** | Over-massive masonry buildings | | **Confinement Enhancement** | Columns with inadequate lap-splice length | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following are the **most-frequently-tested formulas** in KTU ESE for Retrofit & Strengthening. Box this for quick revision. $$ \begin{aligned} \phi &= \text{diameter of column / member (mm)} \\ P_{\text{strengthened}} &= 0.4 \, f_{ck,\text{new}} \, A_{c,\text{total}} + 0.67 \, f_{y} \, A_{\text{sc,total}} \\ P_{\text{axial increase}} &= \Delta P = 0.4 \, f_{ck,\text{new}} \, (A_{c,\text{new}} - A_{c,\text{old}}) \\ \sigma_{\text{FRP allowable}} &= \varepsilon_{\text{fu}} \, E_{\text{f}} \, \psi \, \le 0.55 \, f_{\text{fu}} \\ M_{\text{enhanced}} &= 0.87 \, f_{y} \, A_{s,\text{add}} \, d_{\text{eff}} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{s,\text{add}} f_{y}}{b d_{\text{eff}} f_{ck}}\right) \\ \text{Confinement ratio } \lambda &= \dfrac{f_{cc}' - f_{c}'}{f_{c}'} \propto k_{e} \, k_{a} \, \dfrac{2 t_{\text{j}} f_{\text{j}}}{D \, f_{c}'} \\ \text{Displacement Ductility } \mu_{\Delta} &= \dfrac{\Delta_{u}}{\Delta_{y}} \ge 3.0 \end{aligned} $$ | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |--------|---------|------| | $f_{ck}$ | Characteristic compressive strength of concrete | MPa | | $f_y$ | Yield strength of steel | MPa | | $A_{sc}$ | Total area of longitudinal steel in column | mm² | | $f_{fu}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of FRP | MPa | | $E_f$ | Elastic modulus of FRP | MPa | | $t_j$ | Jacket thickness | mm | | $D$ | Column diameter / width | mm | | $k_e, k_a$ | Effectiveness & arching factors (Lam & Teng) | dimensionless | | $\psi$ | FRP strength reduction factor (≈ 0.85 for RC) | dimensionless | ## 2.4 Why "How" — Engineering Logic > [!NOTE] > **Why jacket a column from outside instead of adding bars and recasting?** > Because the **existing structure remains in service** during the intervention (minimal downtime), and confinement through the jacket converts a brittle unconfined concrete into a ductile confined core — this directly addresses the **weak-column strong-beam** failure mode that killed many buildings in the **2001 Bhuj** and **2015 Nepal** earthquakes. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Post-earthquake campaign**: After 2001 Bhuj, Gujarat Housing Board retrofitted ~ **4,500 schools** using RC jacketing and shear-wall addition. - **Bridge strengthening**: The **Howrah Bridge** and **Vidyasagar Setu** have used external prestressing. - **Heritage conservation**: **Rashtrapati Bhavan** uses base isolation to protect against future quakes without altering appearance. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Increase in Axial Capacity of a Column by Concrete Jacketing > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most-tested 14-mark derivation** under "Strengthening of Columns" in KTU ESE Paper-II questions. ### Problem Statement An existing square RC column of size **300 mm × 300 mm** is reinforced with **4 bars of 20 mm dia (Fe 415)**. Concrete is **M 20** ($f_{ck} = 20$ MPa). The column is jacketed on all four sides with **75 mm thick M 25 concrete** and additional **4 bars of 16 mm dia (Fe 415)**. Determine the enhanced axial load capacity. ### Step 1 — Identify Original Section Properties Existing column cross-section: $$ B_{\text{old}} \times D_{\text{old}} = 300 \times 300 \, \text{mm} $$ $$ A_{c,\text{old}} = 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,old}} = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^{2} = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ ### Step 2 — Identify Jacketed Section Properties After 75 mm jacket on all four sides, the new size becomes: $$ B_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{new}} = 300 + 2(75) = 450 \text{ mm} $$ $$ A_{c,\text{new (total)}} = 450 \times 450 = 202{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,add}} = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^{2} = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,total}} = 1256.64 + 804.25 = 2060.89 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply IS 456:2000 Cl. 39.3 (Short Axial Capacity) The IS 456 design expression for a short column subjected to axial load only is: $$ P_{u} = 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_{c} + 0.67 \, f_{y} \, A_{sc} $$ For the **original** column ($f_{ck}=20$, $f_y=415$): $$ P_{u,\text{old}} = 0.4 \times 20 \times 90{,}000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1256.64 $$ $$ P_{u,\text{old}} = 720{,}000 + 348{,}872 = 1{,}068{,}872 \, \text{N} \approx 1069 \, \text{kN} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Jacketed Capacity For the jacketed section, the **stronger concrete (M 25, $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa)** is used for the **new added concrete**; the original core may conservatively retain $f_{ck}=20$ MPa. KTU examiner-friendly approach: use $f_{ck,\text{new}} = 25$ MPa for the *added* area and 20 MPa for the core. $$ A_{c,\text{added}} = A_{c,\text{new}} - A_{c,\text{old}} = 202{,}500 - 90{,}000 = 112{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ P_{u,\text{new}} = 0.4 (20)(90{,}000) + 0.4 (25)(112{,}500) + 0.67 (415)(2060.89) $$ $$ P_{u,\text{new}} = 720{,}000 + 1{,}125{,}000 + 572{,}886 = 2{,}417{,}886 \, \text{N} \approx 2418 \, \text{kN} $$ ### Step 5 — Net Gain and Percentage Increase $$ \Delta P_{u} = P_{u,\text{new}} - P_{u,\text{old}} = 2418 - 1069 = 1349 \, \text{kN} $$ $$ \text{Percentage Increase} = \dfrac{1349}{1069} \times 100 = 126.2\% $$ ### Step 6 — Verification (Bending Consideration, IS 15988) > [!NOTE] > In real seismic strengthening, axial alone is not enough. **P-M interaction** must be re-checked. IS 15988:2013 (Guidelines for Retrofitting of RC Structures) recommends **SP-16 interaction diagram** to plot $(P_{u,\text{new}}, M_{u,\text{new}})$ and compare with the **demand point** from analysis. The point must lie inside the P-M envelope of the jacketed section. | Quantity | Original | Jacketed | Gain | |----------|----------|----------|------| | Concrete area (mm²) | 90,000 | 202,500 | +125% | | Steel area (mm²) | 1,256.6 | 2,060.9 | +64% | | Axial capacity (kN) | 1,069 | 2,418 | +126% | ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Design Decision | Member Problem | Best Strengthening | Why | |----------------|--------------------|-----| | Flexural deficit in beam | **EBR / FRP laminate at tension face** | Direct tension fibre, no section enlargement | | Shear deficit in beam | **FRP U-wraps or side bonding** | Adds transverse capacity without disturbing slab | | Axial + ductility deficit in column | **Concrete / FRP jacketing** | Confinement converts brittle → ductile | | Lap-splice failure in column | **FRP wrap at splice zone** | Hoop confinement prevents slip | | Soft-storey collapse risk | **RC shear walls added** | System-level stiffness fix | | Pounding risk at expansion joint | **Energy dissipators + gap cover** | Displacement-controlled retrofit | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Intervention ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Existing Structure Deficiency Identified] --> B{Is it a member
or system issue?} B -- Member --> C{Member type?} B -- System --> D{Seismic or
Gravity upgrade?} C -- Column --> E{Axial or
flexural deficit?} C -- Beam --> F{Shear or
flexural deficit?} E -- Axial | ductility --> G[RC Jacketing or FRP Wrap] E -- Flexural --> H[Section Enlargement + Steel] F -- Shear --> I[FRP U-Wrap or Steel Plate Stirrups] F -- Flexural --> J[EBR / FRP Laminate on Tension Face] D -- Seismic --> K{Period-based strategy?} K -- Short-period stiff --> L[Add Shear Walls or Braces] K -- Long-period flexible --> M[Add Dampers or Base Isolators] D -- Gravity only --> N[Strengthen Specific Loaded Members] G --> O[Check P-M Interaction per IS 15988] H --> O I --> P[Check Shear Capacity per IS 456 Cl 40] J --> Q[Check Flexure IS 456 Cl 38] L --> R[Run Pushover Analysis] M --> R N --> S[Check Local Capacity vs Demand] ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Strengthening System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Inputs] A1[Member Geometry] A2[Material Properties] A3[Demand from Analysis] end subgraph PROCESS[Strengthening Design Engine] B1[Capacity vs Demand Check] B2[Select Technique: Jacketing EBR FRP Prestress] B3[Sizing and Detailing] end subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs] C1[Enhanced Member Capacity] C2[Updated P-M or M-V Envelope] C3[Construction Sequence Note] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B3 --> C3 ``` ## 4.3 Typical Section: Concrete Jacketing of a Column ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph COL[Square RC Column with Concrete Jacket] C1[Original Core 300x300] --> C2[New Jacket 450x450] C2 --> C3[Stirrups 8mm @ 100 c/c] C2 --> C4[New Longitudinal Bars 4 nos 16mm] C2 --> C5[Shear Connectors Dowels 12mm @ 300 c/c] end C1 -. dowel embed. .-> C5 C5 -. welded. .-> C4 C4 --> C3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguard Check**: > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (`C1`, `B2`, `A1`). > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as node labels. > - No bold/italic/HTML formatting inside double-quoted labels. > - All labels are clean uppercase text. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *Distinguish between Repair, Strengthening, and Rehabilitation of structures.* **Model Answer:** **Repair** is a *local* activity aimed at restoring the *original* load-carrying capacity of a damaged member (e.g., epoxy injection of a cracked beam). **Strengthening** is a *member-level* activity that enhances the capacity *beyond* the original design value (e.g., FRP wrapping a column to increase its axial load capacity). **Rehabilitation** is a *comprehensive intervention* combining repair + strengthening + retrofit, intended to bring the structure to a *modern acceptable performance level* in line with current codes (e.g., IS 456:2000, IS 1893:2016). Repair is reactive and cosmetic-cum-structural, strengthening is proactive, while rehabilitation is holistic. (3 marks) **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *List any four situations that necessitate retrofitting of an existing RC building.* **Model Answer:** The four situations are: (i) seismic vulnerability of buildings designed before the latest IS 1893 code revisions, (ii) material deterioration such as chloride-induced corrosion or carbonation, (iii) change in functional use leading to higher live loads (e.g., warehouse conversion), and (iv) design or construction deficiencies like inadequate lap-splice length or insufficient cover. (3 marks) ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Strengthening of a Column `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** A short RC column of size **300 mm × 300 mm** is reinforced with **6 bars of 20 mm diameter (Fe 415)** and concrete of grade **M 20**. It is jacketed with **100 mm thick M 25 concrete** on all four sides and **additional 6 bars of 16 mm (Fe 415)**. Determine the original and enhanced axial load capacity using IS 456:2000. Comment on the percentage increase. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of **shear connectors / dowels** in concrete jacketing. Why is surface preparation considered the most critical step in jacketing works? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Original column data:** $$ B_{\text{old}} = D_{\text{old}} = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad f_{ck} = 20 \text{ MPa}, \quad f_y = 415 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ A_{c,\text{old}} = 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,old}} = 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^{2} = 6 \times 314.16 = 1884.96 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Original axial capacity (IS 456 Cl. 39.3):** $$ P_{u,\text{old}} = 0.4 f_{ck} A_{c} + 0.67 f_{y} A_{sc} $$ $$ P_{u,\text{old}} = 0.4(20)(90{,}000) + 0.67(415)(1884.96) $$ $$ P_{u,\text{old}} = 720{,}000 + 523{,}571 = 1{,}243{,}571 \text{ N} \approx 1244 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 3 — Jacketed section data:** $$ B_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{new}} = 300 + 2(100) = 500 \text{ mm} $$ $$ A_{c,\text{total}} = 500 \times 500 = 250{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,add}} = 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^{2} = 6 \times 201.06 = 1206.37 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{sc,total}} = 1884.96 + 1206.37 = 3091.33 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Enhanced axial capacity (added concrete grade M 25 = 25 MPa):** $$ A_{c,\text{added}} = 250{,}000 - 90{,}000 = 160{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ $$ P_{u,\text{new}} = 0.4(20)(90{,}000) + 0.4(25)(160{,}000) + 0.67(415)(3091.33) $$ $$ P_{u,\text{new}} = 720{,}000 + 1{,}600{,}000 + 859{,}489 = 3{,}179{,}489 \text{ N} \approx 3180 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 5 — Increase:** $$ \Delta P_u = 3180 - 1244 = 1936 \text{ kN} $$ $$ \text{Percentage Increase} = \dfrac{1936}{1244} \times 100 \approx 155.6\% $$ **Conclusion:** The jacketing more than **doubles** the column capacity, but the engineer must also re-check the **P-M interaction envelope** and the **foundation adequacy** to support the larger dead load. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Role of Shear Connectors / Dowels:** Shear connectors (also called **dowels**) are short reinforcing bars (typically 12 mm dia, 300–500 mm long) embedded into the existing concrete using a non-shrink grout or epoxy, and tied into the new jacket reinforcement. They perform three critical functions: (i) **Transfer shear** between the old concrete core and the new jacket concrete at the interface, ensuring **composite action** rather than independent slip behaviour. (ii) **Transfer tensile splitting forces** that develop under axial load, preventing the jacket from separating from the core. (iii) **Anchor the new longitudinal bars** of the jacket into the parent member so that the additional steel participates in carrying axial and flexural loads. As per IS 15988:2013, dowels should be provided at a spacing **not exceeding 300 mm c/c** in both directions and should extend at least **150 mm** into the parent concrete and the full development length into the new jacket. **Why Surface Preparation is the Most Critical Step:** Surface preparation is critical because (i) **bond strength at the old–new interface** controls whether the jacket acts as a **composite section** or simply as a dead load sitting on the column, (ii) all **laitance, dust, carbonation layer, oil, paint, and weak concrete** must be removed by **chipping, sandblasting, or hydro-jetting** to expose sound aggregate, (iii) the surface must be **roughened to a minimum amplitude of 5 mm** (IS 15988) for mechanical interlock, and (iv) a **bonding agent / epoxy primer** must be applied on a **saturated-surface-dry (SSD)** substrate to avoid plastic-shrinkage debonding. Failure in surface preparation results in **premature debonding**, leading to jacket spalling under cyclic seismic loads — a very common field failure. (7 marks) --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Retrofit Strategy at System Level `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** A four-storey RC frame building constructed in **1985** is to be retrofitted for **seismic Zone IV** as per IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016.
