Determine the predicted carbonation depth and assess whether the reinforcement is at risk of corrosion. Given: clear cover provided = **30 mm**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**

The carbonation depth follows the square-root-of-time law:

$$x_c = k \sqrt{t}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**

Given:
- $k = 4.5 \text{ mm/year}^{0.5}$
- $t = 25 \text{ years}$

$$x_c = 4.5 \times \sqrt{25}$$

**Step 3: Compute the square root.**

$$\sqrt{25} = 5.0 \text{ year}^{0.5}$$

**Step 4: Compute the carbonation depth.**

$$x_c = 4.5 \times 5.0 = 22.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compare with cover depth.**

Clear cover provided = $30$ mm.
Carbonation depth $x_c = 22.5$ mm.
Margin remaining = $30 - 22.5 = 7.5$ mm.

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

Since the carbonation front ($22.5$ mm) has **not yet reached** the reinforcement level ($30$ mm), the steel is currently safe. However, the **safe remaining life** is limited.

**Step 7: Predict the time for carbonation to reach the rebar.**

Set $x_c = 30$ mm and solve for $t$:

$$30 = 4.5 \times \sqrt{t}$$

$$\sqrt{t} = \frac{30}{4.5} = 6.667$$

$$t = (6.667)^2 = 44.4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 8: Calculate remaining service life before depassivation begins.**

$$t_{remaining} = 44.4 - 25 = 19.4 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Even though the steel is currently safe, only **~19 years** remain before carbonation reaches the rebar. **Preventive action** (anti-carbonation coatings, surface sealers) should be recommended **now** to extend the life.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Chloride Ingress Time Prediction (Simplified Fick's Law)

### Problem Setup
A marine bridge pier is exposed to a chloride-rich environment. The surface chloride concentration is $C_s = 0.8\%$ by mass of cement. The initial chloride content in concrete is $C_i = 0.05\%$. The chloride diffusion coefficient is $D_c = 2 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. Determine the **time** required for chlorides to reach the **critical threshold of 0.4%** at a depth of **50 mm** (rebar location).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State Fick's 2nd law solution.**

$$C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c \cdot t}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute known values.**

- $C(x,t) = 0.4\%$
- $C_s = 0.8\%$
- $C_i = 0.05\%$
- $x = 0.050$ m
- $D_c = 2 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s

$$0.4 = 0.8 - (0.8 - 0.05) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{0.050}{2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$0.4 = 0.8 - 0.75 \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{0.050}{2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}}\right)$$

$$0.75 \cdot \text{erf}(\cdot) = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4$$

$$\text{erf}(\cdot) = \frac{0.4}{0.75} = 0.5333$$

**Step 4: Invert the error function.**

From standard error function tables:

$$\text{erf}^{-1}(0.5333) \approx 0.500$$

**Step 5: Equate to the argument of erf.**

$$\frac{0.050}{2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}} = 0.500$$

**Step 6: Solve for t.**

$$\frac{0.050}{0.500} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}$$

$$0.10 = 2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}$$

$$0.05 = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t}$$

$$(0.05)^2 = 2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t$$

$$0.0025 = 2 \times 10^{-12} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{0.0025}{2 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.25 \times 10^{9} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7: Convert to years.**

$$t = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{9}}{365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} \approx 39.6 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Chloride-induced corrosion of the pier reinforcement is expected to initiate in **~40 years** of service. This is a **critical time-to-corrosion** parameter used in **service life design** of marine structures (e.g., per **DuraCrete**, **Life-365** models, and **fib Bulletin 34**).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Mechanism → Effect → Diagnosis → Repair

| **S.No.** | **Cause** | **Mechanism** | **Visible Effect** | **Diagnostic Test** | **Typical Repair Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbonation | $CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3$ | Cracks along rebar, spalling | Phenolphthalein spray test | Anti-carbonation coating, patch repair |
| 2 | Chloride attack | Diffusion of Cl⁻, pitting corrosion | Rust staining, pitting | Chloride profile test | Cathodic protection, epoxy coating |
| 3 | Sulfate attack | Ettringite formation | Map cracking, softening | Chemical analysis of groundwater | Use sulfate-resisting cement (SRC) |
| 4 | Freeze-thaw | Pore water expansion (9%) | Surface scaling, pop-outs | Visual, ASTM C666 | Air-entrained concrete, sealers |
| 5 | ASR | Alkali-silica gel expansion | Map cracking, gel exudation | Petrographic examination | Lithium nitrate treatment, replacement |
| 6 | Corrosion | Electrochemical (anode + cathode) | Cover cracking, spalling | Half-cell potential (ASTM C876) | Patch repair, CP, corrosion inhibitors |
| 7 | Overload | Stress > design capacity | Flexural / shear cracks | Load test, structural analysis | Strengthening (FRP, section enlargement) |
| 8 | Fire | Loss of bound water, phase change | Spalling, discolouration | Petrography, rebound hammer | Replacement of damaged concrete, jacketing |
| 9 | Foundation settlement | Differential settlement | Diagonal cracks at corners | Level survey, plumb check | Underpinning, grouting |
| 10 | Construction defects | Poor cover, honeycombing | Early-age cracking | Cover meter survey | Patch repair, protective coatings |

---

## 3.4 Engineering Judgement Framework

The following decision tree is used in professional practice to **prioritize repairs** based on the cause-effect severity:

```
Effect Severity  →  Action
─────────────────────────────────
Cosmetic only        →  Monitor / Surface treatment
Minor cracking       →  Sealants / Surface coatings
Active corrosion     →  Patch + Anti-corrosion treatment
Structural damage    →  Strengthening (FRP / Jacketing)
Critical (life-safe) →  Load restriction / Replacement
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Practice:** Per **IS 15914:2011** (Strengthening of Concrete Structures), repair priority must be based on **defect severity grading** (Grade 1 = cosmetic, Grade 5 = structural collapse risk).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cause-Mechanism-Effect Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External / Internal Agent] --> B[Transport Mechanism]
    B --> C[Physical / Chemical Reaction]
    C --> D[Deterioration of Material]
    D --> E[Visible / Measurable Damage]
    E --> F[Loss of Serviceability]
    F --> G[Loss of Safety / Collapse]
    
    A1[Moisture] --> A
    A2[CO2] --> A
    A3[Chlorides] --> A
    A4[Sulfates] --> A
    A5[Heat / Fire] --> A
    A6[Overload] --> A
    
    G --> H[Repair Strategy Selection]
    H --> I[Rehabilitation Works]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Carbonation Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric CO2] --> B[Diffusion through Pores]
    B --> C[Reaction with CaOH2 in Pore Water]
    C --> D[Formation of CaCO3]
    D --> E[Reduction in pH below 11.5]
    E --> F[Depassivation of Steel]
    F --> G[Active Corrosion of Rebar]
    G --> H[Volume Expansion 2-6 times]
    H --> I[Internal Tensile Stress]
    I --> J[Cracking and Spalling of Cover]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Chloride Attack Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine / De-icing Salt Source] --> B[Diffusion / Capillary Transport]
    B --> C[Accumulation at Rebar Depth]
    C --> D{Cl Content exceeds 0.4 percent}
    D -- Yes --> E[Breakdown of Passive Film]
    E --> F[Pitting Corrosion Initiation]
    F --> G[Localized Loss of Steel Section]
    G --> H[Cracking and Spalling]
    D -- No --> I[Safe Zone - Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Corrosion Cell Electrochemistry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode Fe to Fe2 plus 2e] --> B[Electron Flow through Steel]
    B --> C[Cathode O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e to 4OH]
    C --> D[OH minus Migrates through Pore Water]
    D --> E[Fe2 plus and OH minus form FeOH2]
    E --> F[Further Oxidation to Fe2O3 Rust]
    F --> G[Volume Expansion 2-6 times]
    G --> H[Cracking and Spalling of Cover Concrete]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Effect Hierarchy and Repair Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Damage on Structure] --> B{Cracks Present}
    B -- Yes --> C{Active or Dormant}
    B -- No --> D[Discolouration Stains]
    C -- Active --> E[Investigate Moisture / Corrosion]
    C -- Dormant --> F[Seal and Monitor]
    D --> G[Check for Corrosion Underneath]
    E --> H[Carbonation / Chloride Test]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Depth and Extent Assessment}
    I --> J[Minor: Surface Treatment]
    I --> K[Moderate: Patch Repair plus Coatings]
    I --> L[Severe: Strengthening or Replacement]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Service Life Framework (Tuutti Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Construction Complete] --> B[Initiation Phase ti]
    B --> C[Depassivation of Steel]
    C --> D[Propagation Phase tp]
    D --> E[Active Corrosion and Damage]
    E --> F[Limit State Reached]
    F --> G{Serviceability or Ultimate}
    G --> H[Repair or Replacement Required]
    
    style B fill:#fff2cc
    style D fill:#f8cecc
    style F fill:#e06666
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tuutti's Two-Stage Model** (1982) is the **gold standard framework** used in durability engineering. **Initiation period ($t_i$)** = time for aggressive agents to reach the rebar and break passivation. **Propagation period ($t_p$)** = time from corrosion initiation to acceptable limit state. **Total service life = $t_i + t_p$**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Drawing Tip:** Even a simple **hand-drawn block diagram** of the cause-effect chain earns **1–2 marks** in descriptive answers. Always include a diagram wherever possible.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the mechanism of carbonation in concrete and its effect on reinforced concrete structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Carbonation is the chemical reaction between atmospheric carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) and calcium hydroxide [$Ca(OH)_2$] present in the hydrated cement paste, forming calcium carbonate ($CaCO_3$).

**Reaction:**
$$CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$$

**Mechanism (1 mark):** Atmospheric $CO_2$ diffuses through the porous concrete cover and reacts with the alkaline pore solution. The pH of the pore water drops from approximately 12.5 to below 9.0, destroying the passive protective oxide film around the steel reinforcement.

**Effects on RCC structures (1 mark):**
- Depassivation and subsequent corrosion of steel reinforcement.
- Rust occupies 2 to 6 times the volume of original steel, generating internal bursting pressure.
- Results in cracking, spalling, and delamination of cover concrete.
- Loss of bond between steel and concrete; reduction in load-carrying capacity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List any six causes of deterioration of concrete structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major causes of deterioration of concrete structures are:

1. **Carbonation** of concrete leading to reinforcement corrosion.
2. **Chloride attack** from marine exposure or de-icing salts causing pitting corrosion.
3. **Sulfate attack** from soil or groundwater leading to expansion and disintegration.
4. **Freeze-thaw cycles** causing surface scaling and internal cracking.
5. **Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR)** producing expansive gel and map cracking.
6. **Fire exposure** causing spalling and loss of strength of concrete and steel.
7. **Structural overloading** causing flexural and shear cracks.
8. **Construction defects** such as inadequate cover, poor compaction, and honeycombing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner's Pitfall):** Students often list *symptoms* (e.g., "cracking") as causes. Always distinguish between **causes** (agents) and **effects** (damage manifestations). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 1]`

**Q. (a) Discuss in detail the various causes of deterioration of concrete structures with emphasis on chemical causes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Concrete structures deteriorate due to a combination of physical, chemical, mechanical, and biological causes. Among these, chemical causes are the most critical because they initiate reinforcement corrosion, which is the leading cause of structural damage globally.

**Classification of Chemical Causes (2 marks):**

1. **Carbonation** — atmospheric $CO_2$ reacting with $Ca(OH)_2$.
2. **Chloride attack** — ingress of chloride ions causing pitting corrosion.
3. **Sulfate attack** — formation of expansive ettringite and gypsum.
4. **Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR)** — expansive gel formation.
5. **Acid attack** — dissolution of cement paste by industrial/acidic water.
6. **Delayed Ettringite Formation (DEF)** — late expansion due to high curing temperatures.

**Detailed discussion of each cause (3 marks):**

- **Carbonation:** Reaction $CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$ reduces pore-water pH below 11.5, destroying the passive film on steel. Carbonation depth follows $x_c = k\sqrt{t}$.

- **Chloride attack:** Critical threshold 0.4% by mass of cement. Chlorides break down passive film locally causing pitting corrosion. Ingress follows Fick's second law of diffusion.

- **Sulfate attack:** Sulfates react with tricalcite aluminate (C₃A) and portlandite to form ettringite, which has greater volume, causing expansion and disintegration.

- **ASR:** Reactive silica in aggregates reacts with alkalis in cement forming hygroscopic gel that expands upon water absorption, causing map-pattern cracking.

**Effects (1 mark):** All chemical causes lead to cracking, spalling, corrosion, loss of strength, and reduced service life.

---

**(b) Explain the mechanism of reinforcement corrosion. How does it affect the durability of RCC structures? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Corrosion (3 marks):**

Corrosion of steel in concrete is an **electrochemical process** requiring four essential elements: an **anode**, a **cathode**, an **electrolyte** (moist concrete), and an **electrical connection** (steel itself).

**At the anode (oxidation):**
$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$$

**At the cathode (reduction):**
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

**Overall reaction:**
$$4Fe + 3O_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3$$

**Conditions for initiation:** The alkaline pore water (pH ~12.5) forms a passive $Fe_2O_3$ film protecting the steel. This film breaks down when:
- pH falls below 11.5 (carbonation), OR
- Chloride content exceeds 0.4% (chloride attack).

**Effects on Durability of RCC (3 marks):**

1. **Volume expansion:** Rust occupies 2 to 6 times the volume of the original steel, generating internal bursting pressure (15–40 MPa) on surrounding concrete.

2. **Cracking and spalling:** The bursting pressure causes longitudinal cracks along the line of reinforcement, followed by spalling of cover concrete.

3. **Loss of steel cross-section:** Reduction in effective area of rebar → reduced load-carrying capacity.

4. **Loss of bond:** Spalling and cracking destroy the steel-concrete bond.

5. **Reduced service life:** The structure may reach its limit state well before its design life (typically 50–100 years).

6. **Increased maintenance cost:** Requires expensive rehabilitation (patch repair, cathodic protection, FRP strengthening).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Reinforcement corrosion is the **single most critical durability issue** for RCC structures. Preventive measures (adequate cover, low w/c ratio, pozzolanic cement, corrosion inhibitors) and timely repairs are essential.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 1 — Internal Choice]`

**Q. (a) Describe the effects of fire and freeze-thaw cycles on concrete structures. What remedial measures can be adopted? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Effects of Fire (3 marks):**

1. **Loss of strength:** Concrete loses strength progressively above 300 °C due to dehydration of C-S-H gel. At 600 °C, residual strength is only ~30–50%.

2. **Steel strength loss:** Rebar loses yield strength rapidly above 250 °C. At 600 °C, only ~50% strength remains.

3. **Spalling:** Explosive spalling occurs due to:
   - Pore pressure build-up from water vapor.
   - Thermal stresses between hot outer surface and cooler inner core.
   - Aggregate expansion (especially siliceous aggregates at 573 °C — quartz inversion).

4. **Discolouration:** Concrete changes colour with temperature — pink (300 °C), whitish grey (> 600 °C).

5. **Cracking:** Thermal gradients cause surface cracking.

**Effects of Freeze-Thaw Cycles (2 marks):**

1. **Surface scaling** — progressive loss of surface mortar.
2. **Pop-outs** — local spalling due to expansion of frozen water.
3. **D-cracking** — characteristic D-shaped pattern cracks in bridge decks.
4. **Internal micro-cracking** reducing overall durability.

**Mechanism:** Water in pores freezes at < 0 °C, expanding by ~9%, generating internal hydraulic pressure (~14 MPa).

**Remedial Measures (2 marks):**

For fire damage:
- Replacement of damaged concrete.
- Jacketing of columns and beams.
- FRP wrapping for strengthening.
- Application of fire-resistant coatings in new construction.

For freeze-thaw damage:
- Use of air-entrained concrete (4–6% air content) in new construction.
- Surface sealers to prevent moisture ingress.
- Polymer-modified mortars for repair.

---

**(b) Explain in detail the Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR). What are its effects and how is it controlled? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR) is a chemical reaction between the **alkali hydroxides** (Na⁺, K⁺, OH⁻) present in the pore solution of cement paste and the **reactive silica** ($SiO_2$) present in certain aggregates. The reaction produces an **alkali-silica gel** that is hygroscopic and expands upon water absorption, causing internal expansion and cracking of concrete.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
- Reactive forms of silica (opal, chalcedony, volcanic glass, strained quartz) dissolve in the highly alkaline pore solution.
- Form alkali-silica gel: $Na_2SiO_3$ or $K_2SiO_3$.
- Gel absorbs water and swells.
- Expansion generates internal tensile stresses → cracking.

**Conditions for ASR (the "Three Pillars"):**
1. **Reactive aggregate** (sufficient amount).
2. **High alkali content** in cement (typically > 0.6% Na₂O equivalent).
3. **Moisture** availability.

**Effects of ASR (2 marks):**

1. **Map-pattern (pattern) cracking** on concrete surface.
2. **White gel exudation** at crack openings.
3. **Expansion and displacement** of structural elements.
4. **Reduction in stiffness and strength.**
5. **Surface pop-outs and discoloration.**
6. **In severe cases, loss of structural integrity.**

**Control and Preventive Measures (2 marks):**

1. **Use non-reactive aggregates** (petrographic examination as per **ASTM C295**).
2. **Limit alkali content of cement** to < 0.6% Na₂O equivalent (per **IS 456:2000 Annex E**).
3. **Use low-alkali cement or blended cements** (fly ash, slag, silica fume — supplementary cementitious materials).
4. **Use lithium nitrate admixtures** ($LiNO_3$ replaces sodium/potassium ions in the gel, forming non-expansive lithium-silica gel).
5. **Control moisture** — use of sealers and proper drainage.
6. **Use of ASR-resistant mix designs** following **IS 383:2016** and **ASTM C1260 / C1293** tests.

**Conclusion:** ASR is a long-term durability issue; once it initiates, it cannot be reversed, only controlled. **Prevention is better than cure** — the design stage must incorporate ASR-resistant measures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Areas (KTU Examiner's Pitfall List):**
>
> 1. **Confusing causes and effects** — listing "cracking" or "spalling" as causes instead of effects. Always structure as **Cause → Mechanism → Effect**.
> 2. **Skipping the chemical equations** — Examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for writing the balanced chemical reaction (e.g., $CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$).
> 3. **Not citing IS/ACI codes** — Always reference **IS 456:2000**, **IS 15914:2011**, **ASTM C876**, **ACI 546R-14** to score higher bands.
> 4. **Writing only definitions without mechanisms** — Marks are awarded for *explanation of mechanism*, not just the name of the cause.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention numerical thresholds** — e.g., 0.4% chloride, pH 11.5, 30 mm cover, 0.3 mm crack width.
> 6. **No diagrams** — Even a small flow chart or sketch of the cause-effect chain earns **1–2 easy marks**.
> 7. **In Part B, poor time management** — Allocate 7 minutes for (a) and 7 minutes for (b). Don't over-elaborate on one sub-part at the cost of the other.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- **Carbonation:** $CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$; pH drops < 11.5; depth = $k\sqrt{t}$.
- **Chloride attack:** Threshold = 0.4% by mass of cement; pitting corrosion; Fick's 2nd law applies.
- **Sulfate attack:** Forms **ettringite**; causes expansion and map cracking.
- **ASR:** Requires **reactive aggregate + alkalis + moisture**; produces hygroscopic gel.
- **Freeze-thaw:** Water expands ~9% on freezing; use **air-entrained concrete** (4–6% air).
- **Fire:** Steel loses 50% strength at 600 °C; spalling common above 300 °C.
- **Corrosion:** Electrochemical; anode-cathode-electrolyte-steel; rust volume = 2–6× steel volume.
- **Cover:** Minimum 30 mm for moderate exposure (IS 456:2000); tolerance ±5 to +10 mm.
- **Crack width limit:** 0.3 mm for moderate exposure (IS 456:2000).
- **Half-cell potential:** > −200 mV = 90% no corrosion; < −350 mV = 90% active corrosion (ASTM C876).
- **Tuutti Model:** Service life = $t_i$ (initiation) + $t_p$ (propagation).
- **Mnemonic for causes:** **"4C + 2M + 1F"** = Carbonation, Chlorides, Cracking (constructional), Construction defects, Moisture, Mechanical overload, Fire.
- **Mnemonic for effects:** **"CSD-LS"** = Cracking, Spalling, Discolouration, Loss of strength, Serviceability loss.
- **Most important chemical equations to memorize:** Carbonation, ASR (gel formation), Corrosion (anode + cathode), Ettringite formation.
- **Standards to cite:** IS 456:2000, IS 15914:2011, IS 383:2016, ASTM C876, ACI 546R-14, fib Bulletin 34.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Strategy:** Read the **Formula Sheet (Section 2.3)** + the **Cause-Effect Mapping Table (Section 3.3)** + the **Mnemonics** above. These cover 80% of marks expected in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Damages to Masonry Structures - Various Damages to Masonry Structures and Causes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Masonry Damage** refers to the degradation, deterioration, or loss of structural and aesthetic integrity of masonry elements (brick, stone, block, or composite walls) caused by physical, chemical, biological, or mechanical actions that alter the original properties of the constituent materials and the assemblage as a whole.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET634)**, masonry damage assessment is the *systematic identification, classification, and root-cause analysis* of defects in load-bearing and non-load-bearing masonry systems, forming the foundational step before any repair, retrofitting, or rehabilitation strategy is adopted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Masonry is one of the oldest construction materials. Damages in masonry are unique because they often manifest as **surface symptoms** but originate from **deep-rooted structural, environmental, or material-related causes**. A correct diagnosis is *half the repair*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **brick wall as a human body**:

- **Bones** = Masonry units (bricks/blocks) — the hard structural skeleton
- **Joints/Cartilage** = Mortar — the flexible binder that holds everything together
- **Skin** = Plaster/render — the protective outer coating
- **Blood circulation** = Moisture movement through pores
- **Immune system** = Protective coatings and damp-proof course (DPC)

When the "joints" (mortar) decay, the "skeleton" (bricks) lose alignment. When "moisture circulation" goes wrong, "diseases" like efflorescence or damp patches appear. When the "skin" (plaster) cracks, "infections" (water ingress, salts) enter the body. Just as a doctor treats symptoms **after diagnosing the cause**, a rehabilitation engineer must identify the **root cause** of masonry damage before prescribing repairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A crack in a wall is rarely the *problem* — it is usually the *symptom*. The actual problem could be foundation settlement, sulfate attack, thermal movement, or corrosion of hidden reinforcements.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Masonry Damage Assessment

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crack width | $w$ | 0.1 mm – 25 mm | Severity indicator |
| Crack length | $L_c$ | mm to m | Extent of damage |
| Spalling depth | $d_s$ | 5 mm – 100 mm | Surface loss measurement |
| Moisture content | $M\%$ | 0% – 25% | Dampness evaluation |
| Efflorescence rating | — | Nil / Slight / Moderate / Heavy / Serious | As per **IS 3495 / BRE grading** |
| Compressive strength | $f_c$ | 3.5 – 35 N/mm² | Residual capacity |
| Carbonation depth | $x_c$ | 0 – 50 mm | Long-term durability |
| Sulfate content | $SO_3$ | < 0.5% (safe) | Chemical attack indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Damages are commonly classified as per **IS 15912 (Code of Practice for Structural Use of Unreinforced Masonry)** and **IS 1905 (Code of Practice for Structural Use of Masonry)**, with repair guidance from **IS 2212 (Code of Practice for Brickwork)** and **ACI 546R (Concrete Repair Guide)**.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Masonry Damages

Masonry damages can be broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Cracks** (Hairline, Structural, Non-structural, Settlement, Thermal)
2. **Surface Deterioration** (Spalling, Scaling, Peeling, Weathering)
3. **Moisture-Related Damages** (Rising damp, Condensation, Damp patches)
4. **Chemical Damages** (Efflorescence, Sulfate attack, Carbonation)
5. **Structural Distortions** (Bulging, Buckling, Tilting, Out-of-plumb)
6. **Joints and Mortar Failures** (Mortar erosion, Loss of bond, Joint failure)
7. **Biological Damages** (Plant growth, Microbial attack, Insect attack)
8. **Fire and Thermal Damages** (Fire spalling, Thermal cracks)
9. **Foundation-Related Damages** (Settlement, Heave, Rotation)

---

## 1.5 Why Study Masonry Damages?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> 1. **Safety:** Damaged masonry poses collapse risks to occupants.
> 2. **Serviceability:** Cracks and dampness reduce usability.
> 3. **Durability:** Untreated damages accelerate structural decay.
> 4. **Economy:** Early diagnosis saves 60–80% of repair costs.
> 5. **Heritage Conservation:** Critical for restoring historical masonry structures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Damages to Masonry Structures

### A. CRACKS IN MASONRY

Cracks are the **most common and visible form of damage** in masonry. They vary in width, length, orientation, and pattern — each pattern indicating a specific cause.

#### A.1 Types of Cracks (Based on Cause)

**(i) Hairline Cracks**
- Width: **< 0.5 mm**
- Causes: Shrinkage of plaster/mortar, minor thermal movement
- Severity: Cosmetic, generally non-structural
- Pattern: Random, shallow, often in plaster only

**(ii) Structural Cracks**
- Width: **> 1.5 mm**, often > 3 mm
- Causes: Overloading, foundation movement, design deficiency
- Severity: **Critical** — affects load transfer
- Pattern: Diagonal, stepped (following mortar joints), vertical through units

**(iii) Settlement Cracks**
- Cause: Differential settlement of foundation
- Pattern: **Diagonal cracks** at corners of openings, widening towards one end
- Common location: Near windows, doors, and wall corners
- Crack width **widens progressively** if settlement continues

**(iv) Thermal Movement Cracks**
- Cause: Differential expansion/contraction due to temperature variation
- Pattern: **Vertical or horizontal** straight cracks, often at corners
- Typical width: 1–3 mm

**(v) Shrinkage Cracks**
- Cause: Drying shrinkage of mortar, bricks, or concrete
- Pattern: Random, often map-pattern (craquelure)
- Common in new construction within first 1–2 years

**(vi) Lateral Movement Cracks (Out-of-plane bulging)**
- Cause: Lateral pressure, lack of lateral restraint
- Pattern: **Horizontal cracks** at mid-height, with visible bulge
- Severity: **High** — sign of impending wall failure

#### A.2 Crack Width Classification (As per BRE Digest 251 / IS Guidelines)

| Category | Crack Width | Description | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Negligible | < 0.1 mm | Hairline | Monitor |
| Very slight | 0.1 – 1 mm | Fine | Cosmetic repair |
| Slight | 1 – 2 mm | Easily filled | Repaint/plaster |
| Moderate | 2 – 5 mm | Visible | Repointing needed |
| Severe | 5 – 15 mm | Wide | Structural repair |
| Very severe | 15 – 25 mm | Major | Specialist intervention |
| Extreme | > 25 mm | Critical | Emergency support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If a crack **follows the mortar joints** (stepped pattern), it indicates **masonry movement**. If it **cuts through the bricks**, it indicates **excessive stress or unit failure**.

---

### B. SURFACE DETERIORATION DAMAGES

#### B.1 Spalling
- **Definition:** Breaking away of surface layers of bricks or stones in flakes or chunks.
- **Causes:** Freeze-thaw cycles, salt crystallization, corrosion of embedded metal, fire damage.
- **Mechanism:** Water enters the pores → freezes → expands (~9% volume increase) → internal pressure → surface flaking.
- **Common in:** Exposed parapets, cornices, window sills.

#### B.2 Scaling
- **Definition:** Loss of surface mortar exposing aggregate.
- **Similar to spalling** but shallower and more uniform.
- **Common in:** Concrete block masonry exposed to weathering.

#### B.3 Peeling & Blistering
- **Cause:** Moisture trapped behind plaster/render.
- **Pattern:** Bubbles, hollow patches, eventual detachment.

#### B.4 Weathering
- **Causes:** Wind-driven rain, acid rain, atmospheric pollutants ($SO_2$, $NO_x$), UV radiation.
- **Result:** Gradual loss of surface, change in color, roughening.

---

### C. MOISTURE-RELATED DAMAGES

#### C.1 Rising Damp
- **Definition:** Upward movement of groundwater through capillary action in masonry.
- **Visible signs:** Tide marks, dark patches up to ~1.5 m height, salt deposits at damp line.
- **Causes:** Failed/missing **DPC (Damp-Proof Course)**, bridging of DPC, ground contact.
- **Maximum height (Jurin's Law governed):**

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}$$

Where:
- $h$ = height of capillary rise (m)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of water (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $r$ = capillary radius (m)
- $\rho$ = density of water (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)

> [!NOTE]
> For typical brick pores of radius 0.1–10 µm, the theoretical rise can be **up to 15 m**, but practical rise is limited to **0.5–1.5 m** due to evaporation equilibrium.

#### C.2 Condensation
- **Cause:** Warm humid air meeting cold masonry surface (below dew point).
- **Visible signs:** Black mould, peeling paint, musty smell.
- **Common in:** Kitchens, bathrooms, poorly ventilated rooms.

#### C.3 Penetrating Damp
- **Cause:** Water ingress through defects (cracks, faulty joints, porous units).
- **Result:** Localized patches, often horizontal bands below defects.

#### C.4 Hygroscopic Salts
- **Cause:** Soluble salts absorb atmospheric moisture.
- **Result:** Persistent dampness even in dry weather.

---

### D. CHEMICAL DAMAGES

#### D.1 Efflorescence
- **Definition:** White crystalline deposits on masonry surface due to soluble salt migration.
- **Chemistry:** Water dissolves salts ($Na_2SO_4$, $K_2SO_4$, $CaSO_4$) inside masonry → migrates to surface → evaporates → deposits salt crystals.
- **Reaction:**

$$Na_2SO_4 \cdot 10H_2O \text{ (Thenardite/Mirabilite) crystallization}$$

- **Grading (per BRE Digest 401):**
  - **Nil** — No visible efflorescence
  - **Slight** — Up to 5% of surface, no flaking
  - **Moderate** — 5–10% with minor flaking
  - **Heavy** — 10–50% with surface powdering
  - **Serious** — > 50% with thick crust and spalling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Efflorescence is **water-soluble** (white washable deposits) — usually cosmetic. **Cryptoflorescence** is salt crystallization **inside** the pores, causing internal expansion and spalling — much more damaging.

#### D.2 Sulfate Attack
- **Cause:** Sulfates (from soil, groundwater, polluted air, or contaminated bricks) react with **tricalcite aluminate (C₃A)** in mortar.
- **Reaction (Ettringite Formation):**

$$C_3A + 3CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 26H_2O \rightarrow C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O \text{ (Ettringite)}$$

- **Effect:** Ettringite crystals have **8× greater volume** than original reactants → internal expansion → cracking, softening, loss of strength.
- **Critical limit:** $SO_3$ content in mortar should not exceed **0.5% by mass**.

#### D.3 Carbonation
- **Reaction:** $Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$
- **Effect:** Reduces alkalinity of mortar (pH drops from ~12.5 to ~8.5) → loss of passive protection to embedded steel → corrosion of ties/anchors.

#### D.4 Acid Rain Attack
- **Reaction:** $CaCO_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + H_2O + CO_2$
- **Effect:** Dissolution of calcareous stones (limestone, marble), surface erosion.

---

### E. STRUCTURAL DISTORTIONS

#### E.1 Bulging
- **Cause:** Lateral pressure, moisture expansion, corrosion of embedded steel pushing outward.
- **Detection:** Plumb-line test, laser scan.
- **Limit:** As per **IS 1905**, wall out-of-plumb > 1/150 of height is structurally significant.

#### E.2 Buckling
- **Cause:** Slenderness failure, eccentric loading.
- **Common in:** Tall slender walls, gable walls.

#### E.3 Tilting
- **Cause:** Foundation rotation, eccentric loading, lateral sway.

---

### F. JOINTS AND MORTAR FAILURES

#### F.1 Mortar Erosion
- **Cause:** Weathering, freeze-thaw, salt attack, poor original mix.
- **Effect:** Loss of mortar, exposed joints, water ingress.

#### F.2 Loss of Bond
- **Cause:** Differential movement, poor adhesion, contamination of unit surface.
- **Effect:** Units become loose, wall loses integrity.

#### F.3 Failed Pointing
- **Cause:** Ageing, weathering, mechanical damage.
- **Repair:** **Repointing** (raking out old mortar to 2× joint depth and re-filling).

---

### G. BIOLOGICAL DAMAGES

- **Plant growth:** Roots penetrate joints → mechanical disruption.
- **Microbial attack:** Bacteria/fungi produce acids → chemical erosion.
- **Insect attack:** Termites and wood-borers attack timber lintels/bond beams within masonry.

---

### H. FIRE AND THERMAL DAMAGES

- **Temperature ranges:**
  - < 300°C: No significant damage to masonry
  - 300–600°C: Mortar deterioration begins, color change
  - 600–900°C: Brick vitrification, loss of strength
  - > 900°C: Severe damage, possible melting
- **Fire spalling:** Sudden explosive flaking due to rapid steam formation.
- **Residual strength estimation (per IS 1642 / ACI 216.1):**

$$f_{c,T} = f_{c,20°C} \times k_T$$

Where $k_T$ is a temperature-dependent reduction factor.

---

### I. FOUNDATION-RELATED DAMAGES

- **Settlement cracks:** Diagonal, widen with time
- **Heave cracks:** Upward movement (clay swelling, tree roots) → cracks near corners
- **Rotation:** Asymmetric foundation movement

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}$ | Capillary rise (rising damp) | m |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{Ettringite} = \dfrac{V_{prod} - V_{react}}{V_{react}} \approx 7$ | Volume expansion in sulfate attack | ratio |
| 3 | $f_{c,T} = f_{c,20} \cdot k_T$ | Residual strength after fire | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal strain | dimensionless |

| 7 | $C = \dfrac{x_c}{\sqrt{t}}$ | Carbonation depth (Fick's 1st law) | mm/√year |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law) for Rising Damp

### Statement
Derive the maximum height to which water can rise by capillary action in a porous masonry material.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Force balance setup**

Consider a capillary tube of radius $r$ dipped in water. Water rises to height $h$ due to surface tension.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Upward force (surface tension)} &= 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta \\
\text{Downward force (weight of water column)} &= \pi r^2 h \rho g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Equate forces at equilibrium**

$$
2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g
$$

**Step 3: Simplify to obtain Jurin's Law**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta}{\pi r^2 \rho g} \\
h &= \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitution of values for typical brick**

For a brick with effective pore radius $r = 10\,\mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= 0.0728 \text{ N/m (water at 20°C)} \\
\theta &= 0° \text{ (perfect wetting, cos} \theta = 1) \\
\rho &= 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
g &= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
h &= \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 9.81} \\
h &= \frac{0.1456}{0.0981} \\
h &\approx 1.484 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The theoretical capillary rise in fine brick pores is ~1.5 m, which matches the typical observed height of rising damp patches in walls. This confirms the **Jurin mechanism** as the governing physical principle for rising damp.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sulfate Attack — Volume Expansion of Ettringite

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the molar masses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(C_3A) &= 3(40) + 1(102) = 222 \text{ g/mol} \\
M(CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O) &= 40 + 32 + 64 + 36 = 172 \text{ g/mol} \\
M(Ettringite: C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O) &= 1255 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute molar volumes using densities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{C_3A} &= \frac{222}{3.04 \times 10^6} = 7.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} \\
V_{3(CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O)} &= \frac{3 \times 172}{2.32 \times 10^6} = 22.24 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} \\
V_{26 H_2O} &= \frac{26 \times 18}{1.0 \times 10^6} = 4.68 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} \\
V_{Ettringite} &= \frac{1255}{1.73 \times 10^6} = 72.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volume expansion ratio**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{products} - V_{reactants}}{V_{reactants}} = \frac{72.54 - (7.30 + 22.24 + 4.68)}{7.30 + 22.24 + 4.68}
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{72.54 - 34.22}{34.22} \approx 1.12 \text{ or } 112\%
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation**

This 112% volume expansion in a confined pore generates **expansive pressure** of up to **40–110 MPa** in dense mortar, far exceeding its tensile strength (~3–5 MPa), causing **internal cracking and spalling**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crack Width Calculation from Thermal Movement

### Given
A 6 m long brick wall is subjected to a temperature rise of $\Delta T = 40°C$. Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 7 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Expansion joint spacing $L = 6$ m.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Free thermal strain**

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 7 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 2.8 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2: Free thermal expansion**

$$
\Delta L = \varepsilon_{th} \cdot L = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 6000 \text{ mm} = 1.68 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: If movement is fully restrained, induced stress**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 9000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2.8 \times 10^{-4} = 2.52 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compare with masonry tensile strength** ($f_t \approx 0.3$ N/mm²)

Since $2.52 > 0.3$ N/mm², **cracking will occur**.

**Step 5: Width of crack formed**

$$
w_{crack} = \Delta L - \frac{f_t \cdot L}{E} = 1.68 - \frac{0.3 \times 6000}{9000} = 1.68 - 0.20 = 1.48 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why **expansion joints** are mandatory in long masonry walls. As per **IS 1905**, expansion joints in brick masonry should be provided at intervals of **30–45 m maximum** for tropical climates.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Causes → Damages → Repair Mapping

| Cause | Damage Type | Visible Symptom | Diagnostic Test | Typical Repair |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rising damp | Moisture damage | Tide marks, salt efflorescence | Carbide meter, electrical resistivity | DPC injection, tanking |
| Sulfate attack | Chemical damage | Crumbling mortar, white deposits | Chemical analysis of mortar | Mortar replacement, sulfate-resisting cement |
| Foundation settlement | Structural cracks | Diagonal cracks at openings | Plumb test, level survey | Underpinning, grouting |
| Thermal movement | Thermal cracks | Vertical/horizontal cracks | Monitoring with tell-tales | Expansion joints, flexible sealants |
| Salt crystallization | Spalling | Surface flaking | Phenolphthalein test, salt analysis | Poultice, salt-resistant coatings |
| Corrosion of ties | Bulging, cracking | Lateral bulge | Endoscopy, cover meter | Tie replacement, grouting |
| Freeze-thaw | Spalling | Surface scaling | Visual + moisture content | Replacement, breathable coatings |
| Fire | Loss of strength | Spalling, color change | Hammer test, ultrasonic pulse velocity | Replacement, shotcrete |
| Vegetation | Joint failure | Roots in joints | Visual | Root removal, repointing |
