# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

---

### **Question 1**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**

List any **four** common damages observed in masonry structures.

#### Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks):

1. **Cracks** — Hairline, settlement, thermal, and structural cracks.
2. **Surface Deterioration** — Spalling, scaling, peeling, and weathering of the exposed surface.
3. **Moisture Damage** — Rising damp, condensation, and penetrating damp leading to tide marks and salt deposits.
4. **Chemical Damage** — Efflorescence, sulfate attack, and carbonation of mortar.
5. **Structural Distortion** — Bulging, buckling, and tilting due to foundation movement or lateral pressure.

*(Any 4 will fetch full marks)*

---

### **Question 2**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**

What is **efflorescence** in masonry? How does it differ from **cryptoflorescence**?

#### Model Answer:

**Efflorescence:**
Efflorescence is the deposition of **white crystalline salts** (such as sodium sulfate, potassium sulfate, or calcium carbonate) on the **exterior surface** of masonry when water-soluble salts migrate from within the masonry to the surface and evaporate, leaving salt deposits. It is largely a **cosmetic issue** and is water-washable.

**Cryptoflorescence:**
Cryptoflorescence is the crystallization of salts **within the pores** of masonry, just beneath the surface. The internal salt crystals expand (sometimes by 2–3 times in volume), generating **internal expansive pressures** that cause **spalling, cracking, and disintegration** of the masonry units. It is a **structural and durability issue**, far more damaging than efflorescence.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | Efflorescence | Cryptoflorescence |
|---|---|---|
| Location | External surface | Internal pores |
| Visibility | Visible as white deposits | Hidden, damage is internal |
| Severity | Cosmetic | Structural |
| Cause of damage | Salt accumulation on surface | Salt crystallization pressure |
| Repair | Brushing, water washing | Poulticing, repointing, unit replacement |

*(3 marks for clear definition + tabular comparison)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various types of damages observed in masonry structures** with neat sketches. Classify them based on cause, severity, and visibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **causes and mechanisms of chemical damages** in masonry, including efflorescence, sulfate attack, and carbonation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Masonry structures are vulnerable to a wide range of damages due to environmental exposure, material ageing, and structural actions. Proper classification of these damages is the first step toward effective repair and rehabilitation.

**Classification of Damages (5 marks — with 1 mark for neat sketches):**

**(i) Based on Cause:**

1. **Physical Damages** — Cracks, spalling, scaling, weathering caused by mechanical and environmental actions.
2. **Chemical Damages** — Efflorescence, sulfate attack, carbonation, acid rain attack.
3. **Biological Damages** — Plant roots, microbial growth, insect attack.
4. **Moisture-Related Damages** — Rising damp, condensation, penetrating damp.
5. **Structural Damages** — Bulging, buckling, settlement, tilting.

**(ii) Based on Severity (per BRE Digest 251):**

1. Negligible (< 0.1 mm)
2. Very slight (0.1–1 mm)
3. Slight (1–2 mm)
4. Moderate (2–5 mm)
5. Severe (5–15 mm)
6. Very severe (15–25 mm)
7. Extreme (> 25 mm)

**(iii) Based on Visibility:**

1. **Surface damages** — Visible (cracks, spalling, efflorescence).
2. **Subsurface damages** — Hidden (carbonation, internal sulfate attack, internal moisture).

**Sketches Expected (1 mark):**
- Diagram of stepped crack (settlement type)
- Diagram of vertical thermal crack
- Diagram of rising damp with tide mark and salt deposits

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A thorough understanding of damage types and their classification helps in selecting the correct diagnostic techniques and appropriate repair methodologies.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Efflorescence (2.5 marks):**

- **Definition:** White salt deposits on the masonry surface.
- **Mechanism:**
  - Soluble salts ($Na_2SO_4$, $K_2SO_4$, $CaSO_4$) present in bricks, mortar, or groundwater dissolve in water.
  - Water migrates to the surface by capillary action.
  - Water evaporates, leaving salt crystals on the surface.
- **Grading (BRE Digest 401):** Nil, Slight, Moderate, Heavy, Serious.
- **Effect:** Primarily cosmetic, but indicates active moisture ingress.

**2. Sulfate Attack (2.5 marks):**

- **Definition:** Chemical reaction between sulfate ions (from soil, water, or polluted air) and the aluminate phases in cement mortar.
- **Mechanism:**
  - Sulfates react with tricalcium aluminate ($C_3A$) to form **ettringite** ($C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O$).
  - Ettringite has **8× the volume** of original reactants.
  - This expansive reaction causes **internal cracking, spalling, and loss of strength**.
- **Critical limits:** $SO_3$ content in cement < 3% (as per IS 269), in mortar < 0.5%.
- **Mitigation:** Use of sulfate-resisting Portland cement (SRC), low $C_3A$ cement, dense concrete, protective coatings.

**3. Carbonation (2 marks):**

- **Definition:** Reaction of atmospheric $CO_2$ with calcium hydroxide in cement mortar.
- **Reaction:** $Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$
- **Effect:**
  - Reduces pH of mortar from ~12.5 to ~8.5.
  - Depassivates embedded steel, leading to corrosion of ties and reinforcements.
  - Corrosion products occupy **2–6× the original volume** of steel, causing internal pressure and cracking.
- **Rate (Fick's law):** $x_c = k\sqrt{t}$, where $k$ depends on exposure conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of rising damp** in masonry walls. Discuss the **causes, signs, and remedial measures** for rising damp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **various types of cracks** observed in masonry structures. How do you distinguish between **structural cracks and non-structural cracks**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Mechanism of Rising Damp (2 marks):**
Rising damp is the **upward movement of groundwater** through the pores and capillaries of masonry by **capillary action (Jurin's Law)**. The height of rise depends on pore radius, governed by:

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}$$

For typical brick pores ($r = 10\,\mu m$), the rise is approximately **0.5 to 1.5 m**, which matches the practical height of damp patches observed in walls.

**Causes of Rising Damp (1.5 marks):**
1. **Absence of Damp-Proof Course (DPC)** in old buildings.
2. **Failure/bridging of DPC** due to age, structural movement, or external rendering.
3. **High groundwater table** in coastal or low-lying areas (very relevant in Kerala).
4. **Porous masonry units** with interconnected capillary network.
5. **Defective site drainage** causing water pooling near foundations.

**Signs of Rising Damp (1.5 marks):**
1. **Tide marks** — Horizontal dark line marking the maximum height of dampness.
2. **Peeling plaster, blistering paint** at lower portions of walls.
3. **White salt deposits (efflorescence)** at the upper limit of damp zone.
4. **Decaying skirting boards** and rotting wooden flooring.
5. **Musty smell** and visible mould growth.
6. **Damp patches up to ~1.5 m height** from floor level.

**Remedial Measures (2 marks):**
1. **DPC Installation** — Chemical DPC injection (silicon-based fluids) under pressure.
2. **Physical DPC** — Inserting a damp-proof membrane by section-cutting the wall.
3. **Tanking** — Applying waterproof coating on internal wall surface.
4. **Electro-osmotic DPC** — Applying low voltage electric current to reverse capillary flow.
5. **Replastering** — Using waterproof render with salt-resistant additives.
6. **Site drainage improvement** — Proper plinth protection, drainage away from walls.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Types of Cracks in Masonry (3.5 marks):**

1. **Hairline Cracks** — < 0.5 mm, due to plaster shrinkage, non-structural.
2. **Settlement Cracks** — Diagonal cracks near openings, caused by differential foundation settlement.
3. **Thermal Cracks** — Vertical/horizontal cracks due to temperature-induced expansion/contraction.
4. **Shrinkage Cracks** — Map-pattern (craquelure) cracks in new masonry.
5. **Structural Cracks** — Wide (> 1.5 mm) cracks due to overloading or design deficiency.
6. **Lateral Movement Cracks** — Horizontal cracks with wall bulge due to lateral pressure.
7. **Cracks at Corners** — At junctions of walls, often due to differential movement.

**Distinction Between Structural and Non-Structural Cracks (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Structural Cracks | Non-Structural Cracks |
|---|---|---|
| **Width** | Generally > 1.5 mm | < 1.5 mm |
| **Pattern** | Stepped (through joints) or through units | Random, hairline |
| **Growth** | Progressive, increasing with time | Stable after initial period |
| **Location** | Near load-bearing elements, openings | Surface plaster, non-critical areas |
| **Cause** | Overloading, foundation failure, design flaw | Shrinkage, thermal movement, minor settlement |
| **Effect** | Affects safety and load transfer | Cosmetic, no structural impact |
| **Repair** | Specialist intervention (grouting, underpinning, FRP) | Simple patching and repainting |
| **Urgency** | High — immediate attention | Low — routine maintenance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** Always **monitor cracks** with tell-tales (glass strips or gypsum markers) for at least 3–6 months to determine if they are active (still growing) or dormant (stable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ **Do NOT confuse** efflorescence with whitewash or paint defects. Examiners expect the **chemistry** of salt migration.
> 2. ❌ **Do NOT skip** the **ettringite reaction** in sulfate attack — it carries direct marks. Mention the volume expansion factor (~8×).
> 3. ❌ **Do NOT mix up** rising damp (capillary action) with penetrating damp (rain ingress). They have different mechanisms and remedies.
> 4. ❌ **Always mention units** in numerical derivations. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in formula-based answers.
> 5. ❌ In classification questions, **mention the source/standard** (e.g., BRE Digest 251, IS 1905) to score high.
> 6. ❌ For 14-mark questions, **draw neat labelled diagrams** — at least 1–2 sketches are mandatory for full marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-KNOW Concepts for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 1:**

### ✅ Cracks
- [ ] Six types: hairline, settlement, thermal, shrinkage, structural, lateral
- [ ] Crack width classification: Negligible to Extreme (BRE Digest 251)
- [ ] Stepped pattern = masonry movement; through-unit = stress failure
- [ ] Settlement cracks widen with time → need monitoring

### ✅ Surface Deterioration
- [ ] Spalling (flakes off) vs. Scaling (shallow) vs. Peeling (plaster)
- [ ] Freeze-thaw mechanism: 9% volume expansion of water
- [ ] Fire damages by temperature: < 300°C (safe), 600°C (mortar damage), > 900°C (severe)

### ✅ Moisture Damages
- [ ] Rising damp: Jurin's Law, $h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r\rho g}$, ~1.5 m typical
- [ ] Three damp types: Rising, Condensation, Penetrating
- [ ] DPC injection, tanking, electro-osmosis as remedies

### ✅ Chemical Damages
- [ ] Efflorescence: salt migration to surface (cosmetic)
- [ ] Cryptoflorescence: internal salt crystallization (structural damage)
- [ ] Sulfate attack: $C_3A$ + sulfate → Ettringite (8× volume)
- [ ] Carbonation: $Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3$, pH drops to 8.5

### ✅ Structural Distortions
- [ ] Bulging (outward push), Buckling (slenderness failure), Tilting (rotation)
- [ ] Permissible out-of-plumb: 1/150 of height (IS 1905)

### ✅ Joint Failures
- [ ] Mortar erosion, loss of bond, failed pointing
- [ ] Repointing technique: rake out to 2× joint depth

### ✅ Causes — Diagnosis — Repair Triangle
- [ ] Always identify **root cause**, not just symptom
- [ ] Non-destructive tests: Rebound hammer, UPV, IR thermography
- [ ] Lab tests: Chemical analysis, petrography, compressive strength

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Jurin's Law (capillary rise)
- [ ] Ettringite expansion (112% volume increase)
- [ ] Carbonation depth ($x_c = k\sqrt{t}$)
- [ ] Crack width grading (BRE 251)
- [ ] IS 1905: $f_k = k \cdot f_b^2 \cdot \sqrt{f_m}$
- [ ] Permissible out-of-plumb: $S \leq \frac{1}{150}$

### ✅ Standards to Remember
- [ ] IS 1905 — Structural use of masonry
- [ ] IS 2212 — Brickwork code
- [ ] IS 15912 — Unreinforced masonry
- [ ] BRE Digest 251 — Crack classification
- [ ] BRE Digest 401 — Efflorescence grading
- [ ] ACI 546R — Concrete repair guide
- [ ] IS 1642 / ACI 216.1 — Fire engineering

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Confusing rising damp vs. condensation
- [ ] Missing the ettringite reaction in sulfate attack
- [ ] Forgetting to mention units in numerical answers
- [ ] Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers

---

*End of Topic Notes — Module 1, Damages to Masonry Structures*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — PECET634*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damage Diagnosis and Assessment - Various Aspects of Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Damage Diagnosis and Assessment - Various Aspects of Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Damage Diagnosis and Assessment** in the context of structural engineering is the systematic, scientific, and engineering process of identifying, locating, quantifying, and evaluating the nature, extent, and root causes of distress, deterioration, or defects in existing structures. It forms the **first and most critical phase** of any repair and rehabilitation strategy because "the correct repair is the one that addresses the actual cause, not just the visible symptom."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Damage diagnosis involves the **identification of the type, location, and severity** of defects, while **assessment** refers to the **evaluation of residual capacity, structural adequacy, and remaining service life** of the structure. Together, they form the basis for deciding whether a structure needs **repair, restoration, strengthening, or demolition**.

### Sub-Components of the Topic: "Various Aspects of Inspection"

1. **Preliminary / Visual Inspection** – First-hand non-instrumented observation.
2. **Detailed / Instrument-Based Inspection** – Use of NDT and partially destructive techniques.
3. **Continuous / Long-Term Monitoring** – Periodic or real-time health monitoring.
4. **Testing of Materials** – Sampling and laboratory analysis of concrete, steel, and masonry.
5. **Documentation & Reporting** – Mapping, photographic records, distress catalogs, and final diagnosis report.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Think of an **aging building as a patient visiting a doctor**:

- The patient walks in complaining of pain (the **client/owner** sees cracks, leakage, deflection).
- The doctor first asks questions, observes posture, and checks superficially → this is the **visual inspection**.
- Then the doctor prescribes **blood tests, X-rays, MRI** to "see inside" without cutting → these are the **Non-Destructive Tests (NDT)** like Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV), Rebound Hammer.
- If a tissue sample is needed, a small **biopsy** is taken → this is the **partially destructive test** (core cutting, pull-out test).
- After diagnosis, the doctor prescribes medicine or surgery → this is the **repair/strengthening scheme**.
- Periodic follow-ups are scheduled → this is the **structural health monitoring (SHM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for students:** You cannot prescribe the correct repair without first correctly diagnosing the disease. Skipping the diagnosis step is the **#1 reason repair projects fail** in real-world practice.

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Reference | Typical Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|
| Concrete cube compressive strength (IS 516) | $f_{ck}$ | 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 N/mm² |
| Carbonation depth (phenolphthalein) | IS 14630 | < **5 mm** for new RC structures |
| Chloride content threshold | IS 456:2000 | < **0.4 %** by mass of cement |
| UPV velocity (good concrete) | IS 13311 (Part 1) | > **4.5 km/s** |
| Rebound number (good quality) | IS 13311 (Part 2) | > **30** |
| Half-cell potential | IS 13311 (Part 3 / ASTM C876) | > **-200 mV** = low corrosion risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always cite the **relevant Indian Standard (IS code)** in your KTU answers. Examiners award marks specifically for correct code references.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Damage Assessment Framework

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to write answers in a **structured, sequential manner**. The accepted industry-standard framework (used by CPWD, NABL, and most consulting firms) is:

### Stage 1: Preliminary Investigation
- Review of **original design drawings, construction records, and maintenance history**.
- Identification of **exposure conditions, age, and loading history**.
- Preliminary visual survey with **sketches, photographs, and chainage-wise location tagging**.

### Stage 2: Detailed Visual Inspection
- Mapping of **all visible defects** — cracks, spalling, damp patches, rust stains, deflection, tilting, honeycombing.
- Use of **distress catalogs** such as ACI 201.1R "Guide for Making a Condition Survey of Concrete in Service."
- Measurement of crack widths using **crack width gauges** (units: mm, with **0.2 mm** and **0.3 mm** being critical thresholds for durability and serviceability).

### Stage 3: Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)
- **Rebound Hammer Test** (IS 13311 Part 2) – Surface hardness → empirical correlation with compressive strength.
- **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV)** (IS 13311 Part 1) – Transit time of ultrasonic pulse → concrete quality and flaw detection.
- **Half-Cell Potential (HCP) Meter** (IS 13311 Part 3 / ASTM C876) – Corrosion activity of rebars.
- **Cover Meter / Profometer** – Location, depth, and diameter of rebars; measurement of concrete cover.
- **Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)** – Detection of voids, delamination, tendon ducts, embedded objects.
- **Infrared Thermography** – Detection of moisture ingress, delamination, voids (uses emissivity concept, typical emissivity of concrete $\approx$ **0.94**).
- **Impact-Echo / Acoustic Emission** – Internal flaw detection in slabs, walls, and prestressed elements.

### Stage 4: Destructive / Partially Destructive Testing
- **Core cutting and testing** (IS 516, BS 1881 Part 120) – Direct compressive strength of in-situ concrete.
- **Pull-Out / Pull-Off Test** – Bond strength of surface repair material.
- **Chemical Analysis** – Chloride content, sulphate content, pH, carbonation depth.
- **Petrographic Examination** – Microstructure, alkali-silica reaction (ASR), delayed ettringite formation (DEF).

### Stage 5: Structural Analysis, Reporting & Recommendation
- Use field data to **re-validate structural capacity** (rebar size, cover, strength, loads).
- Estimate **residual service life** (e.g., using Tuutti's two-stage corrosion model).
- Prepare a **Condition Assessment Report (CAR)** with rating, classification, and recommended remedial strategy.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly asked in KTU University Examinations**, including numerical problems. Memorize the units carefully.

### A. Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV)

The pulse velocity through concrete is the path length divided by the transit time:

$$V = \frac{L}{T}$$

where:
- $V$ = pulse velocity (km/s or m/s)
- $L$ = path length (mm or m)
- $T$ = transit time ($\mu$s or s)

### B. Rebound Hammer Correlation (Schmidt Hammer)

The empirical relationship between rebound number $R$ and compressive strength $f_c$ is typically expressed as:

$$f_c = a \cdot R^2 + b \cdot R + c$$

where $a, b, c$ are constants obtained by **calibration curves** specific to the hammer and concrete mix. A simplified linear form often used:

$$f_c = k \cdot R + c_0$$

### C. Half-Cell Potential (Copper / Copper Sulphate Electrode, CSE)

Measured potential $E$ (in mV) versus CSE is interpreted as:

$$\text{Corrosion Risk} = f(E_{CSE})$$

No closed-form equation; it is interpreted through ranges (e.g., $E < -350$ mV = high risk).

### D. Carbonation Depth (Phenolphthalein Test)

The carbonation rate follows a **square-root-of-time law** (Fick's first law approximation):

$$x_c = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

where:
- $x_c$ = carbonation depth (mm)
- $k$ = carbonation coefficient (mm/$\sqrt{\text{year}}$), typically **1 – 5 mm/$\sqrt{\text{yr}}$**
- $t$ = exposure time (years)

### E. Chloride-Induced Corrosion Initiation Time

Using Fick's second law, the time to reach a critical chloride concentration at the rebar depth is:

$$C(x, t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D \cdot t}} \right)$$

where:
- $C(x, t)$ = chloride content at depth $x$ and time $t$ (% by mass of cement)
- $C_s$ = surface chloride content
- $C_i$ = initial chloride content
- $D$ = apparent diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $x$ = cover depth (m)
- $t$ = exposure time (s)

### F. Concrete Quality Classification by UPV (IS 13311 Part 1)

$$V_{concrete} \; \text{quality} = \begin{cases} \text{Doubtful} & V < 3.0 \text{ km/s} \\ \text{Mediocre} & 3.0 \le V < 3.5 \\ \text{Medium} & 3.5 \le V < 4.0 \\ \text{Good} & 4.0 \le V < 4.5 \\ \text{Excellent} & V \ge 4.5 \end{cases}$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Health Monitoring:** NDT + SHM sensors detect progressive damage and prevent collapse (e.g., Morandi Bridge, Italy, 2018).
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Mandatory periodic inspection of containment structures using GPR and acoustic emission.
- **High-Rise Buildings:** Routine assessment of fire-damaged, earthquake-affected, or aged structures using rebound + UPV + cover survey.
- **Heritage Restoration:** Non-invasive methods protect monument integrity while diagnosing hidden decay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection of NDT method depends on the **defect being investigated**. UPV detects internal flaws; Rebound Hammer measures surface hardness; Cover Meter maps rebar; HCP detects active corrosion. **No single NDT method is sufficient alone** — a combination is always recommended.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations

### Derivation 1: UPV Pulse Velocity — Full Worked Derivation

The Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity method is based on the **principle of elastic wave propagation** through a medium. The fundamental wave equation in a 1D elastic bar (or in an infinite medium under uniaxial stress) is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = V^2 \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

where $u(x, t)$ is the particle displacement, $V$ is the wave speed, $x$ is the position, and $t$ is the time.

The general solution of this second-order wave equation (by d'Alembert's method) is:

$$u(x, t) = f(x - V t) + g(x + V t)$$

This represents two waves traveling in opposite directions. For an **ultrasonic pulse generated at one face of a concrete member** and received at the opposite face, only the direct (forward-traveling) wave is of interest. Hence:

$$u(x, t) = f(x - V t)$$

**Setting up the boundary conditions:**

- At $t = 0$ (pulse generation), the wave is at the transmitting transducer face located at $x = 0$:

$$u(0, 0) = f(0) = u_0$$

- The pulse is received at the receiving transducer placed at $x = L$ at time $t = T$ (transit time):

$$u(L, T) = f(L - V T) = u_0$$

For the receiver to register the leading edge of the pulse, the argument of $f$ must equal zero:

$$L - V \cdot T = 0$$

**Solving for V:**

$$V \cdot T = L$$

$$V = \frac{L}{T}$$

**Final result:**

$$V = \frac{L}{T} \quad \text{(units: mm/}\mu\text{s = km/s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the most commonly asked **derivation question** in KTU Module 1. Practice writing it cleanly — the examiner awards full 7 marks only if the wave equation, d'Alembert solution, boundary conditions, and final isolated expression are all present.

---

### Derivation 2: Carbonation Depth — Square-Root-of-Time Law

Carbonation of concrete is governed by the **diffusion of CO₂** into the porous cement paste matrix. CO₂ reacts with calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂] to form calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), reducing pH from ~12.5 to ~9. Fick's first law of diffusion is:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

where:
- $J$ = flux of CO₂ (kg/m²·s)
- $D$ = effective diffusion coefficient of CO₂ in carbonated concrete (m²/s)
- $\frac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient

Assuming **quasi-steady-state** diffusion (concentration profile is linear in the carbonation zone), and that the depth of carbonation $x_c$ advances as the integrated flux equals the available Ca(OH)₂ content:

$$x_c \cdot C_{Ca(OH)_2} = D \cdot (C_{s} - C_{i}) \cdot t$$

Rearranging for $x_c$:

$$x_c = \sqrt{ \frac{D \cdot (C_s - C_i)}{C_{Ca(OH)_2}} } \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

Defining the **carbonation coefficient** as:

$$k = \sqrt{ \frac{D \cdot (C_s - C_i)}{C_{Ca(OH)_2}} }$$

**Final result:**

$$x_c = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

**Units check:**
- $x_c$: mm
- $k$: mm/$\sqrt{\text{year}}$
- $t$: years

---

### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Question)

**Problem:** In a phenolphthalein spray test on a 25-year-old reinforced concrete column, the average carbonation depth was measured as **12 mm**. Assuming a carbonation coefficient of **2.0 mm/$\sqrt{\text{year}}$**, determine the **time to reach the rebar level** if the clear cover is **40 mm**. Also, calculate the **residual time** before corrosion initiation begins.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use $x_c = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ to find the carbonation rate, or find the time at $x_c = 40$ mm.

Rearranging for $t$:

$$t = \left( \frac{x_c}{k} \right)^2$$

$$t = \left( \frac{40}{2.0} \right)^2$$

$$t = (20)^2$$

$$t = 400 \; \text{years}$$

**Step 2:** Residual service life before corrosion begins:

$$t_{residual} = 400 - 25 = 375 \; \text{years}$$

**Interpretation:** The structure is **not at immediate risk** of carbonation-induced corrosion. A more critical cover or a more aggressive environment (higher $k$) would reduce this time dramatically.

---

### Derivation 3: Half-Cell Potential — Nernst Equation Reference

The half-cell potential $E$ of corroding steel in concrete, measured with respect to a Copper / Copper Sulphate reference electrode (CSE), is governed by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \cdot \ln \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$$

For the iron corrosion reaction $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$:

$$E = E^0_{Fe/Fe^{2+}} + \frac{RT}{2F} \cdot \ln [\text{Fe}^{2+}]$$

**Standard interpretation (ASTM C876):**

| $E_{CSE}$ (mV) | Probability of Corrosion |
|---|---|
| $E > -200$ | $< 10\%$ (low risk) |
| $-350 < E \le -200$ | $\approx 50\%$ (uncertain / intermediate) |
| $E \le -350$ | $> 90\%$ (high risk) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Nernst equation derivation is rarely asked in full in KTU; however, the **table of interpretations** is frequently tested as a 2–3 mark short question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of NDT Method

| Defect to Detect | Best NDT Method | Standard Code | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface hardness / strength | Rebound Hammer | IS 13311 Part 2 | Surface-only, needs calibration |
| Internal flaws / voids / cracks | UPV | IS 13311 Part 1 | Cannot detect rebar |
| Active rebar corrosion | Half-Cell Potential | ASTM C876 / IS 13311 Part 3 | Qualitative only |
| Rebar location, size, cover | Cover Meter (Profometer) | BS 1881 Part 204 | Cannot detect deep bars |
| Voids, delamination, tendon ducts | Ground Penetrating Radar | ASTM D6087 | Expensive, skilled operator |
| Moisture, delamination | Infrared Thermography | ASTM C478 | Weather-sensitive |
| Direct strength in-situ | Core cutting | IS 516 / BS 1881 Part 120 | Localized, destructive |
| Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR) | Petrographic + Chemical | ASTM C856 | Time-consuming lab test |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Damage Assessment Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Client Complaint OR Scheduled Audit] --> B0[Stage 1: Preliminary Investigation]
    B0 --> B1[Review Drawings and History]
    B1 --> C0[Stage 2: Detailed Visual Inspection]
    C0 --> C1[Crack Mapping]
    C0 --> C2[Spalling and Deflection Mapping]
    C0 --> C3[Photographic Documentation]
    C1 --> D0[Stage 3: Non Destructive Testing NDT]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> D1[Rebound Hammer IS 13311 Part 2]
    D0 --> D2[UPV IS 13311 Part 1]
    D0 --> D3[Cover Meter]
    D0 --> D4[Half Cell Potential ASTM C876]
    D0 --> D5[Ground Penetrating Radar]
    D1 --> E0[Stage 4: Destructive and Lab Tests]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0
    D5 --> E0
    E0 --> E1[Core Cutting and Compression Test]
    E0 --> E2[Chloride and Sulphate Analysis]
    E0 --> E3[Carbonation Depth by Phenolphthalein]
    E0 --> E4[Petrographic Examination]
    E1 --> F0[Stage 5: Analysis and Reporting]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0
    F0 --> F1[Condition Assessment Report CAR]
    F0 --> F2[Residual Service Life Estimation]
    F0 --> F3[Repair Restoration Strengthening or Demolition Recommendation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow logic for students writing theory answers:** Always write the stages in this exact order — Preliminary → Visual → NDT → Destructive → Analysis/Report. Examiners deduct marks for jumbled or skipped stages.

---

## 4.2 NDT Method Selection Logic (Decision Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[What defect do you want to detect] --> B0{Is it on the surface}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0[Use Rebound Hammer]
    B0 -- No --> D0{Is it an internal flaw void or crack}
    D0 -- Yes --> E0{Is the member accessible from two opposite faces}
    E0 -- Yes --> F0[Use UPV Direct Transmission]
    E0 -- No --> G0[Use UPV Indirect Transmission or GPR or Impact Echo]
    D0 -- No --> H0{Is the defect rebar related}
    H0 -- Yes --> I0{What do you need}
    I0 --> K0[Location and cover] --> L0[Use Cover Meter]
    I0 --> K1[Corrosion activity] --> L1[Use Half Cell Potential Meter]
    I0 --> K2[Deep tendon and duct mapping] --> L2[Use Ground Penetrating Radar]
    H0 -- No --> M0{Is the defect chemical or mineralogical}
    M0 -- Yes --> N0[Use Lab Tests: Chemical, Chloride, Sulphate, Petrographic]
    M0 -- No --> O0[Reassess defect type and consult specialist]
```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Typical UPV Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Concrete Specimen] --> A1[Left Face of Specimen]
    A1 --> A2[Transmitting Transducer 50 kHz]
    A3[Receiving Transducer 50 kHz] --> A4[Right Face of Specimen]
    A2 -- Ultrasonic Pulse through L mm --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Transit Time T microseconds measured by UPV Meter]
    A5 --> A6[Velocity V = L divided by T in km per second]
```

**Test configurations** (commonly asked in KTU viva and short notes):

1. **Direct Transmission** (most accurate): transducers on opposite faces, $V = L/T$, used for columns and beams.
2. **Semi-Direct Transmission**: transducers on adjacent faces, $L$ is the diagonal path.
3. **Indirect (Surface) Transmission**: both transducers on the same face; least accurate, used for one-sided access (slabs, walls).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** in this topic for:
> 1. Not citing the **correct IS code** (e.g., writing "IS 13311" without specifying Part 1, Part 2, or Part 3).
> 2. Confusing **rebound hammer** (surface hardness) with **UPV** (internal flaw detection) — they are NOT substitutes.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **calibration** of NDT equipment on a standard anvil before use.
> 4. Writing "carbonation depth" without mentioning the **phenolphthalein indicator** (1% phenolphthalein in alcohol) which turns **pink in non-carbonated** and **colorless in carbonated** concrete.
> 5. Skipping the **safety and access planning** stage in inspection of high-rise or post-fire structures.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECET634]**
**"List the various aspects of inspection involved in damage diagnosis of a concrete structure. State any three non-destructive tests used."**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

The various aspects of inspection involved in damage diagnosis are:

1. **Preliminary Inspection** – Study of drawings, exposure conditions, age, and history of the structure.
2. **Detailed Visual Inspection** – Mapping of cracks, spalling, deflection, damp patches, and rust staining.
3. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** – Instrument-based evaluation without damaging the structure.
4. **Destructive / Partially Destructive Testing** – Core cutting, chemical analysis, pull-out tests.
5. **Continuous / Long-Term Monitoring** – Periodic inspection, structural health monitoring (SHM) using embedded sensors.

**Three NDT methods (any three, 1 mark each):**

- **Rebound Hammer Test** (IS 13311 Part 2): measures surface hardness, correlated empirically to compressive strength.
- **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test** (IS 13311 Part 1): measures velocity of ultrasonic pulses through concrete to assess quality and detect internal flaws.
- **Half-Cell Potential Method** (IS 13311 Part 3 / ASTM C876): measures electrochemical potential of rebars to assess corrosion activity.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET634]**
**"Explain the phenolphthalein test for determining carbonation depth in concrete. State the colour change observed."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **phenolphthalein test** is a simple, in-situ chemical test used to determine the **depth of carbonation** in hardened concrete. The procedure is:

1. A **fresh fracture surface** of concrete is exposed (by breaking a small piece or drilling a hole and spraying).
2. The surface is **cleaned** of dust and loose particles.
3. A **1% phenolphthalein solution in alcohol** (with a few drops of distilled water) is **sprayed** onto the freshly exposed surface.

**Colour change observed:**
- **Pink / magenta** colour → concrete is **non-carbonated** (pH > ~9, healthy, passivating rebar environment).
- **Colourless (no change)** → concrete is **carbonated** (pH < ~9, loss of passivation, corrosion risk).

**Carbonation depth** is the average distance from the concrete surface to the colour-change boundary, measured at several points using a ruler or scale.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECET634, Module 1, Q1]**
**(a)** Explain the various stages involved in the damage assessment of a concrete structure. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) test**. Mention the velocity ranges for classification of concrete quality as per **IS 13311 (Part 1)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

The damage assessment of a concrete structure is carried out in the following **five systematic stages**:

**Stage 1: Preliminary Investigation (1 mark)**
- Collection of original design drawings, construction records, specifications, and maintenance history.
- Identification of **exposure conditions** (coastal, industrial, alkaline soil, freeze-thaw), **age of structure**, and **loading history**.
- Preliminary site visit to identify accessible areas and safety hazards.

**Stage 2: Detailed Visual Inspection (2 marks)**
- Systematic **crack mapping** on elevation drawings and plans. Cracks are classified by **width** (hairline < 0.2 mm; fine 0.2–0.3 mm; medium 0.3–1.0 mm; wide > 1.0 mm), **pattern** (longitudinal, transverse, diagonal, map-pattern, D-cracks), and **location**.
- Recording of **spalling, honeycombing, efflorescence, damp patches, rust staining, deflection, and tilting** of members.
- Use of **distress catalogs** (e.g., ACI 201.1R), **crack width gauges**, **binoculars**, and **drones** for inaccessible areas.
- Comprehensive **photographic documentation** with date, location, and reference scale.

**Stage 3: Non-Destructive Testing (2 marks)**
- **Rebound Hammer Test** (IS 13311 Part 2): surface hardness → empirical compressive strength.
- **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity Test** (IS 13311 Part 1): internal flaw detection, concrete uniformity.
- **Half-Cell Potential Measurement** (IS 13311 Part 3 / ASTM C876): corrosion activity of rebars.
- **Cover Survey** using a profometer: location, depth, and size of reinforcement.
- **Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)**: voids, tendon ducts, embedded objects.
- **Infrared Thermography**: delamination and moisture detection.
- **Impact-Echo / Acoustic Emission**: deep internal flaw detection in slabs and pavements.

**Stage 4: Destructive / Partially Destructive Testing (1 mark)**
- **Core cutting and compression testing** (IS 516, BS 1881 Part 120).
- **Pull-out / Pull-off tests** for bond strength of repair materials.
- **Chemical tests**: chloride content, sulphate content, pH, carbonation depth (phenolphthalein).
- **Petrographic examination** (ASTM C856): ASR, DEF, microstructure analysis.

**Stage 5: Analysis, Reporting, and Recommendation (1 mark)**
- Compilation of all data into a **Condition Assessment Report (CAR)**.
- Re-evaluation of structural capacity using measured material properties.
- Estimation of **residual service life** using models like Tuutti's two-stage corrosion model.
- Final recommendation: **Repair / Restoration / Strengthening / Demolition** with prioritized action plan.

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**UPV Test — Principle, Procedure, and Classification**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity test is based on the principle of **propagation of elastic stress waves** (longitudinal, shear, and surface waves) through a solid medium. An **electro-acoustical transducer** generates a high-frequency (typically **20–150 kHz**, standard 50 kHz) ultrasonic pulse that is transmitted through the concrete. The pulse travels through the concrete and is received at the opposite end by a similar transducer. The transit time $T$ is measured by a high-precision electronic timing circuit.

The pulse velocity is:

$$V = \frac{L}{T}$$

where $L$ is the path length and $T$ is the transit time.

**Neat Sketch (2 marks):**

```
   Transmitting            CONCRETE           Receiving
   Transducer <=====Pulse travels through===== Transducer
        \                                          /
         \   _________                              /
          \ |         |                            /
           \|         | <-  Path Length L (mm) -> /
            |  M E M  |                            /
            |  B E R  |                            /
            |_________|                            /
                                                 /
   <======== Transit Time T (µs) ==============>/
                    UPV Meter Display
```

**Test Configurations (1 mark):**
1. **Direct Transmission** (opposite faces, most reliable).
2. **Semi-Direct Transmission** (adjacent faces, less accurate).
3. **Indirect / Surface Transmission** (same face, least accurate, used when only one face is accessible).

**Classification of Concrete Quality as per IS 13311 (Part 1) — 2 marks:**

| Pulse Velocity $V$ (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
|---|---|
| $V \ge 4.5$ | **Excellent** |
| $4.0 \le V < 4.5$ | **Good** |
| $3.5 \le V < 4.0$ | **Medium** (questionable) |
| $3.0 \le V < 3.5$ | **Mediocre** (poor) |
| $V < 3.0$ | **Doubtful** (very poor / defective) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always quote the table **exactly as in IS 13311 Part 1** during the exam. Examiners cross-check the boundary values.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice for Internal Option)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET634, Module 1, Q1]**
**(a)** Explain the **Rebound Hammer Test** (Schmidt Hammer) as per **IS 13311 (Part 2)**. Discuss its **principle, procedure, limitations, and the factors affecting the rebound number.** **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Write short notes on **(i) Half-Cell Potential Method**, **(ii) Carbonation Depth Measurement**, and **(iii) Cover Meter Survey.** **(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 marks distribution)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (1 mark):**
The Rebound Hammer (also called **Schmidt Hammer**) test is a **surface hardness test** based on the principle that the **rebound of an elastic mass** from a concrete surface depends on the **surface hardness**, which in turn is related to the **compressive strength** of the concrete. The test is governed by **IS 13311 (Part 2):1992**.

**Apparatus (1 mark):**
- A spring-loaded hammer mass (impact energy **2.207 N·m** for the standard N-type hammer, used for normal-weight concrete of strength **10–70 N/mm²**).
- A plunger that strikes the concrete surface.
- A scale and slider that records the **rebound number $R$** (dimensionless, range 10–100).
- Calibration anvil (standard test block of hardness ~ 80–84 $R$).

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The concrete surface is **smooth, clean, and dry**. Loose material, plaster, and paint are removed.
2. The hammer is **held perpendicular** to the surface and pressed until the spring releases and the mass impacts the surface.
3. The **rebound number $R$** is read from the scale.
4. The test is repeated at **at least 10–12 locations** on the same member; the **average $R$** (after discarding outliers) is taken.
5. The compressive strength is estimated using the **calibration chart** (provided by the manufacturer) or empirical relation:

$$f_c = a \cdot R^2 + b \cdot R + c$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Tests only the **surface layer** (about 25–30 mm deep) and is influenced by **surface carbonation**, moisture, and roughness.
2. Does **not detect internal flaws**; complementary NDT (UPV, GPR) is required.
3. **Empirical correlation** between $R$ and $f_c$ must be established by **calibration on cores** from the same structure.
4. Affected by member **type, size, age, surface finish**, and **direction of impact** (horizontal, vertical-up, vertical-down give different $R$).

**Factors Affecting Rebound Number (1 mark):**
- **Surface texture, moisture, and carbonation** (carbonated surface gives a falsely high $R$).
- **Type of aggregate, mix proportions, and age** of concrete.
- **Direction of impact** (gravity affects the rebound value).
- **Curing conditions and temperature.**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Half-Cell Potential Method (2 marks):**
This test, governed by **ASTM C876** and **IS 13311 (Part 3)**, is used to assess the **corrosion activity of reinforcing steel** embedded in concrete. A **Copper / Copper Sulphate reference electrode (CSE)** is placed on the concrete surface, and a **voltmeter** is connected between the electrode and an exposed rebar (connection made by breaking out a small concrete patch to expose the rebar). The **electrical potential difference** (in mV) is measured. Interpretation per ASTM C876:

- $E > -200$ mV: **< 10% probability** of active corrosion.
- $-350 < E \le -200$ mV: **~50% probability** (intermediate / uncertain corrosion).
- $E \le -350$ mV: **> 90% probability** of active corrosion.

**(ii) Carbonation Depth Measurement (2 marks):**
As detailed in Q.A above, the **phenolphthalein test** is used. A **1% phenolphthalein solution in alcohol** is sprayed on a freshly exposed concrete surface. The **non-carbonated zone turns pink** (pH > 9), while the **carbonated zone remains colourless** (pH < 9). The **average depth** of the colourless zone from the concrete surface is taken as the **carbonation depth** $x_c$. If $x_c$ exceeds the clear cover to the rebar, the **passivation of steel is lost** and corrosion may initiate.

**(iii) Cover Meter Survey (3 marks):**
A **cover meter (Profometer)** is a non-destructive instrument that uses the principle of **electromagnetic induction** to detect the presence, location, depth, and approximate diameter of **ferrous reinforcement** in concrete. A search head containing a coil is moved over the concrete surface; the presence of steel changes the **magnetic flux** and induces a signal that is displayed on the meter (either as an analog needle deflection or a digital reading).

**Uses:**
- Locating **rebar position** before drilling or cutting.
- Measuring **clear cover** for durability and corrosion-risk assessment.
- Estimating **rebar diameter** (using a sizing bar or two-frequency instruments).

**Limitations:**
- Affected by **closely spaced bars** (congestion leads to misinterpretation).
- Cannot detect **non-ferrous** reinforcement (eGFRP, CFRP, aluminium).
- Limited **depth range** (typically up to 100 mm for standard instruments).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Damage Diagnosis ≠ Damage Assessment.** Diagnosis identifies **what and where** the defect is; assessment evaluates **how serious it is and how long the structure will last**.
- The **five-stage framework** (Preliminary → Visual → NDT → Destructive → Reporting) is the **expected KTU answer structure** for any 14-mark question.
- Always **cite IS codes** with the **Part number** (e.g., IS 13311 Part 1 for UPV, Part 2 for Rebound, Part 3 for HCP).
- **UPV** detects **internal flaws** but cannot find rebar; **Cover Meter** finds rebar but not internal flaws. They are **complementary, not substitutes**.
- **Rebound Hammer** gives **surface hardness**, which is then empirically correlated to **compressive strength** using $f_c = aR^2 + bR + c$ — calibration on cores is essential.
- **Phenolphthalein turns pink in non-carbonated** and **colourless in carbonated** concrete. If $x_c$ > clear cover, corrosion risk is high.
- **Half-Cell Potential** (ASTM C876): $E > -200$ mV = safe; $E \le -350$ mV = high corrosion probability.
- **Carbonation follows** $x_c = k \sqrt{t}$ (Fickian diffusion-based square-root law).
- **Tuutti's two-stage corrosion model** distinguishes **initiation phase** (no damage, just depassivation) and **propagation phase** (visible damage, cracking, spalling).
- **No single NDT method is sufficient** — a combination (UPV + Rebound + Cover + HCP) is always recommended for a reliable diagnosis.
- For KTU answers, **always include a sketch** for any test you describe — examiners award at least 1–2 marks specifically for neat labelled diagrams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Before writing the answer, spend 30 seconds outlining the 5 stages and which IS code applies. This structured approach alone typically gains you 2–3 extra marks compared to unstructured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment Procedure for Evaluating a Damaged Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assessment Procedure for Evaluating a Damaged Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Assessment of a Damaged Structure** is a systematic, multi-stage engineering investigation process that involves the qualitative and quantitative evaluation of a structure's existing condition, residual strength, durability, and serviceability after the onset of distress. The procedure integrates **visual inspection**, **non-destructive testing (NDT)**, **destructive testing**, and **structural analysis** to determine the *extent*, *cause*, and *severity* of damage, thereby establishing the basis for deciding whether a structure requires **repair, rehabilitation, strengthening, or demolition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The assessment procedure is not a single test — it is a **sequenced protocol** starting from preliminary desk study to detailed in-situ testing and final analytical evaluation. Examiners expect students to recall the *sequence of investigations* in the correct order.

### The Standard Sequence of Assessment (KTU Recommended Protocol)

The assessment procedure is conventionally divided into **four logical stages**, executed sequentially:

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| **Stage I** | Preliminary / Desk Study | Historical data, drawings, original design |
| **Stage II** | Visual Inspection & Condition Survey | Mapping of visible defects |
| **Stage III** | Detailed In-situ Testing (NDT + Destructive) | Material & strength properties |
| **Stage IV** | Structural Analysis & Final Report | Decision on repair strategy |

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Check-up"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a damaged building the way a doctor examines a sick patient:
> - **Stage I (Desk study)** → Reading the patient's medical history file.
> - **Stage II (Visual inspection)** → The doctor looking at the patient, noting rashes, swelling, deformities.
> - **Stage III (NDT & Testing)** → Blood tests, X-rays, MRI scans — these give **quantitative** readings of what's happening inside.
> - **Stage IV (Structural analysis + Report)** → Diagnosis + prescription (repair, rehab, or demolition).
>
> A doctor never prescribes medicine without tests, and an engineer never prescribes a repair without proper assessment. **"Diagnose before you treat."**

## Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary (High-Frequency in KTU Papers):**
> - **Distress** — Visible manifestation of damage (cracks, spalling, deflection).
> - **Defect** — A flaw originating from construction or design phase.
> - **Deterioration** — Loss of performance over time due to environmental/loading actions.
> - **Residual Strength** — The remaining load-carrying capacity after damage.
> - **Serviceability** — The ability of the structure to perform its intended function (deflection, cracking, vibration limits).
> - **Durability** — Resistance against weathering, chemical attack, and aging.

## Typical Causes of Damage to Structures

Before assessing a structure, the engineer must know *what* to look for. The **common causes** classified by KTU syllabus are:

1. **Mechanical causes** — Overloading, impact, vibration, fatigue, abrasion.
2. **Chemical causes** — Corrosion of steel, sulfate attack, alkali-silica reaction (ASR), carbonation-induced corrosion.
3. **Physical causes** — Freeze-thaw cycles, fire damage, moisture ingress, thermal expansion.
4. **Environmental causes** — Marine exposure, industrial pollution, acid rain.
5. **Design & construction errors** — Insufficient cover, poor concrete mix, inadequate reinforcement, bad detailing.
6. **Natural calamities** — Earthquakes, floods, landslides.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Stage I — Preliminary / Desk Study

The assessment officially begins **before** the engineer physically visits the site. A desk study compiles:

- **Original design documents** — structural drawings, design calculations, load assumptions.
- **Construction records** — concrete mix design, cube test results, material test certificates, contractor's quality control records.
- **Service history** — Date of construction, modifications, repairs done earlier, exposure conditions.
- **Geotechnical data** — Soil investigation reports.
- **Complaints / symptoms reported** by occupants (cracks on walls, leakage, door jamming, sagging beams).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** A 30-year-old structure showing fresh cracks may either be experiencing *new distress* or *latent defects* from the original construction. The desk study tells the engineer where to focus during site inspection.

## Stage II — Visual Inspection & Condition Survey

A **systematic walk-through** of the structure, documenting every visible defect with photographs, sketches, and measurements. Tools used: **measuring tape, crack-width gauge, plumb bob, magnifying glass, camera, flashlight, marker pens**.

### Defect Mapping — What to Document

For **each defect**, the following six parameters must be recorded (a KTU favourite question):

1. **Type** of defect (crack, spalling, delamination, efflorescence, corrosion, deflection).
2. **Location** — Grid reference, elevation, member identification.
3. **Extent** — Length, area, volume of affected zone.
4. **Pattern** — Map, longitudinal, transverse, diagonal, random, honeycomb, etc.
5. **Severity / Width** — For cracks, measured in mm using a crack microscope or gauge.
6. **Probable cause** — Tentative inference based on pattern and experience.

### Crack Pattern — The Engineer's "X-Ray"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cracks tell a story. KTU examiners love asking "what does this crack pattern indicate?"**
>
> | Crack Pattern | Probable Cause |
> |---------------|----------------|
> | Vertical cracks in beams at mid-span | Flexural cracks due to overload / under-reinforcement |
> | Diagonal (45°) shear cracks in beams near supports | Shear failure / insufficient shear reinforcement |
> | Vertical cracks in columns (mid-height) | Flexural cracks due to axial + bending |
> | Horizontal cracks in columns | Spalling of cover, corrosion-induced |
> | Map (pattern) cracks on slabs | Shrinkage, ASR, or freeze-thaw |
> | Cracks parallel to reinforcement | Corrosion of rebar (rust occupies 6x volume of steel) |
> | D-shaped cracking in slabs | Corrosion of top reinforcement |

## Stage III — Detailed In-Situ Testing

This is the **quantitative heart** of the assessment. Tests are broadly classified into:

### A. Non-Destructive Tests (NDT)

NDTs do **not** damage the structure and can be repeated on the same member.

#### 1. Rebound Hammer Test (Schmidt Hammer) — IS 13311 (Part 2):1992

- **Principle:** A spring-loaded mass strikes the concrete surface; the rebound distance is read on a scale.
- **Indicator:** **Rebound Number (R)** — higher R = harder, stronger surface concrete.
- **Output:** Estimate of **surface hardness → indicative compressive strength** using manufacturer's correlation charts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitations (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - Tests only the **surface layer** (~30 mm depth).
> - Highly sensitive to **surface texture, moisture, carbonation, and operator skill**.
> - Should be **calibrated** using the specific concrete in question via core tests.

#### 2. Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test — IS 13311 (Part 1):1992

- **Principle:** An ultrasonic pulse is sent through concrete; the **transit time (t)** is measured between transmitter and receiver.
- **Velocity Formula:**

$$V = \frac{L}{t}$$

where $V$ = pulse velocity (km/s), $L$ = path length (mm), $t$ = transit time (microseconds).

- **Transducer arrangements:**
  - **Direct (through transmission)** — most accurate.
  - **Semi-direct** — transducers on adjacent faces.
  - **Indirect (surface)** — least accurate, used when only one face is accessible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 13311 Velocity Quality Grading (Must Memorize for KTU):**
>
> | Pulse Velocity (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
> |----------------------|------------------|
> | > 4.0 | Excellent |
> | 3.5 – 4.0 | Good |
> | 3.0 – 3.5 | Medium |
> | < 3.0 | Doubtful (questionable) |

#### 3. Cover Meter Survey (Profometer Test) — IS 13311 (Part 3):1992

- Detects **location, depth, and diameter** of reinforcement without breaking the concrete.
- **Output:** Actual cover to reinforcement, bar spacing, bar size — essential for **corrosion assessment**.

#### 4. Half-Cell Potential (HCP) Test — ASTM C876

- **Principle:** Measures the **electrical potential difference** between the reinforcing bar (acting as one electrode) and a standard reference electrode (Cu/CuSO₄) placed on the concrete surface.
- **Output:** Probability of **active corrosion** at the test location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASTM C876 Corrosion Probability (KTU favourite):**
>
> | Half-Cell Potential (mV vs Cu/CuSO₄) | Corrosion Probability |
> |--------------------------------------|----------------------|
> | > -200 | > 90% probability of no corrosion |
> | -200 to -350 | Corrosion activity uncertain |
> | < -350 | > 90% probability of active corrosion |

#### 5. Carbonation Depth Test (Phenphthalein Spray Test)

- **Principle:** A freshly broken concrete surface is sprayed with **1% phenolphthalein indicator**.
- **Result:** Carbonated concrete stays **colourless** (pH < 8.3); non-carbonated turns **pink/red** (pH > 8.3).
- **Measure:** Depth of colourless zone = **carbonation depth** in mm.
- **Significance:** When carbonation front reaches the rebar, the protective passive layer (due to high pH) breaks down → corrosion initiates.

#### 6. Chloride Content Test

- Determines the **chloride ion concentration** in concrete (typically at different depths).
- **Threshold:** Chloride content > **0.4% by mass of cement** for ordinary RC and > **0.2%** for pre-stressed concrete (as per IS 456:2000 / ACI 222R) indicates significant corrosion risk.

#### 7. Resistivity Test

- Measures the **electrical resistivity** of concrete; low resistivity indicates high moisture and chloride ingress → high corrosion risk.

### B. Partially Destructive Tests

#### 1. Core Sampling & Testing — IS 516:1959 (Reaffirmed 2018)

- A cylindrical core is **drilled out** of the structure (typically 100 mm or 150 mm diameter).
- Tested in compression to obtain **actual in-situ compressive strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> Core test is considered the **most reliable** estimate of in-situ concrete strength and is used to **calibrate** rebound hammer and UPV readings.

#### 2. Pull-Out Test

- A steel rod is anchored into concrete and pulled; the force required to extract it correlates with compressive strength.

#### 3. Penetration Resistance Test (Windsor Probe)

- A probe is driven into concrete by a powder charge; penetration depth inversely correlates with strength.

### C. Destructive / Localized Tests

- Rebar extraction for tensile testing.
- Petrographic examination of concrete samples (for ASR, sulfate attack, etc.).
- Load testing of structural members (rarely performed, only when mandated).

## Stage IV — Structural Analysis & Final Report

Based on the data from Stages I–III, the engineer performs:

1. **Re-analysis** of the structure using **actual measured material properties** (reduced strength due to damage) and **current loading** (as per latest IS codes).
2. **Comparison** of demand vs. capacity at member and structural level.
3. **Decision matrix** — repair, strengthen, or demolish.
4. **Final report** with:
   - Condition assessment summary.
   - Cause analysis.
   - Test results and interpretation.
   - Structural adequacy statement.
   - Recommended remedial measures with cost estimates.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains every formula a KTU student needs for this topic.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Notation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | $V$=velocity, $L$=path length, $t$=transit time | km/s, mm, µs | UPV test |
| 2 | $f_{ck,\text{est}} = f(R)$ (correlation chart) | $R$ = rebound number | MPa | Rebound hammer |
| 3 | $f_{ck,\text{core}} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi D^2}$ | $P$=load, $D$=core diameter | MPa | Core test |
| 4 | $E_{c,\text{dyn}} = \rho V^2 \dfrac{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)}$ | $\rho$=density, $\nu$=Poisson's ratio | MPa | Dynamic modulus via UPV |
| 5 | $E_{c} = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | $E_c$ = elastic modulus | MPa | Static modulus (IS 456) |
| 6 | Carbonation depth = measured uncoloured zone length | — | mm | Phenolphthalein test |
| 7 | $\text{Cl}^- \text{ threshold} = 0.4\% \text{ of cement mass}$ | — | % | Corrosion initiation (IS 456) |
| 8 | HCP > $-200$ mV = safe; < $-350$ mV = corroding | Cu/CuSO₄ ref. | mV | Corrosion probability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## Worked Example 1 — UPV Test Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A UPV test is conducted on a 300 mm thick RCC column. The transit time recorded between the two transducers placed on opposite faces is 78.5 microseconds. Determine the pulse velocity and grade the concrete quality as per IS 13311 (Part 1).

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Data

- Path length, $L = 300$ mm (since transducers are on opposite faces → **direct transmission**)
- Transit time, $t = 78.5 \, \mu s$

### Step 2 — Apply the UPV Formula

$$V = \frac{L}{t}$$

$$V = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{78.5 \, \mu s}$$

### Step 3 — Unit Conversion (Critical KTU Step)

We must convert mm/µs to km/s because IS 13311 grades use **km/s**.

Since $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-6} \text{ km}$ and $1 \mu s = 10^{-9} \text{ s}$:

$$1 \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\mu s} = \frac{10^{-6} \text{ km}}{10^{-9} \text{ s}} = 10^{3} \, \frac{\text{km}}{\text{s}}$$

Therefore:

$$V = \frac{300}{78.5} \times 10^{3} \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\mu s}$$

$$V = 3.821 \times 10^{3} \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\mu s}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 3.82 \text{ km/s}}$$

### Step 4 — Grade the Concrete as per IS 13311 (Part 1)

Referring to the standard grading:

| Velocity (km/s) | Quality |
|----------------|---------|
| 3.82 | Falls in range 3.5 – 4.0 |

**Conclusion:** The concrete is of **"Good" quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** While the concrete is rated "Good", the surface-near regions may show lower velocity in some cases. Always test multiple locations to capture variability.

---

## Worked Example 2 — Core Test Strength Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 100 mm diameter core extracted from a damaged RCC beam fails in compression at a load of 540 kN. Estimate the in-situ compressive strength. The L/D ratio of the core is 1.95.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Data

- Core diameter, $D = 100$ mm
- Failure load, $P = 540 \text{ kN} = 540{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $L/D = 1.95$ (close to standard 2.0; minor correction needed)

### Step 2 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (100)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 10{,}000}{4} = 2500\pi \approx 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Apparent Cylinder Strength

$$f_{c,\text{apparent}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{540{,}000 \text{ N}}{7853.98 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$f_{c,\text{apparent}} \approx 68.75 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the L/D Correction Factor (IS 516)

For $L/D = 1.95$, the correction factor is read from IS 516 table (interpolated):

| L/D | Correction Factor |
|-----|-------------------|
| 2.00 | 1.00 |
| 1.75 | 0.98 |
| 1.50 | 0.96 |

For $L/D = 1.95$, linear interpolation gives:

$$k = 1.00 - \frac{(2.00 - 1.95)}{(2.00 - 1.75)} \times (1.00 - 0.98)$$

$$k = 1.00 - \frac{0.05}{0.25} \times 0.02 = 1.00 - 0.004 = 0.996$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Corrected Core Strength

$$f_{ck,\text{core}} = k \times f_{c,\text{apparent}} = 0.996 \times 68.75$$

$$\boxed{f_{ck,\text{core}} \approx 68.48 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

The original design was probably for **M20 or M25** grade concrete. The current strength (~68 MPa) is *higher* than design, suggesting the distress is **not due to strength loss** but could be due to **corrosion, overloading, or serviceability issues** (e.g., excessive deflection or cracking).

---

## Worked Example 3 — Rebound Hammer & Core Correlation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A rebound hammer test on a column gives an average rebound number of 38. The manufacturer's chart for the hammer indicates that R = 38 corresponds to a compressive strength of 32 MPa. A core taken from the same column gives 26 MPa. Calculate the strength correction factor and the estimated in-situ strength.

### Step 1 — Given Data

- Rebound number, $R = 38$
- Strength from rebound chart, $f_{rh} = 32$ MPa
- Actual core strength, $f_{core} = 26$ MPa

### Step 2 — Compute the Correction Factor

$$\text{Correction Factor} = \frac{f_{core}}{f_{rh}} = \frac{26}{32} = 0.8125$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Factor to All Rebound Readings

For other columns where rebound readings are taken, the **corrected estimated strength** is:

$$f_{ck,\text{est}} = 0.8125 \times f_{rh,\text{at that location}}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The rebound hammer **overestimated** the strength in this case (32 vs. 26 MPa actual). This commonly happens because:
> - Surface carbonation artificially hardens the surface.
> - Surface finishing (trowelling) increases surface density.
>
> Hence, **calibration of NDT with at least 3 cores per 100 m³ of concrete** is a standard KTU-recommended practice.

---

## Worked Example 4 — Carbonation Depth and Time to Rebar Corrosion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 30-year-old RCC building has a clear cover of 25 mm. Phenolphthalein test shows carbonation depth of 18 mm. Estimate the rate of carbonation and predict when corrosion of rebar is likely to commence.

### Step 1 — Apply the Square-Root-Time Law of Carbonation

The depth of carbonation follows a diffusion-controlled relationship:

$$x = k \sqrt{t}$$

where $x$ = carbonation depth (mm), $k$ = carbonation coefficient (mm/√year), $t$ = exposure time (years).

### Step 2 — Solve for k (Coefficient)

$$k = \frac{x}{\sqrt{t}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{30}}$$

$$\sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$$

$$k = \frac{18}{5.477} \approx 3.286 \text{ mm/}\sqrt{\text{year}}$$

### Step 3 — Predict Time to Reach Rebar (x = 25 mm)

$$t = \left( \frac{x}{k} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{25}{3.286} \right)^2$$

$$t = (7.607)^2 \approx 57.87 \text{ years}$$

### Step 4 — Years Until Corrosion Initiates

$$\Delta t = 57.87 - 30 = 27.87 \text{ years}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion

**Corrosion is likely to initiate in approximately 28 more years** if remedial measures are not taken. The current carbonation front is 18 mm; the rebar is at 25 mm; a buffer of only 7 mm remains.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Trap: Many students forget to subtract the *current age* from the *total time to corrosion*, leading to inflated remaining-life predictions. Always compute $\Delta t = t_{\text{rebar}} - t_{\text{current}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
