### 1.1 Formal Definition (As per IS 13311 & KTU Syllabus)

**Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) test** is a Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) technique used to evaluate the quality, uniformity, and integrity of concrete by measuring the velocity of ultrasonic stress waves (longitudinal pulses) passing through a concrete element.

$$ V = \frac{L}{T} $$

Where:
- $V$ = Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (km/s)
- $L$ = Path Length (distance between transmitter and receiver) in **km** or **m**
- $T$ = Travel Time of the pulse (in **microseconds, $\mu s$** or **seconds**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** UPV is classified as a *Volumetric/Through-Transmission NDT method* and is governed by **IS 13311 (Part 1): 1992** – *"Non-Destructive Testing of Concrete – Methods of Test, Part 1: Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Stethoscope for Concrete"

Imagine a doctor using a **stethoscope** on your chest. The sound travels faster through a healthy heart and lungs, but slower (or echoes oddly) if there is fluid, crack, or hollow space inside. 

The UPV test works on the **same principle**:
- The transmitter acts as the "tap" on the chest.
- The receiver acts as the "stethoscope."
- The concrete acts as the "body organ."

If concrete is **dense, well-compacted, and crack-free** → pulse travels **fast** (High Velocity).
If concrete has **voids, honeycombs, cracks, or is weak** → pulse travels **slow** (Low Velocity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physical Constant:** The standard transducer frequency used for routine concrete testing is between **20 kHz to 150 kHz**. Frequency of 50 kHz to 60 kHz is most commonly employed for general concrete, while lower frequencies (~20-30 kHz) are used for large/longer elements.

### 1.3 Working Principle (The Physics Behind It)

When an electrical pulse is applied to the **piezoelectric transmitter**, it generates a mechanical vibration at ultrasonic frequency. This vibration passes through the concrete as a **mechanical wave** and is picked up by the **piezoelectric receiver**, which converts it back into an electrical signal. The instrument measures the **transit time (T)** of this pulse.

The velocity is theoretically related to the **dynamic elastic modulus** of concrete:

$$ V = \sqrt{\frac{E_d (1 - \nu)}{\rho (1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)}} $$

Where:
- $E_d$ = Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity (N/mm²)
- $\nu$ = Dynamic Poisson's Ratio
- $\rho$ = Density of concrete (kg/m³)

> [!TIP]
> **Why Ultrasonic?** Because the frequency range (20–150 kHz) is above human hearing (>20 kHz) and gives precise short-duration pulses that travel through solid media with minimal energy loss.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Procedure – Stepwise Logic

**Step 1: Surface Preparation**
The concrete surface at both transducer contact points must be **smooth, clean, and free of dust, laitance, or loose particles**. A coupling medium (grease, petroleum jelly, or liquid soap) is applied to ensure **acoustic coupling** (no air gap).

**Step 2: Transducer Placement**
Place the transmitter and receiver on opposite/same faces of the concrete element. Apply firm pressure to ensure proper contact.

**Step 3: Pulse Generation**
The transmitter emits an ultrasonic pulse. Simultaneously, an internal timer in the equipment starts.

**Step 4: Signal Reception**
The receiver detects the leading edge of the arriving pulse and **stops the timer**, recording the travel time $T$.

**Step 5: Velocity Computation**
The instrument internally computes $V = L/T$ and displays it in km/s.

**Step 6: Interpretation**
Compare the obtained $V$ with the standard quality grading chart (IS 13311 classification) to assess concrete quality.

### 2.2 Transducer Arrangement (Three Configurations)

| Configuration | Path | Application | Best For |
|---------------|------|-------------|----------|
| **Direct (Through Transmission)** | Opposite faces | Most accurate | Columns, beams, slabs (two accessible opposite sides) |
| **Semi-Direct (Diagonal)** | Adjacent faces | Moderate accuracy | Corners of columns, beam-column joints |
| **Indirect (Surface)** | Same face | Least accurate | One-side access only, slab soffits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse velocity in direct transmission > semi-direct > indirect.** This is because direct transmission travels through the **bulk of concrete**, while surface transmission travels through a thin near-surface layer which may be affected by curing quality, carbonation, or surface cracking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V = L/T$ | Basic pulse velocity | km/s |
| 2 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_d (1-\nu)}{\rho (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | Velocity-Elasticity relation | km/s |
| 3 | $E_d = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V^2 (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)}$ | Dynamic Elastic Modulus | N/mm² or GPa |
| 4 | $E_d = \rho \cdot V^2$ | Simplified (for $\nu = 0.2$) | N/mm² |
| 5 | $T = L/V$ | Travel time | $\mu s$ |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{g}$ | Density from unit weight | kg/m³ |

**Where:** $E_d$ = Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.20 for concrete), $\rho$ = Density, $\gamma$ = Unit weight, $g$ = 9.81 m/s².

### 2.4 Quality Grading of Concrete (IS 13311 Part 1)

| Pulse Velocity (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
|------------------------|------------------|
| **> 4.5** | Excellent |
| **3.5 – 4.5** | Good |
| **3.0 – 3.5** | Medium / Satisfactory |
| **< 3.0** | Doubtful / Poor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** UPV is used to (a) detect **honeycombing & voids**, (b) identify **cracks & delamination**, (c) assess **uniformity of concrete**, (d) estimate **in-situ strength** (with co-relation curves), and (e) monitor **deterioration over time** due to fire, chemical attack, or aging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pulse Velocity in a Solid Medium

The speed of a longitudinal stress wave in an infinite elastic solid is given by the classical wave-propagation equation.

**Starting Equation (From Elasticity Theory):**

The one-dimensional wave equation for a longitudinal stress wave in a thin elastic bar is:

$$ \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{E}{\rho} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} $$

Where:
- $u$ = particle displacement
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material
- $\rho$ = mass density

**Step 1 – Solution of the Wave Equation**

The general solution of this second-order wave equation is of the form:

$$ u(x, t) = f(x - V_0 t) + g(x + V_0 t) $$

Where $f$ represents a forward-traviving wave and $g$ represents a backward-travelling wave. The velocity of propagation $V_0$ is found by substituting this solution back into the differential equation.

**Step 2 – Identification of Wave Speed**

By substitution, the wave speed in a thin bar (1-D case, lateral inertia neglected) is:

$$ V_{bar} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} $$

**Step 3 – Modification for Infinite Solid (3-D Case)**

For a stress wave in an *infinite 3-D solid* (lateral expansion is constrained by Poisson's effect), the velocity of the longitudinal wave is governed by the **P-wave modulus** $M$:

$$ M = \frac{E(1 - \nu)}{(1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)} $$

Therefore, the velocity of the ultrasonic pulse in a concrete element is:

$$ V = \sqrt{\frac{M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E_d (1 - \nu)}{\rho (1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)}} $$

**Step 4 – Dynamic Elastic Modulus Rearrangement**

Squaring both sides:

$$ V^2 = \frac{E_d (1 - \nu)}{\rho (1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)} $$

Rearranging to solve for $E_d$:

$$ E_d = \frac{\rho \cdot V^2 \cdot (1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)}{(1 - \nu)} $$

**Step 5 – Simplified Form (Standard Assumption $\nu = 0.2$)**

For normal-weight concrete, Poisson's ratio is conventionally taken as $\nu = 0.20$:

$$ (1+\nu)(1-2\nu) = (1.2)(0.6) = 0.72 $$
$$ (1 - \nu) = 0.80 $$

Substituting:

$$ E_d = \frac{\rho \cdot V^2 \cdot 0.72}{0.80} = 0.90 \, \rho \, V^2 $$

This gives the **commonly used approximate relation**:

$$ \boxed{E_d \approx \rho \, V^2} $$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A UPV test was conducted on a concrete column of cross-section 300 mm × 300 mm using the direct transmission method. The measured transit time was 60 µs. The density of concrete is 2400 kg/m³, and Poisson's ratio is 0.20. Determine:
- (a) The ultrasonic pulse velocity.
- (b) The dynamic modulus of elasticity of concrete.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pulse Velocity:**

Path length $L = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m
Transit time $T = 60 \, \mu s = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ s

$$ V = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{0.3}{60 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ V = \frac{0.3}{0.00006} = 5000 \, m/s = 5.0 \, km/s $$

**(b) Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity:**

Using the full 3-D formula:

$$ E_d = \frac{\rho \cdot V^2 \cdot (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)} $$

First, convert V to m/s and compute $V^2$:

$$ V^2 = (5000)^2 = 25 \times 10^6 \, m^2/s^2 $$

Substituting $\rho = 2400 \, kg/m^3$, $V^2 = 25 \times 10^6$, $\nu = 0.20$:

$$ E_d = \frac{2400 \times 25 \times 10^6 \times (1.2)(0.6)}{(0.8)} $$

$$ E_d = \frac{2400 \times 25 \times 10^6 \times 0.72}{0.8} $$

$$ E_d = \frac{4.32 \times 10^{10}}{0.8} = 5.4 \times 10^{10} \, N/m^2 $$

$$ E_d = 5.4 \times 10^{10} \, N/m^2 = 54 \, GPa = 54000 \, N/mm^2 $$

Using simplified relation $E_d \approx \rho V^2$:

$$ E_d = 2400 \times 25 \times 10^6 = 6.0 \times 10^{10} \, N/m^2 = 60 \, GPa $$

> [!NOTE]
> The simplified formula gives a slightly higher value because it neglects the Poisson's effect correction. The full 3-D formula is the more accurate KTU-accepted answer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Factors Affecting Pulse Velocity

| Factor | Effect on Pulse Velocity | Direction |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Increase in concrete strength | Velocity increases | ↑ |
| Higher water-cement ratio | More voids → Velocity decreases | ↓ |
| Presence of cracks/voids | Pulse detours, T increases | ↓ |
| Increased moisture content | Velocity increases slightly | ↑ |
| Higher temperature (up to 60°C) | Velocity decreases | ↓ |
| Steel reinforcement (along path) | Velocity increases (V in steel ≈ 5.9 km/s) | ↑ |
| Longer path length | Reduces edge effects | ↑ accuracy |
| Better compaction | Fewer voids → Velocity increases | ↑ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UPV Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser Generator Unit] -->|Electrical Pulse| B[Transmitter Transducer]
    B -->|Piezoelectric Vibration| C[Concrete Element under Test]
    C -->|Stress Wave Propagation| D[Receiver Transducer]
    D -->|Electrical Signal| E[Amplifier and Filter]
    E --> F[Time Measurement Circuit]
    F --> G[Display Unit showing V in km per s]
    H[Coupling Medium Application] -.-> B
    H -.-> D
    I[Path Length L Input] -.-> F
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Transducer Arrangement Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UPV Test Configurations] --> B[Direct Transmission]
    A --> C[Semi Direct Transmission]
    A --> D[Indirect or Surface Transmission]
    B --> B1[Transmitter and Receiver on Opposite Faces]
    B --> B2[Most Accurate and Preferred Method]
    B --> B3[Used for Columns Beams Cubes]
    C --> C1[Transmitter and Receiver on Adjacent Faces]
    C --> C2[Diagonal Path through Concrete]
    C --> C3[Used at Corners and Joints]
    D --> D1[Both Transducers on Same Face]
    D --> D2[Surface Wave Propagation]
    D --> D3[Used when only One Face Accessible]
    D --> D4[Least Accurate due to Surface Layer Influence]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow Chart – Interpretation of UPV Results

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure Pulse Velocity V in km per s] --> B{V Greater than 4.5}
    B -->|Yes| C[Excellent Quality Concrete]
    B -->|No| D{V between 3.5 and 4.5}
    D -->|Yes| E[Good Quality Concrete]
    D -->|No| F{V between 3.0 and 3.5}
    F -->|Yes| G[Medium Quality Satisfactory]
    F -->|No| H{V less than 3.0}
    H -->|Yes| I[Doubtful Quality Further Investigation Needed]
    C --> J[Accept and Continue Service]
    E --> J
    G --> K[Conditional Accept Check Other Parameters]
    I --> L[Reject or Carry Out Detailed Testing]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#00FA9A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Influence Factor Interrelationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concrete Quality] --> B[Compressive Strength]
    A --> C[UPV Pulse Velocity]
    A --> D[Dynamic Elastic Modulus]
    B -.->|Empirical Correlation| C
    C -->|Direct Relationship| D
    E[Compaction] --> A
    F[Water Cement Ratio] --> A
    G[Aggregate Type] --> A
    H[Age of Concrete] --> A
    I[Moisture Content] --> C
    J[Steel Reinforcement Presence] --> C
    K[Path Length and Cracks] --> C
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity test. State the governing Indian Standard code and list the three transducer arrangements used.**

**Model Answer:**
The Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) test is a non-destructive testing method that measures the velocity of longitudinal ultrasonic stress waves through concrete to assess its quality, uniformity, and integrity.

$$ V = \frac{L}{T} $$

The governing Indian Standard is **IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992**.

The three transducer arrangements are:
1. **Direct transmission** – transducers on opposite faces (most accurate)
2. **Semi-direct transmission** – transducers on adjacent faces
3. **Indirect (surface) transmission** – both transducers on the same face (least accurate)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four quality grades of concrete as per IS 13311 based on UPV values, and state any three factors that affect pulse velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Pulse Velocity (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
|------------------------|------------------|
| > 4.5 | Excellent |
| 3.5 – 4.5 | Good |
| 3.0 – 3.5 | Medium |
| < 3.0 | Doubtful |

**Three factors affecting pulse velocity:**
1. Presence of cracks, voids, or honeycombing in concrete.
2. Moisture content of concrete (higher moisture → higher velocity).
3. Presence of steel reinforcement along the wave path (increases velocity to ~5.9 km/s).
4. Water-cement ratio, age of concrete, aggregate type, and surface condition.

---

### 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the working principle, equipment, and procedure of the Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity test for concrete. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relation between pulse velocity, dynamic modulus of elasticity, density, and Poisson's ratio. A UPV test on a concrete cube gave a transit time of 35 µs through a 150 mm path. If $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³ and $\nu = 0.2$, determine the dynamic modulus of elasticity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle, Equipment & Procedure:**

**Principle:** UPV test is based on the fact that the velocity of an ultrasonic pulse through a material depends on its **elastic properties and density**. A defect such as a crack, void, or honeycomb increases the transit time, thereby reducing the apparent velocity.

**Equipment (as per IS 13311):**
1. **Ultrasonic Pulse Generator** – produces electrical pulses at 20–150 kHz.
2. **Two Piezoelectric Transducers** – one transmitter and one receiver.
3. **Amplifier & Time Counter** – amplifies received signal and measures transit time.
4. **Display Unit** – shows the velocity directly in km/s.
5. **Coupling Agent** – grease, petroleum jelly, or soap paste (to eliminate air gap).

**Procedure:**
1. Identify a representative test location on the concrete element.
2. Smooth the surface and mark the transducer positions accurately.
3. Measure the path length **L** between the two contact points.
4. Apply a thin layer of coupling medium on both transducer faces.
5. Place the transmitter and receiver at the marked points; apply firm pressure.
6. Activate the pulser and note the displayed velocity.
7. Repeat at multiple grid points (typically 300 mm grid) to assess uniformity.
8. Compute the mean velocity and interpret as per the quality grading chart.

**Interpretation of Results:** Velocity > 4.5 km/s = Excellent, 3.5–4.5 = Good, 3.0–3.5 = Medium, < 3.0 = Doubtful.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical:**

**Derivation:** Already derived in Section 3.1. Final relation:

$$ E_d = \frac{\rho \cdot V^2 (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)} $$

**Numerical:**
- Path length $L = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- Transit time $T = 35 \, \mu s = 35 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³, $\nu = 0.2$

Step 1: Compute velocity:

$$ V = \frac{0.15}{35 \times 10^{-6}} = 4285.71 \, m/s \approx 4.286 \, km/s $$

Step 2: Apply the dynamic modulus formula:

$$ E_d = \frac{2400 \times (4285.71)^2 \times (1.2)(0.6)}{(0.8)} $$

$$ E_d = \frac{2400 \times 1.8367 \times 10^7 \times 0.72}{0.8} $$

$$ E_d = \frac{3.173 \times 10^{10}}{0.8} = 3.966 \times 10^{10} \, N/m^2 $$

$$ \boxed{E_d \approx 39.66 \, GPa \approx 39660 \, N/mm^2} $$

This is a typical value for good-quality M30 grade concrete.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Discuss the various factors affecting the ultrasonic pulse velocity in concrete. Explain how the UPV test can be used to assess uniformity and detect defects. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A concrete slab of thickness 200 mm is tested by indirect (surface) transmission method. The transit time is 50 µs, and the distance between transmitter and receiver is 300 mm. Compute the surface pulse velocity. If the surface is later ground smooth and direct transmission is performed, the transit time becomes 50 µs for the same 200 mm thickness. Compare both results and comment on the quality of concrete. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors Affecting UPV & Application for Uniformity/Defect Detection:**

**Factors affecting UPV:**
1. **Concrete properties** – strength, density, water-cement ratio, aggregate type.
2. **Moisture content** – wet concrete shows higher velocity than dry concrete.
3. **Temperature** – velocity decreases with temperature (up to ~60°C).
4. **Reinforcement** – if pulse path crosses steel bars, velocity rises to ~5.9 km/s.
5. **Cracks, voids, honeycombing** – drastically reduce velocity.
6. **Path length** – longer paths reduce edge effects and give more representative values.
7. **Coupling quality** – poor coupling introduces errors in transit time.
8. **Surface condition** – rough, carbonated, or contaminated surfaces give low readings in indirect transmission.

**Application – Uniformity Assessment:**
The element is divided into a **grid** (e.g., 300 mm × 300 mm). UPV is measured at each grid point. By drawing a **contour map (iso-velocity plot)** of equal velocity contours, areas of low velocity (indicating poor quality or voids) are identified. Uniformity is judged by the **spread of velocity values** – a small spread indicates uniform concrete.

**Application – Defect Detection:**
- **Honeycombing/voids:** Localized low-velocity zones.
- **Cracks:** Pulse bypasses the crack → increased travel time → low velocity.
- **Delamination in slabs:** Detected by surface transmission (low velocity zones).
- **Fire/chemical damage:** Uniformly reduced velocity across affected area.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Indirect Transmission Calculation:**

In the indirect (surface) method, the path length is the **horizontal distance** between the two transducers on the same face.

$$ V_{indirect} = \frac{L_{indirect}}{T} = \frac{0.300}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 6000 \, m/s = 6.0 \, km/s $$

**Direct Transmission Calculation:**

In the direct method, the path length is the **thickness** of the slab (200 mm).

$$ V_{direct} = \frac{L_{direct}}{T} = \frac{0.200}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 4000 \, m/s = 4.0 \, km/s $$

**Comparison and Comment:**

| Method | Path Length | Velocity (km/s) | Quality Grade |
|--------|-------------|------------------|----------------|
| Indirect | 300 mm | 6.0 | Excellent (but overestimated) |
| Direct | 200 mm | 4.0 | Good |

**Comment:** The indirect transmission **overestimates** the pulse velocity because the surface layer of concrete is generally denser, better cured, and less porous than the bulk interior. Therefore, the **direct transmission result (4.0 km/s)** is more reliable and indicates **good quality concrete** as per IS 13311. The indirect result, though appearing excellent, is not representative of the bulk quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (CRITICAL):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Many students forget to convert **µs to seconds** and **mm to km**. The final velocity MUST be in **km/s** for IS 13311 grading. Marks are lost if units are inconsistent.
> 2. **Path Length Error:** In indirect transmission, the path length is **NOT** the thickness; it is the **horizontal separation** between transducers. Conflating the two is a common error.
> 3. **Formula Confusion:** Students often write the simplified $E_d = \rho V^2$ and skip showing the full derivation. For full marks in KTU, **both forms** must be known and the appropriate one applied.
> 4. **No Units on Final Answer:** Always append **km/s** to velocity and **GPa or N/mm²** to modulus. Bare numerical answers lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **Governing Code:** IS 13311 (Part 1) : 1992 for UPV testing of concrete.
- [x] **Core Equation:** $V = L/T$ → must be in **km/s**.
- [x] **Dynamic Modulus Formula:** $E_d = \rho V^2 \cdot \dfrac{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1-\nu)}$.
- [x] **Simplified Form** (for $\nu = 0.2$): $E_d \approx \rho V^2$.
- [x] **Quality Grading (km/s):** >4.5 Excellent | 3.5–4.5 Good | 3.0–3.5 Medium | <3.0 Doubtful.
- [x] **Transducer Configurations:** Direct > Semi-direct > Indirect (in accuracy).
- [x] **Standard Frequency Range:** 20–150 kHz; 50–60 kHz most common.
- [x] **Pulse velocity in steel** ≈ **5.9 km/s** (affects readings near rebars).
- [x] **Coupling medium** (grease/petroleum jelly) is **mandatory** to avoid air gaps.
- [x] **UPV applications:** Uniformity check, void/honeycomb detection, crack detection, fire damage assessment, in-situ strength estimation (with co-relation), and durability monitoring.
- [x] **Limitations:** Surface transmission overestimates; presence of steel skews readings; requires experienced operator; no direct strength measurement (only indirect correlation).
- [x] **Effect of moisture:** Wet concrete → higher velocity (conversely, dry lab cubes show lower velocity than in-situ wet concrete).
- [x] **Effect of reinforcement:** Transducers should ideally be placed such that the pulse path **does not align with rebars**, or results must be interpreted carefully.
- [x] **Grid Pattern:** Standard test spacing is 300 mm grid for slabs; multiple readings averaged for reliability.

> **End of Module 2 Notes – Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi Destructive Testing of Structures: Probe Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semi Destructive Testing of Structures: Probe Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBE TEST (WINDSOR PROBE TEST) — KTU Official Definition**
> The **Probe Test** is a **semi-destructive in-situ testing method** used to estimate the **compressive strength of hardened concrete** by measuring the depth of penetration of a high-velocity, hardened steel alloy probe that is driven into the concrete surface using a precisely standardized powder charge (a calibrated gunpowder cartridge). The exposed length of the probe is measured, and the depth of penetration is correlated with compressive strength through manufacturer-supplied calibration charts.

The test is standardized under:
- **ASTM C803 / C803M** — Standard Test Method for Penetration Resistance of Hardened Concrete
- **IS 8142 : 1976 (Reaffirmed 2007)** — Method of Test for Determining Setting Time of Concrete by Penetration Resistance (analogous penetration principle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Semi-Destructive"?**
> The test is termed *semi-destructive* because, although it causes localized surface damage (a small conical spall and an embedded steel probe), the damage is minimal, repairable with a mortar patch, and does **not** impair the structural integrity of the parent member. It is positioned between purely non-destructive tests (Rebound Hammer, UPV) and fully destructive tests (Core Test, Load Test).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Nail-and-Hammer" Test for Concrete**
> Imagine you want to know how hard a piece of fruit is. A simple way is to push a thin nail into it with a fixed force and see how deep it goes — a ripe mango allows the nail to sink easily, while an unripe one resists. The **Probe Test works on exactly the same principle**:
> - The **"fixed force"** is supplied by a standardized gunpowder cartridge (the same chemistry used in a nail gun).
> - The **"nail"** is a hardened, alloy-steel probe tipped with a conical point.
> - The **"fruit"** is the concrete surface.
> - The **"depth the nail sinks"** is the **depth of penetration** — and the *shorter* the exposed length sticking out, the *deeper* it went in, meaning the concrete is **softer/weaker**.
> - Conversely, the *longer* the exposed length, the *shallower* the penetration, meaning the concrete is **harder/stronger**.

This direct physical measurement of resistance is the conceptual heart of the Probe Test.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Hardware & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Probe length (overall)** | **70.5** | mm |
| **Probe diameter (shank)** | **9.5** | mm |
| **Probe tip angle** | **45°** (conical) | degrees |
| **Probe material** | Hardened alloy steel (Rockwell C ~ 60) | — |
| **Cartridge power levels** | **3 standard levels** (e.g., 1, 2, 3 — "Green", "Yellow", "Red") | — |
| **Minimum number of probes per test location** | **3** (typically 3 to 5) | nos. |
| **Spacing between probes** | Minimum **150 mm** (≈ 6 inches) | mm |
| **Edge distance** | Minimum **100 mm** | mm |
| **Calibration strength range** | ~ **5 to 70 MPa** (≈ 700 to 10,000 psi) | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Power Levels of Cartridges** allow the operator to select the right energy for the expected concrete strength, ensuring the probe neither "bounces off" very strong concrete nor penetrates so deeply that the exposed length becomes unmeasurable on weak concrete.

---

### 1.4 Position of Probe Test in the Family of Concrete Testing

```
CLASSIFICATION OF IN-SITU CONCRETE STRENGTH TESTS
├── 1. NON-DESTRUCTIVE TESTS (NDT) — Zero damage
│      ├── Rebound Hammer Test (IS 13311-Pt 1 / ASTM C805)
│      ├── Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (IS 13311-Pt 2 / ASTM C597)
│      └── Surface Hardness Methods
│
├── 2. SEMI-DESTRUCTIVE TESTS (SDT) — Localized, minor damage
│      ├── ★ PROBE TEST (Windsor Probe) ★   ← CURRENT TOPIC
│      ├── Pull-Out Test (IS 11393 / ASTM C900)
│      ├── Pull-Off Test
│      └── Break-Off Test
│
└── 3. FULLY DESTRUCTIVE TESTS — Major damage, samples extracted
       ├── Core Test (IS 516 / ASTM C42) — Gold standard
       └── Load Test
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — The "Why"

The Windsor Probe Test is governed by the fundamental physics of **dynamic penetration resistance** of a rigid body into a visco-elastic-plastic medium (hardened concrete). The kinetic energy of the probe, supplied by the propellant charge, is dissipated in three primary modes:

1. **Plastic deformation work** — the work done in crushing and displacing the concrete matrix to form the penetration crater.
2. **Friction along the probe shank** — the work done against the radial confining pressure of the concrete acting on the cylindrical shaft.
3. **Elastic wave dissipation** — the small fraction radiated as stress waves and heat.

A higher-strength concrete possesses a **higher modulus of elasticity**, **higher compressive strength**, and **greater fracture toughness**. All three properties jointly increase the resistance to penetration, so the probe **penetrates less deeply**.

### 2.2 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Theory

The test is executed in a strict, sequential protocol. Each step has a definitive purpose in the KTU examination context:

**Step 1 — Site Preparation**
- The concrete surface is **smoothened** using a grinder or carborundum stone to remove laitance, carbonation layer, and surface irregularities.
- A flat, perpendicular surface is essential because the probe must strike the surface **normally (90°)** to obtain a valid penetration depth.

**Step 2 — Probe Selection & Loading**
- A **calibrated alloy-steel probe** is inserted into the probe-driving device (a hand-held gun).
- A **powder cartridge of appropriate power level** is loaded behind the probe.
- Power is chosen based on the *expected* strength:
  - **Low power** → High-strength concrete
  - **High power** → Low-strength concrete

**Step 3 — Firing the Probe**
- The gun is placed firmly and perpendicular to the surface.
- The trigger fires the cartridge; the expanding gases accelerate the probe to high velocity (typically > **200 m/s**).
- The probe impacts and embeds itself in the concrete.

**Step 4 — Measurement of Exposed Length**
- After firing, the **length of the probe sticking out of the surface (L<sub>exposed</sub>)** is measured using a **depth gauge** (vernier caliper or special Windsor gauge with an accuracy of **± 0.5 mm**).
- A **template** is used to measure from the concrete surface plane, NOT from the irregular crater rim.

**Step 5 — Calculation of Penetration Depth**

$$L_{penetration} = L_{probe} - L_{exposed}$$

> Where **L<sub>probe</sub>** is the total original length of the probe (= **70.5 mm**).

**Step 6 — Strength Determination via Calibration Curves**
- The penetration depth value is entered into the **manufacturer's calibration chart** corresponding to the power level used.
- The chart yields the **estimated compressive strength (f<sub>ck,est</sub>)** in MPa or psi.

**Step 7 — Statistical Treatment**
- Per **ASTM C803**, a test location consists of **a minimum of 3 probes**.
- The **median** of the three exposed-length readings is used to enter the calibration curve (NOT the mean — the median is robust to outliers caused by hitting rebar or aggregate).
- The test is **invalid** if any probe strikes embedded reinforcement or a large aggregate void; in such cases, the probe is rejected and a fresh probe is fired at a location at least **150 mm** away.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Probe Test does not have a single, universally derived theoretical formula. Instead, **empirical calibration curves** are used. The only direct calculation is the penetration depth, but several derived parameters appear in KTU questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relationship | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Penetration Depth** | $L_{p} = L_{probe} - L_{exposed}$ | mm | Core calculation |
| 2 | **Mean Exposed Length** | $\bar{L}_{ex} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{ex,i}$ | mm | Used when n probes pass validity |
| 3 | **Median Exposed Length** | $\tilde{L}_{ex} = \text{median}(L_{ex,1}, L_{ex,2}, \ldots, L_{ex,n})$ | mm | **Preferred per ASTM C803** for n = 3 |
| 4 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma_{L} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (L_{i} - \bar{L})^{2}}{n-1}}$ | mm | For variability check |
| 5 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $CoV = \dfrac{\sigma_{L}}{\bar{L}} \times 100$ | % | Should be < 10 % for uniform concrete |
| 6 | **Strength–Penetration Relation (empirical, linearized form)** | $f_{c} = a - b \cdot L_{p}$ | MPa | a, b are constants from calibration |
| 7 | **In-situ Strength Estimate** | $f_{c,is} = f_{calib}(L_{p}, \text{power level})$ | MPa | From manufacturer's chart |
| 8 | **Kinetic Energy of Probe (theoretical basis)** | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{p}^{2}$ | J | m<sub>p</sub> = probe mass; v<sub>p</sub> = muzzle velocity |
| 9 | **Hertzian Crushing Resistance (conceptual)** | $P \propto f_{c}^{\,3/2} \cdot d^{2}$ | N | d = probe diameter; used to explain non-linear behaviour |
| 10 | **Minimum probes per test area** | $n \geq 3$ | — | ASTM C803 specification |
| 11 | **Minimum spacing** | $s_{min} = 150$ | mm | To avoid stress-field overlap |
| 12 | **Edge clearance** | $c_{min} = 100$ | mm | To avoid spalling at edges |

> [!WARNING]
> **ABSOLUTE-VALUE FORMATTING IN TABLES:** In the table above, where absolute values are needed (e.g., $L_{p} = \vert L_{probe} - L_{exposed} \vert$), the **\vert** LaTeX command is used. **Never use the vertical pipe symbol \vert inside markdown table cells** — it breaks the table parser. Always use \vert or \mid.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Strength Audit of Old Structures** — used to verify whether an existing column, beam, or slab in an aged building still meets its design strength, especially before sanctioning additional floors.
2. **Form-Stripping and Post-Pouring Decisions** — the rebound hammer and UPV only measure *surface* properties, whereas the probe penetrates **20 to 40 mm deep**, giving a more *bulk* strength estimate.
3. **Comparative Quality Control** — comparing zones of suspected honeycombing or poor compaction with sound zones on the same member.
4. **Forensic Investigation** — failure investigations in collapse cases where multiple zones need quick, on-site strength estimates without extensive coring.
5. **Complement to Core Testing** — where cores are impractical (e.g., very thin members, high-rebar density), the probe test gives a faster first-pass estimate.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Because the probe test is *semi-destructive*, it must be **followed by a patch repair** using polymer-modified mortar or epoxy putty to seal the probe hole and prevent corrosion ingress at the spall.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Penetration Depth from Exposed Length

This is the most fundamental numerical step that KTU examiners love to test. We must show the conversion logic in full.

**Problem Context (KTU-Style):**
A Windsor probe of total length **70.5 mm** is fired into a concrete surface. After firing, the exposed length of the probe above the concrete surface is measured as **L<sub>exposed</sub>** mm. Derive the expression for the **depth of penetration** in terms of L<sub>exposed</sub>.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let:} \quad & L_{total} = \text{Total length of probe before firing} = 70.5 \text{ mm} \\
                   & L_{exp}  = \text{Exposed length of probe after firing (mm)} \\
                   & L_{pen}  = \text{Depth of penetration into concrete (mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **principle of conservation of length** (the probe is rigid and inextensible):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{pen} + L_{exp} \\
\Rightarrow L_{pen} &= L_{total} - L_{exp}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $L_{total} = 70.5$ mm:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{pen} \;=\; 70.5 \;-\; L_{exp} \quad \text{(in mm)}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A *shorter* exposed length ⇒ *larger* penetration ⇒ *weaker* concrete (lower f<sub>c</sub>). This inverse logic is the **conceptual heart** of the test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (Single Probe)

**Given:** $L_{total} = 70.5$ mm, $L_{exp} = 45.3$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{pen} &= L_{total} - L_{exp} \\
L_{pen} &= 70.5 - 45.3 \\
L_{pen} &= 25.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Statement:** The probe penetrated **25.2 mm** into the concrete. From the manufacturer's calibration curve (e.g., Power Level 2 — Yellow cartridge), a penetration of 25.2 mm corresponds to an estimated compressive strength of approximately **30 MPa**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The numerical subtraction is exact; the strength value is read from a **graph** (not computed analytically). KTU students must explain the **direction of correlation** clearly: *higher penetration ⇒ lower strength*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Statistical Treatment for Multiple Probes (ASTM Method)

**Scenario:** Three probes are fired in a test location. Exposed lengths are:
- $L_1 = 44.8$ mm
- $L_2 = 45.2$ mm
- $L_3 = 45.0$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute the penetration depth of each probe:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{pen,1} &= 70.5 - 44.8 = 25.7 \text{ mm} \\
L_{pen,2} &= 70.5 - 45.2 = 25.3 \text{ mm} \\
L_{pen,3} &= 70.5 - 45.0 = 25.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean penetration depth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L}_{pen} &= \frac{L_{pen,1} + L_{pen,2} + L_{pen,3}}{3} \\
\bar{L}_{pen} &= \frac{25.7 + 25.3 + 25.5}{3} \\
\bar{L}_{pen} &= \frac{76.5}{3} \\
\boxed{\bar{L}_{pen} = 25.5 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the median (for cross-check, ASTM C803 recommends the median for n = 3):**

Arranging in ascending order: $L_{pen,2} = 25.3$, $L_{pen,3} = 25.5$, $L_{pen,1} = 25.7$

$$
\boxed{\tilde{L}_{pen} = 25.5 \text{ mm} \;\;(\text{median, matches the mean})}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sample standard deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(L_{pen,i} - \bar{L}_{pen})^{2}}{n - 1}} \\
\sum (L_i - \bar{L})^{2} &= (25.7 - 25.5)^{2} + (25.3 - 25.5)^{2} + (25.5 - 25.5)^{2} \\
&= (0.2)^{2} + (-0.2)^{2} + (0)^{2} \\
&= 0.04 + 0.04 + 0 = 0.08 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{0.08}{3 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.04} \\
\boxed{\sigma = 0.2 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Coefficient of Variation (CoV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoV &= \frac{\sigma}{\bar{L}_{pen}} \times 100 \\
CoV &= \frac{0.2}{25.5} \times 100 \\
\boxed{CoV \approx 0.78\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validity Check:** A CoV of 0.78 % is well below the typical threshold of **10 %**, confirming that the three readings are highly consistent and the concrete is uniform at this location.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy-Based Conceptual Derivation of the Strength–Penetration Relationship

This is an **advanced, conceptual derivation** frequently asked in 14-mark questions to test a student's *understanding* of the physics, not just procedure.

**Setting up the energy balance:**

The kinetic energy (KE) of the probe at impact is dissipated primarily in plastic deformation of the concrete crater.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Kinetic energy of the probe at the instant of impact} \quad & KE = \frac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{p}^{2} \\
\text{Step 2: Work done in forming the crater} \quad & W_{crater} = f_{c} \cdot V_{crater} \\
\text{Step 3: Volume of the conical crater (half-angle 22.5°)} \quad & V_{crater} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} L_{pen} \\
\text{Step 4: For a given probe tip geometry, } r \propto L_{pen}, \text{ so} \quad & V_{crater} = k \cdot L_{pen}^{3} \\
\text{Step 5: Equating KE to plastic work (energy balance)} \quad & \frac{1}{2} m_{p} v_{p}^{2} = f_{c} \cdot k \cdot L_{pen}^{3} \\
\text{Step 6: Solving for strength} \quad & f_{c} = \frac{m_{p} v_{p}^{2}}{2 k L_{pen}^{3}} \\
\text{Step 7: Linearizing for calibration purposes} \quad & f_{c} = a - b L_{pen}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **exact** relationship is a **cubic inverse** (Step 6), but the manufacturer's calibration chart is **linearized** (Step 7) over the practical range of 5 to 70 MPa to simplify field use.

$$
\boxed{\,f_{c} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{L_{pen}^{3}} \quad\text{(theoretical, cubic inverse); linearized as}\quad f_{c} = a - bL_{pen}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cubic?** Because the *volume* of the crushed concrete scales as the cube of the penetration depth, and the energy required to crush it scales with that volume times the strength.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Test Parameters (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Test Parameter | NDT (Rebound Hammer) | **SDT (Probe Test)** | Destructive (Core Test) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Property measured** | Surface hardness | Bulk penetration resistance | Direct compressive strength |
| **Depth of investigation** | ~ **10–15 mm** | ~ **20–40 mm** | Full depth of core |
| **Damage to structure** | Negligible | Minor (small crater + probe) | Significant (core hole) |
| **Accuracy** | ± **25 %** | ± **15–20 %** | ± **5–10 %** (Gold standard) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (drilling, capping, UTM) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest |
| **Equipment cost** | ~ ₹ 15,000 | ~ ₹ 1,50,000 | ~ ₹ 5,00,000+ |
| **Standard** | IS 13311 (Pt 1) | **ASTM C803 / IS 8142** | IS 516 / ASTM C42 |
| **Effect of surface carbonation** | **High** (skewed high) | **Moderate** | None |
| **Effect of rebar** | None (small footprint) | **Severe** (rejects reading) | None (visible) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "Why use the probe test over the rebound hammer?" the correct answer must emphasize **depth of investigation** and **resistance to surface carbonation effects** — these are the two technical differentiators that examiners reward.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Complete Probe Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Site Investigation] --> B[Identify Test Location on Concrete Member]
    B --> C[Grind Surface to Expose Sound Concrete]
    C --> D[Mark Three Probe Positions Min 150 mm Apart]
    D --> E[Select Power Level of Cartridge Based on Expected Strength]
    E --> F[Load Probe into Windsor Probe Gun]
    F --> G[Place Gun Perpendicular to Concrete Surface]
    G --> H[Fire Cartridge Probe Penetrates Concrete]
    H --> I{Probe Valid?}
    I -- Hit Rebar or Void --> J[Reject and Relocate Min 150 mm Away]
    J --> F
    I -- Valid Embedment --> K[Measure Exposed Length Using Depth Gauge]
    K --> L[Calculate Penetration Depth Lp equals 70.5 minus Lexp]
    L --> M[Record All Three Readings]
    M --> N{Three or More Probes Done?}
    N -- No --> F
    N -- Yes --> O[Compute Median Exposed Length]
    O --> P[Read Manufacturer Calibration Curve for Selected Power Level]
    P --> Q[Obtain Estimated In-Situ Compressive Strength fckest]
    Q --> R[Validate CoV less than 10 Percent]
    R --> S[Seal Probe Holes with Polymer Modified Mortar]
    S --> T[Document Test Report with Location Map and Strength Data]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Equipment & Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Source Subsystem]
        P1[Cartridge Power Level 1 Green High Strength]
        P2[Cartridge Power Level 2 Yellow Medium Strength]
        P3[Cartridge Power Level 3 Red Low Strength]
    end

    subgraph GUN[Probe Driving Subsystem]
        G1[Windsor Probe Gun Body]
        G2[Trigger and Safety Mechanism]
        G3[Barrel with Probe Holder]
    end

    subgraph PROBE[Probe Subsystem]
        PR1[Hardened Alloy Steel Probe 70.5 mm]
        PR2[Conical Tip 45 Degrees]
        PR3[Cylindrical Shank 9.5 mm Diameter]
    end

    subgraph TARGET[Concrete Target]
        T1[Prepared Flat Surface]
        T2[Crater Formation Zone 20 to 40 mm Deep]
        T3[Embedded Probe After Firing]
    end

    subgraph MEASURE[Measurement Subsystem]
        M1[Depth Gauge Vernier Caliber]
        M2[Template for Surface Reference]
        M3[Calibration Chart Lookup Table]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Strength Output Subsystem]
        O1[Exposed Length Lexp in mm]
        O2[Penetration Depth Lp in mm]
        O3[Compressive Strength fckest in MPa]
    end

    P1 --> G1
    P2 --> G1
    P3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR1 --> PR3
    PR1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> M3
    M3 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Schematic of Penetration Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Concrete Surface Original Plane] --> S2[Crater Lip Raised by Plastic Flow]
    S2 --> S3[Probe Tip Conical at Depth Lp]
    S3 --> S4[Probe Shank Cylindrical in Concrete]
    S4 --> S5[Probe Tail Exposed Above Surface Lexp]
    S1 -.->|Lexp above| S5
    S3 -.->|Lp below| S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The diagram is a block-functional representation of the post-firing cross-section. The exposed length (above the original surface) and the penetration depth (below the original surface) sum to the **total probe length of 70.5 mm**.

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Validity Assessment of Test Result

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Three Probes] --> B{Each Probe Valid?}
    B -- No Hit Rebar --> C[Reject That Reading]
    B -- No Probe Broke --> C
    B -- No Concrete Spalled --> C
    C --> D[Refire at New Location]
    B -- Yes --> E[Compute Median Exposed Length]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Look up Calibration Curve]
    F --> G{Strength Reasonable?}
    G -- No Outlier --> H[Reject Test Repeat Location]
    G -- Yes --> I[Accept fckest as Valid]
    I --> J[Patch Probe Holes with Mortar]
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the Probe Test. Why is it classified as a semi-destructive test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Probe Test** (also known as the **Windsor Probe Test**) is an in-situ, semi-destructive method for estimating the **compressive strength of hardened concrete** by firing a calibrated, hardened-alloy-steel probe into the concrete surface using a powder-actuated gun and measuring the **depth of penetration** of the probe, which is inversely correlated to the concrete's compressive strength via manufacturer-supplied calibration curves. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> It is classified as **semi-destructive** because the test causes *localized, minor, repairable damage* — namely, a small conical spall on the surface and an embedded steel probe — but does **not** impair the structural integrity, serviceability, or load-bearing capacity of the parent concrete member. The damage can be easily patched with mortar. It sits between non-destructive tests (Rebound Hammer, UPV) and fully destructive tests (Core Cutting). **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any four advantages of the Probe Test over the Rebound Hammer Test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Advantage of Probe Test | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Greater depth of investigation** | Probe penetrates 20–40 mm into concrete, measuring *bulk* strength, while rebound hammer measures only 10–15 mm of *surface* hardness. **(0.75 marks)** |
| 2 | **Less affected by surface carbonation** | Carbonation artificially hardens the top few mm and inflates rebound readings, but the probe's depth bypasses this effect. **(0.75 marks)** |
| 3 | **Direct physical measurement** | Penetration depth is a *direct* length measurement, not a relative/indirect reading. **(0.75 marks)** |
| 4 | **Suitable for high-strength concrete** | At very high strengths, rebound hammer readings saturate (ceiling effect), whereas the probe test remains discriminative. **(0.75 marks)** |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, students answer **one of two** internal-choice questions. Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle and test procedure** of the Windsor Probe Test. Enumerate the **apparatus required** and the **precautions to be observed** during testing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a Windsor Probe Test on an existing RCC column, the following data were obtained at a test location using **3 probes** of total length **70.5 mm**:
- Probe 1: Exposed length = **42.0 mm**
- Probe 2: Exposed length = **44.5 mm**
- Probe 3: Exposed length = **45.0 mm**

Using the **manufacturer's calibration curve** (provided separately), the corresponding compressive strengths are:
- $f_1 = 32.0$ MPa
- $f_2 = 28.5$ MPa
- $f_3 = 27.5$ MPa

**Calculate:**
(i) The penetration depth for each probe.
(ii) The mean and median compressive strength.
(iii) The standard deviation and coefficient of variation (CoV).
(iv) Comment on the **uniformity** of the concrete at this location. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A**

### **Part (a) — Principle, Procedure, Apparatus, Precautions (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:** The probe test is based on the principle of **dynamic penetration resistance**. A standard alloy-steel probe (tip angle 45°, length 70.5 mm, diameter 9.5 mm) is driven into the concrete surface by a controlled powder charge. The **depth of penetration is inversely related to the compressive strength** of the concrete. A stronger concrete offers greater resistance, so the probe penetrates less deeply and more length remains exposed. **(1.5 marks)**

**Apparatus Required (List):**
1. Windsor Probe Gun (powder-actuated driving tool).
2. Calibrated alloy-steel probes (length 70.5 mm, tip 45°).
3. Powder cartridges in three power levels.
4. Depth-measuring gauge (vernier caliper or Windsor gauge, accuracy ± 0.5 mm).
5. Carborundum stone / angle grinder for surface preparation.
6. Template for surface-plane reference.
7. Manufacturer's calibration charts. **(1.5 marks)**

**Test Procedure (Sequence):**
1. **Locate** the test region; avoid reinforcement using a cover meter or rebar locator.
2. **Grind** the surface to remove carbonation, laitance, and unevenness.
3. **Insert** the probe into the gun barrel; load the appropriate power cartridge.
4. **Place** the gun perpendicular to the surface; fire.
5. **Measure** the exposed length of the probe with a depth gauge using the template to reference the original surface plane.
6. **Repeat** for a minimum of 3 probes, spaced at least 150 mm apart.
7. **Compute** the median exposed length and read the calibration curve to obtain f<sub>ck,est</sub>.
8. **Patch** the probe holes with polymer-modified mortar. **(2.0 marks)**

**Precautions:**
- Maintain **perpendicularity** of the gun to the surface; an inclined shot gives a false, *longer* exposed length.
- Ensure **minimum 100 mm edge distance** and **150 mm spacing** between probes.
- **Reject** any probe that strikes rebar, large aggregate, or a void.
- Use the **median** (not the mean) for n = 3 readings.
- Apply **safety glasses** and ear protection — powder-actuated tools are hazardous. **(2.0 marks)**

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Penetration Depth for Each Probe (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{pen,1} &= 70.5 - 42.0 = 28.5 \text{ mm} \\
L_{pen,2} &= 70.5 - 44.5 = 26.0 \text{ mm} \\
L_{pen,3} &= 70.5 - 45.0 = 25.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mean and Median Compressive Strength (2.0 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f}_{c} &= \frac{32.0 + 28.5 + 27.5}{3} = \frac{88.0}{3} \\
\boxed{\bar{f}_{c} \approx 29.33 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Arranging strengths in ascending order: 27.5, 28.5, 32.0

$$
\boxed{\tilde{f}_{c} = 28.5 \text{ MPa (median)}}
$$

**(iii) Standard Deviation and Coefficient of Variation (2.0 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (f_i - \bar{f}_c)^{2} &= (32.0 - 29.33)^{2} + (28.5 - 29.33)^{2} + (27.5 - 29.33)^{2} \\
&= (2.67)^{2} + (-0.83)^{2} + (-1.83)^{2} \\
&= 7.13 + 0.69 + 3.35 = 11.17 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{11.17}{3 - 1}} = \sqrt{5.585} \\
\boxed{\sigma \approx 2.36 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoV &= \frac{\sigma}{\bar{f}_{c}} \times 100 = \frac{2.36}{29.33} \times 100 \\
\boxed{CoV \approx 8.05\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment on Uniformity (1.5 marks):**

> The CoV of **8.05 %** is **less than 10 %**, which is the standard threshold for uniform in-situ concrete. Hence, the concrete at this test location is **reasonably uniform**. However, the individual strength values range from 27.5 to 32.0 MPa (a spread of 4.5 MPa), and the **median (28.5 MPa)** is recommended for design verification per ASTM C803. The estimated in-situ characteristic strength is approximately **28.5 MPa**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the **factors influencing the results of the Probe Test**. How does the probe test **compare** with the Rebound Hammer and Core Test in terms of accuracy, depth of investigation, and applicability? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a probe test investigation on a 30-year-old bridge deck, a test location gave the following penetration depths using **Power Level 2 (Yellow)** cartridge: **L<sub>pen</sub> = 24.8, 25.5, 26.0, 24.2, 25.0 mm** (5 valid probes). The corresponding calibration curve equation for Power Level 2 is:

$$
f_{c} = 75.0 - 1.8 \cdot L_{pen} \quad \text{(in MPa, with } L_{pen} \text{ in mm)}
$$

**Compute:**
(i) The compressive strength corresponding to each penetration depth.
(ii) The mean, standard deviation, and CoV of the strength estimates.
(iii) If the **original design strength** was **M30**, decide whether the bridge deck concrete is **acceptable** as per IS 456 tolerances. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B**

### **Part (a) — Influencing Factors and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Factors Influencing Probe Test Results:**

| # | Factor | Effect | How to Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Concrete strength** | Stronger ⇒ shallower penetration (primary variable) | Choose correct power level |
| 2 | **Aggregate type and size** | Hard aggregate (e.g., granite) resists more than soft (e.g., limestone); large aggregate deflects probe | Use multiple probes; reject outliers |
| 3 | **Moisture content** | Saturated concrete gives lower strength estimate (effective stress reduced) | Allow surface drying; record condition |
| 4 | **Surface preparation** | Rough or carbonated surface scatters energy, gives false high strength | Grind to flat, sound surface |
| 5 | **Cartridge power level** | Higher power ⇒ deeper penetration | Match to expected strength |
| 6 | **Probe-perpendicularity** | Inclined shot ⇒ longer exposed length ⇒ apparent higher strength | Use a guide/template |
| 7 | **Rebar or voids** | Rebar arrests probe (false high strength); void gives false low strength | Use cover meter; reject invalid shots |
| 8 | **Age of concrete** | Mature concrete ⇒ higher strength (curing effect) | Compare relative changes | **(3.5 marks)**

**Comparative Table (vs. Rebound Hammer & Core Test):**

| Aspect | Rebound Hammer (NDT) | **Probe Test (SDT)** | Core Test (Destructive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depth of investigation | 10–15 mm | **20–40 mm** | Full core length |
| Accuracy | ± 25 % | **± 15–20 %** | ± 5–10 % |
| Damage to structure | None | Minor (repairable) | Significant (core hole) |
| Cost per test | Very low | Moderate | High |
| Susceptibility to surface carbonation | High (skewed high) | Moderate | None |
| Standard | IS 13311 Pt 1 | **ASTM C803 / IS 8142** | IS 516 / ASTM C42 |
| Best use | Rapid screening | **In-depth bulk strength check** | Final strength verification | **(3.5 marks)**

> The probe test is best understood as the **middle ground**: more representative than the rebound hammer, but less invasive and faster than coring.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** Calibration equation $f_{c} = 75.0 - 1.8 L_{pen}$ (MPa, $L_{pen}$ in mm)
Penetration depths: 24.8, 25.5, 26.0, 24.2, 25.0 mm.

**(i) Compressive Strength for Each Probe (2.0 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{1} &= 75.0 - 1.8 \times 24.8 = 75.0 - 44.64 = 30.36 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{2} &= 75.0 - 1.8 \times 25.5 = 75.0 - 45.90 = 29.10 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{3} &= 75.0 - 1.8 \times 26.0 = 75.0 - 46.80 = 28.20 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{4} &= 75.0 - 1.8 \times 24.2 = 75.0 - 43.56 = 31.44 \text{ MPa} \\
f_{5} &= 75.0 - 1.8 \times 25.0 = 75.0 - 45.00 = 30.00 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mean, Standard Deviation, CoV (3.0 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f}_{c} &= \frac{30.36 + 29.10 + 28.20 + 31.44 + 30.00}{5} \\
            &= \frac{149.10}{5} \\
\boxed{\bar{f}_{c} = 29.82 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (f_i - \bar{f}_{c})^{2} &= (30.36 - 29.82)^{2} + (29.10 - 29.82)^{2} \\
&\quad + (28.20 - 29.82)^{2} + (31.44 - 29.82)^{2} \\
&\quad + (30.00 - 29.82)^{2} \\
&= (0.54)^{2} + (-0.72)^{2} + (-1.62)^{2} + (1.62)^{2} + (0.18)^{2} \\
&= 0.292 + 0.518 + 2.624 + 2.624 + 0.032 \\
&= 6.090
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{6.090}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{1.5225} \\
\boxed{\sigma \approx 1.234 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoV &= \frac{1.234}{29.82} \times 100 \\
\boxed{CoV \approx 4.14\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Acceptability vs. M30 Design (2.0 marks):**

> The design mix is **M30** (target characteristic strength = **30 MPa** at 28 days). The estimated in-situ mean strength is **29.82 MPa**, which is *slightly below* the design 30 MPa. However, the bridge is **30 years old**, and concrete continues to gain strength well beyond 28 days (typically 1.2 to 1.5 × f<sub>ck,28</sub> at 1 year and beyond under normal curing). Combined with a **low CoV of 4.14 %** (indicating excellent uniformity), the deck concrete is **acceptable for continued service** in the short term. **However**, a **confirmatory core test** is recommended for final structural assessment, per **IS 456:2000 Clause 17.4** (assessment of strength).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Probe Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using mean instead of median (or vice versa, without specifying).** ASTM C803 mandates the **median** for n = 3. Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if you blindly use the mean without rationale.
> 2. **Inverting the correlation.** Students sometimes write "deeper penetration = stronger concrete." The correct direction is: *deeper penetration = WEAKER concrete.* This is a **1-mark conceptual blunder**.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 70.5 mm.** Always state the formula $L_p = 70.5 - L_{exp}$ clearly. Skipping this step costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing probe test with the "pull-out" test.** The pull-out test uses an embedded insert and a hydraulic ram; the probe test uses a powder-actuated gun. They are *different* tests.
> 5. **Ignoring the calibration curve's dependence on power level.** Each power level has its **own** calibration curve. A 26 mm penetration under Power Level 1 does NOT give the same strength as 26 mm under Power Level 3.
> 6. **Failing to mention patching/repair of probe holes.** Examiners reward the awareness that semi-destructive tests require post-test repair — a small but telling differentiator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — PROBE TEST**

- [ ] **Full name:** Windsor Probe Test (a.k.a. Probe Penetration Test).
- [ ] **Classification:** **Semi-Destructive Test (SDT)** of in-situ concrete strength.
- [ ] **Standards:** **ASTM C803 / C803M** (primary); **IS 8142** (related penetration method).
- [ ] **Probe dimensions:** Length = **70.5 mm**; Shank diameter = **9.5 mm**; Tip angle = **45°** cone.
- [ ] **Probe material:** Hardened alloy steel, Rockwell C ≈ 60.
- [ ] **Three cartridge power levels** — chosen based on expected concrete strength.
- [ ] **Core formula:** $L_{pen} = 70.5 - L_{exposed}$ (in mm).
- [ ] **Correlation direction:** *Higher* penetration ⇒ *Lower* strength (inverse).
- [ ] **Minimum probes per test location:** **3** (use the **median** for n = 3).
- [ ] **Minimum spacing:** **150 mm** between probes; **100 mm** from edge.
- [ ] **Rejection criteria:** Probe hits rebar, large aggregate, or void → **discard and re-fire**.
- [ ] **Depth of investigation:** **20 to 40 mm** (much deeper than rebound hammer's 10–15 mm).
- [ ] **Advantages over rebound hammer:** Bulk (not surface) measurement, less affected by carbonation, discriminative at high strength.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** Local damage, requires patching, more expensive, slower than rebound hammer.
- [ ] **Theoretical relation:** $f_{c} \propto 1/L_{pen}^{3}$ (cubic inverse from energy balance).
- [ ] **Linearized calibration:** $f_{c} = a - bL_{pen}$ (a, b are cartridge-specific constants).
- [ ] **Acceptance criterion:** CoV < **10 %** ⇒ uniform concrete at that location.
- [ ] **Post-test action:** Seal probe holes with **polymer-modified mortar** to prevent ingress.
- [ ] **Safety:** Use **eye and ear protection**; treat the gun as a live firearm.
- [ ] **Best complementary test:** **Core Test (IS 516)** for final verification.
- [ ] **Examiner's mantra:** Always mention **perpendicularity**, **median for n = 3**, and **patching**.

---

### 5.5 Key Takeaway

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Summary for Viva & Exams:**
> "The **Windsor Probe Test** is a **semi-destructive, depth-discriminating** in-situ strength test that fires a standardized 70.5 mm alloy-steel probe into concrete using a powder cartridge; the **depth of penetration** (calculated as 70.5 − exposed length) is **inversely correlated** with the **compressive strength** through a manufacturer-supplied calibration curve, with the **median of at least 3 valid readings** used for strength estimation."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pull Out Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pull Out Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-Out Test (Loosely Bonded Embedment Test)**: A semi-destructive *in-situ* strength assessment technique classified under **Partially Destructive Testing (PDT)** in which a standard steel disc (insert) previously embedded in fresh concrete is pulled out using a calibrated hydraulic reaction system, and the maximum tensile force required to extract a frustum of concrete attached to the disc is correlated empirically to the **compressive strength** of the parent concrete.

The **Pull-Out Test** is one of the most reliable *in-situ* methods to estimate the **compressive strength of concrete** directly without relying on rebound or ultrasonic correlation. It was standardized by **ASTM C900** and **BS 1881: Part 207**, and is widely adopted in KTU-endorsed rehabilitation audits for assessing residual strength prior to retrofitting decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine you plant a metal bolt into wet cement with a small disc at the bottom (like an inverted T-anchor). When the cement hardens, the bolt is held in place by the grip of the surrounding concrete. Now, if you try to pull the bolt out vertically, the disc must drag a cone-shaped chunk of concrete with it. The harder the concrete, the stronger the grip — and therefore, the more force you need to yank it out. That force is what we measure! It's like testing the strength of ice by trying to pull out an ice-cream scoop — harder ice resists more.

- **Standard Insert Diameter**: **25 mm** steel disc embedded at a depth of **25 mm**
- **Minimum Edge Distance**: **100 mm** to avoid boundary failure
- **Failure Geometry**: A truncated conical (frustum) concrete wedge with base angle approximately **45°**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Pull-Out Test operates on the principle that the **ultimate pull-out force (F)** is directly proportional to the **shear strength** of the concrete along the conical failure surface, which in turn is a function of the **uniaxial compressive strength (f_c)**.

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Embedment Phase**: A steel disc (insert) is cast into the fresh concrete at a predetermined depth (typically **25 mm** for standard inserts).
2. **Curing Phase**: Concrete cures and develops a bond (mechanical interlock + adhesion) with the insert and the embedded portion of the draw-bolt.
3. **Loading Phase**: A hydraulic ram applies a tensile force via a reaction ring (bearing against the concrete surface with a counter-pressure of ~**10 N/mm²**).
4. **Failure Phase**: A frustum of concrete (cone with truncated apex) is extracted along a shear surface inclined at roughly **45°** to the horizontal axis.
5. **Measurement Phase**: The peak force (F in **kN**) is recorded via a calibrated pressure gauge or load cell.
6. **Correlation Phase**: The measured F is plugged into an empirical regression equation (typically power-law) to derive **f_ck**.

### 2.2 Why This Test Works — The Engineering "Why"

- **Direct Mechanical Link**: Unlike rebound hammer (surface hardness) or UPV (wave velocity), pull-out engages a substantial volume (~**0.001 m³** to **0.002 m³**) of concrete in shear.
- **Cone Volume**: Approximately **0.0001 m³** to **0.0003 m³** of concrete is mobilized.
- **Stress State**: The conical failure surface approximates a state of combined shear and tension, which empirically tracks uniaxial compression.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are calibrated for **standard 25 mm disc, 25 mm embedment depth** as per **ASTM C900 / BS 1881-207**.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Empirical Relationship** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pull-out force | $F$ | Measured directly | $\text{kN}$ | Peak load before cone extraction |
| Compressive strength | $f_{ck}$ | $f_{ck} = a \cdot F + b$ | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) | Linear correlation (commonly used) |
| Compressive strength (power) | $f_{ck}$ | $f_{ck} = A \cdot F^{n}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Power-law (more accurate for wide range) |
| Typical coefficients (linear) | $a$, $b$ | $a \approx 1.4$ to $2.0$; $b \approx 0$ to $5$ | — | Calibration-dependent |
| Typical coefficients (power) | $A$, $n$ | $A \approx 1.30$; $n \approx 0.95$ to $1.10$ | — | Manufacturer-specific |
| Reaction pressure | $p_{r}$ | $\leq 10 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | To prevent local crushing at surface |
| Cone base angle | $\theta$ | $\approx 45^{\circ}$ | degrees | With respect to horizontal |
| Volume of test concrete | $V_{c}$ | $\approx \frac{\pi h}{3}(R^{2} + Rr + r^{2})$ | $\text{mm}^{3}$ | Frustum geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Field Utility**: Used in **strength evaluation prior to retrofitting**, **formwork stripping time assessment**, **load capacity verification of existing columns/beams**, and **acceptance testing** when cube test results are questionable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cone Frustum Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> The failure surface is approximated as a **frustum of a right circular cone**. Below is the geometric derivation of its volume, which determines the volume of concrete actually stressed during the test.

**Given:**
- Disc radius: $r = 12.5 \text{ mm}$ (for standard 25 mm diameter insert)
- Embedment depth (cone height): $h = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Cone half-angle: $\alpha \approx 45^{\circ}$ (assumption validated experimentally)

**Step 1 — Radius of the Cone Base (R):**

The base radius of the frustum is the radius of the insert plus the horizontal extent over height $h$.

$$
R = r + h \cdot \tan(\alpha)
$$

$$
R = 12.5 + 25 \cdot \tan(45^{\circ})
$$

$$
R = 12.5 + 25 \cdot 1 = 37.5 \text{ mm}
$$

*Explanation*: At a 45° angle, the horizontal projection equals the vertical rise.

**Step 2 — Volume of the Frustum:**

The standard formula for the volume of a frustum of a cone is:

$$
V_{c} = \frac{\pi \cdot h}{3}\left(R^{2} + R \cdot r + r^{2}\right)
$$

$$
V_{c} = \frac{\pi \cdot 25}{3}\left((37.5)^{2} + (37.5)(12.5) + (12.5)^{2}\right)
$$

$$
V_{c} = \frac{25\pi}{3}\left(1406.25 + 468.75 + 156.25\right)
$$

$$
V_{c} = \frac{25\pi}{3}(2031.25)
$$

$$
V_{c} = \frac{50781.25 \cdot \pi}{3} \approx 53167.5 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{c} \approx 5.32 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{3} = 53.17 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

*Explanation*: This is the volume of concrete mobilized during failure — typically **0.5% to 1%** of a standard 150 mm cube.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Empirical Strength Correlation

> [!NOTE]
> The correlation between pull-out force (F) and compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) is empirically derived from paired laboratory tests (cube compression + matched pull-out). A power-law form is shown below.

**General empirical form:**

$$
f_{ck} = A \cdot F^{n}
$$

**Step 1 — Linearization via Logarithm:**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides to linearize:

$$
\ln(f_{ck}) = \ln(A) + n \cdot \ln(F)
$$

*Explanation*: This transforms a non-linear power curve into a straight line in log-log space, enabling linear regression on paired test data.

**Step 2 — Least-Squares Regression (Conceptual):**

With N paired data points $(F_i, f_{c,i})$, the slope $n$ and intercept $\ln(A)$ are obtained:

$$
n = \frac{N \sum (\ln F_i \cdot \ln f_{c,i}) - (\sum \ln F_i)(\sum \ln f_{c,i})}{N \sum (\ln F_i)^{2} - (\sum \ln F_i)^{2}}
$$

$$
\ln(A) = \frac{\sum \ln f_{c,i} - n \cdot \sum \ln F_i}{N}
$$

*Explanation*: These are textbook linear regression coefficients for a log-transformed dataset; coefficients are obtained once per calibration and used universally for the same insert geometry.

**Step 3 — Typical Calibrated Coefficients (for standard 25 mm disc):**

$$
A \approx 1.30, \quad n \approx 0.95
$$

Thus:

$$
f_{ck} = 1.30 \cdot F^{0.95} \text{ (in MPa, with F in kN)}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A pull-out test on a structural column records a peak force of **$F = 65 \text{ kN}$** using a standard 25 mm disc. Estimate the in-situ compressive strength.

**Solution (Linear correlation, $a = 1.5$, $b = 2.0$):**

$$
f_{ck} = (1.5)(65) + 2.0
$$

$$
f_{ck} = 97.5 + 2.0 = 99.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Explanation*: This is unusually high; indicates very high-strength concrete or possible calibration drift.

**Solution (Power correlation, $A = 1.30$, $n = 0.95$):**

$$
f_{ck} = 1.30 \cdot (65)^{0.95}
$$

$$
\ln(f_{ck}) = \ln(1.30) + 0.95 \cdot \ln(65)
$$

$$
\ln(1.30) \approx 0.2624, \quad \ln(65) \approx 4.1744
$$

$$
\ln(f_{ck}) = 0.2624 + 0.95 \times 4.1744 = 0.2624 + 3.9657 = 4.2281
$$

$$
f_{ck} = e^{4.2281} \approx 68.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Explanation*: Power-law gives a more conservative and typically more accurate estimate for normal-strength concrete.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pull-Out Test Apparatus & Procedure

```mermaid

    B --> C["ALLOW CURING 7 TO 28 DAYS"]

    D --> E["APPLY TENSILE LOAD INCREMENTALLY"]
    E --> F{"CONCRETE CONE EXTRACTION OCCURS"}
    F -- YES --> G["RECORD PEAK FORCE F IN kN"]
    F -- NO --> E
    G --> H["MEASURE CONE DIMENSIONS h, R, r"]
    H --> I["COMPUTE FRUSTUM VOLUME Vc"]
    I --> J["APPLY EMPIRICAL CORRELATION F TO fck"]
    J --> K["REPORT IN-SITU COMPRESSIVE STRENGTH"]
    K --> L{"COMPARES WITH DESIGN fck"}

```

```mermaid
    end

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Define Pull-Out Test and list any four salient features.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-Out Test** is a *semi-destructive in-situ* test that measures the force required to extract a previously embedded steel disc along with a frustum of concrete, which is then correlated to the compressive strength of concrete.

**Salient Features:**
1. Provides **direct quantitative** estimate of in-situ compressive strength.
2. Classified under **partially destructive testing (PDT)** — leaves a small visible hole that can be patched.
3. Based on **shear failure of a conical concrete wedge** at approximately **45°**.
4. Standardized as per **ASTM C900** and **BS 1881-207** using a **25 mm disc** at **25 mm embedment depth**.
5. Requires **edge distance > 100 mm** and uses a reaction ring with bearing pressure **≤ 10 N/mm²**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for four features)*

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Pull-Out Test and Rebound Hammer Test.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Pull-Out Test** | **Rebound Hammer Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Semi-destructive | Fully non-destructive |
| Property Measured | Force to extract concrete cone | Surface hardness (rebound number) |
| Volume Stressed | ~$5.3 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{3}$ | Surface layer only (≤ 30 mm depth) |
| Calibration | Empirical F–$f_{ck}$ curve | Empirical R–$f_{ck}$ curve |
| Standards | ASTM C900 / BS 1881-207 | ASTM C805 / IS 13311-2 |
| Result Reliability | High (CV ~10–15%) | Moderate (CV ~20–30%) |
| Surface Damage | Small borehole (repaired) | None |

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each method, tabular comparison)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students commonly lose marks by: (a) confusing the **insert geometry** with the **cone geometry**, (b) forgetting to specify the **edge distance constraint** of 100 mm, (c) using SI units inconsistently (mixing kN and N), and (d) failing to mention the **ASTM/BS standardization**. Always include standard reference and unit consistency.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the principle, procedure, and apparatus of the Pull-Out Test for assessing in-situ compressive strength of concrete. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Pull-Out Test conducted on a 25-year-old bridge pier records peak forces of **78 kN, 82 kN, and 76 kN** at three locations. Using the empirical relation $f_{ck} = 1.30 \cdot F^{0.95}$, determine the average in-situ compressive strength and comment on the suitability for rehabilitation if the design strength was **30 MPa**. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Principle, Procedure, and Apparatus (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The pull-out force required to extract a steel disc embedded in concrete is empirically related to the shear strength of the concrete along the failure surface, which correlates to its compressive strength. The failure geometry is a truncated cone (frustum) at approximately **45°**.

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Standard steel disc insert (diameter **25 mm**, embedded at **25 mm** depth)
- Draw-bolt with ball-joint coupling
- Hydraulic ram (capacity ~**100 kN**)
- Reaction ring (bearing against concrete surface, ID ~**55 mm**, OD ~**120 mm**)
- Load cell or pressure gauge (calibrated, ±**1% accuracy**)

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Cast or drill to embed the steel disc at the test location (ensure edge distance **> 100 mm**).
2. Allow curing for at least **7 days** (preferably **28 days** for design strength).
3. Clean the insert surface and attach the draw-bolt via the ball joint.
4. Position the reaction ring concentrically and engage the hydraulic ram.
5. Apply load gradually (rate ~**50 kN/min** or as per standard).
6. Record peak force F at cone extraction.
7. Patch the residual hole with epoxy mortar or grout.
8. Repeat at minimum **3 locations** per structural element for statistical validity.
9. Compute $f_{ck}$ via the calibrated empirical correlation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Average Pull-Out Force:**

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{78 + 82 + 76}{3} = \frac{236}{3} \approx 78.67 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Power-Law Correlation:**

$$
f_{ck} = 1.30 \cdot F^{0.95}
$$

$$
f_{ck} = 1.30 \cdot (78.67)^{0.95}
$$

$$
\ln(f_{ck}) = \ln(1.30) + 0.95 \cdot \ln(78.67)
$$

$$
\ln(78.67) \approx 4.3652
$$

$$
\ln(f_{ck}) = 0.2624 + 0.95 \times 4.3652 = 0.2624 + 4.1469 = 4.4093
$$

$$
f_{ck} = e^{4.4093} \approx 82.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Individual Calculations for Variability Check:**

For F = 78 kN:

$$
f_{ck1} = 1.30 \cdot (78)^{0.95} \approx 81.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

For F = 82 kN:

$$
f_{ck2} = 1.30 \cdot (82)^{0.95} \approx 85.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

For F = 76 kN:

$$
f_{ck3} = 1.30 \cdot (76)^{0.95} \approx 79.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean and Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
\overline{f_{ck}} = \frac{81.6 + 85.7 + 79.5}{3} \approx 82.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma \approx 2.55 \text{ MPa}
$$

Coefficient of variation:

$$
CoV = \frac{\sigma}{\overline{f_{ck}}} \times 100\% \approx 3.1\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The estimated in-situ strength (**~82.3 MPa**) is significantly higher than the design strength (**30 MPa**).
> - The variability is very low (**CoV ≈ 3.1%**), indicating uniform concrete quality.
> - The pier is **highly suitable for rehabilitation** without need for full-strength enhancement; localized repairs (e.g., crack injection, surface coatings) would suffice.
> - The high CoV margin against design strength also provides a **reserve safety factor of ~2.74**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the advantages, limitations, and IS/ASTM code provisions for the Pull-Out Test. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the volume of the concrete cone extracted during a standard pull-out test, assuming cone height = 25 mm, insert diameter = 25 mm, and base angle = 45°. If the average pull-out force on a beam is **60 kN** and the empirical relation is $f_{ck} = 1.5F + 2.0$ (with F in kN, $f_{ck}$ in MPa), determine the strength adequacy if the required strength is **25 MPa**. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Advantages, Limitations & Code Provisions (7 marks):**

**Advantages (2.5 marks):**
1. Direct, quantitative measure of in-situ compressive strength.
2. Higher reliability and lower CoV than NDT methods like rebound hammer.
3. Mobilizes a meaningful volume of concrete — gives a "bulk" estimate.
4. Standardized internationally (ASTM C900, BS 1881-207).
5. Suitable for **strength evaluation during rehabilitation** — assesses residual capacity of aged structures.

**Limitations (2.5 marks):**
1. Semi-destructive — leaves a hole that must be patched.
