4. **Removal of defective concrete** — by chipping, scabbling, or hydrodemolition. Cut square edges; minimum depth ≥ 6 mm; expose corroded rebar with ≥ 50 mm lap. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Cleaning of cavity** — compressed air, vacuum, water jet. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Quality verification** — pull-off test (≥ 1.7 MPa), moisture test, pH check, soundness test. *(1 mark)*
7. **Bonding agent application** — slurry bond coat, applied wet-on-wet. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Curing** — moist cure for 3–7 days. *(0.5 mark)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — ICRI CSP Classification (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** The ICRI Concrete Surface Profile (CSP) is a standardized visual/tactile comparison scale (CSP 1 to CSP 10) developed by the **International Concrete Repair Institute** in **ICRI Guideline 210.1R** to describe the surface roughness/amplitude of prepared concrete.

**Classification (4 marks):**

| CSP | Amplitude (mm) | Texture Description | Typical Application |
|-----|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1   | < 0.3          | Smooth, nearly flat | Sealers, thin-film coatings |
| 2   | 0.3–0.6        | Light texture       | Acrylic coatings |
| 3   | 0.6–1.0        | Slightly rough      | Epoxy primers |
| 4   | 1.0–1.5        | Light scabbling     | Self-levelers |
| 5   | 1.5–2.0        | Medium texture      | Polymer-modified mortars |
| 6   | 2.0–2.5        | Medium-rough        | Epoxy mortar overlays |
| 7   | 2.5–3.5        | Rough               | Hand-applied repairs |
| 8   | 3.5–4.5        | Very rough          | Shotcrete, trowel-grade mortars |
| 9   | 4.5–5.5        | Very coarse         | Thick repairs |
| 10  | > 5.5          | Extremely coarse    | Specialty repairs |

**Importance (2 marks):** The CSP guides the choice of preparation method (e.g., grinding gives CSP 1–3, abrasive blast gives CSP 4–7, hydrodemolition gives CSP 7–10). The **repair material manufacturer's data sheet always specifies the required CSP range** — failure to achieve it leads to debonding.

---

### **📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Compare **hydrodemolition** and **conventional chipping** as methods of concrete removal. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and suitable applications. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **SSPC standards** for steel surface preparation. List the grades and their typical applications in repair/rehabilitation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Hydrodemolition vs. Chipping (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Hydrodemolition | Chipping |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| **Mechanism** | Ultra-high-pressure water (70–250 MPa) impacts concrete | Pneumatic/hand-held chisel strikes surface |
| **Selectivity** | Excellent — removes only weak concrete, preserves sound | Poor — damages sound concrete, induces micro-cracks |
| **Surface quality** | Exposes clean aggregate, ideal for bond (CSP 7–9) | Irregular, may damage aggregate |
| **Depth control** | Precise; differential water-pressure threshold | Variable, operator-dependent |
| **Dust & noise** | Low dust, very high noise (~110 dB) | Moderate dust, high noise |
| **Vibration** | None | High — risk of micro-cracking in surrounding concrete |
| **Rate of removal** | Very fast (~1 m²/5 min at 40 mm depth) | Slow, labor-intensive |
| **Water management** | Required (7+ m³ per 100 m² typical) | None |
| **Cost** | High (equipment + water handling) | Low (cheap tools, local labor) |
| **Best for** | Bridge decks, large horizontal areas, mass concrete | Small patches, edges, corners, vertical surfaces |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Hydrodemolition is the **preferred modern method** for large, quality-critical projects, while chipping remains practical for small-scale and resource-constrained repairs.

#### **Model Solution (b) — SSPC Standards for Steel (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The **Steel Structures Painting Council (SSPC)** — now merged with NACE as **AMPP (Association for Materials Protection and Performance)** — has published joint SSPC/NACE standards that are **internationally recognized specifications for steel surface cleanliness** prior to coating or repair.

**Key Grades (4 marks):**

| Standard | Name | Cleaning Level | Visual Reference | Typical Use |
|----------|------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| **SSPC-SP 1** | Solvent Cleaning | Removal of oil, grease, dirt, salts | — | Pre-cleaning before any other method |
| **SSPC-SP 2** | Hand Tool Cleaning | Loose rust, mill scale, paint removed | Wire brush, scraper | Minor maintenance |
| **SSPC-SP 3** | Power Tool Cleaning | Same as SP 2 but using power tools | Power wire brush, needle scaler | Spot repair |
| **SSPC-SP 5 (NACE 1)** | White Metal Blast | ≥ 95% clean, no visible contaminants | Pure white | Immersion, severe environments |
| **SSPC-SP 6 (NACE 3)** | Commercial Blast | ≥ 67% clean, tight mill scale OK | Two-thirds clean | Most protective coatings |
| **SSPC-SP 10 (NACE 2)** | Near-White Blast | ≥ 95% clean, light shadows OK | Near-white | High-performance coatings, bridges |
| **SSPC-SP 13** | Concrete Surface Prep | Specific to concrete | — | Pre-overlay concrete prep |

**Application in repair (2 marks):**
- For **corroded rebar** exposed during concrete repair, **SSPC-SP 6 (Commercial)** is the minimum acceptable grade, with **SSPC-SP 10 (Near-White)** preferred for critical structural members.
- For **structural steel** in coastal or industrial atmospheres, **SSPC-SP 10** is specified before applying high-build epoxy or polyurethane protective coatings.
- **SSPC-SP 1 (Solvent Cleaning)** is *always* the first step, regardless of the final blast grade.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Forgetting the SSD condition:** Many students describe surface preparation but omit the critical pre-saturation step required for cementitious repairs. This is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing SSPC grades:** Students often interchange SP 5, SP 6, and SP 10. Remember: **SP 5 = white metal (highest)**, **SP 10 = near-white**, **SP 6 = commercial**. Memorize the cleaning percentages: **95%, 95%, 67%**.
> 3. **Mixing ICRI CSP with SSPC:** ICRI CSP is for **concrete**; SSPC/NACE is for **steel**. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you cite the wrong standard for the wrong substrate.
> 4. **Ignoring environmental and safety aspects:** Modern KTU questions expect a brief mention of **dust control, water management, PPE, and waste disposal**, especially when discussing hydrodemolition or abrasive blasting.
> 5. **Skipping quality control:** Always end your answer with a reference to **pull-off test (≥ 1.7 MPa)** and **CSP verification** — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark pickup.
> 6. **Confusing bond mechanisms:** Mechanical interlock ≠ chemical adhesion. Examiners often ask the difference. Use the terms correctly.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

- ☐ **Surface preparation = substrate cleaning + removal of unsound material + profiling + quality verification.**
- ☐ **It governs > 50% of repair durability** — more important than repair material choice.
- ☐ **Three objectives:** (1) Remove unsound material, (2) Eliminate bond inhibitors, (3) Create adequate profile.
- ☐ **ICRI CSP 1 → 10** is for **concrete**; lower number = smoother, higher = rougher.
- ☐ **SSPC SP 1 → SP 10** is for **steel**; SP 5 (white), SP 10 (near-white), SP 6 (commercial).
- ☐ **Hydrodemolition = best selectivity**; **abrasive blasting = most universal**; **chipping = lowest cost**.
- ☐ **Pre-saturation + SSD condition** prevents the substrate from desiccating the repair mortar.
- ☐ **Minimum repair depth = 6 mm**; cut square edges; **never feather** edges.
- ☐ **Expose rebar with 50 mm lap** and **25 mm clearance behind bar**.
- ☐ **Pull-off tensile bond test** must give **≥ 1.7 MPa** for cementitious repairs.
- ☐ **Repair material manufacturer's data sheet specifies the required CSP** — always check.
- ☐ **Moist cure for 3–7 days** is the gold standard for cementitious repairs.
- ☐ **Bonding agent** must be applied **wet-on-wet**; never let it dry before repair placement.
- ☐ **Modern methods (hydrodemolition, vacuum blasting)** are preferred in urban/occupied sites for **dust and vibration control**.
- ☐ **Cite the right standard:** ICRI for concrete profile, SSPC/NACE for steel cleanliness, ASTM C1583 for bond strength, ASTM D4263 for moisture.
- ☐ **Always end answers** with quality control (pull-off, moisture, pH, soundness) and a mention of curing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcing Steel Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reinforcing Steel Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Reinforcing Steel Cleaning – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures**, **Reinforcing Steel Cleaning** (also called *rebar cleaning* or *steel substrate preparation*) refers to the systematic removal of all corrosion products, loose mill scale, concrete slurry, dust, oil, grease, chlorides, and other bond-breaking contaminants from the surface of exposed reinforcement bars prior to the application of a bonding agent, anti-corrosion coating, or repair mortar. It is the **single most critical operation** that determines the long-term durability, bond strength, and service life of any concrete patch repair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** As per Module 2 of PECET634, students must understand the different cleaning techniques, the **Swedish Standard SIS 05 5900 / ISO 8501-1** visual assessment grades, and the link between surface preparation grade and the bond achieved between old concrete, exposed rebar, and new repair material.

The performance of a repaired RC member is governed by three interfaces:

1. Old concrete → New repair material
2. **Exposed rebar → Anti-corrosion primer / repair mortar** ← (governed by steel cleaning)
3. Old concrete → Existing un-corroded rebar

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Wound Treatment" Analogy

Imagine a doctor treating a deep, infected wound on a patient's arm.

- The doctor does **not** simply apply medicine over the dirty wound. Instead, the wound is first **cleaned, debrided (removing dead tissue), and disinfected** so that new healthy tissue can grow.
- If the doctor skips cleaning and just puts a bandage over rust and grime, the infection persists under the bandage and the wound never heals.

In exactly the same way, a structural engineer repairing a spalled RC beam behaves like a doctor:

- The **corroded reinforcement bar** = the *infected wound*.
- **Loose concrete, rust, and chlorides** = the *dead/diseased tissue*.
- **Anti-corrosion primer + repair mortar** = the *bandage and medicine*.
- **Steel cleaning** = the *debridement process*.

> [!NOTE]
> Without proper cleaning, no primer, no epoxy, and no polymer-modified mortar will develop a durable bond with the steel. Cleaning is therefore non-negotiable.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (KTU Standard Values)

| Parameter | Standard Reference | Typical Value |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| Minimum surface cleanliness | ISO 8501-1 | **Sa 2½** (commercial blast cleaning) |
| Maximum dust allowed | ISO 8502-3 | **Class 2** (≤ 0.2 g/m²) |
| Maximum chloride contamination | ISO 8502-6/8 | **≤ 30 mg/m² (30 ppm)** |
| Surface profile (roughness) | ISO 8503 | **50 – 100 µm** |
| Moisture content of concrete | BS 8204 | **< 5 %** by mass |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**Sa = Sand, St = Solvent, Ma = Manual**" – SSPC uses *Sa* for abrasive blast, *St* for solvent clean, and *Ma* for manual/tool cleaning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis – The "Why" and "How"

## 2.1 Why is Reinforcing Steel Cleaning Necessary?

The corrosion of steel in concrete is an **electro-chemical process** driven by:

1. **Carbonation** (CO₂ lowers pore-water pH from ~12.5 to < 9, breaking the passive Fe₂O₃ layer).
2. **Chloride ingress** (threshold ≈ **0.4 % by mass of cement** or **0.05 % by mass of concrete**, per ACI 222R).
3. **Cracking and spalling** (creates a direct path for O₂, moisture, and chlorides).

When corrosion initiates, the products formed are:

$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad (\text{anodic reaction})$$

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (\text{cathodic reaction})$$

The voluminous rust occupies **2 to 6 times** the original volume of the parent steel. This expansion cracks the concrete cover, accelerates corrosion, and destroys bond. If this loose rust is not removed:

- The new repair material will bond to **rust**, not to **steel**.
- The rust layer is *porous, friable, and hygroscopic* → retains moisture → re-corrosion begins within months.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student misconception:** "Wire brushing once is enough." – Wire brushing only removes *loose* rust. It cannot remove *tightly adherent mill scale or pitted corrosion*. KTU expects awareness of **Sa 2½ or equivalent**.

## 2.2 Classification of Cleaning Methods

Cleaning methods are classified into three families:

### A. Mechanical / Abrasive Cleaning
- **Hand tool cleaning** (wire brush, scraper, chipping hammer) → SSPC-**SP 2**
- **Power tool cleaning** (rotary wire wheel, needle scaler, angle grinder) → SSPC-**SP 3 / SP 11**
- **Abrasive blast cleaning** (sand, grit, shot, slag) → SSPC-**SP 5, SP 6, SP 10**
- **Hydro-jetting / Ultra-high pressure water jetting (UHPWJ)** → SSPC-**SP 12 / WJ 1–4**
- **Vacuum blast / dustless blasting** → SSPC-**SP 10**

### B. Chemical Cleaning
- **Acid pickling** (dilute HCl, H₂SO₄, phosphoric acid)
- **Alkaline cleaning** (caustic soda for oil/grease)
- **Chelating / rust-converter chemicals** (tannic acid-based)

### C. Thermal / Flame Cleaning
- **Oxy-acetylene flame** (limited use, removes only thick mill scale)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard / Value | Unit |
|---------------|---------|------------------|------|
| Sa 1 | Light blast cleaning | ISO 8501-1 | – |
| Sa 2 | Thorough blast cleaning | ISO 8501-1 | – |
| **Sa 2½** | **Commercial blast cleaning (recommended for rebar)** | **ISO 8501-1** | – |
| Sa 3 | Blast cleaning to visually clean steel | ISO 8501-1 | – |
| SP 2 | Hand tool cleaning | SSPC | – |
| SP 3 | Power tool cleaning | SSPC | – |
| **SP 10 / Sa 2½** | **Near-white metal blast** | **SSPC / ISO** | – |
| R_z | Surface roughness (peak-to-valley) | ISO 8503 | µm |
| R_a | Average roughness | ISO 8503 | µm |
| Chloride threshold (rebar) | [Cl⁻] for corrosion onset | **0.4 %** by mass of cement | % |
| pH of uncarbonated concrete | – | **12.5 – 13.5** | – |
| pH of carbonated concrete | – | **< 9.0** | – |
| Rust volume expansion factor | V_rust / V_steel | **2 – 6** | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "what is the minimum surface preparation grade for rebar in patch repair?" – the answer is **Sa 2½ / SP 10** or equivalent. Hand-tool cleaning (SP 2) is acceptable only for *small, non-critical* repairs and only when blast cleaning is impractical.

## 2.4 Decision Logic – Selecting a Cleaning Method

| Site Condition | Recommended Method | Achievable Grade |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Loose rust, small area, no power | Hand tool (wire brush) | **SP 2 / St 2** |
| Moderate rust, accessible | Power tool (needle scaler, grinder) | **SP 3 / SP 11** |
| Heavy rust, pitting, large area | Abrasive blast (sand/shot/grit) | **Sa 2½ / SP 10** |
| Corrosion + chloride contamination | Hydro-jetting + abrasive blast | **Sa 2½ + WJ 2** |
| Oil/grease contamination | Solvent/emulsion + alkaline wash | **SSPC-SP 1** |
| Tight schedule, low dust tolerance | Vacuum / dustless blasting | **SP 10** |
| Highly congested rebar (multi-layer) | UHPWJ (1000–2500 bar) | **SP 12 WJ 2** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridges & Marine structures** – Chloride-laden environments demand **Sa 2½ + protective primer**.
- **Heritage restoration** – Use **micro-abrasive (soda-blast, dry-ice)** to avoid damaging parent concrete.
- **Nuclear containment** – Use **vacuum blast + HEPA filtration** to control radioactive dust.
- **Tunnels** – UHPWJ is preferred because of confined space and minimum dust.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Methodology, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Standard Abrasive Blast Cleaning (Sa 2½)

### Step 1 – Pre-assessment
Visually classify the rust grade per **ISO 8501-1** reference photographs:

- **A grade** – Steel covered with adherent mill scale, little rust.
- **B grade** – Mill scale beginning to flake, slight rust.
- **C grade** – Mill scale rusted away, light pitting visible.
- **D grade** – Heavy pitting, extensive rust.

> For corroded rebar exposed by spalling, the typical starting condition is **C or D grade**.

### Step 2 – Delimit the repair area
Cut concrete perpendicular to the surface to a minimum depth of **10 mm behind the rebar** (IS 13311, ACI 546R) so that at least **two-thirds of the rebar circumference** is exposed.

### Step 3 – Remove loose concrete
Use a light chipping hammer (≤ 7 kg) or hand tools. Heavy breakers (≥ 15 kg) cause micro-cracks in the parent concrete, which is undesirable.

### Step 4 – Initial mechanical removal
Remove loosely adhering rust and concrete slurry using a **needle scaler, wire brush, or scraper**. This reduces the burden on the abrasive blast step.

### Step 5 – Abrasive blast cleaning
Direct a stream of abrasive (sand, garnet, copper slag, or steel grit) propelled by compressed air (0.5 – 0.7 MPa) onto the rebar.

- Abrasive size: **0.5 – 1.5 mm** for rebar.
- Nozzle-to-surface distance: **100 – 300 mm**.
- Nozzle angle: **45° – 90°** to surface.

Continue until **at least 95 % of the surface area is free of visible residue** (Sa 2½ requirement).

### Step 6 – Clean the surrounding concrete
Blast (or brush) the **inner face of the concrete substrate** for at least **100 mm beyond the rebar** to remove micro-contaminants.

### Step 7 – Dust removal
Use **oil-free compressed air, industrial vacuum, or a clean brush** to remove dust. Dust must be ≤ **Class 2 / Class 3** per **ISO 8502-3**.

### Step 8 – Chloride testing (optional but recommended)
Sample the concrete fines (e.g., by drilling) and test for chlorides per **ISO 8502-6/8** or **ASTM C1152**.

- If [Cl⁻] > **0.4 %** of cement mass, consider additional measures (e.g., electrochemical chloride extraction or use of corrosion-inhibitor admixture).

### Step 9 – Apply anti-corrosion primer
Within **4 hours** of cleaning (to prevent re-rusting, the so-called *"flash-rust"* window), apply a zinc-rich epoxy primer, a polymer-modified cementitious slurry, or an active coating.

### Step 10 – Re-bedding
Place the repair mortar / micro-concrete while the primer is still tacky ("wet-on-wet") to ensure monolithic bond.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example – Surface Roughness Calculation

A corroded rebar of nominal diameter **16 mm** has lost, on average, **1.5 mm** of its radius due to pitting corrosion. The half-circumference exposed to the atmosphere is cleaned using an abrasive blast that creates an average surface roughness **R_a = 75 µm**.

**Given:**
- Original radius, r₀ = 8 mm
- Corrosion loss in radius, Δr = 1.5 mm
- Residual radius, r₁ = r₀ – Δr
- Average surface roughness, R_a = 75 µm = 0.075 mm

**Required:** Effective load-bearing cross-section of the cleaned rebar and the percentage reduction in capacity.

### Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \pi r_0^{\,2} \\
&= \pi (8)^2 \\
&= 64\pi \;\text{mm}^2 \\
&\approx 201.06 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

The residual cross-section after corrosion is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \pi r_1^{\,2} \\
&= \pi (8 - 1.5)^2 \\
&= \pi (6.5)^2 \\
&= 42.25\pi \;\text{mm}^2 \\
&\approx 132.73 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

The *effective* load-bearing area, after deducting the surface roughness (assumed uniformly distributed across the residual radius), is conservatively reduced by an additional 2·R_a:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{\text{eff}} &= r_1 - R_a \\
&= 6.5 - 0.075 \\
&= 6.425 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{eff}} &= \pi r_{\text{eff}}^{\,2} \\
&= \pi (6.425)^2 \\
&\approx 129.66 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

The percentage reduction in cross-sectional area is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{loss} &= \frac{A_0 - A_{\text{eff}}}{A_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{201.06 - 129.66}{201.06} \times 100 \\
&\approx 35.5\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A loss of only 1.5 mm radius in a 16 mm bar causes ~35 % loss in steel area. This exceeds the typical **10 %** threshold beyond which ACI 562 / IS 456 recommend **supplementary reinforcement** (e.g., welded rebar laps or external CFRP wrapping).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Method vs. Quality vs. Cost vs. Time

| Method | Surface Finish | Dust | Noise | Speed | Cost (₹/m²) | Best Application |
|--------|---------------|------|-------|-------|-------------|------------------|
| Hand tool (SP 2) | Poor–Fair | Low | Low | Slow | 50–100 | Cosmetic, light rust |
| Power tool (SP 3) | Fair | Medium | High | Med | 100–200 | Field repairs, small areas |
| Needle scaler (SP 11) | Good | Low | High | Med | 150–250 | Pitted rebar, tight spaces |
| Sand / grit blast (Sa 2½) | Excellent | **High** | High | Fast | 250–500 | Heavy corrosion, large works |
| UHP water jet (WJ 2) | Excellent | **None** | High | Med | 500–900 | Chloride removal, dust-free zones |
| Dry-ice (CO₂) blast | Good | None | Low | Slow | 800–1500 | Heritage, food-grade areas |
| Acid pickling (chemical) | Good (chem.) | None (Fumes) | Low | Slow | 100–300 | Prefab / yard cleaning |

## 3.4 Evaluating Cleanliness – Verification Procedure

1. **Visual comparison** against ISO 8501-1 photographic reference standards.
2. **Tape / cloth test** – Press a clean white cotton glove or adhesive tape on the surface; if no grey/black dust transfers, the surface is dust-clean.
3. **Solvent wipe test** – Wipe with a clean white cloth dampened in solvent; absence of residual grime indicates cleanliness.
4. **Chloride swab test** (Quantab strip) – Quick field test to verify [Cl⁻] ≤ 30 ppm.
5. **Surface profile** – Measured using **replica tape (Testex Press-O-Film)** or a **profilometer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams, Schematics & Process Flowcharts

## 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow – Rebar Cleaning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Exposed Corroded Rebar] --> B[Visual Rust Grading]
    B --> B1{A, B Grade}
    B --> B2{C, D Grade}
    B1 --> C1[Hand Tool SP2]
    C1 --> D1[Wire Brush + Scraper]
    D1 --> E[Surface Check]
    B2 --> C2[Power Tool SP3]
    C2 --> D2[Needle Scaler / Grinder]
    D2 --> F{Heavy Pitting?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Abrasive Blast Sa 2.5]
    F -->|No| E
    G --> H[Compressed Air Cleaning]
    H --> I[Dust Test ISO 8502-3]
    I --> J{Dust Pass?}
    J -->|No| H
    J -->|Yes| K[Chloride Test]
    K --> L{Cl <= 30 ppm?}
    L -->|No| M[Hydrojet or Inhibitor]
    L -->|Yes| N[Apply Primer within 4 hrs]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Place Repair Mortar Wet on Wet]
    O --> P[End: Rebar Ready for Repair]
    E --> P
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Classification of Cleaning Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Cleaning Methods] --> B[Mechanical]
    A --> C[Chemical]
    A --> D[Thermal]
    B --> B1[Hand Tool SP2]
    B --> B2[Power Tool SP3 SP11]
    B --> B3[Abrasive Blast SP5 SP6 SP10]
    B --> B4[Hydrojetting SP12 WJ1-4]
    C --> C1[Acid Pickling]
    C --> C2[Alkaline Degreasing]
    C --> C3[Rust Converters]
    D --> D1[Oxy-Acetylene Flame]
    style A fill:#222,color:#fff
    style B fill:#3a7
    style C fill:#a73
    style D fill:#73a
```

## 4.3 Process Sequence – From Spalled Concrete to Repaired Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Spalled Concrete Exposing Rebar] --> S2[Mark Cut Boundary at 90 deg]
    S2 --> S3[Remove Unsound Concrete with Light Chipping Hammer]
    S3 --> S4[Expose Full Circumference of Rebar]
    S4 --> S5[Clean Rebar to Sa 2.5 Grade]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Anti-Corrosive Primer]
    S6 --> S7[Optional Bonding Agent on Substrate]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Repair Mortar or Micro Concrete]
    S8 --> S9[Cure for 7 Days]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Protective Coating if Required]
    S10 --> END[End: Repair Complete]
```

## 4.4 Schematic – Cross Section of a Cleaned Rebar with Primer and Repair Mortar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB[Substrate Old Concrete]
        direction TB
        A1[Old Concrete Matrix]
    end
    subgraph PR[Primer and Repair Zone]
        direction TB
        P1[Anti-Corrosive Primer Layer 50 to 100 micron]
        P2[Repair Mortar or Micro Concrete]
    end
    subgraph RB[Reinforcing Steel]
        direction TB
        R1[Cleaned Rebar Surface Sa 2.5]
        R2[Residual Steel Core]
    end
    A1 --- P2
    P2 --- P1
    P1 --- R1
    R1 --- R2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading tip for the diagrams:** Follow the arrows in the direction of the arrowhead. The diamond-shaped nodes (`{}`) are *decision points*; the rectangular nodes (`[]`) are *process steps*; the rounded nodes (`()`) are *start/end points*.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three methods of cleaning corroded reinforcement steel and state the surface preparation grade achieved by each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hand tool cleaning** – wire brushing, scraping. Achieves **SSPC-SP 2 / St 2** grade. Suitable only for light, loose rust.
2. **Power tool cleaning** – needle scaler, rotary wire wheel, angle grinder. Achieves **SSPC-SP 3 / SP 11** grade. Used for moderate, tightly adhering rust.
3. **Abrasive blast cleaning** – sand, grit, or shot propelled by compressed air. Achieves **SSPC-SP 10 / ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½** (commercial / near-white metal blast). Recommended for heavy rust and chloride-laden rebar.

*(1 mark per method with grade – total 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is surface preparation of reinforcement considered the most critical step in concrete patch repair?**

**Model Answer:**

1. The long-term bond between the **rebar, anti-corrosion primer, and repair mortar** is established only on a *clean* steel surface. (1 mark)
2. Residual rust, mill scale, dust, and chlorides act as **bond-breakers** and **moisture-retentive layers**, leading to *re-corrosion within months* of repair. (1 mark)
3. Studies show that **80 % of premature repair failures** are traceable to inadequate surface preparation. Hence, even the best primer cannot compensate for poor cleaning. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) – Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for **abrasive blast cleaning** of corroded reinforcement, including the equipment, abrasive selection, technique, and the standard surface finish achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **visual grades of cleanliness and rust** as per **ISO 8501-1**, and explain how a **site engineer** would decide between **SP 2, SP 3, and SP 10** for a given repair situation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

1. **Definition & purpose** (1 mark): Abrasive blast cleaning is the propelling of abrasive particles by compressed air (or by centrifugal wheels) against a steel surface to remove rust, mill scale, and old coatings, achieving a *near-white metal* finish.

2. **Equipment** (2 marks):
   - Air compressor (0.5 – 0.7 MPa; capacity ≥ 3 m³/min).
   - Blast nozzle (Venturi-type, ceramic or tungsten carbide; orifice 6 – 12 mm).
   - Abrasive hopper with metering valve, dead-man handle, and air-fed helmet.
   - Oil/moisture traps in the air line.
   - Vacuum recovery unit (for dustless blasting).

3. **Abrasive selection** (1 mark): Copper slag, garnet, aluminium oxide, or chilled iron grit of size **0.5 – 1.5 mm**. Avoid silica sand in confined spaces (silicosis risk).

4. **Procedure** (2 marks):
   - Direct the nozzle at **45° – 90°** to the surface, distance **100 – 300 mm**.
   - Move the nozzle in slow, overlapping passes until **≥ 95 %** of every square inch is free of visible residue.
   - Verify against ISO 8501-1 reference photographs.
   - Remove dust with oil-free compressed air or industrial vacuum.

5. **Standard finish achieved** (1 mark): **SSPC-SP 10 / ISO Sa 2½** (commercial / near-white metal blast). This is the *minimum recommended* grade for rebar in structural patch repair.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

1. **ISO 8501-1 rust grades A–D** (3 marks):
   - **A** – Mill scale intact, minor rust.
   - **B** – Mill scale flaking, slight rust.
   - **C** – Mill scale rusted away, light pitting.
   - **D** – Heavy pitting, extensive rust.

2. **ISO 8501-1 cleanliness grades Sa 1 / Sa 2 / Sa 2½ / Sa 3** (1 mark).

3. **Decision logic for site engineer** (3 marks):
   - **SP 2 (Hand tool):** A or early-B grade rust; small area; no power available; non-critical patch.
   - **SP 3 (Power tool):** Late B or C grade rust; moderate area; need to work fast but no blasting rig available.
   - **SP 10 (Abrasive blast):** C or D grade rust with pitting; large area; structural importance; chloride-contaminated environment. **Always preferred for structural RC repair.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **mechanism of corrosion of steel in concrete** and show how the **volume of corrosion products** contributes to cracking and spalling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on **chemical methods of cleaning reinforcement** (acid pickling, alkaline cleaning, rust converters), including their advantages, limitations, and typical application procedure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

1. **Mechanism of corrosion** (3 marks): Reinforcing steel in concrete is normally protected by a thin, stable, passive Fe₂O₃ film maintained by the high alkalinity (pH 12.5 – 13.5) of the pore solution. Carbonation (CO₂ ingress) and/or chloride contamination destroy this passive film, after which the steel behaves as a galvanic cell:

   - **Anode (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻):** Dissolution of iron in pits or areas of low oxygen.
   - **Cathode (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻):** Oxygen reduction in surrounding passive areas.
   - The corrosion products (Fe(OH)₂, Fe(OH)₃, hydrated Fe-oxy-hydroxides) are deposited at the anode.

2. **Volume expansion** (2 marks): The molar volume of these hydrated oxides is **2 to 6 times** that of the parent steel. The expansive stresses (often **15 – 40 MPa**) exceed the tensile strength of concrete (≈ 3 – 5 MPa), causing internal radial cracking.

3. **Cracking and spalling** (2 marks): The radial cracks propagate to the surface, allowing further ingress of moisture, oxygen, and chlorides, leading to **delamination** and **spalling of the cover concrete**. This exposes the rebar, accelerating the cycle.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

1. **Acid pickling** (3 marks):
   - **Procedure:** Immerse (or swab) the rebar in **5 – 10 % HCl** (or 10 – 20 % H₂SO₄ / 10 – 20 % phosphoric acid) for 10 – 30 minutes, then rinse with clean water and immediately neutralize with an alkaline wash (e.g., 5 % Na₂CO₃).
   - **Advantages:** Effective removal of mill scale and rust even in pits.
   - **Limitations:** Acid residue if not neutralized → promotes re-corrosion; produces fumes; cannot be used on in-situ vertical rebar (run-off issues).

2. **Alkaline cleaning** (2 marks):
   - **Procedure:** Hot alkaline solution (NaOH or Na₂CO₃ with surfactants) applied to remove oils, greases, and form-release agents.
   - **Advantages:** Safe for in-situ work; removes organic contaminants.
   - **Limitations:** Ineffective against rust; must be followed by acid pickling or blast cleaning.

3. **Rust converters** (2 marks):
   - **Procedure:** Apply tannic acid or phosphoric acid-based formulations that *convert* the rust (Fe³⁺) into a stable, paintable iron-tannate or iron-phosphate film.
   - **Advantages:** No need to remove all rust; easy brush application; suitable for field use.
   - **Limitations:** Surface must be *sound* (not loose/flaky); long curing time (24 – 48 h); not a substitute for blast cleaning in critical repairs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** write "rust converters can replace abrasive blasting" – they only *convert* rust; they do not remove it. Expect 1 – 2 marks deducted.
> 2. Do **not** skip mentioning the **4-hour "flash-rust" window** between cleaning and primer application – this is a frequent KTU question.
> 3. Always state the **standard reference** (e.g., *SSPC-SP 10, ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½*) – generic answers like "clean thoroughly" fetch only partial credit.
> 4. In numerical problems, remember to use **consistent units** (mm vs. cm vs. m). A simple dimensional mismatch costs a full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Cleaning is the removal of rust, mill scale, dust, oil, and chlorides from exposed rebar to enable durable bond.
- ✅ **Why?** Poor cleaning = premature re-corrosion; 80 % of repair failures are preparation-related.
- ✅ **Corrosion theory:** Carbonation + chlorides break passive Fe₂O₃ → anodic Fe dissolution → voluminous rust (2 – 6× volume) → cracking → spalling.
- ✅ **Chloride threshold:** **0.4 %** by mass of cement (or 30 ppm on steel).
- ✅ **pH drop:** From **12.5** to **< 9** triggers depassivation.
- ✅ **Method grades (minimum for rebar):** Hand SP 2 → Power SP 3 / SP 11 → Blast **SP 10 / Sa 2½** → UHPWJ **SP 12 WJ 2**.
- ✅ **Abrasive:** Sand, garnet, copper slag, or steel grit (0.5 – 1.5 mm).
- ✅ **Air pressure:** 0.5 – 0.7 MPa; nozzle distance 100 – 300 mm; angle 45° – 90°.
- ✅ **Surface profile:** R_a 50 – 100 µm.
- ✅ **Flash-rust window:** Apply primer within **4 hours** of cleaning.
- ✅ **Dust class:** ≤ **Class 2 / 3** per ISO 8502-3.
- ✅ **Concrete behind rebar:** Expose at least **two-thirds of circumference** and clean **≥ 100 mm** of adjacent substrate.
- ✅ **Standards to memorize:** ISO 8501-1 (cleanliness), ISO 8502-3 (dust), ISO 8503 (roughness), SSPC-SP 2/3/10/11/12, ACI 546R, IS 13311.
- ✅ **Chemical methods:** Acid pickling (HCl/H₂SO₄/H₃PO₄) + neutralization, alkaline degreasing, rust converters (tannic/phosphoric).
- ✅ **Verification tools:** Visual reference cards, replica tape (Testex), chloride swab (Quantab), profilometer.
- ✅ **Decision shortcut:** Light rust + small area → SP 2; heavy rust + chloride → **SP 10 + inhibitor**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner for viva:** "*Clean steel is half the repair.*" A KTU answer that mentions **Sa 2½**, the **4-hour flash-rust window**, and **chloride threshold of 0.4 %** will always be treated as a high-quality response.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair Materials - Various Repair Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Repair Materials - Various Repair Materials ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Repair Material** is defined (per ACI 546R & IS 15912) as a specially formulated composite — cementitious, polymer-modified, or purely polymeric — used to **restore structural integrity, durability, geometry, and serviceability** of a deteriorated reinforced concrete or masonry element by replacing the lost material and re-establishing monolithic action with the parent substrate. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, repair materials are the **"engineered substances"** that form the backbone of any rehabilitation strategy. They are not ordinary concrete or mortar; they are designed to possess: - High early strength - Low shrinkage - Excellent bond to old concrete - Compatibility with the substrate (thermal, chemical, and mechanical) - Durability equal to or greater than the parent concrete ### 1.2 Classification of Repair Materials (Broad Overview) The materials can be classified based on their **binder system** and **functional use**. | Basis of Classification | Categories | |---|---| | By Binder Type | Cementitious, Polymer-Modified Cementitious, Purely Polymeric, Bituminous | | By Function | Bonding/Priming, Patch Repair, Injection Grouting, Surface Coatings, Structural Strengthening | | By Form | Pre-bagged Powder, Pre-mixed Paste, Liquid Resin, Mortar, Grout | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite Line:** "The choice of repair material is dictated not by cost, but by the **diagnosis of the cause of deterioration** and the **required performance characteristics** of the repaired zone." ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Metaphor Think of a damaged concrete structure as a **human patient with a fracture**: - **The Doctor's Diagnosis** = Condition Assessment (Non-Destructive Testing, Rebar scanning). - **The Bone Cement / Plaster Cast** = Cementitious or Polymer-Modified Repair Material. - **The Surgical Glue (Fevikwik)** = Epoxy Bonding Agent / Primer. - **The Titanium Plate** = Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) / Carbon Fiber Wrap for strengthening. - **The Antibiotic Cream** = Anti-corrosion coating for rebar. Just as you would **not** use a regular plaster-of-paris for a compound fracture, you **cannot** use ordinary cement mortar for a structural repair that has active corrosion or chemical attack. The repair material must be **engineered to address the root cause**. > [!TIP] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "use M20 concrete for repair" — this is **wrong**. Repair requires special materials with **low w/c ratio (0.35–0.40)**, polymer modification, or chemical resistance, depending on exposure. ### 1.4 Ideal Requirements of a Repair Material (Per IS 15912:2012 & ACI 546R) A perfect repair material must satisfy: 1. **Dimensional Stability** — Negligible drying shrinkage (typically $< 0.04\%$ at 28 days). 2. **Bond Strength** — Greater than the tensile strength of the parent concrete ($\ge 1.7$ MPa for structural repairs). 3. **Compressive Strength** — Equal to or higher than the substrate (typically $\ge 40$ MPa for structural patches). 4. **Modulus of Elasticity** — Compatible with substrate (24–30 GPa for cementitious; 3–15 GPa for polymers). 5. **Chemical Resistance** — Sulfate, chloride, and acid resistance as per exposure. 6. **Thermal Compatibility** — Coefficient of thermal expansion close to concrete ($\approx 7-12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$). 7. **Workability** — Adequate pot life and workable time. 8. **Durability** — Resistance to weathering, freeze-thaw, and aging. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Cementitious Repair Materials These are the **most widely used** family of repair materials, based on Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) with various mineral and chemical admixtures. #### 2.1.1 Plain Cementitious Mortars / Concretes - Made by partial replacement of cement with **silica fume (micro-silica)**, **fly ash**, **GGBS (Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag)**, or **metakaolin**. - Water-binder ratio strictly controlled between **0.35 and 0.40**. - Used for non-structural or lightly structural patches. #### 2.1.2 Polymer-Modified Mortars (PMM) and Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC) > [!IMPORTANT] > The addition of polymers (typically **SBR — Styrene Butadiene Rubber**, **acrylics**, or **redispersible polymer powders**) to cement mortar **transforms** a brittle material into a **tough, flexible, dense, and durable** composite. **Mechanism of Polymer Modification:** 1. Polymer particles disperse in the mix water. 2. As hydration proceeds and water evaporates, polymer films form within the capillary pores. 3. These films **bridge micro-cracks** and seal the pore structure. 4. Result: Improved flexural strength, bond, and impermeability. **Typical Properties of PMM (with 15–20% polymer solids by weight of cement):** | Property | Plain Mortar | Polymer-Modified Mortar | |---|---|---| | Compressive Strength (MPa) | 30–40 | 40–70 | | Flexural Strength (MPa) | 5–7 | 10–18 | | Bond Strength (MPa) | 1.0–1.5 | 2.5–4.0 | | Drying Shrinkage | Higher | **40–60% reduction** | | Water Absorption (%) | 8–12 | 2–4 | #### 2.1.3 Fiber-Reinforced Concrete (FRC) for Repair - **Steel Fibers** (hooked or crimped): Improve tensile, flexural, and impact resistance. - **Polypropylene Fibers**: Control plastic shrinkage cracking. - **Glass Fibers** (alkali-resistant): For thin-section repairs and GRC (Glass Reinforced Concrete) panels. - **Carbon Fibers**: High-performance strengthening. **Fiber Dosage:** Typically **0.5% to 1.5% by volume**. #### 2.1.4 Ferrocement - A composite of cement mortar and **multiple layers of closely spaced wire mesh** (chicken mesh / weld mesh). - Thickness usually **10–25 mm**. - Excellent for thin overlays, jacketing of columns, and waterproofing of water tanks. #### 2.1.5 Shrinkage-Compensating Cement - Uses **expansive cement** (Type K, Type M, Type S per ASTM 845). - Calcium sulfoaluminate hydrates form and cause **controlled expansion**, offsetting drying shrinkage. - Useful for large area patch repairs and grouting. ### 2.2 Polymer-Based Repair Materials (Resin Systems) These are **100% solid, reactive, thermosetting** systems that cure by chemical reaction (no water loss). #### 2.2.1 Epoxy Resins (Most Common for Structural Repair) - **Two-component system:** Resin (Part A) + Hardener (Part B). - Bond strength: **3–7 MPa** (can exceed substrate tensile strength). - Used for: crack injection, bonding new concrete to old, structural patches, rebar coating. **Limitations:** - Sensitive to moisture during curing. - High modulus → can cause **stress concentrations** at edges. - UV degradation (needs protective top coat). - Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) limits use in high-temperature zones. #### 2.2.2 Polyester Resins - Cheaper than epoxy, faster curing. - Used in GFRP laminates and non-critical patches. - **High styrene emission** — environmental concern. #### 2.2.3 Polyurethane Resins - **Flexible and elastomeric**. - Used for **waterproofing**, joint sealants, and flexible crack injection (live cracks). - Excellent chemical resistance. #### 2.2.4 Acrylic / Methacrylate Resins - Very **low viscosity** ($\approx 1$ cP) — perfect for **hairline crack injection** ($< 0.1$ mm width). - Cure rapidly using promoters (e.g., DMA — Dimethyl Aniline). - Used for slab and pavement repairs. #### 2.2.5 Comparison of Polymer Resins for Repair | Property | Epoxy | Polyester | Polyurethane | Methacrylate | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 50–90 | 40–70 | 20–60 | 30–70 | | Elongation (%) | 1–8 | 1–5 | 100–600 | 1–10 | | Viscosity (cP) | 100–10000 | 200–2000 | 500–5000 | 1–100 | | Bond to Concrete | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | | Moisture Tolerance | Poor–Fair | Fair | Excellent | Fair | | Cost (Relative) | High | Low–Med | Med | Med–High | ### 2.3 Specialty Repair Materials #### 2.3.1 Micro-Silica (Silica Fume) Based Systems - **Particle size:** 0.1–0.2 microns (100× finer than cement). - **Pozzolanic reaction** produces additional C-S-H gel → denser, stronger matrix. - Typical dosage: **5–15% by weight of cement**. #### 2.3.2 GGBS-Based Repair Materials - Latent hydraulic binder, sustainable, **resistant to chloride and sulfate attack**. - Long-term strength gain continues for **90+ days**. #### 2.3.3 Magnesium Phosphate Cement (MPC) - **Rapid-setting** (sets in 10–30 minutes). - Used for emergency repairs of concrete roads and runways. - Excellent bond to old concrete. #### 2.3.4 Chemical-Resistant Mortars - Furan resins, vinyl ester, or sulfur-impregnated mortars. - Used in **chemical plants, effluent treatment plants, and battery rooms**. #### 2.3.5 Geopolymer Repair Materials - **Alkali-activated alumino-silicate** binder (no OPC). - Eco-friendly, low carbon footprint. - Excellent fire and chemical resistance. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these formulas — they appear directly in numerical problems.** | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Bond strength from pull-off test (MPa) | | 2 | $\varepsilon_{sh} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | Drying shrinkage strain (%) | | 3 | $f_{ck,patch} \ge f_{ck,parent} + 5 \text{ MPa}$ | Recommended patch strength (KTU/IS rule) | | 4 | $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (MPa) | Modulus of elasticity of concrete (IS 456) | | 5 | $CTE_{match} = \vert CTE_{repair} - CTE_{substrate} \vert < 2 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$ | Thermal compatibility criterion | | 6 | $V_f (\%)= \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Fiber volume fraction (%) | | 7 | $w/c_{eff} = \dfrac{W}{C + k \cdot SF}$ | Effective w/c with silica fume (k ≈ 3–4 effectiveness factor) | | 8 | $t_{pot} = \dfrac{V_{mix}}{V_{applied}} \times T_{cure}$ | Pot life estimation (mins) | | 9 | $\sigma_{adh} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{core}}$ | Adhesive tensile bond strength (pull-off) | | 10 | $\Delta T_{crit} = \dfrac{f_t}{E_c \cdot \Delta CTE}$ | Critical temperature difference causing de-bond | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Water-Cement Ratio with Silica Fume Replacement Silica fume is a **highly active pozzolanic material** and acts as if it were **3 to 4 times its weight of cement** in terms of water demand and binding efficiency. We must compute the effective w/c ratio for a repair mix. **Given Data (Sample Problem):** - Cement (C) = **400 kg/m³** - Silica Fume (SF) = **40 kg/m³** (i.e., 10% replacement) - Water (W) = **160 kg/m³** - Effectiveness factor of silica fume, **k = 3** **Step 1 — Identify the standard formula for effective binder content.** $$C_{eff} = C + (k \cdot SF)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$C_{eff} = 400 + (3 \times 40)$$ $$C_{eff} = 400 + 120 = 520 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the effective water-binder ratio formula.** $$\left(\dfrac{w}{c}\right)_{eff} = \dfrac{W}{C_{eff}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate the effective ratio.** $$\left(\dfrac{w}{c}\right)_{eff} = \dfrac{160}{520}$$ $$\left(\dfrac{w}{c}\right)_{eff} = 0.3077 \approx 0.31$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** > The effective w/c of **0.31** is **very low**, which explains why silica fume-modified repair materials exhibit high strength and low permeability. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pull-Off Bond Strength of a Repair Patch The **pull-off test** (ASTM C1583 / IS 15912) is the most common field test to verify the quality of a repair. **Given Data:** - Diameter of core (d) = **50 mm** - Failure load (F) = **18.5 kN** **Step 1 — Area of the core.** $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times d^2$$ **Step 2 — Numerical evaluation.** $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (50)^2$$ $$A = 0.7854 \times 2500 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute bond stress.** $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{F}{A}$$ $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{18{,}500 \text{ N}}{1963.5 \text{ mm}^2}$$ $$\sigma_b = 9.42 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Verify against the acceptance criterion.** > [!NOTE] > **IS 15912 minimum pull-off strength = 1.7 MPa** (structural repair). The test value of **9.42 MPa** is **excellent** (failure occurred in substrate, not at the bond plane). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Temperature Difference for Bond Failure When the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) of the repair material differs from the substrate, **thermal cycling** can cause de-bonding. **Given Data:** - $CTE_{substrate} = 10 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$ (typical concrete) - $CTE_{repair} = 25 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$ (polymer-rich mortar) - $f_t$ (substrate tensile strength) = **3.0 MPa** - $E_c$ = **28,000 MPa** **Step 1 — Differential CTE.** $$\Delta CTE = \vert CTE_{repair} - CTE_{substrate} \vert$$ $$\Delta CTE = \vert 25 - 10 \vert \times 10^{-6} = 15 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ C$$ **Step 2 — Free thermal strain per °C of temperature change.** $$\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta CTE \times \Delta T$$ **Step 3 — Equate restrained strain to tensile capacity.** $$\varepsilon_{th} \le \dfrac{f_t}{E_c}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta T$.** $$\Delta T \le \dfrac{f_t}{E_c \cdot \Delta CTE}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.** $$\Delta T_{crit} = \dfrac{3.0}{28{,}000 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta T_{crit} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.42}$$ $$\Delta T_{crit} = 7.14 ^\circ C$$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result.** > A temperature swing of more than **7°C** will cause the patch to de-bond. Hence, for outdoor repairs in Kerala (where diurnal temperature swing can be 8–10°C), a **CTE-matched** repair material is essential. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix — Choosing the Right Repair Material | Distress Type | Cause | Recommended Material | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Honeycomb, Spalling | Poor construction | Polymer-Modified Mortar | High bond, low shrinkage | | Corrosion-induced cracking | Carbonation / Chloride | Cementitious + Anti-corrosive primer + PMM | Protects rebar, restores cover | | Active cracks (live, $> 0.3$ mm) | Thermal / structural movement | Polyurethane injection | Elastomeric, accommodates movement | | Dormant cracks (static) | Shrinkage, settlement | Epoxy injection | High strength, rigid bond | | Chemical attack (acid) | Industrial exposure | Vinyl Ester / Furan mortar | Chemical resistance | | Seawater exposure | Chloride ingress | GGBS + Micro-silica mortar | Low permeability, chloride binding | | Mass concrete / thick patches | Heat of hydration | GGBS + Fly ash + Retarder | Low heat evolution | | Emergency / winter repair | Low temperature | Magnesium Phosphate Cement | Fast set, low-temp cure | | Waterproofing of terrace | Leakage | Polyurethane / Acrylic coating | Elastomeric, seamless | | Heritage masonry | Lime-based distress | Lime-pozzolana mortar | Compatible with heritage fabric | --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Mix Design Logic (Polymer-Modified Mortar) A typical mix proportion for a structural PMM patch: | Component | Quantity (per m³) | Function | |---|---|---| | OPC 53 Grade | 500 kg | Primary binder | | Silica Fume | 50 kg (10%) | Pozzolanic densifier | | Well-graded sand ( $< 4.75$ mm) | 1250 kg | Fine aggregate | | SBR Polymer (50% solids) | 100 kg (20% of cement wt) | Flexibility + bond | | Superplasticizer | 5 L | Workability at low w/c | | Water | 130 L | Low w/c = 0.26 | | Defoamer (anti-foam agent) | 1 L | Removes entrapped air from polymer | **Key Observation:** The w/c of **0.26** is only possible with the combined action of SBR (which carries water internally) and superplasticizer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Classification of Repair Materials (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[REPAIR MATERIALS] --> B[CEMENTITIOUS BASED] A --> C[POLYMER MODIFIED] A --> D[PURE POLYMERIC RESINS] A --> E[SPECIALTY MATERIALS] B --> B1[OPC Mortar and Concrete] B --> B2[Shrinkage Compensating Cement] B --> B3[Micro Silica Modified] B --> B4[GGBS Based] C --> C1[SBR Latex Modified] C --> C2[Acrylic Modified] C --> C3[Epoxy Modified Cement] C --> C4[Fiber Reinforced Cementitious] D --> D1[Epoxy Resin] D --> D2[Polyester Resin] D --> D3[Polyurethane Resin] D --> D4[Acrylic and Methacrylate] E --> E1[Ferrocement] E --> E2[Geopolymer] E --> E3[Magnesium Phosphate Cement] E --> E4[Chemical Resistant Mortar] E --> E5[Anti Corrosion Coatings] C4 --> C4A[Steel Fiber] C4 --> C4B[Polypropylene Fiber] C4 --> C4C[Glass Fiber GRC] C4 --> C4D[Carbon Fiber] ``` ### 4.2 Selection Logic Diagram (Decision Tree for Material Choice) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[START Diagnosis of Distress] --> S1{Is the crack
active or dormant?} S1 -->|Active Live| S2[Use POLYURETHANE
Flexible Injection] S1 -->|Dormant Static| S3{Crack width?} S3 -->|Less than 0.1 mm| S4[Use METHACRYLATE
Low Viscosity Resin] S3 -->|0.1 to 2 mm| S5[Use EPOXY
Structural Injection] S3 -->|Greater than 2 mm| S6[Use EPOXY PLUS
Non Shrink Grout] S0 --> P0{Spalling or Cover Loss?} P0 -->|Yes| P1{Is there active
rebar corrosion?} P1 -->|Yes| P2[Anti Corrosion Primer
PLUS Polymer Modified Mortar] P1 -->|No| P3[Polymer Modified
Mortar with Bonding Agent] P0 -->|No| Q0{Chemical Exposure?} Q0 -->|Acid| Q1[Vinyl Ester or Furan Resin] Q0 -->|Sulfate| Q2[GGBS and Micro Silica Mortar] Q0 -->|Chloride| Q3[Polymer Modified plus
Anti Corrosion Coating] Q0 -->|None| Q4[Standard Cementitious
Repair Mortar] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Repair Material System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PREP[Surface Preparation] P1[Substrate Cleaning] P2[Concrete Removal to Sound Concrete] P3[Rebar Cleaning and Priming] end subgraph PRIME[Priming Layer] PR1[Bonding Agent Epoxy or SBR] end subgraph REPAIR[Repair Layer] R1[Polymer Modified Mortar] R2[Steel or Polypropylene Fibers] R3[Anti Corrosion Migrating Inhibitor] end subgraph PROTECT[Protection Layer] PT1[Sealer or Anti Carbonation Coating] PT2[Curing Compound] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> PR1 PR1 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> PT1 PT1 --> PT2 ``` ### 4.4 Polymer Modification Mechanism (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD M0[Fresh Cement Mortar Mix] --> M1[Polymer Dispersion in Water] M1 --> M2[Cement Hydration Begins] M2 --> M3[Polymer Particles Migrate to Pore Walls] M3 --> M4[Water Evaporation and Capillary Suction] M4 --> M5[Polymer Films Form Continuous Network] M5 --> M6[Interlocking with C-S-H Gel] M6 --> M7[Final Composite Dense Strong and Flexible] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question]** **Q1. Differentiate between Polymer-Modified Cementitious Mortar (PMM) and Pure Epoxy Mortar as repair materials. List three advantages and two limitations of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | PMM | Epoxy Mortar | |---|---|---| | Binder | OPC + Polymer latex (SBR/Acrylic) | Pure epoxy resin + amine hardener | | Strength Development | Hydration (7–28 days) | Chemical cure (24–72 hours) | | Thickness Limit | Up to 75 mm in one layer | Best $< 25$ mm (heat dissipation issues) | | Moisture Tolerance | Good (cement requires water) | Poor (amine blushing) | | Cost | Moderate | High | | UV Resistance | Good | Poor (chalking) | **3 Advantages of PMM:** 1. Compatible modulus with parent concrete. 2. Allows moisture vapor transmission. 3. Lower cost; uses cement as base. **2 Advantages of Epoxy Mortar:** 1. Very high early strength. 2. Excellent chemical resistance. **2 Limitations of Epoxy Mortar:** 1. Brittle; high modulus mismatch with concrete. 2. Cannot be applied on wet substrates. --- **Q2. What are the ideal requirements of a repair material as per IS 15912? Mention any four.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four ideal requirements are: 1. **Bond Strength** — Should exceed $1.7$ MPa (pull-off) so that the repair is **monolithic** with the substrate. 2. **Dimensional Stability** — Drying shrinkage should be less than $0.04\%$ at 28 days to avoid de-bonding. 3. **Compressive Strength** — Equal to or greater than the parent concrete, with a recommended margin of $+5$ MPa. 4. **Chemical Compatibility** — The repair material should not introduce alkalis, chlorides, or sulfates that would harm the substrate or rebar. *(Other valid points: modulus compatibility, thermal compatibility, freeze-thaw resistance, workability/pot life.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **(a)** Classify the various **repair materials** used in concrete rehabilitation. Explain the working mechanism and applications of **Polymer-Modified Cementitious Mortars (PMCM)**. Discuss why the polymer-to-cement (P/C) ratio is critical. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Classification of Repair Materials:** ``` 1. Cementitious Systems - Plain OPC mortars - Shrinkage-compensating cement - Micro-silica / GGBS / Fly ash modified 2. Polymer-Modified Cementitious - SBR / Acrylic / Epoxy-modified - Fiber-reinforced cementitious composites (FRCC) 3. Purely Polymeric Resins - Epoxy, Polyester, Polyurethane, Methacrylate 4. Specialty Systems - Ferrocement, Geopolymer, Magnesium Phosphate Cement - Chemical-resistant mortars (Furan, Vinyl Ester) - Anti-corrosion primers and migrating inhibitors ``` **Working Mechanism of PMCM (Co-film formation theory):** 1. Polymer latex is added during mixing; it is dispersed in water. 2. As cement hydrates, water is consumed and the polymer particles concentrate in the capillary pores. 3. Water evaporation and capillary pressure coalesce the polymer particles into a **continuous film**. 4. This polymer film **intertwines with the C–S–H gel**, forming a co-matrix. 5. The result is a **dense, low-permeability, high-bond, and more flexible** mortar. **Critical Role of P/C Ratio:** | P/C Ratio | Effect | |---|---| | $< 5\%$ | Insufficient polymer; brittle, low bond | | $10$–$20\%$ | **Optimum** for most structural repairs | | $> 25\%$ | Excessive polymer; high cost, reduced stiffness, moisture sensitivity | **Applications:** Patch repairs, structural overlays, rebar cover restoration, bridge deck repairs, water tank linings. --- **(b)** What are the **selection criteria** for repair materials? Describe how compatibility of **modulus of elasticity, thermal expansion, and shrinkage** influence the choice. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** The **selection of repair material is governed by**: 1. **Type and extent of distress** (active vs dormant, structural vs non-structural). 2. **Exposure conditions** (chloride, sulfate, chemical, freeze-thaw). 3. **Substrate condition** (sound concrete, moisture content, surface roughness). 4. **Performance requirements** (strength, bond, durability). 5. **Constructability constraints** (pot life, application thickness, weather). 6. **Cost and life-cycle economics**. **Modulus of Elasticity (E) Compatibility:** If $E_{repair} \gg E_{substrate}$, the repair will attract more stress under load and may **crack or de-bond at the interface**. A **mismatch of more than $25\%$** is considered unacceptable. IS 456 recommends an $E$ ratio of $0.8$ to $1.25$. **Thermal Expansion (CTE) Compatibility:** Concrete CTE $\approx 7-12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$. Pure polymers can have CTE up to $60 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$. A **differential CTE of $> 2 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$** causes cyclic shear at the interface, leading to progressive de-bonding. **Shrinkage Compatibility:** Drying shrinkage of the repair mortar must be **minimized** because any shrinkage will induce **tensile stresses at the interface**. The rule of thumb is: $$\varepsilon_{sh,repair} \le 0.6 \times \varepsilon_{sh,substrate}$$ This is achieved by using: - Low w/c ratio - Shrinkage-reducing admixtures (SRA) - SBR/acrylic polymer modification - Pre-bagged, pre-mixed formulations - Proper curing --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **(a)** Explain the **properties, mechanism, and applications of Epoxy Resins** as a repair material. Compare epoxy with polyurethane and methacrylate resins. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **Epoxy Resins — Mechanism:** - Epoxy is a **two-component thermosetting system**: Resin (diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A — DGEBA) + Hardener (amines, polyamides, or anhydrides). - On mixing, the **epoxide ring opens** and undergoes step-growth polymerization forming a **3-D cross-linked network**. - The cured resin is **rigid, strong, and chemically resistant**. **Key Properties:** - Tensile strength: $50$–$90$ MPa - Compressive strength: $80$–$150$ MPa - Bond strength to concrete: $3$–$7$ MPa - Low shrinkage ($< 0.1\%$, primarily thermal) - Excellent chemical resistance to alkalis, oils, and most solvents - Poor UV resistance; high glass transition temperature ($T_g = 60$–$80^\circ C$) **Applications of Epoxy in Repair:** - **Crack injection** (structural crack repair) - **Bonding agent** between old and new concrete - **Patch repair** of spalled concrete (epoxy mortar) - **Rebar coating / anti-corrosion primer** - **FRP strengthening** (resin matrix for CFRP/GFRP) **Comparison Table:** | Property | Epoxy | Polyurethane | Methacrylate | |---|---|---|---| | Tensile Strength (MPa) | $50$–$90$ | $20$–$60$ | $30$–$70$ | | Elongation (%) | $1$–$8$ (rigid) | $100$–$600$ (flexible) | $1$–$10$ | | Viscosity (cP) | $100$–$10000$ | $500$–$5000$ | $1$–$100$ | | Best Use | Structural cracks, bonding | Live cracks, waterproofing | Hairline cracks ($< 0.1$ mm) | | Cure Time | $6$–$24$ h | $1$–$4$ h | $30$ min – $2$ h | --- **(b)** Discuss the use of **Ferrocement, Geopolymer, and Magnesium Phosphate Cement** as specialty repair materials. Highlight their unique advantages and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **1. Ferrocement:** - **Composition:** Cement mortar (1:2 to 1:3) reinforced with **multiple layers of wire mesh** (chicken mesh, weld mesh) and sometimes skeletal steel. - **Thickness:** $10$–$25$ mm. - **Advantages:** High tensile strength, crack-resistant, no need for formwork in many cases, suitable for complex shapes. - **Limitations:** Labour-intensive mesh placement, requires skilled labour, corrosion of mesh if cover is inadequate. - **Applications:** Water tank linings, swimming pools, column jacketing, rural housing, ductile overlays. **2. Geopolymer Repair Material:** - **Composition:** Alkali-activated **fly ash, GGBS, or metakaolin** with alkali activators (NaOH, $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3$). - **Advantages:** Eco-friendly (no OPC, up to $80\%$ lower $\text{CO}_2$), excellent fire resistance ($> 1000^\circ C$), superior chemical and sulfate resistance, low shrinkage. - **Limitations:** Alkali activator handling risk (corrosive), need for heat curing in some systems, limited field data on long-term durability. - **Applications:** Industrial floors, marine structures, fire-damaged repairs, sustainable construction. **3. Magnesium Phosphate Cement (MPC):** - **Composition:** Dead-burned magnesia + soluble phosphate + retarder. - **Advantages:** **Rapid setting** ($10$–$30$ min), high early strength ($> 30$ MPa in 1 hour), excellent bond to old concrete, low temperature curing, repair of airfield and highway pavements. - **Limitations:** **High cost**, short working time, requires strict mix control, may cause efflorescence in humid conditions. - **Applications:** Runways, highway patches, emergency repairs, cold-weather concreting. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list many materials but **fail to justify the choice**. Always write **"this material is suitable because…"** and quote at least one **quantitative property** (strength, bond, shrinkage value, or exposure test result). Marks are heavily docked for generic statements like *"epoxy is strong and good for repair"* without specifying the application, mix ratio, or performance number. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Night Revision):** - ✅ Repair material = **engineered composite**, NOT ordinary concrete. - ✅ Classification: **Cementitious, Polymer-Modified, Pure Polymer, Specialty**. - ✅ **Most important cementitious additives:** Silica fume, GGBS, Fly ash, Metakaolin. - ✅ **Most common polymer modifiers:** SBR latex, Acrylic, Redispersible powder. - ✅ **PMM ideal P/C ratio:** $10$–$20\%$ by weight of cement. - ✅ **Epoxy:** Two-component (resin + hardener); best for **rigid structural** repair. - ✅ **Polyurethane:** Best for **flexible / live crack** injection. - ✅ **Methacrylate:** Lowest viscosity — for **hairline cracks ($< 0.1$ mm)**. - ✅ **Pull-off bond strength** minimum: **$1.7$ MPa** (structural repair per IS 15912). - ✅ **Drying shrinkage of patch** should be **$< 0.04\%$** at 28 days. - ✅ **Modulus of elasticity** match: $\vert E_{repair}/E_{substrate} \vert \le 1.25$. - ✅ **CTE mismatch** must be **$< 2 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$**. - ✅ **Silica fume effectiveness factor (k)** in effective w/c: usually **3 to 4**. - ✅ **Ferrocement:** Wire mesh + mortar; thickness $10$–$25$ mm. - ✅ **Geopolymer:** Alkali-activated binder; zero-OPC, eco-friendly. - ✅ **Magnesium Phosphate Cement:** Sets in $10$–$30$ min; emergency/road repairs. - ✅ **Anti-corrosion coatings:** Migrating corrosion inhibitors (MCI), zinc-rich primers, epoxy-phenolic. - ✅ **KTU Favourite Questions:** "Classify repair materials", "Differentiate PMM and epoxy", "Selection criteria with compatibility". - ✅ **Always** mention **IS 15912** and **ACI 546R** for KTU answers. - ✅ **For numerical problems**, remember: $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$, $\sigma = F/A$. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Repair Materials: Various Repair Materials** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PECET634*

topics.title: Criteria for Material Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Criteria for Material Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Selection Criteria** in the context of Repair and Rehabilitation (R&R) of structures refers to the systematic, multi-attribute decision-making process used to identify the most technically appropriate, economically viable, and durably compatible repair material for restoring or upgrading an existing structural element, while ensuring long-term serviceability, dimensional conformity, and structural integrity of the parent (substrate) concrete/steel/masonry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):** The selection is governed by the fundamental principle that the repair material must not only restore the original function but must also behave as a *co-engineered partner* with the substrate throughout its design life.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor treating a fractured bone. The orthopaedic surgeon cannot simply use a *stronger* metal plate that is 10 times stiffer than the bone — the bone would simply atrophy and the joint would fail. The doctor selects a plate based on:
- **Stiffness match** (biocompatibility)
- **Bonding** with the body (osseointegration)
- **Corrosion resistance** to bodily fluids
- **Long-term fatigue performance**

Similarly, in structural repair, a repair material that is **too strong** or **too stiff** compared to the parent concrete is *not automatically better* — it is a clinical mismatch that will delaminate, crack, or spall prematurely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** *"A repair is only as good as the compatibility between the patch and the parent material."* — This is the single most tested concept in KTU board exams on this topic.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Material Selection (KTU 2024 Framework)

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups selection criteria into three primary pillars:

1. **Performance Criteria** (Mechanical & Physical Compatibility)
2. **Durability Criteria** (Environmental & Chemical Resistance)
3. **Constructability & Economic Criteria** (Workability, Availability, Cost)

**Highlighted Quantitative Benchmarks (Industry Standards):**
- **Modulus of Elasticity ratio (E_repair / E_substrate):** **0.85 – 1.10** (ideal range)
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) match:** within **±1.0 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** of substrate
- **Minimum bond strength (slant shear):** **≥ 2.0 MPa** at 28 days (as per ACI 546R)
- **Shrinkage (drying) limit:** **< 0.06%** for repair mortars
- **Permeability:** Repair material should have **equal or lower** permeability than substrate

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 12 Canonical Selection Criteria (KTU Board-Favourite List)

The repair material is evaluated against the following twelve canonical criteria. Examiners frequently frame 7-mark or 14-mark questions around any 5–7 of these:

#### A. Mechanical & Structural Compatibility
1. **Compressive Strength** — must meet or slightly exceed substrate strength (typical: **25–60 MPa** for structural repairs).
2. **Tensile / Flexural Strength** — critical for crack-bridging repairs.
3. **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** — must be **closely matched** to substrate to prevent differential stress concentrations.
4. **Bond Strength (Adhesion)** — adhesion to substrate is the *primary load-transfer mechanism*. Tested via:
   - Pull-off test (≥ **1.7 MPa** per EN 1542)
   - Slant shear test (≥ **2.0 MPa** per ASTM C882)

#### B. Dimensional & Deformational Compatibility
5. **Drying Shrinkage** — lower shrinkage = less internal restrained-stress = less delamination.
6. **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE / α)** — must match to prevent thermal cycling failures.
7. **Creep Behaviour** — repair material creep must be considered for sustained loads.

#### C. Durability & Environmental Resistance
8. **Permeability & Water Absorption** — must be **equal or lower** than substrate to prevent moisture ingress at the interface.
9. **Chemical Resistance** — resistance to chlorides, sulphates, acids (especially in industrial/marine environments).
10. **Freeze-Thaw Resistance** — critical in cold climates; tested as per ASTM C666.
11. **Carbonation & Chloride Resistance** — for reinforced concrete repairs.

#### D. Constructability & Serviceability
12. **Workability, Pot Life, Setting Time, Cost, Availability, Reversibility** — especially for heritage structures.

### 2.2 The "WHY" Behind Each Criterion (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Modulus of Elasticity (E) match critical?**
> When a composite beam (substrate + repair) is loaded, both materials must deform together (assuming perfect bond). If E differs significantly, **plane sections do NOT remain plane**, and the interface develops shear and normal stresses.
>
> **Why is CTE match critical?**
> Under thermal cycling (day/night, summer/winter), differential expansion/contraction generates interface shear stresses proportional to (α₁ - α₂)·ΔT·E. A 1% mismatch can cause delamination within 50–100 thermal cycles.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Condition | Symbol | Units | Acceptable Range |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Strain Compatibility Ratio** | $n = \dfrac{E_{repair}}{E_{substrate}}$ | $n$ | — | 0.85 – 1.10 |
| 2 | **Differential Thermal Stress** | $\sigma_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_{eff}$ | $\sigma_{th}$ | MPa | < 0.5 MPa (recommended) |
| 3 | **Effective Composite E** (parallel action) | $E_{eff} = \dfrac{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}{A_1 + A_2}$ | $E_{eff}$ | MPa | — |
| 4 | **Bond Stress Demand (shear)** | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{V \cdot S}{I \cdot b}$ | $\tau_{bond}$ | MPa | < τ_allowable |
| 5 | **Shrinkage Restraint Stress** | $\sigma_{sh} = \varepsilon_{sh} \cdot E_{repair} \cdot (1 - \phi)$ | $\sigma_{sh}$ | MPa | < f_t (tensile) |
| 6 | **Pull-off Bond Strength** | $f_b = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | $f_b$ | MPa | ≥ 1.7 MPa |
| 7 | **Permeability Ratio (ideal)** | $k_{repair} \leq k_{substrate}$ | $k$ | m/s | ≤ 1.0 |
| 8 | **Slant Shear Bond** | $f_{ss} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{slant}}$ | $f_{ss}$ | MPa | ≥ 2.0 MPa |
| 9 | **CTE mismatch index** | $\Delta \alpha = \vert \alpha_{repair} - \alpha_{substrate} \vert$ | $\Delta \alpha$ | /°C | ≤ 1×10⁻⁶ |
| 10 | **Interfacial Shear due to differential shrinkage** | $\tau_i = \dfrac{G \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_{sh}}{t}$ | $\tau_i$ | MPa | < 0.3 MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\alpha$ = CTE, $\tau$ = shear stress, $\phi$ = creep relaxation factor (≈ 0.5 for cementitious), $G$ = Shear modulus, $t$ = repair thickness, $P$ = pull-off load, $d$ = dolly diameter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Deck Repairs** → low-shrinkage, low-permeability, chloride-resistant mortars (e.g., latex-modified or polymer-modified systems).
- **Heritage Restoration** → lime-based, reversible mortars with matched porosity and vapour permeability.
- **Marine Structures** → calcium aluminate cement or geopolymer systems for sulphate resistance.
- **Industrial Floors** → epoxy or polyurethane screeds for chemical abrasion resistance.
- **Seismic Upgrades** → Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) wraps, selected based on E-ratio and bond characteristics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Thermal Stress at the Repair Interface

Consider a repair patch bonded to a substrate. Both are subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$. Since they are constrained to deform together (perfect bond assumed), a shear stress develops at the interface.

**Step 1: Free thermal strain in each material (unrestrained):**
$$\varepsilon_{th,1} = \alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T$$
$$\varepsilon_{th,2} = \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2: Differential unrestrained strain (driving force for stress):**
$$\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3: Apply compatibility — the interface must remain continuous. The mismatch strain is shared between the two materials as elastic strain energy, generating a normal stress at the interface:**

$$\sigma_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_{eff}$$

where the effective modulus (for equal areas, plane strain case) is:

$$E_{eff} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$

**Step 4: Substituting:**

$$\sigma_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot \frac{E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$$

**Step 5: Condition for safe design:**

$$\sigma_{th} \leq f_{t,interface}$$

If the CTE mismatch $(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$ exceeds **$1 \times 10^{-6} /°C$**, microcracking initiates at the interface within 50–100 thermal cycles.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shrinkage-Induced Interfacial Shear Stress

A cementitious repair shrinks by an amount $\varepsilon_{sh}$ after casting. Since it is bonded to the rigid substrate, this shrinkage is restrained, producing tensile stress in the repair and shear at the interface.

**Step 1: Free shrinkage strain in repair (unrestrained):**
$$\varepsilon_{sh,free} = \varepsilon_{sh}$$

**Step 2: Restrained shrinkage strain (after bond and creep relaxation):**
$$\varepsilon_{sh,r} = \varepsilon_{sh} \cdot (1 - \phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the creep relaxation coefficient (≈ **0.5** for typical cementitious repair).

**Step 3: Tensile stress generated in the repair layer:**
$$\sigma_{t,repair} = \varepsilon_{sh,r} \cdot E_{repair} = \varepsilon_{sh} \cdot (1 - \phi) \cdot E_{repair}$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium at the repair-substrate interface (force balance per unit length):**

The tensile force in the repair must be transferred to the substrate via interface shear:

$$\tau_i \cdot b = \sigma_{t,repair} \cdot t \cdot b$$

**Step 5: Solving for interface shear stress:**
$$\tau_i = \frac{\sigma_{t,repair} \cdot t}{L} = \frac{\varepsilon_{sh} \cdot (1 - \phi) \cdot E_{repair} \cdot t}{L}$$

where $L$ is the bonded length and $t$ is the repair thickness.

**Step 6: Design check:**
$$\tau_i \leq \tau_{allowable} \approx 0.3 \text{ MPa (for cementitious repair on concrete)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** This equation reveals that increasing repair *thickness* $t$ dramatically increases the risk of debonding. Most KTU examiners expect students to identify that **thick repairs demand low-shrinkage materials**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Compatibility Matrix (Decision Logic)

A systematic selection matrix (used in professional practice and examinable in 14-mark questions):

| Criterion | Weight (%) | Repair Material A (Epoxy Mortar) | Repair Material B (Polymer-Modified Cementitious) | Repair Material C (OPC Mortar) |
|-----------|------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Compressive Strength | 10 | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| E-modulus match | 15 | ★★ (too high) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Bond to substrate | 20 | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Drying shrinkage | 15 | ★★★★★ (≈0) | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| CTE match | 10 | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Permeability | 10 | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| Chemical resistance | 10 | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| Workability & Cost | 10 | ★★ (costly) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Weighted Score** | **100** | **3.65** | **4.00** | **3.05** |

→ **Material B (Polymer-modified cementitious)** emerges as the optimal choice for general concrete repair (high E-match, low shrinkage, balanced cost).

---

### 3.4 Strain Compatibility in a Repaired Beam Section

For a flexural member repaired on the tension face, plane-sections must remain plane. The strain at the extreme fibre of the repair must equal that of the substrate at the same depth.

$$\varepsilon_{substrate}(y) = \varepsilon_{repair}(y) = \frac{y}{R}$$

where $R$ is the radius of curvature. The corresponding stress in each material:

$$\sigma_i(y) = E_i \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$

The total moment resistance:
$$M = \int_A \sigma(y) \cdot y \cdot dA = \frac{1}{R} \int_A E(y) \cdot y^2 \cdot dA = \frac{EI_{eff}}{R}$$

This leads to the **transformed section concept**, where the repair area is transformed using the modular ratio $n = E_{repair} / E_{substrate}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Selection Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Deterioration Type] --> B{Substrate Material?}
    B -- Concrete --> C[Concrete Repair Path]
    B -- Steel --> D[Steel Repair Path]
    B -- Masonry --> E[Masonry Repair Path]
    C --> F[Assess Load Demand]
    F --> G[Structural vs Non-Structural?]
    G -- Structural --> H[Apply 12 Selection Criteria]
    G -- Non-Structural --> I[Apply Constructability & Cost Criteria]
    H --> J[Compute E-ratio n]
    J --> K{n between 0.85 and 1.10?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Proceed to CTE Check]
    K -- No --> M[Reject or Use Transition Layer]
    L --> N{CTE Mismatch below 1e-6?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Evaluate Bond Strength]
    N -- No --> P[Add Flexible Coupling Layer]
    O --> Q{Bond above 1.7 MPa?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Final Selection Approved]
    Q -- No --> S[Use Primer or Coupling Agent]
    I --> T[Final Selection Approved]
    R --> U[Document and Execute]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    P --> U
    M --> U
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Mapping (Why Bad Selections Fail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[High E Repair] --> A2[Stress Concentration at Interface] --> A3[Debonding]
    B1[High Shrinkage] --> B2[Tensile Stress in Repair] --> B3[Map Cracking]
    C1[CTE Mismatch] --> C2[Thermal Cycling Fatigue] --> C3[Delamination]
    D1[High Permeability] --> D2[Moisture Ingress at Joint] --> D3[Re-Corrosion]
    E1[Chemical Incompatibility] --> E2[Alkali-Aggregate or Acid Attack] --> E3[Spalling]
    F1[Poor Bond] --> F2[Loss of Composite Action] --> F3[Repair Dislodgement]
```
