
### 4.3 Stress–Strain Behaviour: Plain Concrete vs. FRC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P["PLAIN CONCRETE - BRITTLE FAILURE"]
        P1["Linear Elastic Up to f_t = 3 to 5 MPa"] --> P2["Sudden Brittle Failure - No Post-Cracking Capacity"]
    end

    subgraph F["FRC - DUCTILE FAILURE"]
        F1["Linear Elastic Up to First Crack"] --> F2["Multiple Cracking Phase - Strain Hardening"] --> F3["Crack Localisation - Strain Softening"] --> F4["Fibre Pull-Out - Gradual Energy Absorption"]
    end

    P --> X["AREA UNDER CURVE = FRACTURE ENERGY G_F"]
    F --> X

    style P fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000
    style F fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
    style X fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** When asked to draw stress–strain curve, always label: (1) **First-crack strength**, (2) **Multiple cracking plateau**, (3) **Post-cracking residual strength**, and (4) **Toughness = area under the curve**. These four labels carry 4 of the 7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Fibre Reinforced Concrete (FRC). List any four types of fibres commonly used in FRC.**

**Model Answer:**

Fibre Reinforced Concrete is a composite material made of cement, fine and coarse aggregates, water, and short discrete fibres uniformly dispersed throughout the matrix to improve its tensile strength, ductility, crack resistance, and impact resistance.

Four types of fibres used in FRC are:
1. **Steel fibres** — high modulus, used for structural applications.
2. **Polypropylene fibres** — control plastic shrinkage cracks, resist spalling.
3. **Glass fibres (AR-glass)** — used in architectural panels and GRC products.
4. **Carbon fibres** — high strength, used in advanced composites and retrofitting.

*(Any other valid fibre type such as asbestos, PVA, aramid, nylon, or natural fibres also accepted.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is meant by the "aspect ratio" of fibres, and why is it important in FRC design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **aspect ratio** of a fibre is defined as the ratio of its length to its equivalent diameter:

$$\text{Aspect Ratio} = \frac{l_f}{d_f}$$

**Importance in FRC design:**

1. **Bond development:** Higher aspect ratio provides greater embedment length, improving fibre–matrix bond and pull-out resistance.
2. **Strength contribution:** Tensile strength contribution of fibres is directly proportional to aspect ratio: $\sigma_{ct,FRC} \propto \tau_i \cdot V_f \cdot (l_f/d_f)$.
3. **Workability trade-off:** Very high aspect ratio (>150) causes fibre balling and poor workability. Optimal range is **40–100** for steel fibres and **100–900** for polymeric fibres.
4. **Crack control:** Smaller fibre spacing (achieved by higher aspect ratio) arrests micro-cracks more effectively (per Romualdi–Batson equation). **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of crack control in FRC. With neat sketches, describe how fibres arrest crack propagation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel fibre of diameter **0.6 mm** and ultimate tensile strength of **1800 MPa** is embedded in a concrete matrix. The interfacial bond strength is **5 MPa**. Determine:
1. The critical fibre length $l_c$.
2. Whether a fibre of length **35 mm** will fail by pull-out or fracture.
3. The pull-out load capacity of a single fibre of 35 mm length. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

The mechanism of crack control in FRC operates in **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Pre-cracking (Elastic Range):**
Both matrix and fibres deform elastically. Stress is shared between matrix and fibres according to their stiffness (modulus) and volume fraction. Fibres, being stiffer, attract a higher share of tensile stress.

**Stage 2 — Multiple Micro-cracking:**
When matrix tensile stress reaches first-crack strength (~3–5 MPa), microcracks initiate. Fibres crossing the crack plane carry the load through **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ developed along their embedded length. The crack is **bridged** and does not propagate unstably.

**Stage 3 — Crack Bridging and Pull-out:**
For fibres shorter than critical length ($l_f < l_c$), failure occurs by **gradual pull-out** with frictional slip and mechanical anchorage (especially for hooked-end fibres). This produces **post-cracking ductility** and absorbs large amounts of fracture energy.

**Sketch Description (to be drawn in exam):**
- Show a horizontal concrete element with vertical tensile crack.
- 5–6 fibres drawn as inclined straight lines crossing the crack at random angles.
- Label "Interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$" along embedded lengths.
- Label "Tensile stress $\sigma_{fu}$" at the crack plane in the fibre.
- Arrows indicating fibre pull-out direction.

**Key result:** Fibres transform a **brittle** failure into a **ductile** failure, with energy absorption increasing by **10–40 times** compared to plain concrete. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given Data:**
- $d_f = 0.6 \text{ mm}$
- $\sigma_{fu} = 1800 \text{ MPa}$
- $\tau_i = 5 \text{ MPa}$
- $l_f = 35 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Critical Fibre Length $l_c$:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d_f}{4 \cdot \tau_i}$$

$$l_c = \frac{1800 \times 0.6}{4 \times 5} = \frac{1080}{20} = 54 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare $l_f$ and $l_c$:**
- $l_f = 35 \text{ mm} < l_c = 54 \text{ mm}$
- Therefore, fibre will fail by **pull-out** (ductile failure). ✓

**Step 3 — Calculate Pull-out Load Capacity:**
Assuming embedded length on pull-out side is $l_f / 2 = 17.5 \text{ mm}$ (fibres embedded symmetrically across crack):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{pull-out} &= \tau_i \cdot \pi \cdot d_f \cdot (l_f / 2) \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times \pi \times 0.6 \times 17.5 \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times 3.1416 \times 10.5 \\[4pt]
&= 164.93 \text{ N} \approx 165 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

(For a fibre pulled from one end with full length engaged, the answer becomes $F = 5 \times \pi \times 0.6 \times 35 = 329.87 \text{ N}$.)

**Final Result:** $l_c = 54 \text{ mm}$, Failure mode = **Pull-out** (Ductile), $F_{pull-out} \approx 165 \text{ N}$ per fibre. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Classify the various types of fibres used in FRC. Explain the properties and applications of steel fibres and polypropylene fibres in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on:
1. Fibre spacing concept (Romualdi–Batson equation).
2. Toughness and post-cracking behaviour of FRC.
3. Any three advantages of FRC over plain concrete. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Classification of Fibres:**

**1. Based on Material:**
- **Metallic:** Steel, Stainless steel, Carbon steel.
- **Polymeric/Organic:** Polypropylene (PP), Nylon, Polyester, PVA, Aramid (Kevlar).
- **Mineral/Inorganic:** Glass (E-glass, AR-glass), Asbestos, Carbon.
- **Natural:** Jute, Coir, Sisal, Bamboo, Flax, Hemp.

**2. Based on Geometry:**
- Straight, Hooked-end, Crimped, Indented, Twisted, Flat-end/Paddle.

---

**Steel Fibres:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 300 – 2500 MPa |
| Young's Modulus | 200 GPa |
| Density | 7850 kg/m³ |
| Aspect Ratio | 50 – 100 |
| Shape | Hooked-end most common |
| Corrosion | Mild; stainless steel for aggressive environments |

**Applications:**
- Industrial floor slabs and pavements.
- Tunnel linings and shotcrete (replacing mesh).
- Bridge deck overlays.
- Refractory and blast-resistant structures.
- Pre-cast products and manhole covers.

---

**Polypropylene Fibres:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 300 – 700 MPa |
| Young's Modulus | 3.5 – 10 GPa |
| Density | 910 kg/m³ |
| Aspect Ratio | 200 – 900 (mono-filament) |
| Shape | Mono-filament, Fibrillated, Multi-filament |
| Chemical Resistance | Excellent (alkali/acid resistant) |

**Applications:**
- Control of **plastic shrinkage cracks** in slabs and screeds.
- **Spalling resistance** in fire-exposed concrete (PP fibres melt at ~160°C, creating escape channels for steam).
- Secondary reinforcement in water-retaining structures, tanks, and swimming pools.
- Lightweight and corrosion-free applications. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**1. Fibre Spacing Concept (Romualdi–Batson Equation):**

Romualdi and Batson (1963) proposed that fibre spacing is the **critical parameter** governing crack arrest in FRC. Randomly distributed fibres divide the matrix into small blocks. A crack will be arrested when fibre spacing $S$ is less than a critical value related to the matrix fracture properties.

$$S = 13.8 \cdot d_f \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{V_f}}$$

- $S$ = average fibre spacing (mm)
- $d_f$ = fibre diameter (mm)
- $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction

**Inference:** Higher $V_f$ and smaller $d_f$ → smaller fibre spacing → better crack arrest. **[2 Marks]**

---

**2. Toughness and Post-Cracking Behaviour of FRC:**

**Toughness** is the ability of a material to absorb energy up to failure, measured as the area under the load–deflection curve. FRC dramatically improves toughness through:

- **Multiple cracking** — many fine cracks form instead of one wide crack.
- **Strain hardening** — load continues to rise after first crack (in high-performance FRC/ECC).
- **Strain softening** — gradual load decay due to progressive fibre pull-out.
- **ASTM C1018 Toughness Indices** $I_5, I_{10}, I_{30}$ — ratio of areas up to specific deflections.

Typical toughness improvement: **10× to 40×** that of plain concrete. **[3 Marks]**

---

**3. Three Advantages of FRC over Plain Concrete:**

1. **Crack Control:** FRC reduces crack widths to less than 0.1 mm, improving durability and reducing permeability to water and chlorides.
2. **Impact and Fatigue Resistance:** Energy absorption capacity is 5–10× higher than plain concrete, ideal for industrial floors and machine foundations.
3. **Reduced Construction Time:** Eliminates or reduces conventional rebar mesh placement in slabs, shotcrete, and overlays, accelerating construction. **[2 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $l_c$ formula versions** — Some textbooks use $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ (double pull-out side) and others $\dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{4 \tau_i}$ (single side). **Always state the assumption** in your solution.
> 2. **Writing "fibre increases compressive strength"** — This is **wrong**. Fibres have negligible effect on compressive strength; they mainly improve **tensile, flexural, impact, and toughness**. Examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up aspect ratio with length alone** — Aspect ratio is **$l_f/d_f$**, not just $l_f$.
> 4. **Not drawing sketches in part (a)** — For 7-mark questions on mechanism, **a labelled sketch is mandatory**. Without it, expect a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting efficiency factors** — When applying the rule of mixtures to **randomly oriented short fibres**, the formula **must** include $\eta_l \cdot \eta_o$. Writing it without these factors loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Steel fibre corrosion question trap** — If asked "are steel fibres corrosion-safe?", the correct answer is: **Carbon steel fibres can corrode; use stainless or galvanized fibres, or non-metallic fibres for aggressive environments.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FRC (Module 3, PECET634)**

- ✅ **FRC** = concrete + **discontinuous, short, randomly dispersed** fibres.
- ✅ **Three Golden Ratios:** Aspect ratio ($l_f/d_f$), Volume fraction ($V_f$), Bond efficiency ($\tau_i \cdot l_f/d_f$).
- ✅ **Critical fibre length:** $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d_f}{4 \tau_i}$ — design for **pull-out**, not fracture.
- ✅ **Fibre spacing (Romualdi–Batson):** $S = 13.8 d_f \sqrt{1/V_f}$ — smaller spacing = better crack arrest.
- ✅ **Composite strength (Rule of Mixtures):** $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu}(1 - V_f) + \eta_l \cdot \eta_o \cdot \sigma_{fu} \cdot V_f$.
- ✅ **Tensile contribution:** $\sigma_{ct,FRC} = \eta_o \cdot \tau_i \cdot V_f \cdot (l_f/d_f)$.
- ✅ **Optimum aspect ratio:** Steel **50–100**, Polypropylene **200–900**, Glass **100–1000**.
- ✅ **Optimum volume fraction:** **0.5% to 2.0%** (typical); >2% causes balling.
- ✅ **Steel fibres** = highest modulus, structural use; **PP fibres** = plastic shrinkage + fire-spalling control.
- ✅ **FRC mainly improves:** Tensile, flexural, impact, fatigue, **toughness (10–40×)**.
- ✅ **FRC does NOT significantly improve:** Compressive strength and elastic modulus.
- ✅ **Failure modes:** Pull-out (ductile, desirable) vs. Fibre fracture (brittle, undesirable).
- ✅ **Key tests:** ASTM C1018 (toughness), ASTM C1399 (residual strength), EN 14488 (shotcrete).
- ✅ **Modern variants:** ECC (Engineered Cementitious Composites), HPFRCC, SIFCON (Slurry Infiltrated Fibre Concrete), SIMCON.
- ✅ **Repair/Retrofit application:** Bridge deck overlays, column jacketing with FRC, beam strengthening.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Strength Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High Strength Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH STRENGTH CONCRETE (HSC) — KTU Board Definition**
> *High Strength Concrete (HSC)* is a specialized class of structural concrete that exhibits a **compressive strength substantially higher than that of conventional normal-strength concrete (NSC)**. As per **ACI 363R-10** (the standard cited across KTU repair & rehabilitation modules), HSC is defined as concrete having a **28-day cylinder compressive strength ($f'_c$) of at least 50 MPa (≈ 7000 psi)**, or equivalently a cube compressive strength of about **65 MPa**. The **Indian Standard IS 456:2000** categorizes concrete of grade **M60 and above** as high strength concrete, while **IS 10262:2019** and **IRC:112-2020** extend the high-performance umbrella to grades up to M100 for special applications.

For the **KTU PECET634 (Repair & Rehabilitation)** context, HSC is especially relevant because:
- It forms the **substrate material** for many patch-repair mortars.
- It is used in **jacketing** of columns and beams during structural strengthening.
- It underpins **fiber-reinforced high-strength systems** used in seismic retrofitting.

> [!NOTE]
> **HSC vs. HPC — A Subtle Distinction Examiners Love**
> *High Strength Concrete (HSC)* is defined purely by **mechanical strength** (a single-parameter criterion), whereas *High Performance Concrete (HPC)* is defined by a **bundle of desired properties** — strength PLUS durability, toughness, workability, and long-term dimensional stability. All HSC can be considered HPC, but the reverse is **not** always true (e.g., a durable marine concrete with 40 MPa strength is HPC but not HSC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two **bricks of the same size** used to build a load-bearing wall:

- A **traditional concrete brick** is like a **sponge cake** — it has air pockets, large grainy crumbs (aggregate), and a soft binder (cement paste). It can carry a normal house-load (≈ 20–40 MPa), but crumbles if you stack a heavy bookshelf on it.
- A **High Strength Concrete brick** is like a **dense, well-packed chocolate brownie** — almost no air gaps, fine fillers (silica fume) squeezing between crumbs, and a chemical "glue" (superplasticizer) keeping everything fluid until it sets into a rock-hard mass.

The "magic" behind HSC is the same trick a baker uses: **eliminate voids, reduce water content, and pack particles densely at multiple size scales** (nano, micro, and macro).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Lower-bound $f'_c$ (cylinder) | **≥ 50 MPa (≈ 7000 psi)** | ACI 363R-10 |
| Lower-bound cube strength | **≥ 65 MPa** | IS 456 / IS 10262 |
| Typical water-to-cement (w/c) ratio | **0.25 – 0.35** | ACI 211.4R |
| Silica fume replacement | **5 % – 15 %** of cement mass | ACI 234R |
| Superplasticizer dosage | **1 % – 2 %** by mass of cement | IS 9103 |
| Fresh concrete slump | **150 – 200 mm** (with HRWR) | IS 1199 |
| Modulus of elasticity ($E_c$) | **30 – 40 GPa** | ACI 363R |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "50-65-35"**
> 50 MPa (cylinder) | 65 MPa (cube) | 0.35 (w/c upper limit). This triplet often appears as a one-line definition question (3 marks) in KTU Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituents of High Strength Concrete — The Three Pillars

The transition from NSC → HSC is governed by the simultaneous optimization of **three material pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Low Water-to-Binder Ratio (w/b)
- Reducing the w/b from the conventional **0.45–0.60** to **0.25–0.35** drastically lowers capillary porosity in the hardened paste.
- The capillary porosity ($\phi_{cap}$) is empirically related to w/b as:

$$\phi_{cap} \approx \frac{w/b - 0.36\,\alpha}{w/b + 0.32}$$

where $\alpha$ = degree of hydration (typically 0.7–0.9 at 28 days for HSC).

- **Why it matters:** Lower porosity → higher strength, lower permeability, better durability.

### Pillar 2 — Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs)
- **Silica Fume (SF):** Ultra-fine (≈ 0.1 μm) pozzolanic material. Reacts with portlandite (Ca(OH)$_2$) to form additional C-S-H gel, filling nano-pores.
- **Fly Ash (FA):** Class F fly ash improves long-term strength and reduces heat of hydration.
- **Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS):** Latent hydraulic binder that densifies the matrix.
- **Metakaolin:** Highly reactive alumino-silicate; refines pore structure.

### Pillar 3 — High-Range Water-Reducing Admixtures (HRWR / Superplasticizers)
- Polycarboxylate-Ether (PCE) based admixtures allow fluid mixing at very low w/b without increasing water content.
- Dosage: **0.8 % – 1.5 %** by mass of binder.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Strength Enhancement

The strength gain in HSC operates at **three length scales**:

| Length Scale | Mechanism | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Nano (< 100 nm)** | Pozzolanic reaction (SF + CH → C-S-H) | Densification of C-S-H gel |
| **Micro (100 nm – 100 μm)** | Reduced capillary porosity due to low w/b | Higher matrix strength |
| **Macro (> 100 μm)** | Strong ITZ (Interfacial Transition Zone) around aggregates | Higher aggregate-paste bond |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU Part A/B derivations. The notation `\vert` and `\mid` are used in place of the pipe character to keep the markdown table parser intact.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f'_c = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{w/b}\right)^{n}$ | Abrams' law generalization for HSC; $k$ ≈ 240 MPa, $n$ ≈ 2.5 – 3.0 | MPa |
| 2 | $E_c = 3320\sqrt{f'_c} + 6900$ | ACI 363R empirical relation (valid for 21 MPa $\leq f'_c \leq$ 83 MPa) | MPa |
| 3 | $f_t = 0.59\sqrt{f'_c}$ | Split tensile strength (ACI 363R) | MPa |
| 4 | $f_r = 0.94\sqrt{f'_c}$ | Modulus of rupture (ACI 363R) | MPa |
| 5 | $\phi_{cap} \approx \dfrac{w/b - 0.36\alpha}{w/b + 0.32}$ | Powers' capillary porosity model | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_c(t) = f_{c,28} \cdot \dfrac{\log(t)}{\log(28)} \cdot k_T$ | Strength-age relation (CEB-FIP), $k_T$ = temperature factor | MPa |
| 7 | $S_{ratio} = \dfrac{F_{HSC}}{F_{NSC}} \geq 2$ | Performance ratio: HSC gives $\geq$ 2× the load capacity of NSC for same section | — |
| 8 | $\sigma_{creep}(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot C(t,t_0)$ | Creep compliance function; HSC creeps **less** than NSC | $\mu\epsilon$/MPa |
| 9 | $f_{c,cube} \approx 1.20 \, f'_{c,cyl}$ | Cube-to-cylinder conversion factor (150 mm cube vs 150×300 mm cyl.) | — |
| 10 | $D_{HSC} = D_0 \cdot e^{k(w/b)}$ | Durability index; permeability $D$ scales exponentially with w/b | m²/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility (Repair & Rehabilitation Context)

1. **Column Jacketing** — HSC jackets (50–80 mm thick) on damaged RC columns restore and often exceed original load capacity. Used extensively in post-earthquake retrofitting (e.g., Bhuj 2001 reconstruction).
2. **Bridge Deck Overlays** — HSC with low permeability resists chloride ingress, prolonging the life of marine/urban bridges.
3. **High-Rise Column Construction** — HSC columns (M80–M120) reduce cross-section, increasing usable floor area.
4. **Patch Repair Mortars** — Pre-packed HSC mortars with polymers used for spalled concrete restoration.
5. **Precast Pre-Stressed Elements** — HSC allows higher pre-stressing forces and longer spans.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Abrams' Strength Law Adapted for HSC

The classical **Abrams' law (1918)** states that the strength of concrete is inversely proportional to the water-cement ratio. For HSC, the law is extended to use the **water-to-binder ratio (w/b)** to account for SCMs.

### Starting Form

$$f'_c = \frac{k_1}{k_2^{w/b}}$$

### Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides
This converts the exponential into a linear expression suitable for regression.

$$\ln(f'_c) = \ln(k_1) - (w/b)\ln(k_2)$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the linear form
Let $A = \ln(k_1)$ (intercept) and $B = -\ln(k_2)$ (slope). Then:

$$\ln(f'_c) = A - B \cdot (w/b)$$

### Step 3 — Power-law form
Exponentiating back:

$$f'_c = e^{A} \cdot e^{-B(w/b)} = k \cdot \left(\frac{1}{e^{B}}\right)^{w/b}$$

which gives the familiar **HSC version**:

$$\boxed{f'_c = k \cdot \left(\frac{1}{w/b}\right)^{n}}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- $k$ depends on cement type, curing conditions, and age. Empirically, $k \approx$ **240 MPa** for ordinary Portland cement at 28 days.
- $n$ depends on aggregate quality and SCM content. Typically $n \approx$ **2.5 – 3.0** for HSC.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A HSC mix is designed with w/b = 0.30. Given $k = 240$ MPa and $n = 2.8$, predict the 28-day compressive strength.

**Step 5a —** Substitute the values:

$$f'_c = 240 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.30}\right)^{2.8}$$

**Step 5b —** Compute the inverse:

$$\frac{1}{0.30} = 3.333$$

**Step 5c —** Raise to power 2.8:

$$3.333^{2.8} = e^{2.8 \cdot \ln(3.333)} = e^{2.8 \cdot 1.2040} = e^{3.3712} \approx 29.12$$

**Step 5d —** Final result:

$$f'_c = 240 \times 29.12 = 6988.8 \text{ MPa}$$

This is an **unrealistic** value — the empirical fit breaks down at very low w/b. In practice, the relation is **piecewise linear** in $\ln(f'_c)$ vs $w/b$, and a modified form is used:

$$f'_c = A \cdot (w/b)^{-B} \quad \text{with } A = 96 \text{ MPa, } B = 2.5 \text{ (for HSC)}$$

**Corrected Step 5d' —**

$$f'_c = 96 \cdot (0.30)^{-2.5} = 96 \cdot e^{-2.5 \ln(0.30)} = 96 \cdot e^{-2.5 \cdot (-1.2040)} = 96 \cdot e^{3.010}$$

$$f'_c = 96 \times 20.30 \approx \boxed{1949 \text{ MPa}}$$

This still exceeds realistic values, illustrating that **A and B must be calibrated for the specific cement-SCM system** (the "A ≈ 96 MPa" figure is illustrative only and must be determined experimentally per IS 10262).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** apply classical NSC Abrams' coefficients ($k_1 = 9840$, $k_2 = 11.0$) to HSC. These were calibrated for w/b in 0.40–0.80 range. For w/b $< 0.35$, use HSC-specific regression constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus of Elasticity of HSC (ACI 363R Form)

The **ACI 318/363R** empirical relation is:

$$E_c = w_c^{1.5} \cdot 0.043 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c} \quad \text{(in MPa, with } w_c \text{ in kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

For HSC, a simpler form is widely used:

$$E_c = 3320 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c} + 6900$$

### Derivation via Composite Materials Theory

Concrete is modeled as a **two-phase composite**: aggregate (volume fraction $V_a$, modulus $E_a$) embedded in cement paste (volume fraction $V_p = 1 - V_a$, modulus $E_p$).

**Step 1 — Hashin-Shtrikman upper bound** (assuming rigid inclusions in a softer matrix):

$$E_{upper} = E_p + \dfrac{V_a}{\dfrac{1}{E_a - E_p} + \dfrac{(1 - V_a)}{3E_p}}$$

**Step 2 — For HSC, $V_a \approx 0.65$**, $E_a \approx 70$ GPa (granite), $E_p \approx 25$ GPa.

**Step 3 —** Substituting:

$$E_{upper} = 25 + \dfrac{0.65}{\dfrac{1}{70 - 25} + \dfrac{0.35}{3 \times 25}}$$

$$E_{upper} = 25 + \dfrac{0.65}{\dfrac{1}{45} + \dfrac{0.35}{75}}$$

$$E_{upper} = 25 + \dfrac{0.65}{0.02222 + 0.004667} = 25 + \dfrac{0.65}{0.02689}$$

$$E_{upper} = 25 + 24.17 \approx 49.17 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 —** This theoretical upper bound exceeds the ACI 363R empirical value, which is expected because real HSC contains micro-cracks and ITZ defects. Empirically, for $f'_c$ = 60 MPa:

$$E_c = 3320\sqrt{60} + 6900 = 3320 \times 7.746 + 6900 = 25{,}716 + 6900 = 32{,}616 \text{ MPa} \approx 32.6 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches the **field-measured** modulus of HSC (30–35 GPa) far better than the theoretical upper bound.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capillary Porosity Reduction in HSC

The Powers' model gives the capillary porosity of hardened paste as a function of w/c (or w/b) and degree of hydration $\alpha$.

### Step 1 — Initial capillary porosity
At hydration $\alpha = 0$:

$$\phi_0 = \dfrac{w/c}{w/c + 0.32} \quad \text{(volume basis)}$$

### Step 2 — Capillary porosity after partial hydration
As water is consumed in forming C-S-H, capillary volume decreases:

$$\phi_{cap}(\alpha) = \phi_0 - 0.367\alpha \cdot \dfrac{C}{C + W + 0.32C}$$

For HSC with w/b = 0.30 and $\alpha = 0.85$:

**Step 2a —** Compute $\phi_0$:

$$\phi_0 = \dfrac{0.30}{0.30 + 0.32} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.62} = 0.4839$$

**Step 2b —** Per unit mass of cement, $C = 1$, $W = 0.30$:

$$\phi_{cap} = 0.4839 - 0.367 \times 0.85 \times \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.30 + 0.32}$$

$$\phi_{cap} = 0.4839 - 0.312 \times \dfrac{1}{1.62} = 0.4839 - 0.312 \times 0.6173$$

$$\phi_{cap} = 0.4839 - 0.1926 = \boxed{0.2913 \text{ (i.e., 29.1 %)}}$$

### Step 3 — Comparison with NSC
For NSC with w/c = 0.55 and $\alpha = 0.85$:

$$\phi_0 = \dfrac{0.55}{0.55 + 0.32} = 0.6322$$

$$\phi_{cap} = 0.6322 - 0.312 \times \dfrac{1}{1.87} = 0.6322 - 0.1668 = 0.4654 \text{ (46.5 %)}$$

**Conclusion:** HSC has **roughly half the capillary porosity** of NSC, which directly translates to **lower permeability, higher strength, and superior durability** — the core justification for using HSC in repair & rehabilitation of structures exposed to aggressive environments.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HSC vs NSC vs UHPC

> [!NOTE]
> **UHPC = Ultra-High Performance Concrete** ($f'_c$ ≥ 150 MPa, with steel fibers). Beyond the KTU syllabus but useful for comparative conceptual clarity.

| Property | NSC (M25) | HSC (M60–M80) | UHPC (M150+) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 28-d $f'_c$ (cylinder) | 20–25 MPa | 50–80 MPa | 150–200 MPa |
| w/b ratio | 0.45–0.60 | 0.25–0.35 | 0.15–0.25 |
| Silica fume content | 0–5 % | 5–15 % | 20–30 % |
| Superplasticizer | 0.5–1 % | 1–2 % | 2–5 % |
| $E_c$ | 25–30 GPa | 32–40 GPa | 45–55 GPa |
| Creep coefficient | 2.0–2.5 | 1.5–2.0 | 0.5–1.0 |
| Drying shrinkage ($\mu\epsilon$) | 400–600 | 300–500 | 100–300 |
| Chloride permeability (coulombs) | 2000–4000 | 500–1000 | < 100 |
| Typical application | General construction | High-rise, bridges | Defense, special structures |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — HSC Mix Design Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target Strength f'c] --> B[Select Cement Type OPC 53 / PPC]
    B --> C[Choose SCMs: SF + FA + GGBS]
    C --> D[Fix w/b Ratio 0.25-0.35]
    D --> E[Select HRWR Dosage 1-2%]
    E --> F[Determine Aggregate Grading]
    F --> G[Compute Mix Proportions per IS 10262]
    G --> H[Conduct Trial Mixes]
    H --> I{Strength Achieved?}
    I -->|No| J[Reduce w/b further or Increase SF]
    J --> H
    I -->|Yes| K[Verify Workability Slump 150-200mm]
    K --> L{Work OK?}
    L -->|No| M[Adjust HRWR dosage]
    M --> H
    L -->|Yes| N[Final Mix Design Approved]
    N --> O[Quality Control: Cube Tests at 7d and 28d]
    O --> P[End: HSC Production]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each block represents a decision node in the KTU 2024 recommended mix-design workflow (based on IS 10262:2019 and ACI 211.4R). The two "feedback loops" (I→J and L→M) mirror the iterative nature of HSC mix optimization.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constituent Materials & Their Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Binders
        C[OPC Cement]
        SF[Silica Fume]
        FA[Fly Ash]
        GG[GGBS]
    end
    subgraph Liquids
        W[Mix Water]
        SP[Superplasticizer HRWR]
    end
    subgraph Aggregates
        FA1[Fine Aggregate Sand]
        CA1[Coarse Aggregate 10-20mm]
    end

    C --> MIX[Concrete Matrix]
    SF --> MIX
    FA --> MIX
    GG --> MIX
    W --> MIX
    SP --> MIX
    FA1 --> MIX
    CA1 --> MIX

    MIX -->|Hydration| CSH[C-S-H Gel Dense]
    MIX -->|Pozzolanic Reaction| CSH
    CSH --> STR[High Compressive Strength f'c ≥ 50 MPa]
    CSH --> DUR[Low Permeability Durable]
    STR --> APP[Application: Repair Jacketing Bridges]
    DUR --> APP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram shows that **strength and durability in HSC are not independent** — they both emerge from the **C-S-H gel** formed by combined cement hydration and pozzolanic activity. This is a recurring KTU concept-question theme.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Production & Quality Control Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step1: Batching of Constituents] --> S2[Step2: Dry Mixing 30s]
    S2 --> S3[Step3: Add Water + 50% HRWR]
    S3 --> S4[Step4: Wet Mixing 90s]
    S4 --> S5[Step5: Add Remaining HRWR]
    S5 --> S6[Step6: Final Mixing 60s]
    S6 --> S7[Step7: Slump Flow Test 150-200mm]
    S7 --> S8{QC Pass?}
    S8 -->|No| S9[Reject Batch Adjust Mix]
    S9 --> S1
    S8 -->|Yes| S10[Step8: Casting of Cubes and Cylinders]
    S10 --> S11[Step9: Compaction by Vibration]
    S11 --> S12[Step10: Curing 27 plus or minus 1.5 days at 27 plus or minus 2 C]
    S12 --> S13[Step11: Compression Testing at 7d and 28d]
    S13 --> S14{Strength ≥ Target?}
    S14 -->|No| S15[Investigate Redesign]
    S14 -->|Yes| S16[Acceptance: HSC Production Cleared]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET634]
**Define High Strength Concrete (HSC). Differentiate it from High Performance Concrete (HPC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **HSC:** Concrete with 28-day cylinder compressive strength $f'_c \geq$ **50 MPa** (ACI 363R), or cube strength $\geq$ **65 MPa** (IS 456). It is essentially defined by **a single parameter — strength**.

> **HPC:** Concrete engineered to meet **multiple performance criteria simultaneously** — strength, durability, toughness, workability, and dimensional stability. HPC may or may not be HSC.

> **Key Distinction:** HSC ⊂ HPC (all HSC are HPC, but not vice-versa). Example: M60 concrete is HSC; a chloride-resistant M40 concrete is HPC but not HSC.

*(Allocation: 1 mark HSC definition + 1 mark HPC definition + 1 mark distinction example.)*

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET634]
**List any four essential constituents of HSC and state the role of silica fume.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC 53 grade)** — primary binder.
2. **Silica Fume (SF)** — supplementary cementitious material (5–15 % by mass of cement).
3. **High-Range Water-Reducing Admixture (HRWR / Superplasticizer)** — maintains workability at low w/b.
4. **Well-Graded Aggregates** — dense packing at the macro scale.

> **Role of Silica Fume:** It is an ultra-fine (≈ 0.1 μm) pozzolanic material. It reacts with **calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)$_2$)** liberated during cement hydration to form **additional C-S-H gel**, which:
> - Fills nano-pores in the ITZ,
> - Reduces capillary porosity,
> - Increases long-term strength and durability.

*(Allocation: 1 mark listing 4 constituents + 1 mark silica fume role description + 1 mark supporting mechanism.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET634]

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **materials and mix design principles of High Strength Concrete**. Include a note on the role of superplasticizers and supplementary cementitious materials. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **production, placement, and curing practices** recommended for HSC. How do these differ from those used for normal strength concrete? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Materials & Mix Design (7 marks):**

**1. Cement (1 mark):**
- Ordinary Portland Cement of grade **53 MPa** (conforming to IS 12269) is preferred.
- Cement content typically ranges from **450–550 kg/m³** in HSC, higher than NSC (300–350 kg/m³).
- Low C$_3$A content is desirable to reduce early heat of hydration and improve sulfate resistance.

**2. Supplementary Cementitious Materials (2 marks):**
- **Silica Fume (SF):** 5–15 % of cement mass. Particle size ≈ 0.1 μm (100× finer than cement). Pozzolanic activity index ≥ 105 % at 28 days (per ASTM C1240).
  - Reacts with Ca(OH)$_2$: SF + CH → C-S-H (additional gel).
  - **Packing effect:** Fills interstitial voids between cement grains.
- **Fly Ash (Class F):** 10–25 %. Improves long-term strength and reduces heat of hydration.
- **GGBS:** 20–50 %. Latent hydraulic binder; refines pore structure.
- **Metakaolin:** 5–10 %. Highly reactive, accelerates early strength gain.

**3. Chemical Admixtures (2 marks):**
- **High-Range Water-Reducing Admixtures (HRWR)** — Polycarboxylate-Ether (PCE) based, conforming to IS 9103 / ASTM C494 Type F & G.
- Dosage: 0.8–1.5 % by mass of cement.
- **Function:** Disperses cement flocs via electrostatic + steric repulsion, allowing fluid mix at w/b = 0.25–0.35.
- **Viscosity-Modifying Admixtures (VMA)** may be added to prevent segregation at very low w/b.

**4. Aggregates (1 mark):**
- **Coarse aggregate:** Crushed granite / basalt, max size 10–20 mm (smaller than NSC's 20 mm to reduce stress concentrations).
- **Fine aggregate:** Natural river sand or manufactured sand (M-sand), FM = 2.6–3.0, with low fines content.
- Aggregates must be **strong, dense, and free from reactive silica** (to avoid ASR).

**5. Mix Design Approach (1 mark):**
- **ACI 211.4R** — "Guide for Selecting Proportions for High-Strength Concrete."
- **IS 10262:2019** — Indian code for concrete mix proportioning (extended to higher grades).
- Key steps: fix w/b → choose aggregate-cement ratio based on workability → compute absolute volumes → convert to batch weights → trial mixes.

---

**Part (b) — Production, Placement & Curing (7 marks):**

**1. Batching & Mixing (2 marks):**
- **Batching accuracy:** ± 1 % for cement, water, admixtures; ± 2 % for aggregates (IS 4925).
- **Mixing sequence:**
  1. Dry mix all solids for 30 s.
  2. Add water + 50 % HRWR → mix 90 s.
  3. Add remaining HRWR → final mix 60 s.
- **Total mixing time ≈ 3 minutes** — shorter than NSC (to avoid frictional heating and slump loss).

**2. Transportation (1 mark):**
- HSC has **rapid slump loss** (up to 50 mm in 30 min) due to low w/b and SCM activity.
- Use **agitator trucks** for short hauls; avoid long-distance pumping without retarders.
- **Retarding admixtures** may be added to extend workability life to 60–90 min.

**3. Placement & Compaction (2 marks):**
- HSC is **highly cohesive and self-consolidating** (often classified as SCC-like).
- **Avoid over-vibration** — segregation can occur at high fluidity.
- Use **internal vibrators with care**; preferably use SCC-compatible placement.
- Place within **45 minutes** of mixing (per IS 456).

**4. Curing (2 marks):**
- **Extended curing** is critical — minimum **7 days** (vs 3 days for NSC), preferably 14 days.
- **Methods:**
  - Water ponding (most effective for slabs).
  - Wet burlap + polythene (for columns and beams).
  - **Membrane-forming curing compounds** (e.g., acrylic-based, ASTM C309) for HSC where water curing is impractical.
- **Why critical:** HSC's low w/b leaves little free water for *autogenous* curing; external moisture prevents **self-desiccation** and ensures continued pozzolanic reaction of silica fume.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students **omit the curing duration distinction** (HSC needs ≥ 7 days, not the 3-day NSC default). Examiners specifically test whether students understand that HSC's low w/b demands *external* water supply for full strength development.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET634]

> **(a)** Explain the **mechanical properties** of HSC: compressive strength, modulus of elasticity, tensile strength, and creep. Use **ACI 363R empirical relations** in your answer. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **applications of HSC in repair and rehabilitation** of structures. Cite at least three field scenarios with appropriate justification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Mechanical Properties (7 marks):**

**1. Compressive Strength (1.5 marks):**
- 28-day cylinder strength $f'_c \geq$ **50 MPa**; cube strength $f_{cube} \approx 1.20 \, f'_c$.
- HSC exhibits **slower strength gain after 28 days** if SCMs like FA dominate, but with **SF-rich mixes**, 90-day strength can be **15–25 % higher** than 28-day strength.

**2. Modulus of Elasticity (1.5 marks):**
- ACI 363R empirical relation:

$$E_c = 3320 \sqrt{f'_c} + 6900 \quad \text{(in MPa)}$$

- For $f'_c$ = 60 MPa:

$$E_c = 3320 \sqrt{60} + 6900 = 3320 \times 7.746 + 6900 = 32{,}616 \text{ MPa} \approx 32.6 \text{ GPa}$$

- For $f'_c$ = 80 MPa:

$$E_c = 3320 \sqrt{80} + 6900 = 3320 \times 8.944 + 6900 = 37{,}694 \text{ MPa} \approx 37.7 \text{ GPa}$$

- **Note:** ACI 363R cautions that this relation is valid up to $f'_c \approx$ 83 MPa. Beyond this, **measured values** should be used.

**3. Tensile Strength (1.5 marks):**
- **Split tensile strength** (ASTM C496):

$$f_t = 0.59 \sqrt{f'_c}$$

- For $f'_c$ = 60 MPa: $f_t = 0.59 \times 7.746 = 4.57$ MPa.
- **Modulus of rupture** (ASTM C78):

$$f_r = 0.94 \sqrt{f'_c}$$

- For $f'_c$ = 60 MPa: $f_r = 0.94 \times 7.746 = 7.28$ MPa.
- HSC is **more brittle** than NSC — tensile strength does not increase proportionally with compressive strength.

**4. Creep (1.5 marks):**
- Creep coefficient $\phi(t,t_0) = 1.5 - 2.0$ for HSC (vs. 2.0 - 2.5 for NSC).
- HSC creeps **less** because:
  - Lower w/b → denser paste → less evaporable water.
  - Higher aggregate stiffness (more vol. fraction of aggregate in mix).
  - SCMs (SF) refine capillary pores, reducing moisture movement.
- **Practical implication:** HSC columns exhibit **smaller long-term shortening**, beneficial for tall buildings.

**5. Drying Shrinkage (1 mark):**
- Typical values: **300–500 μstrain** (vs 400–600 μstrain for NSC).
- Lower due to reduced capillary water and SCM-induced pore refinement.

---

**Part (b) — Applications in Repair & Rehabilitation (7 marks):**

**Scenario 1: Column Jacketing for Seismic Retrofit (2.5 marks):**
- **Problem:** RC columns in old buildings (pre-1970s) have inadequate confinement and low strength.
- **HSC Solution:** Apply a 50–80 mm thick **HSC jacket** (M60–M80) around the column, often with additional longitudinal bars and stirrups.
- **Justification:** HSC's high strength allows **smaller jacket thickness**, preserving floor space; its low creep ensures **long-term dimensional compatibility** with the original column; its high $E_c$ (35 GPa) shares load efficiently with the parent concrete.

**Scenario 2: Bridge Deck Overlays in Coastal Zones (2.5 marks):**
- **Problem:** Marine/urban bridges suffer from **chloride-induced corrosion** of reinforcement.
- **HSC Solution:** Remove the top 40–75 mm of deteriorated concrete and cast an **HSC overlay (M70)** containing 8–10 % silica fume.
- **Justification:** HSC with SF has **chloride permeability < 1000 coulombs** (per ASTM C1202), compared to 2000–4000 coulombs for NSC. This drastically slows chloride ingress and extends the bridge's service life by 30–50 years.

**Scenario 3: Patch Repair of Spalled Beams/Columns (2 marks):**
- **Problem:** Spalling due to corroded reinforcement exposes the rebar and reduces the section.
- **HSC Solution:** Apply a **polymer-modified HSC mortar** (often pre-packaged) to rebuild the lost section.
- **Justification:** HSC mortar's high early strength (≥ 30 MPa in 24 hours with SF + accelerator) allows **rapid return to service**, which is critical for infrastructure like hospitals and highways.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> - **(i)** Writing generic "HSC is strong" without citing **specific MPa values** (50/65/80) for the application. Always quote a numerical benchmark.
> - **(ii)** Confusing **column jacketing** (which HSC is excellent for) with **beam strengthening** (which often needs CFRP/U-Wrap, not HSC alone, due to flexural demands).
> - **(iii)** Failing to mention **substrate preparation** (sandblasting, cleaning, bonding agent application) — examiners in PECET634 specifically test the **compatibility between old NSC and new HSC** in repair contexts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Strength Concrete**

- ✅ **Definition:** $f'_c \geq 50$ MPa (cylinder) / 65 MPa (cube) — ACI 363R / IS 456.
- ✅ **HSC ⊂ HPC** — HSC is a strength-based subset of the broader HPC family.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Low w/b (0.25–0.35) + SCMs (SF, FA, GGBS) + HRWR (PCE-based).
- ✅ **Silica fume** is the most critical SCM for HSC — 5–15 % replacement, reacts with Ca(OH)$_2$.
- ✅ **HRWR** dosage: 0.8–1.5 % by mass of binder — enables workability at very low w/b.
- ✅ **Max aggregate size:** 10–20 mm (smaller than NSC's 20 mm).
- ✅ **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $E_c = 3320\sqrt{f'_c} + 6900$ (ACI 363R)
  - $f_t = 0.59\sqrt{f'_c}$ (split tensile)
  - $f_r = 0.94\sqrt{f'_c}$ (modulus of rupture)
  - $f_{cube} \approx 1.20 \, f'_{c,cyl}$
- ✅ **Capillary porosity** of HSC ≈ 0.29 vs. NSC ≈ 0.47 (Powers' model) — explains durability.
- ✅ **Production:** Strict batching accuracy, rapid placement (45 min), avoid over-vibration.
- ✅ **Curing:** Minimum 7 days, preferably 14 — critical due to self-desiccation at low w/b.
- ✅ **Mechanical properties:** Higher $f'_c$, higher $E_c$, **more brittle** (tensile strength grows slower than compressive), **lower creep and shrinkage** than NSC.
- ✅ **Repair applications:** Column jacketing, bridge deck overlays, patch repair mortars.
- ✅ **Standard codes:** ACI 363R-10, ACI 211.4R, IS 456:2000, IS 10262:2019, IS 12269, IS 9103, ASTM C1202, ASTM C494.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** **"SF-HRWR-LW"** — *Silica Fume + HRWR + Low Water* — the three musketeers of HSC.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — High Strength Concrete**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PECET634 — Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures. Prepared per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 academic protocol.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High Performance Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Performance Concrete (HPC)** is defined by the **American Concrete Institute (ACI) Committee 363** as *"concrete meeting special combinations of performance and uniformity requirements that cannot always be achieved routinely using conventional constituents and normal mixing, placing, and curing practices."*

In the context of **PECET634 – Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures**, HPC is a carefully engineered cementitious composite formulated to exhibit **superior strength**, **enhanced durability**, **low permeability**, and **controlled volume change** simultaneously, making it the material of choice for **patching**, **overlays**, **jacketing**, and **strengthening** of distressed structural elements.

The performance criteria typically demanded of HPC in repair scenarios are quantified as follows:

| Performance Index | Target Range |
|---|---|
| **Compressive Strength (f'c)** | $\geq 60 \text{ MPa}$ (typically 60–120 MPa) |
| **Water–Binder Ratio (w/b)** | $\leq 0.35$ (often as low as 0.25) |
| **Chloride Permeability (Coulombs, ASTM C1202)** | $\leq 1000 \text{ Coulombs}$ (Very Low) |
| **Freeze–Thaw Resistance (DF, ASTM C666)** | $\geq 90\%$ at 300 cycles |
| **Drying Shrinkage (ASTM C157)** | $\leq 400 \text{ microstrain at 28 days}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** Do *not* confuse HPC with **Ultra-High Performance Concrete (UHPC)**. UHPC typically exhibits compressive strengths $\geq 150$ MPa with steel fiber reinforcement and specialized powder-based matrices, whereas HPC remains the practical workhorse for routine repair engineering.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of conventional concrete (M20/M25 grade) as a **basic cotton shirt**: it is comfortable, affordable, and serves everyday purposes, but it cannot withstand a tropical storm or a marathon run.

Now, picture **High Performance Concrete (HPC)** as a **high-tech sports jacket** built from a blend of advanced materials — a moisture-wicking base layer (chemical admixtures), reinforced synthetic fibers (mineral/steel fibers), and tightly woven fibers (low w/b ratio with supplementary cementitious materials). The result is a garment that is simultaneously **light, strong, durable, flexible, and protective** against extreme conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Highway Analogy" for HPC in Repair:** Imagine repairing a pothole on the **Munnar-Kochi highway** with two materials:
> - **Conventional concrete**: Patches the hole but develops new cracks within 2 monsoon seasons.
> - **HPC patch**: The repair outlasts the surrounding original pavement because of its low permeability, high bond strength, and resistance to chloride/sulfate attack.

This is precisely *why* the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the study of HPC within a **repair and rehabilitation** module — it is the **strategic material** used when distressed structures must be upgraded to match modern durability and load demands.

---

## 1.3 Why HPC is Indispensable in Repair Engineering

| Conventional Concrete Limitation | HPC Counter-Strategy |
|---|---|
| High permeability leads to corrosion | Densified microstructure via low w/b + pozzolans |
| Low tensile strength causes cracking | Fiber reinforcement and high cohesion |
| Poor bond with parent (old) concrete | Polymer-modified or latex-modified HPC systems |
| Susceptible to sulfate/chloride ingress | SCM-rich matrix (silica fume, GGBS) consumes Ca(OH)$_2$ |
| Long-term creep and shrinkage | Low paste content, optimized aggregate grading |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Performance-Based"** design philosophy of HPC directly aligns with the **NEP 2020** outcome-based education (OBE) framework adopted by KTU 2024, where the engineer specifies *desired performance* rather than *recipe proportions*.

---

## 1.4 Constituents at a Glance

HPC is not a single material but a **system**. Its performance arises from the **synergistic interaction** of the following:

1. **Binder System** (Cement + SCMs):
   - Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC 53 grade)
   - **Silica Fume** (5–10% by mass of cement): densifies ITZ
   - **Fly Ash** (Class F, 15–30%): improves workability and long-term strength
   - **Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS)** (30–70%): enhances durability
   - **Metakaolin** (5–15%): refines pore structure

2. **Chemical Admixtures**:
   - **High Range Water Reducers (HRWR / Superplasticizers)**: polycarboxylate ether (PCE) based, dosage 0.5–2.0% by mass of binder
   - Viscosity Modifying Admixtures (VMA) for SCC-type HPC
   - Shrinkage Reducing Admixtures (SRA)

3. **Aggregates**:
   - Well-graded, low-absorption crushed aggregates
   - Optimized packing density (often using modified Andreasen or Funk-Dinger model)

4. **Fibers** (optional but common in repair HPC):
   - Steel fibers (hooked-end or crimped): 30–80 kg/m$^3$
   - Polypropylene / PVA fibers: 0.9–1.8 kg/m$^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework of HPC Design

The design of HPC is fundamentally **performance-based** and follows a top-down engineering logic, in stark contrast to the prescriptive **IS 10262** approach used for ordinary concrete.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Define Performance Requirements**
   - Target compressive strength ($f'_c$)
   - Target durability index (chloride permeability, sulfate resistance, freeze-thaw)
   - Required workability (slump or slump-flow)
   - Exposure conditions (IS 456 Table 3 or ACI 318 Chapter 19)

2. **Select Binder Composition**
   - Choose OPC grade (typically 53 grade for HPC).
   - Determine SCM replacement levels based on strength and durability demands.
   - **General KTU guideline**:
     - For $f'_c \leq 70$ MPa: OPC + 10% silica fume + 20% fly ash
     - For $f'_c \leq 90$ MPa: OPC + 8% silica fume + 30% GGBS
     - For $f'_c \geq 100$ MPa: OPC + 10% silica fume + 5% metakaolin

3. **Establish Water–Binder Ratio ($w/b$)**
   - Use the **Abrams' Law** inverse relationship to estimate initial $w/b$.
   - Verify against durability tables (IS 456: minimum $w/b = 0.40$ for severe exposure).

4. **Determine HRWR Dosage**
   - Selected to achieve target workability at low $w/b$.
   - Modern PCE-based admixtures permit $w/b$ as low as 0.25 with slump $\geq 200$ mm.

5. **Optimize Aggregate Skeleton**
   - Use the **Modified Andreasen Model** (also known as the Dreux–Gorisse approach for HPC) to maximize packing density:

$$
P(D) = \left(\frac{D}{D_{\max}}\right)^q
$$

   - where $P(D)$ = cumulative percent finer than particle size $D$, and $q$ = distribution modulus (typically 0.30–0.45 for HPC with high SCM content).

6. **Trial Mixes & Iteration**
   - Minimum 3 trial mixes, test at 7, 28, and 56/90 days.
   - Adjust HRWR, water, and SCM ratios iteratively.

7. **Validate Durability**
   - Rapid Chloride Permeability Test (RCPT, ASTM C1202)
   - Sorptivity test
   - Water absorption by immersion (IS 1199 Part 2)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** for PECET634. Master the units, the boundary conditions, and the typical numerical ranges.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Variables | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f'_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{w/b}}$ | **Abrams' Law**; A, B are empirical constants (28-day, for OPC A$\approx$96 MPa, B$\approx$7.4) | $f'_c$ in MPa |
| 2 | $f_t = 0.33 \sqrt{f'_c}$ (ACI 363R) | Splitting tensile strength of HPC | $f_t$ in MPa |
| 3 | $E_c = 3320 \sqrt{f'_c} + 6900$ (ACI 363R) | Modulus of elasticity of HPC | $E_c$ in MPa |
| 4 | $f_{r} = 0.62 \sqrt{f'_c}$ | Modulus of rupture of HPC | $f_r$ in MPa |
| 5 | $\text{Permeability} \propto (w/c)^n$ | Power-law relationship; $n \approx 3\text{ to }4$ for OPC concretes | Dimensionless index |
| 6 | $\text{Poisson's ratio } \nu = 0.20 \pm 0.02$ | For HPC, slightly lower than conventional ($\nu = 0.20$) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Creep coefficient } \phi(t,t_0) = \phi_\infty \cdot \dfrac{(t-t_0)^{0.6}}{10 + (t-t_0)^{0.6}}$ | ACI 209R creep model | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_{\text{concrete}} = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (typical) | Density of HPC (slightly higher due to lower air content) | kg/m$^3$ |
| 9 | $\text{Air content} = 1.5 \text{ to } 3.0\%$ | Lower than conventional concrete | % by volume |
| 10 | $\text{Strain capacity (peak)} = 0.0025 \text{ to } 0.0035$ | At maximum stress | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for HPC**: The ACI 318 formula $E_c = 4700\sqrt{f'_c}$ **underestimates** HPC stiffness. Always use the **ACI 363R** form $E_c = 3320\sqrt{f'_c} + 6900$ for $f'_c \geq 60$ MPa.

---

## 2.3 Microstructure – The Hidden Engine of HPC Performance

HPC performance is governed by **microstructure refinement**, primarily at the **Interfacial Transition Zone (ITZ)** — the weakest link in conventional concrete.

| Microstructural Element | Conventional Concrete | HPC |
|---|---|---|
| **ITZ thickness** | 20–50 $\mu$m | 5–10 $\mu$m |
| **Capillary porosity** | 15–25% | 5–10% |
| **Ca(OH)$_2$ crystals at ITZ** | Large, oriented, weak | Consumed by pozzolana, replaced by C-S-H |
| **Pore size distribution** | Broad, with macropores | Narrow, dominated by gel pores |
| **Permeability** | High (10$^{-10}$ to 10$^{-12}$ m/s) | Very low (10$^{-13}$ to 10$^{-14}$ m/s) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why silica fume works:** Silica fume ($\text{SiO}_2 \geq 85\%$) is **~100 times finer than OPC** (particle size $\approx 0.1 \mu$m vs. $\approx 10 \mu$m). It acts by:
> 1. **Filler effect** (physical): fills capillary pores.
> 2. **Pozzolanic reaction** (chemical): consumes $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ → additional C-S-H gel.
> 
> The combined result: ITZ is densified, permeability drops dramatically, and strength surges.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of HPC in Repair

| Repair Application | HPC Property Exploited | Typical Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Column jacketing** | High compressive strength + bond strength | Bridge piers, marine columns |
| **Patch repair of corrosion-damaged beams** | Low permeability to chlorides | Coastal/residential structures |
| **Bridge deck overlays** | Freeze-thaw + abrasion resistance | High-altitude bridges in Munnar/Wayanad |
| **Seismic retrofit** | High tensile strength with fiber reinforcement | Heritage buildings, hospital retrofits |
| **Industrial floor repairs** | Abrasion + chemical resistance | Warehouse floors, chemical plants |
| **Nuclear/hazardous facility shielding** | Density + low permeability | Containment structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In repair engineering, the **bond between new HPC and substrate (old concrete)** is as critical as the HPC's intrinsic strength. The KTU syllabus implicitly tests your understanding that an *over-strong patch* with poor bond is structurally inferior to a *well-bonded, moderately strong patch*. Always consider **substrate preparation** (sandblasting, water jetting, application of bonding agent) as integral to HPC repair.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Abrams' Law and its Application to HPC Mix Design

**Statement:** The compressive strength of concrete is inversely related to the water-to-cement (or water-to-binder) ratio.

**Empirical Form (Abrams, 1918):**

$$
f'_c = \frac{A}{B^{(w/c)}}
$$

where:
- $f'_c$ = compressive strength at 28 days (MPa)
- $w/c$ = water-to-cement ratio
- $A$ and $B$ = empirical constants depending on cement type, age, and curing

**Step-by-step Derivation (Inversion to find $w/b$):**

Given the design requirement $f'_c = 70$ MPa, and material-specific constants $A = 96$ MPa, $B = 7.4$:

**Step 1:** Substitute target strength into the equation.

$$
70 = \frac{96}{7.4^{(w/b)}}
$$

**Step 2:** Invert both sides to isolate the exponential term.

$$
7.4^{(w/b)} = \frac{96}{70}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the right-hand side.

$$
7.4^{(w/b)} = 1.3714
$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm on both sides.

$$
(w/b) \cdot \ln(7.4) = \ln(1.3714)
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the logarithms.

$$
(w/b) \cdot 2.00148 = 0.31533
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $w/b$.

$$
w/b = \frac{0.31533}{2.00148} = 0.1576
$$

**Step 7:** Round to engineering precision and apply durability constraint.

$$
w/b = 0.30 \text{ (practical lower bound for workable HPC)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The pure Abrams' Law result of $w/b = 0.158$ is *theoretically* unworkable (zero workability). In practice, the lower bound is constrained by:
> - Workability requirements ($\text{slump} \geq 200$ mm)
> - Mixing energy and time
> - Admixture performance limits
> 
> Hence, the *practical $w/b$* is set to a value that satisfies **both Abrams' Law and workability**, then **HRWR dosage** is increased to maintain slump. This is the central paradox of HPC design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Elasticity of HPC

The ACI 363R formula for HPC is:

$$
E_c = 3320 \sqrt{f'_c} + 6900 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Example Calculation:** For $f'_c = 80$ MPa:

**Step 1:** Compute the square root.

$$
\sqrt{80} = 8.9443
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by coefficient.

$$
3320 \times 8.9443 = 29695.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Add the constant term.

$$
E_c = 29695.1 + 6900 = 36595.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Express in GPa.

$$
E_c \approx 36.6 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison with IS 456 formula:** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f'_c}$ yields $E_c = 5000 \times 8.9443 = 44721$ MPa = 44.7 GPa for $f'_c = 80$ MPa. The **IS 456 formula overestimates** by ~22%. This is a frequent KTU question — *always justify the use of ACI 363R for HPC.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Splitting Tensile Strength of HPC

**Formula (ACI 363R):**

$$
f_t = 0.33 \sqrt{f'_c} \text{ MPa}
$$

**For $f'_c = 80$ MPa:**

$$
f_t = 0.33 \times 8.9443 = 2.952 \text{ MPa}
$$

**For $f'_c = 100$ MPa:**

$$
f_t = 0.33 \times 10.0 = 3.30 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is tensile strength of HPC relatively lower than predicted by IS 516 ($f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f'_c}$)?** Because HPC is a *brittle* material. The ACI 363R coefficient (0.33) reflects this brittleness and the influence of aggregate-paste bond on tensile failure, which differs significantly from conventional concrete. This is critical in **flexural repair design** where the patch must resist shear and tension.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mix Design Parameter Sensitivity

The following table summarizes the **sensitivity of HPC properties** to key mix parameters — frequently tested in KTU problems.

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $f'_c$ | Effect on Permeability | Effect on Workability | Effect on Drying Shrinkage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $w/b$ ratio | $\downarrow$ Strongly | $\uparrow$ Strongly | $\uparrow$ Strongly | $\uparrow$ |
| Silica fume content | $\uparrow$ up to $\sim$10%, then $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ Strongly | $\downarrow$ (more water demand) | Mixed |
| Fly ash content (Class F) | $\downarrow$ early, $\uparrow$ later | $\downarrow$ (long-term) | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| HRWR dosage | Minimal | Minimal | $\uparrow$ strongly (up to saturation) | Minimal |
| Coarse aggregate volume | $\uparrow$ (modulus) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| Steel fiber volume | $\uparrow$ (flexural/tensile) | Minimal | $\downarrow$ | Minimal |
| Curing temperature (up to 60°C) | $\uparrow$ early, $\downarrow$ long-term | $\downarrow$ | N/A | Mixed |
| Curing duration | $\uparrow$ (long-term) | $\downarrow$ strongly | N/A | $\downarrow$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: HPC Mix Proportioning for Repair (KTU Standard)

**Design Requirements:**
- Target compressive strength: $f'_c = 70$ MPa at 28 days
- Maximum aggregate size: 12.5 mm (for thin repair sections)
- Slump requirement: 150–200 mm
- Exposure: Severe (coastal/marine)
- Minimum binder content: 450 kg/m$^3$ (per IS 456 for severe exposure with $\text{cover} = 50$ mm)

**Step 1: Select $w/b$ ratio**
- From Abrams' Law (using $A = 96$, $B = 7.4$): theoretical $w/b = 0.16$
- From durability (IS 456 severe exposure): $w/b_{\max} = 0.40$
- **Adopt $w/b = 0.32$** (engineering compromise)

**Step 2: Calculate water content**
- For 12.5 mm aggregate and 150–200 mm slump (no HRWR): water $\approx 210$ kg/m$^3$
- **With HRWR (1.2% PCE)**: water can be reduced by 25%, so water = 158 kg/m$^3$
- **Adopt water content = 160 kg/m$^3$**

**Step 3: Calculate binder content**

$$
\text{Binder} = \frac{\text{Water}}{w/b} = \frac{160}{0.32} = 500 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> This satisfies the minimum binder requirement (450 kg/m$^3$).

**Step 4: Design binder composition (HPC system)**

| Component | % of Total Binder | Mass (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|---|
| OPC 53 grade | 75% | 375 |
| Silica fume | 10% | 50 |
| Fly ash (Class F) | 15% | 75 |
| **Total Binder** | **100%** | **500** |

**Step 5: Calculate aggregate content (absolute volume method)**

- Total volume of concrete = 1 m$^3$ = 1,000,000 cm$^3$
- Air content = 2% = 20,000 cm$^3$
- Volume of binder = $\frac{500}{3150} \times 10^6 = 158,730$ cm$^3$ (specific gravity 3.15)
- Volume of water = $\frac{160}{1000} \times 10^6 = 160,000$ cm$^3$
- Volume of HRWR (negligible, included in water for simplicity)

**Step 6: Compute total aggregate volume**

$$
V_{\text{aggregate}} = 1{,}000{,}000 - 20{,}000 - 158{,}730 - 160{,}000 = 661{,}270 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 7: Split between fine and coarse (typical 40:60 for HPC with 12.5 mm aggregate)**

| Aggregate | Volume Fraction | Volume (cm$^3$) | Mass (kg) | SG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fine (M-sand) | 40% | 264,508 | $\frac{264508}{10^6} \times 2650 = 701$ | 2.65 |
| Coarse (crushed) | 60% | 396,762 | $\frac{396762}{10^6} \times 2700 = 1071$ | 2.70 |
| **Total** | **100%** | **661,270** | **1772** | — |

**Step 8: Final Mix Proportions (kg/m$^3$)**

| Material | Quantity (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|
| OPC 53 | 375 |
| Silica fume | 50 |
| Fly ash | 75 |
| Water | 160 |
| Fine aggregate | 701 |
| Coarse aggregate | 1071 |
| HRWR (1.2% of binder) | 6.0 |
| **Total** | **2438** |

> [!TIP]
> **Validation against target:** Using **Bolomey** or **Feret** type empirical relationships, this mix will yield $f'_c$ in the range of **65–75 MPa** at 28 days with proper curing, satisfying the design target. **For exam purposes**, you can state: *"The mix is expected to achieve the design strength of 70 MPa, subject to trial mix validation."*

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for **PECET634**. Marks are distributed as **Part A: 3 marks × 2 = 6 marks** and **Part B: 14 marks × 1 = 14 marks** for a total of **20 marks** in the selected category. Each Part B question carries an internal choice.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Define High Performance Concrete (HPC). List any four key performance requirements that distinguish HPC from conventional concrete.**

**Model Answer:**

High Performance Concrete (HPC) is an engineered cementitious composite designed to meet specific performance criteria that cannot be achieved with conventional constituents and standard practices (ACI Committee 363R).

**Four key performance requirements:**

1. **High Compressive Strength**: $f'_c \geq 60$ MPa at 28 days (vs. 20–40 MPa for conventional).
2. **Low Permeability**: Chloride ion permeability $\leq 1000$ Coulombs at 28 days (very low per ASTM C1202).
3. **High Durability**: Resistance to freeze-thaw cycles, sulfate attack, and carbonation; designed service life often $\geq 100$ years.
4. **Workability Control**: Maintained slump $\geq 150$ mm at low $w/b$ ratio ($\leq 0.35$) using HRWR admixtures.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Explain the role of Silica Fume in High Performance Concrete. Write the pozzolanic reaction involved.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Silica Fume:**
Silica fume is a by-product of silicon metal or ferrosilicon alloy production, consisting of extremely fine spherical particles ($\approx 0.1 \mu$m diameter) with $\text{SiO}_2$ content $\geq 85\%$.

It contributes to HPC in two ways:
1. **Filler effect (physical)**: Fills capillary pores between cement grains, refining the pore structure.
2. **Pozzolanic reaction (chemical)**: Reacts with calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)$_2$] liberated during cement hydration to form additional C-S-H gel.

**Pozzolanic Reaction:**

$$
\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C-S-H gel}
$$

The consumption of Ca(OH)$_2$ at the **Interfacial Transition Zone (ITZ)** between aggregate and paste significantly increases the strength and reduces permeability, which is central to HPC durability.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically carrying 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided *between two full questions*, not within sub-parts. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are full alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the constituents of High Performance Concrete in detail. Discuss the role of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an HPC mix for the following requirements:
- Target 28-day compressive strength: 80 MPa
- Slump: 175–200 mm
- Maximum aggregate size: 20 mm
- Exposure: Severe (IS 456)
- Cement: OPC 53 grade (specific gravity 3.15)

Compute the mix proportions per cubic metre. Use a binder composition of OPC 75%, Silica Fume 10%, Fly Ash 15%. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Constituents of HPC:**

1. **Binders**:
   - **OPC 53 grade**: Primary binder; provides early strength.
   - **Silica Fume**: 5–10% of binder mass; densifies ITZ and reduces permeability.
   - **Fly Ash (Class F)**: 15–30% of binder mass; improves workability and long-term strength.
   - **GGBS**: 30–70% replacement; enhances sulfate and chloride resistance.
   - **Metakaolin**: 5–15%; refines pore structure; provides early strength gain.

2. **Chemical Admixtures**:
   - **HRWR (Superplasticizers)**: Polycarboxylate ether (PCE) based, dosage 0.5–2.0% of binder mass. Permits very low $w/b$ while maintaining workability.
   - **Viscosity Modifying Admixtures (VMA)**: For self-compacting HPC.
   - **Shrinkage Reducing Admixtures (SRA)**: Reduce drying shrinkage by 20–50%.
   - **Corrosion Inhibitors**: Calcium nitrite-based, for added protection in marine environments.

3. **Aggregates**:
   - Must be **strong, dense, clean, and well-graded**.
   - **Fine aggregate**: Manufactured sand (M-sand) preferred; FM 2.6–3.2; silt content $< 3\%$.
   - **Coarse aggregate**: Crushed granite/basalt; 12.5 mm or 20 mm nominal size; water absorption $< 1\%$.
   - **Aggregate packing optimization** is critical: Modified Andreasen model is used to minimize voids and reduce paste demand.

4. **Fibers** (optional):
   - **Steel fibers** (hooked-end or crimped): 30–80 kg/m$^3$; improve flexural strength, impact resistance, and crack control.
   - **Polypropylene fibers**: 0.9–1.8 kg/m$^3$; reduce plastic shrinkage cracking.
   - **Glass fibers** (alkali-resistant): For thin-section repairs.

**Role of SCMs — Detailed:**

| SCM | Particle Size | Mechanism | Key Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silica Fume | $\sim 0.1 \mu$m | Pozzolanic + Filler | Highest strength and lowest permeability |
| Fly Ash (F) | $\sim 10 \mu$m | Pozzolanic + Ball-bearing effect | Long-term strength, workability |
| GGBS | $\sim 15 \mu$m | Latent hydraulic | Sulfate resistance, low heat of hydration |
| Metakaolin | $\sim 1 \mu$m | Pozzolanic | Early strength, white color, refined pores |

**Conclusion:** The synergy of OPC with SCMs is what makes HPC a fundamentally different material from ordinary concrete — not just a stronger version of the same material.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Mix Design for $f'_c = 80$ MPa, Severe Exposure**

**Step 1: Water–Binder Ratio**

Using Abrams' Law with $A = 96$ MPa, $B = 7.4$:

$$
80 = \frac{96}{7.4^{(w/b)}}
$$

Solving:

$$
7.4^{(w/b)} = 1.20
$$

$$
(w/b) \cdot \ln(7.4) = \ln(1.20)
$$

$$
w/b = \frac{0.1823}{2.0015} = 0.091
$$

Adopting **practical** $w/b$ with HRWR and durability constraint (IS 456 severe: $w/b \leq 0.40$):

$$
\boxed{w/b = 0.30}
$$

**Step 2: Water Content**

For 20 mm aggregate, target slump 175–200 mm:
- Without HRWR: $\sim 195$ kg/m$^3$
- With HRWR (1.5% PCE), 20% reduction possible:

$$
\text{Water} = 0.80 \times 195 = 156 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Adopt $\text{Water} = 160$ kg/m$^3$ (matches 80 kg bags with small adjustment).

**Step 3: Total Binder Content**

$$
\text{Binder} = \frac{160}{0.30} = 533.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Adopt $\text{Binder} = 535$ kg/m$^3$ (within IS 456 severe exposure minimum).

**Step 4: Binder Composition**

| Component | % | Mass (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|---|
| OPC 53 | 75% | 401.25 |
| Silica Fume | 10% | 53.50 |
| Fly Ash (F) | 15% | 80.25 |
| **Total** | 100% | **535.00** |

**Step 5: Aggregate Content (Absolute Volume Method)**

Assume air content = 1.5% (typical for HPC, low due to dense matrix).

- Volume of binder:
$$
V_{\text{binder}} = \frac{535}{3150} \times 10^6 = 169{,}841 \text{ cm}^3
$$

- Volume of water:
$$
V_{\text{water}} = \frac{160}{1000} \times 10^6 = 160{,}000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

- Volume of air:
$$
V_{\text{air}} = 0.015 \times 10^6 = 15{,}000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

- Volume of HRWR: $\approx \frac{8}{1100} \times 10^6 = 7273$ cm$^3$ (negligible contribution, included in water for simplicity).

- Volume of aggregates:
$$
V_{\text{agg}} = 10^6 - 169{,}841 - 160{,}000 - 15{,}000 = 655{,}159 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 6: Split Fine vs. Coarse (45:55 for HPC with 20 mm aggregate)**

| Aggregate | Volume (cm$^3$) | SG | Mass (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fine (M-sand) | 294,822 | 2.65 | $\frac{294822}{10^6} \times 2650 = 781.3$ |
| Coarse (crushed) | 360,337 | 2.70 | $\frac{360337}{10^6} \times 2700 = 972.9$ |

**Final Mix Proportions:**

| Material | Quantity (kg/m$^3$) |
|---|---|
| OPC 53 grade | 401.25 |
| Silica Fume | 53.50 |
| Fly Ash (Class F) | 80.25 |
| Water | 160.00 |
| Fine aggregate (M-sand) | 781.30 |
| Coarse aggregate (crushed, 20 mm) | 972.90 |
| HRWR (1.5% of binder) | 8.00 |
| **Total** | **2457.20** |

**Validation Statement:** The mix satisfies IS 456 severe exposure requirements ($w/b \leq 0.40$, binder $\geq 450$ kg/m$^3$), and the use of 10% silica fume with low $w/b = 0.30$ will yield $f'_c$ in the range of **75–85 MPa** at 28 days, meeting the design target.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare the modulus of elasticity values predicted by **ACI 363R** and **IS 456** for HPC of strength 90 MPa. Discuss which is more appropriate and why. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the microstructure of HPC with reference to the Interfacial Transition Zone (ITZ). Explain how supplementary cementitious materials refine the ITZ and improve durability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Comparison of $E_c$ for $f'_c = 90$ MPa:**

**IS 456 Formula:**

$$
E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f'_c}
$$

$$
E_c = 5000 \times \sqrt{90} = 5000 \times 9.4868 = 47{,}434 \text{ MPa} \approx 47.43 \text{ GPa}
$$

**ACI 363R Formula (for HPC):**

$$
E_c = 3320 \sqrt{f'_c} + 6900
$$

$$
E_c = 3320 \times 9.4868 + 6900 = 31{,}496 + 6900 = 38{,}396 \text{ MPa} \approx 38.40 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Percentage Difference:**

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{47.43 - 38.40}{38.40} \times 100 = 23.5\%
$$

**Which is more appropriate?**

The **ACI 363R formula is more appropriate** for HPC. The reasons are:

1. **IS 456 formula was developed** based on data for conventional concrete with compressive strengths typically $\leq 50$ MPa. Extrapolation to 90 MPa overestimates stiffness.

2. **HPC is more brittle** with a stiffer matrix but also contains **more microcracks** at the aggregate-paste interface due to the higher autogenous shrinkage of the dense matrix. This **reduces the effective modulus** compared to the IS 456 prediction.

3. **Empirical calibration**: ACI 363R was derived from a database of HPC mixes (50–125 MPa), making its predictions more accurate for the HPC regime.

4. **Pozzolanic SCMs** (silica fume, fly ash) reduce the actual modulus slightly by altering the C-S-H gel structure, an effect captured in the ACI 363R form but not in IS 456.

**Engineering Implication:** Overestimation of $E_c$ leads to **underestimation of deflection** in serviceability limit state (SLS) design. This is critical for slender structural elements (long-span beams, tall columns) where HPC is often used in repair/retrofit.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Microstructure of HPC and the ITZ:**

**What is the ITZ?**
The Interfacial Transition Zone is the 20–50 $\mu$m thick region surrounding aggregate particles in concrete. It is the **weakest link** in conventional concrete because of:
- Higher local $w/c$ ratio (water films on aggregate surface cause higher local porosity).
- Preferential growth of large, oriented **Ca(OH)$_2$ crystals** (portlandite).
- Higher capillary porosity (15–25% by volume).

In HPC, the ITZ is dramatically refined (thickness 5–10 $\mu$m) due to:

1. **Low $w/b$ ratio (0.25–0.35)**:
   - Reduces free water, leaving fewer capillary pores.
   - Shrinks the ITZ physically.

2. **Silica Fume**:
   - Pozzolanic reaction with Ca(OH)$_2$ produces **additional C-S-H gel**, filling voids in the ITZ.
   - Filler effect further densifies the region.

3. **Fly Ash (Class F)**:
   - Pozzolanic activity (slower than silica fume) provides long-term refinement.
   - Spherical particles act as micro-aggregates, improving particle packing near aggregate surfaces.

4. **GGBS**:
   - Latent hydraulic reaction continues for months, providing ongoing densification.
   - Reduces CH content in ITZ substantially.

**Microstructural Outcomes:**

| Property | Before SCM Refinement | After SCM Refinement |
|---|---|---|
| ITZ thickness | 20–50 $\mu$m | 5–10 $\mu$m |
| Capillary porosity | 15–25% | 5–10% |
| CH crystals | Large, oriented | Replaced by C-S-H |
| Permeability coefficient | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-12}$ m/s | $10^{-13}$ to $10^{-14}$ m/s |
| Diffusion coefficient (Cl$^-$) | $5 \times 10^{-12}$ m$^2$/s | $0.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m$^2$/s |

**Durability Implications:**

- **Chloride resistance**: Reduced diffusion coefficient delays corrosion initiation by 5–10×.
- **Sulfate resistance**: Lower CH content reduces ettringite formation.
- **Carbonation resistance**: Densified matrix slows CO$_2$ ingress.
- **Freeze-thaw resistance**: Lower permeability means less freezable water.
- **Drying shrinkage**: Reduced due to lower paste volume and densified structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students describe ITZ refinement as *"filling the voids"* without naming the **specific SCM, the reaction (pozzolanic vs. filler), and the measurable outcome (permeability coefficient, ITZ thickness)**. This costs **2–3 marks** per question. Always quantify microstructural changes with specific numbers.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in HPC Questions:**
> 1. **Using IS 456 formulas blindly** for HPC ($E_c = 5000\sqrt{f'_c}$, $f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f'_c}$). Always use ACI 363R for HPC and justify why.
> 2. **Confusing HPC with HSC** (High Strength Concrete). HSC focuses only on strength; HPC is a broader, performance-based category.
> 3. **Ignoring workability paradox** in low $w/b$ mixes. Always mention the role of HRWR.
> 4. **Omitting the pozzolanic reaction equation** when asked about SCMs.
> 5. **Not validating the mix** against IS 456 minimum binder and maximum $w/b$ for the given exposure.
> 6. **Using "M-sand" vs. "river sand" interchangeably** without commenting on grading, FM, and silt content.
> 7. **Neglecting curing** in HPC — HPC requires *extended moist curing* (7–14 days minimum) for SCMs to react and densify the matrix.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-minute glance before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ HPC = **performance-based** design, not prescriptive; $f'_c \geq 60$ MPa typical.
- ✅ Key binder components: **OPC + Silica Fume (5–10%) + Fly Ash (15–30%) + GGBS (30–70%)**.
- ✅ **HRWR (PCE-based)** is the *enabler* — it permits low $w/b$ ($\leq 0.35$) while maintaining workability.
- ✅ **Silica fume** = densifies ITZ via pozzolanic reaction: $\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{C-S-H}$.
- ✅ **ITZ refinement** is the *microstructural key* to HPC durability.
- ✅ Use **ACI 363R formulas** for $E_c$ and $f_t$ of HPC, NOT IS 456.
- ✅ Abrams' Law: $f'_c = A / B^{(w/b)}$; solve for $w/b$ using logarithms.
- ✅ **Mix design** uses absolute volume method; binder $\geq 450$ kg/m$^3$ for severe exposure.
- ✅ **Modified Andreasen model** for aggregate packing optimization.
- ✅ **Curing**: minimum 7 days wet curing; 28 days ideal for full SCM reaction.
- ✅ HPC in repair: **bond with substrate** is as critical as the patch's intrinsic strength.
- ✅ **Distinction table**:
  - Conventional concrete: $w/b = 0.45$–$0.60$, $f'_c = 20$–$40$ MPa.
  - HPC: $w/b = 0.25$–$0.35$, $f'_c = 60$–$120$ MPa.
  - UHPC: $w/b \leq 0.25$, $f'_c \geq 150$ MPa, fiber-reinforced.
- ✅ **Key durability tests**: RCPT (ASTM C1202), sorptivity, IS 1199 water absorption, accelerated carbonation.
- ✅ **Repair applications**: jacketing, patch repairs, bridge overlays, seismic retrofit, industrial floors.
- ✅ **Common exam pitfalls**: formula selection, missing pozzolanic reaction, ignoring curing, not validating exposure-based limits.
- ✅ **One-line summary**: *HPC achieves its superior properties not by being a "stronger paste", but by being a **system of densified microstructure**, optimized packing, and targeted chemical admixtures — a true KTU outcome-based engineering material.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vacuum Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vacuum Concrete** (also called **Vacuum Dewatered Concrete, VDC**, or **Suction Concrete**) is a specialized concrete in which excess water and entrapped air are systematically extracted from the freshly placed concrete matrix through the application of a **partial vacuum** to the top surface, immediately after placement and initial vibration, but before initial set. The technique is formally classified under *Special Concreting Methods* and is of major relevance in **Repair and Rehabilitation Engineering** because it produces a denser, low permeability, high early-strength matrix — a critical requirement when restoring deteriorated structural elements that must be returned to service rapidly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Vacuum concrete is produced by applying a vacuum of approximately **0.07 to 0.09 MPa (550–700 mm of Hg)** to the surface of fresh concrete, generating a **pressure differential of ~1 kg/cm² (≈ 101.325 kPa)** across the concrete mass, which physically extracts **15%–25%** of the mixing water, thereby effectively reducing the original water–cement (W/C) ratio.
