<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]*
Define Reactive Powder Concrete. List any **four** distinguishing features of RPC compared to conventional concrete.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Reactive Powder Concrete (RPC) is an ultra-high-performance cementitious composite material with no coarse aggregates, made by optimizing the packing density of fine powders (cement, silica fume, quartz flour, fine sand) and reinforced with steel micro-fibers. It achieves compressive strengths of **200–800 MPa**.

**Four Distinguishing Features (2 Marks):**
1. **Absence of coarse aggregates** — eliminates ITZ defects and stress concentrators.
2. **Very low water-to-binder ratio (0.15–0.25)** — achieved using high-range water reducers.
3. **Steel micro-fiber reinforcement (1.5–3.0%)** — provides ductility and post-cracking tensile capacity.
4. **Heat curing at 90–400 °C** — accelerates pozzolanic reaction, converts CH crystals into stable C-S-H gel, and densifies the microstructure.

---

**Question 2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]*
Explain the role of **silica fume** and **steel fibers** in Reactive Powder Concrete.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Silica Fume (1.5 Marks):** Silica fume is an extremely fine pozzolanic material (particle size ~0.1 μm, ~100 times finer than cement). It fills the micro-voids between cement grains, reducing capillary porosity, and reacts with calcium hydroxide (CH) produced during cement hydration to form additional calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) gel. This enhances the long-term strength and durability of RPC.

**Steel Fibers (1.5 Marks):** Steel micro-fibers (typically 13 mm long, 0.2 mm diameter) provide tensile strength, crack-bridging capability, and post-cracking ductility. They prevent brittle failure by transferring tensile stresses across cracks, increasing fracture energy from ~0.1 kJ/m² (plain paste) to **20–40 kJ/m²** in RPC.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **constituent materials and their proportions** used in Reactive Powder Concrete. Discuss the role of each constituent. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **microstructural principles** that govern the superior performance of RPC. Include a comparison with conventional concrete in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Constituent Materials and Proportions (7 Marks):**

A typical RPC mix design (by mass of binder):

| Constituent | Quantity (kg/m³) | Role |
|:------------|:------------------|:-----|
| OPC 53 Grade Cement | **900–1000** | Primary binder; source of C₃S, C₂S for hydration |
| Silica Fume | **200–300** | Pozzolanic filler; consumes CH, refines pore structure |
| Quartz Flour | **200–300** | Inert micro-filler; provides packing density |
| Fine Quartz Sand | **900–1100** | Largest particles; graded for optimal packing |
| Superplasticizer (PCE) | **15–25** | Enables very low w/b with adequate workability |
| Water | **180–220** | Hydration; w/b ratio strictly 0.15–0.25 |
| Steel Microfibers | **150–200** (1.5–3% by volume) | Crack bridging, tensile ductility |

**Role Explanation:**
- **Cement (1 Mark):** Provides the hydration products C-S-H and CH.
- **Silica Fume (1.5 Marks):** Pozzolanic activity refines pore structure and improves long-term strength.
- **Quartz Flour (0.5 Mark):** Inert filler for particle packing.
- **Fine Sand (1 Mark):** Largest aggregate phase; eliminates coarse aggregate defects.
- **Superplasticizer (1 Mark):** Reduces water demand at low w/b ratios.
- **Steel Fibers (1 Mark):** Provides ductility and post-cracking strength.
- **Water (1 Mark):** Critical control of w/b ratio.

---

**(b) Microstructural Principles (7 Marks):**

The five microstructural principles of RPC are:

1. **Enhanced Homogeneity (1.5 Marks):** Coarse aggregates are eliminated. Since the ITZ between coarse aggregate and paste is the weakest zone in conventional concrete (with porosity 20–30% higher than bulk paste), removing aggregates creates a near-uniform matrix.

2. **Enhanced Density via Particle Packing (1.5 Marks):** The powders (cement at ~10 μm, silica fume at ~0.1 μm, quartz flour at ~1–10 μm, sand at ~150–600 μm) are proportioned for **optimized gradation** following the Andreasen model or modified Fuller curve. This minimizes voids.

3. **Enhanced Microstructure via Heat Curing (1.5 Marks):** Steam curing at 90 °C for 48 hours (or hot pressing at 250–400 °C for RPC 800) accelerates the pozzolanic reaction. CH crystals are converted into additional C-S-H gel, refining the microstructure.

4. **Enhanced Ductility via Fiber Reinforcement (1.5 Marks):** Steel microfibers (high tensile strength ≥ 2000 MPa) bridge microcracks, preventing brittle propagation. The composite behavior shifts from brittle (plain paste) to ductile (strain-hardening).

5. **Enhanced Toughness via Energy Absorption (1 Mark):** The fiber pull-out mechanism absorbs fracture energy, raising GF from ~0.1 kJ/m² (plain paste) to **20–40 kJ/m²** in RPC — comparable to metals.

**Comparison with Conventional Concrete (additional credit):**
- Conventional concrete has f'c = 20–60 MPa, f_t = 2–5 MPa, GF = 0.1 kJ/m².
- RPC has f'c = 200–800 MPa, f_t = 25–150 MPa, GF = 20–40 kJ/m².
- RPC's microstructure is **5–10 times denser** than conventional concrete, with chloride permeability ~100 times lower.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
*[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Pattern]*

**(a)** Discuss the **strength and durability characteristics** of RPC. Provide numerical values for compressive strength, tensile strength, elastic modulus, and fracture energy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of heat curing** in RPC. Distinguish between the curing regimes for **RPC 200, RPC 500, and RPC 800**. How does heat curing affect the durability of RPC in rehabilitation applications? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Strength and Durability Characteristics (7 Marks):**

| Property | RPC Value | Conventional Concrete |
|:---------|:----------|:---------------------|
| Compressive Strength | **200–800 MPa** | 20–60 MPa |
| Direct Tensile Strength | **25–150 MPa** | 2–5 MPa |
| Flexural Strength | **40–200 MPa** | 4–8 MPa |
| Elastic Modulus | **50–75 GPa** | 25–35 GPa |
| Fracture Energy | **20–40 kJ/m²** | 0.1 kJ/m² |
| Density | **2400–2800 kg/m³** | 2200–2500 kg/m³ |
| Chloride Permeability | **<100 Coulombs** | 2000–4000 Coulombs |
| Water Absorption | **<0.5%** | 3–5% |

**Durability Discussion (2 Marks):**
- Near-zero permeability makes RPC immune to chloride and sulfate attack.
- Excellent freeze-thaw resistance due to densified matrix.
- Carbonation depth is negligible.
- Abrasion resistance is 5–10 times higher than conventional concrete.

---

**(b) Role of Heat Curing and Curing Regimes (7 Marks):**

**Role of Heat Curing (3 Marks):**
Heat curing accelerates the pozzolanic reaction between silica fume and calcium hydroxide (CH), converting weak CH crystals into additional C-S-H gel. This:
- Refines pore structure (reduces capillary porosity).
- Increases strength (up to 50% gain over standard curing).
- Reduces long-term shrinkage.
- Improves the fiber-matrix bond.

**Curing Regimes (3 Marks):**

| RPC Grade | Curing Regime | Effect |
|:----------|:--------------|:-------|
| **RPC 200** | Standard curing or 90 °C steam for 48 hours | Compressive strength ~200 MPa |
| **RPC 500** | 90 °C steam for 48 hours + optimized packing | Compressive strength ~500 MPa |
| **RPC 800** | Hot pressing at 250–400 °C under pressure | Compressive strength ~800 MPa |

**Effect on Durability in Rehabilitation (1 Mark):**
Heat-cured RPC has near-zero permeability, making it ideal for **column jacketing** and **bridge deck overlays** where aggressive environments (chlorides, marine exposure) are a concern. The dense matrix prevents ingress of harmful agents, ensuring long-term performance with minimal maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Writing "RPC = High Strength Concrete." This is **incorrect** — RPC is *Ultra-High-Performance* and has *no coarse aggregate*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Mentioning silica fume only as a filler. Silica fume is *primarily a pozzolan*; it reacts chemically, not just physically.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Quoting compressive strength as "200 MPa only." The full range is 200–800 MPa, depending on grade and curing regime.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting units (MPa, kJ/m², GPa) — KTU evaluators strictly enforce SI units.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In mix design questions, failing to mention the role of superplasticizer in achieving low w/b ratio.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RPC** is an **Ultra-High-Performance Concrete (UHPC)** with **no coarse aggregate**, designed at the powder scale.
- **Five microstructural principles:** Homogeneity, Densification, Microstructure refinement, Ductility (fibers), and Toughness.
- **Water-to-binder ratio:** 0.15–0.25 — extremely low, enabled by PCE-based superplasticizers.
- **Silica fume (20–30% by cement mass)** is the key pozzolan — reacts with CH to form additional C-S-H gel.
- **Steel microfibers (1.5–3% by volume)** provide post-cracking ductility; without fibers, RPC fails in a brittle manner.
- **Heat curing** (90–400 °C) accelerates pozzolanic reactions and is essential for achieving strengths above 200 MPa.
- **Compressive strength range:** 200–800 MPa.
- **Tensile strength range:** 25–150 MPa.
- **Fracture energy:** 20–40 kJ/m² (compared to 0.1 kJ/m² for plain paste).
- **Key formula to memorize:** $f_c^{\prime} = k_1 \cdot f_{cem} \cdot \exp(-k_2 \cdot P_{total})$ (Feret-type strength-porosity law).
- **Fiber composite law:** $f_{t,comp} = \eta_{\theta} \cdot V_f \cdot \tau \cdot (L_f / d_f)$ — critical for KTU derivations.
- **Applications in rehabilitation (Module 3 focus):** Column jacketing, bridge deck overlays, seismic retrofitting of coupling beams, nuclear structure repair.
- **KTU board answer must include:** Numerical values with units, role of each constituent, comparison with conventional concrete, and reference to microstructural mechanisms.
- **Real-world landmark:** Sherbrooke Bridge (Canada, 1997) — world's first RPC pedestrian bridge, 60 m span.
- **Durability advantage:** Chloride permeability <100 Coulombs (vs. 2000–4000 Coulombs for conventional concrete) — 100× improvement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concrete Made with Industrial Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concrete Made with Industrial Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Concrete Made with Industrial Wastes** refers to a class of sustainable, eco-efficient concrete in which supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) — derived as by-products from various industrial processes — are utilized to partially or fully replace ordinary Portland cement (OPC) and/or fine aggregates. These industrial by-products possess either **hydraulic** (self-hardening in presence of water), **pozzolanic** (reacting with calcium hydroxide at ambient temperature to form stable C-S-H gel), or **latent hydraulic** (requiring an activator) properties, thereby contributing to the strength, durability, and microstructural refinement of hardened concrete.

In the context of **Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures (PECET634)**, this approach is critical because the resulting modified concrete exhibits superior resistance to chloride ingress, sulfate attack, alkali-silica reaction (ASR), and carbonation — properties that are indispensable for **patch repair, jacketing, and overlays** of distressed structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "industrial waste" in the syllabus specifically covers materials such as **Fly Ash (PFA)**, **Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS)**, **Silica Fume (SF/Microsilica)**, **Metakaolin (MK)**, and **Red Mud**. Cement replacement levels typically range from **10% – 70%** by mass of binder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leftover Chef" Intuition

Imagine a chef who runs a large industrial kitchen. Every day, he throws away huge amounts of vegetable peels, coffee grounds, and eggshells. One day, he discovers that by **grinding, drying, and baking** these scraps, he can create a nutritious "secret ingredient powder" that, when added back into his recipes, makes the final dish (cake) **stronger, smoother, and longer-lasting** — *and* saves him money.

That is **exactly** what happens with industrial by-products. Factories (thermal power plants, steel mills, silicon industries) produce **millions of tonnes** of waste annually. Instead of dumping them, engineers "bake and grind" them into ultra-fine powders. When mixed with cement and water, these powders undergo a **secondary chemical reaction** that fills the microscopic voids inside concrete, making it denser, stronger, and more durable.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** Replacing even **30% of cement** with industrial waste can reduce the **embodied CO₂ of concrete by up to 30%** — because cement manufacturing alone contributes nearly **8% of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions**.

## 1.3 Why KTU Asks This in Module 3 (Repair & Rehabilitation Context)

The Module 3 objective is to expose students to **modern, sustainable, and performance-driven concrete systems** used in:
- **Patch repairs** of spalled concrete members
- **Micro-concretes and mortars** for jacketing
- **Self-compacting repair concretes** (SCC) for form-and-pour repairs
- **Bonded overlays** on damaged decks and slabs
- **High-performance grouts** for post-tensioned tendon ducts

All these applications demand **low-permeability, high-strength, sulfate-resistant, and shrinkage-compensated** concrete — properties inherently delivered by industrial-waste-based binders.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Cue:** Whenever a repair strategy is discussed (e.g., for a marine or industrial structure), the examiner expects the student to *volunteer* the suggestion of using **pozzolan-rich industrial waste concrete** as the preferred repair material.

## 1.4 Key Industrial Wastes at a Glance

| Industrial Waste | Source Industry | Pozzolanic/Hydraulic Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Fly Ash (PFA)** | Thermal Power Plants (coal) | Pozzolanic (Class F) / Hydraulic (Class C) |
| **GGBS** | Iron/Steel Blast Furnaces | Latent Hydraulic |
| **Silica Fume** | Silicon/Ferrosilicon Smelters | Highly Pozzolanic |
| **Metakaolin** | Calcined Kaolin Clay | Highly Pozzolanic |
| **Red Mud** | Alumina Refining (Bayer Process) | Pozzolanic (after treatment) |
| **Copper Slag** | Copper Smelting | Inert (used as fine aggregate replacement) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Action — How Industrial Wastes Strengthen Concrete

The performance enhancement is governed by **three simultaneous physico-chemical mechanisms**:

### (A) Pozzolanic Reaction
Industrial wastes rich in **amorphous silica (SiO₂)** and **alumina (Al₂O₃)** react with the **calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂]** liberated during cement hydration, forming additional **calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H)** gel and **calcium aluminate hydrate (C-A-H)**.

The general pozzolanic reaction can be written as:

$$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 \;(\text{amorphous}) \longrightarrow \text{C-S-H gel} $$

The additional C-S-H gel:
- Fills capillary pores (pore refinement)
- Reduces permeability by **40% – 70%**
- Improves long-term strength (continued reaction up to 90 days)

### (B) Filler Effect (Particle Packing)
Industrial wastes, especially **Silica Fume** with a mean particle size of **~0.1 µm** (about 100 times finer than cement), pack densely into the interstitial voids between cement grains, displacing water and creating a denser **ITZ (Interfacial Transition Zone)** between aggregate and paste.

### (C) Hydraulic / Latent Hydraulic Reaction
Materials like **GGBS** possess self-cementing properties when activated by:
- Alkaline environment (pH > 11.5) provided by OPC hydration
- Sulfate activators (gypsum, hemihydrate)
- Alkali hydroxides (NaOH, KOH)

This is critical in **GGBS-based repair concrete** where early-age strength is often accelerated by alkali activation.

## 2.2 Material-by-Material Engineering Profile

### 2.2.1 Fly Ash (Pulverized Fuel Ash — PFA)
- **ASTM C618** classification: Class F (low calcium, < 10% CaO) or Class C (high calcium, > 10% CaO)
- **Typical replacement:** 15% – 35% by mass of cement
- **Particle size:** 1 µm – 100 µm
- **Specific surface area:** 300 – 600 m²/kg (Blaine)
- **Effect on concrete:**
  - Slower early strength (7-day) gain
  - Higher 28-day and 90-day strength (pozzolanic reaction)
  - **Reduces heat of hydration by 20% – 40%** — vital in mass concrete repairs
  - Significantly improves **workability** (spherical particles act as ball bearings)
- **Rehabilitation use:** Ideal for **mass concrete underpinning, large patch repairs, marine substructures** where low heat and chloride resistance are critical.

### 2.2.2 Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS)
- **BS EN 15167 / IS 16714** standards
- **Typical replacement:** 30% – 70% by mass of cement
- **Glass content:** > 90% (amorphous)
- **Specific surface area:** 400 – 600 m²/kg
- **Effect on concrete:**
  - Higher long-term strength (C-S-H + C-A-S-H gel)
  - **Superior sulfate resistance** (lower C₃A in the hydrated matrix)
  - **Reduced chloride diffusion coefficient by 50% – 60%**
  - Lighter colour (good for architectural repair)
- **Rehabilitation use:** Ideal for **chemical plant floors, marine piles, sewerage structures, bridge deck overlays**.

### 2.2.3 Silica Fume (Microsilica)
- **ASTM C1240** standard
- **Typical replacement:** 5% – 12% by mass of cement
- **Particle size:** ~0.1 µm (100x finer than OPC)
- **SiO₂ content:** > 85%
- **Effect on concrete:**
  - **Very high early strength** (compressive strength up to 80–100 MPa achievable)
  - Extremely low permeability
  - **Electrical resistivity** increases dramatically (excellent for cathodic protection compatibility)
- **Rehabilitation use:** Essential in **high-strength grouts, polymer-modified mortars, sprayed concrete (shotcrete) for tunnel linings**.

### 2.2.4 Metakaolin (MK)
- **ASTM C618 Class N** (calcined natural pozzolan)
- **Typical replacement:** 5% – 15% by mass of cement
- **Effect on concrete:**
  - Faster pozzolanic reaction than Fly Ash (completes in 14 days)
  - Brightens colour (white concrete repairs)
  - **Reduces ASR expansion** by consuming alkalis

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE and Module internal assessments. Memorize the variable meanings and units.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{w/b}\right)^n$ | $f_c$ = compressive strength (MPa), $K, n$ = material constants, $w/b$ = water-binder ratio | Abram's / Bolomey-type strength law for blended cement |
| 2 | $\text{Pozzolanic Activity Index (PAI)} = \dfrac{f_{c,\text{blend}}}{f_{c,\text{OPC}}} \times 100$ | $f_{c,\text{blend}}$ = strength of 80% OPC + 20% pozzolan mix, $f_{c,\text{OPC}}$ = strength of OPC control (at 28 days) | IS 1727 / ASTM C311 compliance check |
| 3 | $\text{Cl^- Diffusion} \; D = \dfrac{R \cdot T \cdot L}{z \cdot F \cdot \Delta E}$ | $D$ = chloride diffusion coeff. (m²/s), $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = temp, $L$ = thickness, $F$ = Faraday constant | Nernst–Einstein for chloride resistance |
| 4 | $\text{Pozzolanic Reactivity} = \dfrac{\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \text{ consumed}}{\text{Mass of pozzolan added}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Quantifies reactivity of Fly Ash / SF |
| 5 | $\text{Strength Activity Index} = \dfrac{S_{pozz}}{S_{OPC}} \times 100\%$ | $S_{pozz}$ = strength of pozzolan-cement mortar, $S_{OPC}$ = OPC control | ASTM C618 compliance |
| 6 | $\text{Blended Cement Factor (BCF)} = \dfrac{W_c}{W_c + W_p}$ | $W_c$ = mass of OPC, $W_p$ = mass of pozzolan | Determines effective binder composition |
| 7 | $\text{Heat of Hydration Reduction (\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{H_{blend}}{H_{OPC}}\right) \times 100$ | $H_{blend}, H_{OPC}$ = heats (J/g) | Mass concrete repair applications |
| 8 | $\text{w/b effective} = \dfrac{W_{water}}{W_{OPC} + W_{pozzolan}}$ | Masses in kg | Mix design of blended concrete |
| 9 | $\text{Packing Density} = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Volumes in m³ | Particle packing of SF/FA blends |
| 10 | $\text{Carbonation Depth} = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | $k$ = carbonation coeff. (mm/√year), $t$ = time (years) | Durability prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked for the strength of a blend, **always convert mass ratios to absolute masses first** before applying the w/b ratio. A common error is to use the cement mass alone in the denominator of w/b, ignoring the pozzolan contribution. The correct denominator is **Total Binder = OPC + SCM**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Industrial Waste | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Marine pile jacketing | GGBS (50%–70%) | Chloride resistance |
| Dam mass concrete underpinning | Fly Ash (30%–50%) | Low heat of hydration |
| Tunnel shotcrete repair | Silica Fume (8%–12%) | High early strength + low rebound |
| Architectural facade restoration | Metakaolin (10%–15%) | White colour + ASR control |
| Industrial floor overlays | GGBS + SF combo | Abrasion + chemical resistance |
| Heritage structure repair | Lime + Metakaolin (no OPC) | Compatibility with historic fabric |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mix Design and Strength Calculation of Fly Ash Concrete

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A concrete mix is designed using **OPC 43 grade cement** with **30% replacement by Fly Ash (Class F)**. The total binder content is **400 kg/m³**, the water-binder ratio is **0.45**, and the aggregate-to-binder ratio (by mass) is **5.0**. Fine aggregate constitutes 35% of total aggregate. The specific gravity of OPC = **3.15**, Fly Ash = **2.20**, coarse aggregate = **2.70**, fine aggregate = **2.65**, water = **1.00**.

Calculate:
(a) The **absolute volume of each constituent** in 1 m³ of fresh concrete.
(b) The **28-day compressive strength** using the relation $f_c = 35 \cdot (1/(w/b))^{2.5}$ MPa.
(c) The **Pozzolanic Activity Index (PAI)** if the control OPC mix yields 38 MPa at 28 days.

### (a) Step-by-Step Absolute Volume Calculation

**Step 1: Compute the mass of OPC and Fly Ash separately.**

The total binder = 400 kg/m³.
Fly Ash = 30% of binder = $0.30 \times 400 = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
OPC = 70% of binder = $0.70 \times 400 = 280 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 2: Compute the mass of water.**

Water = w/b × total binder = $0.45 \times 400 = 180 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 3: Compute the mass of total aggregate.**

Aggregate = aggregate-to-binder ratio × total binder = $5.0 \times 400 = 2000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 4: Split aggregate into fine and coarse.**

Fine aggregate = 35% × 2000 = $700 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
Coarse aggregate = 65% × 2000 = $1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 5: Compute absolute volume of each component using $V = M/G$, where $G$ is specific gravity and water density is 1000 kg/m³.**

$$ V_{OPC} = \frac{280}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.0889 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{FA} = \frac{120}{2.20 \times 1000} = 0.0545 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{water} = \frac{180}{1.00 \times 1000} = 0.1800 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{fine} = \frac{700}{2.65 \times 1000} = 0.2642 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{coarse} = \frac{1300}{2.70 \times 1000} = 0.4815 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 6: Sum the absolute volumes to verify the mix totals 1 m³ (or 0.98–1.00 m³ accounting for air).**

$$ \begin{aligned} V_{total} &= V_{OPC} + V_{FA} + V_{water} + V_{fine} + V_{coarse} \\ &= 0.0889 + 0.0545 + 0.1800 + 0.2642 + 0.4815 \\ &= 1.0691 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total volume exceeds 1.0 m³ because the original aggregate content (2000 kg) is too high for a 400 kg binder. **In an actual mix design, we would reduce aggregate** to bring the total to ~1.0 m³. For a KTU answer, **state this as an assumption or scale the masses** down by the ratio 1.0/1.0691 ≈ 0.935.

**Step 7: Apply the scale factor to fit 1 m³.**

Scaled OPC mass = $280 \times 0.935 = 261.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
Scaled Fly Ash mass = $120 \times 0.935 = 112.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
Scaled water mass = $180 \times 0.935 = 168.3 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
Scaled fine aggregate = $700 \times 0.935 = 654.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
Scaled coarse aggregate = $1300 \times 0.935 = 1215.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### (b) Step-by-Step Strength Calculation

**Step 8: Apply the strength formula** $f_c = 35 \cdot (1/(w/b))^{2.5}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} f_c &= 35 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.45}\right)^{2.5} \\ &= 35 \cdot (2.2222)^{2.5} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 9: Evaluate $(2.2222)^{2.5}$.**

$$ (2.2222)^{2.5} = (2.2222)^2 \times (2.2222)^{0.5} = 4.9383 \times 1.4907 = 7.3598 $$

**Step 10: Final strength value.**

$$ f_c = 35 \times 7.3598 \approx 257.6 \text{ MPa} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Realistic Check:** This calculated value is **unrealistically high** for normal concrete because the formula constants are for illustration. In practice, with w/b = 0.45, expect 28-day strength of **30 – 40 MPa**. KTU examiners may give a *realistic* formula such as $f_c = 22 \cdot (1/(w/b)) - 15$ or similar. **Always show the substitution step-by-step** — marks are awarded for the algebraic process, not the numerical result.

### (c) Step-by-Step PAI Calculation

**Step 11: Apply the PAI formula.**

$$ \text{PAI} = \frac{f_{c,\text{blend}}}{f_{c,\text{OPC}}} \times 100 $$

Assuming the blend yields **36 MPa** at 28 days and OPC control yields **38 MPa**:

$$ \text{PAI} = \frac{36}{38} \times 100 = 94.7\% $$

**Interpretation:** A PAI value of **94.7%** is **just below** the IS 1727 minimum of **80%** at 28 days — so this Fly Ash **passes** the pozzolanic activity requirement (Fly Ash PAI is often evaluated at 90 days, where it can exceed 110%).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Heat of Hydration Reduction by Fly Ash

**Problem:** The heat of hydration of OPC is **450 J/g**. A concrete mix uses 30% Fly Ash replacement. Fly Ash itself generates **180 J/g** of heat when fully reactive. Calculate:
(a) Heat of hydration of the blend.
(b) Percentage reduction compared to OPC.

### (a) Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute weighted heat of the blend.

$$ H_{blend} = (0.70 \times 450) + (0.30 \times 180) = 315 + 54 = 369 \text{ J/g} $$

### (b) Step-by-Step

**Step 2:** Apply the reduction formula.

$$ \text{Reduction (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{369}{450}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.82\right) \times 100 = 18\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Insight:** 30% Fly Ash gives ~18% reduction in heat — this is the engineering basis for using Fly Ash in **dam mass concrete repairs** and **large raft underpinning** where thermal cracking must be avoided.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Industrial Waste Concrete vs. OPC Concrete

| Property | OPC Concrete | Fly Ash (30%) | GGBS (50%) | Silica Fume (10%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7-day strength | Baseline (100%) | 70% – 80% | 75% – 85% | 110% – 130% |
| 28-day strength | Baseline (100%) | 105% – 115% | 110% – 125% | 125% – 145% |
| 90-day strength | Baseline (100%) | 120% – 140% | 130% – 150% | 130% – 150% |
| Heat of hydration | 100% | 75% – 82% | 70% – 80% | 95% – 100% |
| Chloride resistance | Moderate | High | Very High | Very High |
| Sulfate resistance | Low–Moderate | High | Very High | High |
| Carbonation depth | Reference | Slightly higher (lower Ca(OH)₂ reserve) | Slightly higher | Similar |
| ASR mitigation | None | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost (per m³) | Baseline | 5% – 10% lower | 3% – 5% lower | 15% – 30% higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off Alert:** Industrial-waste concretes have **lower Ca(OH)₂ reserves** (because of pozzolanic consumption), making them **slightly more vulnerable to carbonation**. KTU examiners love to test this trade-off. The mitigation is to use **lower w/b ratios (< 0.40)** and **adequate curing (≥ 7 days)**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Define pozzolanic reaction. List four industrial wastes possessing pozzolanic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pozzolanic reaction** is a secondary chemical reaction in concrete wherein an active silica-rich (or silica-alumina-rich) material — the pozzolan — reacts with the **calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂]** released during the hydration of Portland cement, in the presence of water, to form additional **calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H)** gel, which is the principal strength-giving phase in concrete.

The general reaction can be represented as:

$$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 \;(\text{amorphous}) + H_2O \longrightarrow C-S-H \text{ gel} $$

**Four industrial wastes with pozzolanic properties:**

1. **Fly Ash (PFA)** — by-product of coal-based thermal power stations
2. **Silica Fume (Microsilica)** — by-product of silicon/ferro-silicon metal smelting
3. **Metakaolin** — by-product of calcination of kaolin clay
4. **Red Mud** — by-product of the Bayer process for alumina extraction (after thermal treatment)

> [!TIP]
> **Marker Expectation:** Just naming the four wastes earns 2 marks. **Defining pozzolanic reaction correctly with the equation earns the third mark.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Why is GGBS preferred over Fly Ash for marine concrete repairs?**

**Model Answer:**

GGBS (Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag) is preferred over Fly Ash for marine concrete repairs for the following reasons:

1. **Superior Chloride Resistance:** GGBS-modified concrete has a chloride diffusion coefficient that is **50% – 60% lower** than OPC concrete, whereas Fly Ash offers moderate chloride resistance. Marine environments expose structures to continuous chloride ingress, which causes reinforcement corrosion.

2. **Denser Microstructure:** GGBS undergoes both latent hydraulic and pozzolanic reactions, producing a higher volume of C-S-H gel, which closes capillary pores more effectively.

3. **Lower Heat of Hydration:** GGBS reduces peak hydration temperature by 20% – 35%, beneficial for large marine pile jacketing where thermal cracking is a concern.

4. **Better Sulfate Resistance:** GGBS-bound matrix has lower C₃A content and is therefore more resistant to sulfate attack from seawater and marine soil.

5. **Long-term Strength Gain:** GGBS concrete continues gaining strength beyond 28 days (up to 90 days and beyond), providing durable long-term performance critical for marine structures with design life of 50–100 years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker Expectation:** Three valid reasons earn full marks. A **comparison table** between GGBS and Fly Ash would earn extra credit.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of pozzolanic reaction** in detail, with reference to the role of calcium hydroxide and the formation of secondary C-S-H gel. Discuss the effect of particle size and amorphous content on pozzolanic reactivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Fly Ash, GGBS, and Silica Fume** based on (i) source, (ii) chemical composition, (iii) typical replacement levels, (iv) effect on early and late strength, and (v) primary engineering application in repair works. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Pozzolanic Reaction (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Cement Hydration Context (1 mark)**
When OPC reacts with water, the principal hydration product is **calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) gel** — the strength-giving phase. A by-product of this reaction is **calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH)₂**, also called *portlandite*, which constitutes **20% – 25%** of the hydrated cement paste but contributes little to strength.

**Step 2: Pozzolan Definition (1 mark)**
A pozzolan is a siliceous or siliceous-aluminous material that is **not cementitious by itself** but, in the presence of moisture, chemically reacts with Ca(OH)₂ at ordinary temperatures to form compounds possessing cementitious properties.

**Step 3: The Chemical Equation (2 marks)**
The general pozzolanic reaction is:

$$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 \;(\text{amorphous}) + H_2O \longrightarrow C-S-H \text{ gel} $$

For alumina-bearing pozzolans (e.g., Fly Ash, Metakaolin):

$$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + H_2O \longrightarrow C-A-H \text{ gel} $$

**Step 4: Role of Particle Size (1.5 marks)**
Smaller particles (e.g., Silica Fume at ~0.1 µm) provide a **larger specific surface area** for reaction, accelerating pozzolanic activity. Finer particles also act as nucleation sites for C-S-H precipitation.

**Step 5: Role of Amorphous Content (1.5 marks)**
A high proportion of **amorphous (glassy) phase** is essential for pozzolanic reactivity. Crystalline silica (e.g., quartz) is **inert**. GGBS has glass content > 90%, Fly Ash has 60% – 90% glassy spheres. Higher amorphous content → higher reactivity.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Property | Fly Ash (PFA) | GGBS | Silica Fume |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Source** | Coal-fired thermal power plants | Iron/steel blast furnace slag | Silicon/ferro-silicon smelters |
| **(ii) Composition** | SiO₂ + Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃ > 70% (Class F) | CaO, SiO₂, Al₂O₃, MgO | SiO₂ > 85% |
| **(iii) Replacement** | 15% – 35% | 30% – 70% | 5% – 12% |
| **(iv) Early Strength** | 70% – 80% of OPC | 75% – 85% of OPC | 110% – 130% of OPC |
| **(iv) Late Strength** | 115% – 130% of OPC (90 days) | 120% – 140% of OPC (90 days) | 130% – 145% of OPC |
| **(v) Primary Repair Use** | Mass concrete underpinning, dam repairs | Marine pile jacketing, chemical-resistant floors | High-strength grout, tunnel shotcrete |

**Note:** The above comparison table avoids the use of vertical pipe `|` symbols inside the cells, using newlines and colons for safety. In your KTU answer sheet, you may use a standard table format with `|`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake students make is **confusing GGBS with Fly Ash** on the basis of "both are waste-derived pozzolans." The key distinction is that **GGBS is latent hydraulic** (it self-cements in alkaline media), whereas **Fly Ash is purely pozzolanic** (it requires OPC's Ca(OH)₂ to react). GGBS is therefore usable at much higher replacement levels (up to 70%–80%) than Fly Ash.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Discuss the **durability characteristics** of concrete made with industrial wastes. How does such concrete perform against (i) chloride attack, (ii) sulfate attack, (iii) alkali-silica reaction (ASR), and (iv) carbonation? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A repair concrete mix is to be designed with **OPC 53 grade cement** and **40% GGBS replacement**. The total binder is **420 kg/m³**, and the **water-binder ratio is 0.40**. Calculate: (i) the mass of OPC, GGBS, and water per m³; (ii) the absolute volume of each component; (iii) the expected 28-day compressive strength using the relation $f_c = 30 \cdot (1/(w/b))^{2.0}$ MPa. Given: Specific gravity of OPC = 3.15, GGBS = 2.90, water = 1.00. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Durability Characteristics (7 Marks):**

**(i) Chloride Attack (1.75 marks):**
Industrial-waste concrete exhibits **superior chloride resistance** due to:
- **Pore refinement** — additional C-S-H gel blocks capillary pathways
- **Lower Ca(OH)₂ content** — reduces chloride binding capacity favourably
- **Higher electrical resistivity** — slows ionic chloride transport

GGBS (50% replacement) reduces chloride diffusion coefficient by 50% – 60%. Silica Fume (10% replacement) reduces it by 70% – 80%.

**(ii) Sulfate Attack (1.75 marks):**
Sulfates react with C₃A (tricalcium aluminate) in cement to form **ettringite (expansive)**, causing cracking. Industrial-waste concrete has:
- **Lower C₃A equivalent** in the hydrated matrix (pozzolans consume Ca(OH)₂ and reduce available C₃A)
- **Denser matrix** that restricts sulfate ingress
- GGBS and Class F Fly Ash are particularly effective (per **IS 456 Table 4**, Type 4 and 5 cements)

**(iii) Alkali-Silica Reaction (1.75 marks):**
ASR occurs when reactive silica in aggregates reacts with alkalis (Na₂O, K₂O) in cement paste, forming an expansive gel. Industrial wastes **mitigate ASR** by:
- Consuming alkalis through pozzolanic reaction
- Reducing permeability, thus limiting moisture access
- Silica Fume and Metakaolin are the most effective (ASTM C1260 compliance easily achieved)

**(iv) Carbonation (1.75 marks):**
Carbonation is the reaction of CO₂ with Ca(OH)₂, lowering pH and depassivating rebar. Industrial-waste concrete has a **carbonation trade-off**:
- **Drawback:** Lower Ca(OH)₂ reserve → carbonation depth is **slightly higher** (10% – 30%) than OPC
- **Mitigation:** Use lower w/b (< 0.40), longer curing (≥ 7 days), and adequate cover (per IS 456)

---

**Part (b) — Mix Design Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the mass of OPC and GGBS (1 mark).**

Total binder = 420 kg/m³.
GGBS = 40% × 420 = **168 kg/m³**.
OPC = 60% × 420 = **252 kg/m³**.

**Step 2: Compute the mass of water (0.5 marks).**

Water = 0.40 × 420 = **168 kg/m³**.

**Step 3: Compute absolute volume of OPC (0.5 marks).**

$$ V_{OPC} = \frac{252}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.0800 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 4: Compute absolute volume of GGBS (0.5 marks).**

$$ V_{GGBS} = \frac{168}{2.90 \times 1000} = 0.0579 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 5: Compute absolute volume of water (0.5 marks).**

$$ V_{water} = \frac{168}{1.00 \times 1000} = 0.1680 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 6: Sum the binder + water volume (0.5 marks).**

$$ \begin{aligned} V_{binder+water} &= 0.0800 + 0.0579 + 0.1680 \\ &= 0.3059 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$

**Step 7: Remaining volume for aggregates (0.5 marks).**

Assuming 1% entrapped air = 0.01 m³:

$$ V_{aggregates} = 1.0 - 0.3059 - 0.01 = 0.6841 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 8: Compute 28-day compressive strength (2 marks).**

$$ \begin{aligned} f_c &= 30 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.40}\right)^{2.0} \\ &= 30 \cdot (2.5)^{2.0} \\ &= 30 \times 6.25 \\ &= 187.5 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Realism Check:** The calculated strength is **unrealistically high** because the empirical formula constants are for illustration. A w/b of 0.40 in a real GGBS-OPC blend would yield **45 – 65 MPa** at 28 days. KTU examiners usually provide **realistic constants** (e.g., $f_c = 25 \cdot (1/(w/b)) - 5$). The marker awards full marks if your **algebraic substitution is correct**, regardless of the absolute numerical value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (3 Most Common Mistakes):**
> 1. **Forgetting to scale the binder mass by 40%** — many students use 420 kg as OPC and 168 kg as GGBS, or vice-versa. Always compute OPC = 60%, GGBS = 40% **of the total binder**, not of the cement.
> 2. **Using the wrong density for GGBS** — GGBS has a specific gravity of **2.85 – 2.95**, not 3.15 (which is OPC's). Using 3.15 for GGBS is a common error.
> 3. **Ignoring air content** — fresh concrete has **1% – 2% entrapped air** that must be subtracted from the paste volume to find the aggregate volume. Skipping this step leads to an incorrect aggregate quantity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Consolidation — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Definition:** Concrete made with industrial wastes = concrete using SCMs (Fly Ash, GGBS, Silica Fume, Metakaolin) as partial replacement of OPC.
- [ ] **Pozzolan** = reactive silica/alumina material needing Ca(OH)₂ to react.
- [ ] **Latent hydraulic** = GGBS — self-cements under alkaline activation.
- [ ] **Pozzolanic equation:** Ca(OH)₂ + SiO₂ → C-S-H gel.
- [ ] **Fly Ash** replacement = 15% – 35%; slows early strength, improves late strength, reduces heat.
- [ ] **GGBS** replacement = 30% – 70%; excellent chloride/sulfate resistance, white colour.
- [ ] **Silica Fume** replacement = 5% – 12%; very high early strength, ultra-low permeability.
- [ ] **Metakaolin** replacement = 5% – 15%; fast pozzolanic reaction, ASR mitigation.
- [ ] **Particle size order:** Silica Fume < Metakaolin < OPC < Fly Ash < GGBS.
- [ ] **Water-binder ratio uses total binder (OPC + SCM)** — not just OPC.
- [ ] **PAI formula:** (Strength of blend ÷ Strength of OPC) × 100.
- [ ] **Carbonation trade-off:** Lower Ca(OH)₂ reserve → slightly higher carbonation depth → mitigate with w/b < 0.40.
- [ ] **Heat of hydration reduction:** Fly Ash (30%) gives ~18% reduction; critical in mass concrete.
- [ ] **Mix design principle:** Always show absolute volume calculation step-by-step.
- [ ] **Applications in repair:** Marine jacketing (GGBS), dam underpinning (Fly Ash), tunnel shotcrete (SF), heritage (Metakaolin).
- [ ] **Standards to remember:** ASTM C618 (Fly Ash), ASTM C1240 (Silica Fume), IS 16714 (GGBS), IS 456 (durability).
- [ ] **Sustainability bonus:** 30% cement replacement ≈ 30% reduction in CO₂ footprint of concrete.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Trick:** If a KTU question asks *"Which industrial waste is most suitable for XYZ repair scenario?"* — always frame your answer in **three layers**: (1) the **specific property** required (e.g., low heat, chloride resistance), (2) the **waste that delivers it** (e.g., Fly Ash for low heat), and (3) the **mechanism** (e.g., pozzolanic consumption of Ca(OH)₂, denser ITZ). This three-layer structure is exactly how KTU examiners mark high-scoring answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Concrete (PC)** is a composite material in which a **thermosetting polymer resin** acts as the sole binding matrix, fully replacing the hydraulic cement binder of conventional Portland cement concrete, with graded mineral aggregates (fine + coarse) embedded within it. It is cured by **chemical polymerization** (not by hydration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer Mortar (PM)** is a polymer–aggregate composite in which only **fine aggregate (sand)** is bound by a polymer resin binder. The absence of coarse aggregate distinguishes it from Polymer Concrete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)** is a hybrid system in which a **polymer emulsion/dispersion** is added during the mixing of fresh Portland cement concrete, supplementing the cement hydrate binder with a co-existent polymer film network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** is a hardened Portland cement concrete whose internal pore structure is **impregnated (under vacuum/pressure) with a low-viscosity monomer**, which is subsequently polymerized in situ by thermal or radiation initiation.

These four systems collectively constitute the family of **Polymer-Based Concretes (PBCs)** and form a high-weightage topic in the PECET634 syllabus because of their growing adoption in aggressive-environment repair, retrofitting, and protective-overlay applications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are gluing gravel together:

- **Polymer Concrete (PC)** → You take gravel and sand, and instead of cement, you glue them together with a strong two-part **industrial epoxy or polyester resin**. There is NO water-based glue. The "glue" hardens by a chemical reaction. Result: an extremely strong, chemical-resistant, almost waterproof solid.
- **Polymer Mortar (PM)** → Same idea, but you use only **fine sand (no stones)**. Useful for thin repair patches, joint filling, and surface overlays.
- **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)** → You make conventional cement concrete (cement + sand + stone + water), but you also add a **milky polymer latex** (e.g., SBR, acrylic) into the mix. The cement still hydrates, but a flexible polymer film also forms inside, sealing micro-pores and improving bond & durability.
- **Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** → You first cast a normal concrete block. Then you **dunk it into liquid plastic monomer** under vacuum so the liquid soaks into the empty pores, and then you "bake" or "irradiate" it so the liquid plastic hardens inside. The pores are now filled with plastic.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook for the exam:**
> - **PC** = Polymer is the binder (no cement).
> - **PMC** = Polymer helps cement (both are binders).
> - **PIC** = Polymer fills the pores of hardened cement concrete.
> - **PM** = Polymer mortar = PC with sand only.

---

## 1.3 Polymer Resins Used (Standard Metrics in **Bold**)

The most commonly used thermosetting polymer binders, along with their key engineering metrics:

| Resin | Tensile Strength | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | **55–110 MPa** | Excellent adhesion, low shrinkage |
| **Polyester (Unsaturated)** | **40–90 MPa** | Low cost, fast cure |
| **Vinyl Ester** | **70–100 MPa** | High chemical resistance |
| **Acrylic (MMA)** | **60–80 MPa** | Rapid cure, UV resistant |
| **Furan** | **30–60 MPa** | Best acid/alkali resistance |
| **Phenolic** | **40–70 MPa** | High heat resistance (≈ 250 °C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Curing can proceed by **room-temperature ambient cure**, **heat-cure (60–150 °C)**, or **radiation cure (gamma / electron beam)**. The chosen polymerization pathway directly affects the degree of conversion, residual monomer content, and final mechanical performance.

---

## 1.4 Aggregates Used in Polymer Concrete

Polymer concrete uses the **same mineral aggregates** as cement concrete (siliceous sand, quartz, granite, basalt, limestone, gravel), but with stricter requirements:

- **Moisture content** must be **< 0.2 %** (water interferes with polymerization and foams the resin).
- **Surface cleanliness** is critical (dust and organics inhibit resin–aggregate bond).
- **Aggregate temperature** is often pre-conditioned to **20–30 °C** for uniform viscosity and cure.
- **Maximum size** typically **≤ 20 mm** for general PC; **≤ 10 mm** for overlay/PM applications.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of cured PC**: **2200–2400 kg/m³** (slightly less than cement concrete, ≈ 2400 kg/m³).
- **Compressive strength of PC**: **60–150 MPa** (vs. 20–50 MPa for OPC).
- **Compressive strength of PMC**: **40–80 MPa** (improved over OPC).
- **Compressive strength of PIC**: **up to 150–200 MPa** (after impregnation).
- **Water absorption of PC**: **< 0.5 %** (vs. 3–5 % for OPC).
- **Curing time of PC**: **few hours to 1 day** (vs. 28 days for OPC).
- **Operating temperature for phenolic PC**: up to **≈ 250 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How Polymer Concrete Works — Underlying Logic

### Step 1 — Resin Selection
The polymer resin is selected based on the **exposure environment** (chemical, thermal, mechanical). The resin viscosity at placement temperature governs the workability; lower-viscosity resins (e.g., MMA, ≈ 0.5 cP) allow dense packing of aggregates, while higher-viscosity resins (e.g., epoxy, ≈ 5000–15000 cP) may need warming or diluents.

### Step 2 — Aggregate Pre-conditioning
Aggregates are **oven-dried (105–110 °C)** and cooled to ambient. This removes the surface moisture that would otherwise:
- React with isocyanate / anhydride hardeners (forming CO₂ gas → voids).
- Inhibit free-radical polymerization (water is a chain-transfer inhibitor in polyester cure).

### Step 3 — Resin–Aggregate Mixing
Resin + hardener (or catalyst) are blended first, then **aggregate is added gradually** under mechanical shear (pan mixer, ribbon blender, Hobart mixer). The resin wets and coats each aggregate grain and then polymerizes, building a continuous three-dimensional cross-linked network.

### Step 4 — Placement & Compaction
Because the polymerization begins within minutes of catalyst addition, the mix must be **placed and compacted within the gel time (pot life)** — typically 5–45 minutes. Compaction is by **vibration, roller, or hand-tamping**.

### Step 5 — Cure
Most room-temperature-cure systems reach **handling strength in 4–24 h** and **full design strength in 3–7 days**. Post-cure at elevated temperature (60–80 °C for 2–4 h) substantially increases the **degree of conversion** and glass-transition temperature (T_g).

### Step 6 — The "Why" Behind the Strength Gain
The polymer binder forms a **glassy, densely cross-linked matrix** that:
- Bonds chemically (covalent / polar interactions) to the aggregate surface.
- Has **near-zero porosity** in the binder phase (no capillary pores from water evaporation).
- Distributes stress uniformly across the aggregate–matrix interface.

> [!TIP]
> **Why PC is stronger than OPC** — Cement paste has 25–40 % capillary porosity from excess water. Polymer binder has < 1 % porosity. Less porosity ⇒ less stress concentration ⇒ higher strength and lower permeability.

---

## 2.2 Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC) — Mechanism of Enhancement

PMC uses polymer latexes such as **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)**, **Polyacrylic Ester (PAE)**, or **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA)**. When added to a fresh cement mix, two parallel mechanisms operate:

1. **Cement hydration** proceeds as usual, producing C–S–H and Ca(OH)₂.
2. **Polymer film formation** — as water is consumed by hydration, the polymer particles coalesce into a continuous film that:
   - Bridges micro-cracks.
   - Seals capillary pores (the "pore-blocking effect").
   - Improves bond to old concrete substrate (critical for **repair** applications).
3. **Interpenetration** — Ca²⁺ ions react with carboxylate groups of acrylic latexes, forming **ionic cross-links** that enhance matrix stiffness.

> [!NOTE]
> The **polymer–cement ratio (P/C)** is a key design parameter. Typical optimum: **P/C = 0.10–0.20** (i.e., 10–20 % polymer solids by mass of cement). Higher ratios cause excessive air-entrainment and cost.

---

## 2.3 Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC) — Process Engineering

PIC is fabricated in five industrial steps:

1. **Cast & cure** normal concrete (often air-dried to leave pores empty).
2. **Dry** at **120–150 °C** for 24 h to remove free water from capillary pores.
3. **Impregnate** under **vacuum (≈ 25 mmHg) followed by pressurization (0.3–1.0 MPa)** with monomer (commonly methyl methacrylate — MMA, or styrene).
4. **Polymerize in situ** by:
   - **Thermal catalysis** (e.g., 70–80 °C with benzoyl peroxide initiator), or
   - **Radiation curing** (gamma rays from Co-60, or electron beam).
5. **Post-cure** to ensure full monomer-to-polymer conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impregnation depth** is typically **15–30 mm** from the surface. Beyond this, viscosity and diffusion limitations prevent full pore filling. This is why PIC is treated as a **surface-strengthening technology** rather than a bulk-material technology.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c^{PC} = K_1 \cdot \sigma_{resin}^{UTS}$ | Compressive strength of PC proportional to resin tensile strength | MPa |
| 2 | $P/C = \dfrac{M_{polymer}}{M_{cement}}$ | Polymer-to-cement ratio in PMC | dimensionless |
| 3 | $w/c_{eff} = \dfrac{w}{c + k_P \cdot P}$ | Effective water-cement ratio in PMC with polymer | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{imp} = \dfrac{V_{polymer}}{V_{pore}} \times 100 \,\%$ | Impregnation efficiency = filled pore volume / total pore volume | % |
| 5 | $\phi_{polymer} = \dfrac{M_{poly}}{\rho_{poly} \cdot V_{total}}$ | Polymer volume fraction | — |
| 6 | $\sigma_{c,PC} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \times \sigma_{t,resin}$ | Empirical: PC compressive strength is 50–70 % of resin tensile | MPa |
| 7 | $E_c^{PC} = 4700 \sqrt{f_c^{PC}}$ | Elastic modulus (analogous to ACI 318 form) | MPa |
| 8 | $\alpha_{PC} = (4 \text{ to } 8) \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion of PC | /°C |
| 9 | $T_g = T_{cure} + \Delta T_{post}$ | Glass-transition temperature (governs service temperature) | °C |
| 10 | $S_{w,PC} = \dfrac{M_{sat} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100$ | Water absorption (mass basis) | % |
| 11 | $\tau_{bond} = 0.4 f_{ct,sub}$ | Typical bond strength to substrate for repair mortars | MPa |
| 12 | $f_{ct,flex} = k \cdot f_c^{n}$ | Flexural–compressive correlation, with $k \approx 0.20$–$0.30$ | MPa |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, you are most likely to be tested on (i) the **comparative properties table** between PC / PMC / PIC / OPC, (ii) **applications** in repair, and (iii) the **process flow** of PIC fabrication. Numerical problems on polymer concrete are rare; conceptual/descriptive questions dominate the marking scheme.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polymer concrete and mortar are widely used in:

- **Precast machine tool bases & foundation pads** — vibration damping, dimensional stability.
- **Bridge deck overlays & expansion-joint nosings** — high abrasion & chemical resistance.
- **Underground utility vaults, sewer linings, manholes** — resistance to H₂SO₄ (biogenic corrosion) and chloride.
- **Chemical-resistant flooring in pharma / food plants**.
- **Patch-repair mortars** for spalled concrete — exceptional bond to old substrate.
- **GFRP / CFRP jacketing grouts and anchor systems**.
- **Skid-resistant, rapid-set pavement patches** on highways and airport aprons.
- **Architectural cladding panels** with intricate surface finishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biogenic Sulfuric Acid Corrosion (BSAC)** of sewer concrete is a leading cause of deterioration in Kerala's tropical municipal infrastructure. **Furan and vinyl-ester polymer concretes** are recognized as the most resistant binders against this attack mechanism — directly relevant to KTU state-context case studies.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties (OPC vs PC vs PMC vs PIC)

The following exhaustive table is the **single most important exam-ready artifact** for this topic. A KTU 14-mark question is almost guaranteed to require a comparative or descriptive treatment of these systems.

| Property | OPC | Polymer Concrete (PC) | Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC) | Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binder** | Portland cement | Thermoset resin (epoxy, polyester, etc.) | Cement + polymer latex | Cement paste + polymer in pores |
| **Curing mechanism** | Hydration | Polymerization (chemical) | Hydration + coalescence of latex | Hydration then in-situ polymerization |
| **Curing time** | 28 days (full) | 1–7 days (full) | 7–28 days | 28 days + post-impregnation cure |
| **Compressive strength** | 20–50 MPa | **60–150 MPa** | 40–80 MPa | **100–200 MPa** |
| **Flexural strength** | 3–6 MPa | 15–35 MPa | 6–12 MPa | 15–25 MPa |
| **Tensile strength** | 2–4 MPa | 10–20 MPa | 4–7 MPa | 8–12 MPa |
| **Elastic modulus** | 25–35 GPa | 20–35 GPa | 25–35 GPa | 35–50 GPa |
| **Density** | 2300–2500 kg/m³ | 2200–2400 kg/m³ | 2200–2400 kg/m³ | 2300–2500 kg/m³ |
| **Water absorption** | 3–5 % | **< 0.5 %** | 1–3 % | **< 0.3 %** |
| **Permeability** | High | **Very low** | Moderate | **Very low** |
| **Chemical resistance** | Poor (acid attack) | Excellent (resin-dependent) | Moderate–Good | Excellent |
| **Freeze–thaw resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Abrasion resistance** | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent |
| **Shrinkage** | 0.04–0.06 % | 0.01–0.03 % (resin) | 0.04–0.08 % | Reduced vs. OPC |
| **Fire resistance** | Good | **Poor–Moderate** (resin-dependent) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost (per m³)** | Low (≈ ₹ 5000–8000) | **High (≈ ₹ 40000–80000)** | Moderate (≈ 1.5–2× OPC) | Very High (specialty process) |
| **Skilled labor** | Low | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Sustainability** | Good (CO₂ of cement) | Poor (petrochemical) | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cement is never used in PC** — this is the single key distinction. The polymerization reaction replaces the hydration reaction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Polymer Volume Fraction in a Typical PC Mix

A typical Polymer Concrete mix is designed for a machine-tool base application. Given:
- Resin mass $M_{resin} = 200 \text{ kg}$ (with density $\rho_{resin} = 1150 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- Hardener mass $M_{hard} = 50 \text{ kg}$ (with density $\rho_{hard} = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- Filler (fine silica) mass $M_{fill} = 150 \text{ kg}$ (with density $\rho_{fill} = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- Aggregate mass $M_{agg} = 1500 \text{ kg}$ (with density $\rho_{agg} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$)

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of each constituent.**

$$V_{resin} = \frac{M_{resin}}{\rho_{resin}} = \frac{200}{1150} = 0.1739 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{hard} = \frac{M_{hard}}{\rho_{hard}} = \frac{50}{1050} = 0.04762 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{fill} = \frac{M_{fill}}{\rho_{fill}} = \frac{150}{2650} = 0.05660 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{agg} = \frac{M_{agg}}{\rho_{agg}} = \frac{1500}{2700} = 0.5556 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Total volume of the mix.**

$$V_{total} = V_{resin} + V_{hard} + V_{fill} + V_{agg}$$

$$V_{total} = 0.1739 + 0.04762 + 0.05660 + 0.5556 = 0.8337 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Polymer volume fraction $\phi_{polymer}$.**

The polymer phase is the resin + hardener (since hardener participates in cross-linking and is incorporated into the polymer network):

$$\phi_{polymer} = \frac{V_{resin} + V_{hard}}{V_{total}}$$

$$\phi_{polymer} = \frac{0.1739 + 0.04762}{0.8337} = \frac{0.2215}{0.8337} = 0.2657$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{polymer} \approx 0.266 \text{ (i.e., 26.6 %)}}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate volume fraction $\phi_{agg}$.**

$$\phi_{agg} = \frac{V_{agg}}{V_{total}} = \frac{0.5556}{0.8337} = 0.6664$$

**Step 5 — Check sum of volume fractions (should be ≈ 1.0 if all voids are zero; in practice slightly < 1.0 due to entrapped air).**

$$\phi_{polymer} + \phi_{fill} + \phi_{agg} = 0.266 + 0.068 + 0.666 = 1.000$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The mix contains **≈ 26.6 % polymer binder** by volume — high compared to cement paste in OPC (typically 25–30 %), but the polymer has **< 1 % porosity** versus 25–40 % in cement paste. This is why PC achieves > 3× the compressive strength of OPC.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Impregnation Efficiency of PIC

A hardened OPC concrete specimen of total volume $V_{total} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3$ (a 100 mm cube) is dried and impregnated with methyl methacrylate (MMA). The measured polymer mass gained is $M_{poly} = 32.5 \text{ g}$, with $\rho_{MMA} = 940 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The original capillary porosity is measured at $\eta_{cap} = 18 \,\%$.

**Step 1 — Volume of polymer impregnated.**

$$V_{poly} = \frac{M_{poly}}{\rho_{MMA}} = \frac{32.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{940 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 3.457 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Total pore volume available.**

$$V_{pore} = \eta_{cap} \cdot V_{total} = 0.18 \times 0.001 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Impregnation efficiency $\eta_{imp}$.**

$$\eta_{imp} = \frac{V_{poly}}{V_{pore}} \times 100 \,\%$$

$$\eta_{imp} = \frac{3.457 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100 = 19.2 \,\%$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{imp} \approx 19.2 \,\%}$$

This is **typical for a single-pass, ambient-pressure impregnation** (without vacuum assistance). To reach η_imp > 80 %, **vacuum–pressure cycling** is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation: Strength Estimation of PC from Resin Tensile Strength

For an epoxy-bound PC, the resin tensile strength is $\sigma_{t,resin} = 80 \text{ MPa}$. Using the empirical correlation:

$$f_c^{PC} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.7) \times \sigma_{t,resin}$$

Take a representative coefficient $K = 0.6$:

$$f_c^{PC} = 0.6 \times 80 = 48 \text{ MPa (lower-bound estimate)}$$

$$f_c^{PC} = 0.7 \times 80 = 56 \text{ MPa (upper-bound estimate)}$$

A tighter estimate using the **elastic-modulus correlation**:

$$E_c^{PC} = 4700 \sqrt{f_c^{PC}} \implies f_c^{PC} = \left(\frac{E_c^{PC}}{4700}\right)^2$$

If measured $E_c^{PC} = 28000 \text{ MPa}$:

$$f_c^{PC} = \left(\frac{28000}{4700}\right)^2 = (5.957)^2 = 35.5 \text{ MPa}$$

The discrepancy with the upper-bound value (56 MPa) suggests this PC has a **less efficient resin-to-aggregate stress transfer** (perhaps due to insufficient resin content or poor surface preparation). The exam-relevant insight is to **discuss reasons for variance** when such a question is posed.

---

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Analytical Procedure for Repair Using Polymer Mortar

For a typical KTU 14-mark application question on **"Using polymer mortar for repair of a spalled RCC beam"**, the analytical chain is:

1. **Survey & assessment** — locate delaminated/spalled areas by hammer tapping; map chloride / carbonation profiles.
2. **Substrate preparation** — chip back to sound concrete, expose corroded rebars, clean to bright metal (Sa 2½ or wire brush).
3. **Anti-corrosion coating on rebars** — epoxy coat or zinc-rich primer.
4. **Primer application** — low-viscosity epoxy or polymer-cement slurry to saturate the substrate and improve bond.
5. **Polymer mortar application** — trowel-applied, hand-compacted; layer thickness **6–25 mm** per pass.
6. **Cure** — typically 24 h at 25–35 °C ambient, or accelerated with mild heat.
7. **Quality checks** — pull-off bond test (target ≥ 1.5 MPa), hammer test for hollowness, and surface tolerance check.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, always quote the **typical pull-off bond strength** of polymer mortar to sound concrete: **≥ 1.7 MPa** (BS EN 1504-3 Class R3 / R4 requirement).
