# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Distinguish between Polymer Concrete (PC) and Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC).**

**Model Answer:**

- **Polymer Concrete (PC):** The binder is **entirely a polymer resin** (e.g., epoxy, polyester). Cement is **not used**. Curing is by **chemical polymerization**. Used where high chemical resistance and rapid strength are required.
- **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC):** The binder is **cement + polymer latex** (e.g., SBR, acrylic). Cement still hydrates, and the polymer forms a film within the matrix. Used for repair overlays, waterproof plaster, and toppings.

The key contrast: **PC has no cement**; **PMC has both cement and polymer**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2. What is Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)? List its two major fabrication steps.**

**Model Answer:**

PIC is a hardened Portland cement concrete whose **capillary pore system** has been filled with a **monomer (e.g., methyl methacrylate)** which is subsequently **polymerized in situ**, transforming the porous cement matrix into a dense, high-strength composite.

The two major fabrication steps are:

1. **Impregnation** — dried concrete is submerged in a low-viscosity monomer under **vacuum followed by pressure** so that the monomer fills the pore network.
2. **In-situ polymerization** — the impregnated monomer is converted to polymer using **thermal initiators (e.g., benzoyl peroxide at 70–80 °C)** or **ionizing radiation (gamma / electron beam)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the classification of polymer-based concretes. Discuss in detail the composition, properties, and applications of **Polymer Concrete (PC)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the composition, mechanism of strength development, and engineering applications of **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)**. Compare its properties with ordinary Portland cement concrete. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Classification of Polymer-Based Concretes (PBCs):**
The four principal classes are:
- **Polymer Concrete (PC)** — polymer binder + aggregates.
- **Polymer Mortar (PM)** — polymer binder + fine aggregate only.
- **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)** — cement + polymer latex + aggregates.
- **Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** — pre-cured concrete with polymer-filled pores.

**2. Composition of PC:**
- **Binder (8–15 % by mass):** Epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, MMA, furan, or phenolic resin, with a hardener / catalyst.
- **Aggregates (85–92 % by mass):** Siliceous sand, quartz, granite, basalt, or limestone, with strict moisture control (< 0.2 %).
- **Additives:** Fillers (silica flour), shrinkage-reducing admixtures, accelerators, coupling agents (silane).

**3. Properties of PC:**
- Compressive strength: 60–150 MPa.
- Tensile strength: 10–20 MPa.
- Flexural strength: 15–35 MPa.
- Water absorption: < 0.5 %.
- Excellent chemical resistance; rapid strength gain (24 h).
- Low shrinkage (0.01–0.03 %).

**4. Applications of PC:**
- Machine-tool bases and precision equipment foundations.
- Bridge deck overlays and expansion-joint nosings.
- Sewer pipes, manholes, and chemical-resistant tanks.
- Patch repairs of deteriorated concrete.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Composition of PMC:**
- **Cement (OPC)**: typical 350–450 kg/m³.
- **Polymer latex**: SBR, PAE, EVA, or acrylic, at **P/C = 0.10–0.20**.
- **Aggregates**: as in OPC, with W/C adjusted to account for water in latex.
- **Admixtures**: antifoaming agents (to control latex-induced air), superplasticizers.

**2. Mechanism of Strength Development:**
- **Hydration** of cement produces C–S–H and Ca(OH)₂.
- As hydration consumes water, latex particles **coalesce** into a continuous polymer film.
- This film **bridges micro-cracks**, **blocks capillary pores**, and **improves aggregate–paste bond**.
- In acrylic systems, **Ca²⁺** cross-links with carboxylate groups, increasing stiffness.

**3. Comparison with OPC:**

| Property | OPC | PMC |
|---|---|---|
| Compressive strength | 20–50 MPa | 40–80 MPa |
| Flexural strength | 3–6 MPa | 6–12 MPa |
| Bond to old concrete | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Waterproofing | Limited | **Good** |
| Chemical resistance | Poor–Moderate | Good |
| Cost | Low | 1.5–2× OPC |

**4. Applications:**
- Repair overlays on bridge decks and parking garages.
- Waterproof plaster for water tanks and basements.
- Industrial floor toppings.
- Tile adhesives and grouts.
- Bonding bridges between old and new concrete.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **step-by-step process of manufacturing Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)**. Discuss the improvements in strength and durability achieved. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the various **polymer resins used as binders** in polymer concrete. Compare **epoxy, polyester, and furan resins** in terms of mechanical properties, chemical resistance, and cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Manufacturing Process of PIC (with sketch):**

Refer to the flow diagram in Section 4.2. The seven steps are:

1. **Casting & Curing of OPC** — Conventional concrete is cast and water-cured for 28 days, then air-dried.
2. **Oven Drying** — Specimens are dried at **120–150 °C for 24 h** to empty the capillary pores of free water.
3. **Vacuum Soaking** — Specimens are placed in a chamber, evacuated to ≈ **25 mmHg**, then flooded with monomer (commonly **methyl methacrylate — MMA**, with 0.5 % benzoyl peroxide initiator).
4. **Pressure Impregnation** — Pressure of **0.3–1.0 MPa** is applied for 1–2 h to force monomer deep into the pores.
5. **Polymerization** — Specimens are heated to **70–80 °C** (or irradiated with γ-rays from Co-60) to convert monomer → polymer via free-radical initiation.
6. **Post-cure** — Additional heating to **100–120 °C** ensures high degree of monomer conversion (> 95 %).
7. **Cooling & Inspection** — Controlled cooling to avoid thermal shock, followed by surface quality checks.

**Improvements in Strength & Durability:**

| Property | OPC | PIC | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compressive strength | 35 MPa | 140 MPa | **4×** |
| Tensile strength | 3 MPa | 11 MPa | **3.7×** |
| Water absorption | 5 % | 0.3 % | **> 90 % reduction** |
| Freeze–thaw resistance | Moderate | Excellent | — |
| Chemical resistance | Poor | Excellent | — |
| Surface abrasion | Moderate | Excellent | — |

The improvements arise from **pore-filling** (eliminating capillary porosity), **strong polymer–hydrate interlock**, and the **waterproofing effect of polymer** that prevents ingress of aggressive agents.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Polymer Resins Used in Polymer Concrete:**

The principal thermosetting resins used as binders in PC are:

1. **Epoxy Resin**
2. **Unsaturated Polyester Resin**
3. **Vinyl Ester Resin**
4. **Methyl Methacrylate (MMA) / Acrylic**
5. **Furan Resin**
6. **Phenolic Resin**

**Comparative Study: Epoxy vs Polyester vs Furan**

| Property | Epoxy | Polyester | Furan |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | 55–110 MPa | 40–90 MPa | 30–60 MPa |
| Compressive strength (in PC) | 80–120 MPa | 60–100 MPa | 50–80 MPa |
| Bond to substrate | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate |
| Chemical resistance | Very good | Good | **Best (acid + alkali)** |
| Shrinkage on cure | Very low (≈ 0.01 %) | Moderate (1–3 %) | Moderate |
| Curing time | Hours (room temp.) | Minutes–hours | Heat-cure preferred |
| Heat resistance | Up to ≈ 120 °C | Up to ≈ 100 °C | **Up to ≈ 200 °C** |
| Cost (per kg) | **High (₹ 800–1500)** | Low–Moderate (₹ 200–400) | High (₹ 600–900) |
| Typical applications | Patch repairs, machine bases, bridge overlays | Manholes, sewer pipes, general PC | Chemical tanks, acid-resistant linings |

**Conclusion:**
- **Epoxy** is the **premium choice** where bond and mechanical performance are critical (e.g., structural repair).
- **Polyester** is the **economical choice** for general-purpose PC and precast products.
- **Furan** is the **specialty choice** for highly aggressive chemical environments (e.g., pickling tanks, industrial effluent channels).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation for this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating PC and PMC** — Many students write "polymer concrete" generically and describe PMC instead of PC (or vice versa). This is a **3–5 mark penalty**. Always explicitly state whether cement is present.
> 2. **Omitting the role of monomer impregnation under vacuum in PIC** — A common error is to say PIC is "dipped in plastic." The correct process requires **vacuum–pressure cycling** to achieve acceptable impregnation depth.
> 3. **Forgetting aggregate pre-drying** — Aggregate moisture content is critical. Resin polymerization fails in the presence of water.
> 4. **Confusing polymer mortar with epoxy mortar** — Polymer mortar may use **any** resin (polyester, vinyl ester, etc.), not only epoxy. Examiners penalize this conflation.
> 5. **Skipping durability properties** — A descriptive answer that lists only strength properties will lose 2–3 marks. **Durability (chemical resistance, water absorption, freeze–thaw)** is heavily weighted in KTU 2024 marking schemes.
> 6. **Not mentioning limitations** — Fire resistance of PC is **poor** (most resins decompose at 200–400 °C). Always discuss this limitation for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Polymer Concrete & Mortar (Module 3):**

- ☐ **Polymer Concrete (PC)** uses a **thermosetting resin** as the sole binder; **cement is absent**.
- ☐ **Polymer Mortar (PM)** = PC with **fine aggregate only** (no coarse aggregate).
- ☐ **Polymer-Modified Concrete (PMC)** = **cement + polymer latex**; both binders act in parallel.
- ☐ **Polymer-Impregnated Concrete (PIC)** = pre-cast OPC with monomer **impregnated and polymerized in situ** in the pores.
- ☐ Common resins: **Epoxy (premium), Polyester (economical), Vinyl ester (chemical), Furan (acid/alkali), MMA (rapid), Phenolic (heat).**
- ☐ Aggregate moisture must be **< 0.2 %** to avoid polymerization defects.
- ☐ **Pot life** of catalyzed resin is typically **5–45 min**; placement must be fast.
- ☐ PC achieves **60–150 MPa** compressive strength due to **< 1 % binder porosity** (vs. 25–40 % in cement paste).
- ☐ Water absorption of PC: **< 0.5 %**; for PIC: **< 0.3 %**.
- ☐ PC is **excellent** in chemical resistance, abrasion, and freeze–thaw; **poor** in fire resistance.
- ☐ **Pull-off bond strength** of polymer repair mortar: target **≥ 1.7 MPa** (BS EN 1504-3 R3/R4).
- ☐ Key applications: **bridge deck overlays, sewer linings, machine bases, patch repairs, chemical-resistant flooring, joint nosings, FFRP jacketing grouts**.
- ☐ **Impregnation efficiency** in PIC is typically **15–25 %** without vacuum, **> 80 %** with full vacuum–pressure cycle.
- ☐ **Polymer–cement ratio (P/C)** in PMC optimum = **0.10–0.20** (polymer solids / cement).
- ☐ **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** governs the **service temperature limit** of any polymer concrete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quick Setting Compounds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quick Setting Compounds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Quick Setting Compounds (QSC)* are **pre-packaged, factory-proportioned, cementitious or chemically reactive repair materials** that achieve an initial set in **less than 30 minutes** and develop functional/compressive strength (typically **>20 MPa within 24 hours**) at standard laboratory conditions of **27 ± 2 °C** and **65 ± 5 % Relative Humidity**, as defined under **IS 4031 (Part 5)** and **ASTM C928** for packaged, dry, rapid-hardening cementitious materials for concrete repairs.

These compounds are engineered by combining **high-early-strength binders** (such as calcium aluminate cement, calcium sulfoaluminate cement, or Portland cement + accelerators) with **graded silica aggregates, polymer modifiers, and chemical admixtures** (accelerators, shrinkage compensators, bond enhancers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Bandage" Analogy

Think of **Quick Setting Compounds as the "instant bandage" of structural engineering**.

| Medical Analogy | Structural Counterpart |
|---|---|
| A wound on skin needs instant clotting | A damaged patch of concrete needs instant load-bearing |
| Bandage stops bleeding within seconds | QSC sets within 5–30 minutes |
| Skin grows new tissue over time | QSC develops full strength over 28 days |
| Doctor chooses "rapid-set" plaster for fractures | Engineer chooses QSC for runway / highway patches |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine a pothole on a busy national highway at 6 AM. The traffic cannot wait **7 days** for ordinary cement to harden. Engineers use a **quick-setting compound** so that within **2–4 hours**, vehicles can ply over the repaired patch safely. The compound trades a *small premium in cost* for a *massive gain in time and serviceability*.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

The following metrics are **must-know for KTU examinations** (highlighted in **bold**):

- **Initial Setting Time: 5 – 30 minutes**
- **Final Setting Time: 10 – 60 minutes**
- **Compressive Strength at 1 day: ≥ 20 MPa (typically 25–55 MPa)**
- **Compressive Strength at 28 days: ≥ 40 MPa (typically 50–80 MPa)**
- **Bond Strength to substrate: ≥ 1.7 MPa (slant shear per ASTM C882)**
- **Drying Shrinkage: < 0.10 % at 28 days**
- **Working Temperature Range: 5 °C to 35 °C (standard formulations)**

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically categorizes QSC under *"Special repair materials — fast-track and emergency rehabilitation."* This topic is a **favourite 7–14 mark question** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) because it tests both *materials knowledge* and *practical site decision-making*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why & How" Behind Quick Setting

The working principle of a quick-setting compound is rooted in **accelerated hydration kinetics**. Unlike Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC), which hydrates via the slow C₃S → C-S-H reaction (taking 6–10 hours to set), QSC uses one or more of the following mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Mechanism A — Use of Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC / High Alumina Cement)

$$ CA + H_2O \rightarrow CAH_{10} \rightarrow C_2AH_8 \rightarrow C_3AH_6 + AH_3 $$

> **Why it works:** The metastable hexagonal hydrates **CAH₁₀** and **C₂AH₈** form *very rapidly* (minutes), giving flash set. The *conversion* to stable C₃AH₆ is what causes strength loss in CAC (the famous *"conversion problem"*), but in patch repairs this is mitigated by the **small volume** of QSC used.

### 2.1.2 Mechanism B — Use of Calcium SulfoAluminate (CSA) Cement

$$ C_4A_3\bar{S} + 2C\bar{S}H_2 + 34H \rightarrow C_6A\bar{S}_3H_{32} + 2AH_3 $$

> **Why it works:** Ettringite (C₆A S̄₃H₃₂) forms *within minutes* of water contact, expanding the matrix and locking the structure together. **No C₃S / C₂S is present** — so strength develops from ettringite + aluminium hydroxide gel, not from conventional C-S-H.

### 2.1.3 Mechanism C — Chemical Acceleration of OPC

Common accelerators used:
- **Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂)** — 1.5–2 % by mass of cement (deprecated in reinforced concrete due to corrosion)
- **Calcium Nitrite / Calcium Nitrate** — chloride-free alternative
- **Triethanolamine (TEA)** — 0.1–0.5 %
- **Sodium Silicate ("water glass")** — 5–10 % for ultra-rapid set (used in leak sealing)
- **Sodium Aluminate** — sets in 1–5 minutes

### 2.1.4 Mechanism D — Polymer Modification (Latex / SBR / Epoxy)

Adding **styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR)** or **acrylic latex** at **5–20 % of cement mass** improves:
- Bond to old concrete
- Flexural strength
- Waterproofing
- Reduces shrinkage cracking

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Setting time (acceleration) | $t_{set} \approx \dfrac{t_{OPC}}{1 + k \cdot C_a}$ | minutes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Compressive strength gain (Abrams-type) | $f_c(t) = f_{c,28} \cdot \log_{10}(t+1) / \log_{10}(29)$ | MPa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Pore refinement ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{d_{pore,QSC}}{d_{pore,OPC}}$ (typically 0.4–0.6) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Bond strength (slant shear) | $\tau_b = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{slant}}$ | MPa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Drying shrinkage strain | $\varepsilon_{sh} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | microstrain | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Heat of hydration (QSC) | $Q = m_c \cdot H_{hyd}$ where $H_{hyd} = 250\text{–}500$ kJ/kg | kJ | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Water/binder ratio strength law | $f_c = A / (B^{w/b})$ (Feret-Abrams) | MPa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Rate of strength development | $R = \dfrac{f_{c,24h}}{f_{c,28d}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Early-age modulus (empirical) | $E_c(t) = 4700 \sqrt{f_c(t)}$ | MPa | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Pull-off bond test | $f_b = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2}$ | MPa | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** When asked to *compare* QSC vs OPC, students often forget the **conversion factor for strength-time log relationship**. The "log-log" maturity curve is the examiner's favourite differentiator between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quick setting compounds are *mission-critical* in:

- **Aviation:** Runway patch repairs within a 4-hour AOG (Aircraft-on-Ground) window
- **Highways:** Pothole repair in monsoons (India's NHAI uses QSC during monsoon maintenance)
- **Industrial Floors:** Factory floor joints — production cannot halt for 28 days
- **Tunnels / Metro Rail:** Segment grouting, leak-sealing (KTU region: Kochi Metro, Bangalore Metro)
- **Hydraulic structures:** Spillway piers, stilling basins (under continuous water flow)
- **Nuclear / Defence:** Emergency shielding repairs

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maturity-Based Strength Development for QSC

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive an expression for the **24-hour strength** of a QSC patch as a function of the **maturity (M)** and a **rate constant (k)**. This is a *favourite 7-mark analytical question* in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — The Nurse-Saul Maturity Function

The **maturity** of concrete is the product of time and temperature above a reference datum (typically −10 °C for OPC-based systems and **0 °C** for QSC due to exothermic self-heating):

$$ M(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} (T_i - T_{ref}) \cdot \Delta t $$

For an *isothermal* curing condition at $T$:

$$ M(t) = (T - T_{ref}) \cdot t $$

### Step 2 — Hyperbolic Strength-Maturity Relationship (Plowman Equation)

The general Plowman equation relates compressive strength to maturity:

$$ f_c = \dfrac{A \cdot M}{B + M} $$

where:
- $A$ = ultimate strength asymptote (MPa)
- $B$ = maturity at which $f_c = A/2$ (°C·hours)
- $M$ = maturity (°C·hours)

### Step 3 — Solving for 24-hour Strength

At $t = 24$ hours and temperature $T$ (assume $T_{ref} = 0$ °C for QSC):

$$ M_{24} = T \cdot 24 $$

Substituting:

$$ f_{c,24} = \dfrac{A \cdot 24T}{B + 24T} $$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given:**
- QSC patch cured at $T = 27$ °C
- $A = 65$ MPa (asymptotic strength)
- $B = 200$ °C·hours (Plowman constant for a typical CSA-based QSC)

**Find:** $f_{c,24}$

**Solution:**

Step (a): Compute maturity

$$ M_{24} = (27 - 0) \times 24 = 648 \text{ °C·hours} $$

Step (b): Substitute into Plowman equation

$$ f_{c,24} = \dfrac{65 \times 648}{200 + 648} $$

$$ f_{c,24} = \dfrac{42{,}120}{848} $$

$$ \boxed{f_{c,24} \approx 49.67 \text{ MPa}} $$

Step (c): Verify against typical QSC spec

The result of **≈ 50 MPa at 24 hours** is consistent with the standard expectation of **≥ 25–55 MPa at 1 day** — confirming the QSC has been properly proportioned.

### Step 5 — Derivation of Rate Constant for Quick Set

Define the **rate of strength development ratio**:

$$ R = \dfrac{f_{c,24}}{f_{c,28}} \times 100\,\% $$

For our QSC: $f_{c,28} \approx 65$ MPa (asymptote A)

$$ R = \dfrac{49.67}{65} \times 100 \approx 76.4\,\% $$

> **Conclusion:** A *good* QSC achieves **>70 % of its 28-day strength in 24 hours**. Ordinary OPC achieves only **15–25 %** in the same period. This 3–4× jump in early strength is the *engineering value* of QSC.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right QSC

| Project Scenario | Recommended QSC Type | Setting Time | 1-Day Strength | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Highway pothole in monsoon | CSA-based + polymer | 10–15 min | 30–40 MPa | Medium |
| Industrial floor joint | CAC-based + SBR latex | 15–30 min | 35–50 MPa | High |
| Runway patch (AOG) | CSA + silica fume | 5–10 min | 45–55 MPa | High |
| Tunnel leak sealing | Sodium silicate-activated | 1–5 min | 15–20 MPa | Low |
| Cold weather repair | CAC + anti-freeze accelerator | 20–40 min | 25–35 MPa | High |
| Underwater repair | Magnesium phosphate cement (MPC) | 5–20 min | 40–55 MPa | Very High |
| Bridge bearing grout | Non-shrink cementitious + accelerator | 30–60 min | 30–45 MPa | Medium |
| Heritage masonry crack injection | Lime-pozzolana + sodium silicate | 2–10 min | 5–10 MPa | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite:** "Recommend a QSC for an airport runway patch repair that must reopen in 3 hours." — Use this matrix in your answer. It demonstrates *application-level mastery* (Bloom's L4–L5), which fetches full marks.

## 3.3 Limitations — Honest Engineering Assessment

| Limitation | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Higher cost (3–8× OPC) | Specialty binders, polymers | Justify by downtime savings |
| Short working time | Flash hydration | Use retarders; batch in small lots |
| Conversion in CAC | Hex → Cubic hydrate | Limit CAC to patches, not mass concrete |
| Heat of hydration peak | Exothermic CSA/CAC | Use in thin sections; pre-cool aggregates |
| Alkali-silica risk (ASR) | High pH of CSA | Use non-reactive aggregates, SCMs |
| Corrosion of rebar (CaCl₂) | Chloride accelerator | Use nitrite-based accelerators |
| Limited working time in summer | Faster hydration | Use chilled water, shade the work |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quick Setting Compound System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Damaged Concrete Substrate] --> B[Surface Preparation]
    B --> B1[Mechanical Chipping / Hydrodemolition]
    B --> B2[Dust and Laitance Removal]
    B --> B3[Saturation Surface Dry SSD Condition]
    
    B3 --> C[Bonding Bridge Layer]
    C --> C1[Slurry Coat of QSC]
    C --> C2[Acrylic Bonding Agent]
    C --> C3[Epoxy Primer on Rebar]
    
    C1 --> D[Quick Setting Compound Batching]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    
    D --> D1[Powder Component]
    D1 --> D2[CSA or CAC Binder]
    D1 --> D3[Graded Silica Sand]
    D1 --> D4[Polymer Modifier Powder]
    D1 --> D5[Shrinkage Compensator]
    
    D --> E[Liquid Component]
    E --> E1[Water or Acrylic Latex]
    E --> E2[Anti-Foam Agent]
    E --> E3[Accelerator Dose]
    
    D2 --> F[Mixing Stage]
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    D5 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    
    F --> G[Application Window 5 to 30 min]
    G --> H[Initial Set Achieved]
    H --> I[Moist Curing 2 to 4 hours]
    I --> J[Service Load Allowed]
    J --> K[Rehabilitated Structure Restored]
    
    K --> K1[Highway 2 hour reopen]
    K --> K2[Runway 4 hour reopen]
    K --> K3[Floor 6 hour reopen]
```

## 4.2 Hydration Kinetics Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OPC Hydration Pathway] --> A1[C3S and C2S Reaction]
    A1 --> A2[Induction Period 2 to 4 hours]
    A2 --> A3[Acceleration Phase]
    A3 --> A4[Final Set at 6 to 10 hours]
    A4 --> A5[28 day C-S-H matrix]
    
    B[CSA Hydration Pathway] --> B1[Immediate Ettringite Formation]
    B1 --> B2[Set at 5 to 15 minutes]
    B2 --> B3[Rapid Strength Gain]
    B3 --> B4[24 hour 75 percent strength]
    B4 --> B5[Ettringite plus AH3 matrix]
    
    C[CAC Hydration Pathway] --> C1[CA plus H2O Reaction]
    C1 --> C2[CAH10 forms in minutes]
    C2 --> C3[Set at 15 to 30 minutes]
    C3 --> C4[Conversion over years]
    C4 --> C5[Stable C3AH6 matrix]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting QSC Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Repair Time Constraint} -->|Less than 1 hour| Q2{Underwater or Wet}
    Q1 -->|1 to 6 hours| Q3[Choose CSA based QSC]
    Q1 -->|More than 6 hours| Q4[Choose CAC or OPC accelerator]
    
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q5[Choose Magnesium Phosphate QSC]
    Q2 -->|No| Q6[Choose CSA plus Polymer QSC]
    
    Q3 --> R{Structural or Cosmetic}
    R -->|Structural| S1[High early strength CSA with SBR]
    R -->|Cosmetic| S2[Economy grade CSA mortar]
    
    Q4 --> T{Chloride Risk}
    T -->|Yes rebar present| U1[Use nitrite based accelerator]
    T -->|No chloride risk| U2[Use CaCl2 accelerator]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — On-Site Application Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Receive damaged area report] --> P2[Site inspection and marking]
    P2 --> P3[Cut square edges minimum 25 mm depth]
    P3 --> P4[Expose corroded rebar if any]
    P4 --> P5[Clean rebar to SA 2.5 standard]
    P5 --> P6[Apply anti-corrosion coating if needed]
    P6 --> P7[SSD the substrate]
    P7 --> P8[Mix QSC as per TDS water ratio]
    P8 --> P9[Apply bonding slurry]
    P9 --> P10[Place QSC in layers max 50 mm]
    P10 --> P11[Trowel and finish]
    P11 --> P12[Cover with wet hessian cloth]
    P12 --> P13[Cure 2 to 4 hours minimum]
    P13 --> P14[Open to traffic]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU Board Exam:** A well-drawn Mermaid flowchart (or hand-drawn equivalent) showing the *sequence* of QSC application fetches **extra 2–3 marks** in the drawing/figure component. Examiners reward visual clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q.A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define quick setting compounds used in repair of structures. List any four characteristic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Quick setting compounds (QSC) are pre-packaged, factory-blended cementitious or chemical repair materials that achieve initial set in less than 30 minutes and develop at least 20 MPa compressive strength within 24 hours, enabling rapid restoration of service in structures.

**Four Characteristic Properties:**
1. **Initial setting time:** 5–30 minutes (vs. 30 min for OPC, IS 4031).
2. **Rapid strength gain:** 1-day strength ≥ 20 MPa; 28-day strength ≥ 40 MPa.
3. **Excellent bond to old concrete:** slant-shear bond strength ≥ 1.7 MPa (ASTM C882).
4. **Low shrinkage:** typically < 0.10 % at 28 days, reducing cracking risk.
5. *(Bonus fifth point):* Factory pre-proportioned → consistent quality, minimal site error.

---

### **Q.A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC) and Calcium SulfoAluminate (CSA) cement as binders for quick setting compounds.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC) | Calcium SulfoAluminate Cement (CSA) |
|---|---|---|
| Main reactive phase | Monocalcium aluminate (CA) | Ye'elimite (C₄A₃S̄) |
| Hydration product | CAH₁₀, C₂AH₈, C₃AH₆ (with conversion) | Ettringite (C₆A S̄₃H₃₂) + AH₃ |
| Setting time | 15–30 minutes | 5–15 minutes |
| 1-day strength | 30–50 MPa | 25–55 MPa |
| Long-term issue | "Conversion" → strength loss in mass concrete | Dimensionally stable; no conversion |
| pH of pore solution | ~11.5 (lower than OPC) | ~12.0 (mildly lower than OPC) |
| Typical use | Industrial floor patches, refractory repairs | Highway/runway patches, leak sealing |

> **Conclusion:** CSA is preferred for *thin-section, fast-track* repairs, while CAC remains the binder of choice for *floor toppings and high-temperature exposure* repairs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1]**

> **(a)** Explain the composition, mechanism of setting, and engineering properties of **Calcium SulfoAluminate (CSA) cement-based quick setting compounds**. Discuss the role of ettringite formation in rapid strength development. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A runway patch repair using a CSA-based QSC was carried out at 30 °C ambient. The Plowman constants for the compound are *A = 70 MPa* and *B = 180 °C·hours* (with $T_{ref}$ = 0 °C). Calculate **(i)** the maturity at 24 hours, **(ii)** the 24-hour compressive strength, and **(iii)** the rate of strength development ratio if 28-day strength is 68 MPa. Comment on whether the runway can be reopened in 4 hours (assume ≥ 20 MPa is required). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Composition of CSA cement (2 marks):**
- Principal phase: **Ye'elimite (C₄A₃S̄)** — 30–70 %
- Secondary phases: Belite (C₂S) — 20–40 %, Calcium sulfate (C̄S) — 10–25 %
- Minor: Gehlenite (C₂AS), ferrite, free lime
- *Manufactured by* clinkering limestone, bauxite, and gypsum at **1250–1300 °C** (lower than OPC's 1450 °C — saves ~25 % energy)

**2. Mechanism of setting (3 marks):**
When CSA reacts with water in the presence of calcium sulfate:

$$ C_4A_3\bar{S} + 2C\bar{S}H_2 + 34H \rightarrow C_6A\bar{S}_3H_{32} + 2AH_3 $$

- **Ettringite (C₆A S̄₃H₃₂)** — needle-like crystals — forms *within 2–5 minutes* of water contact
- Crystal interlock + volume expansion (~8 %) → **immediate rigidity** = **flash set**
- Aluminium hydroxide gel (AH₃) fills pores → contributes to *impermeability*
- Belite (C₂S) hydrates *slowly* over days/weeks → contributes to *later-age strength*
- No CH (portlandite) is produced → **superior sulfate resistance**

**3. Engineering properties (2 marks):**
- Setting: 5–15 min initial, 10–30 min final
- 1-day strength: 25–55 MPa
- Drying shrinkage: 0.04–0.08 % (very low)
- Carbonation resistance: excellent (no CH available)
- Sulfate resistance: excellent
- pH: ~12.0 (mildly lower than OPC, acceptable for rebar passivity)

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- T = 30 °C, $T_{ref}$ = 0 °C
- A = 70 MPa, B = 180 °C·hours
- $f_{c,28}$ = 68 MPa
- Required strength for reopening: ≥ 20 MPa

**Step 1 — Maturity at 24 hours (2 marks):**

$$ M_{24} = (T - T_{ref}) \times t = (30 - 0) \times 24 $$

$$ M_{24} = 720 \text{ °C·hours} $$

**Step 2 — 24-hour compressive strength (2 marks):**

Using the Plowman equation:

$$ f_{c,24} = \dfrac{A \cdot M}{B + M} = \dfrac{70 \times 720}{180 + 720} $$

$$ f_{c,24} = \dfrac{50{,}400}{900} $$

$$ \boxed{f_{c,24} = 56.0 \text{ MPa}} $$

**Step 3 — Rate of strength development ratio (2 marks):**

$$ R = \dfrac{f_{c,24}}{f_{c,28}} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{56.0}{68} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{R = 82.35\,\%} $$

**Step 4 — 4-hour strength check (1 mark):**

$$ M_4 = 30 \times 4 = 120 \text{ °C·hours} $$

$$ f_{c,4} = \dfrac{70 \times 120}{180 + 120} = \dfrac{8400}{300} = 28 \text{ MPa} $$

Since **28 MPa > 20 MPa** (required threshold), the runway **CAN be reopened in 4 hours safely**.

> **Conclusion:** The 4-hour reopening is feasible because the early strength (28 MPa) exceeds the design requirement. The high R-value of 82 % confirms this is a high-performance QSC suitable for fast-track aviation repairs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2]**

> **(a)** Discuss the various **types of quick setting compounds** used in structural repair, classifying them by binder chemistry. For each type, give one typical application and one limitation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step on-site application procedure** for a quick setting compound used to repair a **damaged industrial concrete floor (50 mm deep spall)**, including surface preparation, mixing, placing, finishing, and curing. Justify each step with the underlying engineering reason. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Classification of Quick Setting Compounds by Binder (4 marks):**

**Type 1 — Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC) Based**
- Setting: 15–30 min; 1-day strength: 30–50 MPa
- *Application:* Industrial floor toppings, refractory patches
- *Limitation:* "Conversion" of CAH₁₀ → C₃AH₆ reduces strength in mass pours

**Type 2 — Calcium SulfoAluminate (CSA) Based**
- Setting: 5–15 min; 1-day strength: 25–55 MPa
- *Application:* Highway pothole repair, runway patches
- *Limitation:* Higher cost; very short working time demands disciplined batching

**Type 3 — Magnesium Phosphate Cement (MPC) Based**
- Setting: 5–20 min; 1-day strength: 40–55 MPa
- *Application:* Underwater repairs, cold-weather repairs
- *Limitation:* Very fast set; requires experienced applicators

**Type 4 — OPC + Chemical Accelerator**
- Setting: 10–60 min (dose-dependent); 1-day strength: 15–30 MPa
- *Application:* General patch repairs, joint sealing
- *Limitation:* Chloride accelerators → corrosion of rebar (use nitrite-based instead)

**Type 5 — Polymer-Modified Cementitious (SBR / Acrylic Latex)**
- Setting: 20–60 min; 1-day strength: 20–40 MPa
- *Application:* Waterproofing overlays, parking deck repairs
- *Limitation:* Sensitive to ambient temperature and humidity during cure

**Type 6 — Geopolymer / Alkali-Activated**
- Setting: 30–90 min; 1-day strength: 25–45 MPa
- *Application:* Eco-friendly repairs, aggressive chemical exposure zones
- *Limitation:* Handling of alkaline activators; lesser field data

**2. Selection Criteria Summary (1 mark):**
- *Time constraint* → CSA / MPC
- *Substrate wetness* → MPC / Sodium silicate
- *Cost sensitivity* → OPC + accelerator
- *Durability* → CSA / Polymer-modified

**3. Common Engineering Mistake (2 marks):**
Selecting CAC for a *thin patch on a hot summer day* — leads to flash set and poor workability. Always check the *exotherm peak temperature* and *working time* at site temperature, not just at lab conditions.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Industrial Floor Spall Repair — 50 mm Deep, 300 × 300 mm Area**

**Step 1 — Marking and Saw-Cutting (1 mark):**
- Mark a square around the spall, extending **at least 25 mm beyond the visible damage**.
- Saw-cut the perimeter to a depth of **≥ 15 mm** to create *square, well-defined edges* (which resist lifting).
- *Reason:* Irregular edges create stress concentrations; square cuts transfer loads uniformly.

**Step 2 — Removal of Damaged Concrete (1 mark):**
- Chip out using a 15-lb hammer or hydrodemolition to expose **sound concrete substrate**.
- Expose corroded rebar (if any) to at least **25 mm beyond the corroded zone**.
- *Reason:* Any unsound concrete left will debond later — *"the repair is only as good as the substrate"*.

**Step 3 — Rebar Cleaning and Protection (0.5 mark):**
- Clean rebar to **Sa 2½ (near-white metal)** finish per **ISO 8501-1**.
- Apply **anti-corrosion epoxy primer** if section loss > 20 %.
- *Reason:* Future corrosion will spall the new patch; *repair without rebar protection is a wasted repair*.

**Step 4 — Substrate Preparation (1 mark):**
- Remove dust, laitance, oil, curing compounds (by sandblasting or grinding).
- Saturate the substrate with clean water **2 hours before** the repair; bring to **SSD condition**.
- *Reason:* SSD prevents the dry, porous old concrete from *absorbing water* from the QSC mix (which would cause weak, crumbly interface and shrinkage cracks).

**Step 5 — Bonding Bridge (0.5 mark):**
- Mix a slurry of the QSC powder + water (or polymer latex) to a paint-like consistency.
- Brush onto the SSD substrate, **2–3 mm thick**.
- *Reason:* The slurry *wets and primes* the substrate, dramatically improving *tensile bond strength* (≥ 1.7 MPa per ASTM C882).

**Step 6 — Mixing of QSC (1 mark):**
- Strictly follow the manufacturer's **water-to-powder ratio** (typically 0.13–0.18 for CSA QSC).
- Mix using a **paddle mixer at < 400 rpm** for 2–3 minutes — *avoid over-mixing* (entrains air).
- Mix only as much as can be placed in **5–10 minutes** (working time).
- *Reason:* Over-watering → weak, porous matrix. Under-watering → poor workability, incomplete hydration.

**Step 7 — Placing in Layers (1 mark):**
- Place the QSC in **layers of ≤ 50 mm**.
- Compact each layer with a trowel to remove entrapped air.
- For a 50 mm deep spall, *a single layer is acceptable* if the heat of hydration is not a concern.
- *Reason:* Thick single layers trap heat → thermal cracks; multiple layers dissipate heat.

**Step 8 — Finishing and Curing (1 mark):**
- Trowel to match the surrounding floor profile.
- **Within 30 minutes of final set**, apply a **wet hessian cloth** or **spray-on curing compound** (e.g., acrylic-based, ASTM C309).
- Keep moist for **at least 2–4 hours** (much shorter than OPC's 7 days!).
- *Reason:* Even QSC needs *moisture* to continue hydration. Curing compounds prevent *plastic shrinkage cracks* during the critical first hour.

**Step 9 — Reopening the Floor:**
- Light foot traffic: **2–4 hours**
- Forklift / vehicle traffic: **24 hours** (verify with hammer-tap test for *ringing sound*)

> **Engineering Justification Summary:** The procedure minimizes *substrate debonding* (SSD + slurry), *rebar corrosion* (primer), *thermal cracking* (layered placement), and *plastic shrinkage* (early curing) — the four most common failure modes of QSC repairs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Quick Setting Compounds":**
>
> 1. **Confusing "rapid hardening" with "quick setting"** — they are *different*. Rapid hardening = high *strength* gain (e.g., 1-day = 3-day of OPC). Quick setting = short *setting time*. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this mix-up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the conversion problem in CAC** — any answer that *recommends CAC* without mentioning *conversion* and *why it is acceptable in thin patches* is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Writing only chemical formulas without engineering interpretation** — the examiner wants you to *connect chemistry to field performance*. Always end a chemistry point with *"this means …"*
>
> 4. **Ignoring the bonding bridge step** in the application procedure — the slurry coat is *the single most important step* for in-service durability. Skipping it = −1 mark.
>
> 5. **Not distinguishing between initial and final setting time** in the definition. Both must be stated.
>
> 6. **Maturity calculation errors** — students often use $T_{ref} = -10$ °C (OPC value) for QSC; the correct datum is **0 °C** for CSA/CAC QSC.
>
> 7. **Using generic "accelerator"** instead of naming specific compounds (CaCl₂, sodium silicate, calcium nitrite). Always name the *specific* chemical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Quick Setting Compounds"**

- ☐ **Definition:** Pre-packaged repair material, set < 30 min, 1-day strength ≥ 20 MPa.
- ☐ **Key Standards:** IS 4031 (Part 5), ASTM C928, ASTM C882 (bond), EN 1504 (European repair standard).
- ☐ **Binder Types:** CAC (CaO·Al₂O₃), CSA (C₄A₃S̄ — Ye'elimite), MPC (Mg-phosphate), OPC + accelerator, Geopolymer.
- ☐ **Setting Mechanism of CSA:** Ettringite (C₆A S̄₃H₃₂) formation within 5–15 min.
- ☐ **Setting Mechanism of CAC:** CAH₁₀ formation in 15–30 min → risk of conversion to C₃AH₆.
- ☐ **Common Accelerators:** CaCl₂ (deprecated for RCC), calcium nitrite, TEA, sodium silicate, sodium aluminate.
- ☐ **1-Day Strength Range:** 20–55 MPa.
- ☐ **28-Day Strength Range:** 40–80 MPa.
- ☐ **Rate of Strength Development Ratio (R):** >70 % for QSC vs. 15–25 % for OPC.
- ☐ **Plowman Equation:** $f_c = A \cdot M / (B + M)$, with $M = (T - T_{ref}) \cdot t$ (T in °C, t in hours, M in °C·hours).
- ☐ **Maturity Reference Temperature:** 0 °C for QSC (NOT −10 °C as for OPC).
- ☐ **Bond Strength (ASTM C882 Slant Shear):** ≥ 1.7 MPa minimum.
- ☐ **Slurry Bonding Bridge:** Mandatory step — never skip.
- ☐ **SSD Condition:** Substrate saturated, surface-dry — prevents water migration from QSC mix.
- ☐ **Curing Duration:** 2–4 hours for QSC (vs. 7 days for OPC).
- ☐ **Working Time Window:** 5–30 minutes — batch in small lots.
- ☐ **Conversion Problem:** CAC loses ~30–50 % strength over years due to hydrate transformation; limit CAC to *thin patches* only.
- ☐ **Chloride Risk:** Avoid CaCl₂ in reinforced concrete — use Ca(NO₂)₂ or Ca(NO₃)₂.
- ☐ **Heat of Hydration Peak:** QSC is exothermic → avoid in thick mass pours.
- ☐ **Cost Index:** 3–8× more expensive than OPC — justify by downtime savings.
- ☐ **Key Applications:** Highway potholes, runway patches, industrial floors, tunnel leaks, hydraulic structures, metro rail segments.
- ☐ **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Time-critical + thin section → CSA; underwater → MPC; cost-sensitive + non-structural → OPC + accelerator; floor topping → CAC; high-temp → CAC; eco-friendly → geopolymer.
- ☐ **Typical Repair Depth:** Patches of 10–75 mm in single layer; deeper in multiple 50 mm lifts.
- ☐ **Reopening Time:** Foot traffic in 2–4 h; vehicle traffic in 24 h (verify with rebound hammer).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic: "CAMP-FAST"** for QSC types
> - **C** — Calcium Aluminate (CAC)
> - **A** — Accelerator-modified OPC
> - **M** — Magnesium Phosphate (MPC)
> - **P** — Polymer-modified cementitious
> - **F** — (none — placeholder for "fast set" reminder)
> - **A** — Alkali-activated (geopolymer)
> - **S** — Sulfoaluminate (CSA)
> - **T** — (none — "Total toolkit" reminder)

---

**END OF NOTES — QUICK SETTING COMPOUNDS | MODULE 3 | PECET634 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.title: Gunite and Shot Crete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gunite and Shotcrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — GUNITING AND SHOTCRETING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Gunite** is a mixture of cement, sand (fine aggregate only) and water that is pneumatically projected under high velocity onto a prepared surface through a hose and nozzle, where the dry ingredients are conveyed by compressed air and water is added at the nozzle. **Shotcrete** is the modern generic term for pneumatically projected mortar or concrete that may be applied by either the **dry-mix** or **wet-mix** process, in which all the constituents (cement, aggregate, water, and admixtures) are mixed before being propelled through a hose and discharged from a nozzle at high velocity onto the receiving surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:**
> Under the 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), students must clearly differentiate **Gunite (dry-mix only)** from **Shotcrete (dry-mix or wet-mix)**. Gunite is essentially a *sub-set* (the dry process) of what is broadly called Shotcrete. Standard reference codes: **IS 9012**, **ACI 506**, and **IS 1343** (for sprayed concrete terminology).

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are spray-painting a wall, but instead of paint, you are spraying a powerful jet of cement-sand mixture at such high speed that when it hits the wall, it **sticks firmly and compacts itself**. That is exactly what Guniting/Shotcreting does:

- 🎨 **Paint Gun Analogy** — A regular spray gun atomizes paint using air pressure; a cement gun atomizes and propels dry cement-sand mix using compressed air at pressures of about **3 to 7 bar**.
- 🧱 **Spatula + Hammer Analogy** — When the high-velocity particles hit the surface, they compact together like a craftsman pressing mortar with the back of a trowel while simultaneously tapping it. This impact compaction is what gives shotcrete its characteristically **high density and low permeability**, even though no vibration is used.
- 🏗️ **Formwork-Free Construction** — Unlike conventional concrete that needs moulds to hold it in place until it hardens, the sprayed mix sticks to vertical and overhead surfaces by itself. This makes it indispensable for **repair and rehabilitation** jobs where formwork cannot be erected.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Indicators

| Parameter | Typical Value | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Application air pressure | **3–7 bar (≈ 0.3–0.7 MPa)** | ACI 506 |
| Water–cement ratio | **0.35 – 0.50** | IS 9012 |
| 28-day compressive strength | **25 – 50 MPa** | IS 9012 |
| In-situ density | **2200 – 2300 kg/m³** | ACI 506R |
| Rebound loss (dry mix, vertical) | **15 – 30 %** | IS 9012 |
| Rebound loss (wet mix, vertical) | **5 – 15 %** | IS 9012 |
| Minimum curing period | **7 days (preferably 14 days)** | IS 9012 |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Define Gunite/Shotcrete", always close with the line *"…pneumatically projected through a hose and nozzle at high velocity onto the prepared surface."* Examiners in Kerala valuation camps award the closing mark specifically for the keyword **"pneumatically projected"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pneumatically Applied Concrete

Sprayed concrete is broadly classified into two processes (per **ACI 506R** and **IS 9012:1978** reaffirmed 2002):

**Process Tree (Exam-Favourite Diagram):**

```
PNEUMATICALLY APPLIED CONCRETE
            │
   ┌────────┴─────────┐
   │                  │
 DRY-MIX            WET-MIX
 (GUNITE)           (SHOTCRETE)
   │                  │
 Dry materials      All ingredients
 conveyed by air    pre-mixed and
 + water added      pumped; air
 at nozzle          added at nozzle
   │                  │
 Lower w/c          Higher slump
 possible           (~50–75 mm)
 Higher rebound     Lower rebound
 Suits thin/        Suits thick
 overhead repairs   mass placements
```

### 2.2 The Dry-Mix Process (Gunite) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Batching & Dry Mixing** — Cement and pre-graded sand (≤ 4.75 mm) are thoroughly dry-mixed in a paddle or pan mixer for at least **2–3 minutes** until a uniform colour is achieved.
2. **Loading the Gun** — The dry mix is fed into the **cement gun** (rotary or double-chamber type). Compressed air at **≈ 4–5 bar** fluidizes the mix and pushes it through the delivery hose.
3. **Conveyance Through Hose** — The dry mix travels as a *fluidized cloud* through the hose (typically 25–50 mm bore). No water is added yet.
4. **Water Injection at the Nozzle** — Just before discharge, a perforated water ring around the nozzle injects pressurised water into the moving stream. The operator controls the water valve manually based on the appearance of the spray.
5. **High-Velocity Projection** — The hydrated mix leaves the nozzle at **≈ 80–100 m/s** and impacts the prepared substrate.
6. **Compaction & Build-up** — Impact velocity compacts the layer; successive layers of **15–50 mm** are built up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the operator matters:** In dry-mix, the **water-cement ratio is operator-controlled in real time**. An inexperienced nozzleman can produce a weak, porous layer or a soupy, sagging layer. This is why KTU questions stress *quality control by skilled nozzleman*.

### 2.3 The Wet-Mix Process (Shotcrete) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Pre-Mixing** — All ingredients (cement + aggregates up to **20 mm** + water + admixtures) are batched and mixed in a conventional concrete mixer to a workable slump of **50–75 mm**.
2. **Pumping** — The plastic concrete is pumped through the delivery hose using a **peristaltic or piston pump** (e.g., Putzmeister, Schwing).
3. **Air Injection at Nozzle** — Compressed air is introduced at the nozzle through a separate ring, accelerating the concrete out at high velocity.
4. **Projection & Build-up** — Sprayed onto the surface; since the concrete is already wet, less rebound occurs. Layers of **50–100 mm** can be placed in a single pass.

### 2.4 Materials Used — Detailed Composition

**a) Cement:** Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC 43 or 53 grade) is most common. For emergency / cold-weather repairs, **Rapid Hardening Cement** or **High Alumina Cement** is used. Sulphate-Resisting Cement is chosen for aggressive environments.

**b) Aggregates:**

| Application | Max Aggregate Size | Sand/Aggregate Proportion |
|---|---|---|
| Gunite (dry-mix) | Sand only, **≤ 4.75 mm** | Sand : Cement = **3 : 1** (by mass) typical |
| Shotcrete (wet-mix) | Up to **10–20 mm** | Aggregate : Cement = **4 : 1** typical |

> Sands should be **clean, well-graded, sharp** and free from clay, silt, and organic impurities (per **IS 383**).

**c) Water:** Potable water, free from oils, acids, alkalis, and organic matter. **w/c ratio kept between 0.35 and 0.50**. Lower w/c gives higher strength but is harder to apply.

**d) Admixtures (per IS 9103):**

- **Accelerators** — Sodium silicate (water-glass), calcium chloride (max **2 %** by mass of cement), sodium aluminate. Used when early strength is needed (e.g., tunnel support).
- **Retarders** — Used in hot weather to delay setting.
- **Superplasticizers** — To reduce water demand while maintaining workability.
- **Air-entraining agents** — For freeze-thaw resistance.
- **Silica fume / fly ash** — For dense, low-permeability mix.

**e) Reinforcement:** Steel welded wire mesh (50 mm × 50 mm, 2–3 mm dia.) anchored to the substrate, or steel/propylene fibres added to the mix (Fibre-Reinforced Shotcrete / **FRS**).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **most frequently tested** under Module 3 of PECET634 (Repair & Rehabilitation of Structures, KTU 2024 Scheme).

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Relation | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressive strength | $f_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Cube/cylinder test (150 mm cube or 100 mm core) |
| 2 | Water–cement ratio (Abrams) | $f_c = \dfrac{K_1}{K_2^{w/c}}$ | dimensionless | Strength vs w/c |
| 3 | Rebound loss (vertical) | $\%R = \dfrac{W_{rebound}}{W_{sprayed}} \times 100$ | % | Quality control & material estimation |
| 4 | Effective material on wall | $W_{wall} = W_{sprayed} \left(1 - \dfrac{\%R}{100}\right)$ | kg | Quantity estimation |
| 5 | Air requirement (conveyance) | $Q_{air} \approx 5 \text{ to } 10 \;\text{m}^3/\text{min}$ for 50 mm bore hose | m³/min | Equipment sizing |
| 6 | Nozzle exit velocity | $v \approx \sqrt{2gh_e}$ (Bernoulli, simplified) | m/s | Design check |
| 7 | Sand–cement ratio (typical) | $S/C = 3 : 1$ to $4 : 1$ | by mass | Mix design |
| 8 | Layer thickness (single pass) | 15–50 mm (gunite), 50–100 mm (shotcrete) | mm | Field practice |
| 9 | In-situ density | 2200–2300 kg/m³ | kg/m³ | Acceptance test |
| 10 | Bond strength with substrate | $\tau_{bond} \geq 0.8$ to $1.7$ MPa | MPa | Pull-off test |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge rehabilitation** — Jacketing of piers and girders where formwork is impossible over a flowing river.
- **Tunnel & underground construction** — Immediate ground support after excavation (NATM method).
- **Marine & hydraulic structures** — Dams, aqueducts, canal linings, swimming pools (low permeability).
- **Heritage restoration** — Domes, vaults, arches where casting is impossible.
- **Fire-damaged structures** — Rapid restoration of cover concrete.
- **Strengthening works** — Addition of web/flange thickness to beams by spraying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rebound Loss & Effective Material Retained on Wall

**Given:** A repair patch of volume $V_{patch}$ is to be filled using dry-mix gunite. Sand–cement ratio = 3:1, w/c = 0.45. Rebound loss on a vertical surface = 20 %.

**Find:** Quantity of cement, sand, and water required *before* accounting for rebound, and the *effective* weight deposited on the wall.

**Step 1 — Establish the dry mix ratio (by mass):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cement : Sand : Water} &= 1 : 3 : 0.45 \quad \text{(by mass)} \\
\text{Total mass per unit} &= 1 + 3 + 0.45 = 4.45 \;\text{parts}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** In dry-mix gunite, water is added only at the nozzle, but for *batch design purposes* the mix ratio is expressed as if water were included. The dry components (cement + sand) sum to 4 parts; the wet component (water) is 0.45 parts.

**Step 2 — Mass fractions of each component:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass fraction of cement} &= \dfrac{1}{4.45} \approx 0.2247 \\
\text{Mass fraction of sand} &= \dfrac{3}{4.45} \approx 0.6742 \\
\text{Mass fraction of water} &= \dfrac{0.45}{4.45} \approx 0.1011
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** These fractions let us convert a total sprayed mass into individual component masses.

**Step 3 — Volume of patch and required wet density:**

Assume patch dimensions: **Length = 4 m, Height = 3 m, Thickness = 50 mm = 0.05 m**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{patch} &= L \times H \times t \\
&= 4 \times 3 \times 0.05 = 0.60 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

In-situ wet density of gunite ≈ **2250 kg/m³**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{on\,wall} &= \rho \times V_{patch} \\
&= 2250 \times 0.60 = 1350 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** This is the *effective* mass that must remain stuck to the wall.

**Step 4 — Account for 20 % rebound loss on vertical surface:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{sprayed} &= \dfrac{W_{on\,wall}}{1 - R} \\
&= \dfrac{1350}{1 - 0.20} \\
&= \dfrac{1350}{0.80} = 1687.5 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Since 20 % bounces off, we must *spray* 25 % more than what stays. Dividing by (1 − R) does this.

**Step 5 — Component-wise quantities to be sprayed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{cement} &= 1687.5 \times 0.2247 \approx 379.2 \;\text{kg} \\
W_{sand} &= 1687.5 \times 0.6742 \approx 1137.7 \;\text{kg} \\
W_{water} &= 1687.5 \times 0.1011 \approx 170.6 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Bags of cement (1 bag = 50 kg):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{bags} &= \dfrac{379.2}{50} = 7.58 \approx 8 \;\text{bags}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Always round up to the next whole bag on site — partial bags cannot be batched accurately.

### 3.2 Derivation: Strength vs Water–Cement Ratio (Abrams' Law, Adapted)

Abrams' Law in its simplest form:

$$f_c = \dfrac{K_1}{K_2^{w/c}}$$

For Ordinary Portland Cement at 28 days, the de Larrard constants are approximately **K₁ ≈ 96 MPa** and **K₂ ≈ 4.0**.

**Problem:** Estimate the 28-day compressive strength of a gunite mix with **w/c = 0.40** and another with **w/c = 0.50**.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{c,\,0.40} &= \dfrac{96}{4.0^{\,0.40}} \\
&= \dfrac{96}{e^{0.40 \cdot \ln 4}} \\
&= \dfrac{96}{e^{0.40 \times 1.386}} \\
&= \dfrac{96}{e^{0.5544}} \\
&= \dfrac{96}{1.741} \approx 55.1 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{c,\,0.50} &= \dfrac{96}{4.0^{\,0.50}} \\
&= \dfrac{96}{4^{0.5}} = \dfrac{96}{2} = 48.0 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation for KTU valuation:** Halving the w/c from 0.50 to 0.40 produces **≈ 15 % higher strength**, but on site this becomes harder to spray because the mix is drier. This is the fundamental trade-off the examiner tests.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Dry-Mix vs Wet-Mix

| Parameter | Dry-Mix (Gunite) | Wet-Mix (Shotcrete) |
|---|---|---|
| Water added at | **Nozzle** | **Mixer** |
| w/c control | By **operator** | By **mix design** |
| Typical w/c | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.40 – 0.50 |
| Rebound loss (vertical) | **15 – 30 %** | **5 – 15 %** |
| Dust / overspray | High | Low |
| Maximum aggregate size | ≤ 4.75 mm (sand only) | Up to 20 mm |
| Layer thickness per pass | 15 – 50 mm | 50 – 100 mm |
| Output rate | 4 – 8 m³/hr | 8 – 20 m³/hr |
| Equipment cost | Lower | Higher |
| Operator skill needed | Very high | Moderate |
| Best suited for | Thin repairs, overhead, complex geometry | Thick linings, mass works |

### 3.4 Quality Control Tests (Numericals Often Asked)

| Test | Standard | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Cube compressive strength (150 mm) | IS 516 | $f_c \geq 25$ MPa at 28 days |
| Core test (in-situ) | IS 516, ASTM C42 | $f_c \geq 0.85 \times f_{ck}$ of original |
| Rebound hammer | IS 13311 (Part 2) | Indicative, not conclusive |
| Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity | IS 13311 (Part 1) | > 3.5 km/s — good quality |
| Pull-off bond test | ASTM C1583 | $\tau \geq 1.7$ MPa |
| Density | IS 1199 | 2200 – 2300 kg/m³ |

> **Exam Tip:** If asked for *acceptance tests*, mention IS 516 (compression) and ASTM C1583 / pull-off (bond). Examiners award the **standards-code mark** explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Dry-Mix (Gunite) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sand Hopper] --> B[Paddle / Pan Mixer]
    C[Cement Bags] --> B
    B --> D[Cement Gun - Rotary Type]
    E[Air Compressor 4-5 bar] --> D
    D --> F[Delivery Hose 25-50 mm bore]
    F --> G[Water Ring at Nozzle]
    H[Water Pump] --> G
    G --> I[Nozzle - Exit Velocity 80-100 m/s]
    I --> J[Prepared Substrate]
    J --> K[Compacted Gunite Layer 15-50 mm]
    K --> L[Rebound Material Falls Down]
    K --> M[Curing Minimum 7 Days]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Wet-Mix (Shotcrete) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aggregate Bins] --> B[Concrete Mixer]
    C[Cement] --> B
    D[Water + Admixtures] --> B
    B --> E[Slump 50-75 mm Wet Concrete]
    E --> F[Piston or Peristaltic Pump]
    G[Air Compressor] --> H[Air Ring at Nozzle]
    F --> I[Delivery Hose]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Nozzle Exit High Velocity]
    J --> K[Substrate - Layer 50-100 mm]
    K --> L[Reduced Rebound 5-15 percent]
    K --> M[Curing 7 to 14 Days]
```

### 4.3 Functional Cross-Section of a Gunite Nozzle (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NOZZLE_BLOCK
        IN1[Dry Mix Hose Inlet - Top] --> NRING[Perforated Water Ring]
        NRING --> CONE[Converging Cone - Internal Diameter Reduces]
        CONE --> THROAT[Throat - Highest Velocity]
        THROAT --> EXIT[Exit Orifice 20-25 mm]
        WATERIN[Pressurised Water Side Inlet] --> NRING
    end
    EXIT --> SPRAY[Atomised Cement-Sand Jet Striking Wall]
    SPRAY --> SUB[Substrate - Impact Compaction]
```

### 4.4 Repair Sequence Block Diagram (Sprayed Concrete Jacketing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Survey and Mark Delaminated Areas] --> S2[Step 2: Remove Loose and Deteriorated Concrete by Chipping]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Clean Substrate - Sandblast or Wire Brush]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Bonding Agent or Slurry Coat if Required]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Fix Welded Wire Mesh 50x50 mm with Anchors]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Pre-wet Substrate to SSD Condition]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Spray Gunite or Shotcrete in Layers]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Trim and Float Surface for Finish]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Cure with Water or Wet Hessian for 7-14 Days]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Quality Tests - Cube, Pull-off, UPV]
```
