<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer / Direct Recall)

---

#### **Q1. Define Gunite. State any two advantages of Guniting over conventional concreting.**
*`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank 2024 Scheme, PECET634, Module 3]`*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (3 marks):**
Gunite is a mixture of cement, sand and water, in which the cement and sand are conveyed through a hose by compressed air, and water is added at the nozzle immediately before projection. The mix is pneumatically sprayed at high velocity onto the prepared surface, where impact compaction produces a dense, strong layer without the need for vibration or formwork.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **No formwork required** — Even on vertical and overhead surfaces, gunite stays in place due to its own cohesion and impact compaction.
2. **Excellent bond** — The high-velocity impact produces a strong mechanical and chemical bond with the substrate, yielding bond strength of **1.0–1.7 MPa**.

> **Examiner Note:** Award full 3 marks only if the student writes "**pneumatically projected**" and "**water added at nozzle**" — these two phrases are mandatory.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Dry-mix and Wet-mix process of shotcreting (any four points).**
*`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Bank 2024 Scheme]`*

**Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Dry-Mix Process | Wet-Mix Process |
|---|---|---|
| Water addition | At the **nozzle** | In the **mixer** |
| Rebound loss | **Higher** (15–30 %) | **Lower** (5–15 %) |
| Aggregate size | Sand only (≤ 4.75 mm) | Up to 20 mm |
| Output rate | Lower (4–8 m³/hr) | Higher (8–20 m³/hr) |
| Operator skill needed | Very high | Moderate |

(Any four of the above earn full 3 marks.)

---

### 📗 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ Question A — 14 Marks *(Choice 1)*

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **dry-mix process of Guniting**. Describe the equipment used and the role of the nozzleman. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the various **applications of Gunite/Shotcrete in repair and rehabilitation** of structures, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer to Q.A(a):

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Guniting (dry-mix process) is the pneumatic application of a cement-sand mortar, where the dry ingredients are conveyed by compressed air and water is added at the nozzle.

**2. Equipment (3 marks):**
- **Cement Gun** — Rotary or double-chamber type, which feeds the dry mix into the delivery hose under air pressure.
- **Air Compressor** — Delivers **4–5 bar** pressure at flow rates of **5–10 m³/min** for a 50 mm bore hose.
- **Water Pump** — Supplies **2–3 bar** pressurised water to the nozzle ring.
- **Delivery Hose** — 25–50 mm bore, fabric-reinforced rubber hose.
- **Nozzle** — Has a perforated water ring just upstream of the orifice.
- **Mixer** — Paddle or pan mixer for dry blending.

**3. Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. Dry-mix cement and sand (1:3 by mass) for 2–3 minutes.
2. Load into cement gun; convey via compressed air.
3. Inject water at nozzle; spray onto prepared substrate at **80–100 m/s**.
4. Build up in **15–50 mm** layers; cure for **7–14 days**.

**4. Role of Nozzleman (1 mark):** The nozzleman is the **quality controller in real time** — he adjusts the water valve to maintain the correct consistency, keeps the nozzle perpendicular and at **0.6–1.5 m** from the surface, and ensures uniform coverage without sagging.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer to Q.A(b):

**Applications in Repair & Rehabilitation:**

**a) Structural Jacketing (2 marks):** Adding concrete cover to beams, columns, and piers where formwork cannot be erected (e.g., over rivers, in basements).

**b) Fire and Corrosion Damage Repair (2 marks):** Restoration of cover concrete lost due to spalling, fire exposure, or rebar corrosion.

**c) Bridge Piers and Girders (1 mark):** Strengthening of pier caps and girders, including underwater portions (using special additives).

**d) Tunnel and Underground Repairs (1 mark):** Repair of existing tunnel linings.

**e) Heritage Structures (1 mark):** Restoration of domes, arches, and shells of historical buildings.

> **Examples:** Restoration of Mattancherry Palace arches, bridge pier jacketing on NH-66, fire-damaged columns in Kerala industries.

---

### ✏️ Question B — 14 Marks *(Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)*

**Q. (a)** Compare the **dry-mix and wet-mix processes** of shotcreting in detail. State their relative merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **materials used in guniting** along with their proportions and the function of each material. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer to Q.B(a):

**Comparison (5 marks) — Tabulated:**

| Criterion | Dry-Mix (Gunite) | Wet-Mix (Shotcrete) |
|---|---|---|
| Water addition | At nozzle | In mixer |
| w/c control | Operator-dependent | Mix-design controlled |
| Rebound | 15–30 % | 5–15 % |
| Aggregate size | ≤ 4.75 mm | ≤ 20 mm |
| Output rate | 4–8 m³/hr | 8–20 m³/hr |
| Dust | High | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Strength | Higher achievable (low w/c) | Slightly lower |

**Merits & Demerits (2 marks):**
- Dry-mix is **preferred for thin, overhead, complex repairs** because the mix can be tailored instantly by the nozzleman. *Demerit:* rebound is high and quality is operator-dependent.
- Wet-mix is **preferred for mass works and tunnel linings** because output is higher and rebound is lower. *Demerit:* equipment cost is higher and starts/stops are slower.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer to Q.B(b):

**Materials & Proportions (7 marks):**

**1. Cement (1.5 marks):** Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC 43/53 grade) is standard. Rapid Hardening Cement for emergency repairs. **Proportion: 1 part by mass.**

**2. Sand (1.5 marks):** Clean, sharp, well-graded sand passing 4.75 mm IS sieve with fineness modulus 2.4–3.0. Free from clay, silt, organic matter. **Proportion: 3 parts by mass.**

**3. Water (1 mark):** Potable, clean, pH > 6. **w/c ratio: 0.35–0.50.** Lower values give higher strength but are harder to spray.

**4. Admixtures (1.5 marks):**
- **Accelerators** (e.g., sodium silicate, calcium chloride up to 2 %) — for early strength.
- **Plasticizers / superplasticizers** — to reduce w/c.
- **Air-entraining agents** — for freeze-thaw durability.
- **Silica fume** — for dense, low-permeability mixes.

**5. Reinforcement (1 mark):** Welded wire mesh **50 mm × 50 mm** with 2–3 mm dia. wires, anchored to the substrate. For FRS (Fibre-Reinforced Shotcrete), 0.5–1.5 % by volume of steel or polypropylene fibres.

**6. Curing water (0.5 marks):** Continuously wet for a minimum of **7 days**, preferably **14 days**.

> **Standard Reference:** IS 9012:1978 (reaffirmed 2002) — recommended practice for shotcreting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgot the word "pneumatically"** — Costs the definition mark. Always write *"…pneumatically projected through a hose and nozzle."*
> 2. **Confused Gunite with Grout** — Gunite is a *sprayed mortar*, not an injected grout. Grout is fluid; gunite is plastic at impact.
> 3. **Mixed up w/c ratios** — Lower w/c = higher strength but harder to spray. Examiners deduct marks if you claim a 0.30 w/c is "easier to apply".
> 4. **Missed the role of the nozzleman** — In dry-mix, the **operator's skill controls the water-cement ratio in real time**. This is the single most tested concept under Module 3.
> 5. **Forgot to mention curing** — KTU valuation keys *explicitly check* for the line *"cured for 7–14 days"*; missing it costs 0.5–1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **No mention of standards/codes** — Always cite **IS 9012**, **ACI 506**, **IS 516**, **ASTM C1583** wherever relevant — examiners award the "standards-code" mark.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Gunite = **Dry-mix only**; Shotcrete = Generic term (dry-mix or wet-mix).
- ✅ Both are **pneumatically projected** at high velocity onto the surface.
- ✅ Water is added **at the nozzle** in dry-mix and **in the mixer** in wet-mix.
- ✅ Typical mix ratio for gunite: **Cement : Sand = 1 : 3** (by mass); w/c = 0.35–0.50.
- ✅ Aggregate size: **≤ 4.75 mm (sand only)** for gunite; **≤ 20 mm** for wet-mix shotcrete.
- ✅ Air pressure: **3–7 bar**; nozzle exit velocity: **80–100 m/s**.
- ✅ Rebound loss: **15–30 %** (dry-mix, vertical); **5–15 %** (wet-mix).
- ✅ Compressive strength: **25–50 MPa** at 28 days; can exceed **50 MPa** with low w/c.
- ✅ In-situ density: **2200–2300 kg/m³**.
- ✅ Layer thickness per pass: **15–50 mm** (gunite), **50–100 mm** (shotcrete).
- ✅ Curing: **Minimum 7 days, preferably 14 days**.
- ✅ Quality tests: **IS 516 cube**, **ASTM C1583 pull-off**, **IS 13311 UPV/Rebound hammer**.
- ✅ Standards to cite: **IS 9012**, **IS 516**, **ACI 506R**, **ASTM C1583**, **IS 383**, **IS 9103**.
- ✅ Applications: Bridge piers, beams, columns, tunnels, domes, fire-damaged structures, marine works, canal linings.
- ✅ Advantages: No formwork, excellent bond, high strength, fast, suits complex shapes.
- ✅ Disadvantages: Skilled nozzleman needed, rebound loss, dust (dry-mix), higher cement content.
- ✅ The **nozzleman is the most critical quality-control element** in the entire process.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expansive Cement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Expansive Cement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expansive Cement** is a special hydraulic binder that, unlike ordinary Portland cement (OPC), undergoes a **controlled, volume-increase reaction** during the early stages of hydration (typically within **3 to 14 days**), thereby deliberately counteracting the inherent drying shrinkage strains of conventional cement concrete.

As per **ASTM C 845**, expansive cements are classified into three principal categories — **Type K**, **Type M**, and **Type S** — each based on the source of expansive compound formation. The purpose of this controlled expansion is to impose a chemical pre-stress on the hardened matrix, minimizing or completely eliminating shrinkage cracking in structural concrete, water-retaining structures, and repair applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a tightly-woven fabric shirt. When you wash it, it shrinks and feels tight. Now imagine if the cloth had tiny **built-in stretchers** that pushed the fibers outward as it dried — the shirt would return to its original size. Expansive cement works exactly this way inside concrete: it contains microscopic "stretchers" (ettringite crystals) that **push the concrete outward** to compensate for the natural shrinkage that occurs as water evaporates.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Linear Expansion Range:** 0.04% to 0.10% (for shrinkage-compensating) and up to 0.30% (for self-stressing cements)
- **Water-Cement Ratio (w/c):** Strictly maintained between **0.40 to 0.55**
- **Curing Period:** Minimum **7 days** of moist curing is mandatory
- **Code Reference:** **ASTM C 845** (Standard Specification for Expansive Hydraulic Cement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The mechanism of ettringite formation (C₃A + 3CaSO₄ + 32H₂O → C₃A·3CaSO₄·32H₂O) is the **most frequently tested concept** in KTU university examinations for this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Expansion — Step-by-Step Logic

The expansion in expansive cement is primarily driven by the formation of **Ettringite** (also known as Candlot's salt or calcium trisulfoaluminate hydrate) crystals during the early hydration phase.

**Step 1: Initial Hydration Phase (0–24 hours)**
- Tricalcium Aluminate (`C₃A`) from the cement clinker reacts aggressively with **Calcium Sulfate (CaSO₄·2H₂O — Gypsum)** added during manufacture.
- This reaction forms needle-like ettringite crystals on the surface of cement grains.

**Step 2: Crystal Growth (1–7 days)**
- The ettringite crystals (`C₃A·3CaSO₄·32H₂O`) continue to grow in size, occupying more volume.
- This **volume increase** is the source of the expansive force.
- Free lime (CaO) in Type K cement hydrates to form `Ca(OH)₂`, contributing additional expansion.

**Step 3: Stabilization Phase (7–28 days)**
- The expansion rate progressively decreases.
- The matrix gradually restrains the expansion, transferring the force into a **chemical pre-stress** in the concrete.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Ettringite Formation Equation | `C₃A + 3CaSO₄ + 32H₂O → C₃A·3CaSO₄·32H₂O` | Hydration reaction |
| Free Lime Hydration | `CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂` | Solid-to-solid volume change |
| Linear Expansion (Shrinkage-Compensating) | $\varepsilon_l$ | **0.04% – 0.10%** |
| Linear Expansion (Self-Stressing) | $\varepsilon_l$ | **0.10% – 0.30%** |
| Confined Expansion Pressure (Self-Stressing) | $\sigma_c$ | **1.5 MPa – 3.5 MPa** (≈ 220 – 500 psi) |
| Water-Cement Ratio (Recommended) | $w/c$ | **0.40 – 0.55** |
| Specific Gravity of Type K | $G$ | **≈ 3.20** |
| Minimum Curing Duration | $t_{cure}$ | **7 days** (moist cure) |
| Initial Setting Time | $t_{i}$ | **≥ 75 minutes** |
| Final Setting Time | $t_{f}$ | **≤ 10 hours** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** "Shrinkage-compensating" cement only counteracts drying shrinkage (mild expansion), whereas "Self-stressing" cement produces a net tensile stress in the reinforcement due to higher expansion — useful for pre-cast pressure pipes and arches.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Water-Retaining Structures:** Used in swimming pools, water tanks, and dam linings to prevent leakage cracks.
- **Bridge Deck Overlays:** Minimizes reflective cracking in repair overlays.
- **Post-Tensioning Substitutes:** Self-stressing expansive cement acts as a **chemical post-tensioning** agent in pre-cast concrete pressure pipes (PCCP) and arches.
- **Industrial Floor Repairs:** Compensates for shrinkage in large floor slabs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation of Ettringite Expansion

The core hydration reaction for ettringite formation is given as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3A \;+\; 3\,CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O \;+\; 26\,H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Tricalcium aluminate (one mole) reacts with three moles of gypsum (which itself carries 2 moles of water of crystallization) plus 26 moles of free mixing water, yielding one mole of ettringite containing 32 moles of water.

### 3.2 Molar Volume Analysis (Why Expansion Occurs)

To understand the volumetric expansion, we calculate the **absolute volume change** between reactants and product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{reactants} &= n(C_3A) \cdot M_V(C_3A) + n(CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O) \cdot M_V(CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O) + n(H_2O) \cdot M_V(H_2O) \\[4pt]
V_{reactants} &= 1 \times 88.8 \;+\; 3 \times 74.2 \;+\; 26 \times 18.0 \\[4pt]
V_{reactants} &= 88.8 + 222.6 + 468.0 = 779.4 \; cm^3/mol
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{product} &= n(C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O) \cdot M_V(ettringite) \\[4pt]
V_{product} &= 1 \times 1456.5 = 1456.5 \; cm^3/mol
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The product (ettringite) has a higher solid-volume because the ettringite lattice traps large amounts of water in its crystalline structure, increasing the overall solid volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{expansion} &= V_{product} - V_{reactants} \\[4pt]
\Delta V_{expansion} &= 1456.5 - 779.4 = 677.1 \; cm^3/mol \\[4pt]
\text{Percent Expansion} &= \left(\frac{\Delta V_{expansion}}{V_{reactants}}\right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \left(\frac{677.1}{779.4}\right) \times 100 \approx 86.9\% \text{ (theoretical solid-volume increase)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Although the theoretical solid-volume increase is ≈ **86.9%**, the **observable bulk expansion** is much smaller (≈ 0.04% – 0.30%) because the expansion is **restrained by the cement paste matrix and aggregates**. The expansive force is what matters — it is the pressure, not the visible volume, that compensates shrinkage.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Types of Expansive Cements

| **Property** | **Type K (K-Cement)** | **Type M (M-Cement)** | **Type S (S-Cement)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expansive Component** | C₃A + CaO (free lime) | Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC) | C₃A + excess CaSO₄ |
| **Sulfate Source** | Added gypsum | CaSO₄ in CAC formulation | High gypsum content |
| **Stability** | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Strength Development** | Comparable to OPC | Slightly faster | Comparable to OPC |
| **Shrinkage Compensation** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Self-Stressing Capacity** | Possible | Yes (Common) | Yes |
| **Common Application** | Repair grouts, overlays | Pressure pipes, arches | General structural use |
| **Cost** | High | Highest | Moderate |

### 3.4 Estimation of Confined Expansion Pressure

For a steel-confined expansive cement paste, the chemical pre-stress can be approximated by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c &= E_{steel} \cdot \varepsilon_l \cdot \left(\frac{A_s}{A_c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_c$ = Confined expansion pressure (MPa)
- $E_{steel}$ = Modulus of steel (≈ **200,000 MPa**)
- $\varepsilon_l$ = Linear expansion of cement (≈ **0.0025** for self-stressing)
- $A_s$ = Cross-sectional area of steel restraint
- $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area of cement

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** This formula is used in the design of pre-stressed concrete pressure pipes (PCCP) where the expansive cement imposes a circumferential pre-stress on the steel cylinder, similar to mechanical pre-stressing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Expansive Cement Reaction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Materials Clinker OPC] --> B[Addition of Expansive Agents]
    B --> C{Type Classification}
    C -->|Type K| D[C3A + Free Lime CaO + Gypsum]
    C -->|Type M| E[Calcium Aluminate Cement CAC + Gypsum]
    C -->|Type S| F[High C3A + Excess Sulfate]
    D --> G[Early Hydration 0-24 hr]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Ettringite Crystal Growth]
    H --> I[Volume Expansion Phase 1-7 days]
    I --> J[Matrix Restrains Expansion]
    J --> K[Chemical Pre-Stress Develops]
    K --> L[Shrinkage Compensation Achieved]
    L --> M[Final Hardened Product No Cracks]
```

### 4.2 Mechanism Flow: Ettringite Formation Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[C3A Tricalcium Aluminate] --> C[Ettringite C3A 3CaSO4 32H2O]
    A2[Gypsum CaSO4 2H2O] --> C
    A3[Mixing Water H2O] --> C
    C --> D[Crystal Growth on Cement Grains]
    D --> E[Needle Shaped Crystals Push Particles]
    E --> F[Bulk Volume Expansion]
    F --> G[Matrix Restraint Converts to Pre Stress]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Performance Diagram: OPC vs. Expansive Cement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OPC [Ordinary Portland Cement]
        OPC1[Initial Volume 100 percent] --> OPC2[Drying Shrinkage -0.05 percent]
        OPC2 --> OPC3[Net Volume 99.95 percent]
        OPC3 --> OPC4[Shrinkage Cracks Form]
    end
    subgraph EC [Expansive Cement]
        EC1[Initial Volume 100 percent] --> EC2[Controlled Expansion +0.07 percent]
        EC2 --> EC3[Shrinkage Occurs -0.05 percent]
        EC3 --> EC4[Net Volume 100.02 percent Crack Free]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define Expansive Cement. List its three main types as per ASTM C 845.

**Model Answer:**
Expansive cement is a special hydraulic cement that expands during early hydration (3–14 days) to counteract drying shrinkage of concrete. The three types per ASTM C 845 are:
1. **Type K** — contains C₃A, free lime (CaO), and gypsum
2. **Type M** — contains calcium aluminate cement (CAC) and calcium sulfate
3. **Type S** — contains high C₃A with excess calcium sulfate

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
State the chemical reaction responsible for expansion in Type K expansive cement.

**Model Answer:**
The expansion is caused by the formation of **ettringite** (calcium trisulfoaluminate hydrate) via the reaction:

$$
C_3A + 3CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 26H_2O \longrightarrow C_3A \cdot 3CaSO_4 \cdot 32H_2O \; (\text{ettringite})
$$

The growth of these ettringite crystals produces the expansive force.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE 3 — INTERNAL CHOICE (Select either A or B)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of expansion in expansive cement with reference to the formation of ettringite. Discuss the role of curing in this process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The mechanism of expansion in expansive cement is governed by the **ettringite formation reaction** during early hydration. The key steps are:

1. **Initial Dissolution:** When water is added, C₃A from the cement clinker and CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum) dissolve in the pore water.
2. **Ettringite Nucleation:** These ions combine to form ettringite crystals `C₃A·3CaSO₄·32H₂O` on the surface of cement grains.
3. **Crystal Growth:** The ettringite crystals grow in needle-like morphology, occupying a larger volume than the reactants (theoretical solid-volume increase ≈ 87%).
4. **Bulk Expansion:** This crystal growth pushes the cement paste outward, creating an **expansive force**.
5. **Restrained Expansion:** If the concrete is restrained (by aggregates, reinforcement, or confinement), the expansion is converted into a **chemical pre-stress** in the matrix.

**Role of Curing:**
- Curing is **critical** because the formation of ettringite requires a **continuous supply of water**.
- A minimum of **7 days of moist curing** is mandatory.
- **Insufficient curing** leads to incomplete ettringite formation, **loss of expansion**, and **secondary ettringite formation (DEF)** later, causing severe expansion and cracking (a durability hazard).
- Type K cement uses both ettringite formation and free lime hydration (`CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂`) for expansion.

**(b)** Compare the three types of expansive cements (Type K, M, and S) with respect to composition, expansive component, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Type K** | **Type M** | **Type S** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expansive Component** | C₃A + Free Lime (CaO) | Calcium Aluminate Cement (CAC) | C₃A + Excess Sulfate |
| **Sulfate Source** | Added gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) | Built into CAC | High gypsum content |
| **Composition Basis** | OPC clinker + CaO + SO₃ | OPC + Calcium Sulfoaluminate | OPC with high C₃A content |
| **Expansion Range** | 0.04 – 0.10% | Up to 0.30% (self-stressing) | 0.04 – 0.10% |
| **Stability** | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Typical Applications** | Slab overlays, repair grouts, water tanks | Pressure pipes, pre-stressed arches, linings | General shrinkage compensation |
| **Cost (Relative)** | High | Highest | Moderate |
| **ASTM Reference** | C 845 Type K | C 845 Type M | C 845 Type S |

**Conclusion:** Type K is the most widely used for general repair work; Type M is preferred for high-expansion (self-stressing) applications; Type S is used in specific structural scenarios.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**

**(a)** What is the difference between **shrinkage-compensating cement** and **self-stressing cement**? Provide two examples of applications for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Shrinkage-Compensating Cement:**
- Produces a **moderate expansion** (0.04% – 0.10%) that just **offsets drying shrinkage**.
- The expansion is typically restrained by mild reinforcement (0.15% – 0.20% steel).
- Net effect: crack-free concrete without significant pre-stress.
- **Applications:**
  1. Water-retaining tanks and reservoirs
  2. Bridge deck overlays and bridge deck repair
  3. Industrial floor slabs
  4. Tunnel linings

**Self-Stressing Cement:**
- Produces a **much higher expansion** (0.10% – 0.30%) that generates a **chemical pre-stress** in the steel reinforcement.
- Requires **heavier reinforcement** (1% – 2.5% steel) to restrain the expansion and convert it to useful pre-stress.
- Produces a net tensile pre-stress in the steel, which results in compressive pre-stress in the concrete.
- **Applications:**
  1. Pre-stressed concrete pressure pipes (PCCP)
  2. Pre-cast concrete arches and railway sleepers
  3. Pre-stressed roof slabs
  4. Foundation anchors

**Key Distinction:** Shrinkage-compensating cement *neutralizes* shrinkage; self-stressing cement *exceeds* shrinkage to create useful structural pre-stress.

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of using expansive cement in repair and rehabilitation works. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Crack Prevention:** Counteracts drying and thermal shrinkage cracks, ensuring a monolithic repair.
2. **Water Tightness:** Eliminates micro-cracks, making repairs impermeable — ideal for water tanks and dams.
3. **Chemical Pre-Stressing:** Self-stressing types eliminate the need for mechanical jacking in pre-cast components.
4. **Improved Bond:** Controlled expansion improves the bond between repair patch and parent concrete.
5. **Durability:** Reduces permeability, hence improves resistance to chloride and sulfate ingress.
6. **Reduced Construction Time:** No need for post-tensioning operations.

**Limitations:**
1. **High Cost:** 3 to 5 times costlier than OPC.
2. **Curing Sensitivity:** Requires strict 7-day moist curing; any lapse causes loss of expansion and cracking.
3. **Storage Limitations:** Has a short shelf life (typically **3 months**) due to air-moisture reaction.
4. **Risk of Delayed Ettringite Formation (DEF):** If curing is interrupted, secondary ettringite can form later, causing harmful expansion.
5. **Temperature Sensitivity:** Expansion rate varies with ambient temperature.
6. **Mixing Water Control:** Requires very accurate `w/c` ratio (0.40 – 0.55) for predictable behavior.
7. **Not a Substitute for Structural Repair:** Cannot compensate for inadequate structural capacity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ettringite formation with delayed ettringite formation (DEF):** Ettringite formation in expansive cement is **intentional and early-stage**, while DEF is a **pathological, late-stage** reaction that causes damage.
> 2. **Wrong expansion values:** Writing "10% expansion" instead of "0.10% linear expansion" is a **common silly mistake** that costs full marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Shrinkage-Compensating" with "Self-Stressing":** Many students interchange these terms; remember that shrinkage-compensating only neutralizes shrinkage, while self-stressing produces net pre-stress.
> 4. **Omitting the role of curing:** Examiners specifically look for a discussion of **7-day moist curing** as a key factor.
> 5. **Not mentioning ASTM C 845:** Citing the relevant standard is mandatory for full marks in KTU exams.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Expansive cement** is a hydraulic binder that expands during early hydration to **compensate shrinkage**.
- ✅ Expansion is driven by **ettringite formation** (`C₃A·3CaSO₄·32H₂O`) — a 32-water-molecule crystalline compound.
- ✅ **ASTM C 845** classifies expansive cements into **Type K, Type M, and Type S**.
- ✅ **Type K** uses C₃A + free lime (CaO) + gypsum as the expansive system.
- ✅ **Type M** uses **calcium aluminate cement (CAC)** as the expansive component.
- ✅ **Type S** uses high C₃A + excess sulfate formulation.
- ✅ **Shrinkage-compensating cement** → expansion 0.04% – 0.10% → neutralizes shrinkage.
- ✅ **Self-stressing cement** → expansion 0.10% – 0.30% → generates chemical pre-stress.
- ✅ **Minimum 7 days of moist curing is mandatory** — interruption leads to DEF and cracking.
- ✅ Water-cement ratio must be **0.40 – 0.55** for predictable expansion behavior.
- ✅ **Solid-volume increase** during ettringite formation is theoretically ≈ **87%**, but bulk expansion is only **0.04–0.30%** due to matrix restraint.
- ✅ **Shelf life** is limited to ≈ **3 months** due to air-moisture reactivity.
- ✅ Cost is **3–5 times** higher than OPC.
- ✅ Common applications: **bridge deck overlays, water tanks, pressure pipes, repair grouts**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ferro Cement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ferro Cement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ferrocement** is a composite structural material consisting of a **hydraulic cement mortar matrix** reinforced with closely spaced, multiple layers of **steel wire mesh** (either woven or welded) and optionally supplemented with **skeletal steel** (reinforcing bars). It is defined by the **American Concrete Institute (ACI Committee 549, 1997)** as:

> "A type of reinforced concrete in which the cement-sand mortar is reinforced with relatively small diameter wire meshes distributed throughout the cross-section."

**KTU 2024 Syllabus-Specific Definition:**
Ferrocement is characterized by its high **specific surface reinforcement** (ratio of reinforcement surface area to volume of mortar), typically ranging between **0.5 to 4.0 cm²/cm³**, which distinguishes it from conventional reinforced cement concrete (RCC) where the specific surface is on the order of **0.05 cm²/cm³**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from RCC:** Ferrocement uses *many layers of small-diameter wires* rather than *few large-diameter rebars*. The reinforcement is uniformly distributed throughout the section rather than concentrated near tensile faces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **wall using cement paste (like thick clay)**. If you simply plaster it, it will crack easily under load. Now imagine **layering multiple sheets of thin chicken wire mesh** inside the cement paste before it sets — like making a **layered composite sandwich** where each layer of wire arrests micro-cracks the moment they try to form.

**Analogy:** Think of ferrocement like **a papier-mâché aircraft wing**: paper layers alone are weak; glue alone is brittle; but alternating paper and glue creates a tough, crack-resistant, lightweight structure. Ferrocement works on exactly this principle — multiple *thin wire layers* embedded in a *thin mortar matrix*.

Another intuitive analogy: **A coconut's husk and shell** — the brittle shell resists compression but the fibrous husk arrests cracks and provides tensile strength. Ferrocement does the same with mortar (shell) and wire mesh (fibers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Surface Reinforcement (SSR):** The single most important parameter defining ferrocement. It is the total surface area of reinforcement (in cm²) divided by the volume of mortar (in cm³). High SSR = true ferrocement; Low SSR = behaves more like RCC.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mortar cover** | **1.5 mm to 3 mm** | Extremely thin cover over outer mesh |
| **Wire diameter** | **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** | Galvanized or mild steel |
| **Mesh opening** | **6 mm to 25 mm** | Hexagonal or square |
| **Section thickness** | **10 mm to 50 mm** | Very thin walls possible |
| **Cement-sand ratio** | **1:1.5 to 1:3** | Rich mortar mix |
| **Water-cement ratio** | **0.35 to 0.50** | Low w/c for strength & durability |
| **Tensile strength** | **6 MPa to 12 MPa** | Far exceeds plain mortar (≈ 2 MPa) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition & Material Components

Ferrocement is a **four-component composite system**. Each component plays a distinct structural role:

1. **Cement Matrix (Mortar):**
   - Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) — Grades 43 or 53 per IS 8112 / IS 12269
   - Fine aggregate: clean river sand passing **600 µm sieve**, free of organic impurities
   - Water: clean, potable, free from chlorides/sulphates
   - Optional: pozzolanic admixtures (fly ash, silica fume) for durability

2. **Reinforcement Mesh:**
   - **Woven mesh** (chicken mesh) — hexagonal openings, highly flexible
   - **Welded mesh** — square grid, stiffer, higher strength
   - **Expanded metal lath** — for plastered ferrocement applications

3. **Skeletal Steel (Optional):**
   - Mild steel or HYSD bars of 6 mm to 12 mm diameter
   - Provides primary load-carrying capacity; mesh distributes & arrests cracks

4. **Admixtures:**
   - Plasticizers / superplasticizers to achieve workable, low w/c mortar
   - Air-entraining agents for freeze-thaw resistance
   - Polymer modifiers (acrylic, SBR latex) for improved bond

## 2.2 Working Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Ferrocement

**Operational Principle:** When ferrocement is loaded in tension, micro-cracks initiate in the brittle mortar. These cracks **cannot propagate freely** because the densely distributed fine wire mesh **bridges the cracks** and carries tensile forces across them. The result is **progressive multi-cracking** rather than catastrophic single-crack failure.

- **Pre-cracking stage:** Mortar and mesh act compositely (strain compatibility).
- **Cracking stage:** Mortar cracks; mesh continues to carry load; cracks remain narrow (< 50 µm).
- **Post-cracking stage:** Mesh yields gradually; structure maintains integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Energy (Gf):** Ferrocement has fracture energy 10 to 50 times that of plain mortar due to crack-bridging mechanism. This is why ferrocement is preferred for **impact-resistant** and **seismic-resistant** retrofits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SSR} = \dfrac{V_r}{V_m} = \dfrac{n \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L}{A_c \cdot t}$ | Specific Surface Reinforcement | cm²/cm³ |
| 2 | $f_{tk} = f_{mc} + f_{mc}' \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_r}{V_m}\right)$ | Tensile strength of ferrocement | MPa |
| 3 | $f_{ck,f} = 0.55 \cdot f_{ck,m}$ | Compressive strength (≈ 55% of mortar cube strength) | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P}{A_s + n \cdot A_m}$ | Stress in mesh (transformed section) | MPa |
| 5 | $P_{ult} = A_s \cdot f_y + 0.003 \cdot E_s \cdot A_m \cdot n$ | Ultimate tensile capacity | kN |
| 6 | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{P}{u_s \cdot L_d}$ | Bond stress between mesh and mortar | MPa |
| 7 | $EI = E_s \cdot I_s + k \cdot E_m \cdot I_m$ | Flexural rigidity of ferrocement section | N·mm² |
| 8 | $M_{cr} = \dfrac{f_{r,f} \cdot I_g}{y_t}$ | First-crack moment | N·mm |
| 9 | $\Delta\sigma_{mesh} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_{tr}} \cdot n$ | Mesh stress increment under bending | MPa |
| 10 | $t_{min} = c_{min} + d_{mesh} + c_{min}$ | Minimum ferrocement thickness | mm |

**Where:**
- $V_r$ = Volume of reinforcement, $V_m$ = Volume of mortar
- $n$ = Number of mesh layers, $d$ = Wire diameter, $L$ = Wire length
- $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area, $t$ = thickness
- $f_{mc}$ = Mortar compressive strength, $f_y$ = Steel yield strength
- $n$ (modular ratio) = $E_s \cdot E_m^{-1}$
- $c_{min}$ = minimum cover (typically 1.5 mm for mesh, 3 mm for skeleton)
- $u_s$ = perimeter of skeletal bar, $L_d$ = development length

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Avoid the pipe symbol \vert inside tables. Use $\mid$ for absolute value notation in equations.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Repair & Rehabilitation

Ferrocement is extensively used in **strengthening and retrofitting** because:

- **Thin sections** (15–50 mm) can be added to existing beams/slabs without major dead-weight increase.
- **High tensile strength & crack control** make it ideal for jacketing RC columns and beams.
- **Formability** allows it to conform to curved surfaces (dome repairs, water tanks, pipes).
- **Corrosion resistance** is high (low cover + dense mortar + fine mesh means minimal crack widths, hence lower ingress).
- **Economy** — uses locally available materials, no heavy formwork.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Surface Reinforcement (SSR)

**Objective:** Derive the formula for SSR in terms of measurable geometric parameters.

**Step 1: Definition.** SSR is the total surface area of all reinforcement wires per unit volume of mortar:

$$
\text{SSR} = \frac{S_r}{V_m}
$$

where $S_r$ is total lateral surface area of all reinforcement wires.

**Step 2: For a single mesh layer of $n_w$ wires, each of diameter $d$ and length $L$,** the lateral surface area of one wire is:

$$
S_{one} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L
$$

**Step 3: For $N$ total wires (across all mesh layers combined),** total surface area becomes:

$$
S_r = N \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L
$$

**Step 4: Volume of mortar $V_m$** in a panel of cross-sectional area $A_c$ and thickness $t$ is approximated as $V_m \approx A_c \cdot t$ (subtracting mesh volume, which is negligible).

**Step 5: Substituting** into the SSR definition:

$$
\text{SSR} = \frac{N \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L}{A_c \cdot t}
$$

**Step 6: For a square mesh panel of side $L$ with mesh opening $s$,** number of wires per meter in each direction is approximately $L/s$. Thus $N \approx 2 \cdot L/s$ (wires in both directions), giving:

$$
\text{SSR} = \frac{2L \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L}{s \cdot A_c \cdot t} = \frac{2\pi d L^{2}}{s A_c t}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This formula allows direct computation of SSR from simple geometric measurements (mesh opening, wire diameter, panel dimensions), which is critical for quality control at rehabilitation sites.

## 3.2 Derivation: First-Crack Tensile Strength

**Step 1: The first-crack strength** of ferrocement $f_{tk}$ exceeds plain mortar $f_{t,m}$ due to mesh contribution. From equilibrium at first crack:

$$
f_{tk} = f_{t,m} + \sigma_{s,cr}
$$

**Step 2: At first crack,** strain compatibility requires strain in mesh equals strain in mortar:

$$
\varepsilon_{s,cr} = \varepsilon_{m,cr} = \frac{f_{t,m}}{E_m}
$$

**Step 3: Stress in mesh at this strain:**

$$
\sigma_{s,cr} = E_s \cdot \varepsilon_{s,cr} = E_s \cdot \frac{f_{t,m}}{E_m} = n \cdot f_{t,m}
$$

**Step 4: Express mesh stress in terms of volume fraction.** If $V_f$ = volume fraction of reinforcement (mesh), then per unit area:

$$
\sigma_{s,cr} \cdot V_f = n \cdot f_{t,m} \cdot V_f
$$

**Step 5: Combining** gives the final expression:

$$
f_{tk} = f_{t,m} \cdot (1 + n \cdot V_f)
$$

Since $V_f$ is small but $n \approx 7$ to 8, the second term is significant.

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why even a 2% volume fraction of mesh can double the tensile strength of a mortar panel.

## 3.3 Derivation: Flexural Strength of Ferrocement Laminate Jacketing

**Step 1: Consider a rectangular ferrocement laminate** of width $b$, thickness $t$, with $N$ layers of mesh at spacing $s_v$ from the neutral axis.

**Step 2: Assume transformed section method.** Modular ratio $m = E_s / E_m$. Transform mesh area into equivalent mortar area:

$$
A_{tr} = A_m + (m - 1) \cdot A_s \approx m \cdot A_s \quad (\text{when } A_m \gg A_s)
$$

**Step 3: Moment of inertia of transformed section:**

$$
I_{tr} = \frac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12} + (m - 1) \cdot A_s \cdot y^{2}
$$

**Step 4: For first-crack moment,** the extreme fibre tensile stress in mortar reaches $f_{r,f}$ (modulus of rupture of ferrocement):

$$
M_{cr} = \frac{f_{r,f} \cdot I_{tr}}{y_t}
$$

where $y_t = t/2$ is distance to extreme tension fibre.

**Step 5: Substituting** $y_t = t/2$:

$$
M_{cr} = \frac{f_{r,f} \cdot I_{tr}}{t/2} = \frac{2 f_{r,f} \cdot I_{tr}}{t}
$$

**Step 6: For ultimate moment** (mesh yields, mortar crushes), using equivalent stress block:

$$
M_u = A_s \cdot f_y \cdot (d - 0.4x) \quad \text{where } x = \frac{A_s \cdot f_y}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck,f} \cdot b}
$$

> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always distinguish first-crack moment $M_{cr}$ from ultimate moment $M_u$. The ratio $M_u / M_{cr}$ for ferrocement is typically **2.0 to 3.0**, indicating high reserve strength beyond first crack — a hallmark of ductile composite behavior.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A ferrocement overlay of **40 mm thickness** is applied on a damaged slab. It contains **4 layers** of welded wire mesh (wire diameter $d = 1.0$ mm, mesh opening $s = 12$ mm). Mortar cube strength $f_{ck,m} = 40$ MPa, $E_s = 2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $E_m = 2.5 \times 10^{4}$ MPa. Calculate:
(a) Specific Surface Reinforcement (SSR)
(b) First-crack tensile strength $f_{tk}$
(c) Ultimate tensile capacity per meter width

**Solution:**

**(a) SSR Computation:**

Number of wires per direction per meter = $1000/s = 1000/12 = 83.33$ wires.
Total wires per meter² (both directions) per layer = $2 \times 83.33 = 166.67$ wires.
For 4 layers: $N = 4 \times 166.67 = 666.67$ wires.
Length per wire = 1000 mm. So $L = 1000$ mm.

$$
S_r = N \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L = 666.67 \times \pi \times 1.0 \times 1000 \text{ mm²}
$$

$$
S_r \approx 666.67 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 = 2.094 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm²}
$$

Mortar volume per m² = $1000 \times 40 = 40000$ mm³ (per unit thickness 40 mm).
Volume of mortar per m² = $1000 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{7}$ mm³.

$$
\text{SSR} = \frac{S_r}{V_m} = \frac{2.094 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 10^{7}} = 0.0524 \text{ mm}^{-1} = 0.524 \text{ cm}^{2}/\text{cm}^{3}
$$

This is **above the ACI 549 threshold of 0.5 cm²/cm³** — confirming it qualifies as true ferrocement.

**(b) First-Crack Tensile Strength:**

Mortar tensile strength ≈ $0.5 \sqrt{f_{ck,m}} = 0.5 \times \sqrt{40} = 3.16$ MPa.
Modular ratio $n = E_s / E_m = 200000 / 25000 = 8$.
Volume fraction of mesh $V_f \approx (N \cdot \pi \cdot d^{2}/4) / (A_c) = (666.67 \times \pi \times 0.25) / (1000 \times 40) = 0.0131$ (≈ 1.31%).

$$
f_{tk} = f_{t,m} \cdot (1 + n \cdot V_f) = 3.16 \times (1 + 8 \times 0.0131)
$$

$$
f_{tk} = 3.16 \times (1 + 0.1048) = 3.16 \times 1.1048 \approx 3.49 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(c) Ultimate Tensile Capacity:**

Cross-sectional area of mesh per m width:
$A_s = N \cdot \pi \cdot d^{2} / 4 = 666.67 \times \pi \times 0.25 = 523.6 \text{ mm}^{2}/\text{m}$.
Assume mesh yield strength $f_y = 250$ MPa.

$$
P_{ult} = A_s \cdot f_y = 523.6 \times 250 = 130,900 \text{ N/m} \approx 130.9 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> **Verification of Design:** This capacity exceeds typical slab shear demands, confirming suitability for strengthening.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]
**Define ferrocement. List its four essential components.**

**Model Answer:**
Ferrocement is a composite material made of cement-sand mortar reinforced with closely spaced wire mesh layers and optionally skeletal steel, characterized by high specific surface reinforcement (SSR > 0.5 cm²/cm³).

Four essential components are:
1. **Cement-sand mortar matrix** (binder)
2. **Steel wire mesh** (welded or woven — primary reinforcement)
3. **Skeletal steel bars** (optional — secondary reinforcement)
4. **Water and admixtures** (workability and durability enhancers)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between ferrocement and reinforced cement concrete (RCC) in terms of reinforcement type and specific surface reinforcement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ferrocement | RCC |
|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement form | Closely spaced fine wire mesh | Discrete large-diameter rebars |
| Wire/rebar diameter | 0.5 mm to 1.5 mm | 6 mm to 32 mm |
| Specific Surface Reinforcement | 0.5 to 4.0 cm²/cm³ | ≈ 0.05 cm²/cm³ |
| Cover required | 1.5 mm to 3 mm | 20 mm to 50 mm |
| Section thickness | 10 mm to 50 mm | > 100 mm typically |
| Crack pattern | Multiple fine cracks | Few wide cracks |
| Tensile strength | 6 to 12 MPa | 2 to 4 MPa |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **manufacturing process of ferrocement** for rehabilitation of an RCC beam. Discuss the role of **specific surface reinforcement (SSR)** in determining the structural behavior. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ferrocement jacketing is provided to a damaged RCC column of size 230 mm × 450 mm. The jacket thickness is **30 mm** on all four sides. The ferrocement contains **6 layers of woven wire mesh** (wire diameter $d = 0.8$ mm, mesh opening $s = 10$ mm). Mortar compressive strength $f_{ck,m} = 35$ MPa; $E_s = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $E_m = 2.6 \times 10^{4}$ MPa. Calculate: **(i)** SSR, **(ii)** First-crack tensile strength, **(iii)** Ultimate tensile capacity per meter height. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Manufacturing Process for Ferrocement Beam Jacketing:**

1. **Surface preparation:** Remove loose concrete, expose sound substrate by chiseling/ sandblasting. Clean with wire brush and water jet. Allow SSD (saturated surface dry) condition.
2. **Application of bonding agent:** Brush epoxy or polymer-modified slurry on substrate for adhesion between old concrete and ferrocement.
3. **Mesh cutting and fixing:** Cut welded/woven mesh to required shape. Fix first layer using nails or anchors at 100–150 mm spacing.
4. **Sequential layering:** Apply 5–8 mm mortar layer; place next mesh; repeat. Maintain minimum 1.5 mm cover over outermost mesh.
5. **Curing:** Wet hessian / water spray for 14 days minimum; 28 days preferred.
6. **Quality checks:** Hammer tapping, cover meter, UPV testing.

**Role of SSR:** SSR (≥ 0.5 cm²/cm³) ensures dense, distributed reinforcement that arrests micro-cracks. Higher SSR → finer crack widths, higher tensile strength, better ductility, and improved impact resistance. SSR < 0.5 behaves like ordinary RCC with localized cracking.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) SSR Calculation:**

Per m² per layer, wires in each direction per m = $1000/s = 1000/10 = 100$ wires.
Wires per layer (both directions) per m² = $2 \times 100 = 200$.
For 6 layers: $N = 6 \times 200 = 1200$ wires per m².
$L = 1000$ mm.

$$
S_r = 1200 \times \pi \times 0.8 \times 1000 = 1200 \times 3.1416 \times 800 = 3.016 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm²}
$$

Per m² area, mortar volume for 30 mm thickness = $10^{6} \times 30 = 3 \times 10^{7}$ mm³.

$$
\text{SSR} = \frac{3.016 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{7}} = 0.1005 \text{ mm}^{-1} = 1.005 \text{ cm}^{2}/\text{cm}^{3}
$$

**SSR > 0.5 confirms true ferrocement.**

**(ii) First-Crack Tensile Strength:**

Mortar tensile strength $f_{t,m} = 0.5 \sqrt{35} = 2.958$ MPa.
Volume fraction of mesh: $V_f = (1200 \times \pi \times 0.32) / (10^{6} \times 30) = 0.00402$ (≈ 0.4%).
Modular ratio $n = 210000 / 26000 = 8.08$.

$$
f_{tk} = f_{t,m} \cdot (1 + n \cdot V_f) = 2.958 \times (1 + 8.08 \times 0.00402)
$$

$$
f_{tk} = 2.958 \times (1 + 0.0325) = 2.958 \times 1.0325 = 3.054 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Ultimate Tensile Capacity:**

$A_s = 1200 \times \pi \times (0.8)^{2}/4 = 1200 \times 0.5027 = 603.2$ mm²/m.

Assume $f_y = 250$ MPa for mesh wire.

$$
P_{ult} = 603.2 \times 250 = 150,800 \text{ N/m} = 150.8 \text{ kN/m}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanical properties of ferrocement** with reference to its tensile, compressive, flexural, and impact behavior. Why is ferrocement preferred for **seismic retrofitting**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare ferrocement with GFRP (Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer) laminates as **strengthening materials** for damaged structural members. List two applications of ferrocement in repair and rehabilitation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Mechanical Properties of Ferrocement:**

1. **Tensile strength:** Ranges from 6 to 12 MPa, about 3 to 6 times that of plain mortar. Strain capacity at failure is 0.5% to 1%, due to multi-cracking.

2. **Compressive strength:** Approximately 0.55 × mortar cube strength (e.g., 20–35 MPa). Dominated by mortar matrix; mesh has minimal compressive contribution.

3. **Flexural strength:** Modulus of rupture is 12 to 25 MPa. The high $M_u / M_{cr}$ ratio (2.0 to 3.0) indicates significant post-cracking reserve strength.

4. **Impact resistance:** 10 to 50 times that of plain mortar. Fracture energy is high due to crack-bridging by fine wire mesh. Important for blast/impact loading.

5. **Fatigue resistance:** Superior to RCC due to controlled crack widths; wire mesh arrests fatigue cracks effectively.

**Why Preferred for Seismic Retrofitting:**
- High ductility and energy absorption capacity.
- Multiple fine cracking instead of catastrophic single cracks.
- Lightweight (low self-weight) — minimal added seismic mass.
- Easily moldable around complex column/beam geometries.
- Compatible deformation with parent concrete (similar modulus).
- Cost-effective and field-friendly application.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Comparison: Ferrocement vs GFRP Laminates**

| Property | Ferrocement | GFRP Laminate |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | 6 to 12 MPa | 200 to 500 MPa |
| Thickness | 25 to 50 mm | 1 to 5 mm |
| Weight addition | Moderate | Very low |
| Bond to substrate | Mechanical + chemical | Adhesive only (epoxy) |
| Fire resistance | Good (inorganic) | Poor (polymer matrix degrades) |
| Cost | Low (local materials) | High (imported fibers/resin) |
| Durability | 30+ years | 15 to 25 years |
| Ductility | High (ductile) | Low (brittle failure) |
| UV resistance | Excellent | Moderate (needs coating) |
| Field skill required | Low to moderate | High (specialist applicator) |

**Two Applications of Ferrocement in Repair & Rehabilitation:**

1. **Column Jacketing:** Wrapping damaged RC columns with ferrocement layers to enhance axial load capacity and confinement. Especially effective for seismic strengthening.

2. **Water Tank Rehabilitation:** Ferrocement lining of cracked/deteriorated RCC water tanks provides waterproof, crack-resistant, durable surface.

*(Other valid answers: Beam strengthening, slab overlay, retaining wall repair, dome/watertight roof restoration, pipe rehabilitation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **confuse SSR (cm²/cm³) units** — write SSR value with explicit unit notation in every step.
> - **Forgetting modular ratio transformation** in flexural calculations — leads to non-conservative results.
> - **Not mentioning ACI 549 / IS 13356** reference standard when asked about ferrocement properties.
> - Confusing **first-crack strength ($M_{cr}$)** with **ultimate strength ($M_u$)** — examiners allocate 2 marks for clearly distinguishing these.
> - **Sketching** in jacketing questions without showing mesh layering, cover thickness, and anchoring details.
> - **Numerical:** Wrong value of wire area per layer (forgetting factor of 2 for both mesh directions). This single error causes 50% mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Ferrocement (Module 3):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Composite of cement-sand mortar + closely spaced wire mesh; ACI 549 definition; characterized by high SSR.
- ✅ **SSR Criterion:** True ferrocement when SSR ≥ **0.5 cm²/cm³**; typical range 0.5 to 4.0.
- ✅ **Mortar:** Rich mix, 1:1.5 to 1:3 cement:sand; w/c = 0.35–0.50; fine sand passing 600 µm.
- ✅ **Mesh types:** Woven (chicken mesh, hexagonal), Welded (square grid), Expanded metal lath.
- ✅ **Wire diameter:** 0.5 mm to 1.5 mm; minimum cover **1.5 mm** (mesh) / **3 mm** (skeletal).
- ✅ **Section thickness:** 10 mm to 50 mm — much thinner than RCC.
- ✅ **Tensile strength formula:** $f_{tk} = f_{t,m}(1 + n \cdot V_f)$
- ✅ **Compressive strength:** $f_{ck,f} \approx 0.55 \cdot f_{ck,m}$
- ✅ **Crack width control:** Ferrocement restricts crack widths below 50 µm due to fine mesh.
- ✅ **Ductility:** $M_u / M_{cr} = 2.0$ to $3.0$; high fracture energy.
- ✅ **Impact resistance:** 10 to 50 × plain mortar; suitable for blast/impact retrofits.
- ✅ **Modular ratio:** $n = E_s / E_m \approx 7$ to $8$ (for typical mortars).
- ✅ **Applications:** Column jacketing, beam strengthening, slab overlay, water tank lining, retaining wall repair, dome restoration.
- ✅ **Advantages over RCC:** Thin sections, high tensile strength, crack control, formability, economy, ductility.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Labor-intensive mesh fixing, requires skilled plastering, time-consuming multi-layer application.
- ✅ **Standards:** ACI 549 (US); IS 13356 (India, ferrocements guidelines); IS 456 for parent concrete.
- ✅ **Quality control:** Hammer test, UPV test, cover meter, cube strength test on mortar.
- ✅ **Why preferred for seismic retrofit:** Ductility, energy absorption, light weight, compatibility, multi-crack behavior.
- ✅ **Comparison point with GFRP:** Ferrocement = thicker, ductile, low cost, fire-resistant; GFRP = thinner, brittle, high strength, short duration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concrete Chemicals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concrete Chemicals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concrete Chemicals** are engineered chemical formulations (organic or inorganic) that are either incorporated into a fresh concrete mix or applied to hardened concrete to **modify, enhance, or restore** one or more properties of the material — including workability, setting time, strength development, durability, impermeability, and bond characteristics.

In the context of **Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures (PECET634)**, concrete chemicals represent the second-largest material category after repair mortars, and are governed primarily by the following standards:

- **IS 9103 : 1999** – Specification for Concrete Admixtures
- **IS 2645 : 2003** – Integral Waterproofing Compounds for Concrete and Mortar
- **ASTM C494 / C494M** – Standard Specification for Chemical Admixtures
- **ASTM C260 / C260M** – Air-Entraining Admixtures
- **ACI 212.3R-16** – Report on Chemical Admixtures for Concrete
- **BS EN 934-2** – Admixtures for Concrete, Mortar and Grout

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Repair & Rehabilitation:** When an existing structural element is distressed, replacing it entirely is often economically and structurally infeasible. Concrete chemicals allow the engineer to **"re-engineer"** the properties of the patch material or the substrate interface to match performance demands, restore lost durability, and extend service life by 20–50 years.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **concrete mix as a baking recipe** (cement + water + sand + aggregates). Now imagine you are a chef who has limited time, harsh oven conditions, or customers with specific dietary needs:

- A **plasticizer** is like **cooking oil** — it makes the batter flow better without adding extra water (extra water would weaken the cake, just as extra water weakens concrete).
- A **superplasticizer** is like a **premium non-stick spray** — it allows a very stiff, strong mix to flow like a liquid, enabling self-compacting concrete that fills every corner without vibration.
- An **accelerator** is like **yeast in a warm spot** — it speeds up the "rise" (hardening) of concrete, which is critical in cold-weather repairs or emergency patches.
- A **retarder** is like **keeping the dough in the fridge** — it slows down setting, essential when concrete must travel long distances or be pumped for hours.
- An **air-entrainer** is like **whisking egg whites** — it traps millions of microscopic, stable air bubbles that act as pressure-relief valves when water inside the concrete freezes and expands.
- A **bonding agent** is like **wood glue between two planks** — it welds new repair concrete to old substrate so they act as one monolithic element.
- A **corrosion inhibitor** is like **rust-proofing paint on steel** — it shields embedded rebar from chloride and carbonation attack.
- A **waterproofer** is like **wax on a car** — it plugs capillary pores and makes concrete water-repellent.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Plasticizer = lubricant, Accelerator = catalyst, Retarder = coolant, Air-entrainer = shock-absorber, Bonding agent = glue, Inhibitor = armour, Waterproofer = shield."*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range | Reference Code |
|---|---|---|
| Plasticizer dosage | **0.1 % – 0.4 %** by mass of cement | IS 9103 |
| Superplasticizer dosage | **0.5 % – 2.5 %** by mass of cement | IS 9103 |
| Water reduction (plasticizer) | **5 % – 15 %** | ASTM C494 Type A |
| Water reduction (superplasticizer) | **12 % – 30 %** | ASTM C494 Type F |
| Air-entrainer dosage | **0.02 % – 0.10 %** by mass of cement | IS 9103 |
| Retarder setting extension | **+30 min to +3 hours** from control | ASTM C494 Type B/D |
| Accelerator strength gain (24 h) | **+30 % to +150 %** over control | ASTM C494 Type C |
| Concrete density | **2200 – 2500 kg/m³** | IS 456 |
| Standard water content (without admixtures) | **186 kg/m³** for 75 mm slump | IS 10262 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Concrete Chemicals

Concrete chemicals used in repair and rehabilitation can be classified along **three independent axes** — by *function*, by *chemistry*, and by *stage of application*.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Function (Most Important for KTU)

| Category | Primary Effect | Common Use in Repair |
|---|---|---|
| **Plasticizers (WRAs)** | Increase workability without extra water | Patch repairs in congested reinforcement |
| **Superplasticizers (HRWRs)** | High-range workability boost | Self-compacting repair concrete, grouting |
| **Accelerators** | Reduce setting time & early strength gain | Cold-weather repair, emergency patches |
| **Retarders** | Delay setting & extend workability life | Long-distance pumped concrete, hot weather |
| **Air-entraining agents (AEAs)** | Stabilize microscopic air voids | Freeze-thaw environments, marine repairs |
| **Corrosion inhibitors** | Protect embedded steel | Chloride-laden repair zones |
| **Shrinkage reducers** | Reduce drying shrinkage cracking | Large-area patches, slab repairs |
| **Viscosity modifiers (VMA)** | Prevent segregation & bleeding | Underwater concrete, tremie repairs |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Chemistry

| Chemical Family | Example Compounds | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Lignosulfonates** | Calcium/sodium lignosulfonate | Electrostatic repulsion on cement grains |
| **Sulfonated Naphthalene Formaldehyde (SNF)** | Condensed naphthalene sulfonate | Surface tension reduction + repulsion |
| **Sulfonated Melamine Formaldehyde (SMF)** | Melamine-based polycondensate | Disperses cement flocs |
| **Polycarboxylate Ethers (PCE)** | Methoxy-PEG-grafted polyacrylate | **Steric hindrance** (modern, dominant) |
| **Calcium chloride (CaCl₂)** | Anhydrous CaCl₂ | Accelerates C₃S hydration |
| **Non-chloride accelerators** | Calcium nitrate, sodium thiocyanate, triethanolamine | Promotes C-S-H nucleation |
| **Sugars & sugar acids** | Glucose, sucrose, gluconic acid | Poison C₃S hydration reactions |
| **Hydroxylated polymers** | Hydroxyethyl cellulose, VMA | Thickens mix water, prevents segregation |
| **Silicates / Silanes / Siloxanes** | Sodium silicate, isooctyltriethoxysilane | Pore-blocking hydrophobic coating |
| **Crystalline admixtures** | Calcium silicate hydrates, active silica | Reactive pore-blocking (self-healing type) |
| **Epoxy resins** | Bisphenol-A diglycidyl ether (DGEBA) | Polymer-network binder, structural bond |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | MDI/TDI-based prepolymers | Flexible sealing, crack injection |
| **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)** | Latex emulsion | Polymer-cement composite, bonding |
| **Acrylic emulsions** | Poly-methyl methacrylate (PMMA) | Surface sealing, dust-proofing |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Stage of Application

1. **Pre-mix admixtures** → added at the batching plant before mixing.
2. **Post-mix admixtures** → added on-site just before placement (superplasticizers are typical).
3. **Surface-applied chemicals** → applied to hardened concrete (silane, siloxane, sodium silicate, lithium silicate).
4. **Injection chemicals** → injected into cracks or voids (epoxy, PU, acrylic grouts).
5. **Bonding bridge chemicals** → applied at the interface (SBR latex, epoxy bonding agent, acrylic bonding admixture).

## 2.2 Mechanism of Action — The "Why" Behind Each Type

### 2.2.1 Plasticizers and Superplasticizers
Fresh cement grains carry **positive and negative charges** at broken edges, causing them to flocculate and trap mix water. Plasticizer molecules **adsorb onto cement grain surfaces**, with their anionic heads attaching to the cement and their long organic tails repelling neighbouring grains (electrostatic repulsion for SNF/SMF, or steric hindrance for PCE). This **deflocculation releases trapped water**, increasing slump by 100–200 mm without adding water.

### 2.2.2 Accelerators
- **CaCl₂** lowers the activation energy of C₃S (tricalcium silicate) hydration, forming more C-S-H gel in early hours.
- **Triethanolamine (TEA)** accelerates the aluminate (C₃A) reaction, producing early ettringite.
- These mechanisms are critical in repair when the patch must develop 10–20 MPa within 24 hours for formwork removal or load transfer.

### 2.2.3 Retarders
Sugars and hydroxycarboxylic acids form **calcium-sugar complexes on cement grain surfaces**, physically blocking water access to C₃S. Hydration pauses until the complex dissolves (typically 1–3 hours later).

### 2.2.4 Air-Entraining Agents
AEAs are **surfactants** (e.g., vinsol resin, synthetic detergents, fatty-acid salts) that lower the surface tension of mix water. During mixing, they stabilize air bubbles in the size range **10 – 300 μm** with proper spacing factor (**< 200 μm** per IS 10262) to provide frost resistance.

### 2.2.5 Corrosion Inhibitors
- **Anodic inhibitors** (sodium nitrite, calcium nitrite) form a passive Fe₂O₃ film on steel.
- **Cathodic inhibitors** (zinc oxide, amines) block oxygen reduction.
- **Mixed inhibitors** (amino-alcohols, phosphonates) work at both sites.

### 2.2.6 Crystalline Waterproofers
These contain **reactive silica + calcium oxide + catalysts**. When water contacts them, they react with free Ca(OH)₂ in concrete to form **needle-like calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) crystals** that fill capillary pores permanently (often self-sealing of microcracks up to **0.4 mm**).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The following formulas are the minimum required for any KTU numerical or short-answer question on concrete chemicals. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Dosage\ (\%) = \dfrac{M_{adm}}{M_{cem}} \times 100$ | $M_{adm}$ mass of admixture, $M_{cem}$ mass of cement | % | Computing actual mass of admixture needed |
| 2 | $(w/c)_{new} = (w/c)_{old} \cdot (1 - R)$ | $R$ = water reduction fraction (e.g., 0.20 for 20 %) | dimensionless | New mix design after superplasticizer |
| 3 | $M_{water}^{reduced} = M_{water}^{orig} \cdot R$ | Reduced water mass | kg/m³ | Saving in mix water |
| 4 | $Abrams\ Law:\ f_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{(w/c)}}$ | $A$, $B$ empirical constants | MPa | Strength prediction (typically $A \approx 100$, $B \approx 7$) |
| 5 | $f_c \propto \left(\dfrac{c}{w}\right)^{n}$ | $c/w$ cement-water ratio, $n \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ | MPa | Relative strength change |
| 6 | $S_2 / S_1 = \left[\dfrac{(w/c)_1}{(w/c)_2}\right]^{n}$ | $S_1$, $S_2$ strengths before/after | dimensionless | Strength gain from water reduction |
| 7 | $A_{ent} = k \cdot D^{a}$ | $A_{ent}$ entrained air %, $D$ dosage, $k$, $a$ empirical | % | Air-entrainer performance curve |
| 8 | $V_{adm} = \dfrac{M_{cem} \cdot D}{100 \cdot \rho_{adm}}$ | $V_{adm}$ volume of liquid admixture, $\rho_{adm}$ density | L or m³ | Converting % dosage to litres on site |
| 9 | $S_{factor} = \dfrac{1}{N_{bubbles}}$ per unit length | Spacing factor for AEA | mm | Freeze-thaw durability check ($\leq 200\ \mu m$) |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{set} = K \cdot \log_{10}(1 + D)$ | $\Delta t_{set}$ retardation, $D$ retarder dose, $K$ constant | hours | Setting time extension by retarder |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear containment repairs** → use crystalline admixtures + corrosion inhibitors for 100-year service life.
- **Bridge deck rehabilitation** → use low-shrinkage concrete with PCE superplasticizer + shrinkage-reducing admixture (SRA) to minimise cracking.
- **Marine jetty repair** → use air-entrainers + calcium nitrite inhibitor + silane surface treatment.
- **Underwater pile repair** → use anti-washout admixtures (viscosity modifiers) + accelerators for tremie concrete.
- **Heritage building restoration** → use lime-compatible pozzolanic-chemical hybrids to match original substrate.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Numerical problems on concrete chemicals are **derivation-heavy** and require students to show the transition from % dosage to actual mass, then to revised water-cement ratio, and finally to strength prediction. Skipping any step leads to **3–4 mark deductions**. The worked examples below illustrate the *exact* valuation pattern.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Percentage Dosage to Volume of Liquid Admixture

### Statement
A repair mix uses **400 kg/m³** of cement. A superplasticizer is dosed at **1.2 % by mass of cement**. The admixture is supplied as a liquid with **density 1.10 kg/L**. Compute (a) the mass of superplasticizer per m³, and (b) the volume to be added per m³ of concrete.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the dosage formula.**

$$Dosage\ (\%) = \frac{M_{adm}}{M_{cem}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Solve for mass of admixture.**

$$M_{adm} = \frac{Dosage \times M_{cem}}{100}$$

$$M_{adm} = \frac{1.2 \times 400}{100}$$

$$M_{adm} = 4.8\ kg/m^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to volume using density.**

$$V_{adm} = \frac{M_{adm}}{\rho_{adm}}$$

$$V_{adm} = \frac{4.8}{1.10}$$

$$V_{adm} \approx 4.36\ L/m^3$$

**Answer:** (a) **4.8 kg/m³**, (b) **≈ 4.36 L/m³**.

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert liquid admixtures from mass to volume before measuring in the field. The density correction is **always** required for liquids (gases, oils, slurries).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Revised Water-Cement Ratio After Superplasticizer Addition

### Statement
A repair concrete has an original mix of **Cement = 400 kg/m³**, **Water = 200 kg/m³**, giving **w/c = 0.50** and **28-day compressive strength ≈ 35 MPa**. A superplasticizer is introduced, giving a **20 % water reduction** at constant workability. Compute the new w/c, the new water content, and the estimated new compressive strength using the simplified Abrams relation with **n = 2.5**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute original water content and w/c.**

$$(w/c)_{old} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.50$$

**Step 2 — Compute water reduction.**

$$M_{water}^{reduced} = M_{water}^{orig} \times R = 200 \times 0.20 = 40\ kg/m^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute new water content.**

$$M_{water}^{new} = 200 - 40 = 160\ kg/m^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute new w/c.**

$$(w/c)_{new} = \frac{160}{400} = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Compute strength ratio using Abrams-like power law.**

$$\frac{S_2}{S_1} = \left[\frac{(w/c)_1}{(w/c)_2}\right]^{n} = \left[\frac{0.50}{0.40}\right]^{2.5}$$

$$\frac{S_2}{S_1} = (1.25)^{2.5}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $(1.25)^{2.5}$.**

$$(1.25)^{2.5} = (1.25)^2 \cdot (1.25)^{0.5}$$

$$(1.25)^2 = 1.5625$$

$$(1.25)^{0.5} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.1180$$

$$(1.25)^{2.5} = 1.5625 \times 1.1180 \approx 1.747$$

**Step 7 — Compute new strength.**

$$S_2 = S_1 \times 1.747 = 35 \times 1.747 \approx 61.1\ MPa$$

**Answer:** New w/c = **0.40**, New water content = **160 kg/m³**, New strength ≈ **61 MPa** (an increase of **~ 75 %**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Air-Entrainer on Freeze-Thaw Durability

### Statement
An air-entraining agent is dosed at **0.05 %** by mass of cement. Cement content is **380 kg/m³**. The empirical constants for the agent are **k = 12.5** and **a = 0.6**. Compute the entrained air percentage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the empirical equation.**

$$A_{ent} = k \cdot D^{a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$A_{ent} = 12.5 \times (0.05)^{0.6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(0.05)^{0.6}$.**

$$(0.05)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln(0.05)}$$

$$\ln(0.05) = \ln(5 \times 10^{-2}) = \ln 5 - 2\ln 10 = 1.6094 - 4.6052 = -2.9957$$

$$0.6 \times (-2.9957) = -1.7974$$

$$e^{-1.7974} \approx 0.1657$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$A_{ent} = 12.5 \times 0.1657 \approx 2.07\ \%$$

**Answer:** Entrained air ≈ **2.07 %** by volume of concrete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For severe exposure (coastal Kerala climate, NE monsoon), IS 456 recommends **4 – 6 % total air content** for 20 mm aggregate. The remaining ~ 2 % must come from entrapped air plus additional dosage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Retarder Setting Time Extension

### Statement
A retarder with constant $K = 4.5$ hours is dosed at **0.3 %** by mass of cement. Compute the additional setting time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the retardation equation.**

$$\Delta t_{set} = K \cdot \log_{10}(1 + D)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\Delta t_{set} = 4.5 \times \log_{10}(1 + 0.3)$$

$$\Delta t_{set} = 4.5 \times \log_{10}(1.3)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\log_{10}(1.3)$.**

$$\log_{10}(1.3) \approx 0.1139$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$\Delta t_{set} = 4.5 \times 0.1139 \approx 0.513\ hours \approx 31\ minutes$$

**Answer:** Setting delayed by **~ 31 minutes**.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Choice of Admixture for Repair Scenarios

| Repair Scenario | Recommended Chemical Family | Dosage Range | Justification (KTU Logic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold-weather patch (T < 10 °C) | Non-chloride accelerator (Ca(NO₃)₂, TEA blend) | 1 % – 3 % | Avoids rebar corrosion; achieves 10 MPa in 24 h |
| Hot-weather pour (T > 35 °C, Kerala summer) | Retarder (sugar-acid or lignosulfonate) | 0.1 % – 0.3 % | Prevents cold-joint in deep patches |
| Congested rebar (column jacketing) | PCE superplasticizer | 0.8 % – 1.5 % | Achieves 200 mm slump at w/c ≈ 0.40 |
| Marine jetty pile repair | Air-entrainer + Ca(NO₂)₂ inhibitor + silane topcoat | 0.05 % + 2 % + surface | Freeze-thaw + chloride resistance |
| Crack injection (0.1 – 3 mm wide) | Low-viscosity epoxy resin (DGEBA + amine hardener) | 100 % binder | Structural restoration, < 100 cP viscosity |
| Damp substrate patch (rising moisture) | SBR-latex modified cement slurry (bonding bridge) | 10 % – 15 % SBR solids | Water-tolerant bond layer |
| Heritage lime-based repair | Potassium silicate + metakaolin | Surface slurry | Compatible modulus with old lime mortar |
| Mass concrete underpinning | Low-heat OPC + retarder + shrinkage reducer (SRA) | 0.2 % + 1 % | Reduces thermal & drying shrinkage cracking |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between plasticizers and superplasticizers with respect to chemical composition, dosage, water-reduction capacity, and typical slump gain.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

| Parameter | Plasticizer (WRA) | Superplasticizer (HRWR) |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical family | Lignosulfonates, hydroxylated polymers | SNF, SMF, PCE |
| Dosage range (% cement mass) | 0.1 – 0.4 | 0.5 – 2.5 |
| Water reduction | 5 – 15 % | 12 – 30 % |
| Slump gain | 75 – 100 mm | 150 – 200 mm |
| ASTM C494 type | Type A / Type D | Type F / Type G |
| Mechanism | Electrostatic repulsion | Steric hindrance (PCE) |
| Slump retention | Moderate | High (especially PCE) |
| Cost per kg | Low | Moderate to high |
| Typical repair use | Patch repairs, simple pours | SCC, congested rebar |
| Limitation | Slump loss within 30 min | Sensitive to over-dosing (segregation) |

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Explain the mechanism of action of calcium-nitrite-based corrosion inhibitors in reinforced concrete repair.**

**Model Answer (≈ 8 lines):**

Calcium nitrite (Ca(NO₂)₂) is an **anodic corrosion inhibitor**. When chloride ions (Cl⁻) penetrate the concrete cover and reach the steel surface, they break down the passive iron-oxide (Fe₂O₃) film and initiate pitting corrosion. The nitrite ion (NO₂⁻) acts as an **oxidising agent** that oxidises ferrous (Fe²⁺) ions to ferric (Fe³⁺) ions, thereby **re-forming and stabilising the passive film** on the rebar surface. The required **molar ratio [NO₂⁻] : [Cl⁻]** at the steel depth must be at least **0.6 : 1.0** to provide effective inhibition. In repair practice, the inhibitor is either dosed into the repair concrete (typically **2 % – 4 % by mass of cement**) or applied as a surface impregnant on the host concrete to **migrate** toward the steel (a process called *electrochemical migratory inhibitor* technology). This makes calcium nitrite a **non-chloride, anodic, passivating inhibitor** widely used in marine and de-icing-salt environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** for every 14-mark Part B question. Both alternatives below are **examination-ready** with full sub-part solutions.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Classify the various **concrete admixtures** used in repair and rehabilitation of structures. Discuss the **mechanism of action** and **specific applications** of the following:
   - (i) Plasticizers and Superplasticizers (4 marks)
   - (ii) Accelerators and Retarders (3 marks)

**(b)** A repair concrete mix has cement content **350 kg/m³** and water content **175 kg/m³**. A superplasticizer providing **25 %** water reduction is used. Calculate:
   - (i) The new water content and new water-cement ratio. (2 marks)
   - (ii) The new 28-day compressive strength, given the original strength is **30 MPa** and using Abrams' power law with exponent **n = 2.0**. (3 marks)
   - (iii) The mass and volume (litres) of superplasticizer required per m³, if dosed at **1.5 % by mass of cement** with admixture density **1.12 kg/L**. (2 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Part (i) — Plasticizers & Superplasticizers (4 marks):**

Plasticizers (Water-Reducing Admixtures, WRAs) and Superplasticizers (High-Range Water Reducers, HRWRs) belong to the same functional class but differ in chemistry, dosage, and effect. **Plasticizers** (ASTM C494 Type A) are based on **lignosulfonates** and **hydroxylated polymers**, used at **0.1 % – 0.4 %** by cement mass, achieving **5 – 15 % water reduction** and a slump gain of 75 – 100 mm. **Superplasticizers** (ASTM C494 Type F/G) are based on **SNF, SMF, or PCE**, used at **0.5 % – 2.5 %** by cement mass, achieving **12 – 30 % water reduction** and a slump gain of 150 – 200 mm.

**Mechanism of action:** Fresh cement grains carry **electrostatic surface charges** that cause them to flocculate and trap mix water inside the flocs. Plasticizer/superplasticizer molecules have a **hydrophilic anionic head** that adsorbs onto the cement grain and a **long organic tail** that creates repulsion between grains. SNF and SMF work via **electrostatic repulsion** (charged tails repel each other), while PCE works via **steric hindrance** (long PEG side chains physically prevent grains from approaching). The deflocculation **releases trapped water**, which becomes available to lubricate the mix — increasing workability without adding water. In repair contexts, this enables **self-compacting concrete** that flows around dense reinforcement without vibration, and **high-strength repair patches** with very low w/c ratios (0.30 – 0.40).

**(a) Part (ii) — Accelerators & Retarders (3 marks):**

**Accelerators** speed up the setting and early strength development of cement. Traditional accelerators are **calcium chloride (CaCl₂)**, which lowers the activation energy of C₃S hydration, producing C-S-H gel faster. Modern **non-chloride accelerators (NCA)** such as **calcium nitrate, sodium thiocyanate, and triethanolamine** are preferred in reinforced concrete because Cl⁻ ions cause rebar corrosion. Accelerators are used in **cold-weather repair, emergency patches, shotcrete, and pre-cast elements** where early formwork removal or load transfer is needed. Typical dosage is **1 % – 3 % by cement mass**, and 24-hour strength can be 30 – 150 % higher than control.

**Retarders** delay the setting of cement. They include **sugars, hydroxycarboxylic acids (gluconic, citric), and lignosulfonates**. Mechanism: retarder molecules form **calcium-sugar complexes** on the cement surface, physically blocking water access to C₃S, thus delaying hydration. Once the complex dissolves (1 – 3 hours), hydration resumes. Retarders are used in **hot-weather concreting, long-distance pumped concrete, and large mass concrete pours** to prevent cold joints. Typical dosage is **0.05 % – 0.3 %**, and setting time is extended by 30 minutes to several hours.

**(b) Part (i) — New water content and w/c (2 marks):**

Original w/c = 175 / 350 = **0.50**

Water reduction = 25 % of 175 = 175 × 0.25 = **43.75 kg/m³**

New water content = 175 − 43.75 = **131.25 kg/m³**

New w/c = 131.25 / 350 = **0.375**

**(b) Part (ii) — New 28-day compressive strength (3 marks):**

Using Abrams' power law:

$$\frac{S_2}{S_1} = \left[\frac{(w/c)_1}{(w/c)_2}\right]^{n} = \left[\frac{0.50}{0.375}\right]^{2.0}$$

$$S_2 = 30 \times (1.333)^{2.0} = 30 \times 1.778 = \mathbf{53.33\ MPa}$$

**(b) Part (iii) — Mass and volume of superplasticizer (2 marks):**

$$M_{adm} = \frac{1.5 \times 350}{100} = \mathbf{5.25\ kg/m^3}$$

$$V_{adm} = \frac{5.25}{1.12} = \mathbf{4.69\ L/m^3}$$

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the following concrete chemicals with respect to their **composition, working mechanism, and use in repair**:
   - (i) Air-entraining admixtures (3 marks)
   - (ii) Corrosion inhibitors (2 marks)
   - (iii) Crystalline waterproofing admixtures (2 marks)

**(b)** For a marine jetty repair, the following data is given:
   - Cement content = 380 kg/m³
   - Target entrained air = **5.0 %** by volume
   - Air-entrainer constants: **k = 14.2**, **a = 0.55**
   - Density of liquid admixture = 1.05 kg/L

Compute:
   - (i) The required dosage of air-entrainer (% by mass of cement). (2 marks)
   - (ii) The mass of air-entrainer per m³ of concrete. (2 marks)
   - (iii) The volume of air-entrainer per m³. (1 mark)
   - (iv) Comment on whether this dosage lies within the IS 9103 recommended range. (2 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Part (i) — Air-Entraining Admixtures (3 marks):**
