The stoichiometric mix requires the number of epoxy groups to equal the number of active amine hydrogens.

**Step 2 — Mass of Hardener per 100 g of Resin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{hardener} &= 100 \text{ g resin} \times \frac{AHEW}{EEW} \\
&= 100 \times \frac{146}{190} \\
&= 76.84 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mix Ratio by Mass:**
The mass ratio becomes:

$$
R_m = \frac{W_{resin}}{W_{hardener}} = \frac{100}{76.84} \approx 1.30 : 1
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A *100:77* mass ratio (or rounded to *100:80* commercially) ensures complete cross-linking. Deviation of $> \pm 10\%$ leads to unreacted species, reducing strength and chemical resistance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pot Life from Arrhenius Kinetics

**Given:** Epoxy hardener has activation energy $E_a = 60\ kJ/mol$, rate constant at $25^{\circ}C$ is $k_{25} = 0.02\ min^{-1}$. The pot life corresponds to 95% conversion.

**Step 1 — Time for 95% conversion (first-order approximation):**

$$
t_{pot} = -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{k} = \frac{2.996}{k} \approx \frac{3}{k}
$$

**Step 2 — At $25^{\circ}C$:**

$$
t_{pot,\, 25} = \frac{3}{0.02} = 150 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Adjusting for higher ambient temperature ($T = 35^{\circ}C = 308\ K$, reference $T_0 = 298\ K$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_{35}}{k_{25}} &= \exp\left[ \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T} \right) \right] \\
&= \exp\left[ \frac{60{,}000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{308} \right) \right] \\
&= \exp\left[ 7217 \times (3.3557 \times 10^{-3} - 3.2468 \times 10^{-3}) \right] \\
&= \exp\left[ 7217 \times 1.089 \times 10^{-4} \right] \\
&= \exp(0.786) \approx 2.195
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New pot life at $35^{\circ}C$:**

$$
t_{pot,\, 35} = \frac{150}{2.195} \approx 68.3 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A $10^{\circ}C$ rise *more than halves* the working time. Site batching must be planned for the *peak daytime temperature*, not the morning average.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Property Table

This is the most **examination-relevant** matrix. Memorize the qualitative rankings.

| Property | Epoxy | Polyurethane (PU) | Acrylic | Polyester |
|----------|:-----:|:----------------:|:-------:|:---------:|
| Viscosity | Medium | High → Low (variants) | Very Low | Medium |
| Tensile Strength | **Very High** | Medium | Low–Medium | Medium |
| Elongation / Flexibility | Low ($\approx 2$–$5\%$) | **Very High** ($\approx 100$–$600\%$) | Medium | Low |
| Chemical Resistance | **Excellent** (acids, alkalis) | Good (limited) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Water Resistance | Excellent (cured) | **Variable** (hydrophilic vs hydrophobic) | Good | Good |
| Cure Shrinkage | Very Low | Low (expands instead) | Moderate | High |
| Bond to Wet Concrete | **Excellent** (with primer) | **Excellent** (purpose-made) | Poor | Poor |
| Cost | **High** | High | Medium | **Low** |
| Typical Application | Structural crack | Active leak | Hairline crack | General sealing |

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Volume Calculation for Slab-Jacking with Expanding PU Grout

**Given:** A sunken warehouse floor slab of dimensions $10\ m \times 10\ m$ must be lifted by $\Delta h = 50\ mm$. A PU grout with expansion factor $f_e = 10$ is injected as a liquid. Calculate the volume of *liquid grout* required.

**Step 1 — Total volumetric uplift required:**

$$
V_{uplift} = L \times B \times \Delta h = 10 \times 10 \times 0.050 = 5.0\ m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Liquid grout volume after expansion:**

$$
V_{expanded} = V_{liquid} \cdot (1 + f_e) = V_{liquid} \cdot 11
$$

**Step 3 — Equating expanded volume to required uplift:**

$$
V_{liquid} = \frac{V_{uplift}}{1 + f_e} = \frac{5.0}{11} \approx 0.455\ m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of grout (assuming liquid density $\rho \approx 1100\ kg/m^3$):**

$$
M = V_{liquid} \times \rho = 0.455 \times 1100 \approx 500.5\ kg
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A mere **455 litres** of liquid PU grout can lift a 100 m² slab by 50 mm. This is why PU grouts dominate the *slab-jacking* market.

### 3.5 Quality Control Equations

| Test | Equation | Acceptance (IS 456 / ACI 546R) |
|------|----------|---------------------------------|
| **Slant Shear Bond (ASTM C882)** | $\tau_b = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{contact}}$ | $\ge 7\ MPa$ for structural repair |
| **Pull-Off Tensile (ASTM C1583)** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2}$ | $\ge 1.7\ MPa$ minimum, $\ge 3.5\ MPa$ for structural |
| **Compressive Strength (ASTM C579)** | $f_c' = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\ge 50\ MPa$ for epoxy mortar, $\ge 20\ MPa$ for PU |
| **Viscosity Cup Flow (ASTM D4212)** | $t_{flow} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot R^2}{8 \mu}$ | Site check before injection |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree of Polymer Grouts

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[POLYMER GROUTS] --> B[Thermosetting Resins]
    A --> C[Thermoplastic Resins]
    B --> D[Epoxy]
    B --> E[Polyurethane PU]
    B --> F[Polyester]
    B --> G[Acrylic Acrylate]
    D --> D1[DGEBA + Amine Hardener]
    D --> D2[Flexible Epoxy with Diluents]
    E --> E1[Hydrophobic PU - MDI Based]
    E --> E2[Hydrophilic PU - Water Reactive]
    F --> F1[Unsaturated Polyester + Styrene]
    F --> F2[Vinyl Ester - Superior Variant]
    G --> G1[Methyl Methacrylate MMA]
    G --> G2[Acrylamide Gel Grouts]
    C --> H[PVAc - Polyvinyl Acetate]
    C --> I[SBR Latex]
    C --> J[Acrylic Latex Emulsions]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Polymer Grout Injection System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Crack / Void Diagnosis] --> B[Substrate Cleaning]
    B --> C[Port Fixing at 200-500mm Spacing]
    C --> D[Surface Sealing with Epoxy Putty]
    D --> E[Resin + Hardener Mixing in Stoichiometric Ratio]
    E --> F{Pressure Injection Pump}
    F -->|Epoxy| G1[0.2 to 0.5 MPa for Structural]
    F -->|PU| G2[0.5 to 1.0 MPa for Active Leaks]
    F -->|Acrylic| G3[0.1 to 0.3 MPa for Hairline]
    G1 --> H[24 to 72 Hour Cure]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Core Sampling and Pull-Off Test]
    I --> J{Quality Pass}
    J -->|Yes| K[Surface Finishing and Re-Sealing]
    J -->|No| L[Re-Injection of Failed Zone]
    L --> F
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Polymer Grout

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: Grout Selection] --> Q1{Is the crack active under movement?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Live| PU[Polyurethane Grout]
    Q1 -->|No, Dormant| Q2{Is water present during injection?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, Wet| EPW[Epoxy with Wet-Bond Primer]
    Q2 -->|No, Dry| Q3{Is structural strength required?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| EP[Epoxy Grout High Modulus]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the crack hairline under 0.2 mm?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| AC[Acrylic or MMA Grout]
    Q4 -->|No, Wider than 0.2 mm| EST[Polyester Grout Cost Effective Seal]
    PU --> FIN[Final Selection]
    EPW --> FIN
    EP --> FIN
    AC --> FIN
    EST --> FIN
```

### 4.4 Bond Mechanism at the Crack Interface

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Concrete Substrate
        CS1[Aggregate]
        CS2[Cement Paste Matrix]
    end
    subgraph Polymer Grout Penetrated Zone

        PG2[Mechanical Interlock at Micro-Pores]
        PG3[Van der Waals Adhesion]
        PG4[Bulk Polymerized Resin]
    end
    CS1 --> PG1
    CS2 --> PG2
    CS1 --> PG3
    PG1 --> PG4
    PG2 --> PG4
    PG3 --> PG4
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between cement grout and polymer grout. List any two advantages of polymer grouts over cement grouts.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Cement Grout | Polymer Grout |
> |---------|--------------|---------------|
> | Binder | C-S-H gel from hydration | Polymerized resin |
> | Particle size | $10$–$80\ \mu m$ | $\approx 0.001\ \mu m$ (molecular) |
> | Bond type | Mechanical | Chemical + mechanical |
> | Setting | Hydraulic cure | Polymerization (minutes to hours) |
> | Flexibility | Brittle | Variable (rigid to elastomeric) |
>
> **Advantages:** (i) Penetrates hairline cracks ($< 0.2\ mm$); (ii) Higher chemical resistance; (iii) Better bond strength; (iv) Faster cure. *(Any two, 1.5 marks each)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is the role of an expanding polyurethane grout in repair works? Give an example application.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Expanding PU grout **fills voids and seals active water leaks** by foaming/expanding 5–35× its liquid volume during cure, exerting gentle pressure on substrate walls. The expansion ensures complete filling even in irregular cavities.
> **Example Application:** *Sealing live water-bearing cracks in basement retaining walls, dam galleries, and tunnel linings — the grout reacts with water, expands, and forms a flexible, watertight seal within 60–90 seconds.* *(1.5 marks for mechanism, 1.5 marks for example)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3 Internal Choice]**

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the chemistry, properties, and application procedure of epoxy grout in the repair of concrete structures. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Chemistry (2 marks):**
Epoxy grout is a two-component system: *Part A* is a liquid **Bisphenol-A based epoxy resin** (DGEBA — Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A) containing epoxide rings. *Part B* is the **hardener**, typically an aliphatic amine (TETA, DETA) or polyamide. Mixing triggers *step-growth addition polymerization* — the amine hydrogens open the epoxide rings to form a cross-linked, three-dimensional network. No by-products are released, hence **low shrinkage** ($\approx 0.5$–$1\%$).

**2. Properties (2 marks):**
- Tensile strength: $30$–$70\ MPa$.
- Compressive strength: $70$–$140\ MPa$.
- Bond strength to concrete: $\ge 3.5\ MPa$ (exceeds parent concrete tensile strength).
- Chemical resistance: Excellent against acids, alkalis, oils, and chlorides.
- Pot life: 15–45 minutes at $25^{\circ}C$.
- Full cure: 7 days (functional strength in 24 hours).

**3. Application Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Crack survey** — width, depth, activity mapped; moisture condition noted.
2. **Surface preparation** — routing a $V$-groove along the crack; cleaning with compressed air; port fixings installed at $200$–$300\ mm$ intervals.
3. **Sealing** — surface is sealed with fast-set epoxy putty, leaving ports exposed.
4. **Mixing** — resin and hardener are mixed in stoichiometric ratio (e.g., $100:30$ by mass for amine-cured systems). Mixing is done slowly to avoid air entrainment.
5. **Injection** — using a pressure pot or pneumatic pump at $0.2$–$0.5\ MPa$, starting from the lowest port and progressing upward.
6. **Cure and validation** — cure for 24–72 hours; quality verified by core drilling and pull-off tests (acceptance: $\ge 1.7\ MPa$ per ASTM C1583).

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the slab-jacking technique using polyurethane grout. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Slab-jacking (also called *slab lifting* or *pressure grouting*) is a method to restore the elevation of settled concrete slabs by injecting an expanding polyurethane grout beneath them. The PU grout expands upon reaction, generating a controlled lifting force.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Survey** — slab elevation mapped with a laser level to determine lift profile.
2. **Drilling** — $12$–$16\ mm$ diameter holes drilled through the slab at $\approx 1.5\ m$ grid spacing.
3. **Injection** — PU grout injected through a delivery hose fitted with a packer. The two-component resin and isocyanate mix at the nozzle tip.
4. **Reaction** — PU grout expands $5$–$10$× in $30$–$60$ seconds, exerting upward pressure of $0.5$–$1.5\ MPa$ on the slab underside.
5. **Monitoring** — slab elevation is monitored in real-time with the laser; injection stops once the target level is reached.

**Schematic (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    SLAB[Sunken Concrete Slab] --> H1[Injection Hole 1]
    SLAB --> H2[Injection Hole 2]
    SLAB --> H3[Injection Hole 3]
    H1 --> PU[Expanding PU Grout Injected]
    H2 --> PU
    H3 --> PU
    PU --> V[Void or Sub-Soil Filled]
    V --> FR[Reaction Generates Vertical Force]
    FR --> LF[Lifting Force 0.5 to 1.5 MPa]
    LF --> LV[Slab Returns to Original Level]
    subgraph UNDER[Below Slab]
        PU
        V
    end
```

**Advantages of PU Slab-Jacking:**
- Fast (lane reopened in 30 minutes).
- Lightweight foam ($\approx 30$–$50\ kg/m^3$).
- Waterproof (closed-cell structure).
- Minimal disruption — no excavation required.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Compare the properties and applications of epoxy, polyurethane, and acrylic polymer grouts. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property / Aspect | Epoxy | Polyurethane (PU) | Acrylic (MMA) |
|-------------------|:-----:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|
| **Viscosity** | $100$–$1000\ cP$ | $200$–$3000\ cP$ | $1$–$10\ cP$ (very low) |
| **Cure mechanism** | Addition (epoxide + amine) | Addition (polyol + isocyanate) | Free-radical chain |
| **Tensile strength** | $30$–$70\ MPa$ (very high) | $1$–$10\ MPa$ (flexible) | $5$–$15\ MPa$ |
| **Elongation** | $2$–$5\%$ (rigid) | $100$–$600\%$ (elastomeric) | $20$–$50\%$ |
| **Volume change** | Shrinks $0.5$–$1\%$ | Expands $5$–$35$× | Slight shrink |
| **Bond to wet surface** | Good with primer | **Excellent** (inherent) | Poor |
| **Best application** | Structural crack repair | Active leak sealing | Hairline crack & duct injection |
| **Cost** | High | High | Medium |
| **Cure time** | 24–72 h | 30–90 s (rapid) | 1–4 h |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Epoxy is selected for *structural strength*, PU for *waterproofing and flexibility*, acrylic for *deep penetration* in fine cracks and tendon ducts.

**(b) Describe in detail the procedure for polymer grout injection in concrete cracks. Mention the quality control tests. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pre-Injection Stage (2 marks):**
1. **Crack Mapping** — width, depth, length, and activity (live/dead) recorded.
2. **Cause Analysis** — thermal, structural, or shrinkage origin identified; if live, flexible grout (PU) chosen.
3. **Surface Preparation** — cracks are cleaned with wire brush and compressed air; loose material removed.

**Injection Stage (3 marks):**
1. **Port Fixing** — injection nipples (ports) installed at intervals of $200$–$500\ mm$ along the crack, bonded with fast-set epoxy putty.
2. **Surface Sealing** — the crack length between ports is sealed with epoxy putty to confine grout flow.
3. **Resin Mixing** — base resin and hardener are mixed in stoichiometric ratio; for small batches, hand-mixing; for large works, mechanical stirrer.
4. **Injection** — using a pressure pot (gravity) or air-driven pump. Injection starts at the lowest port, continues until grout appears at the adjacent upper port. The lower port is sealed and injection shifts upward.
5. **Cure** — left undisturbed for 24 hours (epoxy) to 7 days (full strength).

**Quality Control Tests (2 marks):**
1. **Visual Inspection** — surface checked for un-grouted voids.
2. **Core Drilling and Visual Examination** — cores examined for grout penetration depth.
3. **Pull-Off Tensile Test (ASTM C1583)** — $\sigma_t = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}$; acceptance $\ge 1.7\ MPa$.
4. **Slant Shear Bond Test (ASTM C882)** — bond strength $\ge 7\ MPa$.
5. **Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV)** — to detect internal voids.
6. **Resin Sample Testing** — cubes cast at site, tested for compressive strength at 7 days.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "epoxy grout" with "cement grout":** The examiner expects resin chemistry (epoxide + amine). Generic "two-part grout" earns 0 marks for the chemistry part.
> 2. **Skipping the mix ratio:** Failing to state the *stoichiometric ratio* (e.g., 100:30) loses 1–2 marks in numerical/descriptive answers.
> 3. **Ignoring pot life and ambient temperature:** KTU 2024 specifically tests the temperature dependence of pot life. Always state pot life in *minutes at 25°C*.
> 4. **Wrong application of PU grout:** PU is for *active/leaking* cracks, not dry structural cracks. Epoxy is for structural, dry cracks. Swapping them is a common error.
> 5. **No quality control:** Answers that end at "curing" without mentioning pull-off or core tests are marked down by at least 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Mixing SI units:** Use *MPa* for stress, *cP* for viscosity, *g/mol* for molar mass — not the CGS units.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Polymer Grout** = a fluid polymeric resin (epoxy / PU / acrylic / polyester) used to fill voids, seal cracks, and bond substrates — cures by **polymerization**, not hydration.
- **Cement grout** sets via *hydration* (C-S-H gel); **polymer grout** sets via *polymerization* (cross-linked resin network).
- **Epoxy grout** = *structural grade* → high tensile strength ($30$–$70\ MPa$), excellent chemical resistance, low shrinkage, but **brittle**.
- **Polyurethane grout** = *flexible + expandable* → seals active leaks, expands $5$–$35$×, used in wet/dynamic conditions.
- **Acrylic grout** = *ultra-low viscosity* → penetrates hairline cracks and PT ducts, fast cure.
- **Polyester grout** = *economical* → general non-structural sealing.
- **Mix ratio** is **stoichiometric** — calculate using Epoxy Equivalent Weight (EEW) and Amine Hydrogen Equivalent Weight (AHEW).
- **Pot life** decreases with temperature per *Arrhenius equation* — a $10^{\circ}C$ rise roughly halves it.
- **Bond strength** is verified by **pull-off test** ($\sigma_b = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}$); acceptance $\ge 1.7\ MPa$ (ASTM C1583).
- **Slab-jacking** uses expanding PU grout — small liquid volume produces large uplift force.
- **Crack width dictates grout choice:** $< 0.2\ mm$ → acrylic; $0.2$–$3\ mm$ → epoxy; live/wet cracks → PU.
- **Standard quality tests:** Pull-off, Slant Shear, UPV, Core drilling, Cube compressive strength.
- **Standard codes to cite in KTU answers:** IS 456:2000 (rehabilitation provisions), ACI 546R, ASTM C881 (epoxy grout specification), ASTM C1583 (pull-off test).
- **Cost ranking (low → high):** Polyester < Acrylic < PU $\approx$ Epoxy.
- **Always mention** the *limitation* of the chosen grout in your answer — KTU examiners reward balanced responses.
- **Numerical problems** often revolve around: mix ratio calculation, volume uplift, pot life, bond strength.

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 — Polymer Grouts Notes**
> *All the best for your KTU ESE preparation!* 🚀

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acrylate and Urethane Grouts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acrylate and Urethane Grouts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Chemical Grouting in Repair Engineering

Chemical grouting is a cornerstone technique in the **Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures**, particularly for arresting water ingress, sealing hairline cracks, and consolidating porous masonry. Among the broad family of chemical grouts, **Acrylate Grouts** and **Urethane (Polyurethane) Grouts** occupy a critical position due to their ability to penetrate micro-fissures where conventional cementitious grouts fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECET634, Module 3):**
> The student is expected to understand the **chemistry, mechanism of action, properties, application methodology, and selection criteria** for non-cementitious chemical grouts, with special emphasis on acrylates and urethanes for sealing active water-leaking cracks.

## 1.2 Formal Definition – Acrylate Grouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> **Acrylate grouts** are low-viscosity, water-reactive (or water-miscible) chemical injection grouts based on **acrylic monomers or acrylamide copolymers** that polymerize in situ to form a flexible, elastic, hydrogel or elastomeric seal within cracks, joints, and porous substrates.

**Key chemical constituents** typically include:
- **Acrylamide (AM)** — the reactive monomer
- **N,N′-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA)** — the cross-linking agent
- **Triethanolamine / Ammonium Persulphate** — the catalyst–initiator redox pair
- **Inhibitors** (e.g., hydroquinone) for shelf-life control

The resulting polymer is a **cross-linked polyacrylamide hydrogel** — essentially a superabsorbent, jelly-like solid that swells in the presence of water.

## 1.3 Formal Definition – Urethane Grouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> **Urethane (Polyurethane) grouts** are moisture-reactive or two-component injection resins based on **isocyanate-terminated prepolymers** that react with water (or with a polyol blend) to form a closed-cell or open-cell polyurethane foam/elastomer seal of controllable density and expansion ratio.

**Key chemical constituents** typically include:
- **Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate (MDI)** or **Toluene Diisocyanate (TDI)** — the isocyanate base
- **Polyether polyols** — for flexible/hydrophilic grades
- **Catalysts** (tertiary amines, organometallics) — to control gel time
- **Surfactants and blowing agents** — to control cell structure

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – "The Wound-Closure Doctor"

Imagine a concrete dam or basement wall as a **human body with a slow internal bleed**. Cement grout is like a **thick surgical plaster** — it can only patch large visible cuts. But the real damage is in the **invisible hairline capillaries (micro-cracks)** where water is seeping through. 

- **Acrylate grout** acts like a **clear medical gel that flows into the wound, then solidifies into a soft jelly** that absorbs further moisture. It is the "hydrogel bandage" for fine, hair-thin cracks.
- **Urethane grout** behaves like a **rapidly expanding medical foam** injected into the wound — it meets water, foams up, and **physically plugs** the bleeding channel with a resilient, watertight, rubbery plug.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory anchor:**
> *Acrylates → low-viscosity water-miscible hydrogel seal (fine cracks).*
> *Urethanes → water-reactive expanding foam seal (active leaks & joints).*

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry & IS Reference)

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity of Acrylate Resin** | **1 – 10** | mPa·s (cP) |
| **Viscosity of Urethane Resin** | **50 – 1500** | mPa·s (cP) |
| **Density of cured Acrylate gel** | **~1.00 – 1.10** | g/cm³ |
| **Density of cured Urethane foam** | **0.03 – 0.60** | g/cm³ |
| **Tensile strength (Urethane elastomer)** | **1 – 25** | MPa |
| **Elongation at break (Urethane)** | **100 – 800** | % |
| **Service temperature range** | **−20 to +80** | °C |
| **Pot life / Gel time (adjustable)** | **30 s – 60 min** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chemistry of Acrylate Grout Polymerization

Acrylate grouts set by **free-radical addition polymerization**. The reaction proceeds in three mechanistic stages:

1. **Initiation**: The redox pair (typically **ammonium persulphate + triethanolamine**) generates free radicals (R•).
2. **Propagation**: Acrylamide monomer units ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH–CONH}_2$) successively add to the radical, propagating the chain.
3. **Cross-linking & Termination**: The bifunctional **N,N′-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA)** forms a 3-D network, and the chains terminate by combination or disproportionation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cross-link density (controlled by MBA content) directly governs the **swelling ratio, elasticity, and chemical resistance** of the final hydrogel.

### Simplified Reaction Stoichiometry

$$\text{n}\,(\text{CH}_2=\text{CH–CONH}_2) \xrightarrow[\text{MBA + Redox}]{\text{Initiator}} \text{–[CH}_2\text{–CH(CONH}_2\text{)]}_n\text{–}$$

The reaction is **exothermic** and is often characterized by:

$$Q_{rxn} = n \cdot \Delta H_{poly} \quad [\text{kJ/mol}]$$

where $\Delta H_{poly}$ for acrylamide polymerization is approximately **−78 kJ/mol**.

## 2.2 Chemistry of Urethane Grout Polymerization

Urethane grouts are based on the **isocyanate–polyol reaction** (for two-component systems) or the **isocyanate–water reaction** (for moisture-reactive single-component systems).

### Reaction A – Isocyanate + Polyol (Two-Component)

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'}$$

This produces a **polyurethane elastomer** of controllable hardness.

### Reaction B – Isocyanate + Water (Moisture-Cure)

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

The liberated **CO₂ gas is the blowing agent** that produces the foam. The amine formed immediately reacts with a second isocyanate to give a substituted urea:

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{R–NH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–NH–R}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **molar ratio of water to isocyanate** dictates the **foam expansion ratio**. Roughly 1 mole of water generates 1 mole of CO₂, producing ~22.4 L of gas at STP, which expands the grout.

## 2.3 Mechanism of Water-Stopping Action

| Stage | Acrylate Grout | Urethane Grout |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Injection | Water-thin liquid penetrates micro-cracks | Moderate-viscosity liquid injected under pressure |
| 2. Reaction Trigger | Catalyst + temperature initiate polymerization | Contact with water liberates CO₂ |
| 3. Seal Formation | Solid hydrogel forms in situ | Foam expands to fill void |
| 4. Final State | Flexible, swollen gel | Closed/open-cell foam plug |
| 5. Best Crack Width | 0.05 – 3 mm (hairline to fine) | 0.5 – 25 mm (fine to wide) |

## 2.4 Selection Criteria – Why Choose One Over the Other?

- **Choose Acrylate** when: cracks are hairline (< 1 mm), substrate is damp masonry/brick, long-term chemical resistance is needed, low injection pressure is mandatory.
- **Choose Urethane** when: there is **active, flowing water leakage**, large voids, expansion joints requiring flexibility, rapid set is required, or underwater applications are involved.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Board Examination**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Volumetric injection flow rate | m³/s or L/min |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{2\,\sigma\,L}{r}$ | Laplace pressure for capillary penetration (cylindrical pore of radius *r*) | Pa |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{r^2 \,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille laminar flow velocity in a capillary | m/s |
| 4 | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\mu_{grout}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Relative viscosity ratio (drives injectability) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{exp} = \dfrac{\rho_{liquid}}{\rho_{foam}}$ | Foam expansion ratio (urethane) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $G' = \nu\,k_B T$ | Rubber-elastic shear modulus of cross-linked gel (acrylate) | Pa |
| 7 | $t_{gel} \propto \dfrac{1}{[I]^{0.5}}$ | Gel time inversely proportional to square root of initiator concentration | s |
| 8 | $S.R. = \dfrac{m_{swollen} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}$ | Swelling ratio of acrylate hydrogel | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{inj} = P_{pump} - P_{hydrostatic}$ | Net injection pressure (pump pressure minus water table head) | Pa |
| 10 | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity of shear-thinning grout at shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer scripts, **always state the assumptions** (laminar flow, isothermal, Newtonian approximation) before applying the Hagen–Poiseuille equation.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Hydroelectric Dams & Spillways**: Urethane grouts seal contraction joints against high-velocity water flow.
- **Basement & Sub-structure Waterproofing**: Acrylate gels are used in **curtain injection** behind diaphragm walls.
- **Tunnel Linings (Metro, Railway)**: Both grouts arrest seepage in segmental joints.
- **Sewage Treatment Plants**: Chemical-resistant acrylates seal cracks exposed to aggressive effluents.
- **Marine Piles & Jetties**: Urethanes tolerate wet, dynamic loading.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Penetration Depth of Acrylate Grout

The driving force for grout penetration into a hairline crack is the **Laplace (capillary) pressure**. For a crack approximated as two parallel plates separated by a gap $b$, the capillary pressure is:

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{b}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = surface tension of the grout (≈ 0.04 N/m for acrylate)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between grout and substrate
- $b$ = crack width (aperture)

For complete wetting ($\theta = 0$):

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\,\sigma}{b}$$

### Step-by-Step Equilibrium Penetration Depth

**Step 1**: Set the capillary pressure equal to the hydrostatic head of the grout column at depth $h$:

$$\rho\,g\,h = \frac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{b}$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\rho\,g\,b}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute numerical values for an acrylate grout penetrating a 0.2 mm crack:

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.040 \times \cos(15°)}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0002}$$

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.040 \times 0.9659}{1.962}$$

$$h = \frac{0.0773}{1.962}$$

$$h \approx 0.0394 \text{ m} = 39.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Engineering interpretation**: Pure capillary suction alone drives grout only ~40 mm into a 0.2 mm crack. This is why **active injection pressure** from a pump is mandatory for deep penetration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Through a Crack

A planar crack of width $b$ and length $L$ is modeled as two parallel plates. The volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian grout is:

$$Q = \frac{b^3 \, \Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$$

### Derivation Path

**Step 1**: The Navier–Stokes equation for steady, laminar, fully developed flow between stationary parallel plates reduces to:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 2**: Integrating twice with no-slip boundary conditions at $y = \pm b/2$:

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(y^2 - \frac{b^2}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3**: Integrate the velocity profile across the gap to get volumetric flow per unit width:

$$q = \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} u(y)\,dy = -\frac{b^3}{12\,\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 4**: With $\Delta p/L$ replacing $dp/dx$:

$$Q = \frac{b^3\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L} \quad \text{(per unit width of crack)}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

Given: $b = 0.5$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $\mu_{acrylate} = 5$ cP = 0.005 Pa·s, $\Delta P = 0.5$ MPa = 500,000 Pa.

$$Q = \frac{(0.0005)^3 \times 500{,}000}{12 \times 0.005 \times 0.1}$$

$$Q = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 5 \times 10^{5}}{6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Q = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Q \approx 0.01042 \text{ m}^3/\text{s per meter width}$$

This means a **10 mm wide crack segment** accepts grout at a rate of $\approx 1.04 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s — a critical design input for pump selection.

> [!TIP]
> Note the **cubic dependence on crack width $b$**. Doubling the crack width **increases the flow rate by 8 times** — a powerful KTU numerical illustration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Foam Expansion Ratio for Urethane Grout

The expansion ratio is governed by the **moles of water reacted per mole of isocyanate**.

**Step 1**: Reaction stoichiometry — 1 mole of H₂O produces 1 mole of CO₂.

$$n_{CO_2} = n_{H_2O}$$

**Step 2**: At standard conditions, molar volume of gas:

$$V_m = 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.0224 \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 3**: Volume of foam produced (assuming foam density $\rho_f$):

$$V_{foam} = \frac{n_{CO_2} \cdot V_m \cdot \rho_{CO_2}}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 4**: Define expansion ratio:

$$R_{exp} = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{liquid}}$$

### Numerical Example

A 1 L urethane grout ($V_{liquid} = 1$ L) reacts with water and produces 2.5 mol of CO₂. Final foam density = 0.15 g/cm³ = 150 kg/m³. Liquid density = 1100 kg/m³.

$$V_{foam} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.0224 \times 1.98}{0.15} = 0.739 \text{ m}^3 = 739 \text{ L}$$

$$R_{exp} = \frac{739}{1} = 739 \times$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical MDI-based hydrophobic urethane grout achieves **20 – 40× expansion**, while aggressive hydrophilic grades may exceed **500× expansion** in confined conditions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Grout Selection

| Parameter | Acrylate Grout | Urethane (Hydrophobic) | Urethane (Hydrophilic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity (mPa·s)** | 1 – 10 | 100 – 800 | 50 – 1500 |
| **Crack width range (mm)** | 0.05 – 3 | 0.5 – 25 | 0.5 – 25 |
| **Active water flow** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Chemical resistance** | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **UV resistance** | Moderate | Poor | Poor |
| **Cost (INR / kg)** | ₹ 250 – 600 | ₹ 400 – 900 | ₹ 350 – 800 |
| **Typical application** | Curtain injection | Joint sealing | Wet, dynamic joints |
| **Shelf life** | 6 – 12 months | 12 months | 12 months |
| **Toxicity** | Acrylamide – mild neurotoxin | Isocyanate – sensitizer | Isocyanate – sensitizer |

> [!WARNING]
> Acrylamide is classified as a **probable human carcinogen (IARC Group 2A)**. Modern KTU-aligned specifications increasingly prefer **methacrylate-based** or **acrylamide-free** grouts for the same application.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between acrylate and urethane grouts on the basis of (i) viscosity, (ii) mechanism of cure, and (iii) typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Acrylate Grout | Urethane Grout |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Viscosity** | Very low (**1 – 10 mPa·s**), water-like | Moderate to high (**50 – 1500 mPa·s**) |
| **(ii) Mechanism of cure** | **Free-radical addition polymerization** of acrylamide/methacrylate monomers, triggered by a redox initiator system | **Isocyanate–water (or isocyanate–polyol) reaction** that liberates CO₂ to form a polyurethane/urea foam |
| **(iii) Typical applications** | Sealing **hairline cracks (0.05 – 3 mm)** in masonry, brick, and damp concrete; curtain grouting behind diaphragm walls | Sealing **active water leaks**, large voids, expansion joints, segmental tunnel linings, and underwater applications |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "acrylates cure by mixing two liquids" without specifying the **redox-initiated free-radical mechanism**. Mentioning the *initiator–catalyst* system fetches the full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**Explain why urethane grouts are preferred over acrylate grouts for sealing active water-leaking joints in hydraulic structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Urethane grouts are preferred for **active water-leaking joints** in hydraulic structures (dams, spillways, tunnels) because of the following reasons:

1. **Water-reactive expansion mechanism:** The isocyanate–water reaction **liberates CO₂ gas** in situ, causing the grout to **expand 20 – 40 times** its original volume. This expansion physically **chases the water front** and seals the leak from within.
2. **Rapid gel time control:** Tertiary-amine and organometallic catalysts allow the gel time to be **tuned from 30 seconds to several minutes**, enabling the grout to set *before* it is washed away by flowing water.
3. **Flexible and elastic cured foam:** The resulting polyurethane/urea matrix exhibits **high elongation (100 – 800%)** and accommodates **joint movement and thermal expansion**, which is critical in hydraulic structures subjected to cyclic loading.

In contrast, acrylate grouts have a water-like viscosity and rely on **diffusion-controlled polymerization**, which is unsuitable when water is actively flushing the monomer out of the crack before it sets.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write generic statements like "urethane is better." Always quote **the underlying physical/chemical reason** (expansion, gel time control, elasticity) to secure the full mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks] Discuss in detail the chemistry, mechanism of polymerization, and salient properties of acrylate grouts. How are they applied in field practice for sealing hairline cracks in concrete structures?**

**(b) [7 marks] A 0.4 mm wide horizontal crack of length 200 mm in a basement wall is to be sealed by acrylate grout injection. The grout has viscosity 4 cP and surface tension 0.04 N/m. The injection pump delivers grout at 0.4 MPa. Calculate (i) the capillary penetration depth, (ii) the volumetric flow rate of grout into the crack using the Hagen–Poiseuille relation, and (iii) the time required to fill the crack assuming the effective grout front velocity equals the average flow velocity.**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Chemistry:** Acrylate grouts are based on **acrylamide (CH₂=CH–CONH₂)** or **methacrylate monomers**, combined with the cross-linker **N,N′-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA)**. The catalyst system is a **redox pair** — typically **ammonium persulphate (initiator)** and **triethanolamine (activator)**. A small amount of inhibitor (hydroquinone) is added for shelf stability.

**Mechanism of polymerization:** The reaction proceeds by **free-radical addition polymerization** in three stages — *initiation* (redox pair generates R•), *propagation* (R• attacks monomer C=C, forming a growing chain radical), and *cross-linking/termination* (MBA forms a 3-D network; chains terminate by combination). The setting time is controlled by adjusting the **initiator concentration**, **temperature**, and the **activator–inhibitor balance**.

**Salient properties:**
- Ultra-low viscosity (**1 – 10 cP**) — almost water-like.
- High **penetration capacity** into hairline cracks (> 0.05 mm).
- Cured gel is **flexible, elastic, and chemically resistant** (acids, alkalis, salts).
- Adjustable **gel time from minutes to hours**.
- Compatible with **wet substrates**.
- Good **adhesion to concrete, brick, and stone**.

**Field application procedure:**
1. **Crack survey** and marking using feeler gauges and moisture meters.
2. **Surface preparation** — routing out the crack to a V-groove if necessary.
3. **Installation of injection packers** (mechanical or adhesive) at 150 – 300 mm spacing along the crack.
4. **Flushing** with water to verify communication and remove dust.
5. **Mixing** of A and B components (resin + initiator) immediately before injection.
6. **Injection** at low pressure (**0.2 – 0.8 MPa**) starting from the lowest port.
7. **Port sequencing** — moving to the next port when grout emerges from the adjacent one.
8. **Curing** for 24 hours, followed by packer removal and surface sealing.
9. **Quality control** — water-tightness test and, where specified, **core sampling** for verification.

*(7 marks: 2 for chemistry, 2 for mechanism, 1 for properties, 2 for application procedure)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- Crack width: $b = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Crack length: $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- Grout viscosity: $\mu = 4$ cP $= 0.004$ Pa·s
- Surface tension: $\sigma = 0.04$ N/m
- Contact angle: assume $\theta = 20°$ (typical for grout on damp concrete)
- Injection pressure: $\Delta P = 0.4$ MPa $= 4 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Grout density: $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³ (typical)

**(i) Capillary penetration depth**

$$h = \frac{2\,\sigma\,\cos\theta}{\rho\,g\,b}$$

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.04 \times \cos(20°)}{1050 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.08 \times 0.9397}{4.120}$$

$$h = \frac{0.0752}{4.120}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.01825 \text{ m} = 18.25 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** Capillary suction alone pushes the grout about 18 mm into the crack. **Active pump pressure is essential** for deeper penetration.

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate per unit width (Hagen–Poiseuille)**

$$q = \frac{b^3\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$$

$$q = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 4 \times 10^5}{12 \times 0.004 \times 0.2}$$

$$q = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-11} \times 4 \times 10^5}{0.0096}$$

$$q = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0096}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 2.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m width}}$$

For the actual 200 mm (= 0.2 m) long crack segment, the total flow is $0.2 \times 2.667 \times 10^{-3} = 5.33 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

**(iii) Time to fill the crack**

Crack void volume (per meter width along the crack length):

$$V_{void} = b \times L \times 1 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2 \times 1 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (per m width)}$$

Average velocity in the crack:

$$u_{avg} = \frac{q}{b} = \frac{2.667 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \text{ m/s}$$

Time for grout front to travel length $L$:

$$t = \frac{L}{u_{avg}} = \frac{0.2}{6.667} = 0.03 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This unrealistically high velocity indicates that the **laminar flow assumption is approaching its limit**. In practice, the front velocity is dominated by the **rate of polymerization (gel time)**, not by the pump delivery alone. KTU examiners expect you to **state this assumption clearly** to avoid losing methodology marks.

A more practical field estimate uses the **gel-time-limited fill time**:

$$t_{fill} \approx 0.7 \times t_{gel} \approx 0.7 \times 600 \text{ s} = 420 \text{ s} = 7 \text{ min}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 1 for part (iii) and discussion)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks] Describe the chemistry and foam-formation mechanism of urethane grouts. Explain the difference between hydrophobic and hydrophilic polyurethane grouts, with suitable applications.**

**(b) [7 marks] A 5 L drum of hydrophobic MDI-based urethane grout (density 1120 kg/m³) is injected into a leaking joint. If the grout reacts with 1.8 moles of water per kilogram of resin and forms a foam of density 0.18 g/cm³, calculate (i) the total mass of grout in the drum, (ii) the moles of CO₂ liberated, and (iii) the final volume of the foam and its expansion ratio.**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Chemistry of urethane grouts:** Urethane grouts are formulated from **aromatic isocyanates** — primarily **Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate (MDI)** or **Toluene Diisocyanate (TDI)** — either pre-reacted with polyether polyols to form **isocyanate-terminated prepolymers** (single-component systems) or supplied separately with a polyol hardener (two-component systems). Catalysts (tertiary amines like DABCO, or organotin compounds like dibutyltin dilaurate) and surfactants are added to control gel time and cell morphology.

**Foam-formation mechanism:** When the grout contacts water, the **isocyanate–water reaction** liberates **CO₂ gas**, which acts as the **in-situ blowing agent**. The reaction proceeds in two coupled steps:

1. $\text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$
2. $\text{R–NCO} + \text{R–NH}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–NH–R}$ (urea linkage)

The CO₂ is trapped in the viscous reacting mass, forming a **closed-cell or open-cell polyurethane/urea foam** that expands to fill the void.

**Hydrophobic vs. Hydrophilic Urethane Grouts:**

| Property | Hydrophobic Urethane | Hydrophilic Urethane |
|---|---|---|
| **Base chemistry** | MDI prepolymer with hydrophobic polyether backbone | Prepolymer with **polyethylene oxide (PEO)** segments that attract water |
| **Water absorption** | Negligible | Up to 200 – 400% of dry weight |
| **Behaviour on re-wetting** | Does **not** re-swell if dried out | **Re-swells** upon re-wetting (self-healing) |
| **Typical applications** | One-time sealing of active leaks in tunnels, basements, swimming pools; areas with chemical exposure | Dynamic/expansion joints, **submerged structures**, areas where **cyclic wetting** is expected; **manhole-to-pipe** seals |
| **Durability in wet-dry cycles** | Moderate | Excellent (reversible swelling) |
| **Cost** | Higher (more refined chemistry) | Moderate |

**Field applications of hydrophobic urethanes:** Tunnel linings, dam contraction joints, basement walls with active seepage, drinking-water reservoirs (NSF/ANSI 61 certified grades available).

**Field applications of hydrophilic urethanes:** Sewer manhole rings, pipe penetrations, expansion joints in bridges, wet-well structures in STP, anywhere a **self-healing, reversible** seal is desired.

*(7 marks: 2 for chemistry, 2 for foam mechanism, 1.5 for differences, 1.5 for applications)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- Volume of grout: $V_{liquid} = 5$ L $= 0.005$ m³
- Density of liquid grout: $\rho_{liq} = 1120$ kg/m³
- Water reaction ratio: $n_{H_2O} = 1.8$ mol per kg of resin
- Foam density: $\rho_{foam} = 0.18$ g/cm³ $= 180$ kg/m³
- Molar volume of CO₂ at field conditions (assume ~25 °C, 1 atm): $V_m \approx 0.0245$ m³/mol

**(i) Total mass of grout in the drum**

$$m_{grout} = \rho_{liq} \times V_{liquid}$$

$$m_{grout} = 1120 \times 0.005$$

$$\boxed{m_{grout} = 5.6 \text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Moles of CO₂ liberated**

$$n_{CO_2} = n_{H_2O} = 1.8 \times m_{grout} = 1.8 \times 5.6$$

$$\boxed{n_{CO_2} = 10.08 \text{ mol}}$$

**(iii) Final volume of the foam and expansion ratio**

The mass of the foam = mass of grout + mass of reacted water (10.08 mol × 0.018 kg/mol = 0.1814 kg):

$$m_{foam} = 5.6 + 0.1814 = 5.781 \text{ kg}$$

Final foam volume:

$$V_{foam} = \frac{m_{foam}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{5.781}{180}$$

$$\boxed{V_{foam} \approx 0.0321 \text{ m}^3 = 32.1 \text{ L}}$$

Expansion ratio:

$$R_{exp} = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{liquid}} = \frac{32.1}{5}$$

$$\boxed{R_{exp} = 6.42 \times}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The expansion ratio of ~6.4 is **relatively low** because hydrophobic MDI grouts are typically used in *confined* conditions where expansion is restrained. In an unconfined free-rise scenario, the same grout may achieve 20 – 35× expansion. KTU examiners may ask you to **state this practical caveat** for full marks.

*(7 marks: 1 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 4 for part (iii) including discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Callouts (Acrylate & Urethane Grouts)**
> 1. Do **not** confuse **polyurethane (PU)** with **polyurea**. Polyurea forms by isocyanate + polyamine reaction — a different chemistry.
> 2. Do **not** state that **acrylamide is "safe to handle"** — it is a known neurotoxin and probable carcinogen.
> 3. Always **convert cP to Pa·s** (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s) before using SI-consistent formulas.
> 4. For **expansion ratio problems**, students forget to **add the mass of reacted water** to the foam mass.
> 5. **Hagen–Poiseuille assumes laminar flow.** Always check the Reynolds number $Re = \rho u D_h / \mu < 2300$ for the parallel-plate case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 – Acrylate & Urethane Grouts)**

- ☐ **Acrylate grouts** are water-thin, **redox-polymerized polyacrylamide hydrogels** — best for **hairline cracks (0.05 – 3 mm)**.
- ☐ **Urethane grouts** are **isocyanate-based, water-reactive** foams — best for **active leaks and large voids**.
- ☐ The **two key reactions of urethane** are (a) R–NCO + H₂O → R–NH₂ + CO₂ and (b) R–NCO + R–NH₂ → substituted urea.
- ☐ The **expansion ratio** of urethane grout is governed by the **moles of water reacted**.
- ☐ **Hydrophilic urethanes** can **re-swell on re-wetting** (self-healing); **hydrophobic** ones cannot.
- ☐ The **Hagen–Poiseuille equation for parallel plates** is $Q = b^3 \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$ — note the **cubic dependence on crack width**.
- ☐ The **Laplace pressure** $P_{cap} = 2\sigma\cos\theta / b$ drives initial grout penetration.
- ☐ **Gel time** is inversely proportional to the square root of **initiator concentration** (for acrylates) and is tuned by **catalyst dosage** (for urethanes).
- ☐ **Acrylamide monomer** is a **probable carcinogen (IARC 2A)** — modern formulations are shifting to **methacrylate-based** alternatives.
- ☐ **Isocyanates** are **respiratory sensitizers** — field crews must use **PPE and ventilation** as per OSHA/IS safety codes.
- ☐ The **cure temperature** affects gel time: every 10 °C rise roughly **halves** the gel time (Arrhenius behaviour).
- ☐ Always specify **substrate moisture condition** (SSD, dry, saturated) when selecting grout — urethanes tolerate wet; acrylates prefer damp, not flowing.
- ☐ **Quality control** includes (a) pressure-decay test, (b) core sampling, (c) water-tightness test, and (d) ultrasonic pulse velocity comparison.
- ☐ For KTU numericals, **always state** the assumptions of Newtonian behaviour, laminar flow, isothermal conditions, and complete wetting.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Acrylate and Urethane Grouts**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protective Coatings - Protective Coatings for Concrete and Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protective Coatings - Protective Coatings for Concrete and Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protective Coating (KTU 2024 Definition):** A protective coating is a thin, continuous, adherent layer of organic, inorganic, or metallic material applied to the surface of concrete or steel structural elements to act as a **barrier system** that isolates the substrate from the ingress of aggressive agents (chlorides, CO₂, water, oxygen, sulphates, and UV radiation), thereby preventing deterioration mechanisms such as **corrosion of steel reinforcement, carbonation, freeze-thaw damage, alkali-silica reaction, and chemical attack.**

In the context of KTU syllabus (Module 3 of PECET634), protective coatings are categorised into two fundamentally different material domains owing to the vastly different substrate behaviour:

1. **Protective Coatings for Concrete** — primarily to seal micro-cracks, block porosity, resist chemical ingress, and enhance surface durability.
2. **Protective Coatings for Steel** — primarily to provide cathodic/anodic protection, barrier protection, and chemical resistance to metallic substrates.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Raincoat Analogy (Simple Explanation for Students):**
> Imagine you are going out in heavy rain. You wear a **raincoat**. The raincoat does not make you stronger, nor does it change your body — it simply creates a **protective film** that stops water from reaching your skin.
> Protective coatings for structures work **exactly the same way**. The concrete or steel underneath is already there. The coating is just a "raincoat" for the structure. But here's the catch: a raincoat works only if the surface beneath is **clean, dry, and properly prepared**, and the coat is **applied to the correct thickness**. A torn raincoat (cracked coating) or one applied over a dirty surface (poor preparation) is worse than no raincoat at all.

## 1.3 Performance Metrics & Standards (Must-Know)

| Metric | Standard Reference | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** | ISO 19840 | **100 – 500 µm** |
| **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | ASTM D4414 | Measured during application |
| **Pull-off Adhesion Strength** | ASTM D4541 | **≥ 1.5 N/mm²** for concrete |
| **Salt Spray Resistance** | ASTM B117 | **1000 – 3000 hours** |
| **Surface Profile (Steel)** | ISO 8503 | **50 – 100 µm** |
| **Service Life (Good System)** | ISO 12944 | **15 – 25+ years** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Buzzwords to Memorise:** *Barrier protection, Inhibitor pigmentation, Sacrificial anode, Cathodic disbondment, DFT, WFT, SSPC, Surface profile, Carbonation, Chloride diffusion, Osmosis, Blistering, Delamination.*

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a high-yield Module 3 topic — frequently asked in **ESE (End Semester Examination)** as a 14-mark question.
- The topic tests both **material science** (resin chemistry) and **practical engineering judgement** (selection of system for a given environment).
- It is closely linked to previous modules on **deterioration mechanisms** — so examiners love asking *"which coating suits which deterioration?"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Protective Coatings

The coatings universe is best understood by splitting it into two distinct branches based on substrate:

### A. Protective Coatings for **CONCRETE**

| Coating Type | Resin Base | Key Property | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acrylic Coatings** | Thermoplastic acrylic polymer | UV resistance, water-based | External façade, anti-carbonation |
| **Epoxy Coatings** | Bisphenol-A epoxy + amine hardener | Chemical resistance, hard | Floors, chemical plants, water tanks |
| **Polyurethane (PU) Coatings** | Aliphatic/aromatic isocyanate + polyol | Abrasion + UV resistance | Bridge decks, parking decks |
| **Polysulphide / Polyurea** | Polyamine + isocyanate | Elongation, crack-bridging | Roofs, expansion joints, water-proofing |
| **Cementitious Coatings** | Polymer-modified cement | Vapour permeable, breathable | Reservoirs, basements (negative side) |
| **Bituminous / Coal-Tar Epoxy** | Coal-tar + epoxy | Waterproofing, chemical resistance | Sub-structures, foundations |
| **Silane / Siloxane** | Alkyl-alkoxy-silane | Hydrophobic, invisible | Silane impregnation for façades |
| **Anti-graffiti Coatings** | Sacrificial or permanent fluoropolymer | Easy cleaning | Public buildings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breathability vs Barrier — A Critical Distinction:**
> - Concrete "breathes" (water vapour migrates outward).
> - If a non-breathable coating (e.g., standard epoxy) is applied to a **wet** concrete, osmotic pressure builds and the coating **blisters**.
> - Hence, water tanks from the **negative side** require *cementitious crystalline* or *polymer-modified cementitious* coatings (breathable but watertight).

### B. Protective Coatings for **STEEL**

Steel coatings follow the **ISO 12944** classification which divides atmospheric corrosivity into **C1 to C5-I / C5-M** categories.

| Coating Type | Mechanism | Typical DFT | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zinc-Rich Primers (Inorganic ZRE / Organic ZRO)** | Cathodic (sacrificial) | **75 µm** | First coat for bridges, marine |
| **Epoxy Primers (with inhibitive pigment)** | Barrier + inhibitor | **100 – 200 µm** | Industrial structures |
| **Epoxy MIO (Micaceous Iron Oxide)** | Barrier (lamellar) | **100 – 150 µm** | Intermediate coat, bridges |
| **Polyurethane Topcoat (Aliphatic)** | UV + weather barrier | **50 – 75 µm** | Finish coat (colour + gloss) |
| **Fluoropolymer (PVDF, FEVE)** | Ultra-violet barrier | **25 – 40 µm** | Premium architecture, 30-yr life |
| **Coal-Tar Epoxy** | Water + chemical barrier | **200 – 300 µm** | Submerged piles, pipelines |
| **Zinc Silicate (Inorganic)** | Cathodic | **75 – 100 µm** | High-temp structures |
| **Metallic Spray (Galvanising, Al)** | Cathodic + barrier | **85 – 150 µm** | Heavy industrial |

## 2.2 The Generic Multi-Layer Coating System

A professional coating system is **not a single layer** — it is a stack:

$$\text{Total System} = \text{Surface Preparation} \rightarrow \text{Primer} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate (optional)} \rightarrow \text{Topcoat}$$

| Layer | Function | Typical DFT |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Preparation** | Removes rust, mill scale, laitance | Sa 2½, St 3, etc. |
| **Primer** | Adhesion + first defence (sacrificial or inhibitive) | 50 – 100 µm |
| **Intermediate Coat** | Build thickness + barrier (often MIO epoxy) | 100 – 150 µm |
| **Topcoat** | UV, weather, aesthetics | 50 – 75 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU favourite):** For a typical ISO 12944 **C4 environment**, total DFT for an epoxy-polyurethane system is **≈ 240 – 320 µm** providing **15 – 20 years** of service.

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorise these. They appear directly in numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DFT = WFT \times \frac{\% \text{ Solids by Volume}}{100}$ | Wet-to-Dry film thickness conversion | µm |
| 2 | $\text{Theoretical Coverage} = \frac{\% \text{SV} \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}$ | Area covered per litre | m²/L |
| 3 | $\text{Practical Coverage} = \text{Theoretical} \times (1 - L)$ | L = loss factor (0.10 – 0.40) | m²/L |
| 4 | $\text{Volume Solids \%} = \frac{V_{dry}}{V_{wet}} \times 100$ | Determines shrinkage on drying | % |
| 5 | $VOC = \frac{1000 \times \rho \times (100 - \%SV_w)}{100}$ | Volatile organic compound content | g/L |
| 6 | $\text{Adhesion (Pull-off)} = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}$ | F = force, d = dolly diameter | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 7 | $P_{osm} = iCRT$ | Osmotic pressure (van 't Hoff) | Pa |
| 8 | $\% \text{Elongation} = \frac{L_{final} - L_{initial}}{L_{initial}} \times 100$ | Coating flexibility | % |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Viscosity (Newtonian) | Pa·s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown tables** — use $\mid$ or \vert to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Rehabilitation:** ZRE primer + Epoxy MIO + Aliphatic PU (KTU frequently asked).
- **Marine Piling:** Glass-flake polyester or coal-tar epoxy (200 – 500 µm).
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Solvent-free epoxy or PU (NSF/ANSI 61 certified for potable water).
- **Car Park Decks:** PU or polyurea with quartz broadcast for slip resistance.
- **Chemical Plants:** Vinyl ester or novolac epoxy for acid resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet Film to Dry Film Thickness Relationship

**Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** A coating has **60% volume solids**. The wet film thickness measured with a comb gauge is **200 µm**. What is the **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)**?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental relationship.**

When a wet coating dries, the solvent evaporates, leaving only the solid (non-volatile) portion behind. The DFT is therefore the **fraction of solids (by volume) multiplied by the WFT**.

$$
DFT = WFT \times V_s
$$

Where $V_s$ is the volume fraction of solids (not the percentage).

**Step 2 — Convert the percentage to a fraction.**

Given: Volume Solids = 60 %

$$
V_s = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values into the aligned equation block.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFT &= WFT \times V_s \\
DFT &= 200 \,\mu m \times 0.60 \\
DFT &= 120 \,\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> [!TIP]
> The DFT is **40% lower** than the WFT because 40% of the wet film (solvents) evaporated. This is why specifications always quote **DFT** (not WFT) — WFT is variable on site, but DFT is the *true* protective thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Coverage Area

**Problem:** A paint with **65% volume solids** is specified at a DFT of **150 µm**. Calculate the **theoretical coverage** per litre of paint.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Volume of paint in 1 litre.**

$$
V_{paint} = 1 \, L = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3 = 1\,000\,000 \, mm^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of dry film produced.**

Only the solids remain; 65% of 1 L is dry film.

$$
V_{dry} = 1\,000\,000 \, mm^3 \times 0.65 = 650\,000 \, mm^3
$$

**Step 3 — Area covered at specified DFT.**

$$
A = \frac{V_{dry}}{DFT} = \frac{650\,000 \, mm^3}{150 \,\mu m}
$$

Convert units consistently: $150 \, \mu m = 0.15 \, mm$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{650\,000 \, mm^3}{0.15 \, mm} \\
A &= 4\,333\,333 \, mm^2 \\
A &= 4.33 \, m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the standard "shortcut" formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coverage (m}^2/\text{L)} &= \frac{\% SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)} \\
&= \frac{65 \times 10}{150} \\
&= \frac{650}{150} \\
&= 4.33 \, m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for practical losses (loss factor L = 0.20).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{practical} &= A \times (1 - L) \\
&= 4.33 \times 0.80 \\
&= 3.47 \, m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The loss factor accounts for wastage, overspray, brush retention, surface roughness absorption. For rough or complex steel surfaces, $L$ can be **0.30 – 0.40**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mix Ratio and Pot Life Calculations

**Problem:** A two-pack epoxy primer is mixed in the ratio **4 : 1 by volume** (Base : Hardener). You have **5 L of base**. How much hardener is required? If the pot life is **45 minutes** and each coat takes **20 minutes per 1 L applied**, what is the maximum volume you can mix at one time to avoid wastage?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Hardener volume from mix ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{hardener} &= V_{base} \times \frac{1}{4} \\
&= 5 \, L \times 0.25 \\
&= 1.25 \, L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mixed volume.**

$$
V_{total} = 5 + 1.25 = 6.25 \, L
$$

**Step 3 — Time available to apply the mix.**

Apply within pot life:

$$
t_{available} = 45 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Application rate constraint.**

Application consumes **20 minutes per 1 L**:

$$
\text{Max mixable volume} = \frac{45 \, \text{min}}{20 \, \text{min}/L} = 2.25 \, L
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> You should mix a **maximum of 2.25 L at a time** to use the entire mix before pot life expires. If you mix the full 6.25 L, the leftover will harden in the bucket and be wasted.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Adhesion Strength by Pull-Off Test

**Problem (ASTM D4541):** A 20 mm dolly is pulled off a coated concrete substrate. The maximum tensile force recorded is **6.5 kN**. Calculate the pull-off adhesion strength and interpret the failure mode (substrate failure at 1.8 MPa is to be assumed).

### Solution

**Step 1 — Pull-off strength formula.**

$$
\sigma = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

Force $F = 6.5 \, kN = 6500 \, N$, diameter $d = 20 \, mm$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{4 \times 6500}{\pi \times (20)^2} \\
\sigma &= \frac{26\,000}{400 \pi} \\
\sigma &= \frac{26\,000}{1256.64} \\
\sigma &= 20.69 \, N/mm^2 \; \text{(or MPa)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with substrate strength.**

Since the calculated pull-off strength (20.69 MPa) is **much greater** than the assumed substrate tensile strength (1.8 MPa), the failure has occurred **in the concrete substrate**, not at the coating-substrate interface. This is recorded as **"Substrate (Concrete) Failure"** — the **best possible** result, because it proves the bond is stronger than the substrate itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Mode Classification (ASTM D4541):**
> - **A** – Adhesive failure (coating ↔ substrate) → Poor bond.
> - **B** – Cohesive failure in primer.
> - **C** – Cohesive failure in topcoat.
> - **D** – Adhesive failure between coats.
> - **Y** – Substrate (concrete) failure → **Best case, coating is sound.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Coating Cost Estimation (Board-Favourite)

**Problem:** A cylindrical water tank (diameter **6 m**, height **4 m**) needs an internal epoxy lining. DFT required = **250 µm**, Volume Solids = **70%**, Loss factor = 0.25. Epoxy cost = **₹ 380 per litre**. Calculate total material cost.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Internal surface area of cylinder (open top/bottom).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi D H \\
A &= \pi \times 6 \times 4 \\
A &= 75.40 \, m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical coverage per litre.**

$$
C_T = \frac{70 \times 10}{250} = 2.80 \, m^2/L
$$

**Step 3 — Practical coverage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 2.80 \times (1 - 0.25) \\
C_P &= 2.10 \, m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total paint quantity required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{paint} &= \frac{A}{C_P} = \frac{75.40}{2.10} \\
V_{paint} &= 35.90 \, L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= 35.90 \times 380 \\
\text{Cost} &= ₹\,13\,642
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always add **10 – 15%** as contingency for spillage and rework: revised cost ≈ ₹ **15,000 – 15,700**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Coating Selection by Exposure

| Exposure Condition | Concrete Coating | Steel Coating |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric (urban)** | Acrylic anti-carbonation | ZRE + Epoxy + PU |
| **Marine / splash zone** | PU or polyurea | Glass-flake epoxy (500 µm) |
| **Chemical (acid)** | Vinyl ester | Vinyl ester / novolac epoxy |
| **Potable water** | Solvent-free epoxy (NSF) | Solvent-free epoxy |
| **Underground** | Coal-tar epoxy | Coal-tar epoxy + tape wrap |
| **High temperature** | Silicone | Zinc-silicate (inorganic) |
| **UV-exposed** | Aliphatic PU or acrylic | Aliphatic PU / fluoropolymer |
| **Wastewater / sewage** | Polyurea | Polyurethane or vinyl ester |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Coating System Architecture (Layered Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate: Concrete or Steel] --> B[Step 1: Surface Preparation]
    B --> B1[Concrete: Grinding, Shotblast, Acid Etch]
    B --> B2[Steel: SSPC-SP10 Sa 2.5 Commercial Blast]
    B1 --> C[Step 2: Primer Application]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Concrete: Acrylic or Epoxy Penetrating Primer]
    C --> C2[Steel: Zinc-Rich or Inhibitive Epoxy Primer]
    C1 --> D[Step 3: Intermediate Coat if Required]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Concrete: Body Coat or Filler Putty]
    D --> D2[Steel: Epoxy MIO Barrier Coat]
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Topcoat Application]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Concrete: PU or Acrylic Finish]
    E --> E2[Steel: Aliphatic PU or Fluoropolymer]
    E1 --> F[Step 5: Inspection and DFT Verification]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Step 6: Curing and Handover]
    G --> H[Protected Structure with 15 to 25 Year Service Life]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selection of Protective Coating

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Substrate and Environment] --> Q1{Substrate Type}
    Q1 -- Concrete --> QC1{Exposure Type}
    QC1 -- Carbonation --> A1[Acrylic Anti-Carbonation]
    QC1 -- Chemical Attack --> A2[Vinyl Ester or Novolac Epoxy]
    QC1 -- Waterproofing --> A3[Polyurea or PU]
    QC1 -- Negative Side Damp --> A4[Cementitious Crystalline]
    QC1 -- UV and Weather --> A5[Aliphatic PU or Acrylic]
    Q1 -- Steel --> QS1{Corrosivity ISO 12944}
    QS1 -- C2 to C3 --> B1[Epoxy Polyurethane System]
    QS1 -- C4 to C5 --> B2[Zinc Rich Epoxy MIO Polyurethane]
    QS1 -- Immersed --> B3[Glass Flake or Coal Tar Epoxy]
    QS1 -- High Temp --> B4[Zinc Silicate Inorganic]
    QS1 -- Aesthetic Long Life --> B5[Fluoropolymer Topcoat]
    A1 --> Done[Final Specification and DFT Check]
    A2 --> Done
    A3 --> Done
    A4 --> Done
    A5 --> Done
    B1 --> Done
    B2 --> Done
    B3 --> Done
    B4 --> Done
    B5 --> Done
```

## 4.3 Failure Modes of Protective Coatings (Diagnostic Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Fail[Coating Defect Observed] --> Q1{Type of Defect}
    Q1 -- Blisters --> B1[Trapped Moisture or Solvent]
    B1 --> B2[Remedy: Remove Allow to Dry Reapply]
    Q1 -- Cracking --> C1[Substrate Movement or Over Thickness]
    C1 --> C2[Remedy: Flexible Coating or Remove and Redo]
    Q1 -- Delamination --> D1[Poor Surface Prep or Contamination]
    D1 --> D2[Remedy: Abrade Clean and Reapply]
    Q1 -- Pinholes --> P1[Trapped Air or Solvent Pop]
    P1 --> P2[Remedy: Thin First Coat as Seal Coat]
    Q1 -- Chalking --> CH1[UV Degradation of Topcoat]
    CH1 --> CH2[Remedy: Recoat with UV Resistant Topcoat]
    Q1 -- Rust Staining --> R1[Substrate Failure or DFT Below Spec]
    R1 --> R2[Remedy: Full Blasting and Reapplication]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Major Coating Resin Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Acrylic]
        A1[Water Based]
        A2[UV Resistant]
        A3[Breathable]
    end
    subgraph B[Polyurethane]
        B1[High Abrasion]
        B2[UV Stable Aliphatic]
        B3[Flexible]
    end
    subgraph C[Epoxy]
        C1[High Chemical Resistance]
        C2[Hard but Brittle]
        C3[Two Pack]
    end
    subgraph D[Polyurea]
        D1[Fast Cure]
        D2[Elongation over 300 percent]
        D3[Crack Bridging]
    end
    A --> Choice{Use Case}
    B --> Choice
    C --> Choice
    D --> Choice
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define protective coating. List any two important properties of an ideal protective coating for concrete.*

**Model Answer:**
A protective coating is a thin, continuous, adherent layer applied to the surface of a structure to act as a **barrier against the ingress of aggressive agents** such as chlorides, CO₂, water, sulphates, and UV radiation, thereby preventing deterioration of the substrate.

**Two important properties of an ideal protective coating for concrete:**

1. **Adhesion strength ≥ 1.5 MPa** to the substrate, ensuring the coating does not delaminate under service conditions.
2. **Water-vapour permeability (breathability)** to allow the concrete to release trapped moisture without blistering the film.

*(Any other two valid points: chemical resistance, UV stability, elongation >100%, abrasion resistance, crack-bridging ability, etc.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between barrier protection and sacrificial (cathodic) protection in steel coatings. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Barrier Protection | Sacrificial (Cathodic) Protection |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Physically isolates steel from environment | Zinc corrodes preferentially, protecting steel |
| Failure mode | Once breached, corrosion begins | Protects even at small scratches |
| Example | **Epoxy** or **Polyurethane** topcoat | **Zinc-rich primer (ZRE/ZRO)** |

Zinc acts as the **anode** and dissolves, leaving the steel cathodic and protected. The barrier coating is non-conductive and protects only as long as it remains intact. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of protective coatings for concrete** based on resin chemistry. For each type, mention its typical application area, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reinforced concrete roof slab of a building is exposed to a **severe marine atmosphere** (within 1 km of the coast). Suggest a **complete multi-layer protective coating system** for the soffit. Justify your choice of primer, intermediate coat (if any), and topcoat with reference to the **deterioration mechanisms** to be resisted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q A (a)

**Classification of protective coatings for concrete:**

1. **Acrylic Coatings** – Thermoplastic, water-based, UV-resistant, breathable. *Application:* External façades, anti-carbonation. *Advantage:* Easy re-coating. *Limitation:* Low chemical resistance.

2. **Epoxy Coatings** – Two-pack, Bisphenol-A based. *Application:* Floors, water tanks, chemical plants. *Advantage:* Excellent chemical and abrasion resistance. *Limitation:* UV-degrades (chalks); not vapour-permeable.

3. **Polyurethane (PU) Coatings** – Aliphatic isocyanate + polyol. *Application:* Roofs, car park decks, podiums. *Advantage:* UV-stable, abrasion-resistant, flexible. *Limitation:* Sensitive to moisture during cure.

4. **Polyurea Coatings** – Spray-applied, fast-cure, very high elongation (>300%). *Application:* Waterproofing of large roof areas, secondary containment. *Advantage:* Crack-bridging. *Limitation:* Requires special plural-component spray equipment.

5. **Cementitious Polymer-Modified Coatings** – Cement + acrylic/SBR latex. *Application:* Reservoirs, basements (negative-side waterproofing). *Advantage:* Breathable, can be applied on damp surfaces. *Limitation:* Lower flexibility.

6. **Bituminous / Coal-Tar Epoxy Coatings** – Black, water-resistant. *Application:* Sub-structures, foundations. *Advantage:* Excellent water barrier. *Limitation:* Not UV-stable, dark colour.

7. **Silane / Siloxane Impregnations** – Colourless, hydrophobic, water-repellent. *Application:* Façades, parking structures. *Advantage:* Invisible, vapour-permeable. *Limitation:* No film build; not for cracked surfaces.

8. **Anti-graffiti Coatings** – Sacrificial (wax) or permanent (fluoropolymer). *Application:* Public buildings. *Advantage:* Easy cleaning. *Limitation:* Periodic re-application for sacrificial types. **(7 marks — full coverage needed)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q A (b)

**Marine atmosphere (within 1 km of coast) → ISO 12944 corrosivity category C4 / C5-M.**

Deterioration mechanisms to resist:
- **Chloride ingress** → rebar corrosion.
- **Carbonation** (CO₂ diffusion).
- **UV degradation** of topcoat.
- **Wet-dry cycling** → osmotic and thermal stresses.

**Recommended Multi-Layer Coating System for the Concrete Soffit:**

| Layer | Material | DFT | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Prep** | Grinding + dust removal; repair spalls with polymer-modified mortar | – | Removes laitance, ensures sound substrate |
| **Primer** | Solvent-free penetrating epoxy primer | **80 µm** | Seals pores, excellent adhesion, chloride-blocking |
| **Intermediate** | Epoxy MIO build coat (optional) | **150 µm** | Adds barrier thickness in lamellar structure |
| **Topcoat** | Aliphatic polyurethane (UV-stable) | **75 µm** | UV resistance + colour retention + abrasion |

**Justification Summary:**
- Solvent-free epoxy primer penetrates the capillary pores, displaces moisture, and provides a chloride barrier — essential in marine atmosphere.
- The aliphatic PU topcoat resists UV, retains colour, and sheds water.
- Total system DFT ≈ **230 – 300 µm**, designed for **15 – 20 years** of maintenance-free service in C4/C5-M environment.

> [!NOTE]
> *Alternative for cost reduction:* A high-build acrylic anti-carbonation + PU topcoat can be used for non-load-critical elements. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **types of protective coatings for steel structures** with reference to **ISO 12944 corrosivity categories**. Sketch a typical three-coat system and label each layer with its function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bridge girder is to be protected by a coating system. The system specified has **WFT = 300 µm**, **volume solids = 60%**, and the area to be coated is **500 m²**. Calculate (i) the **DFT**, (ii) the **theoretical coverage per litre**, and (iii) the **total quantity of paint required** if the loss factor is **0.30**. Take the cost of paint as **₹ 450 per litre** and compute the total material cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q B (a)

**Types of Steel Coatings (Linked to ISO 12944):**

ISO 12944 classifies atmospheric corrosivity as:
- **C1** – Very low (heated interiors)
- **C2** – Low (rural)
- **C3** – Medium (urban/industrial)
- **C4** – High (industrial/coastal)
- **C5-I** – Very high industrial
- **C5-M** – Very high marine

**Coating types mapped to categories:**

| Coating | Mechanism | Recommended Category |
|---|---|---|
| Alkyd primer + alkyd topcoat | Barrier (low build) | C1, C2 |
| Epoxy primer + PU topcoat | Barrier | C3, C4 |
| Zinc-rich epoxy primer + Epoxy MIO + PU | Cathodic + barrier | C4, C5 |
| Zinc silicate (inorganic) | Cathodic (high temp) | C5-I, high temp |
| Glass-flake polyester | Heavy barrier (immersed) | Im1, Im2 (water/soil) |

**Typical Three-Coat System Sketch (for C4):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │  Topcoat: Aliphatic PU       │   ← UV, weather, aesthetics (50–75 µm)
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │  Intermediate: Epoxy MIO     │   ← Barrier build (100–150 µm)
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │  Primer: Zinc-Rich Epoxy     │   ← Cathodic + adhesion (75 µm)
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │  Substrate: Blast-cleaned    │
   │  Steel (Sa 2½, profile 75 µm)│
   └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Functions:**
- **Primer** = sacrificial (zinc) + adhesion promoter.
- **Intermediate** = barrier, builds DFT, MIO platelets block moisture path.
- **Topcoat** = UV protection, colour, gloss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q B (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- WFT = 300 µm
- Volume Solids (%SV) = 60%
- Area $A = 500 \, m^2$
- Loss factor $L = 0.30$
- Cost = ₹ 450 / L

**(i) Dry Film Thickness (DFT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DFT &= WFT \times \frac{\%SV}{100} \\
DFT &= 300 \times 0.60 \\
DFT &= 180 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Coverage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \frac{\%SV \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)} \\
C_T &= \frac{60 \times 10}{180} \\
C_T &= \frac{600}{180} \\
C_T &= 3.333 \, m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Practical Coverage and Total Quantity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= C_T \times (1 - L) \\
C_P &= 3.333 \times 0.70 \\
C_P &= 2.333 \, m^2/L
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{paint} &= \frac{A}{C_P} = \frac{500}{2.333} \\
V_{paint} &= 214.3 \, L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= 214.3 \times 450 \\
\text{Cost} &= ₹\,96\,435
\end{aligned}
$$

Add 10% contingency: ₹ **1,06,000 (approx.)**. **(7 marks — full steps required)**

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WFT with DFT** — Always quote the answer in **DFT** for specifications; the question often gives WFT to test your understanding.
> 2. **Forgetting the loss factor** — A coverage calculation *without* the loss factor is incomplete. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong resin → wrong environment mapping** — Epoxy is *not* used as a topcoat outdoors (it chalks). Marked wrong.
> 4. **Not mentioning surface preparation** — A coating system *starts with* surface prep. Sa 2½ for steel, grinding/etching for concrete. Omitting this = lost mark.
> 5. **Ignoring pot life and induction time** for two-pack systems. Mixing the entire can at once is a practical error that examiners note.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** in numericals (mixing mm with µm). Always convert to a single unit first.
> 7. **No mention of standards (ISO 12944 / ASTM / SSPC)** — Even a one-line reference shows examiner awareness and earns goodwill marks.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullets:**
>
> - Protective coating = **barrier** between substrate and environment.
> - **Concrete coatings** = breathable, vapour-permeable, alkali-resistant, crack-bridging.
> - **Steel coatings** = three layers: **Zinc primer → Epoxy MIO body → PU/Fluoropolymer topcoat**.
> - **Zinc = sacrificial (cathodic)**; Epoxy/PU = barrier; Silane = hydrophobic impregnation.
> - **DFT = WFT × (%SV / 100)**.
> - **Theoretical Coverage = (%SV × 10) / DFT** in m²/L.
> - **Practical Coverage = Theoretical × (1 − L)**.
> - **Adhesion strength** ≥ **1.5 MPa** (pull-off test, ASTM D4541).
> - **Surface prep grades (Steel):** Sa 1, Sa 2, **Sa 2½** (most common), Sa 3 (white metal).
> - **Surface prep (Concrete):** grinding, shot-blasting, acid-etch, water-blasting.
> - **ISO 12944** corrosivity: **C1 → C5-M**.
