> - **Failure modes:** blistering (moisture), cracking (over-thickness/substrate movement), delamination (poor prep), chalking (UV), pinholing (solvent pop), rust staining (DFT below spec).
> - **Pot life** of two-pack systems must be respected — mix only what can be used.
> - Always specify **DFT**, not WFT.
> - Always add **10–15% contingency** in cost estimates.
> - Mention relevant **standards** in your answer (ISO 12944, ASTM D4541, SSPC-SP10, ASTM B117) — earns easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FRP Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** FRP Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of FRP Sheets

**Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Sheets** are high-performance composite laminates manufactured by embedding continuous, high-strength fibers within a polymeric resin matrix. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET634), FRP sheets are formally classified as **externally bonded reinforcement systems** used to restore or upgrade the load-carrying capacity of distressed reinforced concrete (RC), prestressed concrete (PSC), timber, and masonry structural elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET634 – Module 3):**
> FRP sheets are defined as composite laminates consisting of unidirectional or bidirectional fiber fabrics impregnated with a thermosetting resin, applied externally to structural members via the *wet lay-up* (hand lay-up) or *pre-preg* (pre-impregnated) technique to enhance flexural, shear, axial, and confinement performance.

The composite behaves as a **pseudo-isotropic or anisotropic material** depending on fiber orientation, where the **fiber phase** carries tensile loads and the **matrix phase** transfers stresses between fibers and protects them from environmental degradation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Fighter Jet Skin" Model

Imagine a **fighter aircraft fuselage**. The aircraft skin is not a single thick metal plate; instead, it is a thin **composite laminate** made of carbon fibers embedded in an epoxy matrix. Although the laminate is only a few millimeters thick, its tensile strength rivals that of structural steel. The fibers act like the *steel reinforcement* in concrete (carrying tensile stress), while the epoxy resin is analogous to the *concrete matrix* (holding fibers together, transferring shear, and protecting them).

In structural rehabilitation, we essentially **"wrap"** or **"bond"** this aircraft-grade skin onto a damaged concrete beam, column, or slab. The result is a hybrid system where:

- **Old concrete** = the load-carrying core
- **FRP sheet** = the externally bonded high-strength skin

This analogy immediately clarifies *why* FRP sheets are so popular — high strength, **low thickness** (typically 1 mm to 5 mm), and **negligible added dead weight**.

---

## 1.3 Primary Constituents of FRP Sheets

> [!NOTE]
> FRP sheets are **two-phase composite materials**. Their structural behaviour is governed by the synergy between the **reinforcement (fibers)** and the **binder (matrix/resin)**.

### (a) Fiber Phase (Reinforcement)

| Fiber Type | Symbol | Key Property | Typical Application |
|------------|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| Carbon | CFRP | Highest strength & stiffness | Critical flexural strengthening |
| Glass | GFRP | Economical, good durability | General-purpose repair |
| Aramid | AFRP | High impact & fatigue resistance | Blast/impact retrofits |

### (b) Matrix Phase (Resin Binder)

| Resin Type | Curing Mode | Key Property |
|------------|-------------|--------------|
| Epoxy | Ambient / Elevated | Best adhesion, low shrinkage |
| Polyester | Ambient | Low cost, moderate durability |
| Vinyl Ester | Ambient | Superior chemical resistance |

---

## 1.4 KTU-Recognized Forms of FRP Reinforcement

In Module 3, the following forms are tested:

1. **FRP Sheets / Fabrics** — unidirectional or bidirectional dry fabrics impregnated on-site.
2. **FRP Laminates** — factory-cured rigid strips (pultruded).
3. **FRP Bars / Rods** — used as near-surface mounted (NSM) reinforcement.
4. **FRP Plates** — thick factory-bonded plates (rare in current KTU syllabus).

The focus of this note is **FRP Sheets (wet lay-up systems)**.

---

## 1.5 Why FRP Sheets? – Driving Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantages Driving KTU-Emphasized Adoption:**
> - **High tensile strength** (typically 2 to 10 times that of mild steel).
> - **Corrosion immunity** (no rusting like steel plates).
> - **Lightweight** — density ranges from **1200 kg/m³ to 2000 kg/m³**.
> - **Minimal sectional enlargement** (preserves architectural clearances).
> - **Rapid installation** with minimal disruption.
> - **Excellent fatigue performance** under cyclic loading.

These drivers justify FRP sheets as the *default external strengthening solution* in modern repair practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Micro-Mechanical Behavior of FRP Sheets

The mechanical response of an FRP sheet is **linear elastic up to brittle failure** — there is *no yielding plateau* like that of mild steel. This fundamental behavioural difference shapes all design equations.

The effective tensile stress in the FRP at any strain level is governed by **Hooke's Law for unidirectional composites**:

$$f_f = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_f$$

Where:
- $f_f$ = tensile stress in the FRP sheet (in MPa or N/mm²).
- $E_f$ = modulus of elasticity of the FRP sheet (in GPa or MPa).
- $\varepsilon_f$ = strain developed in the FRP sheet.

Because FRP behaves **linearly elastic until rupture**, ultimate design must always be controlled by **strain limits**, not by stress limits alone.

---

## 2.2 Governing Design Philosophy (ACI 440.2R – Referenced by KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the **ACI 440.2R-17** "Guide for the Design and Construction of Externally Bonded FRP Systems for Strengthening Concrete Structures."

The design philosophy rests on three cornerstones:

1. **Strain Compatibility** — strain in the FRP equals strain in the substrate concrete at the bonded interface (plane-sections-remain-plane assumption for flexural members).
2. **Force Equilibrium** — internal force in FRP plus internal forces in steel and concrete equal zero.
3. **Strength Reduction** — design strengths are reduced using partial safety factors ($\psi_f = 0.85$ for FRP).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master equation set** required for solving any KTU Module 3 numerical problem on FRP sheets.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area of FRP sheet | $A_f = t_f \cdot w_f$ | mm² | $t_f$ = thickness, $w_f$ = width |
| 2 | Effective tensile stress in FRP | $f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$ | MPa | Hooke's law for composite |
| 3 | Design tensile force in FRP | $T_f = A_f \cdot f_{fe} \cdot \psi_f$ | N | $\psi_f = 0.85$ |
| 4 | Effective strain in FRP (flexural) | $\varepsilon_{fe} = \varepsilon_{cu} \cdot \dfrac{h - c}{c}$ | — | $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.003$ for concrete |
| 5 | Effective strain in FRP (axial wrap) | $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004 \le 0.75 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$ | — | Confinement-limited |
| 6 | Effective bond length | $l_e = \dfrac{23300}{(n_f \cdot t_f)^{0.58}}$ | mm | $n_f$ = number of plies |
| 7 | Maximum allowable strain (debond control) | $\varepsilon_{fd} = \kappa_m \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} \le 0.90 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$ | — | $\kappa_m$ = stiffness factor |
| 8 | Bond strength (shear at interface) | $\tau_b = 0.427 \cdot \beta_w \cdot \sqrt{f'_c}$ | MPa | $\beta_w$ = width ratio factor |
| 9 | Additional flexural capacity (FRP) | $\Delta M_n = T_f \cdot (h - c + y_f)$ | N·mm | $y_f$ = distance from FRP centroid to tension face |

**Width Ratio Correction:**

$$\beta_w = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 - w_f / s}{1 + w_f / s}}$$

Where $s$ = spacing between FRP strips (for discontinuous sheets).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Rehabilitation

FRP sheets are deployed in the following **KTU-favoured scenarios**:

- **Flexural Strengthening of Beams & Slabs** — bonded to the tension face.
- **Shear Strengthening of Beams** — U-wrap or full wrap on sides.
- **Column Confinement** — circular or rectangular wraps for axial load + ductility enhancement.
- **Beam-Column Joint Retrofitting** — L-shaped wraps.
- **Slab Punching-Shear Strengthening** — radial sheet layout around columns.
- **Bridge Girder Upgrades** — increase live-load capacity.

---

## 2.5 Failure Modes of FRP-Strengthened Members (KTU-Exam-Relevant)

> [!WARNING]
> Failure of FRP-strengthened members is governed by the **weakest link** — usually NOT the FRP itself. Students often wrongly assume FRP rupture is the design limit state.

| Failure Mode | Trigger | Brittle? |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| FRP Rupture | Tension-side strain exceeds $\varepsilon_{fu}$ | Yes |
| Concrete Crushing | Compression-side strain exceeds $\varepsilon_{cu}$ | Yes |
| FRP Debonding (IC debonding) | Interface shear > bond strength | Yes (most common) |
| Cover Delamination (CDC) | Tension-induced horizontal crack propagation | Yes |
| End Anchorage Failure | Insufficient development length $l_e$ | Yes |

**Debonding is the governing limit state** in over 70% of practical designs — this is a recurring KTU examination point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Tensile Force in an FRP Sheet (Flexural Case)

### Statement
Derive the expression for the effective tensile force $T_f$ developed in a single-ply FRP sheet bonded to the tension face of an RC beam, assuming **plane sections remain plane** and the concrete in compression attains its ultimate strain $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.003$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Strain Compatibility at Bonded Interface**

By the assumption of plane sections, the linear strain profile extends to the FRP bonded at the extreme tension fibre. Therefore:

$$\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_c \cdot \dfrac{c - d_f}{c}$$

Here, $c$ = neutral axis depth, $d_f$ = depth to centroid of FRP sheet, and $\varepsilon_c$ = strain at the extreme compression fibre.

**Step 2 — Apply Concrete Crushing Limit**

At ultimate flexural capacity, concrete reaches its usable strain limit. For normal-weight concrete:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{cu} = 0.003$$

Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot \dfrac{c - d_f}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law for the FRP**

The tensile stress in the FRP corresponding to this strain:

$$f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Tensile Force**

Multiplying by the effective cross-sectional area of the FRP sheet:

$$T_{f,\text{nom}} = A_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the FRP Strength Reduction Factor**

Following ACI 440.2R, a reduction factor $\psi_f = 0.85$ is applied:

$$T_f = \psi_f \cdot A_f \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{T_f = 0.85 \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot E_f \cdot 0.003 \cdot \dfrac{c - d_f}{c}}$$

This is the canonical KTU-formula for the FRP tensile force in a flexurally strengthened beam.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Bond Length

### Statement
Show that for a bonded FRP sheet, there exists an **effective bond length** $l_e$ beyond which additional length does not increase the force-transfer capacity.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Interfacial Shear Lag**

Bond between FRP and concrete is governed by interfacial shear stress $\tau(x)$ which transfers load from FRP to concrete. The governing differential equation (shear-lag model) is:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \sigma_f}{dx^2} - \dfrac{\tau(x)}{t_f \cdot E_f} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume Elastic-Perfectly-Brittle Bond**

If the bond stress reaches its peak $\tau_b$ and remains constant (calibrated from test data):

$$\dfrac{d^2 \sigma_f}{dx^2} = \dfrac{\tau_b}{t_f \cdot E_f}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Twice**

Applying the boundary condition $\sigma_f(\infty) \ne \infty$ and $\sigma_f(0) = 0$:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \tau_b \cdot E_f \cdot x}{t_f}}$$

**Step 4 — Define Effective Length**

The effective length is defined where 97% of ultimate load is mobilised:

$$\sigma_f(l_e) = 0.97 \cdot f_{fu}$$

Solving for $l_e$:

$$\boxed{l_e = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot f_{fu}^2 \cdot t_f}{\tau_b \cdot E_f}}$$

Empirically fitted (ACI 440.2R):

$$l_e = \dfrac{23300}{(n_f \cdot t_f)^{0.58}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — A Typical 7-Mark KTU Sub-Question

**Problem:** An RC beam of width $b = 250$ mm and effective depth $d = 450$ mm is strengthened with a single-ply CFRP sheet of thickness $t_f = 1.2$ mm, modulus $E_f = 165$ GPa, and ultimate strain $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.012$. Given $f'_c = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa, $A_s = 942$ mm² (4 bars of 20 mm dia), and neutral axis depth $c = 110$ mm, determine the additional flexural capacity $\Delta M_n$ contributed by the FRP sheet. Assume $d_f = 470$ mm (sheet placed at tension face).

### Solution

**Step 1 — Effective Strain in FRP**

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \varepsilon_{cu} \cdot \dfrac{d_f - c}{c}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot \dfrac{470 - 110}{110}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot 3.273 = 0.00982$$

**Step 2 — Check Debond Strain Limit**

$$\varepsilon_{fd} = 0.90 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.90 \cdot 0.012 = 0.0108$$

Since $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.00982 \le \varepsilon_{fd} = 0.0108$, **debonding does not govern.**

**Step 3 — Effective Stress in FRP**

$$f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$$

$$f_{fe} = 165{,}000 \cdot 0.00982 = 1620 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Tensile Force in FRP**

Assuming sheet width $w_f = 200$ mm:

$$A_f = t_f \cdot w_f = 1.2 \cdot 200 = 240 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$T_f = 0.85 \cdot A_f \cdot f_{fe}$$

$$T_f = 0.85 \cdot 240 \cdot 1620 = 330{,}480 \text{ N} \approx 330.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Moment Arm**

Lever arm of the FRP force about the compression centroid:

$$y_f = d_f - \beta_1 \cdot c / 2$$

For $f'_c = 25$ MPa, $\beta_1 = 0.85$ (ACI):

$$y_f = 470 - 0.85 \cdot 110 / 2 = 470 - 46.75 = 423.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Additional Flexural Capacity**

$$\Delta M_n = T_f \cdot y_f$$

$$\Delta M_n = 330{,}480 \cdot 423.25 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\Delta M_n = 1.398 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 139.8 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\Delta M_n \approx 139.8 \text{ kN·m}}$$

This represents the **FRP-only contribution** to flexural capacity — a typical KTU 7-mark numerical expects you to compute this and compare against the unstrengthened beam moment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FRP Strengthening System

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **component interactions** of an FRP-strengthened RC member — from constituent materials to the final strengthened element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fibers
        F1[CARBON FIBER]
        F2[GLASS FIBER]
        F3[ARAMID FIBER]
    end
    subgraph Matrix
        R1[EPOXY RESIN]
        R2[POLYESTER RESIN]
        R3[VINYL ESTER RESIN]
    end
    F1 --> COMPOSITE[FRP SHEET LAMINATE]
    F2 --> COMPOSITE
    F3 --> COMPOSITE
    R1 --> COMPOSITE
    R2 --> COMPOSITE
    R3 --> COMPOSITE
    COMPOSITE --> SYS1[FLEXURAL STRENGTHENING]
    COMPOSITE --> SYS2[SHEAR STRENGTHENING]
    COMPOSITE --> SYS3[AXIAL CONFINEMENT]
    COMPOSITE --> SYS4[DUCTILITY UPGRADE]
    COMPOSITE --> SYS5[IMPACT / BLAST RETROFIT]
    SYS1 --> OUT1[BEAM TENSION FACE BOND]
    SYS2 --> OUT2[U-WRAP OR FULL WRAP]
    SYS3 --> OUT3[CIRCULAR COLUMN CONFINEMENT]
    SYS4 --> OUT4[PLASTIC HINGE ENCASEMENT]
    SYS5 --> OUT5[JOINT OR WALL ENCASEMENT]
```

---

## 4.2 Installation / Application Process Flow

The wet lay-up installation sequence is tested in KTU Part A. Below is the **block-level process architecture**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SUBSTRATE INSPECTION] --> A2[SURFACE PREPARATION GROOVING DUST REMOVAL]
    A2 --> A3[CRACK INJECTION IF REQUIRED]
    A3 --> A4[PRIMER APPLICATION]
    A4 --> A5[PUTTY FOR SURFACE LEVELLING]
    A5 --> A6[RESIN APPLICATION FIRST COAT]
    A6 --> A7[FRP SHEET PLACEMENT]
    A7 --> A8[DEBUBBLING WITH ROLLER]
    A8 --> A9[SECOND RESIN COAT SATURATION]
    A9 --> A10[FIBERGLASS MAT OR FINISHING COAT IF NEEDED]
    A10 --> A11[CURING AMBIENT OR ACCELERATED]
    A11 --> A12[QUALITY CONTROL TESTING]
    A12 --> A13[STRENGTHENED STRUCTURE]
```

---

## 4.3 Typical Strengthening Layouts (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[BEAM FLEXURAL STRENGTHENING] --> L1A[SINGLE PLY AT TENSION FACE]
    L1 --> L1B[MULTIPLE PLIES IF HIGHER CAPACITY REQUIRED]
    L2[BEAM SHEAR STRENGTHENING] --> L2A[SIDE BOND]
    L2 --> L2B[U WRAP]
    L2 --> L2C[FULL WRAP]
    L3[COLUMN CONFINEMENT] --> L3A[CIRCULAR WRAP]
    L3 --> L3B[RECTANGULAR WRAP WITH CORNER RADIUS]
    L4[SLAB STRENGTHENING] --> L4A[UNIDIRECTIONAL STRIPS AT BOTTOM]
    L4 --> L4B[BIDIRECTIONAL FABRIC FOR TWO WAY ACTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams substitute physical drawings (beam cross-sections) with **block-level functional mappings** — a Mermaid-safe approach for complex geometry. The actual physical layout is taught in design classes; the KTU examination focuses on the *decision logic* of *which layout for which problem*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECET634]**

**Q:** Define FRP sheets. List any four advantages of using FRP sheets over conventional steel plate bonding for strengthening RC structures.

**Model Answer:**

FRP sheets are high-strength composite laminates formed by embedding continuous fibers (carbon, glass, or aramid) within a polymeric resin matrix (typically epoxy). They are bonded externally to structural elements to enhance their load-carrying capacity.

**Advantages over steel plate bonding:**
1. **Corrosion-resistant** — no rusting or deterioration in aggressive environments.
2. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — tensile strength 2–10 times that of mild steel at one-fifth the weight.
3. **Minimal cross-sectional enlargement** — sheet thickness typically 1–3 mm versus 6–12 mm steel plates.
4. **Ease of handling and installation** — flexible fabric conforms to curved surfaces.
5. **Excellent fatigue performance** under cyclic loading.
6. **No need for heavy lifting equipment** during installation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, PECET634 Sample]**

**Q:** Explain the role of the matrix (resin) in an FRP sheet composite. Why is epoxy resin the most commonly used matrix for structural strengthening?

**Model Answer:**

The matrix in an FRP sheet serves four primary roles:
1. **Stress Transfer** — distributes applied loads between adjacent fibers through shear.
2. **Fiber Protection** — shields fibers from moisture, chemicals, abrasion, and UV degradation.
3. **Shape Retention** — maintains fiber alignment and geometric stability.
4. **Crack Bridging** — arrests micro-cracks propagating through the laminate.

**Why epoxy is preferred:**
- **Excellent adhesion** to concrete, steel, and timber substrates.
- **Low shrinkage** during curing → minimal residual stress at the interface.
- **High mechanical strength** (tensile strength ~50–90 MPa).
- **Good chemical resistance** to alkalis, acids, and solvents.
- **Wide range of cure times** — ambient to accelerated curing.
- **Superior glass-transition temperature** ($T_g > 60°$C) for service conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECET634 Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the constituents of an FRP sheet system. Explain in detail the types of fibers used in FRP sheets with their typical mechanical properties.

**(b)** Discuss the various failure modes of an RC beam strengthened with externally bonded FRP sheets. With neat sketches, explain the difference between *intermediate crack (IC) debonding* and *concrete cover delamination (CDC)*.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constituents of an FRP Sheet System (7 Marks)**

FRP sheets are **two-phase composites** comprising:

1. **Fiber Phase (Reinforcement):**
   - Provides the primary tensile and stiffness characteristics.
   - Carries axial loads parallel to fiber direction.
   - Fibers are typically continuous, unidirectional, or bidirectional.

2. **Matrix Phase (Resin Binder):**
   - Transfers loads between fibers through interfacial shear.
   - Protects fibers from environmental attack.
   - Holds fibers in alignment and provides shape.

**Types of Fibers and Their Properties:**

| Fiber Type | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elastic Modulus (GPa) | Density (kg/m³) | Key Feature |
|------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Carbon (CFRP)** | 2400 – 4000 | 150 – 600 | 1600 – 1900 | Highest strength and stiffness |
| **Glass (GFRP)** | 500 – 2500 | 35 – 70 | 1800 – 2100 | Economical, good durability |
| **Aramid (AFRP)** | 2400 – 3600 | 60 – 130 | 1380 – 1450 | High impact and fatigue resistance |

**Specific Fiber Sub-Types:**
- **Carbon:** HS (High Strength), HM (High Modulus), UHM (Ultra-High Modulus).
- **Glass:** E-glass (general purpose), S-glass (high strength), AR-glass (alkali-resistant).
- **Aramid:** Kevlar 49, Twaron.

**(b) Failure Modes (7 Marks)**

The principal failure modes of FRP-strengthened RC beams are:

1. **FRP Rupture:** Tensile stress in FRP exceeds its ultimate strength — occurs when steel yields first.
2. **Concrete Crushing:** Compression-side strain exceeds $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.003$.
3. **IC Debonding (Intermediate Crack Debonding):** A flexural or flexural-shear crack initiates within the concrete and propagates horizontally at the FRP-concrete interface.
4. **CDC (Concrete Cover Delamination):** A horizontal crack propagates within the concrete cover just below the bonded FRP, peeling off the entire cover.
5. **End Debonding:** Premature separation at the FRP termination due to high interfacial shear and normal (peeling) stresses.
6. **FRP Anchor Failure:** Failure at anchorage zones (used as mitigation).

**Distinction Between IC Debonding and CDC:**

| Feature | IC Debonding | CDC |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Crack Origin | Flexural crack in mid-span | Pre-existing horizontal crack |
| Location | At FRP-concrete interface | Within concrete cover |
| Initiation | At flexural/shear crack tip | At weakest plane in cover |
| Detected by | Vertical crack widening | Horizontal peeling sound |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 / Model Paper, PECET634 Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the different methods of application of FRP sheets on RC structures. Describe the *wet lay-up* technique in detail.

**(b)** An RC beam of dimensions $b = 300$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $f'_c = 30$ MPa is strengthened with a single-ply GFRP sheet ($t_f = 1.4$ mm, $w_f = 250$ mm, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.022$). Assuming the neutral axis depth $c = 130$ mm, calculate the additional flexural moment capacity $\Delta M_n$ contributed by the FRP sheet.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Methods of FRP Sheet Application (7 Marks)**

There are three primary methods:

1. **Wet Lay-Up (Hand Lay-Up):**
   - Most common on-site technique.
   - Dry fabric is impregnated with liquid resin directly on the substrate.
   - Steps:
     1. Surface preparation (grinding, cleaning).
     2. Primer application for substrate penetration.
     3. Putty levelling for unevenness.
     4. Saturating first resin coat.
     5. Placing fabric sheet.
     6. Rolling to remove air bubbles.
     7. Final saturating resin coat.
     8. Curing (ambient or accelerated).
   - **Advantages:** No heavy equipment, accommodates complex geometry.
   - **Disadvantages:** Quality control sensitive to site conditions.

2. **Pre-Preg Lay-Up:**
   - Factory-impregnated sheets (with B-staged resin).
   - Applied on-site, then cured under heat/pressure.
   - **Advantages:** Higher fibre-volume fraction, uniform quality.
   - **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, requires heating equipment.

3. **Pultruded Laminate Bonding:**
   - Factory-cured rigid strips.
   - Adhered to substrate using structural adhesive.
   - **Advantages:** Very high fibre volume (~60–70%), high stiffness.
   - **Disadvantages:** Limited to flat surfaces, requires lap-joints.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $f'_c = 30$ MPa
- $t_f = 1.4$ mm, $w_f = 250$ mm, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.022$
- $c = 130$ mm, assume $d_f = d + 30 = 530$ mm (sheet at tension face with 30 mm cover beyond rebar)

**Step 1: Effective Strain in FRP**

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = \varepsilon_{cu} \cdot \dfrac{d_f - c}{c}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot \dfrac{530 - 130}{130}$$

$$\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot 3.077 = 0.009231$$

**Step 2: Debond Strain Limit Check**

$$\varepsilon_{fd} = 0.90 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.90 \cdot 0.022 = 0.0198$$

Since $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.009231 < \varepsilon_{fd} = 0.0198$, **debonding does not govern.**

**Step 3: Effective Stress in FRP**

$$f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} = 70{,}000 \cdot 0.009231 = 646.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Tensile Force in FRP**

$$A_f = t_f \cdot w_f = 1.4 \cdot 250 = 350 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$T_f = \psi_f \cdot A_f \cdot f_{fe} = 0.85 \cdot 350 \cdot 646.2$$

$$T_f = 192{,}245 \text{ N} \approx 192.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Moment Arm**

For $f'_c = 30$ MPa: $\beta_1 = 0.836$ (linear interpolation between 0.85 at $f'_c \le 28$ MPa and 0.80 at $f'_c \ge 55$ MPa).

$$\beta_1 = 0.85 - \dfrac{0.05 \cdot (30 - 28)}{55 - 28} = 0.85 - 0.0037 \approx 0.836$$

$$y_f = d_f - \beta_1 \cdot c / 2 = 530 - 0.836 \cdot 130 / 2$$

$$y_f = 530 - 54.34 = 475.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Additional Flexural Capacity**

$$\Delta M_n = T_f \cdot y_f = 192{,}245 \cdot 475.66 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\Delta M_n = 91{,}432{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta M_n \approx 91.43 \text{ kN·m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent Mark-Deduction Zones — Examiner's Eye:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\psi_f = 0.85$ factor** — students often write $T_f = A_f \cdot f_{fe}$ and lose 1 full mark in numericals.
> 2. **Not checking the debond strain limit** ($\varepsilon_{fd} = 0.90 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$) — if $\varepsilon_{fe}$ exceeds this, you MUST reduce it. KTU examiners expect this check.
> 3. **Confusing IC Debonding with CDC** — these are *not* the same. IC debonding initiates at flexural cracks; CDC involves a horizontal crack within the concrete cover.
> 4. **Using $\varepsilon_{fu}$ directly in design** — you must use **effective strain** $\varepsilon_{fe}$, not ultimate strain $\varepsilon_{fu}$. Maximum 1-mark deduction if confused.
> 5. **Forgetting $\beta_1$ correction** for $f'_c > 28$ MPa — leads to wrong lever arm.
> 6. **Writing only the answer without showing strain-compatibility setup** — examiners demand the strain diagram and equilibrium equations to award full 7 marks in Part B.
> 7. **Mixing up CFRP/GFRP/AFRP properties** — use the correct modulus and ultimate strain values for the fiber type specified in the problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FRP Sheets (Module 3, PECET634)**

### Definition
- FRP sheets are fiber-matrix composites externally bonded for strengthening.
- Two phases: **fibers** (carbon, glass, aramid) + **matrix** (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester).

### Properties to Memorize
- **CFRP:** E = 150–600 GPa, $f_{fu}$ = 2400–4000 MPa.
- **GFRP:** E = 35–70 GPa, $f_{fu}$ = 500–2500 MPa.
- **AFRP:** E = 60–130 GPa, $f_{fu}$ = 2400–3600 MPa.
- **Epoxy Resin:** $T_g > 60°$C, tensile strength ~50–90 MPa, low shrinkage.

### Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- $f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}$
- $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.003 \cdot (d_f - c)/c$ (flexural case)
- $\varepsilon_{fd} = 0.90 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$
- $T_f = 0.85 \cdot A_f \cdot f_{fe}$
- $l_e = 23300 / (n_f \cdot t_f)^{0.58}$
- $\Delta M_n = T_f \cdot (d_f - \beta_1 c/2)$

### Failure Modes
1. FRP Rupture
2. Concrete Crushing
3. **IC Debonding** (governing in ~70% cases)
4. **CDC (Cover Delamination)**
5. End Debonding
6. Anchorage Failure

### Installation Methods
1. **Wet Lay-Up** (most common, on-site)
2. Pre-Preg Lay-Up
3. Pultruded Laminate Bonding

### Design Philosophy (ACI 440.2R)
- **Strain Compatibility** + **Force Equilibrium** + **Strength Reduction Factor** ($\psi_f = 0.85$).

### Critical Practical Points
- **Debonding usually governs** design — never ignore $\varepsilon_{fd}$ check.
- **Effective bond length** $l_e$ must not be exceeded at FRP termination.
- **Surface preparation** is the most critical on-site step (80% of bond failures trace back to poor preparation).
- **Fire performance** is a known limitation — FRP loses strength above $T_g$.
- **No yielding** in FRP — strictly linear-elastic brittle response.
- **Creep and fatigue** must be considered under sustained and cyclic loading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crack Repair - Various Methods of Crack Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crack Repair - Various Methods of Crack Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **crack** in a reinforced concrete or masonry structure is a linear discontinuity or separation of the structural material, characterized by a measurable width (typically expressed in **mm** or **microns**), depth, and propagation pattern. Cracks compromise structural integrity, durability, and serviceability by providing pathways for moisture ingress, chloride penetration, carbonation, and corrosion of embedded reinforcement.

**Crack Repair**, in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET634), is defined as the systematic application of engineering techniques, materials, and procedures intended to restore the original structural performance, water-tightness, and aesthetic continuity of a cracked structural element without requiring full demolition and reconstruction. Repair methods are selected based on crack type (active vs. dormant), cause, width, depth, environmental exposure, and structural significance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET634 – Module 4):**
> The student is expected to identify crack types, classify them by cause and activity, and prescribe appropriate **repair methodology** based on field investigation reports. This is a high-weightage topic for both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** assignments and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Cracks (Pre-Repair Diagnostic Framework)

Before any repair method is chosen, a proper diagnosis is mandatory. The following classification governs the choice of method:

| Crack Category | Description | Typical Width | Activity Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hairline / Plastic Shrinkage | Surface-only, caused by early moisture loss | $\leq 0.1$ mm | Dormant |
| Drying Shrinkage | Curing-related volume change cracks | $0.1 \text{ to } 0.3$ mm | Dormant |
| Structural Flexural / Shear | Load-induced cracks in beams, slabs | $0.3 \text{ to } 3.0$ mm | Often Active |
| Thermal / Expansion | Movement-joint related | Variable | Active |
| Corrosion-Induced | Spalling due to rebar rust | $1.0$ to $5.0$ mm | Progressive Active |
| Settlement / Foundation | Differential settlement cracks | Variable | Often Active |

> [!NOTE]
> **Active vs. Dormant Distinction (Examination Favourite):**
> - **Active cracks** move with thermal, load, or moisture variations → require **flexible sealants** or **structural strengthening**.
> - **Dormant cracks** are stable → can be repaired with **rigid materials** like epoxy or cementitious grouts.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Broken Bone Metaphor**
> Imagine a bone fracture in the human body. A doctor does not just plaster over the break; he first **X-rays** (inspection), then **realigns** (structural correction), and finally **bonds** the fracture using specialized resins or pins. Crack repair in concrete works on the same principle:
> 1. **Diagnose** the cause (analogous to X-ray).
> 2. **Clean and prepare** the crack faces (analogous to wound cleaning).
> 3. **Inject or fill** with an appropriate bonding material (analogous to bone cement or plaster).
> 4. **Seal and protect** the surface (analogous to a cast).
>
> Just as using the wrong adhesive on a bone would fail, using cement on an active crack would re-crack within weeks. **Material selection is therefore as critical as the technique itself.**

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

- Cracks reduce the **compressive strength** of concrete by up to **15–30%** when widths exceed **0.3 mm**.
- They increase the **permeability coefficient** of concrete by orders of magnitude, accelerating reinforcement corrosion.
- Untreated cracks may reduce the **service life** of a structure from a designed **60–100 years** to as little as **15–25 years**.
- Effective crack repair can **extend service life by 30–50 years** at a fraction of replacement cost (typically **20–40%** of reconstruction cost).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Crack Repair

The selection and execution of any crack repair method is governed by four engineering principles:

1. **Compatibility Principle** – The repair material must have a **modulus of elasticity**, **thermal expansion coefficient**, and **bond strength** compatible with the parent concrete.
2. **Bond Strength Principle** – The tensile bond strength between repair and substrate must exceed the anticipated in-service tensile stress.
3. **Durability Principle** – The repair must resist the same environmental exposures (freeze-thaw, sulfate attack, chloride ingress) as the original structure.
4. **Movement Accommodation Principle** – For active cracks, the repair system must accommodate anticipated movement without loss of seal.

## 2.2 Detailed Methods of Crack Repair

### 2.2.1 Epoxy Injection

**Principle:** Low-viscosity epoxy resin is injected under pressure (typically **0.2 to 1.0 MPa**) into the crack, where it polymerizes to form a rigid, high-strength bond.

**Why this method:** Epoxy has a tensile strength of **30–60 MPa** and a bond strength to concrete of **2.5–7.0 MPa**, restoring monolithic structural behavior.

**How it works (Stepwise Logic):**
- Surface seal is applied using an epoxy paste.
- Injection ports (T-ports or surface-mounted nozzles) are installed at **150–300 mm** spacing.
- Epoxy is injected from the **lowest port upward**.
- Curing occurs in **6 to 24 hours** depending on ambient temperature.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot be used on **active cracks** (rigid material).
- Requires **dry substrate**; moisture interferes with polymerization.
- Crack width must be **≥ 0.05 mm** for resin to penetrate.
- Epoxy is a **non-breathable** material, may trap moisture.

### 2.2.2 Routing and Sealing

**Principle:** A V-shaped or U-shaped groove is cut along the crack and filled with a sealant (polysulfide, polyurethane, or silicone).

**Why:** Effective for dormant cracks on horizontal surfaces (floors, pavements) and where appearance is secondary.

**How:**
- Rout depth = **6 mm to 25 mm**.
- Rout width = **6× to 10×** the crack width at surface.
- Groove is cleaned by compressed air.
- Bond breaker (polyethylene tape) applied at base.
- Sealant poured or gunned into groove.

### 2.2.3 Stitching (Dog-Bone Stitching)

**Principle:** Holes are drilled on both sides of the crack, and **U-shaped metal staples** (called "dogs" or "stitches") are anchored into the holes to restore tensile transfer across the crack.

**Why:** Provides mechanical load transfer for **dormant structural cracks**.

**How:**
- Holes drilled at **45° to 60°** to the crack plane.
- Spacing between stitches = **300 to 600 mm** depending on crack width and structural demand.
- Stitches grouted with epoxy or non-shrink cementitious grout.
- Crack face is then sealed (often with epoxy injection in between stitches).

### 2.2.4 Drilling and Plugging

**Principle:** A hole is drilled down the length of the crack, and a key (plug) is grouted into the hole to provide shear transfer.

**Why:** Economical method for **longitudinal cracks** in beams where the crack plane is known and accessible from one end.

**How:**
- Hole diameter = **50 to 75 mm** (typically).
- Plug material = non-shrink grout or epoxy mortar.
- This method arrests further propagation by introducing a shear key.

### 2.2.5 Gravity Filling (Surface Application)

**Principle:** Low-viscosity resin or cementitious grout is poured on horizontal surfaces and allowed to flow into the crack by **gravity alone** (no pressure).

**Why:** Used for fine, dormant, horizontal surface cracks.

**How:**
- Surface cleaned and pre-wetted (for cementitious) or primed (for resin).
- Material is poured and spread with a squeegee.
- Multiple applications may be required.

### 2.2.6 Polymer Impregnation

**Principle:** The cracked zone is dried, evacuated under vacuum, and impregnated with a low-viscosity **monomer** (e.g., methyl methacrylate), which is then polymerized in situ by heat or chemical catalyst.

**Why:** Used for critical repairs of precast elements and parking decks.

**How:**
- Member is wrapped in impervious membrane.
- Vacuum applied (**−95 kPa** gauge).
- Monomer introduced, then polymerization triggered.

### 2.2.7 Grouting (Cementitious / Chemical)

**Principle:** Portland cement-based or chemical grouts (sodium silicate, polyurethane) are injected into wider cracks, voids, or delaminated zones.

**Why:** Suitable for **wide cracks (> 3 mm)**, wet conditions, and masonry.

**How:**
- For cementitious: water-cement ratio = **0.4 to 0.6**, often with **plasticizers** or **non-shrink additives**.
- For chemical: two-component polyurethane reacts with water, expanding 5–10× to form a watertight seal.

### 2.2.8 Autogenous Healing

**Principle:** Self-healing of micro-cracks by continued **hydration of unhydrated cement** in the presence of moisture, or by **calcium carbonate deposition** (autogenous healing).

**Why:** Naturally occurs in concrete with ongoing moisture exposure (water tanks, dams).

**How:** Effective only for **cracks ≤ 0.2 mm** in moist environments; cannot be relied upon for structural repairs.

### 2.2.9 Flexible Sealing

**Principle:** Application of **elastomeric sealants** (silicone, polyurethane, polysulfide) capable of accommodating **±25% movement**.

**Why:** Essential for **active cracks** and expansion joints.

**How:**
- Joint/sealant reservoir designed with **2:1 width-to-depth ratio**.
- Bond breaker at base.
- Primer applied for substrate compatibility.

### 2.2.10 Shotcrete / Guniting

**Principle:** Pneumatically projected mortar or concrete applied at high velocity (**80–120 m/s**) onto the prepared substrate, often with reinforcement mesh.

**Why:** Used for **large-scale repairs** of spalled concrete, soffits, and tunnel linings.

**How:**
- Substrate cleaned, roughened (CSP 5–9 profile per ICRI).
- Bonding agent applied.
- Shotcrete applied in layers of **25–50 mm**.
- Wet-mix or dry-mix process used.

### 2.2.11 Jacketing

**Principle:** A new structural layer (concrete, steel, or FRP wrap) is added around the cracked element to share or carry the load.

**Why:** For **severely damaged columns or beams** where epoxy alone is insufficient.

**How:**
- Surface prepared to expose sound concrete.
- Reinforcement added (longitudinal bars + stirrups).
- New concrete (micro-concrete or self-compacting concrete) poured with **minimum 75 mm cover**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the critical engineering equations, limits, and selection criteria that examiners expect KTU students to know for the ESE. **Memorize these before the exam.**

| Parameter | Formula / Limit | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crack width classification | Hairline: $w \leq 0.1$, Fine: $0.1 < w \leq 0.3$, Medium: $0.3 < w \leq 1.0$, Wide: $w > 1.0$ | mm | Method selection |
| IS 456 max flexural crack width (RCC) | $w_{max} = 0.3$ | mm | Serviceability limit |
| Bond strength of epoxy to concrete (typical) | $f_b = 2.5 \text{ to } 7.0$ | MPa | Epoxy injection design |
| Tensile strength of cured epoxy | $\sigma_t = 30 \text{ to } 60$ | MPa | Structural restoration |
| Injection pressure (epoxy) | $P = 0.2 \text{ to } 1.0$ | MPa | Field practice |
| Port spacing for epoxy injection | $s = 150 \text{ to } 300$ | mm | Field practice |
| Routing groove depth (typical) | $d_g = 6 \text{ to } 25$ | mm | Routing & sealing |
| Routing width-to-crack ratio | $W/d = 6 \text{ to } 10$ | dimensionless | Routing & sealing |
| Stitch spacing (dog-bone) | $s_s = 300 \text{ to } 600$ | mm | Stitching |
| Drill/plug diameter | $\phi_p = 50 \text{ to } 75$ | mm | Drilling & plugging |
| Polyurethane expansion ratio | $V_f / V_i = 5 \text{ to } 10$ | dimensionless | Chemical grouting |
| Sealant reservoir width-to-depth | $W/D = 2:1$ | dimensionless | Flexible sealing |
| Sealant movement capability | $\pm 25$ (typical) | % | Flexible sealing |
| Autogenous healing crack limit | $w \leq 0.2$ | mm | Natural healing |
| Shotcrete velocity | $v = 80 \text{ to } 120$ | m/s | Wet/dry mix |
| Shotcrete layer thickness | $t = 25 \text{ to } 50$ | mm per pass | Layered application |
| Jacketing cover requirement | $c \geq 75$ | mm | Column jacketing |
| Service life extension (typical) | $\Delta L = 30 \text{ to } 50$ | years | Repair benefit |
| Cost ratio (repair / replacement) | $C_r / C_{rep} = 0.2 \text{ to } 0.4$ | dimensionless | Economic justification |

### 2.3.1 Critical Selection Logic (Master Decision Flow)

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU-recommended selection logic (memorize this decision tree):**
> 1. **Is the crack active or dormant?**
>    - Active → **Flexible sealing, Jacketing, or Stitching + flexible joint**.
>    - Dormant → proceed to step 2.
> 2. **What is the crack width?**
>    - $w \leq 0.1$ mm → **Surface treatment / Autogenous healing permitted**.
>    - $0.1 < w \leq 0.3$ mm → **Gravity filling or Epoxy injection**.
>    - $w > 0.3$ mm → **Routing and sealing, Epoxy injection, or Grouting**.
>    - $w > 3.0$ mm → **Cementitious grouting or Polymer impregnation**.
> 3. **Is the structural element load-critical?**
>    - Yes → **Epoxy injection + Stitching or Jacketing**.
>    - No → **Surface seal or Routing/sealing sufficient**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Crack Repair Methods

- **Bridges and Flyovers:** Epoxy injection is the most common repair, often combined with FRP wrapping for seismic retrofit.
- **Water Retaining Structures (Tanks, Dams):** Chemical (polyurethane) grouting is preferred for active water leaks.
- **Industrial Floors and Parking Decks:** Routing and sealing, polymer impregnation, and flexible sealants are widely used.
- **Marine and Coastal Structures:** Epoxy injection + cathodic protection + crack sealing for corrosion control.
- **Heritage / Heritage-listed Masonry:** Lime-based grouting and stitching with stainless steel to preserve original fabric.
- **Nuclear Containment Structures:** Epoxy injection under strict quality control with documented bond strength testing (ASTM C881 compliant).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bond Stress Capacity of an Epoxy-Injected Crack

We derive the **shear stress** that the epoxy-concrete interface can transfer across a repaired crack, to justify the design of port spacing.

### Given:
- Tensile strength of cured epoxy: $\sigma_{t,e} = 45$ MPa
- Bond strength of epoxy to concrete: $f_b = 3.5$ MPa
- Effective bond length on each side of crack: $L_b = 75$ mm
- Crack depth (in member): $d = 200$ mm
- Member thickness (2D plane): $t = 300$ mm

### Step 1: Set Up the Force Equilibrium Across the Crack

Let $P_u$ be the ultimate tensile force that the repaired section can transmit across the crack plane. This force is transferred via the **bonded epoxy-concrete interface** on both sides of the crack.

$$
P_u = f_b \cdot A_b
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Bonded Interface Area

The bonded area on one side of the crack equals the crack depth $\times$ member thickness:

$$
A_b = d \times t
$$

$$
A_b = 200 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm} = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Ultimate Transferable Force

$$
P_u = f_b \times A_b
$$

$$
P_u = 3.5 \text{ MPa} \times 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
P_u = 3.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 210{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_u = 210 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 4: Compare with Epoxy Tensile Capacity

We must ensure the **epoxy body** itself does not fail in tension. The cross-sectional area of the epoxy in the crack is:

$$
A_e = w \times t
$$

Assume crack width $w = 0.5$ mm:

$$
A_e = 0.5 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm} = 150 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Tensile capacity of epoxy:

$$
P_{t,e} = \sigma_{t,e} \times A_e
$$

$$
P_{t,e} = 45 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 150 \text{ mm}^2 = 6{,}750 \text{ N} = 6.75 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the Governing Mode

Since $P_{t,e} = 6.75 \text{ kN} \ll P_u = 210 \text{ kN}$, the **epoxy tensile failure** is the governing mode. This implies the crack width $w$ is the **critical parameter** for very narrow cracks.

### Step 6: Maximum Allowable Stress Across the Repaired Section

The actual stress the section can sustain (limited by epoxy tension) is:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{P_{t,e}}{A_b} = \frac{6{,}750 \text{ N}}{60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.1125 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} \approx 0.11 \text{ MPa} = 112.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 7: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For very narrow cracks ($w \leq 0.5$ mm), the **epoxy tensile capacity** governs. The repair is highly effective because the **bond strength** is far greater than the **epoxy tensile strength**, ensuring that failure (if any) will be **cohesive in epoxy**, not **adhesive at the interface**. This confirms the KTU textbook statement that epoxy injection of narrow cracks restores **monolithic behavior**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Routing and Sealing — Volume of Sealant Required

For a routed V-groove of depth $d_g$ and surface width $W_g$, the cross-sectional area of the sealant (triangular) is:

$$
A_s = \frac{1}{2} \times W_g \times d_g
$$

For a length $L$ of crack, the required sealant volume is:

$$
V_s = A_s \times L = \frac{1}{2} \times W_g \times d_g \times L
$$

### Numerical Example

Given:
- Rout depth: $d_g = 12$ mm
- Rout surface width: $W_g = 20$ mm
- Crack length: $L = 5$ m = 5000 mm

$$
A_s = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 12 = 120 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
V_s = 120 \text{ mm}^2 \times 5000 \text{ mm} = 600{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_s = 600{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 600 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.6 \text{ litres}
$$

Adding a **wastage factor of 15%**:

$$
V_{s,actual} = 0.6 \times 1.15 = 0.69 \text{ litres}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stitching — Determination of Stitch Spacing

Stitches transfer tensile force across a crack by anchoring into the parent concrete. For equilibrium of forces at a stitch, the bond capacity on both legs of the U-staple must equal the design tensile demand.

Let:
- $T_d$ = tensile force per stitch (kN)
- $f_b$ = bond strength of grout to concrete = 2.5 MPa
- $d_h$ = hole diameter = 20 mm
- $L_a$ = anchor length per leg = 100 mm

Bond capacity per stitch (both legs combined):

$$
T_{b} = f_b \times \pi \times d_h \times 2 L_a
$$

$$
T_{b} = 2.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times \pi \times 20 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_{b} = 2.5 \times 3.1416 \times 20 \times 200 = 31{,}416 \text{ N} \approx 31.4 \text{ kN}
$$

If the design tensile demand per unit length of crack is $q = 50$ kN/m, then the required stitch spacing is:

$$
s = \frac{T_b}{q} = \frac{31.4 \text{ kN}}{50 \text{ kN/m}} = 0.628 \text{ m} \approx 600 \text{ mm}
$$

This validates the empirical KTU-recommended spacing of **300 to 600 mm**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Selection Table for Examinations

| Method | Crack Width (mm) | Active / Dormant | Structural / Non-Structural | Cost | Skill Required | Durability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Epoxy Injection | $0.05 \text{ to } 3.0$ | Dormant | Both | High | Specialist | Excellent (20+ yrs) |
| Routing & Sealing | $0.3 \text{ to } 6.0$ | Dormant | Non-structural | Low | Moderate | Good (10+ yrs) |
| Stitching | $> 0.3$ | Dormant | Structural | High | Specialist | Excellent |
| Drilling & Plugging | $> 0.5$ | Dormant | Structural | Moderate | Moderate | Good |
| Gravity Filling | $0.1 \text{ to } 1.0$ | Dormant | Non-structural | Low | Low | Fair |
| Polymer Impregnation | $< 0.5$ | Dormant | Structural | Very High | Specialist | Excellent |
| Cementitious Grouting | $1.0 \text{ to } 25$ | Both | Non-structural | Low | Moderate | Good |
| Polyurethane Grouting | $0.5 \text{ to } 10$ | Both (wet) | Waterproofing | Moderate | Specialist | Excellent |
| Flexible Sealing | Any | Active | Both (joints) | Moderate | Moderate | Good (5–10 yrs) |
| Autogenous Healing | $\leq 0.2$ | Dormant | Non-structural | Negligible | None | Variable |
| Shotcrete / Guniting | Any (large area) | Both | Both | High | Specialist | Excellent |
| Jacketing | Any (severe) | Both | Structural | Very High | Specialist | Excellent |
