# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and dormant cracks. Why is this distinction important in selecting a repair method?**

**Model Answer:**

Active cracks are those that continue to move in width, length, or depth over time due to ongoing causes such as thermal expansion-contraction cycles, structural overload, settlement, or moisture variation. Dormant cracks have stabilized; their width no longer changes with time or load.

The distinction is critical because it dictates the **rigidity or flexibility** of the repair material. A rigid material such as epoxy, when injected into an active crack, will **re-crack** within a short time as the structure continues to move, causing repair failure. Therefore, active cracks must be treated with **flexible sealants** (e.g., polyurethane, silicone) that can accommodate movement of $\pm 25\%$, or by structural measures (jacketing, FRP wrapping) that eliminate the cause of movement. Dormant cracks can be permanently repaired with **rigid materials** like epoxy, cementitious grout, or polymer-modified mortars.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for selection logic)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any three situations where epoxy injection is unsuitable as a crack repair method.**

**Model Answer:**

Epoxy injection is unsuitable in the following situations:

1. **Active cracks** – Epoxy is a rigid, non-yielding material; it will fail in tension if the crack continues to move. Flexible sealants should be used instead.

2. **Wet or submerged conditions** – Epoxy resins do not cure properly in the presence of water; moisture interferes with the polymerization reaction and prevents full strength development. Polyurethane or hydrophobic grouts are preferred.

3. **Cracks narrower than 0.05 mm** – Such fine cracks are below the practical penetration limit of even low-viscosity epoxies. The resin cannot flow into the crack void, leaving it unrepaired.

4. (Optional third or fourth) **Where future movement is anticipated**, or **for non-structural cosmetic repairs** where the cost of epoxy is unjustified.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per valid situation with brief justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **method of epoxy injection** for repairing cracks in concrete structures. Include the materials used, the equipment, the step-by-step procedure, and the limitations of the method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reinforced concrete beam has a **dormant flexural crack** of length **2.5 m** and width **0.4 mm**. The crack penetrates to a depth of **180 mm** in a beam of width **300 mm**. The bond strength of the selected epoxy to concrete is **3.5 MPa**, and the tensile strength of cured epoxy is **45 MPa**. Determine:
(i) The ultimate force transferable across the crack.
(ii) Whether the bond or the epoxy governs the failure.
(iii) The maximum allowable tensile stress across the repaired section. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

Epoxy injection is a high-performance, pressure-injection technique used to restore the **monolithic structural behavior** of cracked concrete and masonry. The method is governed by the requirements of **ASTM C881** (Standard Specification for Epoxy-Resin-Base Bonding Systems for Concrete) and the corresponding **IS codes**.

**Materials Used:**
- Two-component epoxy resin: Part A (resin) and Part B (hardener), mixed typically in a 2:1 or 4:1 ratio.
- Viscosity: 100–500 cps for injection grades (low viscosity for fine cracks).
- Surface seal paste: Thixotropic epoxy paste to cap the crack on the surface.
- Injection ports: T-ports, surface-mounted nozzles, or mechanical packers.

**Equipment:**
- Air-driven or electric injection pump capable of **0.2 to 1.0 MPa** pressure.
- Mixing nozzle, hoses, and pressure gauges.
- Surface preparation tools: grinder, wire brush, compressed air blower.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Clean the crack and surrounding area** – Remove dust, laitance, oil, and loose material using wire brush and compressed air. The surface must be **dry** for epoxy to bond.

2. **Install injection ports** – Mount T-ports or surface-mounted ports along the crack at a spacing of **150 to 300 mm**, depending on crack width. Ports are mounted on either side of a **bond-breaking strip** that prevents the surface seal from sealing the crack itself.

3. **Apply surface seal** – Using thixotropic epoxy paste, seal the entire exposed crack length between ports. Allow the seal to cure (typically **4 to 8 hours**) until it can withstand injection pressure.

4. **Inject epoxy** – Beginning at the **lowest port** (for vertical cracks) or one end (for horizontal cracks), inject epoxy under controlled pressure. Injection continues until epoxy appears at the adjacent port. The first port is then capped, and injection proceeds to the next port.

5. **Cure and remove ports** – Allow the system to cure for **6 to 24 hours** depending on ambient temperature. Surface seal and ports are then ground off flush.

6. **Quality assurance** – Pull-off adhesion tests (per ASTM D7234) on representative cores, and visual inspection for exudation or voids.

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for **active cracks** (rigid material).
- Not suitable for **wet or submerged** cracks.
- **Skilled labour** and specialized equipment are required.
- **Cost** is high compared to surface sealing.
- **Health and safety** concerns (epoxy is a sensitizer; proper PPE mandatory).
- Cannot fill **voids** larger than 5 mm; for larger voids, use cementitious grout first.

**(7 marks: 2 marks materials/equipment, 3 marks procedure, 1 mark QA, 1 mark limitations)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Crack length: $L = 2.5$ m = 2500 mm
- Crack width: $w = 0.4$ mm
- Crack depth: $d = 180$ mm
- Beam width: $t = 300$ mm
- Bond strength: $f_b = 3.5$ MPa
- Epoxy tensile strength: $\sigma_{t,e} = 45$ MPa

**(i) Ultimate force transferable across the crack**

Bond-controlled capacity:

$$
P_u = f_b \times d \times t
$$

$$
P_u = 3.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 180 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
P_u = 3.5 \times 54{,}000 = 189{,}000 \text{ N} = 189 \text{ kN}
$$

Epoxy tensile capacity:

$$
A_e = w \times t = 0.4 \times 300 = 120 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
P_{t,e} = \sigma_{t,e} \times A_e = 45 \times 120 = 5{,}400 \text{ N} = 5.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Governing failure mode**

$$
P_{t,e} = 5.4 \text{ kN} \ll P_u = 189 \text{ kN}
$$

The **epoxy tensile strength** governs the failure, since the bond capacity far exceeds the epoxy tensile capacity. Failure will be **cohesive within the epoxy body**.

**(iii) Maximum allowable tensile stress across the repaired section**

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{P_{t,e}}{d \times t} = \frac{5{,}400 \text{ N}}{180 \times 300 \text{ mm}^2} = \frac{5{,}400}{54{,}000} = 0.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} = 0.1 \text{ MPa} = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks for part i, 2 marks for part ii, 3 marks for part iii with full derivations)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **method of routing and sealing** of cracks. Explain when this method is preferred and state the typical geometry of the V-groove prepared. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dormant surface crack on a reinforced concrete slab is to be repaired by **routing and sealing**. The crack length is **8 m**, and a **V-groove of depth 15 mm and surface width 25 mm** is routed. Calculate:
(i) The volume of sealant required (in litres).
(ii) The actual volume to be ordered if a **15% wastage factor** is applied.
(iii) Comment on the suitability of routing and sealing for a **load-critical structural element**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Routing and Sealing Method:**

This is one of the **simplest and most common** techniques for repairing dormant cracks. The method involves cutting a **groove** of defined geometry along the length of the crack, then filling it with a suitable **sealant**.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Rout the crack** – Using a concrete saw or hand router, cut a **V-shaped** or **U-shaped** groove along the entire length of the crack. The rout extends through the crack depth and into sound concrete.

2. **Clean the groove** – Remove all dust, debris, and loose material by compressed air, wire brushing, and water rinsing (if applicable). Allow to dry.

3. **Apply bond breaker** – Place a strip of polyethylene tape or backer rod at the base of the groove. This ensures that the sealant bonds only to the **sides** of the groove, not the base, allowing two-sided movement (preventing three-sided adhesion failure).

4. **Prime the groove** – If required by the sealant manufacturer, apply a compatible primer to the groove walls to improve adhesion.

5. **Apply sealant** – Gun or pour the sealant (polysulfide, polyurethane, silicone, or acrylic) into the groove, ensuring full contact with the sides and elimination of air pockets.

6. **Tool the surface** – Smooth the sealant with a tooling spatula and allow to cure per manufacturer specifications (typically **24 to 48 hours**).

**When Preferred:**
- **Dormant cracks** on horizontal or slightly inclined surfaces.
- **Non-structural or lightly loaded** elements.
- **Water-excluding** repairs on slabs, pavements, and parking decks.
- Where the appearance of the repair is secondary to its function.
- For wide cracks ($w > 0.3$ mm) where gravity filling is insufficient.

**Typical V-Groove Geometry:**
- **Depth:** $d_g = 6$ to $25$ mm
- **Surface width:** $W_g = 6 \times w_{crack} \text{ to } 10 \times w_{crack}$ (i.e., 6 to 10 times the surface crack width)
- **Shape:** V or square-cut
- **Backer rod** placed at depth = $W_g / 2$ (to maintain 2:1 width-to-depth ratio of sealant)

**(7 marks: 3 marks procedure, 2 marks geometry, 2 marks preference and applications)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- Crack length: $L = 8$ m = 8000 mm
- Rout depth: $d_g = 15$ mm
- Rout surface width: $W_g = 25$ mm
- Wastage factor: 15%

**(i) Volume of sealant required**

The cross-section of a V-groove (idealized as triangle) is:

$$
A_s = \frac{1}{2} \times W_g \times d_g = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times 15 = 187.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Volume for length $L$:

$$
V_s = A_s \times L = 187.5 \times 8000 = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Converting to litres ($1 \text{ litre} = 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$):

$$
V_s = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{10^6} = 1.5 \text{ litres}
$$

**(ii) Volume to be ordered with 15% wastage**

$$
V_{ordered} = V_s \times 1.15 = 1.5 \times 1.15 = 1.725 \text{ litres}
$$

**Recommend ordering 2.0 litres** (rounded up to nearest commercially available pack size).

**(iii) Suitability for load-critical structural element**

> [!WARNING]
> Routing and sealing is **NOT suitable** for load-critical structural elements. The reasons are:
> 1. The sealant is **not structural**; it does not transmit tensile or shear forces across the crack.
> 2. The method does not restore the **monolithic behavior** of the section.
> 3. Under load, the sealant acts as a **stress concentrator** and may debond.
> 4. For load-critical elements, **epoxy injection, stitching, or jacketing** must be specified instead.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for part i, 1 mark for part ii, 4 marks for part iii with engineering justification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ESE PECET634:**
> 1. **Confusing active and dormant cracks** – Always state the activity status first, then justify the material choice. Marks are lost if the student uses epoxy for an active crack.
> 2. **Omitting port spacing and injection pressure** – These are numerical questions waiting to be asked. Memorize: **port spacing = 150–300 mm**, **pressure = 0.2–1.0 MPa**.
> 3. **Not mentioning the role of bond breakers in routing and sealing** – Three-sided adhesion is a frequent cause of sealant failure; this is a **3-mark question** every semester.
> 4. **Using the wrong method for wet/submerged conditions** – Epoxy fails underwater. Polyurethane grout is the correct answer. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention QA** – Pull-off tests (ASTM D7234), core sampling, and visual inspection are **mandatory** answers for full marks.
> 6. **Calculation errors in derived quantities** – Always re-check unit conversions (mm² to m², N to kN).
> 7. **Missing the 2:1 width-to-depth ratio** of flexible sealants – This is a board favourite.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – PECET634 Module 4: Crack Repair Methods**

- **Crack Classification:** Hairline ($\leq 0.1$ mm), Fine ($0.1$–$0.3$ mm), Medium ($0.3$–$1.0$ mm), Wide ($> 1.0$ mm).
- **Active vs. Dormant:** Active → flexible sealant; Dormant → rigid repair possible.
- **Epoxy Injection:** Pressure **0.2–1.0 MPa**, port spacing **150–300 mm**, bond strength **2.5–7.0 MPa**, tensile strength **30–60 MPa**, viscosity **100–500 cps**.
- **Routing and Sealing:** V-groove depth **6–25 mm**, width **6× to 10× crack width**, bond breaker mandatory at base.
- **Stitching:** Dog-bone U-staples, spacing **300–600 mm**, drilling angle **45°–60°** to crack plane.
- **Drilling and Plugging:** Plug diameter **50–75 mm**, used for longitudinal cracks.
- **Polymer Impregnation:** Vacuum impregnation, methyl methacrylate monomer, polymerization by heat or catalyst.
- **Cementitious Grouting:** Suitable for wide cracks ($> 3$ mm), water-cement ratio **0.4–0.6**, often with non-shrink additives.
- **Polyurethane Grouting:** Expansion ratio **5–10×**, ideal for active water leaks.
- **Autogenous Healing:** Self-healing only for **$w \leq 0.2$ mm** in moist environments.
- **Flexible Sealing:** Width-to-depth **2:1** ratio, movement capability $\pm 25\%$.
- **Shotcrete:** Velocity **80–120 m/s**, layer thickness **25–50 mm** per pass.
- **Jacketing:** Cover $\geq 75$ mm, used for severely damaged columns/beams.
- **Bond Strength Principle:** $\sigma_{t,epoxy} < f_b$ for safe repair; epoxy tensile capacity governs for very narrow cracks.
- **Decision Tree:** Crack width $\rightarrow$ Method; Crack activity $\rightarrow$ Material flexibility.
- **QA Standards:** ASTM C881 (epoxy), ASTM D7234 (pull-off test), ICRI CSP profile for substrate roughness.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** Repair costs typically **20–40%** of replacement; service life extension **30–50 years**.
- **Active cracks require:** Flexible sealant OR structural strengthening; NEVER use rigid epoxy alone.
- **Wet/submerged cracks require:** Polyurethane or hydrophobic grout; NEVER use standard epoxy.
- **Remember:** Epoxy injection restores **monolithic behavior**; routing and sealing does NOT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grouting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Grouting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grouting** is the process of injecting a fluid material (grout) under pressure into cracks, voids, joints, fissures, or porous media in concrete, masonry, soil, or rock formations to restore structural integrity, seal pathways against water ingress, consolidate weakened strata, and re-establish load-transfer mechanisms in distressed structures.

In the context of KTU's *PECET634 – Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures*, grouting is classified as a **non-destructive / minimally invasive repair technique** that forms a critical part of the *structural retrofitting toolkit*, particularly for:
- Sealing of dormant and active cracks
- Strengthening of substructures
- Underpinning of foundations
- Waterproofing of basements, tunnels, and retaining structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Concrete Jacketing:** Unlike jacketing (which adds external mass), grouting works by *filling voids from within*, preserving original geometry and aesthetics while restoring monolithic behavior.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **sponge cake that has partially crumbled** inside but still looks intact from outside. The cake is your structural element (beam, slab, foundation), and the crumbled interior represents **internal voids, honeycombs, or delaminated zones**.

You cannot see the damage, but if you press a thin **piping bag filled with icing** (the grout) into the sponge and squeeze, the icing flows into all the empty pockets, re-bonding the crumbs. Once it hardens, the cake is whole again — only better, because the icing has sealed the gaps.

> **Grout = Liquid "medicine" injected into a "sick" structure to fill voids and restore strength.**

Similarly, in real engineering:
- The **piping bag** = the grout pump
- The **icing** = the cementitious or chemical grout
- The **sponge cake** = the distressed concrete, masonry, or soil mass
- The **crumbled interior** = cracks, voids, honeycombs, or loose strata

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Properties

The following physical properties govern grout selection (all values are **standard industry benchmarks** as per IS 14343, IS 12993, and ACI 546R):

| Property | Typical Range | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity** | **1 – 1000 cP** (depending on grout type) | ASTM D4016 |
| **Compressive Strength (28 days)** | **20 – 80 MPa** | IS 4031 Part 6 |
| **Bond Strength to Concrete** | **> 1.7 MPa** | ASTM C882 |
| **Particle Size (cement grout)** | **< 85 microns** | IS 269 |
| **Pressure Range** | **0.2 – 7.0 MPa** | ACI 546R |
| **Setting Time** | **15 min – 24 hr** | IS 4031 Part 5 |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Grouting (Overview Map)

```
GROUTING
├── By Material
│   ├── Cementitious Grout (Cement + Water + Admixtures)
│   ├── Chemical Grout (Silicates, Polyurethanes, Acrylics)
│   ├── Epoxy Grout (Resin + Hardener)
│   ├── Bituminous Grout (For water-sealing)
│   └── Bio-grout (Microbial — emerging field)
├── By Application
│   ├── Curtain Grouting (Water barriers)
│   ├── Consolidation Grouting (Mass filling)
│   ├── Contact Grouting (Interface filling)
│   └── Structural Grouting (Load-bearing joints)
└── By Method
    ├── Pressure Grouting
    ├── Gravity Grouting
    └── Vacuum Grouting
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **classification matrix** in 3-mark questions. Memorize the *material-based* classification thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step Logic

Grouting operates on a four-stage physical-chemical cycle:

1. **Stage 1 — Penetration:** The grout, in a low-viscosity fluid state, is forced through **packers** (injection ports) under controlled pressure into the substrate. The fluid exploits **Poiseuille-type laminar flow** through capillary-sized passages.
   - *Why?* Lower viscosity ensures flow into micro-cracks (≥ 0.1 mm width).
   
2. **Stage 2 — Filling:** Once inside, the grout displaces air and water, occupying voids, delaminations, and fissures.
   - *How?* Through hydrostatic displacement and surface tension effects (contact angle < 90°).

3. **Stage 3 — Solidification:** The grout undergoes either:
   - **Hydration** (cementitious): $C_3S + H_2O \rightarrow C-S-H + Ca(OH)_2$
   - **Polymerization** (epoxy/PU): Cross-linking of resin chains
   - **Gelation** (chemical silicates): Sodium silicate + reactant → silica gel

4. **Stage 4 — Strength Development:** Final hardened grout transfers load, seals water pathways, and bonds to substrate via **adhesion** and **mechanical interlock** with pore walls.

---

### 2.2 Types of Grout — Detailed Engineering Analysis

#### A. Cementitious Grout

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Composition** | Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) + Water + Sand (sometimes) + Plasticizers |
| **Water-Cement Ratio** | **0.4 to 0.6** (lower = stronger, higher = more flowable) |
| **Best Use** | Large voids, rock fissures, foundation underpinning |
| **Limitations** | Cannot penetrate cracks < 0.5 mm; shrinks on curing |
| **Additives** | Superplasticizers (for flow), accelerators (for fast set), expansive agents (anti-shrink) |

#### B. Chemical Grout

| Type | Base Chemical | Viscosity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sodium Silicate** | $Na_2SiO_3$ | 1 – 100 cP | Soil stabilization, water-stopping |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | MDI-based resins | 50 – 1000 cP | Active leak sealing, flexible joints |
| **Acrylic** | Acrylamide monomers | < 2 cP | Fine crack sealing, soil grouting |
| **Epoxy** | Bisphenol-A resin | 100 – 500 cP | Structural crack repair |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epoxy grout has the highest bond strength** (~7 MPa typical) but is **rigid** and unsuitable for moving cracks. Polyurethane is **flexible** — best for *active* cracks.

---

### 2.3 Grouting Techniques (Procedure-Based)

#### 2.3.1 Curtain Grouting
Used to create a **vertical or horizontal water barrier** (curtain) in dams, tunnels, basements.

$$ Q = \frac{n \cdot \pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \quad \text{(Darcy-Hagen-Poiseuille analogy)} $$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of grout (m³/s)
- $n$ = number of fissures
- $r$ = equivalent fissure radius (m)
- $\Delta P$ = injection pressure differential (Pa)
- $\mu$ = grout dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = flow path length (m)

#### 2.3.2 Consolidation Grouting
Used to **densify rock mass or soil** below foundations before load application. Primary grouts fill large voids, secondary grouts fill smaller ones — a hierarchical closure approach.

#### 2.3.3 Contact Grouting
Fills the gap between **newly placed concrete and rock/concrete interface** (e.g., dam concrete on rock foundation). Pressure typically **0.2 – 0.5 MPa**.

#### 2.3.4 Structural Injection Grouting
Repairs **load-bearing cracks** in beams, columns, slabs using epoxy or high-strength cementitious grout. Pressure: **0.5 – 7.0 MPa**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Groutability Ratio | $G_r = \dfrac{D_{15,\text{soil}}}{d_{85,\text{grout}}}$ | Determines if grout can permeate soil (must be ≥ 25 for cement grout) |
| 2 | Injection Pressure (empirical) | $P = 0.25 \cdot H$ (kg/cm²) | H = depth in meters; rule-of-thumb |
| 3 | Bingham Fluid Flow | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Cement grouts behave as Bingham plastics |
| 4 | Apparent Viscosity | $\mu_a = \tau_y / \dot{\gamma} + \eta$ | Time-dependent behavior |
| 5 | Bleeding Test | $B = \dfrac{V_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{grout}}} \times 100\%$ | Quality check (must be < 5%) |
| 6 | Strength Gain (cement grout) | $f_c(t) = f_{28} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{t}}{a + b\sqrt{t}}$ | For estimating strength at any age |
| 7 | Bond Stress | $\tau_b = \dfrac{P}{A_b}$ | Between grout and substrate |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dam Rehabilitation:** Curtain grouting in Idukki, Mullaperiyar dams (Kerala heritage structures) to control seepage.
- **Tunnel Repairs:** Used in Kochi Metro and railway tunnels for waterproofing.
- **Heritage Conservation:** Grouting the original lime-mortar joints of ancient Kerala temples.
- **Bridge Bearings:** Epoxy grout under steel base plates for load transfer.
- **Underpinning:** Jet grouting stabilizes soil beneath settled building foundations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Grout Penetration Radius in a Cylindrical Crack

Consider a planar crack of constant aperture $e$ (m), extending radially from an injection point. We derive the **radial penetration distance** $R(t)$ as a function of time.

#### Starting Assumption — Lubrication Theory

The grout flows radially between two parallel plates (the crack faces). The governing equation from lubrication theory is:

$$ \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \frac{12 \mu Q}{e^3 \cdot 2\pi r} $$

Where:
- $P$ = pressure (Pa)
- $r$ = radial coordinate (m)
- $e$ = crack aperture (m)
- $\mu$ = grout viscosity (Pa·s)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s)

#### Step 1 — Conservation of Mass

For an incompressible grout filling a cylindrical crack of height $e$:

$$ Q = 2\pi r \cdot e \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} \cdot \phi $$

Where $\phi$ is the volume fraction of grout (typically $\phi \approx 0.95$ to account for entrapped air).

#### Step 2 — Substitute Q into Pressure Equation

$$ \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \frac{12 \mu}{e^3 \cdot 2\pi r} \left( 2\pi r \cdot e \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} \cdot \phi \right) $$

Simplifying:

$$ \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \frac{12 \mu \cdot \phi}{e^2} \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} $$

#### Step 3 — Integrate Over Radius from $r = 0$ to $R$

Pressure at injection port = $P_0$, pressure at the advancing front $r = R$ = $P_{\text{atm}}$.

$$ \int_{P_0}^{P_{\text{atm}}} dP = \frac{12 \mu \phi}{e^2} \cdot \frac{dr}{dt} \int_0^R dr $$

$$ P_0 - P_{\text{atm}} = \frac{12 \mu \phi}{e^2} \cdot R \cdot \frac{dR}{dt} $$

#### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate Over Time

$$ (P_0 - P_{\text{atm}}) \, dt = \frac{12 \mu \phi}{e^2} \cdot R \, dR $$

Integrating from $t = 0$, $R = 0$ to $t = t$, $R = R(t)$:

$$ (P_0 - P_{\text{atm}}) \cdot t = \frac{12 \mu \phi}{e^2} \cdot \frac{R(t)^2}{2} $$

#### Step 5 — Final Result

$$ \boxed{R(t) = e \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{(P_0 - P_{\text{atm}}) \cdot t}{6 \mu \phi}}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The penetration distance grows with the **square root of time** and **inversely with viscosity**. To penetrate farther:
> - ↓ Reduce viscosity $\mu$ (use chemical grout)
> - ↑ Increase injection pressure $P_0$
> - ↑ Allow more time $t$
> - **Note:** Aperture $e$ has a *linear* effect — doubling crack width doubles penetration distance.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cementitious grout with $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s is injected into a crack of aperture $e = 2$ mm. Injection pressure $P_0 = 0.5$ MPa (gauge), atmospheric = 0.1 MPa. $\phi = 0.95$. Find penetration distance after $t = 30$ minutes.

**Step 1 — Convert Units:**
- $e = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $P_0 - P_{\text{atm}} = (0.5 - 0.1) \times 10^6 = 0.4 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $t = 30 \times 60 = 1800$ s
- $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s
- $\phi = 0.95$

**Step 2 — Plug into Formula:**

$$ R = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.4 \times 10^6 \times 1800}{6 \times 0.05 \times 0.95}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator Inside Square Root:**

$$ 0.4 \times 10^6 \times 1800 = 7.2 \times 10^8 $$

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator:**

$$ 6 \times 0.05 \times 0.95 = 0.285 $$

**Step 5 — Compute Ratio:**

$$ \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^8}{0.285} = 2.526 \times 10^9 $$

**Step 6 — Take Square Root:**

$$ \sqrt{2.526 \times 10^9} = 5.026 \times 10^4 $$

**Step 7 — Final Multiplication:**

$$ R = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5.026 \times 10^4) = 100.5 \text{ m} $$

**Result:** Grout penetrates **~100 m** in 30 minutes — physically unrealistic in field conditions due to friction losses, but illustrates the theoretical upper bound.

---

### 3.3 Groutability Ratio — Analytical Matrix

$$ G_r = \dfrac{D_{15,\text{soil}}}{d_{85,\text{grout}}} $$

| Condition | $G_r$ Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| $G_r \geq 25$ | **Acceptable** for cement grout | Grout can permeate soil |
| $5 \leq G_r < 25$ | **Marginal** — use chemical grout | Cement particles too large |
| $G_r < 5$ | **Reject** cement grout | Use polyurethane or fine chemical grout |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** $D_{15}$ = grain size at which 15% of soil is finer (large pore channel). $d_{85}$ = particle size at which 85% of grout is finer. The grout particles must be smaller than the soil pore throats by factor of 25 for cement grouts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Grouting Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Investigation] --> B[Identify Cracks and Voids]
    B --> C[Surface Preparation and Cleaning]
    C --> D[Drill Injection Holes and Install Packers]
    D --> E[Select Grout Type Based on Crack Width]
    E --> F{Grout Type Decision}
    F -->|Cement Grout| G1[Mix OPC Water Admixtures]
    F -->|Chemical Grout| G2[Prepare Resin or Silicate System]
    F -->|Epoxy Grout| G3[Mix Resin and Hardener]
    G1 --> H[Inject Under Controlled Pressure]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Monitor Pressure and Flow]
    I --> J{Refusal Criteria Met}
    J -->|No| H
    J -->|Yes| K[Seal Packers and Cure]
    K --> L[Quality Control Testing]
    L --> M[Drill Cores and Confirm Strength]
    M --> N[Repair Complete]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Grouting Equipment Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Grout Mixer] --> Q[Grout Pump]
    Q --> R[Pressure Gauge]
    R --> S[Injection Hose]
    S --> T[Packer in Structure]
    T --> U[Crack or Void]
    U -.->|Grout Returns| V[Waste Collection]
    Q -.->|Pressure Feedback| R
```

**Equipment Description:**
- **Grout Mixer:** High-shear colloidal mixer for cement grout (≥ 1500 rpm); paddle mixer for epoxy
- **Grout Pump:** Positive-displacement piston pump for cement; air-driven for chemical
- **Pressure Gauge:** Critical safety and QC instrument (calibrated)
- **Packer:** Mechanical or inflatable device sealing the borehole
- **Injection Hose:** High-pressure rated (≥ 2× working pressure)

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Crack Injection Sequence (Cross-Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 Clean Crack Surface] --> A2[Step 2 Install Surface Seal Ports]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Inject from Lowest Port Upward]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Grout Flows Upward Filling Crack]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Grout Emerges at Next Port]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Cap Lower Port Move to Next]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Continue Until Top Port]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 Allow Full Cure 7 Days]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9 Remove Surface Seal and Finish]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic Block — Grouting Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Crack Width Decision] --> B1[Width Less Than 0.1 mm]
    A0 --> B2[Width 0.1 to 0.3 mm]
    A0 --> B3[Width 0.3 to 3 mm]
    A0 --> B4[Width Greater Than 3 mm]
    B1 --> C1[Use Acrylic Chemical Grout]
    B2 --> C2[Use Polyurethane Grout]
    B3 --> C3[Use Epoxy Grout]
    B4 --> C4[Use Cementitious Grout or Polymer Modified Cement]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
> **Define grouting. List any four types of grout commonly used in repair works.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Grouting is the process of injecting a fluid material (grout) under pressure into cracks, voids, joints, or porous media of a structure to restore strength, seal water ingress, or consolidate the substrate.

> **Four Types of Grout (1 mark):**
> 1. Cementitious grout
> 2. Chemical grout (silicates, polyurethanes)
> 3. Epoxy grout
> 4. Bituminous grout

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]  
> **What is the groutability ratio? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **groutability ratio** is defined as:

$$ G_r = \dfrac{D_{15,\text{soil}}}{d_{85,\text{grout}}} $$

> **Significance (2 marks):** It is a design parameter that determines whether a particular grout can permeate a particular soil/porous medium. A ratio of **≥ 25** is acceptable for cement-based grouts; lower values indicate the need for finer chemical grouts.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choose either Question A OR Question B. Each is a complete 14-mark sub-divided question.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**  
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **different types of grouting techniques** used in repair and rehabilitation of structures. Mention the specific applications of each. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for epoxy injection grouting** of a structural crack in a reinforced concrete beam. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

The four major grouting techniques are:

| Technique | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Curtain Grouting** | Creates vertical/horizontal impermeable barrier by injecting grout in a line of holes | Dams, tunnels, basements |
| **Consolidation Grouting** | Densifies rock mass below foundations in primary-secondary sequence | Foundation preparation for heavy structures |
| **Contact Grouting** | Fills gap at concrete-rock interface | Dam-foundation contact, column base plates |
| **Structural Injection** | Repairs load-bearing cracks | Cracked beams, columns, slabs |
| **Compensation Grouting** | Compensates for ground settlement during tunnelling | Urban tunnelling, adjacent buildings |

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Surface Cleaning:** Remove dirt, laitance, oil from the crack using wire brush and compressed air.

**Step 2 — Install Injection Ports:** Place T-ports (mechanical packers) at intervals of 150–300 mm along the crack length. Surface-mount ports are bonded with epoxy paste.

**Step 3 — Seal the Crack Surface:** Apply surface seal epoxy along the entire crack between ports and allow 24 hr cure.

**Step 4 — Mix Epoxy Resin:** Combine Part A (resin) and Part B (hardener) in ratio 2:1 by volume. Mix slowly to avoid air entrapment.

**Step 5 — Inject Grout:** Start injection from the **lowest port**. Use hand-operated or pneumatic injection gun at 0.2–0.7 MPa. Continue until grout emerges from adjacent port.

**Step 6 — Sequence:** Cap the lower port, move to next port, and repeat upward progression.

**Step 7 — Curing:** Allow 24–48 hours for full epoxy cure.

**Step 8 — Quality Verification:** Drill cores, perform pull-off tests, or use ultrasonic pulse velocity to confirm filling.

**Step 9 — Finishing:** Grind off surface seals and injection ports flush with the parent concrete.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**  
> **(a)** Discuss the **materials used for grouting** with their properties and limitations. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A crack of aperture **2 mm** in a concrete wall is to be injected with epoxy grout of viscosity **0.5 Pa·s** at a pressure of **0.6 MPa (gauge)**. Estimate the **penetration distance after 1 hour**. Take $\phi = 0.9$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

Refer to Section 2.2 above. Key points:

- **Cementitious grout:** Cheap, high strength, but cannot penetrate fine cracks (<0.5 mm). Shrinkage is a problem.
- **Chemical silicate grout:** Very low viscosity, good for soil stabilization, but lower strength and durability concerns.
- **Polyurethane grout:** Flexible, fast-setting, ideal for active water-leaking cracks. Not structural.
- **Epoxy grout:** Highest bond strength (~7 MPa), rigid, suitable for structural cracks. Cannot tolerate movement.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

Using the derived formula:

$$ R(t) = e \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{(P_0 - P_{\text{atm}}) \cdot t}{6 \mu \phi}} $$

**Given:**
- $e = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $P_0 - P_{\text{atm}} = 0.6 - 0.1 = 0.5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $t = 3600$ s
- $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s
- $\phi = 0.9$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$ (0.5 \times 10^6) \times 3600 = 1.8 \times 10^9 $$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$ 6 \times 0.5 \times 0.9 = 2.7 $$

**Step 3 — Inside Square Root:**

$$ \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^9}{2.7} = 6.667 \times 10^8 $$

**Step 4 — Square Root:**

$$ \sqrt{6.667 \times 10^8} = 2.582 \times 10^4 $$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$ R = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.582 \times 10^4) = 51.6 \text{ m} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** time from hours/minutes into seconds (loses ~1 mark).
> 2. **Using gauge pressure without subtracting atmospheric** (~1 mark lost).
> 3. **Unit mismatch** between viscosity (Pa·s) and other units (~1 mark).
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps** — KTU examiners want the full substitution table.
> 5. **Final unit** must be in **meters** for distance — state it clearly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Grouting = injecting fluid material under pressure to fill voids/cracks.
- ✅ **Four Major Types of Grout:** Cementitious, Chemical, Epoxy, Bituminous.
- ✅ **Four Major Techniques:** Curtain, Consolidation, Contact, Structural Injection.
- ✅ **Crack Width Mapping:**
  - < 0.1 mm → Acrylic
  - 0.1 – 0.3 mm → Polyurethane
  - 0.3 – 3 mm → Epoxy
  - > 3 mm → Cementitious
- ✅ **Groutability Ratio formula** must be memorized: $G_r = D_{15}/d_{85}$; threshold = **25**.
- ✅ **Penetration formula** $R(t) = e \sqrt{(P_0 - P_{\text{atm}})t / 6\mu\phi}$ — most-tested equation.
- ✅ **Pressure rule of thumb:** $P = 0.25 \cdot H$ (kg/cm²), where H = depth in meters.
- ✅ **Epoxy for structural cracks, PU for active/leaking cracks, cement for large voids.**
- ✅ **Viscosity order (lowest to highest):** Water < Acrylic < Silicate < Polyurethane < Epoxy < Cement.
- ✅ **Quality Control Tests:** Bleeding test, sand cone test, core drilling, UPV testing, pull-off test.
- ✅ **Bleeding limit:** < 5% by volume for good-quality cement grout.
- ✅ **Bond strength requirement:** > 1.7 MPa (ASTM C882) for repair grouts.
- ✅ **Sequence:** Always inject from **lowest port upward** for vertical cracks.
- ✅ **Always cap the previous port** once grout emerges from the next.
- ✅ **Curing time:** Epoxy 24–48 hr; cement grout 7–28 days.
- ✅ **Standards to mention in answers:** IS 14343, IS 12993, ACI 546R, ASTM C882.

> **End of Module 4 — Grouting Notes (PECET634 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing and Sealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Routing and Sealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routing and Sealing** is a *passive* crack repair technique in which a dormant (non-moving or stable) crack in a concrete or masonry element is first **enlarged along its length and depth** by cutting a V-shaped or U-shaped groove (the *routing* operation), and subsequently **filled with a suitable elastic sealant** (the *sealing* operation) to restore watertightness, prevent ingress of aggressive agents, and improve the aesthetic appearance of the structural surface.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this falls under the broader category of **"Crack Repair Techniques for Dormant Cracks"** and is recommended when the crack is no longer expected to undergo significant movement (movement less than approximately **25% of the joint width**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Garden Wall Crack**
> Imagine a small vertical crack running down your boundary compound wall. You cannot simply smear paint over it because the crack is too narrow for filler to enter and bond properly. What do you do?
> 1. You first use a chisel (or a *grinder with a diamond blade*) to widen the crack into a small "V" or "U" channel — this is **Routing**. By widening, you create a reservoir that is wide enough to accept filler and to which filler can bond on its sides.
> 2. Then you apply a flexible putty (silicone, polyurethane, or polysulfide sealant) into this channel, smooth it off, and let it cure — this is **Sealing**.
>
> The wall now stays "dry" on the inside and looks neat, even though the underlying crack still exists. Routing & Sealing does **not** restore structural strength; it only restores **serviceability and durability** (water-tightness, gas-tightness, and aesthetics).

## 1.3 When is Routing & Sealing Used? (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Dormant (stable) cracks** only — not active/moving cracks.
> - Cracks of width ranging from **0.13 mm to 6.0 mm** (most codes).
> - For non-structural cracks in walls, slabs, beams, water tanks, basements, and pavements.
> - When the objective is to **prevent leakage and corrosion**, not to transfer load.
> - In environments exposed to water, chemicals, or freeze-thaw cycles.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard routing width (W) : **6 mm to 25 mm** depending on crack width.
- Standard routing depth (D) : **6 mm to 25 mm** (typically D ≥ W).
- Depth-to-width ratio (D/W) recommended for sealant pocket : **1 : 1 to 1.5 : 1**.
- Working temperature range for most sealants : **−40 °C to +80 °C**.
- Movement accommodation factor (MAF) of elastomeric sealants : **±25% of joint width**.

## 1.5 Sealing vs. Routing — Quick Disambiguation

| Term | What it means | Why it is done |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| **Routing** | Mechanical enlargement of the crack into a controlled groove | Provides a reservoir of correct geometry and removes unsound concrete along crack edges |
| **Sealing** | Filling the routed groove with an elastic sealant | Restores watertightness, prevents ingress of moisture & chemicals, accommodates minor thermal/moisture movements |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Routing and Sealing process is performed in a strict, sequential order. Skipping or reordering a step compromises the durability of the repair.

**Step 1 — Crack Inspection & Classification**
- Inspect the crack for width, depth, length, orientation, and most importantly, its **activity** (whether it is dormant or live).
- Use a **crack width comparator card** or **handheld digital microscope**.
- A crack is classified as **dormant** if it has stabilised (no measurable movement for 6–12 months).

**Step 2 — Surface Preparation**
- Clean the concrete on both sides of the crack for a width of at least **25–40 mm** beyond the routed groove.
- Remove all dust, laitance, oil, grease, and loose particles.
- Use wire brush, compressed air, or solvent wipe.

**Step 3 — Routing (Groove Cutting)**
- Cut a V-shaped or U-shaped groove along the entire length of the crack using:
  - Hand chisel + hammer (for small jobs),
  - Angle grinder with **diamond tuck-point blade** (most common), or
  - Concrete saw.
- Typical cross-section dimensions:
  - V-groove : width 10–15 mm at surface, depth 10–15 mm.
  - U-groove : width 8–10 mm, depth 10–12 mm.
- Square off any sharp internal corners — sealants perform poorly at sharp re-entrant angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why V or U shape?** Because the sealant needs an **hour-glass or dovetailed geometry** at the surface so that hydrostatic/adhesive forces resist it being pushed out. A pure rectangular slot is the worst geometry for sealant retention.

**Step 4 — Cleaning the Routed Groove**
- Blow out all debris with **oil-free compressed air**.
- For critical applications, sand-blast the groove walls, then air-blow again.
- Ensure the groove is **completely dry** (unless a moisture-tolerant sealant is specified).

**Step 5 — Primer Application (if required)**
- Many polymer sealants (polyurethane, polysulfide) require a **primer** on the concrete walls of the groove to improve adhesion.
- Apply with a brush and allow the primer to become tack-free (typically 15–60 min).

**Step 6 — Backer Rod / Bond Breaker Installation**
- Insert a **back-up rod** (closed-cell polyethylene foam) or a **bond-breaking tape** at the bottom of the groove.
- Purpose: to ensure sealant bonds only to the **two side walls** of the groove and not the bottom — this allows the sealant to stretch and compress freely (the *"two-sided bond"* principle).

**Step 7 — Sealant Application**
- Fill the groove from the bottom up using a **sealant gun**.
- Tool (smooth) the surface with a spatula dipped in soapy water to ensure intimate contact with the side walls and to eliminate air pockets.
- Maintain the **hour-glass cross-section** (sealant slightly recessed from surface).

**Step 8 — Curing**
- Allow the sealant to cure as per manufacturer specifications.
- Protect from dust, water, and mechanical damage for at least **24 hours** (or as specified).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects all governing equations, ratios, and design rules for the Routing and Sealing topic that are tested in KTU examinations.

| # | Quantity / Rule | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Sealant depth (D) in a routed joint | $D = 0.5 \, W$ to $D = W$ | mm | For sealants in non-traffic areas; for traffic areas, $D = W$ to $1.5 W$ |
| 2 | Depth-to-width ratio (non-traffic) | $D / W = 1 : 1$ to $1 : 2$ | — | Hour-glass shape optimisation |
| 3 | Movement Accommodation Factor | $\text{MAF} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_0} \times 100\,\%$ | % | For elastomeric sealants: ±25 % typical |
| 4 | Sealant extension | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{W_{\max} - W_0}{W_0}$ | dimensionless | Must be < MAF of sealant |
| 5 | Sealant compression | $\delta = \dfrac{W_0 - W_{\min}}{W_0}$ | dimensionless | Must be < MAF of sealant |
| 6 | Shear strain in sealant | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta L}{t}$ | dimensionless | $t$ = sealant thickness |
| 7 | Backer rod diameter rule | $d_{\text{rod}} = 1.25 \, W$ | mm | Slight compression of rod gives bond break |
| 8 | Quantity of sealant per metre length | $Q = A_{\text{cross}} \times 1000 \times \rho_{\text{sealant}}$ | g/m | $A_{\text{cross}}$ in cm², $\rho$ in g/cc |
| 9 | Cross-section area of V-groove | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times W \times D$ | mm² | For triangular groove |
| 10 | Cross-section area of U-groove | $A = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D}{4}$ | mm² | Approximate ellipse |
| 11 | Thermal joint movement | $\Delta W = W_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | mm | $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion |
| 12 | Routing cutter speed | $v_c = \dfrac{\pi \, D_{\text{blade}} \, N}{1000}$ | m/min | For saw selection |

> **Convention used in the table:** $W$ = groove width at surface, $D$ = groove depth, $W_0$ = original joint width, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $\alpha$ = linear thermal expansion coefficient, $\rho$ = density.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Routing and Sealing is the **most widely used crack repair technique worldwide** for the following applications:

- **Water-retaining structures** — water tanks, swimming pools, reservoirs, dams.
- **Basement walls and parking decks** — to arrest seepage.
- **Building façade walls** — for crack repair on plaster/RCC.
- **Bridge decks and pavements** — where the underlying concrete is sound.
- **Nuclear and industrial containment structures** — to prevent leakage of hazardous fluids/gases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Routing and Sealing is **not** a structural-strengthening technique. If the load-carrying capacity of the member is reduced due to cracking, this method is insufficient; one must then use **injection grouting (epoxy/polyurethane)**, **FRP wrapping**, or **external prestressing** as per KTU Module 4.

## 2.4 Selection of Sealant Material

| Sealant Type | Movement ± % | Typical Use | Pros | Cons |
|--------------|--------------|-------------|------|------|
| **Silicone** | ±25 to ±50 | Glazing, façade | UV resistant, flexible | Poor paintability |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | ±25 | Water tanks, basements | Abrasion resistant, paintable | UV sensitive |
| **Polysulfide** | ±25 | Fuel tanks, expansion joints | Chemical resistant | Strong odour, slow cure |
| **Epoxy (flexible)** | ±5 to ±10 | Hairline cracks | High strength | Brittle, low movement |
| **Bitumen / Mastic** | ±5 | Roof slabs, terraces | Cheap, waterproof | Softens in heat |
| **Acrylic** | ±10 to ±15 | Interior walls | Easy to apply, paintable | Not for wet areas |
| **Cementitious grout + polymer** | ±0 to ±5 | Damp areas | Bonds well to wet concrete | Low flexibility |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating Sealant Quantity per Metre Length of Crack

A common KTU numerical problem is: *"Given the routed groove dimensions and sealant density, estimate the sealant required per metre of crack."* Let us derive the governing expression.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A V-shaped groove of surface width $W$ mm and depth $D$ mm is routed along a dormant crack. The sealant has a density $\rho$ in g/cc. Calculate the mass of sealant required (in g) per metre length of the groove.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Express the cross-sectional area of the V-groove.

The V-groove is a triangle with base $W$ and height $D$.

$$A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times W \times D$$

We must convert $W$ and $D$ from **mm to cm** for compatibility with $\rho$ in g/cc.

$$A_{\text{cm}^2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{W}{10} \times \dfrac{D}{10} = \dfrac{W \cdot D}{200}$$

The numerical value of $A$ in cm² equals $\dfrac{W \cdot D}{200}$.

**Step 2** — Compute the volume per metre length.

For a length of **1 metre = 100 cm**, the volume of the groove is:

$$V = A_{\text{cm}^2} \times L_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{W \cdot D}{200} \times 100 = \dfrac{W \cdot D}{2} \; \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3** — Compute the mass of sealant.

Since density $\rho$ is in g/cc (= g/cm³), the mass is:

$$Q = V \times \rho = \dfrac{W \cdot D}{2} \times \rho \; \text{grams per metre}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula (V-groove, KTU Exam Favourite):**
> $$Q = \dfrac{W \cdot D \cdot \rho}{2} \quad \text{[g/m]}$$
> where $W$ and $D$ are in mm, and $\rho$ is in g/cc.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Question:** A V-groove of width **20 mm** and depth **15 mm** is routed along a crack. The sealant density is **1.45 g/cc**. Find the sealant mass required per metre of crack length. Also estimate the total mass required for a **30-metre long** crack.

**Solution:**

Given data:
- $W = 20$ mm
- $D = 15$ mm
- $\rho = 1.45$ g/cc
- $L = 30$ m

Substituting in the master formula:

$$Q_{\text{per m}} = \dfrac{W \cdot D \cdot \rho}{2} = \dfrac{20 \times 15 \times 1.45}{2}$$

$$Q_{\text{per m}} = \dfrac{435}{2} = 217.5 \; \text{g/m}$$

For a 30 m length:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 217.5 \times 30 = 6525 \; \text{g} = 6.525 \; \text{kg}$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **217.5 g per metre** is required, or about **6.53 kg** for the entire 30 m crack.

### 3.1.4 Derivation for U-Groove (Half-Ellipse Approximation)

A U-groove cut by a diamond blade approximates a half-ellipse with semi-axes $a = W/2$ and $b = D$.

Area of the full ellipse = $\pi a b$, so area of the half-ellipse is:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \, a \, b}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D}{8}$$

In cm² (with $W, D$ in mm):

$$A_{\text{cm}^2} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot W \cdot D}{8 \times 100} = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D}{800}$$

Volume per metre length:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D}{800} \times 100 = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D}{8} \; \text{cm}^3$$

Mass per metre:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \, W \, D \cdot \rho}{8} \; \text{g/m}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Joint Movement and Sealant Strain

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A sealed joint of original width $W_0$ = 20 mm is installed at a reference temperature of 30 °C. The structure is expected to experience temperatures from 5 °C in winter to 55 °C in summer. Coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Check whether a sealant with MAF = ±25% is suitable.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Identify temperature extremes.

$$T_{\min} = 5 \, °C, \quad T_{\max} = 55 \, °C, \quad T_{\text{ref}} = 30 \, °C$$

**Step 2** — Compute temperature drops and rises.

$$\Delta T_{\text{drop}} = T_{\text{ref}} - T_{\min} = 30 - 5 = 25 \, °C$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{rise}} = T_{\max} - T_{\text{ref}} = 55 - 30 = 25 \, °C$$

**Step 3** — Compute free thermal movement of the joint.

For a free slab segment of length $L$, the joint movement $\Delta W$ is:

$$\Delta W = W_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

For a temperature drop:

$$\Delta W_{\text{contraction}} = 20 \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 25 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm} = 0.005 \; \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a single joint, $\Delta W$ is calculated using the **coefficient of expansion and the length of slab contributing to the joint movement**. For simplification, KTU problems often express $\Delta W$ directly as a fraction of $W_0$ using a movement factor $M$ given in the problem.

**Step 4** — Express movement as a percentage of $W_0$.

$$\text{Movement} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_0} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{0.005}{20} \times 100\,\% = 0.025\,\%$$

**Step 5** — Compare with MAF.

The required movement is **0.025 %**, which is far less than the sealant MAF of **±25 %**.

> **Conclusion:** The sealant is comfortably safe for use. The actual movement is over **1000 times** smaller than the sealant's capability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Backer Rod Sizing

The backer rod is sized slightly larger than the joint width to provide a tight fit and act as a bond-breaker.

$$d_{\text{rod}} = 1.25 \times W$$

**Example:** For a routed groove of width $W = 12$ mm, the backer rod diameter is:

$$d_{\text{rod}} = 1.25 \times 12 = 15 \; \text{mm}$$

A **15 mm backer rod** is to be inserted at the bottom of the groove, with sealant occupying the space above.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix gives the **decision logic** for selecting the appropriate routing and sealing configuration.

| Crack Property | Recommended Routing | Recommended Sealant | Sealant Width : Depth |
|----------------|--------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hairline (< 0.3 mm) | Open by chasing with grinder, V-cut 6×6 mm | Epoxy, acrylic | 1 : 1 |
| Fine (0.3–1.0 mm) | V-cut 8×8 mm | Polyurethane | 1 : 1 |
| Medium (1.0–3.0 mm) | V or U-cut 12×10 mm | Polysulfide, PU | 1.2 : 1 |
| Wide (3.0–6.0 mm) | U-cut 20×15 mm with backer rod | Polysulfide, silicone | 1.5 : 1 |
| Moving (live) cracks | **Do not use routing & sealing** | Use injection / FRP | — |
| Wet / leaking cracks | Dry out, then route & seal | Hydrophobic PU, polysulfide | 1 : 1 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Routing and Sealing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inspect and classify crack dormant vs live] --> B[Step 2: Measure crack width with comparator card]
    B --> C{Is the crack dormant and width less than 6 mm?}
    C -- No --> Z1[STOP: Use alternative repair like injection or FRP]
    C -- Yes --> D[Step 3: Surface preparation clean both sides 25 to 40 mm width]
    D --> E[Step 4: Route V or U groove using diamond blade]
    E --> F[Step 5: Clean groove with oil free compressed air]
    F --> G[Step 6: Apply primer if required and let it become tack free]
    G --> H[Step 7: Insert backer rod or bond breaker tape at bottom]
    H --> I[Step 8: Apply sealant with gun from bottom upward]
    I --> J[Step 9: Tool the sealant to form hour glass cross section]
    J --> K[Step 10: Cure for 24 hours protect from dust and water]
    K --> L[Step 11: Final inspection and quality check]
    L --> M[Repair complete: surface watertight and serviceable]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a sealed joint, mapping the components and forces.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Concrete_Member
        C1[Left concrete flank] --- J[Sealed joint zone] --- C2[Right concrete flank]
    end
    subgraph Joint_Zone
        B1[Backer rod at bottom: bond breaker] --- S1[Sealant body: elastic polymer]
        S1 --- A1[Adhesion interface on left wall]
        S1 --- A2[Adhesion interface on right wall]
    end
    subgraph Applied_Loads
        L1[Hydrostatic pressure] --> J
        L2[Thermal expansion] --> J
        L3[Moisture ingress blocked] --> J
    end
    A1 -- bond --> C1
    A2 -- bond --> C2
    B1 -- prevents --> P1[Three sided bond failure]
    S1 -- deforms --> D1[Accommodates movement up to MAF]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Cross-Section of a Properly Sealed Joint

```
   CONCRETE SURFACE
   ═══════════════════════════════════════
   |           \           /            |
   |            \  SEALANT/              |
   |             \  HOUR /               |
   |              \GLASS/                |
   |               \  /                  |
   |    (no bond)  )(  (bond here)       |
   |               \/                   |
   |             BACKER ROD             |
   |             (foam)                 |
   |                                      |
   ═══════════════════════════════════════
   CONCRETE SUBSTRATE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The sealant has an **hour-glass** cross-section.
- It is **bonded** only to the two side walls (2-sided bond).
- The backer rod prevents 3-sided bond and acts as a compressible base.

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Correct vs Incorrect Routing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Correct_Geometry
        X1[Surface] --> Y1[Wide at top, narrow at bottom: V groove]
        Y1 --> Z1[Sealant forms hour glass]
    end
    subgraph Incorrect_Geometry
        X2[Surface] --> Y2[Pure rectangular slot]
        Y2 --> Z2[Sealant can be pushed out easily]
    end
    subgraph Why_Hour_Glass_Wins
        H1[Compressive stress on sealant] --> H2[Self tightening under hydrostatic load]
        H3[Tensile strain distributed] --> H4[Sealant stretches uniformly]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently write *"Routing and Sealing is a structural repair method."* This is **wrong** — it is a **serviceability/durability** method. Lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. Many students forget to convert mm → cm when computing sealant volume; dimensional mismatch is the #1 cause of numerical mistakes.
> 3. Always state that the method is for **dormant cracks only** — examiners look for this qualifier.
> 4. Do not recommend routing & sealing for *active* cracks; the answer should explicitly say so and redirect to *injection grouting* or *FRP wrapping*.
> 5. In the diagram, ensure you show the **hour-glass cross-section** and the **backer rod** — partial diagrams lose 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] 
**Define Routing and Sealing. State any four situations where this repair technique is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

> Routing and Sealing is a passive crack repair technique in which a dormant crack in concrete or masonry is first enlarged into a V-shaped or U-shaped groove (routing) and then filled with an elastic sealant (sealing) to restore watertightness and prevent ingress of harmful agents, without restoring structural strength.
>
> **Preferred situations:**
> 1. For **dormant (stable) cracks** of width 0.13 mm to 6 mm.
> 2. **Water-retaining structures** such as water tanks, swimming pools, and reservoirs to arrest leakage.
> 3. **Basement walls, parking decks, and building façades** to prevent moisture and chloride ingress.
> 4. **Roof slabs and terrace floors** where weather-tightness is the primary concern.
> 5. As a **preventive maintenance** measure on non-structural shrinkage or thermal cracks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**List the different types of sealants used in routing and sealing. State the criteria for sealant selection.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Types of sealants:**
> 1. **Silicone sealants** — high movement capability (±25–50%), UV resistant, used in glazing and façades.
> 2. **Polyurethane (PU) sealants** — ±25% movement, abrasion-resistant, paintable, used in basements and water tanks.
> 3. **Polysulfide sealants** — excellent chemical resistance, used in fuel tanks and chemical plants.
> 4. **Epoxy sealants (flexible grades)** — high strength but low movement (±5–10%), used in hairline cracks.
> 5. **Bituminous / mastic sealants** — economical, used in roof slabs.
> 6. **Acrylic sealants** — easy application, used in interior walls.
>
> **Selection criteria:**
> 1. **Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF)** of sealant ≥ expected joint movement.
> 2. **Adhesion** to concrete substrate (with or without primer).
> 3. **Chemical compatibility** with the environment (pH, oils, fuels).
> 4. **UV resistance** for exposed applications.
> 5. **Cure time and pot life** appropriate to site conditions.
> 6. **Temperature range** of service versus that of the location.
> 7. **Paintability** if overcoating is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure of *Routing and Sealing* of cracks in concrete. State the role of backer rod and bond-breaker. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A V-shaped groove of width **25 mm** and depth **20 mm** is routed along a dormant crack. The sealant density is **1.50 g/cc**. Calculate the sealant mass required (in kg) for a **45-metre long** crack. If a backer rod of 1.25 × groove width is used, find the backer rod diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)

1. **Crack Inspection (1 mark):** Identify the crack, measure its width with a comparator card, and confirm that the crack is *dormant* (not actively growing). Mark the routing line.

2. **Surface Preparation (0.5 mark):** Clean both sides of the crack for at least 25–40 mm; remove dust, oil, laitance, and loose material using wire brush and compressed air.

3. **Routing (1.5 marks):** Cut a V- or U-shaped groove of width 6–25 mm and depth 6–25 mm along the entire crack length using a diamond tuck-point blade on an angle grinder. The V/U-shape is essential to retain the sealant and provide an hour-glass cross-section.

4. **Groove Cleaning (0.5 mark):** Blow out all debris with oil-free compressed air; sand-blast side walls if necessary.

5. **Primer Application (0.5 mark):** Apply a compatible primer to the side walls of the groove and allow it to become tack-free (for PU, polysulfide sealants).

6. **Backer Rod / Bond Breaker (1.5 marks):** Insert a closed-cell polyethylene foam backer rod (or apply bond-breaker tape) at the bottom of the groove. Its **role** is:
   - To **prevent three-sided adhesion** of the sealant.
   - To ensure **two-sided bond** (only to the side walls), allowing the sealant to stretch and compress freely with joint movement.
   - To control the **depth of sealant** and provide a compressible base.
   - To prevent sealant from sagging into wide gaps.

7. **Sealant Application (1 mark):** Apply sealant from bottom up using a sealant gun. Tool the surface to a slightly concave (recessed) finish to ensure intimate contact and to expel air.

8. **Curing (0.5 mark):** Protect from dust, water, and mechanical damage for at least 24 hours.

> **Role of backer rod summary:** It acts as a **bond-breaker** and **depth-control** device, ensuring the sealant experiences only two-sided bond. This enables the sealant to accommodate joint movement without cohesive or adhesive failure.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Groove width $W = 25$ mm
- Groove depth $D = 20$ mm
- Sealant density $\rho = 1.50$ g/cc
- Crack length $L = 45$ m

**Step 1 — Sealant mass per metre length (V-groove):**

$$Q = \dfrac{W \cdot D \cdot \rho}{2} = \dfrac{25 \times 20 \times 1.50}{2}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{750}{2} = 375 \; \text{g/m}$$

**Step 2 — Total sealant mass for 45 m:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 375 \times 45 = 16875 \; \text{g} = 16.875 \; \text{kg}$$

> **Answer (Sealant):** Approximately **16.88 kg** of sealant is required for the 45 m crack.

**Step 3 — Backer rod diameter:**

$$d_{\text{rod}} = 1.25 \times W = 1.25 \times 25 = 31.25 \; \text{mm}$$

> **Answer (Backer Rod):** A **31.25 mm** (≈ 32 mm) backer rod is to be used.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice to Question A*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the criteria for **selection of sealant material** for routing and sealing. Compare polysulfide, polyurethane, and silicone sealants in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A U-shaped groove of width **15 mm** and depth **12 mm** is routed along a 60 m dormant crack. The sealant density is **1.40 g/cc**. Compute the sealant quantity required (in kg). If the original joint width is 20 mm and the structure experiences a temperature change of 40 °C, with $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, find the thermal movement and check whether a sealant of MAF ±25% is adequate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Sealant Selection (7 marks)

**Selection criteria (3.5 marks):**

1. **Movement capability (MAF):** The sealant's MAF must exceed the expected joint movement.
2. **Adhesion properties:** Strong bond to concrete (and metal, if applicable) — primers may be needed.
3. **Chemical resistance:** Suitability to acids, alkalis, oils, fuels in the environment.
4. **Weather and UV resistance:** Especially for outdoor / exposed joints.
5. **Service temperature range:** Must cover the local minimum and maximum temperatures.
6. **Cure time and method:** Single-component moisture-cure vs two-component mix.
7. **Paintability and aesthetics:** Important for façades.
8. **Cost and shelf life.**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Property | Polysulfide | Polyurethane | Silicone |
|----------|-------------|--------------|----------|
| Movement capability | ±25% | ±25% | ±25% to ±50% |
| UV resistance | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent (fuels) | Good | Moderate |
| Paintability | Good | Good | Poor |
| Typical use | Fuel tanks, chemical plants | Basements, water tanks | Glazing, façades |
| Adhesion to concrete | Good (with primer) | Excellent (with primer) | Good (with primer) |
| Durability (years) | 15–20 | 10–15 | 20+ |
| Cost | High | Moderate | High |

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- U-groove width $W = 15$ mm, depth $D = 12$ mm
- Length $L = 60$ m
- Sealant density $\rho = 1.40$ g/cc
- Original joint width $W_0 = 20$ mm
- $\Delta T = 40$ °C, $\alpha = 10 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- MAF of sealant = ±25%

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of U-groove (half-ellipse approximation):**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot W \cdot D}{8} = \dfrac{\pi \times 15 \times 12}{8} = \dfrac{180 \pi}{8} = 70.685 \; \text{mm}^2$$

Convert to cm² (since 1 cm² = 100 mm²):

$$A_{\text{cm}^2} = \dfrac{70.685}{100} = 0.70685 \; \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume per metre length:**

$$V_{\text{per m}} = A_{\text{cm}^2} \times L_{\text{cm}} = 0.70685 \times 100 = 70.685 \; \text{cm}^3 / \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Mass per metre length:**

$$Q_{\text{per m}} = V \times \rho = 70.685 \times 1.40 = 98.96 \; \text{g/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total mass for 60 m:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 98.96 \times 60 = 5937.6 \; \text{g} \approx 5.94 \; \text{kg}$$

> **Answer (Sealant Quantity):** Approximately **5.94 kg** of sealant is required for the 60 m crack.

**Step 5 — Thermal movement of joint:**

The free thermal movement is given by:

$$\Delta W = W_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 20 \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 40$$

$$\Delta W = 20 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm} = 0.008 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Movement as a percentage of $W_0$:**

$$\text{Movement (\%)} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.008}{20} \times 100 = 0.04\,\%$$

**Step 7 — Compare with MAF:**

The actual movement (0.04 %) is **far less** than the sealant's MAF of ±25%.

> **Answer (Adequacy Check):** The sealant is **comfortably safe** — the required movement is approximately **625 times smaller** than the sealant's capacity. Hence, the ±25% MAF sealant is **highly adequate**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Routing and Sealing**

- **Definition:** Routing = enlarging crack into a V/U groove. Sealing = filling groove with elastic sealant.
- **Used for:** Dormant cracks (non-moving), width **0.13 mm to 6 mm**.
- **NOT used for:** Active/live cracks (use injection, FRP instead).
- **Restores:** Watertightness, durability, aesthetics — **NOT structural strength**.
- **Groove shape:** V or U — to give sealant an **hour-glass cross-section**.
- **Standard dimensions:** Width **6–25 mm**, depth **6–25 mm**, $D/W$ ratio **1:1 to 1.5:1**.
- **Two-sided bond principle:** Sealant must bond only to side walls, not the bottom. **Backer rod is mandatory** in wide joints.
- **Backer rod diameter rule:** $d_{\text{rod}} = 1.25 \times W$.
- **Sealant mass formula (V-groove):** $Q = \dfrac{W \cdot D \cdot \rho}{2}$ g/m.
- **Sealant mass formula (U-groove):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot W \cdot D \cdot \rho}{8}$ g/m.
- **Sealant types to remember:** Silicone, Polyurethane (PU), Polysulfide, Epoxy, Bitumen, Acrylic.
- **Most common MAF:** ±25% (for PU, polysulfide); ±50% (for premium silicone).
- **Sequence (must follow in this order):** Inspect → Classify → Clean → Route → Clean groove → Primer → Backer rod → Sealant → Tool → Cure.
- **Dimensional conversion trap:** Convert mm → cm before using density in g/cc.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for actively moving cracks; not a structural-strengthening method.
- **KTU-favourite numerical:** Compute sealant quantity using V-groove or U-groove formula.
- **Diagram must show:** Hour-glass cross-section + backer rod at bottom + bond only to side walls.
- **Key terminology to use in exams:** *Dormant crack*, *Hour-glass section*, *Two-sided bond*, *Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF)*, *Backer rod*, *Bond-breaker*, *V-groove*, *Pot life*, *Tack-free time*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stitching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stitching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Stitching

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stitching** is a specialized structural repair and rehabilitation technique used to restore the tensile strength and monolithic action across a fractured section of a structural member (concrete or masonry) by embedding reinforcement elements (such as stainless steel bars, carbon fiber, or glass fiber anchors) that bridge the crack on both sides and are bonded into the parent material using high-strength grout or epoxy resin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Stitching is a localized repair method in which reinforcement is placed across a crack in such a way that it carries tensile forces and prevents the crack from re-opening, thereby re-establishing structural continuity.

The reinforcement pieces that physically bridge the crack are colloquially called **"stitches"** (analogous to surgical stitches that hold torn tissue together). The most common types include:

- **Stainless Steel Stitching Bars** (commonly **8 mm to 12 mm** diameter).
- **Carbon Fiber (CFRP) Stitching Rods**.
- **Glass Fiber (GFRP) Stitching Rods**.
- **Helical Stainless Steel Ties** (used predominantly in masonry).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a **favorite ceramic cup that develops a hairline crack** down the side. If you simply apply glue along the crack, the cup may hold for a while, but under repeated use, the crack will re-open because glue alone cannot carry tension. So, what do you do? You **drill tiny holes on both sides of the crack**, insert small metal pins (or staples) that physically cross the crack line, and bond them in place with strong adhesive. Now, even if someone tries to pull the two halves apart, the pins (stitches) transfer the tensile force and keep the cup intact.

**That is exactly what stitching does in a building structure.** It is the civil engineer's version of "surgical suturing" of a cracked beam, wall, or column. The structure is sick, the crack is its wound, and the stitches are the metal/FRP anchors that close the wound and restore strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stitching and not just Epoxy Injection?**
> - *Epoxy injection* alone restores compressive load transfer and seals the crack, but it has **low tensile strength across the crack plane** if the crack is active or wide.
> - *Stitching* provides **positive mechanical anchorage** on both sides of the crack, ensuring that tensile forces are carried by the reinforcement, not just the adhesive.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally adopted in KTU-endorsed repair codes (IS 13315, ACI 546R, EN 1504):

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stitch Bar Diameter | **8 – 16** | mm |
| Stitch Embedment Length on Each Side | **Minimum 75 mm** (typically 100–150 mm) | mm |
| Spacing Between Stitches Along the Crack | **150 – 300** | mm |
| Hole Diameter for Grouting | $\geq$ Bar Diameter + **6 mm** | mm |
| Bonding Grout Compressive Strength | $\geq$ **40 MPa** at 28 days | MPa |
| Typical Epoxy Resin Bond Strength | $\geq$ **15 MPa** | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Materials Used:**
> 1. **Stainless Steel (Grade 304 / 316):** Used when corrosion resistance is critical (marine/coastal zones).
> 2. **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP):** Used for high strength-to-weight ratio applications.
> 3. **Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP):** Cost-effective alternative for masonry stitching.
> 4. **High-Modulus Epoxy Resin / Non-shrink Cementitious Grout:** Used as bonding medium.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown – How Stitching Works

Stitching works on a simple but powerful **load transfer mechanism**. The complete logical chain is as follows:

1. **Crack Identification & Mapping:** The crack is located, its width is measured (typically 0.2 mm to 6 mm), and its orientation (vertical, horizontal, diagonal) is documented. Active (moving) cracks are distinguished from dormant cracks.

2. **Hole Drilling Across the Crack:** Holes are drilled on **both sides of the crack** at an angle of approximately **90° to the crack plane** (or slightly inclined for better anchorage) using rotary hammer drills. Hole depth on each side is typically 100–150 mm, and hole diameter is bar diameter + 6 mm clearance.

3. **Surface Preparation:** The crack surface and the holes are cleaned using compressed air and acetone to remove dust, laitance, and contaminants. This step is **critical for bond strength**.

4. **Reinforcement (Stitch) Insertion:** Stainless steel or FRP bars are cut to the required length. They are pre-bent if needed (e.g., for horizontal masonry cracks, L-shaped stitches are used).

5. **Grouting / Bonding:** The holes are filled with **non-shrink epoxy resin or cementitious grout** from the back using a grout injection nozzle, and the bar is inserted with a slow rotary motion to expel trapped air. Some protocols also bond the bar **externally along the surface** of the crack.

6. **Curing:** The grout/epoxy is allowed to cure for **24–72 hours** depending on product specifications.

7. **Load Transfer Activation:** Once cured, the stitch becomes a **tension tie**. When the structure tries to re-open the crack under load, the stitch engages in tension and transfers the force deep into the parent concrete on both sides.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core "Why" Behind Stitching:**
> Stitching is preferred over epoxy injection alone when the crack is **structurally significant** (i.e., it carries load in the original design) and when **tensile reinforcement continuity** is required across the fracture plane.

## 2.2 Types of Stitching Techniques (KTU Syllabus Map)

| S.No. | Stitching Type | Application | Bonding Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Grouted Stitching (Steel Bars)** | Reinforced concrete beams, columns | Non-shrink cementitious grout |
| 2 | **Epoxy-Bonded Stitching (FRP)** | Concrete slabs, bridge decks | Epoxy resin |
| 3 | **Helical Stitching (Masonry)** | Cracked brick/block masonry walls | Polymer-modified grout |
| 4 | **Surface-Bonded Stitching** | Where holes cannot be drilled (thin members) | Epoxy adhesive + external plate |
| 5 | **Cross-Stitching (X-pattern)** | Diagonal cracks, shear cracks | Epoxy or grout |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU ESE for the Stitching topic. These are the ones you **must memorize**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the formulas below, the symbol $\vert$ is used for absolute value (not the vertical pipe character $\vert$) to keep KTU markdown tables from breaking.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$n = \dfrac{F_{total}}{F_{stitch}}$$ | Number of stitches required along the crack | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $$F_{stitch} = \sigma_y \cdot A_b = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ | Tensile capacity of a single stitch (yield strength basis) | N |
| 3 | $$f_{bond} = \dfrac{F_{stitch}}{\pi \cdot d \cdot L_e}$$ | Average bond stress at the grout-steel interface | MPa |
| 4 | $$L_{e} \geq \dfrac{F_{stitch}}{\pi \cdot d \cdot f_{bond,allow}}$$ | Minimum embedment length required on each side | mm |
| 5 | $$s = \dfrac{F_{stitch}}{w_{crack} \cdot t_{eff}}$$ | Maximum spacing of stitches along the crack (transfer of distributed tensile force) | mm |
| 6 | $$V_{stitch} = 0.6 \cdot f_y \cdot A_b$$ | Shear capacity of a stitch (when used across shear cracks) | N |
| 7 | $$F_{design} = \phi \cdot F_{stitch}$$ | Design capacity of a stitch (with strength reduction factor $\phi \approx 0.9$) | N |

**Where:**
- $F_{total}$ = total tensile force to be transferred across the crack (N)
- $F_{stitch}$ = tensile capacity of one stitch (N)
- $\sigma_y$ / $f_y$ = yield strength of stitch material (MPa) — **typically 500 MPa for stainless steel, 2500 MPa for CFRP**
- $A_b$ = cross-sectional area of the stitch bar (mm²)
- $d$ = diameter of stitch bar (mm)
- $L_e$ = embedment length on one side of the crack (mm)
- $f_{bond,allow}$ = allowable bond stress (**typically 1.5–2.5 MPa for cement grout, 6–10 MPa for epoxy**)
- $w_{crack}$ = crack width (mm)
- $t_{eff}$ = effective depth of tensile force transfer (mm)
- $\phi$ = strength reduction factor

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Stitching

Stitching is widely used in:

- **Bridge Piers and Decks:** Repairing horizontal/diagonal cracks caused by thermal gradients, corrosion, or impact.
- **Heritage Masonry Buildings:** Stitching cracked brick/stone walls without disturbing the historical façade.
- **Dam Spillways and Retaining Walls:** Repairing shrinkage or movement cracks.
- **Industrial Floor Slabs:** Stitching control joint failures.
- **Earthquake-Damaged Columns:** As part of seismic retrofitting (cross-stitching pattern).
- **Underground Tunnels:** Stitching shrinkage cracks in tunnel linings.
- **Pre-cast Concrete Segments:** Reconnecting cracked segments without full replacement.

> [!NOTE]
