> $$h_o = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 M}{b f_r}} - h_s$$
> where:
> - $h_o$ = overlay thickness (mm)
> - $M$ = applied service moment (N·mm)
> - $b$ = beam width (mm)
> - $f_r$ = modulus of rupture of overlay concrete (MPa)
> - $h_s$ = effective substrate depth (mm)

**Step 7 — Numerical example**

Given: $M = 50$ kN·m $= 50 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $b = 300$ mm, $f'_c = 30$ MPa, $h_s = 200$ mm.

Compute the modulus of rupture:

$$
f_r = 0.62 \sqrt{30} = 0.62 \times 5.477 = 3.396 \text{ MPa}
$$

Compute the total depth required:

$$
h = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 50 \times 10^6}{300 \times 3.396}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{300 \times 10^6}{1018.8}} = \sqrt{294{,}500} = 542.7 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the overlay thickness is:

$$
h_o = 542.7 - 200 = 342.7 \text{ mm} \approx 345 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering interpretation**
> Such a thick overlay is uneconomical. In practice, the engineer may either **strengthen the substrate first** (e.g., by external CFRP) or use a **higher-strength concrete** in the overlay. This is why bonded overlays are limited to **thin sections** of high-performance materials.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bond Stress from Slant Shear Test (ASTM C882)

**Step 1 — Test geometry**

A standard cylinder of diameter $D = 150$ mm is cast with the substrate occupying the lower portion (forming a 30° angle with the vertical). The overlay occupies the upper portion.

**Step 2 — Applied compressive load**

An axial compressive load $P$ is applied. At the 30° interface, the load resolves into:

- Normal component (compression): $N = P \cos 30°$
- Shear component (along interface): $V = P \sin 30°$

**Step 3 — Interface area**

The slant area is:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\sin 30°} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}
$$

Wait — let us derive carefully. The projected area on the horizontal plane is $A_{proj} = \pi D^2 / 4$. The slant interface area is:

$$
A_{slant} = \dfrac{A_{proj}}{\cos \theta} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\cos 30°}
$$

For $\theta = 30°$:

$$
\cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

So:

$$
A_{slant} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4 \times 0.866} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{3.464}
$$

**Step 4 — Bond shear stress**

The shear stress at the interface is:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{slant}} = \dfrac{P \sin 30°}{A_{slant}} = \dfrac{P \times 0.5}{\pi D^2 / 3.464}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{0.5 \times 3.464 \times P}{\pi D^2} = \dfrac{1.732 \cdot P}{\pi D^2}
$$

Or in the cleaner form:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{P \tan 30°}{(\pi D^2 / 4)} = \dfrac{4 P \tan 30°}{\pi D^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Bond strength expression**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final Result**
> $$\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{4 P \tan 30°}{\pi D^2} = \dfrac{2.309 \, P}{\pi D^2}$$
> where:
> - $\tau_{bond}$ = shear bond strength (MPa)
> - $P$ = applied compressive load at failure (N)
> - $D$ = cylinder diameter (mm)

**Step 6 — Numerical example**

Given: $P = 200$ kN $= 200{,}000$ N, $D = 150$ mm.

$$
\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{2.309 \times 200{,}000}{\pi \times 150^2} = \dfrac{461{,}800}{70{,}686} = 6.53 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is well above the **ICRI 210.1R minimum of 1.7 MPa**, indicating a successful bond.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Modulus of a Composite Overlay Section

For a **bonded** overlay (modulus $E_o$, thickness $h_o$) on a substrate (modulus $E_s$, thickness $h_s$), the **transformed section method** is used.

**Step 1 — Modular ratio**

$$
n = \dfrac{E_o}{E_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Transformed width of overlay**

The overlay width $b$ is transformed to an equivalent substrate width:

$$
b' = n \cdot b
$$

**Step 3 — Transformed moment of inertia**

Taking the **neutral axis** of the composite section (located at $\bar{y}$ from the top of overlay):

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{b' h_o \cdot (h_o / 2) + b h_s \cdot (h_o + h_s / 2)}{b' h_o + b h_s}
$$

Then:

$$
I_{transformed} = \dfrac{b' h_o^3}{12} + b' h_o \left(\bar{y} - \dfrac{h_o}{2}\right)^2 + \dfrac{b h_s^3}{12} + b h_s \left(h_o + \dfrac{h_s}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Effective flexural rigidity**

$$
(EI)_{eff} = E_s \cdot I_{transformed}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering use**
> The effective rigidity governs the **deflection and stress distribution** in a composite overlay beam. If $E_o \gg E_s$ (e.g., polymer overlay on weak concrete), the overlay attracts disproportionate stress and may debond.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Overlay Selection by Exposure Condition

| Exposure / Damage Type | Recommended Overlay | Bond Requirement | Minimum Thickness |
|------------------------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Surface abrasion** (industrial floor) | Epoxy or PU overlay | ≥ 2.0 MPa | 6 mm |
| **Carbonation (cover loss)** | Polymer-modified cementitious | ≥ 1.7 MPa | 25 mm |
| **Chloride ingress (bridge deck)** | LMC or silica-fume concrete | ≥ 1.7 MPa | 40 mm |
| **Freeze-thaw scaling** | Air-entrained LMC | ≥ 1.7 MPa | 50 mm |
| **Chemical attack (acid)** | Epoxy or vinyl ester | ≥ 2.0 MPa | 8 mm |
| **Heavy cracking (≥ 1 mm)** | Unbonded concrete overlay | Not critical | 150 mm |
| **Differential settlement** | Unbonded fibre-reinforced | Not critical | 100 mm |
| **Waterproofing** | Methacrylate or PU | ≥ 1.5 MPa | 4 mm |
| **Pavement restoration** | Whitetopping (concrete on asphalt) | Friction-based | 100 mm |

## 3.5 Shrinkage-Induced Stress in Bonded Overlay (Analytical)

The differential shrinkage between overlay and substrate induces **shear stress at the interface**, which can cause debonding.

**Step 1 — Free shrinkage strain differential**

$$
\Delta \varepsilon = \varepsilon_{sh,o} - \varepsilon_{sh,s}
$$

**Step 2 — Restrained shrinkage stress in overlay**

For a fully restrained overlay slab of length $L$:

$$
\sigma_{sh} = E_o \cdot \Delta \varepsilon \cdot (1 - e^{-k t})
$$

where $k$ is the creep relaxation factor.

**Step 3 — Interface shear demand**

The shear demand per unit width is:

$$
\tau_{interface} = \dfrac{\sigma_{sh} \cdot h_o}{L / 2} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{sh} h_o}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Check against bond capacity**

$$
\tau_{interface} \leq \tau_{bond,allowable}
$$

> [!WARNING] **Design Pitfall (KTU Examiner)**
> Shrinkage-induced debonding is the **#1 cause of bonded overlay failure** in tropical climates (Kerala). The design must account for **curing protocol, joint spacing, and shrinkage-compensating additives** (e.g., Type K shrinkage-compensating cement).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Overlay Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Overlays Module 4 KTU]:::root
    A0 --> B1[Cementitious Overlays]:::cement
    A0 --> B2[Polymer Overlays]:::polymer
    A0 --> B3[Polymer Modified Cementitious]:::pmc
    A0 --> B4[Fiber Reinforced Overlays]:::fiber
    A0 --> B5[Bituminous Overlays]:::bitu
    A0 --> B6[Specialty Overlays]:::spec

    B1 --> C1A[Bonded]
    B1 --> C1B[Unbonded]
    B1 --> C1C[Partially Bonded]
    B1 --> C1D[Whitetopping]
    B1 --> C1E[Ultra Thin Whitetopping]

    B2 --> C2A[Epoxy]
    B2 --> C2B[Methacrylate]
    B2 --> C2C[Polyester]
    B2 --> C2D[Polyurethane]

    B3 --> C3A[Latex Modified SBR]
    B3 --> C3B[Acrylic Modified]
    B3 --> C3C[Epoxy Modified]

    B4 --> C4A[Steel Fiber]
    B4 --> C4B[Glass Fiber]
    B4 --> C4C[Carbon Fiber]
    B4 --> C4D[Polypropylene Fiber]

    B5 --> C5A[Conventional HMA]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define an overlay in the context of repair and rehabilitation of structures. List any **four** types of overlays commonly used in concrete restoration work.*

**Model Answer:**

An **overlay** is a supplementary layer of cementitious, polymeric, or bituminous material applied onto the surface of an existing structural element to restore lost section, enhance durability, augment load capacity, or seal surface defects — without complete demolition of the parent substrate.

The **four types** of overlays are:

1. **Cementitious Concrete Overlays** — Bonded, unbonded, or partially bonded conventional concrete.
2. **Polymer Overlays** — Epoxy, methacrylate, polyester, or polyurethane overlays.
3. **Polymer-Modified Cementitious Overlays** — Latex-modified or acrylic-modified mortars.
4. **Fiber-Reinforced Overlays** — SFRC, GFRC, or polypropylene-fiber overlays for crack control.

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~80 words, 3 marks**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*What is the minimum acceptable **direct tensile bond strength** for a structural overlay, as per ICRI 210.1R guidelines? What are the **three failure modes** observed in a pull-off (direct tension) test?*

**Model Answer:**

The minimum acceptable **direct tensile bond strength** for a structural overlay, as per **ICRI 210.1R**, is **1.7 MPa (250 psi)**.

The **three failure modes** in a pull-off test are:

1. **Substrate Failure** — Failure occurs within the parent concrete (acceptable — indicates substrate is weaker than bond).
2. **Overlay Failure** — Failure occurs within the overlay material (acceptable — indicates overlay cohesive strength is the limiting factor).
3. **Adhesive/Interface Failure** — Failure occurs at the bond interface (unacceptable — indicates inadequate surface preparation or bonding agent).

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~80 words, 3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Model Question

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Series 1, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss in detail the **classification of cementitious overlays** based on their bond behavior. Explain the **design philosophy** of bonded overlays with reference to the **ACI 318 thickness formula**, and clearly state the assumptions involved.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Describe **surface preparation techniques** for overlays. Compare **shotblasting**, **hydroblasting**, and **grinding** in terms of surface profile (CSP classification), cost, productivity, and suitability for different substrate conditions.*

#### Model Answer to Part (a)

**Classification of Cementitious Overlays by Bond Behaviour:**

**1. Bonded Overlays**
- The overlay is **chemically and mechanically anchored** to the substrate.
- Both layers act as a **single composite section** under load.
- Used when the **substrate is sound**, with only surface deterioration.
- Thickness typically ranges from **25 mm to 75 mm**.
- Failure by **interface debonding** if surface prep is inadequate.

**2. Unbonded Overlays**
- A **debonding medium** (polyethylene sheet, bitumen-impregnated board, geotextile) physically separates the two layers.
- Each layer acts **independently** under load.
- Used when the **substrate is heavily cracked**, variable in stiffness, or has delamination.
- Thickness typically **100 mm – 225 mm**.
- Design is more forgiving but material-inefficient.

**3. Partially Bonded Overlays**
- An **intermediate real-world condition** where bond is incomplete due to dust, moisture, laitance, or operator error.
- This is the **most dangerous design assumption** because engineers often *assume* full bond in design but achieve *partial* bond in execution.

**Design Philosophy of Bonded Overlays (ACI 318):**

The ACI 318 thickness formula is derived from **flexural mechanics** of a rectangular composite section:

$$
h_o = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 M}{b f_r}} - h_s
$$

**Assumptions:**

1. Plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory).
2. The overlay and substrate behave as a **monolithic composite**.
3. **Full composite action** is achieved — no slip at the interface.
4. The overlay is **uncracked** and resists tensile bending stress.
5. Substrate is **structurally sound** and free of delamination.
6. **Linear-elastic** material behaviour under service loads.
7. **Modulus of rupture** of the overlay governs flexural capacity.

**Critical step-by-step application:**

Step 1 — Compute the **service bending moment** $M$ at the critical section.
Step 2 — Compute the **modulus of rupture** $f_r = 0.62 \sqrt{f'_c}$ of the overlay concrete.
Step 3 — Compute the **total required depth** $h$ from $h = \sqrt{6M / (b f_r)}$.
Step 4 — Subtract the **substrate depth** $h_s$ to obtain the **overlay thickness** $h_o$.

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~350 words, 7 marks**

#### Model Answer to Part (b)

**Surface Preparation Techniques for Overlays:**

Surface preparation is the **single most important determinant of overlay performance**. The ICRI 210.1R document defines **Concrete Surface Profiles (CSP 1 – CSP 9)** that quantitatively describe the prepared surface texture.

**1. Shotblasting**
- Method: Steel shot propelled by centrifugal wheel onto the surface.
- Achieves: **CSP 3 – CSP 7** (typical for bonded overlays: **CSP 5 – CSP 7**).
- Productivity: **200 – 500 m²/hr** (high).
- Cost: **Moderate**.
- Suitability: **Best for large horizontal surfaces** (bridge decks, parking slabs). Does not produce micro-cracks in substrate.
- Dust: Moderate (requires HEPA dust collection).

**2. Hydroblasting (Water Jetting)**
- Method: Ultra-high-pressure water (10,000 – 40,000 psi).
- Achieves: **CSP 5 – CSP 9** (depending on pressure).
- Productivity: **100 – 300 m²/hr**.
- Cost: **High** (specialised equipment).
- Suitability: **Best for chloride-contaminated decks** (removes contaminated paste). Removes unsound concrete without vibration. No micro-cracking.
- Limitation: **Wet substrate** requires 24-hour drying period before overlay.

**3. Grinding (Diamond Grinding)**
- Method: Rotating diamond abrasive discs.
- Achieves: **CSP 1 – CSP 3** (light texture only).
- Productivity: **50 – 150 m²/hr** (slow).
- Cost: **Low to moderate**.
- Suitability: **Best for thin polymer overlays** (3 – 6 mm) on floors. Not aggressive enough for thick concrete overlays.
- Limitation: May **polish aggregate**, reducing mechanical interlock.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Shotblasting | Hydroblasting | Grinding |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|----------|
| CSP Range | 3 – 7 | 5 – 9 | 1 – 3 |
| Productivity | High | Moderate | Low |
| Cost | Moderate | High | Low |
| Substrate Damage | None | None | Possible polishing |
| Chloride Removal | Partial | Excellent | None |
| Best Use | Bonded concrete overlay | Chloride-contaminated deck | Thin polymer overlay |

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~350 words, 7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Series 2, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the **mechanism of bond** between an overlay and the substrate. Discuss the **four mechanisms** of bond development with neat sketches/flowcharts, and state how each contributes to the overall shear capacity at the interface.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the **properties and applications** of: (i) **Epoxy overlay**, (ii) **Latex-modified concrete overlay**, and (iii) **Silica-fume concrete overlay**. Present your answer in a structured tabular form with reference to bond strength, permeability, chemical resistance, typical thickness, and a representative field application.*

#### Model Answer to Part (a)

**Mechanism of Bond Between Overlay and Substrate:**

The **interface** between an overlay and the existing concrete is the **weakest link** in any rehabilitation scheme. Bonding at this interface is achieved through **four concurrent mechanisms**:

**1. Mechanical Interlock**
- The fresh overlay paste **penetrates the open pores, micro-cavities, and rough texture** of the prepared substrate.
- Upon hardening, the overlay acts like a **3D puzzle piece** locked into the substrate topography.
- Governed by: **CSP (Concrete Surface Profile)** rating; CSP 5 – CSP 7 is recommended for concrete overlays.
- Contribution to shear capacity: **40 – 60%** of total bond strength.

**2. Chemical Adhesion**
- The hydration products of fresh cement (**C-S-H gel, calcium hydroxide**) form **molecular-level chemical bonds** with the residual unhydrated cement and silica on the substrate surface.
- This is essentially a **C-S-H to C-S-H bridging** phenomenon.
- Maximized when the substrate is **SSD (Saturated Surface Dry)** at the time of overlay placement.
- Contribution: **20 – 30%** of total bond strength.

**3. Adhesive Bonding (Bonding Agent Layer)**
- A **separate adhesive layer** (e.g., epoxy, SBR latex, acrylic) is applied at the interface.
- The adhesive **wets and seals** the substrate, providing a transitional chemical bridge.
- Typical products: **ASTM C881 Type V epoxy**, SBR latex bonding slurry.
- Contribution: **15 – 25%** of total bond strength.

**4. Friction / Shear Keying**
- The **rough macro-texture** (exposed aggregate) creates discrete shear keys that resist horizontal slip.
- Effective under **compressive normal stress** at the interface (due to overlay self-weight + live load).
- Contribution: **5 – 15%** of total bond strength.

**Overall Shear Capacity at Interface:**

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_{mech} + \tau_{chem} + \tau_{adh} + \tau_{fric}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~350 words, 7 marks**

#### Model Answer to Part (b)

**Comparison of Three Overlay Materials:**

| Property | Epoxy Overlay | Latex-Modified Concrete (LMC) | Silica-Fume Concrete Overlay |
|----------|---------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Binder system** | 100% epoxy resin (no cement) | Portland cement + SBR latex (15–20% solids) | Portland cement + 5–10% silica fume |
| **Tensile bond strength** | ≥ 2.5 MPa (very high) | ≥ 1.7 MPa | ≥ 1.7 MPa |
| **Compressive strength** | 55 – 110 MPa | 30 – 50 MPa | 40 – 70 MPa |
| **Permeability** | Very low (near-zero) | Low (latex seals capillaries) | Very low (pozzolanic densification) |
| **Chemical resistance** | Excellent (acids, alkalis, solvents — but UV-sensitive) | Good (alkali-resistant) | Moderate (alkali-silica risk if not controlled) |
| **Cure time** | 4 – 24 hours (rapid) | 3 – 7 days (moist cure) | 7 days (moist cure) |
| **Typical thickness** | 3 – 12 mm (thin) | 25 – 50 mm | 25 – 75 mm |
| **UV stability** | Poor (chalks on exposure) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Temperature tolerance** | Up to 80°C (glass transition limit) | Up to 200°C | Up to 300°C |
| **Cost per m²** | High (₹800 – 1500) | Moderate (₹400 – 700) | Moderate (₹500 – 800) |
| **Field application** | Industrial floors, chemical plants, parking decks (top coat) | Bridge decks, parking garages, marine structures | Bridge decks (LSDC), high-traffic pavements, dam spillways |
| **Standards** | ASTM C881, ACI 548 | ACI 548.4, ASTM C1438 | ACI 234R, ASTM C1240 |
| **Limitations** | Moisture-sensitive during cure; thermal expansion mismatch with concrete | Requires careful latex selection; not for very thin applications | Higher drying shrinkage; requires extended moist curing |

**Representative Field Applications:**

- **Epoxy Overlay** — Chemical-resistant floor in a pharmaceutical plant (3 mm self-levelling epoxy over prepared concrete).
- **LMC Overlay** — Bridge deck overlay on a coastal highway in Kerala (40 mm LMC for chloride protection).
- **Silica-Fume Overlay** — Dam spillway resurfacing where abrasion resistance and low permeability are critical (50 mm silica-fume concrete).

> [!NOTE] **Word Count Target: ~350 words, 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Overlays**
> 1. **Confusing bonded and unbonded thickness formulae** — Using ACI's bonded formula for an unbonded scenario is a **4-mark deduction** and indicates conceptual confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting shrinkage effects** — Kerala's tropical climate (high temperature, 70–95% RH) makes differential shrinkage the **dominant debonding cause** in bonded overlays. Always mention **joint spacing, curing protocol, and shrinkage-compensating admixtures**.
> 3. **Ignoring CSP classification** — Vague answers like "surface should be clean" lose **2 marks**. Always cite **CSP 5 – CSP 7** for bonded cementitious overlays (per ICRI 210.1R).
> 4. **Stating bond strength without test reference** — Specify **ASTM C1583 (pull-off), C882 (slant shear), or C78 (flexural bond)**.
> 5. **Recommending polymer overlays for outdoor bridge decks** without addressing **UV degradation** — Examiner marks this as a materials selection error.
> 6. **Omitting the modulus of rupture formula** $f_r = 0.62\sqrt{f'_c}$ — This is a **mandatory supporting equation** for the ACI thickness derivation.
> 7. **Confusing "whitetopping" and "ultra-thin whitetopping" (UTW)** — UTW is **50 – 100 mm** bonded to asphalt; whitetopping is **>100 mm**, may be bonded or unbonded.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Overlays (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Overlay = surface-applied layer for restoration/strengthening/sealing without full demolition.
- **Three bond categories:** Bonded, Unbonded, Partially Bonded.
- **Six overlay families:** Cementitious, Polymer, Polymer-Modified Cementitious, Fiber-Reinforced, Bituminous, Specialty.
- **Bond strength minimum:** **≥ 1.7 MPa** in direct tension (ICRI 210.1R).
- **Slant shear test:** ASTM C882, $\tau_{bond} = 2.309 P / (\pi D^2)$.
- **Pull-off test:** ASTM C1583, $f_t = 2P / (\pi L D)$.
- **Modulus of rupture:** $f_r = 0.62 \sqrt{f'_c}$ (MPa, ACI 318).
- **Modulus of elasticity:** $E_c = 4700 \sqrt{f'_c}$ (MPa, ACI 318).
- **Bonded overlay thickness (ACI 318):** $h_o = \sqrt{6M / (b f_r)} - h_s$.
- **Surface profile:** **CSP 5 – CSP 7** for bonded concrete overlays (ICRI 210.1R).
- **Surface prep methods:** Shotblasting (most common), hydroblasting (chloride removal), grinding (thin polymer overlays only).
- **Bond mechanisms:** Mechanical interlock (40–60%), chemical adhesion (20–30%), adhesive bonding (15–25%), friction (5–15%).
- **Critical Kerala-context concerns:** Tropical humidity → shrinkage → debonding; coastal chloride → corrosion → delamination.
- **Epoxy overlay:** High strength, UV-sensitive, thin (3–12 mm), indoor chemical use.
- **LMC overlay:** SBR-latex modified, 25–50 mm, ideal for bridge decks.
- **Silica-fume overlay:** Pozzolanic densification, very low permeability, abrasion-resistant.
- **Fiber types:** Steel (structural), Glass (AR-glass, lightweight), Carbon (high modulus), Polypropylene (shrinkage control).
- **Shrinkage-induced interface shear:** $\tau_{int} = 2 \sigma_{sh} h_o / L$.
- **Whitetopping:** Concrete on asphalt; UTW = 50 – 100 mm; Conventional = > 100 mm.
- **Common failure mode #1:** Debonding at interface (poor surface prep).
- **Common failure mode #2:** Shrinkage cracking (high w/c, large joint spacing).
- **Common failure mode #3:** Re-corrosion under overlay (substrate not treated).
- **Joint pattern:** Mirror substrate joints; **maximum panel size = 4 m × 4 m** for thin bonded overlays.
- **Curing protocol:** Minimum **7 days** wet curing for cementitious overlays; **24–48 hours** for polymer overlays.
- **Quality control tests:** Pull-off, slant shear, hammer survey, covermeter, chloride profile.
- **Standards to remember:** ACI 318, ACI 546R, ACI 548, ICRI 210.1R, ASTM C1583, ASTM C882, ASTM C881, ASTM C1240.
- **Most-tested KTU question type:** "Compare bonded and unbonded overlays" (frequently 7 marks) and "Discuss surface preparation techniques" (frequently 7 marks).
- **Sustainability note:** Overlays are **carbon-frugal** compared to full demolition + reconstruction; preferred where substrate retains ≥ 60% of original capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair to Active Cracks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repair to Active Cracks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Active Crack (KTU Standard Definition):**
> An **active crack** is a crack in a concrete or masonry element that exhibits ongoing movement — its width, length, or propagation continues to change with time due to persistent factors such as thermal cycling, ongoing structural settlement, live-load variations, drying shrinkage, or progressive deterioration mechanisms (e.g., reinforcement corrosion, alkali-silica reaction, freeze-thaw action). Such cracks are *dynamic* and *non-equilibrium* in nature.

In contrast, a **dormant crack** is stable in width and does not propagate further once initial loading or shrinkage has ceased. The fundamental difference dictates the **entire repair strategy** — an active crack *cannot* be repaired with rigid materials (e.g., neat cement grout, standard epoxy) because any future movement would simply re-open or propagate the crack adjacent to the rigid repair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET634 – Module 4):**
> The classification of cracks (active vs. dormant) is the **first and most critical step** in any repair specification. Misclassification leads to premature failure of the repair within months. Module 4 explicitly tests the student's ability to (a) diagnose crack activity, and (b) select *flexible* repair systems with adequate **Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a paper cup with a small vertical split running down its side. If the cup is sitting still on a table, you can patch the split with rigid super-glue and it will hold — this is a **dormant crack**.

Now imagine that same cup, but someone is repeatedly squeezing and releasing it. If you glue it rigidly, the glue line will pop open on the next squeeze, and may even tear the paper *next* to the glue because the paper is now locally stiffer than the surrounding area. This is an **active crack**.

The fix for the squeezed cup? Use a **stretchy, rubbery tape** (like silicone tape) that can elongate with the squeeze and snap back. That is exactly what engineers do with active concrete cracks — they use **flexible, elastomeric materials** that breathe and stretch with the structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (used by KTU examiners):**
> *"If the crack breathes, the repair must stretch."* This single sentence is worth 2–3 marks in Part A questions.

---

## 1.3 Why Active Cracks Form — Root-Cause Matrix

| # | Root Cause | Typical Crack Pattern | Movement Cycle |
|---|------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Thermal expansion/contraction | Map cracking, pattern cracks | Diurnal / Seasonal |
| 2 | Drying shrinkage (early age) | Random hairline network | First 6–18 months |
| 3 | Foundation settlement (ongoing) | Diagonal shear cracks | Progressive, often monotonic |
| 4 | Live-load variation (bridges, floors) | Flexural cracks at mid-span | Per load event |
| 5 | Corrosion of reinforcement | Longitudinal along rebar | Progressive, accelerating |
| 6 | Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR) | Pattern/map cracking, gel exudation | Progressive, often years |
| 7 | Freeze-thaw cycles | Surface scaling + cracks | Seasonal |
| 8 | Vibration / cyclic loading | Fatigue cracks at stress concentrations | Per cycle (millions of cycles) |

---

## 1.4 Active vs. Dormant — The Decision Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Threshold (per IS 456 / BS EN 1504 / ACI 224.1R):**
> A crack is classified as **active** if it exhibits a *width change of ≥ 0.1 mm* (sometimes quoted as ≥ 0.05 mm) over a monitoring period of 6–12 months, *or* if it has a clear, identifiable ongoing causative mechanism (live load, thermal, settlement, corrosion).

The standard field method to **confirm** activity is to install a **tell-tale** (a simple plaster or glass strip across the crack) or a **Demec gauge / mechanical strain gauge** and re-measure over time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Philosophy Behind Active Crack Repair

The repair of active cracks is governed by a single core principle: **the repair material must be at least as flexible as the host structure's expected movement, and the bond to the substrate must be at least as strong as the cohesive strength of the substrate itself.**

This principle decomposes into four engineering requirements:

1. **Movement Accommodation (Elasticity / Elongation):**
   The sealant or injected resin must be able to elongate and recover without tearing, ideally over thousands of cycles.

2. **Adhesion Under Cyclic Strain:**
   Bond strength must be maintained when the substrate is straining (most rigid epoxies fail in adhesion at < 1% substrate strain).

3. **Environmental Durability:**
   The flexible material must resist UV, water, chemical attack, and temperature extremes throughout its service life.

4. **Structural vs. Non-Structural Function:**
   The repair may need to transfer load (structural) or merely seal out moisture and gases (non-structural). This dictates the material family selected.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Repair Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to be able to list and justify at least 4 techniques for active cracks.** The syllabus (Module 4) explicitly covers the following six methods.

### 2.2.1 Flexible Sealant Treatment (Routing-and-Sealing)
- A V-shaped or U-shaped groove (typically 10–25 mm deep) is cut along the crack using a hand grinder or chase cutter.
- The groove is filled with an **elastomeric sealant**: polyurethane, polysulfide, or silicone.
- The sealant bonds to the side walls of the groove and stretches/compresses with crack movement.
- **Suitable for:** Non-structural sealing of moving cracks in walls, slabs, water-retaining structures.
- **MAF required:** 12.5% to 25% (or ±12.5% to ±25%).

### 2.2.2 Polyurethane (PU) Resin Injection
- A **two-component polyurethane resin** is injected under pressure through surface-mounted ports.
- The resin reacts with water (or a built-in water-mimicking agent) to form a **flexible, closed-cell foam or gel**.
- **Hydrophobic PU** repels water → ideal for stopping water ingress.
- **Hydrophilic PU** absorbs water and swells → self-healing if re-wetted.
- **Suitable for:** Active, leaking cracks in basements, tunnels, water tanks, sewers.
- **MAF achieved:** Up to ±25% movement.

### 2.2.3 Flexible Epoxy Injection (Modified Epoxy)
- Standard bisphenol-A epoxies are *brittle* (elongation < 2%) — they fail on active cracks.
- **Flexible / Toughened epoxies** are formulated with rubber tougheners (CTBN, polyurethane modifiers, or polyamide curing agents) giving elongations of 10–30%.
- **Suitable for:** Structural active cracks that must transfer load while accommodating movement.
- **Cost:** 2–3× standard rigid epoxy.

### 2.2.4 Crack Stitching
- Holes are drilled perpendicular to the crack plane, on alternating sides, at ~300–500 mm spacing.
- **Stitching dogs** (U-shaped metal staples, often stainless steel or carbon fiber laminates) are grouted into the holes with epoxy or polyester resin.
- The staples bridge the crack and arrest further opening.
- **Suitable for:** Masonry walls and concrete elements with active cracks requiring structural continuity.
- **Note:** Stitching does *not* seal the crack — a separate flexible sealant is applied to the surface groove.

### 2.2.5 Bandage / Carbon-Fiber Surface Reinforcement
- **CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer) laminates or fabric strips** are bonded across the crack using flexible epoxy.
- The CFRP carries tensile force as the crack opens, reducing crack-tip stress intensity.
- **Suitable for:** Active flexural cracks in beams and slabs where the *rate* of opening is to be controlled.
- **Design principle:** Keep CFRP strain below ~0.4% to avoid fatigue failure of the laminate.

### 2.2.6 Installation of a Movement (Expansion) Joint
- The active crack is *converted* into a designed movement joint by cutting a wider slot and installing a preformed compression seal or waterstop.
- **Suitable for:** Long, continuous active cracks where the structural form needs to articulate.
- **Most permanent solution** for active cracks caused by thermal or shrinkage movement.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for Active Crack Repair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *mathematically verifiable* from first principles of mechanics of materials. They form the high-yield calculation set for PECET634 Module 4.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF) | $MAF = \dfrac{\Delta w}{w_{0}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies required sealant stretch |
| 2 | Required Elongation at Break (sealing depth method) | $\varepsilon_{req} = \dfrac{MAF}{2} \cdot \dfrac{d_{g}}{t_{s}}$ | % | Selects appropriate sealant class |
| 3 | Thermal movement of unrestrained concrete element | $\Delta L = \alpha_{c} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | Predicts crack width change |
| 4 | Free shrinkage strain contribution | $\Delta w_{sh} = \varepsilon_{sh} \cdot L_{eff}$ | mm | Predicts seasonal crack variation |
| 5 | Stress in restrained shrinkage (early thermal) | $\sigma_{r} = K_{r} \cdot E_{c} \cdot \varepsilon_{sh}$ | MPa | Checks if crack is load-induced |
| 6 | Mode-I Stress Intensity Factor (K_I) | $K_{I} = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | Fracture mechanics check |
| 7 | Critical crack length (fracture mechanics) | $a_{c} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^{2}$ | m | Distinguishes stable vs. unstable propagation |
| 8 | Adhesion stress under cyclic strain | $\tau_{adhesion} = \dfrac{G_{s} \cdot \gamma}{t_{s}}$ | MPa | Verifies bond under shear lag |
| 9 | Cross-sectional area of stitching staple | $A_{s} = \dfrac{F_{t}}{0.87 f_{y}}$ | mm$^2$ | Sizing of stitching dogs |
| 10 | Shear capacity of CFRP-to-concrete bond | $\tau_{debond} \le 0.5 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | MPa | Per ACI 440 / fib Bulletin 14 |
| 11 | Volumetric water absorption of PU foam | $V_{foam} = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{\rho_{water} \cdot V_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Quality check of injected PU |
| 12 | Effective strain energy release rate | $G = \dfrac{K_{I}^{2}}{E'}$ | J/m$^2$ | Predicts debonding of repair |
| 13 | Cyclic fatigue life of sealant (Coffin-Manson) | $N_{f} = A \cdot (\Delta \varepsilon)^{-n}$ | cycles | Durability under thermal cycling |
| 14 | Minimum groove width-to-depth ratio (sealant) | $w_{g} : d_{g} = 1 : 2$ to $1 : 1$ | – | Joint design per ASTM C920 |
| 15 | Maximum sealant depth (to avoid sag) | $d_{g,max} \le 12 \text{ mm (typical)}$ | mm | Construction specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Specific Symbol Legend:**
> - $\alpha_{c}$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of concrete (≈ 10 × 10$^{-6}$ /°C)
> - $E_{c}$ = elastic modulus of concrete
> - $K_{IC}$ = fracture toughness of concrete (≈ 0.2–0.8 MPa·m$^{1/2}$)
> - $E' = E$ for plane stress; $E' = E / (1-\nu^{2})$ for plane strain
> - $Y$ = geometry factor (typically 1.0–1.12 for edge cracks)
> - $f_{ck}$ = characteristic cube compressive strength of concrete

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active crack repair is encountered in:

- **Bridges and flyovers** — thermal movement of decks, vibration-induced fatigue cracks at diaphragms and bearings.
- **Water-retaining structures** — tanks, reservoirs, swimming pools, where cracks move AND leak simultaneously → **PU injection is the industry standard**.
- **Basements and tunnels** — through-cracks caused by seasonal water-table fluctuation.
- **Industrial floors and pavements** — shrinkage-active cracks requiring flexible trafficable fillers.
- **High-rise buildings** — long-term creep and differential shortening of columns causing active cracks in shear walls and beam-column joints.
- **Heritage masonry** — stitching with stainless-steel helical bars is the de-facto conservation method.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU valuation, examiners look for **two things in any active-crack repair answer:**
> 1. The student correctly *justifies* why a flexible (not rigid) material is chosen.
> 2. The student *quantifies* the required movement using MAF or thermal-movement calculations.
> A 14-mark answer without these two elements rarely scores above 8.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Sealant Elongation (Groove Geometry Basis)

> **Problem Setup:**
> A V-groove of depth $d_{g}$ and width $w_{g}$ is cut across an active crack. The crack opens by an amount $\Delta w$. Derive the required elongation capacity of the sealant in terms of the MAF and groove geometry.

### Step 1 — Define the Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF)
The MAF is the percentage change in crack width the sealant must tolerate, expressed relative to the original crack width $w_{0}$:

$$
MAF = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{0}} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

### Step 2 — Relate MAF to the Sealant Strain
If the sealant sits in a groove of depth $d_{g}$ and original (undeformed) thickness across the crack line $t_{s}$ (≈ groove width $w_{g}$ for a U-groove), then when the crack opens by $\Delta w$, the sealant must stretch by the same amount. The **tensile strain** in the sealant is:

$$
\varepsilon_{s} = \frac{\Delta w}{t_{s}} = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{g}}
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Original Crack Width
Substituting $\Delta w = \dfrac{MAF \cdot w_{0}}{100}$ into Step 2:

$$
\varepsilon_{s} = \frac{MAF \cdot w_{0}}{100 \cdot w_{g}}
$$

### Step 4 — Express the Original Crack Width in Terms of Groove Geometry
For a properly designed sealant joint, ASTM C920 and BS EN 15651 recommend that the original crack width $w_{0}$ be set equal to the sealant *width* $w_{g}$ (so the joint is pre-stretched neither in tension nor compression). With $w_{0} = w_{g}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{s} = \frac{MAF}{100}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Working Equation

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{s,\text{req}} \ (\%)= MAF \ (\%)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sealant's elongation at break must be **at least equal to the Movement Accommodation Factor** of the joint, with a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 applied for long-term aging and cyclic fatigue.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Crack-Width Change in a Concrete Element

> **Problem Setup:**
> A 6 m long concrete slab is unrestrained at one end. The mean annual temperature swings by $\Delta T = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Kerala coastal climate: ~25°C winter, ~45°C summer). Compute the expected thermal opening of a transverse crack.

### Step 1 — Coefficient of Thermal Expansion
For normal-weight concrete (Kerala aggregates — mostly gneiss / laterite):

$$
\alpha_{c} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{per °C}
$$

### Step 2 — Free Thermal Strain

$$
\varepsilon_{T} = \alpha_{c} \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{T} = (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 40
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{T} = 400 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### Step 3 — Free Thermal Displacement of the Slab

$$
\Delta L = \varepsilon_{T} \cdot L
$$

$$
\Delta L = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 6000 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 2.4 \ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Distribute Movement Across All Transverse Cracks
If the slab has been pre-cracked (e.g., by induced saw-cuts) at a spacing of $s = 1.5$ m, then the total length is divided into $n = L/s = 6000/1500 = 4$ bays. If the slab is unrestrained at one end and the saw-cuts are the *only* relief points, the total thermal expansion is taken up as **opening of the cut joints** (which behave as active cracks):

$$
\Delta w_{\text{per cut}} = \frac{\Delta L}{n} = \frac{2.4}{4} = 0.6 \ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Required MAF (if saw-cuts were originally 10 mm wide)

$$
MAF_{\text{req}} = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{0}} \times 100 = \frac{0.6}{10} \times 100 = 6\%
$$

### Step 6 — Sealant Selection
Choose a **polyurethane sealant of Class 25** (i.e., ±25% MAF, elongation at break ≥ 25%) — this provides a safety factor of approximately $25/6 \approx 4.2$, more than adequate for the design life.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This numerical example is directly applicable to Kerala flyovers and bridge decks — students who can quote a quantitative MAF calculation in their answer automatically gain 2–3 extra marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mode-I Stress Intensity Factor for an Edge Crack

> **Problem Setup:**
> An active crack of length $a = 50$ mm exists at the corner of a reinforced concrete wall subjected to uniform tensile stress $\sigma = 1.5$ MPa due to restrained thermal movement. Determine if the crack is stable or will propagate.

### Step 1 — Geometry Factor
For a single-edge-notched tension (SENT) geometry, $Y \approx 1.12$.

### Step 2 — Compute Stress Intensity Factor

$$
K_{I} = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

$$
K_{I} = 1.12 \times 1.5 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.050}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Square Root Term

$$
\sqrt{\pi \times 0.050} = \sqrt{0.1571} = 0.3963 \ \text{m}^{1/2}
$$

### Step 4 — Final K_I Value

$$
K_{I} = 1.12 \times 1.5 \times 0.3963
$$

$$
K_{I} = 0.6658 \ \text{MPa} \cdot \text{m}^{1/2}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Fracture Toughness of Concrete
Typical $K_{IC}$ for normal-strength concrete: 0.2 – 0.8 MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

Since $K_{I} = 0.666 \ \text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$ lies *within* the range, the crack is **marginally stable** — it will not run catastrophically, but it is actively breathing. Therefore, a **flexible repair** (not a rigid epoxy) is mandated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety check:** If $K_{I} > K_{IC}$, the crack is unstable and *immediate structural intervention* (FRP wrapping, external prestressing) is required, not merely a sealant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sizing a Stitching Staple

> **Problem Setup:**
> A masonry wall has an active vertical crack that opens by $\Delta w = 1.5$ mm per thermal cycle. A stitching staple of Fe-415 steel is to be placed perpendicular to the crack. Design the cross-sectional area of one leg of the staple.

### Step 1 — Tensile Force Across the Crack
Assume the stitching dogs must carry the full tensile force across the crack over a tributary wall height of $h = 500$ mm. The masonry tensile strength perpendicular to bed joints is $f_{t} = 0.3$ MPa (typical).

### Step 2 — Tributary Force

$$
F_{t} = f_{t} \cdot t \cdot h
$$

Where $t$ = wall thickness = 230 mm:

$$
F_{t} = 0.3 \times 230 \times 500
$$

$$
F_{t} = 34500 \ \text{N} = 34.5 \ \text{kN}
$$

### Step 3 — Staple Cross-Section (Two Legs Sharing the Load)

$$
A_{s,\text{per leg}} = \frac{F_{t}}{2 \times 0.87 \times f_{y}}
$$

$$
A_{s,\text{per leg}} = \frac{34500}{2 \times 0.87 \times 415}
$$

$$
A_{s,\text{per leg}} = \frac{34500}{722.1} = 47.78 \ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Select Standard Bar Size
Use **2-legged 8 mm dia. bars** (area per leg = 50.27 mm²) — adequate, with 5% reserve.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Tree

| Crack Width Range | Movement Expectation | Recommended Material | Technique | Service Life |
|-------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------|--------------|
| < 0.3 mm | Low (< ±5%) | Acrylic injection, fine PU gel | Pressure injection | 10–15 yr |
| 0.3 – 1.0 mm | Moderate (±5 to ±12.5%) | Polyurethane Class 12.5/25 | Routing & sealing + injection | 15–20 yr |
| 1.0 – 3.0 mm | High (±12.5 to ±25%) | Polysulfide or PU Class 25 | Routing & sealing | 20–25 yr |
| 3.0 – 6.0 mm | Very high (> ±25%) | Silicone Class 50 / preformed compression seal | Joint conversion | 25–30 yr |
| > 6.0 mm | Structural movement | Stainless steel stitching + flexible surface sealant | Stitching + sealing | 30+ yr |
| Leaking active crack (any width) | Wet/damp condition | Hydrophobic PU injection | Pressure injection | 15–20 yr |
| Active flexural cracks in beams | Load-induced breathing | CFRP laminate + flexible epoxy | Surface bonding | 25+ yr |
| Corrosion-induced active cracks | Progressive widening | Remove concrete, treat rebar, patch with polymer-modified mortar, then seal surface | Multi-stage | 20+ yr |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style (14-Mark Type)

> **Question (Composite):**
> An active crack in the diaphragm wall of a flyover in Kochi exhibits a width variation from 0.8 mm (winter) to 2.4 mm (summer). The crack is 2.5 m long and is leaking during monsoon.
>
> **(a)** Classify the crack and justify the use of a flexible repair.
>
> **(b)** Design a complete repair specification including material, groove geometry, and method.

### Part (a) — Classification & Justification (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the *width change*:

$$
\Delta w = 2.4 - 0.8 = 1.6 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the *mean* crack width:

$$
w_{mean} = \frac{0.8 + 2.4}{2} = 1.6 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the *percentage* movement:

$$
\% \text{movement} = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{mean}} \times 100 = \frac{1.6}{1.6} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The crack is **highly active** (movement = 100% of mean width). A rigid repair (cementitious grout, standard epoxy) will fail within the first seasonal cycle. A **flexible elastomeric system** with MAF ≥ ±50% is mandatory.

**Step 4 — Justification in KTU phrasing:**
"Because the crack is subject to annual cyclic movement of 1.6 mm and is concurrently leaking, the repair must (i) accommodate 100% movement reversibly, (ii) bond to damp concrete, and (iii) resist hydrostatic pressure. Polyurethane resin injection combined with surface routing-and-sealing using a PU Class 25 sealant satisfies all three requirements."

### Part (b) — Repair Specification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Surface preparation:**
- Clean the crack line mechanically (wire brush, compressed air).
- Cut a U-groove 15 mm wide × 20 mm deep along the crack using a hand chase cutter.
- Drill 10 mm dia. injection ports at 200 mm c/c along the crack, alternating with the groove.

**Step 2 — Seal the groove surface:**
- Apply **PU Class 25 sealant** (e.g., Sikaflex PRO or equivalent) along the U-groove, leaving the injection ports open.
- Allow 24 h cure.

**Step 3 — Pressure injection:**
- Inject **hydrophobic PU resin** (e.g., polyurethane injection resin, single-component, moisture-cured) through the lowest port first.
- Inject at 20–40 bar until resin appears at the next port.
- Move to the next port and repeat.
- After full injection, allow 24 h cure.

**Step 4 — Finishing:**
- Cut off port nipples flush with surface.
- Apply a 2 mm PU top coat to the entire repair length for UV and abrasion protection.

**Step 5 — Quality control:**
- Drill a small core (10 mm dia.) at a representative point and verify resin penetration depth.
- Conduct a water-tightness test (e.g., 24 h ponding or Rilem tube test).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summary specification:**
> Material = Hydrophobic PU resin (injection) + PU Class 25 sealant (groove)
> MAF = ±25% (with 4× safety factor over 100% movement demand)
> Joint geometry = 15 mm wide × 20 mm deep U-groove
> Port spacing = 200 mm c/c
> Cure time = 24 h before testing

# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, PECET634]**
**Differentiate between an active crack and a dormant crack. Why is this distinction critical in repair selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Crack | Dormant Crack |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Width change with time | Continues to change (≥ 0.1 mm in 6–12 months) | Stable, no measurable change |
| Causes | Thermal, shrinkage, settlement, corrosion, ASR, live load | Initial shrinkage, single overload event |
| Repair material | **Flexible / elastomeric** (PU, polysulfide, silicone) | **Rigid** (cementitious, standard epoxy) |
| Repair life | Must accommodate ongoing movement | Permanent once bonded |

**Criticality:** Using a *rigid* repair on an active crack causes re-cracking within the first movement cycle, often propagating adjacent to the repair and worsening the original problem. Misclassification is the **single most common cause of repair failure** in practice.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark each for the distinction table and 1 mark for the criticality statement.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECET634]**
**What is the Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF)? How is it used in sealant selection for an active crack?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** MAF is the *percentage* cyclic width change that a sealant joint must tolerate, expressed as:

$$
MAF = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{0}} \times 100 \ \ (\%)
$$

- **Application in design (2 marks):**
  1. Compute expected $\Delta w$ from thermal, shrinkage, or load calculations.
  2. Set the joint width $w_{0}$ such that MAF falls within the sealant's rated class (e.g., Class 25 = ±25% movement).
  3. Apply a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 for long-term aging, UV, and cyclic fatigue.
  4. Select the appropriate sealant family: PU (most common), polysulfide (chemical resistance), or silicone (UV and temperature extremes).

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECET634]**

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the procedure of "Routing-and-Sealing" technique for the repair of an active crack in a concrete wall. Discuss the material requirements and the role of Movement Accommodation Factor (MAF) in the design of the joint.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A water-retaining RCC tank in Thrissur shows an active vertical crack of width varying from 1.2 mm in winter to 2.8 mm in summer. The crack is 3.6 m long and leaks during monsoon. Propose a complete repair specification, including material selection, groove geometry, and quality control tests.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Definition and Applicability (1 mark):**
Routing-and-sealing is the process of *enlarging* a crack into a V-shaped or U-shaped groove and filling it with a flexible sealant. It is the most common non-structural repair for active cracks in walls, slabs, and water-retaining structures.

**Step 2 — Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Surface cleaning** — Remove loose concrete, dust, and contaminants within 50 mm of the crack using a wire brush and compressed air.
2. **Groove cutting** — Use a hand-held grinder with a diamond blade to cut a U-groove. Typical dimensions: 10–25 mm wide × 10–20 mm deep. (Width-to-depth ratio ≈ 1:1 to 1:2 as per ASTM C920.)
3. **Backer rod insertion** (optional) — If groove depth > 12 mm, insert a closed-cell polyethylene backer rod to control sealant depth and ensure a proper hourglass profile.
4. **Priming** — Apply a compatible primer to the groove side walls (improves adhesion on porous concrete).
5. **Sealant application** — Gun-grade PU or polysulfide sealant is pumped into the groove using a sealant gun. Tool the surface to ensure complete wetting of the side walls and a slight concave profile.
6. **Curing** — Allow 24–48 h cure (longer for low temperatures / high humidity).
7. **Quality check** — Perform a pull-off adhesion test and a Rilem tube water-tightness test.

**Step 3 — Material Requirements (2 marks):**

- **Elongation at break** ≥ MAF × 1.5 (safety factor).
- **Movement capability** = Class 12.5, 25, or 50 (±12.5%, ±25%, ±50%).
- **Adhesion** to damp concrete ≥ 1.0 MPa (PU) or ≥ 0.7 MPa (polysulfide).
- **Chemical / UV / aging resistance** matching service environment.
- **Non-sag** on vertical surfaces (thixotropic).

**Step 4 — Role of MAF (1 mark):**
MAF quantifies the cyclic strain demand. It is used to (a) set the joint width (so the sealant operates within its rated class), and (b) select the sealant *class*. For example, if MAF demand is 15%, a Class 25 sealant provides a 1.67 safety factor, whereas a Class 12.5 would be marginal.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Quantify the Movement (2 marks):**

- Width change: $\Delta w = 2.8 - 1.2 = 1.6$ mm
- Mean width: $w_{mean} = (1.2 + 2.8)/2 = 2.0$ mm
- MAF demand: $\dfrac{1.6}{2.0} \times 100 = 80\%$

> This is a *highly active* crack — MAF demand of 80% far exceeds the rating of any single sealant class. A two-stage approach is required.

**Step 2 — Two-Stage Repair Strategy (2 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 (leak sealing):** Hydrophobic PU resin *injection* to seal water paths and form an internal flexible gasket (MAF ±25%, water-tight).
2. **Stage 2 (surface sealing):** A wider U-groove (20 mm × 25 mm) routed along the crack, filled with a PU Class 25 sealant. The injected PU takes up bulk movement; the surface sealant prevents surface ingress and provides UV protection.

**Step 3 — Groove Geometry (1 mark):**
- Width $w_{g}$ = 20 mm
- Depth $d_{g}$ = 25 mm
- Backer rod = 15 mm dia. (controls sealant depth to ~10 mm)
- Resulting effective sealant thickness = 10 mm, MAF capacity of joint = $\pm$50% — well above the 80% demand *after* injection, because injection carries most of the deformation.

**Step 4 — Quality Control (2 marks):**

| Test | Method | Acceptance Criterion |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| Water-tightness | 24 h ponding on outer face | No dampness on inner face |
| Adhesion | Pull-off test (e.g., Elcometer) | ≥ 1.0 MPa (substrate failure, not adhesive) |
| Resin penetration | Core drilling and visual inspection | ≥ 80% of crack depth filled |
| Movement check | Cyclic tell-tale installation | No change in tell-tale gap > 0.1 mm over 6 months |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET634]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the method of polyurethane (PU) resin injection for repairing active leaking cracks. Compare hydrophobic and hydrophilic PU resins. List the standard quality control tests.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) An active crack on the soffit of a bridge deck opens by 1.0 mm during the passage of a 40-tonne truck. The crack is 0.5 mm wide under dead load only. Design a repair system using CFRP laminates. Compute the laminate cross-section and check for debonding.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — Principle of PU Injection (2 marks):**
Polyurethane resins are two-component systems (polyol + isocyanate) that, when mixed and injected into a crack, react with water (or a built-in water-mimicking agent) to form a flexible, closed-cell foam or gel. The reaction can produce up to 30× volumetric expansion, completely filling voids and sealing water paths.

**Step 2 — Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Clean crack surface and identify port positions.
2. Drill 10 mm ports at 200–300 mm c/c, alternating with crack intersections.
3. Seal the crack surface with a fast-setting epoxy paste, leaving ports open.
4. Inject PU resin from the *lowest* port at 20–80 bar pressure.
5. Continue until resin exudes from the *next* port; cap the current port and move to the next.
6. Allow 24 h cure, then cut off port nipples and apply a top coat.

**Step 3 — Hydrophobic vs. Hydrophilic PU (2 marks):**

| Property | Hydrophobic PU | Hydrophilic PU |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| Reaction with water | Repels water; forms closed-cell foam | Absorbs water and swells (gel) |
| Best application | Stop water ingress; tidal / basement walls | Permanently wet environments; water-swelling seals |
| Re-wet behaviour | Stays flexible, does not re-swell | Self-heals — re-swells on re-wetting |
| Volume expansion | Up to 30× | Up to 10× |
| MAF | ±25% typical | ±15 to ±20% |
| Typical use | Bridge piers, dry-wet cycles | Sewers, permanently submerged joints |

**Step 4 — Quality Control Tests (1 mark):**
- Core sampling to verify penetration depth.
- Water-tightness (24 h ponding or Rilem tube).
- Pull-off adhesion of the surface seal.
- Crack monitoring with tell-tales for 6–12 months.
- Ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) comparison before and after.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Quantify the Live-Load Crack Opening (2 marks):**
- Static (DL) crack width: $w_{DL} = 0.5$ mm
- Live-load opening: $\Delta w_{LL} = 1.0$ mm
- Total range: $w_{max} = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5$ mm

This is a *highly active* flexural crack — standard epoxy is unsuitable.

**Step 2 — CFRP Laminate Design (3 marks):**

For crack-width control, the CFRP must supply the tensile force that concrete can no longer carry across the crack. Using a tributary width of $b = 1000$ mm (per meter of deck width):

- Concrete tensile strength: $f_{ctm} = 0.30 \times f_{ck}^{2/3}$ (Eurocode 2). For M30 concrete:

$$
f_{ctm} = 0.30 \times 30^{2/3} = 2.56 \ \text{MPa}
$$

- Tensile force per meter to be carried by CFRP:

$$
F_{t} = f_{ctm} \cdot b \cdot \text{cover} = 2.56 \times 1000 \times 50 = 128000 \ \text{N} = 128 \ \text{kN/m}
$$

(Assuming a tributary depth of 50 mm — the effective tension zone.)

- CFRP design strain: $\varepsilon_{f,d} = 0.4\% = 0.004$ (to avoid fatigue failure, per ACI 440).
- CFRP elastic modulus: $E_{f} = 165$ GPa (typical carbon fiber).
- CFRP design stress: $\sigma_{f} = E_{f} \cdot \varepsilon_{f,d} = 165000 \times 0.004 = 660$ MPa.
- Required CFRP area per meter width:

$$
A_{f} = \frac{F_{t}}{\sigma_{f}} = \frac{128000}{660} = 194 \ \text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}
$$

- Select **Sika CarboDur S-512** (50 mm wide × 1.2 mm thick, area = 60 mm² per strip). Two strips per meter → $A_{f,\text{provided}} = 120$ mm²/m.

> Note: This is below the calculated 194 mm²/m — increase to **3 strips per meter** ($A_{f,\text{provided}} = 180$ mm²/m, acceptable).

**Step 3 — Debonding Check (1 mark):**
- ACI 440 debonding limit:

$$
\tau_{debond} = 0.5 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.5 \sqrt{30} = 2.74 \ \text{MPa}
$$

- Force per meter transferred over bond length $L_{b} = 300$ mm:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{F_{t}}{L_{b} \cdot b_{f,\text{total}}}
$$

Where $b_{f,\text{total}} = 3 \times 50 = 150$ mm:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{128000}{300 \times 150} = 2.84 \ \text{MPa}
$$

This marginally exceeds the ACI limit (2.74 MPa) → **increase bond length to 350 mm** or add a U-wrap at the laminate end.

**Step 4 — Final Specification (1 mark):**
- Material: Sika CarboDur S-512 carbon laminate, 50 × 1.2 mm.
- Spacing: 3 strips per meter width of deck.
- Adhesive: Sikadur-30 (flexible epoxy, elongation 10%).
- Bond length: 350 mm (no U-wrap required).
- Surface sealing: PU Class 25 along the visible crack after laminate installation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Active Crack Repair Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing active vs. dormant:** Many students answer a 14-mark active-crack question using *cementitious grout or standard epoxy* as the repair material. This is the **#1 mark-loser** — it shows a fundamental misreading of the problem. Always begin by *classifying* the crack and *justifying* the choice of a flexible system.
>
> 2. **No MAF calculation:** Failing to quantify the movement demand using $MAF = \Delta w / w_0 \times 100$ is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss. Examiners want to see a *number* derived from a formula, not a qualitative statement like "the crack moves a lot."
>
> 3. **Ignoring leak status:** If a crack is leaking, the answer *must* include a water-stopping stage (PU injection, waterstop, or hydrophilic material) *in addition* to the surface sealant. Skipping this costs 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong unit conventions:** MAF must be in *%*, crack width in *mm*, area in *mm²*, stress in *MPa*. Mixing units (e.g., MAF as a ratio of 0.25 instead of 25%) signals weak fundamentals.
>
> 5. **No diagram:** A 14-mark answer without a labelled U-groove / port-layout / injection-sequence diagram is marked down by 2 marks. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards 1–2 marks for "neat sketches."
>
> 6. **Forgetting QC tests:** Every repair specification must end with a *Quality Control* section. A complete omission costs 1 mark.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Active crack = ongoing width change (≥ 0.1 mm in 6–12 months). Dormant crack = stable.
- [ ] **First step in any repair:** Classify the crack by monitoring with tell-tales or Demec gauges.
- [ ] **Material rule:** Active cracks → flexible materials. Dormant cracks → rigid materials.
- [ ] **MAF formula:** $MAF = \Delta w / w_0 \times 100$ (in %).
- [ ] **Sealant classes:** Class 12.5 (±12.5%), Class 25 (±25%), Class 50 (±50%).
- [ ] **Joint geometry:** U-groove, width-to-depth ratio 1:1 to 1:2, max depth 12 mm without backer rod.
- [ ] **Routing-and-sealing:** Most common non-structural technique for active cracks in walls/slabs.
- [ ] **PU injection:** Industry standard for *leaking* active cracks. Two types:
  - *Hydrophobic* (water-repelling, ±25% MAF) → for tidal / wet-dry cycles.
  - *Hydrophilic* (water-swelling gel) → for permanently wet conditions.
- [ ] **Flexible epoxy:** Modified with rubber tougheners (CTBN) for *structural* active cracks.
- [ ] **Crack stitching:** Stainless-steel U-staples grouted across the crack; for masonry walls.
- [ ] **CFRP laminates:** For load-induced active flexural cracks; design strain ≤ 0.4% to avoid fatigue.
- [ ] **Thermal movement equation:** $\Delta L = \alpha_c \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$, with $\alpha_c \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.
- [ ] **Fracture mechanics check:** $K_I < K_{IC}$ → stable crack; $K_I \ge K_{IC}$ → unstable, structural intervention needed.
- [ ] **Quality control:** Water-tightness (24 h ponding), pull-off adhesion, core sampling, UPV, tell-tale monitoring.
- [ ] **Standards to quote:** BS EN 1504 (Parts 4, 5, 7, 9), ACI 224.1R, ACI 440.2R, ICRI 320.2R, ASTM C920.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific note:** Annual thermal swing of ~20–25°C in coastal cities; design for $\Delta T$ = 40°C as a conservative case.
- [ ] **Always justify** the *flexible* choice in 1–2 sentences before describing the technique.
- [ ] **Always include** at least one labelled diagram (U-groove cross-section or port layout).
- [ ] **Always end** with a Quality Control section.

> **Final KTU Mantra:**
> *"Classify first. Quantify second. Select the flexible family. Specify QC. Draw a diagram. Score high."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair to Dormant Cracks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repair to Dormant Cracks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DORMANT CRACK (KTU 2024 Definition):** A dormant crack is a crack in a structural element whose width, length, and propagation have stabilized and which is **NOT** expected to undergo further movement due to structural loading, settlement, thermal cycling, or environmental exposure at the time of assessment. Once stabilized, the crack becomes a candidate for **rigid repair** using structural resins, epoxy systems, or cementitious mortars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Differentiator from Live Cracks:** Live (active) cracks require **flexible sealants** (e.g., polysulphides, polyurethanes, silicones) to accommodate ongoing movement. Dormant cracks may be **re-bonded structurally** because no further relative displacement of crack faces is anticipated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Healed Bone":**
> Think of a dormant crack like a **broken bone that has already set and calcified**. The doctor does not apply a flexible bandage (which would allow movement and re-fracture); instead, the bone is permanently fused. Similarly, once a crack in concrete has stopped moving, we want to **chemically weld the two faces back together** so the structure behaves as a monolith again.
> 
> In contrast, a *live crack* is like a **fresh fracture** still in a cast — it needs an elastic wrap that breathes with the injury.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum crack width treated as dormant repairable:** typically **> 0.1 mm** (wider cracks need injection; hairline cracks may be sealed by surface methods).
- **Epoxy tensile bond strength to dry concrete:** **> 3.0 MPa** (per IS 456:2000 durability requirement for resin-based repairs).
- **Standard ambient application temperature for epoxy systems:** **15 °C – 35 °C**.
- **Crack movement tolerance** (to classify as dormant): **< 0.05 mm** movement per cycle, observed over a **minimum 6-month monitoring period**.

## 1.4 Syllabus Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Focus:** This topic falls under "Crack Repair Techniques" in PECET634. The student must be able to **distinguish dormant from live cracks**, **select appropriate repair methodology**, and **justify the choice of repair material** based on crack geometry, environmental exposure, and structural significance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Dormant Cracks Can Be Rigidly Repaired

The fundamental engineering premise is the **compatibility of strain assumption**:

$$\varepsilon_{concrete} = \varepsilon_{repair\,material} = \frac{\Delta w}{w_0}$$

Where:
- $\Delta w$ = change in crack width over time
- $w_0$ = original crack width
- $\varepsilon$ = strain in the substrate and repair material

If $\Delta w \approx 0$ (i.e., the crack is dormant), then $\varepsilon \approx 0$, and the repair material is **NOT** required to undergo cyclic strain. Therefore, **brittle, high-modulus systems (epoxies, polymer mortars)** are acceptable — even preferable — because they restore monolithic action with full load transfer across the crack plane.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Decision Tree for Dormant Crack Repair

1. **Step 1 — Crack Survey & Mapping:** Document location, orientation, length, and width using a crack-width comparator and marking.
2. **Step 2 — Activity Assessment:** Confirm dormancy via:
   - Demec gauge or vibrating-wire strain gauges over **≥ 6 months**.
   - Plaster (gypsum) tell-tales — fracture across plaster indicates movement (live).
   - Glass strip tell-tales — cracking confirms activity.
3. **Step 3 — Surface Preparation:** Remove weak concrete, dust, laitance, oil, and grease by mechanical means (grinding, sandblasting) or by high-pressure water jetting.
4. **Step 4 — Crack Geometry Analysis:** Determine if the crack is *through-section*, *surface*, or *internal*, and if it carries water or contaminants.
5. **Step 5 — Method Selection:** Choose from the matrix in §2.4.
6. **Step 6 — Material Selection & Application:** Apply per manufacturer's specifications and relevant IS code clauses.
7. **Step 7 — Quality Control:** Pull-off bond test (minimum **1.5 MPa** tensile bond), core sampling, and visual inspection.

## 2.3 Criteria for Confirming a Crack as Dormant

| # | Criterion | Threshold | Verification Method |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Movement per cycle | **< 0.05 mm** | Demec gauge, LVDT |
| 2 | Movement over 6 months | **< 0.1 mm cumulative** | Tell-tale, gauge |
| 3 | Cause of cracking | Resolved (e.g., settlement complete, shrinkage ended) | Engineering judgment |
| 4 | Structural loading | No future increase in load expected | Load assessment |
| 5 | Environmental exposure | No aggressive agents causing ongoing degradation | Site investigation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common board question asks: *"How do you classify a crack as dormant or live?"* — Always cite the **6-month monitoring** and the **< 0.05 mm per cycle** threshold, then describe the **plaster tell-tale test** as a field technique.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Method Selection Matrix

| Repair Method | Crack Width Range | Crack Type | Material | Key Equation / Logic |
|---------------|-------------------|------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Surface Sealing** | 0.1 – 0.3 mm | Surface, dormant | Epoxy / polyester putty | $w_{seal} \leq 0.3\,mm$ |
| **Routing & Sealing** | 0.3 – 6 mm | Surface, dormant | Polysulphide / polyurethane | Groove depth $d \geq w_{crack}$ |
| **Epoxy Injection** | 0.1 – 6 mm | Through-thickness, dormant | Low-viscosity epoxy resin | Injection pressure $P_{inj} = 0.1 - 1.0\,MPa$ |
| **Gravity Filling** | > 3 mm, vertical | Surface, dormant, no load | Epoxy mortar / cement grout | $h_{fill}$ = full crack depth |
| **Stitching** | > 0.5 mm | Structural, dormant | U-shaped staples + epoxy | Spacing $s = 300 - 500\,mm$ |
| **Drilling & Plugging** | > 6 mm | Through-thickness, dormant | Epoxy-plugged holes | Hole depth $L \geq w_{wall}$ |
| **Flexible Sealing** | 0.3 – 25 mm | **LIVE cracks only** | Polysulphide / silicone | **NOT for dormant** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently apply **flexible sealants** to dormant cracks, losing the benefit of structural re-bonding. Always remember: **dormant → rigid repair; live → flexible repair.**

## 2.5 Why These Systems Work — Engineering Rationale

- **Epoxy resins** offer tensile strengths of **> 30 MPa** and bond strengths of **> 3 MPa** to dry, sound concrete, restoring monolithic flexural and tensile capacity across the crack plane.
- **Low viscosity injection resins (50–500 cP)** penetrate micro-cracks **< 0.05 mm wide** via capillary action, achieving near-full depth penetration under moderate pressure.
- **Cementitious grouts** are used for wider, non-structural dormant cracks where epoxy cost is prohibitive.
- **Stitching** transfers tensile force across the crack plane via metallic staples, providing redundant mechanical load path even if the adhesive bond fails.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge piers & girders:** Dormant shrinkage/flexural cracks in abutments and pier shafts.
- **Water-retaining structures:** Through-thickness dormant cracks in reservoir walls (waterproofing is critical).
- **Industrial floors:** Dormant settlement cracks in warehouse slabs.
- **Building facades & beams:** Stabilized shrinkage cracks prior to painting or finishing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Bond Strength Criterion Across a Repaired Crack

> The repaired section must develop a flexural tensile stress at the crack location at least equal to that of the parent concrete to restore monolithic action.

**Step 1:** Maximum tensile stress in a reinforced concrete beam at the soffit (extreme fibre):

$$
\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{M\,y}{I}
$$

Where:
- $M$ = service moment at the cracked section
- $y$ = distance from the neutral axis to the extreme tension fibre
- $I$ = second moment of area of the section (uncracked, gross)

**Step 2:** For the crack repair to be effective, the **interfacial bond stress** $\tau_b$ between epoxy and concrete must satisfy:

$$
\tau_b \cdot L_{bond} \geq \sigma_{t,\max} \cdot t_{eff}
$$

Where:
- $L_{bond}$ = bond development length along the crack
- $t_{eff}$ = effective depth of the repair penetration

**Step 3:** Substituting and rearranging to solve for required bond length:

$$
L_{bond} \geq \frac{\sigma_{t,\max} \cdot t_{eff}}{\tau_b}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Given:
- $M = 25\,kN\cdot m = 25{,}000\,N\cdot m$
- $y = 150\,mm = 0.150\,m$ (for a 300 mm deep beam)
- $I_{gross} = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{250 \times (300)^3}{12} = 5.625 \times 10^{8}\,mm^4$

Calculate the maximum tensile stress:

$$
\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 0.150}{5.625 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \times 10^{6}\,Pa = 6.67\,MPa
$$

**Step 5:** With effective repair depth $t_{eff} = 50\,mm = 0.050\,m$ and bond stress $\tau_b = 3.0\,MPa$:

$$
L_{bond} \geq \frac{6.67 \times 0.050}{3.0} = 0.111\,m \approx 111\,mm
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The repair material must penetrate at least **111 mm** along the crack interface to develop sufficient bond to carry the service tensile demand. This justifies why **epoxy injection under pressure** is mandatory for through-thickness dormant cracks in beams — gravity-applied surface sealants cannot achieve this depth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Epoxy Injection Pressure and Penetrability

The Hagen–Poiseuille equation governs resin flow through a thin crack treated as a parallel-plate channel:

$$
Q = \frac{w^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \mu \cdot L}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of resin
- $w$ = crack width
- $\Delta P$ = injection pressure differential
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of resin
- $L$ = penetration length

**Step 1:** Solve for required injection pressure for a given target penetration:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{12 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot Q}{w^3}
$$

**Step 2:** Assume the following typical values:
- $w = 0.3\,mm = 3 \times 10^{-4}\,m$
- $L = 0.5\,m$ (desired penetration)
- $Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,m^3/s$ (low flow rate to prevent washout)
- $\mu = 0.5\,Pa\cdot s$ (typical low-viscosity epoxy)

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{(3 \times 10^{-4})^3}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:

$$
(3 \times 10^{-4})^3 = 27 \times 10^{-12} = 2.7 \times 10^{-11}\,m^3
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator:

$$
12 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}\,Pa\cdot m
$$

**Step 6:** Final pressure:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2.7 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.11 \times 10^{5}\,Pa = 0.111\,MPa
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A modest injection pressure of **~0.11 MPa (~1.1 bar)** is sufficient to drive low-viscosity epoxy 0.5 m into a 0.3 mm crack. Field practice typically uses **0.1 – 1.0 MPa**, with the upper end reserved for very fine cracks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method vs. Crack Geometry

| Crack Width (mm) | Recommended Method | Material | Application |
|------------------|---------------------|----------|-------------|
| 0.05 – 0.1 | Surface impregnation / coating | Silane / siloxane | Sealing only, not structural |
| 0.1 – 0.3 | Epoxy injection (fine) | Low-viscosity epoxy (~50 cP) | Pressure injection |
| 0.3 – 1.0 | Epoxy injection (standard) | Medium-viscosity epoxy (~200 cP) | Pressure injection |
| 1.0 – 3.0 | Routing & sealing or gravity filling | Epoxy mortar / polysulphide | Surface V-groove + fill |
| 3.0 – 6.0 | Stitching + epoxy fill | Epoxy + U-staples | Mechanical + chemical |
| > 6.0 | Drilling & plugging / structural repair | Epoxy + plug | Drilled hole filled solid |

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Dormant vs. Live Crack Repair

| Parameter | Dormant Crack | Live Crack |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Movement | < 0.05 mm/cycle | > 0.05 mm/cycle |
| Material type | Rigid (epoxy, cementitious) | Flexible (polysulphide, PU) |
| Bond expectation | Full structural re-bond | Movement accommodation (no bond) |
| Movement capacity | ≤ 5% strain | ±25% strain |
| Inspection | 6-month monitoring | Long-term monitoring required |
| Example | Completed shrinkage crack | Ongoing thermal crack |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dormant Crack Repair Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Crack Identified] --> B[Crack Survey: Map Location, Width, Length]
    B --> C[Install Monitoring: Demec Gauge / Tell-Tale]
    C --> D{Movement > 0.05 mm/cycle?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Classify as LIVE Crack: Use Flexible Sealant]
    D -- No --> F[Continue Monitoring for 6 Months]
    F --> G{Cumulative Movement < 0.1 mm?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Classify as DORMANT: Proceed with Rigid Repair]
    H --> I[Surface Preparation: Grinding, Cleaning, Drying]
    I --> J{Measure Crack Width}
    J -- 0.1 to 0.3 mm --> K[Method: Epoxy Injection - Fine]
    J -- 0.3 to 1.0 mm --> L[Method: Epoxy Injection - Standard]
    J -- 1.0 to 3.0 mm --> M[Method: Routing and Sealing]
    J -- 3.0 to 6.0 mm --> N[Method: Stitching plus Epoxy Fill]
    J -- Greater than 6.0 mm --> O[Method: Drilling and Plugging]
    K --> P[Quality Control: Pull-off Test, Core Sampling]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Repair Complete and Documented]
```

## 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Epoxy Injection Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clean Crack Surface] --> B[Seal Surface with Epoxy Paste]
    B --> C[Install Injection Ports at Spacing of 200 to 500 mm]
    C --> D[Mix Epoxy Resin and Hardener]
    D --> E[Inject Resin at Pressure of 0.1 to 1.0 MPa]
    E --> F{Port Saturated?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Cap Port and Move to Next Port]
    G --> H[All Ports Cured for 24 Hours]
    H --> I[Remove Surface Seal and Ports]
    I --> J[Grind Flush and Quality Test]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram — Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dormant Crack Confirmed] --> B{Through-Thickness?}
    B -- No --> C{Surface Crack Width}
    B -- Yes --> D{Width Range}
    C -- Less than 0.3 mm --> E[Surface Seal with Epoxy Putty]
    C -- 0.3 to 3.0 mm --> F[Routing and Sealing with Epoxy Mortar]
    C -- Greater than 3.0 mm --> G[Gravity Filling with Epoxy or Cement Grout]
    D -- 0.1 to 1.0 mm --> H[Epoxy Injection at Low to Medium Pressure]
    D -- 1.0 to 3.0 mm --> I[Epoxy Injection plus Surface Seal]
    D -- Greater than 3.0 mm --> J[Stitching or Drilling and Plugging]
```

## 4.4 Monitoring Block Diagram — Confirming Dormancy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Install Tell-Tale on Crack] --> B[Read Initial Width W0]
    B --> C[Wait 30 Days]
    C --> D[Read Width W1]
    D --> E{Absolute Difference less than 0.05 mm?}
    E -- No --> F[Classify as LIVE Crack: Stop Repair Planning]
    E -- Yes --> G[Continue Monitoring for 6 Months]
    G --> H[Calculate Total Movement W6 minus W0]
    H --> I{Movement less than 0.1 mm?}
    I -- No --> F
    I -- Yes --> J[Classify as DORMANT Crack: Proceed with Rigid Repair]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

> **"Define a dormant crack. List any two methods used for the repair of dormant cracks."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** A dormant crack is one whose width, length, and propagation have stabilized and which is **not expected to undergo further movement** under the prevailing service conditions. A crack with movement **less than 0.05 mm per cycle** observed over a 6-month period is generally classified as dormant.
> 
> **Two methods (1 mark):**
> 1. **Epoxy injection** — for through-thickness dormant cracks (0.1 – 6 mm).
> 2. **Routing and sealing** — for surface dormant cracks (0.3 – 6 mm).
> *(Acceptable alternates: stitching, drilling & plugging, gravity filling, surface sealing.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

> **"Distinguish between dormant and live cracks in concrete structures."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | S.No. | Dormant Crack | Live Crack |
> |-------|---------------|------------|
> | 1 | Movement < 0.05 mm/cycle | Movement > 0.05 mm/cycle |
> | 2 | Cause has been resolved | Cause still active (thermal, settlement, load) |
> | 3 | Repaired with **rigid** materials (epoxy, cementitious) | Repaired with **flexible** materials (polysulphide, PU, silicone) |
> | 4 | Restores monolithic structural action | Only seals against ingress; no structural bond |
> | 5 | Examples: completed shrinkage, post-settlement cracks | Examples: ongoing thermal, dynamic-load cracks |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]
