> **a) Explain in detail the procedure for confirming a crack as dormant. State the relevant monitoring duration and movement threshold. (7 Marks)**
>
> **b) Describe the epoxy injection method for repairing through-thickness dormant cracks, including material properties, equipment, and step-by-step procedure. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Definition of Dormancy (2 marks):** A dormant crack is one that has stabilized and shows no significant movement under service conditions. The **6-month monitoring** period and **< 0.05 mm per cycle / < 0.1 mm cumulative** thresholds are the standard KTU-referenced criteria (per ACI 224.1R and IS 456:2000 commentary).
> 
> **2. Field Monitoring Techniques (3 marks):**
> - **Demec (demountable mechanical) gauge:** Mechanical strain gauge with two pre-fixed studs straddling the crack; readings taken at regular intervals to ±0.001 mm accuracy.
> - **Vibrating-wire strain gauge:** Electronic sensor embedded across the crack for continuous remote monitoring.
> - **Plaster (gypsum) tell-tales:** A small plaster patch applied across the crack. If the crack is live, the plaster will crack within weeks.
> - **Glass strip tell-tale:** A thin glass strip epoxied across the crack; fractures confirm activity.
> 
> **3. Threshold & Justification (2 marks):**
> - Per-cycle movement: **< 0.05 mm** is within the elastic recovery range of the repair material and acceptable for rigid re-bonding.
> - Cumulative movement: **< 0.1 mm over 6 months** confirms stability under seasonal thermal and moisture variations.
> - The 6-month duration captures at least one full seasonal cycle (summer/winter or wet/dry), which is essential to rule out seasonal movement.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Suitability (1 mark):** Epoxy injection is suitable for **through-thickness dormant cracks** of width **0.1 – 6 mm** in beams, columns, walls, and slabs.
> 
> **2. Material Properties of Injection Epoxy (2 marks):**
> - Viscosity: **50 – 500 cP** (low enough to penetrate micro-cracks).
> - Tensile bond strength to dry concrete: **> 3.0 MPa**.
> - Compressive strength: **> 50 MPa** after 7 days cure.
> - Pot life: **20 – 60 minutes** at 25 °C.
> - Cure time: **24 hours** for full strength.
> 
> **3. Equipment (1 mark):** Air-driven or hand-operated injection pump, surface-mounted injection ports (T-ports or pneumatic packers), resin mixer, surface sealing compound (epoxy paste), and personal protective equipment.
> 
> **4. Step-by-Step Procedure (3 marks):**
> 1. **Surface preparation:** Clean both sides of the crack by grinding or wire brushing; remove dust, laitance, and contaminants; dry the surface.
> 2. **Surface sealing:** Seal the crack surface and around ports with epoxy paste; allow to cure.
> 3. **Port installation:** Fix injection ports at **200 – 500 mm spacing** along the crack, starting from the lowest point for vertical cracks.
> 4. **Resin injection:** Inject low-viscosity epoxy at **0.1 – 1.0 MPa** starting from the lowest port; continue until resin flows from the adjacent port.
> 5. **Port closure:** Cap the filled port and move to the next.
> 6. **Cure & finish:** Allow 24-hour cure; remove surface seal and ports; grind flush.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]

> **a) What are the different methods of repair for dormant cracks? Explain any three methods with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**
>
> **b) A reinforced concrete beam of cross-section 250 mm × 450 mm (effective depth 400 mm) develops a dormant through-thickness flexural crack of width 0.4 mm at the soffit. The service moment at the cracked section is 32 kN·m. Determine the bond development length required for an epoxy injection repair if the bond stress is 2.5 MPa and the effective repair depth is 60 mm. Comment on whether surface sealing alone is adequate. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Methods (2 marks):** Surface sealing, routing & sealing, epoxy injection, gravity filling, stitching, drilling & plugging.
> 
> **Method 1 — Epoxy Injection (1.5 marks):** Suitable for through-thickness cracks 0.1–6 mm. Low-viscosity epoxy is injected under pressure of 0.1–1.0 MPa via surface-mounted ports. Restores monolithic action.
> 
> **Method 2 — Routing and Sealing (1.5 marks):** Suitable for surface dormant cracks 0.3–6 mm. A V-shaped groove is cut along the crack using a grinder; the groove is filled with a rigid sealant (epoxy mortar) or, if future flexibility is desired, a flexible sealant.
> 
> **Method 3 — Stitching (2 marks):** Suitable for structural dormant cracks > 0.5 mm. Holes are drilled on both sides of the crack at 300–500 mm spacing, and U-shaped metallic staples (stitches) are grouted into the holes with epoxy. The staples transfer tensile force across the crack plane, providing redundant mechanical load transfer even if the adhesive fails.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $b = 250\,mm$, $d_{eff} = 400\,mm$, $y = d_{eff} = 400\,mm$ (taking extreme tension fibre distance approximately equal to effective depth for first-order analysis).
- $M = 32\,kN\cdot m = 32{,}000\,N\cdot m$
- $\tau_b = 2.5\,MPa = 2.5 \times 10^{6}\,Pa$
- $t_{eff} = 60\,mm = 0.060\,m$

**Step 1: Calculate the gross second moment of area.**

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12} = \frac{0.250 \times (0.450)^3}{12} = \frac{0.250 \times 0.0911}{12} = 1.898 \times 10^{-3}\,m^4
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum tensile stress at the soffit.**

$$
\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{32{,}000 \times 0.400}{1.898 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.74 \times 10^{6}\,Pa = 6.74\,MPa
$$

**Step 3: Apply the bond development length equation.**

$$
L_{bond} \geq \frac{\sigma_{t,\max} \cdot t_{eff}}{\tau_b}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
L_{bond} \geq \frac{6.74 \times 10^{6} \times 0.060}{2.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{4.044 \times 10^{5}}{2.5 \times 10^{6}} = 0.1618\,m
$$

**Step 5: Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{L_{bond} \approx 162\,mm \approx 0.16\,m}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A 162 mm penetration depth is required to develop sufficient bond. **Surface sealing alone is NOT adequate** because the maximum achievable depth of a surface-applied sealant is typically 5–10 mm. Therefore, **pressure epoxy injection** is mandatory to achieve the required 162 mm penetration and restore monolithic flexural capacity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing dormant with live cracks (–2 marks typical):** Students often apply flexible sealants to dormant cracks, demonstrating a fundamental misunderstanding. Always state the movement threshold (< 0.05 mm/cycle) and the consequence (rigid vs. flexible repair).
> 
> 2. **Skipping the 6-month monitoring justification (–1 to 2 marks):** Examiners expect a one-sentence justification — *the 6-month period captures at least one full seasonal thermal cycle.* Without this, the dormancy classification is unsupported.
> 
> 3. **Numerical errors in bond length derivations (–1 to 2 marks):** Common mistakes include using **effective depth d** instead of **distance to neutral axis y** in the flexural formula, or unit-mismatch between MPa and N/m². Always show units in the working.
> 
> 4. **Omitting port spacing and pressure range (–1 mark):** In epoxy injection, examiners look for **port spacing (200–500 mm)** and **injection pressure (0.1–1.0 MPa)** as standard site-practice parameters.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to mention surface preparation (–1 mark):** Surface cleaning is a non-negotiable first step in any crack repair; omitting it loses a mark in descriptive questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Repair to Dormant Cracks**

- **Dormant crack** = movement **< 0.05 mm/cycle** and **< 0.1 mm cumulative over 6 months**; cause resolved.
- **Live crack** = movement > 0.05 mm/cycle; ongoing cause.
- **Dormant → rigid repair; Live → flexible repair.** *(Most important one-liner for KTU exams.)*
- **Epoxy injection** is the gold-standard method for through-thickness dormant cracks (0.1 – 6 mm width).
- **Injection pressure:** **0.1 – 1.0 MPa**; **port spacing:** **200 – 500 mm**; **cure time:** **24 hours**.
- **Routing & sealing** is used for **surface** dormant cracks (0.3 – 6 mm); cut a V-groove and fill with rigid epoxy mortar.
- **Stitching** uses U-shaped metallic staples at 300–500 mm spacing for structural cracks > 0.5 mm.
- **Drilling & plugging** is for very wide (> 6 mm) through-thickness cracks.
- **Bond development length equation:** $L_{bond} \geq \dfrac{\sigma_{t,\max} \cdot t_{eff}}{\tau_b}$.
- **Flexural stress at extreme fibre:** $\sigma_t = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_{gross}}$.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille for resin flow:** $Q = \dfrac{w^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$.
- **Tell-tale tests:** Plaster patches and glass strips fracture on live cracks; if intact after 30 days, suspect dormancy — continue for 6 months.
- **Pull-off bond test** for QC: minimum tensile bond strength **≥ 1.5 MPa** (acceptance) or **≥ 3.0 MPa** (preferred).
- **Always start surface preparation** with mechanical cleaning (grinding, sandblasting) before any repair.
- **Epoxy properties to remember:** viscosity 50–500 cP, tensile strength > 30 MPa, bond to concrete > 3 MPa, pot life 20–60 minutes.
- **Cementitious grouts** are acceptable for non-structural wide dormant cracks where epoxy is uneconomical.
- **KTU 14-mark questions** typically combine a **methodology part (7 marks)** with a **numerical derivation (7 marks)** — practice both formats.
- **Key IS code reference:** IS 456:2000 (plain and reinforced concrete), ACI 224.1R (causes, evaluation, and repair of cracks), and IS 13311 (non-destructive testing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion of Embedded Steel in Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion of Embedded Steel in Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion of Embedded Steel in Concrete** is an *electrochemical deterioration process* in which the reinforcing steel embedded in a hardened cementitious matrix undergoes oxidative degradation, converting metallic iron (Fe⁰) into thermodynamically stable iron oxide/hydroxide corrosion products. It constitutes the single most dominant durability-driven distress mechanism in reinforced concrete structures (RCC).

In the discipline of structural repair and rehabilitation (as per KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECET634**), corrosion of steel is classified as a **premature material-level distress** that leads to serviceability and ultimate limit-state failures, demanding systematic diagnosis, prognosis, and rehabilitation.

### The Electrochemical Premise

Steel in concrete is *normally protected* by a thin, tightly adherent, amorphous iron oxide film — known as the **passive layer** — which forms because the highly alkaline pore solution of hardened cement paste maintains a pH in the range of **12.5 to 13.5**. In this regime, the steel is in a state of *thermodynamic immunity* (the Pourbaix diagram classifies this zone as "Passivation").

Corrosion initiates when this passive layer is destroyed, either by:
1. **Carbonation** — atmospheric CO₂ reacting with Ca(OH)₂ to lower pH below ~9.
2. **Chloride contamination** — Cl⁻ ions exceeding a critical threshold (~0.4 % by mass of cement for ordinary structures) locally breaking down the passive film.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Rusty Pipe in a Tonic

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a brass button left inside a sealed glass of club soda for years. Although submerged, the button remains shiny because the mildly alkaline water forms a protective oxide film (like passivation). Now add a pinch of salt (NaCl) to the soda. Within weeks, the button develops greenish corrosion spots. *Why?* The chloride ions (Cl⁻) locally perforate the protective film and create a tiny battery on the metal's surface — anodes (where the metal dissolves) and cathodes (where oxygen is reduced) connected by the conductive liquid (the electrolyte). 
> 
> Embedded steel in concrete behaves **identically**: the pore solution acts as the electrolyte, the concrete cover protects the steel just as the glass protects the button, and chloride ions (or carbonation-induced pH drop) are the "salt" that destroys passivity.

### The Two Fundamental Reactions

$$\text{Anodic (Oxidation of Iron):} \quad \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

$$\text{Cathodic (Reduction of Oxygen):} \quad \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

The overall reaction produces **iron hydroxides** that further oxidize to form **hydrated ferric oxide (Fe₂O₃·H₂O)** — the familiar *red-brown rust* — whose molar volume is **2 to 6 times** that of the parent steel, generating destructive internal expansive pressures (≈ 15–40 MPa) sufficient to crack and spall the surrounding concrete.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key markers examiners look for in 14-mark answers:**
> - Identification of the **anode–cathode–electrolyte** triad forming a **galvanic macrocell**.
> - Recognition of **pH drop below 11** (carbonation) or **Cl⁻/OH⁻ ratio > 0.6** (chloride attack) as initiation thresholds.
> - Recognition that **rust occupies 2–6× the volume of original steel** — the *root cause of cracking*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pre-Requisites of the Corrosion Cell (Tuutti's Model)

For corrosion to occur, the following must *co-exist simultaneously* on the embedded steel surface:

1. **Loss of passivity** — pH < 11, OR critical [Cl⁻] reached.
2. **Presence of an electrolyte** — moist concrete pore solution (resistivity < 100 kΩ·cm).
3. **Oxygen availability** — required at the cathode (diffuses through cover concrete).
4. **Electrode potential difference** — ΔE between anodic and cathodic zones > 100 mV.
5. **Electrical connectivity** — ionic path through pore solution + electronic path through steel.

### 2.2 Stages of Corrosion Deterioration — Tuutti's Two-Phase Model

The Swedish researcher **K. Tuutti** (1982) established the canonical time-domain description:

- **Phase I — Initiation Period (t₀):** Time during which aggressive agents (Cl⁻, CO₂) penetrate the cover concrete to the rebar depth. *No visible damage yet.* Governed by diffusion kinetics.
- **Phase II — Propagation Period (t₁):** Active corrosion, loss of steel cross-section, cracking, spalling, and bond deterioration. Governed by electrochemical reaction rates (Faraday's law).

$$t_{\text{design}} = t_0 + t_1$$

### 2.3 Carbonation Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

1. Atmospheric CO₂ diffuses through cover concrete (Fickian diffusion).
2. Reacts with calcium hydroxide: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
3. Also decomposes C-S-H gel: $\text{C-S-H} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$
4. Pore solution pH drops from **≈ 12.5** to **< 9** at the carbonation front.
5. Passive Fe₂O₃ layer becomes thermodynamically unstable.
6. Corrosion cell activates.

> Carbonation depth follows $x_c = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$, where $k$ is the carbonation coefficient (mm/√year).

### 2.4 Chloride-Induced Corrosion

Chlorides penetrate via:
- **Diffusion** (concentration gradient) — dominant in submerged/saturated concrete.
- **Capillary absorption** — dominant in splash/tidal zones.
- **Permeation** — under pressure (less common).

The classical **Fick's Second Law** solution for chloride ingress (constant surface concentration $C_s$):

$$C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i) \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)$$

where the critical time to initiation $t_0$ is found by setting $C(x_c, t_0) = C_{cr}$:

$$t_0 = \dfrac{x_c^{\,2}}{4 D_c} \cdot \left[\text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i}\right)\right]^{2}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use SI units unless otherwise stated. No pipe characters are used in the table to preserve markdown compatibility.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law — mass of steel dissolved | grams |
| 2 | $CR = 0.0116 \cdot i_{corr}$ | Corrosion rate from current density (for Fe, n = 2) | mm/year |
| 3 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I}{A_{\text{anode}}}$ | Definition of corrosion current density | μA/cm² |
| 4 | $C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)$ | Fick's 2nd law — chloride concentration profile | % by cement mass |
| 5 | $t_0 = \dfrac{x_c^{\,2}}{4 D_c}\left[\text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i}\right)\right]^2$ | Time to corrosion initiation | years |
| 6 | $x_c = k\sqrt{t}$ | Carbonation depth (empirical) | mm |
| 7 | $P_{\text{rust}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxides}} - V_{\text{steel}}}{V_{\text{steel}}} \times \rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot g$ | Expansive pressure due to rust (molar volume ratio 2–6) | MPa |
| 8 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | Nernst equation — electrode potential | Volts |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{concrete}} \downarrow \Rightarrow i_{corr} \uparrow$ | Resistivity ↔ corrosion rate correlation | kΩ·cm |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{an}} = \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer (Tafel) overpotential | V |

### 2.6 Engineering Significance

- **Half-cell potential (HCP) surveys** (ASTM C876) are *non-destructive* diagnostic tools used in service-life assessment of bridges and marine structures.
- A measured potential **more negative than –350 mV (vs. Cu/CuSO₄ electrode)** indicates ≥ 90 % probability of active corrosion.
- The **molar volume expansion** of rust is what translates a microscopic electrochemical event into macroscopic structural damage (cover cracking → spalling → loss of bond → reduced flexural capacity).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are presented in exhaustive step-by-step format suitable for full 7-mark KTU solutions. No algebraic transitions are skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate Conversion Factor (0.0116)

**Objective:** Show that a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 1\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ corresponds to a steel penetration rate of **0.0116 mm/year**.

**Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:**

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where  
- $\Delta m$ = mass of metal dissolved (g)  
- $I$ = current (A)  
- $t$ = time (s)  
- $M$ = molar mass of Fe = **55.847 g/mol**  
- $n$ = valency electrons transferred = **2** (Fe → Fe²⁺)  
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96 485 C/mol**  

**Step 1 — Express mass loss per unit area and per unit time using current density $i_{corr}$ (μA/cm²):**

$$\dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad \text{(A = 1 cm}^2\text{, t = 1 s)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $i_{corr}$ from μA/cm² to A/cm²:**

$$i_{corr}\ (\text{A/cm}^2) = i_{corr}\ (\mu\text{A/cm}^2) \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear penetration rate $CR$ using $\rho_{Fe} = 7.874\ \text{g/cm}^3$:**

$$CR = \dfrac{\text{Volume dissolved per unit area per unit time}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{\Delta m / (A \cdot t)}{\rho_{Fe}}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M \times 10^{-6}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Fe}} \quad \text{[cm/s]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert from cm/s to mm/year.** A year has $365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^7$ s. Also 1 cm = 10 mm.

$$CR\ \text{(mm/year)} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M \times 10^{-6} \times 3.156 \times 10^7 \times 10}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Fe}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot 55.847 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.156 \times 10^7 \times 10}{2 \times 96485 \times 7.874}$$

$$CR = i_{corr} \times \dfrac{55.847 \times 3.156 \times 10^{2}}{2 \times 96485 \times 7.874}$$

$$CR = i_{corr} \times \dfrac{17\,625.3}{1\,519\,475.4} = i_{corr} \times 0.01160 \;\;\tfrac{\text{mm}}{\text{year}\cdot\mu\text{A/cm}^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,CR\ (\text{mm/year}) = 0.0116 \times i_{corr}\ (\mu\text{A/cm}^2)\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Time to Structural End-of-Service (14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A simply supported RCC bridge girder has a main longitudinal bar of **20 mm diameter** with a clear cover of **50 mm**. Half-cell potential survey reports a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 1.5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ on the bar surface. The owner requires the bar to retain a residual diameter of at least **16 mm** before repair is triggered.
> 
> Determine:
> **(a)** The annual steel penetration rate in mm/year.
> **(b)** The annual cross-sectional area loss in % of the original.
> **(c)** The time (in years) to reach the trigger residual diameter of 16 mm.

**Given Data:**

$$d_0 = 20\ \text{mm}, \quad d_{\text{residual}} = 16\ \text{mm}, \quad i_{corr} = 1.5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

**Part (a) — Annual Penetration Rate:**

Using the derived formula:

$$CR = 0.0116 \times 1.5 = 0.0174\ \text{mm/year}$$

> The corrosion front advances into the bar at **17.4 μm per year**.

**Part (b) — Annual Cross-Sectional Area Loss:**

Original cross-sectional area:

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (20)^2 = 314.16\ \text{mm}^2$$

Residual area after **1 year** (bar diameter reduced by $2 \times CR$ since corrosion acts on both the top and bottom faces in a uniform macrocell — but for conservative design we use *one-sided* reduction of $CR$ per year, i.e. radius loss = $CR$):

$$d_1 = 20 - 2(0.0174) = 19.9652\ \text{mm}$$

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (19.9652)^2 = 313.08\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$\% \text{ loss} = \dfrac{314.16 - 313.08}{314.16} \times 100 = 0.344\ \%/\text{year}$$

**Part (c) — Time to Reach $d = 16$ mm:**

Radius loss required:

$$\Delta r = \dfrac{20 - 16}{2} = 2.0\ \text{mm}$$

Time required:

$$t_1 = \dfrac{\Delta r}{CR} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.0174} = 114.94\ \text{years}$$

> **Conclusion:** Under the *assumed uniform* $i_{corr} = 1.5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$, the bar retains structural adequacy for **≈ 115 years** before triggering repair. However, KTU examiners will accept this only with the caveat that **pitting corrosion** (localised) can reduce this service life to **a fraction** (often 5–10× faster local penetration).

---

### 3.3 Initiation Time via Fick's Second Law (Chloride)

**Given (typical Kerala coastal scenario):**  
- Cover $x_c = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$  
- Surface chloride $C_s = 0.8\ \%$ (by mass of cement)  
- Initial chloride $C_i = 0.05\ \%$  
- Critical chloride $C_{cr} = 0.4\ \%$  
- Apparent diffusion coefficient $D_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Argument of erfc:**

$$\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i} = \dfrac{0.8 - 0.4}{0.8 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.75} = 0.5333$$

**Step 2 — Lookup inverse complementary error function:**

$$\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.5333) \approx 0.466$$

**Step 3 — Compute $t_0$ in seconds, then convert to years:**

$$t_0 = \dfrac{(0.05)^2}{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}} \times (0.466)^2$$

$$t_0 = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-12}} \times 0.2172 = 4.167 \times 10^{8} \times 0.2172$$

$$t_0 = 9.05 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s} = \dfrac{9.05 \times 10^{7}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2.87\ \text{years}$$

> **Striking Result:** With $D_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (a value typical of poor-quality coastal concrete), corrosion initiates in **less than 3 years**. This is why KTU examiners stress the importance of *low w/c ratio* and *pozzolanic additions* (GGBS, fly ash) which lower $D_c$ by **one to two orders of magnitude**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Macrocell Corrosion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reinforcing Steel in Concrete] --> B[Alkaline Pore Solution pH 12.5 to 13.5]
    B --> C[Passive Fe2O3 Film Forms]
    C --> D{Aggressive Agent Arrives}
    D -->|CO2 Diffusion| E1[Carbonation: pH drops below 9]
    D -->|Cl- Ions| E2[Chloride Threshold Exceeded]
    E1 --> F[Passive Film Destabilised]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Anodic Zone: Fe to Fe2 plus 2e]
    F --> H[Cathodic Zone: O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e to 4OH]
    G --> I[Fe2 plus migrates through pore solution]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Fe2 plus plus 2OH minus to Fe OH 2]
    J --> K[Fe OH 2 plus O2 to Fe2O3 dot H2O Red Rust]
    K --> L[Volume Expansion 2x to 6x]
    L --> M[Internal Tensile Pressure 15 to 40 MPa]
    M --> N[Cover Cracking and Spalling]
    N --> O[Loss of Steel to Concrete Bond]
    O --> P[Reduced Flexural and Shear Capacity]
    P --> Q[Structural End of Service Life]
```

### 4.2 Tuutti's Two-Stage Service-Life Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Service Life Begins] -->|t0 Initiation| B[Corrosion Initiates]
    B -->|t1 Propagation| C[Cover Cracking]
    C --> D[Spalling]
    D --> E[Structural Limit State]
    subgraph init[Initiation Phase t0]
      A
    end
    subgraph prop[Propagation Phase t1]
      B
      C
      D
    end
```

### 4.3 Electrochemical Macrocell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph anodic_zone[Anodic Region Passivity Lost]
      A1[Fe Anode] --> A2[Fe2 plus plus 2e]
    end
    subgraph cathodic_zone[Cathodic Region Passive]
      C1[Fe Cathode] --> C2[O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e to 4OH minus]
    end
    A2 -- electrons through steel --> C2
    C2 -- OH minus through pore solution --> A2
    A2 -- Fe2 plus through pore solution --> J[Fe OH 2 Rust Product]
    C2 -- ionic current --> J
```

### 4.4 Interpreting Half-Cell Potential (HCP) Readings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Measured HCP vs CuCuSO4] --> Q1{Potential more positive than minus 200 mV}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Greater than 90 percent probability NO corrosion]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Potential between minus 200 and minus 350 mV}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Intermediate zone uncertain active or passive]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Potential more negative than minus 350 mV}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Greater than 90 percent probability ACTIVE corrosion]
    R3 --> Action[Trigger Detailed Investigation and Repair]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Note:** The HCP survey is part of the *non-destructive testing* toolkit in Module 4. Although our course topic is on *corrosion mechanism*, the **diagnostic-prognostic linkage** is examinable — particularly in 14-mark questions where the question may ask for both *the cause* and *the testing protocol*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the phenomenon of corrosion of steel in concrete. List the five pre-requisites for the formation of a corrosion cell on embedded reinforcement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Corrosion of embedded steel in concrete is the electrochemical oxidation of iron (Fe⁰ → Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺) at the steel–concrete interface, producing expansive iron oxide/hydroxide products that damage the surrounding concrete.

> **Five pre-requisites (2 marks):**
> 1. **Depassivation** of the protective Fe₂O₃ film (caused by pH drop or Cl⁻).
> 2. **Presence of an electrolyte** (moist pore solution with resistivity < 100 kΩ·cm).
> 3. **Oxygen availability** at the cathode.
> 4. **Electrode potential difference** (ΔE) between anodic and cathodic regions.
> 5. **Electrical continuity** — ionic path through pore solution + electronic path through the steel bar.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain why rust occupies 2 to 6 times the volume of the parent steel, and state the engineering consequence of this volumetric expansion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Why volume expansion (2 marks):** The parent iron has a high atomic packing density (BCC structure, molar volume ≈ 7.1 cm³/mol). The corrosion products — Fe(OH)₂, Fe(OH)₃, Fe₂O₃·H₂O (hydrated ferric oxide) — have lower density, more open crystal structures, and include chemically bound water molecules within the lattice. This makes the *molar volume* of the oxides 2× to 6× that of the parent iron.

> **Engineering consequence (1 mark):** The confined expansion generates internal **tensile hoop stresses of 15 to 40 MPa** around the bar, exceeding the tensile strength of concrete (≈ 3–5 MPa). This causes **cover cracking parallel to the bar**, followed by **spalling** of the cover concrete and loss of bond, ultimately reducing the load-carrying capacity of the structural member.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **electrochemical mechanism** of corrosion of embedded steel in concrete. Describe the anodic and cathodic half-reactions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 16 mm diameter rebar with 40 mm cover is reported to have a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 2.0\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ uniformly distributed. Calculate:  
    (i) the annual steel penetration rate in mm/year, and  
    (ii) the time (in years) for the bar diameter to reduce to 12 mm. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

> 1. **Sketch of the macrocell (3 marks):** Draw a labelled diagram showing the rebar in concrete, identifying the anodic region (passivity broken, e.g., by chloride), the cathodic region (still passive, well-oxygenated), the pore solution as the electrolyte, and the arrows showing electron flow inside the steel and ionic current in the pore solution.
> 2. **Anodic half-reaction (2 marks):**  
> $$\text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$$  
> This is the *oxidation* reaction; metallic iron dissolves into the pore solution as ferrous ions, releasing electrons.
> 3. **Cathodic half-reaction (2 marks):**  
> $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$  
> The electrons released at the anode travel through the steel to the cathode, where dissolved oxygen is reduced to hydroxide ions in the presence of water.
> 4. **Product formation (1 mark):** Fe²⁺ migrates to OH⁻-rich cathodic zones, precipitating as Fe(OH)₂, which further oxidises to Fe(OH)₃ / Fe₂O₃·H₂O (red rust). The 2–6× volume expansion cracks the cover.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $d_0 = 16$ mm, $x_c = 40$ mm (cover), $i_{corr} = 2.0\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$, $d_{\text{final}} = 12$ mm.

**Sub-question (i) — Annual penetration rate (3 marks):**

$$CR = 0.0116 \times i_{corr} = 0.0116 \times 2.0 = 0.0232\ \text{mm/year}$$

> **Answer:** The corrosion front penetrates **0.0232 mm/year** (i.e. **23.2 μm/year**).

**Sub-question (ii) — Time to reach 12 mm diameter (4 marks):**

Radius loss required:

$$\Delta r = \dfrac{d_0 - d_{\text{final}}}{2} = \dfrac{16 - 12}{2} = 2.0\ \text{mm}$$

Time to reach this loss:

$$t_1 = \dfrac{\Delta r}{CR} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.0232} = 86.21\ \text{years}$$

> **Answer:** **≈ 86.2 years** for the bar to lose 25 % of its cross-sectional area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget that the **diameter loss is 2× the radial penetration** (corrosion attacks from *both* sides in uniform macrocell). Use $\Delta d / 2$ when computing $\Delta r$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to convert $i_{corr}$ to mm/year using the 0.0116 factor — examiners award full marks only when the formula is **explicitly cited**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Neglecting to mention that this calculation assumes **uniform corrosion**; in reality, pitting corrosion (macrocell attack) may reduce local service life to **as little as 1/5 to 1/10** of the calculated value.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Tuutti's service-life model** for corrosion-affected reinforced concrete, clearly distinguishing between the *initiation* and *propagation* phases. State the controlling physical phenomenon in each phase. (7 marks)

**(b)** A coastal RCC pile in Kerala has a clear cover of 60 mm, surface chloride concentration $C_s = 0.7\ \%$ (by mass of cement), initial chloride $C_i = 0.0\ \%$, critical chloride $C_{cr} = 0.4\ \%$, and apparent chloride diffusion coefficient $D_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.  
Using Fick's second law of diffusion, estimate the **time to corrosion initiation** in years. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

> 1. **Conceptual description (2 marks):** Tuutti (1982) proposed that the *service life* of an RC member subject to reinforcement corrosion is the sum of two sequential phases: $t_{SL} = t_0 + t_1$, where $t_0$ is the *initiation* period and $t_1$ is the *propagation* period.
> 2. **Initiation phase (2.5 marks):** Begins at construction and ends when aggressive agents (Cl⁻ or CO₂) penetrate the cover to reach the rebar and destroy passivity. *No visible damage* occurs in this phase. The controlling phenomenon is **mass transport by diffusion** (Fick's second law for chlorides; $x_c = k\sqrt{t}$ for carbonation).
> 3. **Propagation phase (2 marks):** Begins with active corrosion and ends when an *ultimate* or *serviceability* limit state is reached (e.g., cover cracking, spalling, or excessive steel loss). The controlling phenomenon is **Faraday's electrochemical kinetics** — corrosion rate in mm/year is given by $CR = 0.0116 \cdot i_{corr}$.
> 4. **Significance (0.5 mark):** Design strategies (e.g., increased cover, low w/c, pozzolanic additions, coatings, cathodic prevention) aim to *extend $t_0$*, while inspection and repair scheduling are based on *estimating $t_1$*.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $x_c = 0.06$ m, $C_s = 0.7\ \%$, $C_i = 0.0\ \%$, $C_{cr} = 0.4\ \%$, $D_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Argument of erfc:**

$$\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i} = \dfrac{0.7 - 0.4}{0.7 - 0.0} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.7} = 0.4286$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Inverse erfc lookup:**

$$\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.4286) \approx 0.566 \quad \text{(from standard erfc tables / Abramowitz \& Stegun)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute into $t_0$ formula:**

$$t_0 = \dfrac{x_c^{\,2}}{4 D_c} \left[\text{erfc}^{-1}(\cdot)\right]^2 = \dfrac{(0.06)^2}{4 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-12}} \times (0.566)^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Evaluate the squared terms:**

$$(0.06)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$(0.566)^2 = 0.3204$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{8.0 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.5 \times 10^{8}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Multiply and convert to years:**

$$t_0 = 4.5 \times 10^{8} \times 0.3204 = 1.442 \times 10^{8}\ \text{s}$$

$$t_0 = \dfrac{1.442 \times 10^{8}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}} = 4.57\ \text{years}$$

> **Answer:** Corrosion initiates in **approximately 4.6 years** for this coastal pile — a distressingly short service life that justifies the use of *blended cements* (PPC, PSC with high GGBS) and *low w/c ratios* to reduce $D_c$ by an order of magnitude.

**Final remark (1 mark):** Note that $D_c$ is *time-dependent* in reality ($D_c = D_{c,0} (t_0/t)^n$ with $n \approx 0.3$–$0.6$ for blended cements); the constant-$D_c$ assumption is conservative and acceptable for first-pass KTU examinations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert $C_i$ correctly (here it is 0 %, not 0.05 %).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to convert **seconds to years** at the final step (loses 1 full mark).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using the wrong erfc lookup — students often confuse erfc and erf. erfc(z) = 1 − erf(z), and as $z \to 0$, erfc → 1; as $z \to \infty$, erfc → 0.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Citing Fick's first law (steady-state) instead of Fick's second law (transient). The latter is correct for chloride ingress into concrete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Corrosion of embedded steel = electrochemical oxidation of Fe in concrete pore solution; an anodic–cathodic–electrolyte triad.
- **Passivity:** Stable due to Fe₂O₃ film at pH 12.5–13.5; destroyed below pH ≈ 9 (carbonation) or above Cl⁻/OH⁻ ratio ≈ 0.6 (chloride).
- **Tuutti's Model:** $t_{SL} = t_0 + t_1$ — initiation (diffusion-controlled) + propagation (Faraday-controlled).
- **Faraday's Law:** $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — used to convert current to mass loss.
- **Critical Conversion:** $CR\ (\text{mm/year}) = 0.0116 \times i_{corr}\ (\mu\text{A/cm}^2)$ — the *single most important* formula in this module.
- **Rust Volume Expansion:** 2× to 6× the parent steel → internal pressure 15–40 MPa → cover cracking and spalling.
- **Chloride Diffusion:** Fick's 2nd law, $C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i)\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_c t}}\right)$.
- **Initiation Time:** $t_0 = \dfrac{x_c^{\,2}}{4 D_c}\left[\text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i}\right)\right]^2$.
- **Carbonation Depth:** $x_c = k\sqrt{t}$ (empirical; $k$ depends on w/c, cement type, exposure).
- **HCP Survey:** E < –200 mV (Cu/CuSO₄) → passive; –200 to –350 mV → uncertain; < –350 mV → active corrosion.
- **Numerical Constants to Memorise:** $F = 96\,485$ C/mol; $M_{Fe} = 55.847$ g/mol; $\rho_{Fe} = 7.874$ g/cm³; $n = 2$ for Fe²⁺; 1 year ≈ $3.156 \times 10^7$ s.
- **Differentiate** *uniform* corrosion (covers whole bar) from *pitting* corrosion (localised, up to 5–10× faster locally).
- **Kerala context:** Coastal atmosphere (Kochi, Kozhikode, Alappuzha backwaters) → high $C_s$, high humidity → low concrete resistivity → **chloride-induced corrosion dominates**.
- **Common mitigation strategies** (just for context, not derivation): increased cover, low w/c, pozzolanic cements (PPC/PSC), corrosion inhibitors, epoxy-coated bars, cathodic protection, concrete sealers — these belong to *Module 5* of the syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Deterioration Mechanisms in Concrete Structures

A **deterioration mechanism** is a scientifically established physical, chemical, electrochemical, or biological process that progressively degrades the engineering properties (strength, durability, serviceability) of a structural element by altering its constituent materials at the micro-structural level. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures*, mechanisms are broadly classified as:

- **Physical Mechanisms** – Mechanical loading, thermal cycling, freeze-thaw, abrasion, cavitation, fire.
- **Chemical Mechanisms** – Sulfate attack, acid attack, alkali-aggregate reaction (AAR/ASR), leaching.
- **Electrochemical Mechanisms** – Reinforcement corrosion (carbonation-induced, chloride-induced).
- **Biological Mechanisms** – Microbially Induced Corrosion (MIC), biogenic sulfate attack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Every repair strategy is *symptomatic* until the root **mechanism** is correctly identified. A misdiagnosed mechanism (e.g., treating chloride attack as carbonation) is the single largest cause of repeated repair failure reported in field case studies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a concrete structure as a **human body**:

- The **concrete matrix** = the skin and flesh.
- The **steel reinforcement** = the bones holding everything together.
- The **mechanisms** = diseases, injuries, or aging.

Just as a doctor must first identify whether a patient has an infection (chemical attack), a fracture (mechanical damage), or osteoporosis (loss of passivation), a structural engineer must first **identify the mechanism** before prescribing grouting, jacketing, or cathodic protection. A bandage (cosmetic patching) on a fractured bone (structural mechanism) will never heal the patient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** The **Service Life** of a structure, denoted $T_{SL}$, is the time until a defined **limit state** (e.g., corrosion initiation, cracking, spalling) is reached. The mechanism dictates which limit state governs.

$$\boxed{T_{SL} = f(\text{Mechanism}, \text{Environment}, \text{Material Quality})}$$

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|:------:|:----------|:--------------|:----:|
| $D_{CO_2}$ | Effective CO₂ diffusion coefficient | $1 \times 10^{-8}$ to $1 \times 10^{-7}$ | m²/s |
| $D_{Cl^-}$ | Effective chloride diffusion coefficient | $1 \times 10^{-12}$ to $5 \times 10^{-12}$ | m²/s |
| $E_{corr}$ | Corrosion potential | $-200$ to $-500$ | mV (SCE) |
| $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion current density | $< 0.1$ (passive), $> 1.0$ (active) | μA/cm² |
| $T_{cr}$ | Critical chloride threshold | $0.4$ to $1.0$ (by wt. of cement) | % |
| $pH$ | Concrete pore solution pH | $12.5$ to $13.5$ | – |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Deterioration Mechanisms

### Pillar I – Chemical Attack Mechanisms

#### 2.1.1 Sulfate Attack
Sulfate ions (SO₄²⁻) from soil, groundwater, or seawater react with tricalcite aluminate (C₃A) and calcium hydroxide (CH) to form **ettringite** and **gypsum**. Ettringite has a larger molar volume than its parent phases, causing **expansive internal stress** and cracking.

$$C_3A + 3\,CSH_2 + 26\,H \longrightarrow C_6A\bar{S}_3H_{32} \quad (\text{Ettringite})$$

$$CH + SO_4^{2-} + 2H \longrightarrow C\bar{S}H_2 \quad (\text{Gypsum})$$

- **Molar volume of ettringite:** ~735 cm³/mol
- **Molar volume of reactants:** ~310 cm³/mol
- **Net expansion:** ~137 % volumetric increase → tensile rupture of paste.

#### 2.1.2 Acid Attack
Acids (organic or mineral) lower the **pore-water pH** below the passivation threshold (~9.5), depassivating steel and dissolving the cement matrix (calcite leaching).

$$Ca(OH)_2 + 2\,H^+ \longrightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2\,H_2O$$

$$CaCO_3 + H^+ \longrightarrow Ca^{2+} + HCO_3^-$$

#### 2.1.3 Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR)
Reactive silica in aggregates (e.g., opal, chert) reacts with alkali hydroxides (K⁺, Na⁺) in pore solution to form a hygroscopic **alkali-silica gel** that swells by imbibing moisture.

$$SiO_2 + NaOH + H_2O \longrightarrow Na_2SiO_3 \cdot nH_2O \quad (\text{ASR Gel})$$

- Expansion pressure: up to **10–20 MPa**, exceeding the tensile strength of concrete (~3–5 MPa).
- Diagnostic feature: **map-pattern (pattern) cracking**.

### Pillar II – Electrochemical Corrosion of Steel

Steel in highly alkaline concrete ($pH > 12.5$) forms a passive $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ film. Depassivation occurs via:
1. **Carbonation** – atmospheric CO₂ reduces pH to < 9.5.
2. **Chloride ingress** – Cl⁻ ions break the passive film locally (pitting).

The **electrochemical cell** operates with:
- **Anode:** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$
- **Cathode:** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
- **Corrosion product (rust):** Volume expansion of **2 to 6 times** the original steel volume → cracking and spalling.

#### 2.1.4 Carbonation Mechanism
CO₂ diffuses through the porous cover and reacts with calcium hydroxide:

$$Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \longrightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O$$

The carbonation front advances according to Fick's 1st law.

### Pillar III – Physical Damage Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Driving Force | Visible Symptom |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------|
| Freeze-Thaw | Hydraulic + osmotic pressure from ice | Surface scaling, D-cracking |
| Abrasion/Erosion | Mechanical wear (tires, water-borne silt) | Loss of cover, exposed aggregate |
| Fire/Heat | Thermal stress, spalling (300–600 °C) | C-S-H dehydroxylation, explosive spalling |
| Cavitation | Vapor bubble collapse (high-velocity water) | Pitting in hydraulic structures |

### Pillar IV – Combined / Synergistic Mechanisms

In real field exposure, mechanisms **rarely act in isolation**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes *synergistic deterioration*:

> [!NOTE]
> **Synergy Example:** A marine pile in tropical Kerala experiences *chloride attack + carbonation + sulfate attack + biological fouling + cyclic wet-dry* simultaneously. A repair design assuming a single mechanism will under-specify the intervention.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $C(x,t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i)\,erf\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D \cdot t}}\right)$ | Fick's 2nd Law (chloride / CO₂ penetration) | Cover-depth vs. time | $C$ in %, $x$ in m, $t$ in s |
| 2 | $X_c = k \sqrt{t}$ | Carbonation depth (simplified) | Carbonation front | $X_c$ in mm, $t$ in years |
| 3 | $T_{corr} = \dfrac{x^2}{4 D}\left[\,erf^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{C_{cr}}{C_s}\right)\right]^{-2}$ | Time to corrosion initiation (Tuutti model) | Service-life prediction | years |
| 4 | $P_{ice} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot u}{3}\left(1.09 - \dfrac{1}{S}\right) \cdot \dfrac{dw}{dx}$ | Hydraulic pressure (Powers) | Freeze-thaw | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta V = V_{rust} - V_{steel} = (2 \text{ to } 6) V_{steel}$ | Corrosion expansion | Cracking, spalling | – |
| 6 | $ASR_{exp} = \alpha \cdot R \cdot M \cdot H$ | ASR expansion (multivariate) | ASR damage | strain |
| 7 | $E_{corr}, i_{corr}$ | Electrochemical diagnostics | Corrosion assessment | mV, μA/cm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** In the table above, absolute value / modulus expressions are written with `\left[\dots\right]` or `\vert\dots\vert` so they **never break** the table cell parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Mechanism | Affected Structures in Kerala | Engineering Action |
|:----------|:------------------------------|:-------------------|
| Chloride attack | Coastal bridges, fishing jetties (Kochi, Kozhikode) | Epoxy coating, CP, low $w/c$ |
| Carbonation | Urban RC frames (Kerala backwater humidity) | Adequate cover ≥ 30 mm, low-alkali cement |
| Sulfate attack | Pile foundations in lateritic soil | Sulfate-Resisting Cement (SRC / IS 12330) |
| ASR | Quarries with reactive silica (Pathanamthitta) | Low-alkali cement, SCMs (GGBS, fly ash) |
| Fire damage | Industrial buildings | Post-fire audit, jacketing, replacement |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fick's 2nd Law for Chloride Ingress (Tuutti's Service-Life Model)

The governing equation for one-dimensional diffusion of chloride ions into saturated concrete is **Fick's 2nd Law**:

$$\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 1 – Initial Condition (IC)
At time $t = 0$, the chloride concentration in the concrete is the **background** or **initial** value $C_i$, uniform throughout the cover depth:

$$C(x, 0) = C_i \quad \text{for } x \geq 0$$

### Step 2 – Boundary Condition (BC) at the Exposed Surface
The surface is in continuous contact with the chloride source (e.g., seawater splash), so the concentration at $x = 0$ equals the **surface concentration** $C_s$, which is approximately constant for long-duration exposure:

$$C(0, t) = C_s \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

### Step 3 – Boundary Condition at Infinite Depth
As depth $x \rightarrow \infty$, the concentration remains the initial value (semi-infinite domain assumption):

$$C(\infty, t) = C_i \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

### Step 4 – Apply the Method of Separation / Boltzmann Transform
Introduce the similarity variable $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D t}}$ so that the PDE reduces to an ODE in $\eta$. Differentiating using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \left(-\frac{\eta}{2t}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} = \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 \eta}{\partial x^2} = \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} \cdot \frac{1}{4 D t}$$

### Step 5 – Substitute into the PDE
The governing ODE becomes:

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $4Dt$:

$$-2\eta \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

### Step 6 – Integrate Twice
Let $p = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$. Then $\dfrac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} = \dfrac{dp}{d\eta}$. The ODE becomes:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta \, p = 0$$

This is a first-order linear ODE with integrating factor $e^{\eta^2}$:

$$\frac{d}{d\eta}\!\left(p \, e^{\eta^2}\right) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p \, e^{\eta^2} = A_1 \;\Rightarrow\; p = A_1 e^{-\eta^2}$$

Integrating again:

$$C(\eta) = A_1 \int e^{-\eta^2} d\eta + A_2 = A_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\text{erf}(\eta) + A_2$$

### Step 7 – Apply Boundary Conditions
At $\eta = 0$ (i.e., $x = 0$ or $t \to \infty$ with finite $x$): $\text{erf}(0) = 0$, hence $A_2 = C_s$.
As $\eta \to \infty$: $\text{erf}(\infty) = 1$, hence $C_i = A_1 \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + C_s$, giving:

$$A_1 = \frac{2(C_i - C_s)}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

### Step 8 – Final Solution
Substituting $A_1$ and $A_2$ and reverting $\eta \to \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$:

$$\boxed{\,C(x, t) = C_s - (C_s - C_i)\, \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,}$$

### Step 9 – Inversion: Time to Corrosion Initiation $T_{corr}$
The mechanism of corrosion begins when the chloride concentration at the steel surface ($x = c$, the cover) reaches the **critical chloride threshold** $C_{cr}$. Setting $C(c, T_{corr}) = C_{cr}$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{C_{cr} - C_s}{C_i - C_s} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{c}{2\sqrt{D\,T_{corr}}}\right)$$

Taking the inverse error function on both sides and isolating $T_{corr}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{corr} = \frac{c^2}{4D} \left[\text{erf}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i}\right)\right]^{-2}\,}$$

This is the **Tuutti equation** (Tuutti, 1982) and is the cornerstone of performance-based service-life design in KTU 2024.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A coastal pile in Kochi has cover $c = 50$ mm, surface chloride $C_s = 4.5$ % by mass of cement, initial chloride $C_i = 0.05$ %, critical chloride $C_{cr} = 0.6$ %, and apparent diffusion coefficient $D = 2.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. Predict the time to corrosion initiation.

**Solution:**

Step 1 – Compute the inverse argument:

$$\frac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i} = \frac{4.5 - 0.6}{4.5 - 0.05} = \frac{3.9}{4.45} = 0.8764$$

Step 2 – Look up or interpolate:

$$\text{erf}^{-1}(0.8764) \approx 1.1436$$

Step 3 – Square the cover in metres:

$$c^2 = (0.050)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

Step 4 – Substitute into Tuutti's equation:

$$T_{corr} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-12}} \times \frac{1}{(1.1436)^2}$$

$$T_{corr} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-11}} \times \frac{1}{1.3078} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7647$$

$$\boxed{T_{corr} \approx 1.91 \times 10^{8} \;\text{s} \approx 6.06 \;\text{years}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert cover from **mm to m** when using $D$ in m²/s, leading to answers off by a factor of $10^6$. Always check units first.

---
