# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *Alkali-Silica Reaction (ASR)*. List two diagnostic symptoms of ASR in a concrete structure.

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Alkali-Silica Reaction is a chemical deterioration mechanism in which reactive silica minerals present in aggregates react with alkali hydroxides (Na⁺, K⁺) from cement pore solution to form an expansive **alkali-silica gel**. This gel imbibes water, swells, and generates internal pressures that exceed the tensile capacity of concrete, causing cracking.
- **Two Diagnostic Symptoms:**
  1. **Map (pattern) cracking** on the concrete surface, often with white or dark gel exudations at the cracks.
  2. **Discoloration (dark reaction rims)** around affected aggregate particles and **pop-outs**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is the *critical chloride threshold* for corrosion initiation in reinforced concrete? State the typical range.

**Model Answer:**
The **critical chloride threshold** ($C_{cr}$) is the minimum concentration of chloride ions (expressed as % by mass of cement or as free Cl⁻ in pore solution) at the steel-concrete interface at which the passive $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ film on the reinforcement becomes unstable and pitting corrosion initiates.

- **Typical range:**
  - **0.4 % to 1.0 %** by mass of cement (total chloride).
  - For free chloride in pore solution: **0.15 % to 0.35 %** by mass of cement.
- The value depends on $C_3A$ content, pH, and supplementary cementitious materials.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanism of chloride-induced corrosion** of steel reinforcement in concrete. With a neat sketch, describe the electrochemical cell and the role of passivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reinforced concrete beam in a coastal environment has the following data: cover $c = 60$ mm, surface chloride $C_s = 5.0$ %, initial chloride $C_i = 0.10$ %, critical chloride $C_{cr} = 0.7$ %, diffusion coefficient $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. Calculate the **time to corrosion initiation** using Tuutti's model.

#### Model Solution – Q.A(a)

1. **Passivation Stage:** In fresh concrete, the high alkalinity ($pH \approx 12.5 - 13.5$) of the pore solution causes the formation of a thin, tightly adherent, invisible **passive ferric oxide film** ($\gamma$-Fe₂O₃) on the steel surface. This film is **thermodynamically stable** only above $pH \approx 9.5$ (the Pourbaix passivation zone for iron). In this state, the corrosion rate is negligible ($i_{corr} < 0.1$ μA/cm²).

2. **Depassivation by Chlorides:** Chloride ions from seawater, de-icing salts, or marine aerosols diffuse through the porous cover. Above a critical local concentration $C_{cr}$, chlorides penetrate the passive film locally, creating **pits** (anodic sites). The mechanism of breakdown is **adsorption-induced film displacement** and **point-defect creation** in the oxide lattice.

3. **Electrochemical Cell Operation:** Once depassivated, a macro-cell forms:
   - **Anode (pit):** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ (oxidation).
   - **Cathode (adjacent passive area):** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ (reduction).
   - The pore solution acts as the **electrolyte**, completing the circuit.

4. **Corrosion Product Formation:** $Fe^{2+}$ migrates and reacts with $OH^-$ and $O_2$ to form hydrated iron oxides (rust):

$$Fe^{2+} + 2OH^- \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 \quad \rightarrow \quad FeOOH \,(\text{goethite}) \quad \rightarrow \quad Fe(OH)_3$$

5. **Mechanical Consequence:** The molar volume of rust is **2 to 6 times** that of the parent steel, generating expansive tensile stresses (~15–40 MPa) that crack the cover in two stages:
   - **Stage 1:** Internal cracking at the steel-paste interface.
   - **Stage 2:** Visible **longitudinal cracks** parallel to the bar, followed by **spalling**.

6. **Sketch (refer to Block Diagram in Section 4.2).**

#### Model Solution – Q.A(b)

Step 1 – Inverse argument:

$$\frac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i} = \frac{5.0 - 0.7}{5.0 - 0.10} = \frac{4.3}{4.9} = 0.8776$$

Step 2 – Inverse error function (interpolation):

$$\text{erf}^{-1}(0.8776) \approx 1.146$$

Step 3 – Cover squared:

$$c^2 = (0.060)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

Step 4 – Apply Tuutti's equation:

$$T_{corr} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}} \times \frac{1}{(1.146)^2} = 6.0 \times 10^{8} \times 0.7615$$

$$T_{corr} \approx 4.57 \times 10^{8} \;\text{s} \approx 14.49 \;\text{years}$$

$$\boxed{T_{corr} \approx 14.5 \;\text{years}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Unit Conversion:** The most common error is using $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s directly with $c$ in mm. This inflates the answer by $10^6$. Always **convert cover to metres** and keep $D$ in m²/s for SI consistency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of sulfate attack** on concrete. Discuss the role of ettringite and gypsum formation, and explain how the type of cement influences sulfate resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **carbonation-induced** and **chloride-induced** corrosion mechanisms. Tabulate any four distinguishing parameters.

#### Model Solution – Q.B(a)

1. **Source of Sulfates:** Sulfate ions ($SO_4^{2-}$) originate from:
   - **External sources:** Soil sulfate, groundwater sulfate, seawater (in tidal/splash zones), industrial effluents, and fertilizers.
   - **Internal sources:** Excess gypsum in cement or sulfate-contaminated aggregates.

2. **Chemical Mechanism:** The reaction proceeds in two parallel pathways:

   - **Ettringite Formation (delayed ettringite formation – DEF or classical):**
     $$C_3A + 3\,C\bar{S}H_2 + 26\,H \longrightarrow C_6A\bar{S}_3H_{32}$$
     Tricalcium aluminate + gypsum + water → ettringite (trisulfate hydrate).
   - **Gypsum Formation:**
     $$Ca(OH)_2 + SO_4^{2-} + 2H_2O \longrightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$$
     Portlandite + sulfate + water → gypsum (calcium sulfate dihydrate).

3. **Expansive Pressure Generation:**
   - Solid molar volume of ettringite = **735 cm³/mol** (from crystallographic data).
   - Solid molar volume of $C_3A$ + gypsum + water (reactants) ≈ **309 cm³/mol**.
   - **Net volumetric expansion ≈ 138 %**, generating crystallization pressure up to **110 MPa** in confined pores — well above the tensile strength of concrete (3–5 MPa).

4. **Cement Type Influence on Sulfate Resistance (per IS 456:2000 & ASTM C150):**

   | Cement Type | Max $C_3A$ % | Sulfate Resistance |
   |:-----------:|:------------:|:-------------------|
   | Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) | 9 – 12 | Low |
   | Moderate Sulfate Resisting | 5 – 8 | Moderate |
   | Sulfate-Resisting Cement (SRC) | ≤ 5 | High |
   | Supersulfated Cement | 0 | Very High |
   | Blended cements (with ≥ 30 % GGBS / fly ash) | Reduced effective $C_3A$ | Significantly improved |

5. **Role of SCMs (Supplementary Cementitious Materials):**
   - **Fly ash (Class F):** Pozzolanic reaction consumes CH → less gypsum formation.
   - **GGBS:** Dilutes $C_3A$, refines pore structure → lower permeability → slower ingress.

#### Model Solution – Q.B(b)

| S.No | Parameter | Carbonation-Induced Corrosion | Chloride-Induced Corrosion |
|:----:|:----------|:------------------------------|:----------------------------|
| 1 | **pH at steel surface** | Drops below 9.5 (uniform) | Remains alkaline (pH > 11) but Cl⁻ disrupts passive film |
| 2 | **Corrosion Type** | **General (uniform)** over the bar length | **Pitting (localized)** at discrete anodic sites |
| 3 | **Anode-Cathode Area Ratio** | $A_c/A_a \to 1$ (micro-cell) | $A_c/A_a \gg 1$ (macro-cell) → high corrosion current |
| 4 | **Cracking Pattern** | Longitudinal, often accompanied by spalling of large areas | Localized spalling, "ring" or "patch" corrosion |
| 5 | **Rate of Cover Damage** | Slow, predictable via $X_c = k\sqrt{t}$ | Often sudden, accelerates rapidly post-initiation |
| 6 | **Key Trigger** | Atmospheric $CO_2$ ingress; reduces $Ca(OH)_2$ | Free Cl⁻ exceeding $C_{cr}$; breaks passive film |
| 7 | **Diagnostic Test** | Phenolphthalein spray (pink = alkaline, colourless = carbonated) | Chloride profile (acid-soluble & water-soluble) at various depths |
| 8 | **Mitigation** | Increased cover, low $w/c$, anti-carbonation coatings | Epoxy-coated rebar, CP, low $C_3A$ cement, corrosion inhibitors |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Mechanism Confusion:** Many students state *"carbonation causes chloride attack"* or vice versa. Carbonation **lowers pH uniformly**, while chlorides **destroy passivity locally without lowering pH**. This distinction is the **single most-marked** concept in KTU valuation for this module.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Mechanism)**

- [ ] Always identify the **mechanism** *before* prescribing repair — symptom ≠ cause.
- [ ] **Four broad classes:** Physical, Chemical, Electrochemical, Biological.
- [ ] **Sulfate attack → Ettringite (C₆AŜ₃H₃₂) & Gypsum** → ~138 % volumetric expansion.
- [ ] **ASR → Alkali-silica gel** → map-pattern cracking, gel exudation.
- [ ] **Carbonation → pH < 9.5** → uniform corrosion; use **phenolphthalein** for diagnosis.
- [ ] **Chloride attack → pitting corrosion**; **Tuutti's equation** is the KTU 2024 backbone.
- [ ] **Tuutti Model:** $T_{corr} = \dfrac{c^2}{4D}\left[\text{erf}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_s - C_{cr}}{C_s - C_i}\right)\right]^{-2}$
- [ ] **Carbonation depth (simplified):** $X_c = k \sqrt{t}$ — verify with $X_c = c \cdot \sqrt{t/t_0}$ form.
- [ ] **Rust expansion = 2 to 6 ×** steel volume → cover cracking and spalling.
- [ ] **Sulfate resistance:** Low $C_3A$ cement (≤ 5 %) — IS 12330 (SRC) — and SCMs (GGBS, fly ash).
- [ ] **Critical chloride threshold:** 0.4 – 1.0 % by mass of cement.
- [ ] **Powers' hydraulic pressure** governs freeze-thaw scaling — air entrainment is the cure.
- [ ] **Cover ≥ 30 mm** (IS 456) is the **first line of defense** against both carbonation and chloride attack.
- [ ] **Real-world deterioration is synergistic** — design repair for *combined* exposure.
- [ ] **Differential diagnosis tools:** Phenolphthalein (pH), $\text{AgNO}_3$ spray (Cl⁻), SEM/petrography (ASR), sulfate titration.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific mechanisms:** Coastal chloride attack (Kochi/Kozhikode), lateritic sulfate soils, tropical humidity-accelerated carbonation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Deterioration Mechanisms – "SAFE-CRAB":**
> - **S** → Sulfate attack
> - **A** → ASR (Alkali-Silica Reaction)
> - **F** → Freeze-thaw
> - **E** → Ettringite expansion
> - **C** → Carbonation
> - **R** → Reinforcement corrosion (chloride-induced)
> - **A** → Acid attack
> - **B** → Biological / Bacterial

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stages of Corrosion Damage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stages of Corrosion Damage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosion Damage** in reinforced concrete structures refers to the progressive electrochemical degradation of embedded steel reinforcement caused by the breakdown of its passive protective layer, leading to a sequence of physical, chemical, and mechanical deterioration events that compromise the durability, serviceability, and ultimate load-carrying capacity of the structural element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The **Stages of Corrosion Damage** describe the chronological sequence through which embedded reinforcing steel transitions from a passive (protected) state to active deterioration, ultimately resulting in cracking, spalling, delamination, and structural failure. The framework classifies damage into **four principal stages**: *Initiation*, *Early Propagation*, *Advanced Propagation*, and *Terminal/Deterioration*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rust Cancer" of Concrete

Imagine a healthy reinforced concrete beam as a human body with an intact immune system. The **alkaline concrete cover** (pH ≈ 12.5 to 13.5) acts like an "immune shield" that protects the steel "organs" (rebar) from the "disease" of corrosion. This is called **passivation** — a thin, stable, invisible oxide layer ($\gamma$-Fe₂O₃) that forms naturally on steel in highly alkaline environments.

Now, two aggressive "pathogens" breach this shield:
1. **Chloride ions (Cl⁻)** from marine environments or de-icing salts — they act like a virus that punches holes in the shield.
2. **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** from the atmosphere — it acts like acid rain that gradually neutralizes the shield's alkalinity (a process called **carbonation**).

Once the shield breaks, **iron atoms in the steel lose electrons** (oxidation → anodic reaction), and these electrons travel to cathodic regions where they combine with oxygen and water to form hydroxide ions. The result: **rust (Fe(OH)₂ / Fe₂O₃·H₂O)** that occupies **2 to 6 times the original volume** of the parent steel. This expansion generates internal bursting pressures (≈ 15 to 40 MPa), which crack, delaminate, and ultimately destroy the surrounding concrete — exactly like a tumor breaking through healthy tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Corrosion damage is **not** a single event but a **multi-stage progressive disease**. Recognizing the stage is critical because repair strategies differ dramatically: early-stage intervention is *preventive and economic*; late-stage intervention is *reactive and expensive* (often 5–10× the cost of prevention).

## 1.3 Standardized Performance Metrics (ASTMC876 & IS 516 Part 5)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Corrosion current density | $i_{corr}$ | **µA/cm²** |
| Half-cell potential | $E_{corr}$ | **mV (vs Cu/CuSO₄)** |
| Concrete electrical resistivity | $\rho$ | **kΩ·cm** |
| Chloride threshold | $[Cl^-]$ | **% by mass of cement** |
| Carbonation depth | $d_c$ | **mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Stages — A Structured Breakdown

### **STAGE I: INITIATION (Depassivation Phase)**

This is the "silent incubation" period. The reinforcement is still structurally sound, but the protective environment has been compromised.

**Mechanism:**
- **Pathway A — Chloride-induced depassivation:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate via capillary suction, diffusion, and permeation. Once the chloride concentration at the steel surface exceeds the **critical chloride threshold** (typically **0.4% to 0.5% by mass of cement** for ordinary Portland cement, lower for blended cements), the passive film locally breaks down → *pitting corrosion* initiates.
- **Pathway B — Carbonation-induced depassivation:** Atmospheric CO₂ reacts with Ca(OH)₂ in concrete to form CaCO₃. The pH drops from ≈ 12.5 to below **9 to 10**, at which point the passive film becomes thermodynamically unstable and dissolves.

**Time Duration:** Months to decades (often 5 to 25 years), depending on cover depth, concrete quality, and exposure class.

**Visual Signs:** **None visible on the surface.** This is the most dangerous stage because deterioration is invisible.

**Detection Methods (Non-Destructive):**
- Half-cell potential mapping (ASTM C876)
- Chloride profiling (laboratory analysis of dust samples)
- Phenolphthalein spray test for carbonation depth
- Concrete resistivity measurement (Wenner 4-probe)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "When does corrosion start?", the technical answer is: *"When the chloride concentration at the rebar depth exceeds the critical threshold, OR when the carbonation front reaches the rebar surface and pH drops below ≈ 9.5."* Do not confuse initiation with propagation.

---

### **STAGE II: EARLY PROPAGATION (Rust Formation Phase)**

Now corrosion is active but not yet structurally damaging. Iron begins converting to expansive corrosion products.

**Electrochemistry (simplified):**

At the **anode** (corroding site):
$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$$

At the **cathode** (passive site, oxygen-rich):
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

In the presence of oxygen and water, the ferrous ions oxidize further:
$$4Fe^{2+} + O_2 + (4 + 2n)H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot nH_2O + 8H^+$$

The final rust product (hydrated ferric oxide) occupies **2× to 6× the original volume** of the parent steel.

**Visual Signs:** *Staining* (brown/orange rust streaks) on the concrete surface. Hairline cracks may begin to appear directly above the rebar.

**Damage Quantification:**
- $i_{corr} \approx$ 0.1 to 0.5 µA/cm² (low to moderate corrosion rate)
- Cross-section loss: ≈ 1% to 5%
- Cover cracking: not yet initiated, OR very fine (width < 0.1 mm)

**Repair Strategy:** *Preventive* — surface sealers, corrosion inhibitors, re-alkalization, cathodic prevention (impressed current or galvanic anodes).

---

### **STAGE III: ADVANCED PROPAGATION (Cracking and Spalling Phase)**

This is when damage becomes **visually obvious** to the naked eye and is the most common reason property owners call for repair.

**Mechanism:** Rust expansion generates internal tensile stresses in the cover concrete. When these stresses exceed the tensile strength of concrete (≈ 3 to 5 MPa), cracks form. The classic crack pattern is **longitudinal cracking** parallel to the rebar (because the bursting stress is radial).

**Cascading Sequence:**
1. Internal micro-cracking at the steel-concrete interface
2. Formation of **longitudinal cracks** along the rebar
3. Delamination (horizontal separation of cover concrete from the core)
4. **Spalling** (cover concrete detaches and falls off)
5. Exposure of corroded reinforcement

**Visual Signs (High-Confidence Indicators):**
- Longitudinal cracks aligned with rebar position
- Rust staining on crack faces
- Hollow-sounding concrete when tapped (delamination)
- Spalled areas with visible, sectionally-reduced rebar
- Map cracking in extreme cases

**Damage Quantification:**
- $i_{corr} \approx$ 0.5 to 1.0 µA/cm² (moderate to high)
- Cross-section loss: ≈ 5% to 25%
- Crack width: 0.2 mm to 3 mm

**Repair Strategy:** *Localized* — break out delaminated concrete, clean/repair rebar, apply patch repair mortars, optionally install galvanic anodes for future protection.

---

### **STAGE IV: TERMINAL / DETERIORATION (Structural Compromise)**

The structure is no longer merely "deteriorated" — its **load-bearing capacity is actively compromised**.

**Mechanism:** Severe cross-sectional loss of rebar, loss of steel-concrete bond, pitting corrosion leading to stress concentrations, and in extreme cases, necking/fracture of individual bars.

**Visual Signs:**
- Severely exposed, necked, or fractured rebar
- Wide cracks (> 3 mm) with active efflorescence
- Dislocated/loose concrete fragments
- Visible deflection, sag, or deformation of structural elements
- Buckling of compression reinforcement

**Damage Quantification:**
- $i_{corr} >$ **1.0 µA/cm²** (very high)
- Cross-section loss: > 25% (often 40% to 80%)
- Bond strength reduction: > 50%
- Loss of structural capacity: potentially > 30%

**Repair Strategy:** *Major / Structural* — full-depth concrete replacement, rebar splicing/supplementing, external post-tensioning, fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) strengthening, section enlargement, or in extreme cases, demolition and reconstruction.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning / Use | Units / Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{meas}}{A_{steel}}$ | Corrosion current density (Faraday-derived from linear polarization) | µA/cm² ; passive < 0.1, active > 0.5 |
| $P = 0.005 \times \dfrac{\Delta V}{r}$ | Pitting intensity factor (ratio of deepest pit to average penetration) | dimensionless ; > 4 indicates pitting-dominated corrosion |
| $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | Concrete electrical resistivity (Wenner 4-probe) | kΩ·cm ; > 20 low risk, < 5 high risk |
| $X_c = k \sqrt{t}$ | Carbonation depth (Fick's 1st law analog) | mm ; $k$ = 0.25 to 1.0 mm/√year, $t$ = years |
| $C(x,t) = C_s \left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$ | Chloride concentration profile (Fick's 2nd law, semi-infinite medium) | % by mass of cement ; $D$ = effective diffusion coefficient (≈ $10^{-12}$ m²/s) |
| $\dfrac{\Delta D}{D_0} = 2k_v$ | Bar diameter loss: $\Delta D = D_0 - D(t)$ over time $t$ | mm/year ; $k_v$ = corrosion penetration rate |
| $\sigma_{burst} = \dfrac{E_{rust}}{r} \cdot \dfrac{(V_r - 1)}{(1 + \nu)}$ | Internal bursting stress (Porter's model) | MPa ; 15 to 40 MPa typical peak |
| $i_{corr} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot \Delta m}{M \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Faraday's law linking mass loss to current | g (mass loss) ; $n$ = valence, $F$ = 96485 C/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** When applying Faraday's law, **n = 2** for Fe → Fe²⁺ and **n = 3** for Fe → Fe³⁺. The atomic mass **M = 55.85 g/mol** for iron. Faraday's constant **F = 96485 C/mol**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Asset Management:** Transportation authorities (NHAI, MORTH) use stage classification to prioritize maintenance budgets. Stage I/II structures receive preventive treatments; Stage III/IV require major capital expenditure.
- **Forensic Engineering:** Insurance claims and litigation rely on stage assessment to determine whether damage was *foreseeable*, *design-related*, or *maintenance-related*.
- **Repair Specification:** IS 1834, ACI 546R, and EN 1504 map specific repair materials and methods to each damage stage.
- **Service Life Prediction:** Models such as DuraCrete, Life-365, and fib Bulletin 34 use the initiation-propagation framework (Tuutti's model) to predict remaining useful life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Mass Loss from Corrosion Current

**Problem:** A reinforced concrete beam is under accelerated corrosion testing. A constant anodic current of $I = 50$ mA is applied for $t = 720$ hours. Compute the mass of steel lost and the equivalent penetration depth, assuming a uniform exposed steel surface area $A = 100$ cm².

**Step 1: Convert time to seconds (SI units).**

$$t = 720 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Compute total charge passed (Q = I × t).**

$$Q = I \times t = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \times 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 129{,}600 \text{ C}}$$

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.**

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where $M = 55.85$ g/mol (atomic mass of iron), $n = 2$ (Fe → Fe²⁺), and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{129{,}600 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{7{,}238{,}160}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta m \approx 37.51 \text{ g}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the average corrosion penetration depth ($x_p$).**

The volumetric loss is:
$$V_{loss} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_{Fe}} = \dfrac{37.51}{7.87} \approx 4.77 \text{ cm}^3$$

The average uniform penetration (assuming uniform corrosion over area $A$) is:
$$x_p = \dfrac{V_{loss}}{A} = \dfrac{4.77}{100}$$

$$\boxed{x_p \approx 0.0477 \text{ cm} = 0.477 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5: Corrosion current density check (for stage classification).**

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{50 \text{ mA}}{100 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.5 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 500 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is **two orders of magnitude higher** than typical in-service corrosion rates (which range from 0.1 to 10 µA/cm²). Such elevated $i_{corr}$ values are typical of *accelerated laboratory testing* (e.g., impressed current technique per ASTM G109) used to evaluate repair systems in compressed timeframes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time to Cover Cracking (Morinaga's Model)

**Problem:** Estimate the time $t_{crack}$ for a 16 mm diameter rebar with 30 mm cover to cause visible longitudinal cracking, given a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 1.0$ µA/cm².

**Step 1: Compute the critical mass of rust required to crack the cover.**

Empirical correlations (e.g., from Japanese research, Andisheh et al.) suggest that the critical penetration $x_{crit}$ is:

$$x_{crit} \approx 0.0114 \times (c/\phi) + 0.0024 \quad [\text{cm}]$$

where $c$ = cover (cm), $\phi$ = bar diameter (cm).

With $c = 3.0$ cm and $\phi = 1.6$ cm:

$$x_{crit} = 0.0114 \times \dfrac{3.0}{1.6} + 0.0024 = 0.0214 + 0.0024 = 0.0238 \text{ cm} = 0.238 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Convert $i_{corr}$ to penetration rate.**

Using Faraday's law at the bar surface:

$$\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Fe}}$$

$$= \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2)(55.85 \text{ g/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})(7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3)}$$

$$= \dfrac{55.85 \times 10^{-6}}{1{,}518{,}754} \text{ cm/s}$$

$$\approx 3.677 \times 10^{-11} \text{ cm/s}$$

Converting to mm/year:

$$\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = 3.677 \times 10^{-11} \times 10 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \approx 0.0116 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute time to cracking.**

$$t_{crack} = \dfrac{x_{crit}}{dx_p/dt} = \dfrac{0.238 \text{ mm}}{0.0116 \text{ mm/year}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{crack} \approx 20.5 \text{ years}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At a sustained corrosion rate of 1.0 µA/cm², it takes roughly **20 years** to initiate visible cover cracking — consistent with field observations of structures reaching Stage III after 20–30 years of service in aggressive environments.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Stage Identification in the Field

| Observation | Likely Stage | Confidence | Action Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| No visible defects; $E_{corr} < -200$ mV; chloride < threshold | **I — Initiation** | High | Monitor annually |
| Faint rust staining; no cracks; $i_{corr} = 0.1$–0.5 µA/cm² | **II — Early Propagation** | Moderate | Apply surface protection within 2 years |
| Longitudinal cracks 0.2–1.0 mm; rust staining | **III — Advanced Propagation** | High | Localized patch repair within 6 months |
| Spalling, exposed rebar, delamination | **III–IV** | Very High | Urgent repair; structural assessment |
| Severely necked/broken bars, deflection, wide cracks | **IV — Terminal** | Certain | Emergency shoring; major rehabilitation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stage Progression and Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Life Begins] --> B[Stage I: Initiation]
    B --> B1[Cl ingress or CO2 penetration]
    B --> B2[Depassivation of rebar]
    B2 --> C{Threshold exceeded?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Stage II: Early Propagation]
    D --> D1[Active Fe2+ dissolution]
    D --> D2[Rust volume expansion 2x to 6x]
    D2 --> E{Internal stress > ft concrete?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Stage III: Advanced Propagation]
    F --> F1[Longitudinal cracks form]
    F --> F2[Delamination and spalling]
    F2 --> G{Severity assessment}
    G -- Moderate --> G1[Localized repair feasible]
    G -- Severe --> H[Stage IV: Terminal Deterioration]
    H --> H1[Major rebar cross-section loss]
    H --> H2[Bond failure and capacity loss]
    H2 --> I{Rehab vs Demolish?}
    I -- Rehab --> I1[FRP strengthening / section enlargement]
    I -- Demolish --> I2[Full reconstruction]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electrochemical Corrosion Cell in Concrete

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Env [External Environment]
        Clq[Chloride Ions]
        CO2q[Carbon Dioxide]
        O2q[Oxygen and Water]
    end

    subgraph Cover [Concrete Cover Zone]
        Cdiff[Diffusion Front]
        Carb[Carbonation Front]
    end

    subgraph Steel [Embedded Reinforcement]
        Anode[Anodic Site: Fe to Fe2+ + 2e-]
        Cathode[Cathodic Site: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-]
        Pass[Passive Film: gamma-Fe2O3]
    end

    subgraph Products [Deterioration Products]
        Rust[Fe2O3 · nH2O Rust, 2x to 6x volume]
        Crack[Longitudinal Cracks and Spalling]
    end

    Clq --> Cdiff
    CO2q --> Carb
    O2q --> Cathode
    Cdiff --> Pass
    Carb --> Pass
    Pass -.Breaks down.-> Anode
    Anode --> Rust
    Cathode --> Anode
    Rust --> Crack
```

    dateFormat  YYYY

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: List the four stages of corrosion damage in reinforced concrete structures and indicate one distinguishing visual feature of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Name | Distinguishing Visual Feature |
|---|---|---|
| I | Initiation | No visible surface defects; detectable only by half-cell potential or chloride testing |
| II | Early Propagation | Rust staining (brown/orange streaks) on concrete surface |
| III | Advanced Propagation | Longitudinal cracks parallel to rebar, delamination, spalling |
| IV | Terminal Deterioration | Severely exposed, necked, or fractured rebar; visible deflection of member |

> [!NOTE]
> The examiner's marking scheme allocates **1 mark for correct stage names**, **1 mark for visual features**, and **1 mark for clarity and order**.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Define the "critical chloride threshold" and state its typical value for ordinary Portland cement concrete.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical chloride threshold** is the minimum concentration of chloride ions at the depth of the reinforcing steel that is sufficient to break down the passive oxide layer ($\gamma$-Fe₂O₃) and initiate active pitting corrosion.

For ordinary Portland cement (OPC) concrete, the commonly accepted value is:

$$[Cl^-]_{crit} \approx 0.4\% \text{ to } 0.5\% \text{ by mass of cement}$$

(equivalent to approximately **0.05% to 0.07% by mass of concrete** for typical mix designs).

> [!NOTE]
> For blended cements (e.g., fly ash, GGBS, silica fume), the threshold is **lower (0.2% to 0.35%)** because the refined pore structure binds chlorides differently.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Tuutti two-stage service life model** for corrosion of reinforcement, with neat sketches of the corrosion rate versus time curve. Discuss the dominant deterioration mechanism in each stage.

**Q: (b) [7 Marks]** A reinforced concrete column exposed to a marine splash zone is observed to have longitudinal cracks of width 1.2 mm, rust staining, and minor spalling. The half-cell potential measured was $E_{corr} = -480$ mV (vs Cu/CuSO₄). Using the data below, classify the corrosion damage stage and recommend an appropriate repair strategy.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Concrete cover | 50 mm |
| Bar diameter | 20 mm |
| Measured $i_{corr}$ | 0.85 µA/cm² |
| Carbonation depth | 8 mm |
| Chloride at rebar depth | 0.62% by mass of cement |

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Tuutti's Model (1982)** divides the service life of a corroding RC structure into two principal stages:

**Stage 1 — Initiation Period ($t_i$):** The time during which aggressive agents (Cl⁻ or CO₂) penetrate the concrete cover and reach the threshold concentration at the rebar depth. During this period, the reinforcement remains passive and the corrosion rate is essentially **zero** (or negligibly small).

**Stage 2 — Propagation Period ($t_p$):** The time from the onset of active corrosion until an unacceptable limit state is reached (e.g., cover cracking, spalling, or loss of bond). The corrosion rate in this stage is **non-zero and often increases** with time as the cover cracks and aggressive agents gain easier access.

**The Corrosion Rate vs Time Curve:**

```
icorr
  ^
  |                       ___________
  |                  ____/
  |              ___/
  |          ___/        Stage 2 (Propagation)
  |     ____/
  |____/   Stage 1 (Initiation)
  |__________________________> time (years)
  0      t_i
```

**Deterioration Mechanisms:**

- **Stage 1 (Initiation):** Diffusion-controlled transport of Cl⁻ (Fick's 2nd law) or progressive neutralization by CO₂. No visible damage.
- **Stage 2 (Propagation):** Electrochemical dissolution of iron → expansive rust → internal pressure → cracking, spalling, and loss of cross-section. Corrosion rate generally rises over time.

**Design Service Life:**
$$t_{design} = t_i + t_p$$

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Stage classification based on visual evidence.**

The observed features (longitudinal cracks 1.2 mm wide, rust staining, minor spalling) correspond to **Stage III — Advanced Propagation** per the KTU 2024 damage classification.

**Step 2: Stage verification by electrochemical parameters.**

- $E_{corr} = -480$ mV (vs Cu/CuSO₄): According to **ASTM C876**, this indicates a **> 90% probability of active corrosion**.
- $i_{corr} = 0.85$ µA/cm²: This is in the **moderate-to-high corrosion range** (0.5 to 1.0 µA/cm²).
- Chloride content = 0.62% by mass of cement: This **exceeds the critical threshold** of 0.4% to 0.5%, confirming depassivation.

**Step 3: Cross-check with carbonation data.**

Carbonation depth = 8 mm < cover = 50 mm, so carbonation has **not** yet reached the rebar. The dominant deterioration mechanism is therefore **chloride-induced pitting corrosion**, not carbonation.

**Step 4: Recommended repair strategy.**

Given Stage III damage, the appropriate intervention is a **localized patch repair system**, specifically:

1. **Breaking out** all delaminated and spalled concrete to expose sound substrate (minimum 25 mm behind the outermost layer of rebar).
2. **Cleaning corroded rebar** to Sa 2½ (ISO 8501-1) standard, OR replacing rebar where cross-section loss exceeds 20%.
3. **Applying a zinc-rich primer** to the cleaned rebar for sacrificial protection.
4. **Installing discrete galvanic anodes** (e.g., "point anodes") in the patch area to mitigate the incipient anode / cathode effect at the patch boundary.
5. **Patching with a polymer-modified cementitious mortar** compatible with the parent concrete (e.g., EN 1504 Class R3 or R4).
6. **Applying a silane/siloxane hydrophobic surface treatment** over the entire element to slow future chloride ingress.
7. **Future monitoring:** repeat half-cell potential mapping annually.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Many students write "Stage II" because they see only "minor" spalling — but the **crack width > 1 mm combined with active rust staining and $i_{corr} > 0.5$** is a textbook Stage III indicator. Always cross-check visual evidence with quantitative parameters.
> 2. Students often recommend only "patch repair" without addressing the **incipient anode effect** (the corrosion cell that re-establishes at the patch boundary). Examiners specifically look for **anti-corrosion measures** beyond mere concrete replacement.
> 3. Do not confuse chloride-induced and carbonation-induced corrosion in the recommendation. They have **different repair priorities** — chloride cases need *sealing/anodes*; carbonation cases need *re-alkalization*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of chloride-induced corrosion** of steel in concrete. Explain why the passive film breaks down locally (pitting) rather than uniformly.

**Q: (b) [7 Marks]** A pre-stressed concrete bridge girder shows 30% cross-section loss in the bottom reinforcement after 22 years of service in a coastal environment. Discuss the stage of damage, structural safety implications, and suitable rehabilitation techniques.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Mechanism of Chloride-Induced Corrosion:**

1. **Penetration Phase:** Chloride ions enter concrete through capillary absorption (wet-dry cycling), diffusion (concentration gradient), and permeation (pressure-driven). Penetration follows Fick's 2nd law: $C(x,t) = C_s \left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$

2. **Depassivation:** Once $[Cl^-]$ at the rebar exceeds the critical threshold (≈ 0.4% to 0.5% by mass of cement), chloride ions locally disrupt the passive $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ film. Chloride acts as a *catalyst* — it does not consume the iron but lowers the activation energy for dissolution.

3. **Pitting Initiation:** Breakdown is **local** rather than uniform because:
   - **Micro-structural heterogeneity:** Variations in the steel surface (inclusions, mill scale, micro-cracks) and in the concrete pore solution (pH gradients, oxygen gradients) make some sites more vulnerable.
   - **Localized acidification:** At the pit bottom, the hydrolysis reaction $Fe^{2+} + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 + 2H^+$ produces $H^+$ ions that lower local pH, preventing repassivation.
   - **Oxygen differential cell:** Pits form where Cl⁻ is high and oxygen is low (anode); surrounding passive areas become cathodes where oxygen is high. This galvanic pair drives accelerated localized attack.

4. **Pit Growth:** The anodic dissolution $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ is autocatalytic. Pitting penetration rates can reach **0.3 to 1.0 mm/year** — far higher than uniform corrosion rates of 0.01 to 0.05 mm/year.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Stage Classification: Stage IV — Terminal Deterioration.**

A **30% cross-section loss** in pre-stressed reinforcement is **catastrophic**:
- Pre-stressed steel is highly sensitive to corrosion pitting (notch sensitivity → stress corrosion cracking and hydrogen embrittlement).
- A single fracture of a pre-stressing tendon can cause progressive collapse.
- The structure is **unsafe for continued service** without intervention.

**Structural Safety Implications:**
- Loss of pre-stressing force → reduced moment capacity → cracking and excessive deflection.
- Loss of bond between tendons and concrete.
- Potential for sudden, brittle failure without warning.
- IS 1343 and IRC 112 require immediate load restriction or shoring.

**Rehabilitation Techniques:**

1. **External Post-Tensioning:** Install additional external tendons to restore pre-stressing force. Most cost-effective for girder strengthening.
2. **FRP Wrapping:** Bond carbon or glass fiber composites to the soffit and sides to provide additional flexural and shear capacity.
3. **Section Enlargement:** Add reinforced concrete overlay to the bottom flange, properly anchored and bonded.
4. **Strand/Tendon Replacement:** If accessible, individual failed strands can be replaced and re-anchored (specialized repair).
5. **Load Transfer:** Prop the girder, transfer loads to adjacent members, then perform full-depth repair.
6. **Continuous Monitoring:** Install structural health monitoring (strain gauges, fiber-optic sensors) post-repair.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Many students apply generic "Stage III" labels without considering the **special vulnerability of pre-stressed steel** (notch sensitivity, stress corrosion cracking). Always mention that PSCC is **more dangerous** than RCC at equivalent cross-section loss.
> 2. The phrase "30% loss" must trigger a **structural safety statement**, not just a repair recommendation. Examiners want to see the student's ability to link damage stage to **load-carrying capacity**.
> 3. Do not recommend "patch repair" for a pre-stressed element with this level of damage. That is a Stage III solution, not a Stage IV solution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Corrosion is electrochemical**, not purely chemical — it requires an anode, cathode, electrolyte, and electrical connection.
- [ ] The **passive film** ($\gamma$-Fe₂O₃) protects steel in alkaline concrete (pH > 11.5). It breaks down at pH < 9.5 or when $[Cl^-] > 0.4$% by mass of cement.
- [ ] **Rust occupies 2× to 6× the volume of parent steel**, generating internal bursting pressures of **15 to 40 MPa**.
- [ ] **Four Stages:** Initiation → Early Propagation → Advanced Propagation → Terminal Deterioration.
- [ ] **ASTM C876 thresholds:** $E_{corr} < -350$ mV (Cu/CuSO₄) → > 90% probability of active corrosion.
- [ ] **$i_{corr}$ classification:** < 0.1 (passive), 0.1–0.5 (low), 0.5–1.0 (moderate), > 1.0 µA/cm² (high).
- [ ] **Resistivity classification:** > 20 kΩ·cm (low risk), 10–20 (moderate), 5–10 (high), < 5 kΩ·cm (very high).
- [ ] **Tuutti's model:** $t_{service} = t_{initiation} + t_{propagation}$.
- [ ] **Carbonation** follows $X_c = k\sqrt{t}$ — non-linear, slows with time.
- [ ] **Chloride ingress** follows Fick's 2nd law — error function profile.
- [ ] **Pitting corrosion** is more dangerous than uniform corrosion due to local penetration rates 10×–100× higher.
- [ ] **Pre-stressed steel** is **NOTCH-SENSITIVE** — pitting causes stress corrosion cracking and brittle failure.
- [ ] **Repair cost increases ~5–10×** from Stage I to Stage IV. Prevention is always cheaper.
- [ ] **Incipient anode effect** must be addressed at patch boundaries via galvanic anodes.
- [ ] **Faraday's law constants:** $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $n = 2$ (Fe²⁺).
- [ ] **Conversion:** 1 µA/cm² ≈ 0.0116 mm/year of uniform penetration.
- [ ] **Key standards:** IS 1834, IS 516 Part 5, ACI 546R, EN 1504, ASTM C876, ASTM G109, ISO 8501-1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Always classify damage in **three layers** — (1) visual/qualitative, (2) electrochemical/quantitative, and (3) structural implication. Examiners reward students who integrate these three perspectives rather than relying on a single line of evidence. Cite the relevant **IS code / ASTM standard** wherever possible to demonstrate syllabus alignment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair of Various Corrosion Damages of Structural Elements by Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Repair of Various Corrosion Damages of Structural Elements by Cathodic Protection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metallic structure to be protected is made the **cathode** of an intentionally engineered electrochemical cell. By supplying a controlled direct current (DC) — either from a more active sacrificial metal or from an external impressed-current source — the entire surface of the protected element is shifted into a region of thermodynamic immunity (or near-immunity) on the Pourbaix diagram, thereby arresting the anodic (oxidative) half-reaction that drives metal loss. The method is governed by international standards such as **EN 12954**, **NACE SP0169**, and **ISO 12696**, which prescribe the following protection criteria for steel in typical soil/water environments: - A polarized potential of **−850 mV** or more negative measured against a **Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE)** reference electrode (with IR drop considered). - A minimum **polarization shift of 100 mV** (decay criterion) measured with an instantaneous-off reading. - A **net protective current density** of **10 to 200 mA/m²** depending on the electrolyte aggressivity. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sacrificial Bodyguard" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy:** Imagine corrosion as a swarm of "rust bullets" fired randomly at a steel bridge. A **cathodic protection system** is like hiring a *bodyguard* (the anode) who deliberately steps in front of every bullet. The bodyguard absorbs all the hits (oxidises sacrificially) so that the principal (the structure) remains completely safe. In the **sacrificial version**, the bodyguard is expendable and dies slowly. In the **impressed-current version**, the bodyguard is constantly recharged from a small "pacemaker" (the DC rectifier). ### 1.3 Two Principal Variants of Cathodic Protection | Variant | Driving Force | Energy Source | Typical Anode Materials | Common Applications | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP)** | Galvanic potential difference (≈ **0.25 V to 0.75 V**) | Self-driven by metallurgical activity | Zinc, Magnesium, Aluminium-Zinc-Indium | Small isolated structures, ship hulls, short pipelines, rebar in concrete patch repairs | | **Impressed Current CP (ICCP)** | External DC rectifier (≈ **5 V to 50 V**) | Mains electricity, solar, or thermoelectric generator | High-Silicon Cast Iron, MMO/Ti, Graphite, Pb-Ag, Scrap Steel | Large tanks, long pipelines, bridge substructures, marine piles, reinforced concrete decks | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For reinforced concrete repair, **hybrid systems** combining patch repair, **galvanic discrete anodes (GDA)** of zinc embedded in repair mortar, and ICCP are the most frequently examined repair strategy in the syllabus. ### 1.4 Essential Electrochemical Vocabulary - **Anode:** The electrode where **oxidation** (electron release) occurs. In CP, this is the auxiliary metal that *sacrifices* itself. - **Cathode:** The electrode where **reduction** occurs. In CP, this is the **structure being protected**. - **Electrolyte:** The ionically conductive medium surrounding the structure (soil, seawater, concrete pore solution). - **Reference Electrode:** A stable, non-polarising electrode (CSE, Ag/AgCl, SCE, Zn) used to measure the structure-to-electrolyte potential. - **Polarisation:** The shift of electrode potential away from its open-circuit (corrosion) value due to applied current. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Electrochemical Basis of Cathodic Protection Corrosion of steel in an oxygenated neutral electrolyte proceeds as: $$\text{Anodic (oxidation):} \quad \mathrm{Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-}$$ $$\text{Cathodic (reduction):} \quad \mathrm{O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-}$$ $$\text{Overall:} \quad \mathrm{2Fe + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe(OH)_2}$$ The mixed (corrosion) potential **Ecorr** of steel in aerated soil is typically between **−0.45 V and −0.65 V vs CSE**. To achieve protection, we must force the steel potential to a value at which the anodic dissolution current (ia) is negligible — practically **below −0.77 V vs CSE** (the Fe/Fe(OH)2 equilibrium line on the Pourbaix diagram) and ideally **−0.85 V vs CSE**. ### 2.2 The Three Laws Governing CP Design 1. **Ohm's Law (voltage–current relation in electrolyte):** $$V = I \cdot R_{soil}$$ where **Rsoil** is the electrolyte resistance (Ω), **I** is the protective current (A), and **V** is the anode-to-cathode driving voltage. 2. **Faraday's Law (mass of metal consumed):** The mass of sacrificial metal dissolved per unit time is directly proportional to the current passed. This is the core equation for *anode life design*. 3. **Tafel / Butler–Volmer Kinetics (polarisation behaviour):** At higher current densities the polarisation curve departs from linearity, limiting how far the cathodic shift can be pushed without hydrogen embrittlement or coating disbondment. ### 2.3 Selection Logic — SACP vs ICCP ``` Is the structure electrically isolated from other metallic systems? ├─ YES, low resistivity environment (R < 2000 Ω·cm), small current demand (I < 5 A) │ → Choose SACP (Galvanic Anode) │ └─ NO, or high-resistivity electrolyte, or large current demand (I > 5 A) → Choose ICCP (Impressed Current) ``` ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | m = mass of metal consumed (g); I = current (A); t = time (s); M = atomic mass (g/mol); n = valence electrons; F = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol** | Compute anode mass required for a given CP current over a design life | | 2 | $I_{total} = j_{design} \cdot A_{surface}$ | jdesign = protective current density (A/m²); A = bare surface area to be protected (m²) | Sizing the rectifier or anode count | | 3 | $A_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{j_{anode,max}}$ | Maximum permissible current density of anode material (A/m²) | Sizing individual anode area (especially for MMO/Ti ribbon) | | 4 | $R_{anode,soil} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | ρ = soil resistivity (Ω·m); L = anode length (m); r = equivalent radius (m) (Dwight's formula for vertical anodes) | Calculate anode-to-earth resistance for ICCP anode bed design | | 5 | $\eta_{anode,life} = \dfrac{m_{anode,actual} \cdot n \cdot F}{I_{total} \cdot 86400 \cdot 365}$ | η = design life in years | Verify anode replacement interval | | 6 | $E_{off} \le -0.85\ \mathrm{V}\ \text{vs CSE}$ | Instantaneous-off potential (IR-drop-free) | Standard protection criterion (NACE/EN 12954) | | 7 | $\Delta E_{polarisation} \ge 100\ \mathrm{mV}$ | Decay over 4 h after CP interruption | Alternative protection criterion | | 8 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_{p}}$ | B = Stern–Geary constant (≈ **26 mV** for active steel); Rp = polarisation resistance (Ω·cm²) | Used in coupon-based verification of CP effectiveness | > [!NOTE] > **Why these matter in KTU exams:** Marks are awarded for correctly applying Faraday's law to *find anode mass*, computing *current demand* from current density, and verifying the *polarisation criterion*. Always quote the **Faraday constant = 96 485 C/mol** and standard reference potentials (CSE = +0.318 V vs SHE). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pipelines:** Cathodic protection combined with Fusion-Bonded Epoxy (FBE) coating is the **primary corrosion control** for buried oil/gas trunk lines; it can shift annual corrosion loss from **0.5–1.0 mm/yr to virtually 0 mm/yr**. - **Marine Piers & Bridge Substructures:** Splash-zone reinforcement corrosion is mitigated by embedding **zinc discrete anodes** in patch-repair mortars. - **Prestressed Concrete:** CP is *carefully* applied because excessive cathodic polarisation can produce **hydrogen embrittlement** of high-tensile steel — a classic KTU pitfall question. - **Industrial Storage Tanks:** Bottom plates are protected by deep-well impressed-current anode beds using **MMO/Ti tubular anodes**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Sacrificial Anode Consumed (Faraday's Law) **Statement:** *The mass of a metal dissolved by an impressed current is proportional to the total charge passed.* **Starting relation** (charge × molar mass per electron): $$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{[Coulombs]}$$ The number of moles of electrons transferred is: $$n_{e} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$ Since each metal atom requires **n** electrons to dissolve, the moles of metal consumed is: $$n_{metal} = \dfrac{n_{e}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$ Multiplying by the molar mass **M** gives the mass consumed: $$\boxed{\,m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}\,}$$ **Numerical Check (Zinc Anode, I = 0.5 A, t = 1 year):** - M(Zn) = **65.38 g/mol** - n = **2** (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻) - t = 365 × 24 × 3600 = **3.1536 × 10⁷ s** - F = **96 485 C/mol** $$m = \dfrac{0.5 \times 3.1536\times10^7 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{1.0307\times10^{9}}{1.9297\times10^{5}} = 5341.5\ \mathrm{g} \approx 5.34\ \mathrm{kg}$$ This matches the well-known empirical rule: **≈ 11 kg of zinc consumed per ampere-year**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anode Design Life (Service Period) Given a packaged zinc anode of mass **ma** (kg) and utilisation factor **u** (typically 0.85 for zinc, 0.50 for magnesium), the useful service life is: $$\eta = \dfrac{u \cdot m_{a} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot 3.1536\times10^{7}}\ \text{[years]}$$ **Worked Example:** - Anode: Magnesium, ma = **8.0 kg**, u = 0.50 - Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇒ n = 2, M = 24.31 g/mol - Output current I = **0.2 A** Step 1 — Compute total electrons that can be supplied: $$Q_{max} = \dfrac{u \cdot m_{a} \cdot n \cdot F}{M} = \dfrac{0.50 \times 8000 \times 2 \times 96485}{24.31}$$ $$Q_{max} = \dfrac{7.7188\times10^{8}}{24.31} = 3.175\times10^{7}\ \mathrm{C}$$ Step 2 — Convert to service years: $$\eta = \dfrac{3.175\times10^{7}}{0.2 \times 3.1536\times10^{7}} = \dfrac{3.175\times10^{7}}{6.307\times10^{6}} = 5.03\ \mathrm{years}$$ **Interpretation:** A standard 8-kg magnesium anode with 50% utilisation will last about **5 years** at 0.2 A discharge. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Anode Mass for Specified Life Rearranging Faraday's law for the required anode mass to obtain a target service life **η** (years): $$m_{a} = \dfrac{I \cdot M \cdot \eta \cdot 3.1536\times10^{7}}{n \cdot F \cdot u}$$ **Design Problem (KTU-style):** > A buried pipeline requires an ICCP installation. Total protective current = **15 A**, design life = **30 years**, anode = high-silicon cast iron, utilisation factor = **0.75**, M(Fe) = 55.85 g/mol, n = 2. $$m_{a} = \dfrac{15 \times 55.85 \times 30 \times 3.1536\times10^{7}}{2 \times 96485 \times 0.75}$$ Compute numerator: $15 \times 55.85 = 837.75$; $\times 30 = 25\,132.5$; $\times 3.1536\times10^{7} = 7.926\times10^{11}$ Compute denominator: $2 \times 96485 \times 0.75 = 144\,727.5$ $$m_{a} = \dfrac{7.926\times10^{11}}{1.4473\times10^{5}} = 5.476\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{g} = 5476\ \mathrm{kg}$$ Thus approximately **5.5 tonnes of anode material** are needed — which is why such installations use **multiple discrete anodes** distributed along the pipeline. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Design Current Densities | Environment | Steel Condition | Recommended jc (mA/m²) | Reference Electrode | |---|---|---|---| | Dry, well-aerated soil, ρ > 10 000 Ω·cm | Bare / coated | **5 – 15** | CSE | | Wet soil, ρ 1 000 – 10 000 Ω·cm | Bare / coated | **15 – 30** | CSE | | Aggressive marine soil, ρ < 200 Ω·cm | Bare | **50 – 100** | CSE / Ag-AgCl | | Seawater (submerged) | Bare | **100 – 200** | Ag-AgCl / Zn | | Tidal/splash zone | Bare | **150 – 300** | Ag-AgCl | | Atmospherically exposed reinforced concrete | Coated rebar | **5 – 20** | Ag-AgCl (embedded) | | Carbonated concrete | Coated rebar | **10 – 30** | Ag-AgCl | | Chloride-contaminated concrete | Coated rebar | **20 – 50** | Ag-AgCl / embedded MnO₂ | ### 3.5 Failure Mode Matrix for CP Installations | Observed Symptom | Probable Cause | Corrective Action | |---|---|---| | Structure potential **not reaching −850 mV** | Insufficient current, multiple structures interfering | Increase rectifier output; add anodes | | **Over-protection** (potential < −1.10 V vs CSE) | Excessive current; hydrogen evolution at cathode | Reduce rectifier voltage; add resistance | | Anode **consuming too quickly** | Under-sized anodes; wrong alloy | Replace with higher-capacity anodes | | **Stray-current corrosion** on nearby metal | ICCP interfering with foreign structures | Install **drainage bonds**; reverse current switches | | Coating **disbondment** | Alkaline generation at cathode interface | Reduce current density; improve coating | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Hydrogen Embrittlement:** In **prestressed concrete**, never let the off-potential drop below **−0.95 V vs CSE**. Beyond this, atomic hydrogen generated by water reduction can diffuse into high-tensile steel, causing **hydrogen-assisted cracking (HAC)** — a frequently examined *adverse effect of CP*. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) System ```mermaid flowchart LR AC[AC Mains Supply] --> REC[DC Rectifier and Controller] REC -- Positive Terminal --> ANODE[Impressed Current Anode Bed
MMO Ti or HSCI] ANODE -- Current flows through electrolyte --> STRUCT[Protected Steel Structure
Cathode] STRUCT -- Negative return cable --> REC REF[Permanent Reference Electrode
Cu CuSO4 CSE] --> MON[Potential Monitoring Unit] MON --> REC REC --> LOG[Data Logger and Alarm Module] ``` ### 4.2 Block Architecture — Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) System ```mermaid flowchart TD ZA1[Zinc Anode 1] -->|Galvanic discharge| EL[Electrolyte Soil or Water] ZA2[Aluminium Alloy Anode 2] -->|Galvanic discharge| EL ZA3[Magnesium Anode 3] -->|Galvanic discharge| EL EL -->|Protective current| ST[Steel Structure Cathode] ST -->|Bonding cable| ZA1 ST -->|Bonding cable| ZA2 ST -->|Bonding cable| ZA3 ST -.->|Test station| REF[Portable CSE Reference Electrode] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Choose CP Strategy] --> Q1{Is the structure
electrically isolated?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is soil or water resistivity
below 2000 ohm cm?} Q1 -- No --> ICCP1[Use ICCP] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is required current
less than 5 A?} Q2 -- No --> ICCP2[Use ICCP] Q3 -- Yes --> SACP[Use Sacrificial Anode SACP] Q3 -- No --> ICCP3[Use ICCP] ICCP1 --> OUT1[Deploy rectifier anodes and reference electrodes] ICCP2 --> OUT1 ICCP3 --> OUT1 SACP --> OUT2[Install zinc or magnesium anodes with backfill] ``` ### 4.4 Hybrid CP System for Reinforced Concrete Repair ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DAMAGED_ZONE[Damaged Concrete Element] PR[Patch Repair Mortar] ZDA1[Galvanic Discrete Anode Zinc 1] ZDA2[Galvanic Discrete Anode Zinc 2] ZDA3[Galvanic Discrete Anode Zinc 3] RB[Rebar Cathode Protected] end ZDA1 --> RB ZDA2 --> RB ZDA3 --> RB PR --- ZDA1 PR --- ZDA2 PR --- ZDA3 REF[Embedded Ag AgCl Reference Electrode] -.->|Measures potential| RB MON[Portable Corrosion Rate Monitor] -->|Reads polarisation decay| RB ``` ### 4.5 Process Logic — Verification of Protection (KTU Hot Topic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Switch off CP rectifier] --> B[Wait 1 to 4 hours for depolarisation] B --> C[Measure Eoff against CSE] C --> D{Eoff more negative
than minus 0.85 V?} D -- Yes --> E[Protection criterion met
EN 12954 Criterion 1] D -- No --> F[Check polarisation shift] F --> G{Delta E greater than
100 mV decay?} G -- Yes --> H[Protection criterion met
EN 12954 Criterion 2] G -- No --> I[Increase current
Add anodes] I --> A ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define cathodic protection. Distinguish between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection systems.* **Model Answer (10–12 lines):** > **Cathodic protection** is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the cathode of a galvanic or impressed-current cell, suppressing its anodic dissolution. > > **Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP):** The protective current is generated by connecting a more electrochemically active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) to the structure. The sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially. No external power is required, and the driving voltage is limited to **0.25–0.75 V** (galvanic). Best suited for small, well-coated, low-resistivity installations. > > **Impressed Current CP (ICCP):** An external **DC rectifier** (5–50 V) drives current from an inert anode (MMO/Ti, HSCI, graphite) to the structure through the electrolyte. It is used for **large or high-resistivity** systems and allows **continuous adjustment** of the protective current. > > In short, SACP is *self-powered but low-current*, whereas ICCP is *externally powered, high-capacity, and adjustable*. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State any three protection criteria used to verify the effectiveness of cathodic protection of steel structures.* **Model Answer:** The three internationally accepted protection criteria for steel in soil/water (per **EN 12954 / NACE SP0169**) are: 1. **Potential Criterion:** The "instantaneous-off" potential of the structure shall be **−850 mV or more negative** measured against a **Copper/Copper Sulphate reference electrode (CSE)**, excluding the IR-drop component. 2. **Polarisation Shift Criterion:** A minimum polarisation (decay) shift of **100 mV** shall be developed between the structure and a stable reference electrode, measured over a period of at least 4 hours after CP interruption. 3. **Net Protective Current Criterion:** The impressed current shall be sufficient to overcome the anodic reaction completely, verified by a sustained negative potential shift relative to the native Ecorr of at least **300 mV** for passive systems. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Instructions (KTU pattern):** Answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks. #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]* **(a)** A buried steel pipeline, 12 km long and 600 mm in diameter, runs through soil of resistivity 3000 Ω·cm. The design protective current density is **20 mA/m²**. The structure is to be protected by an **ICCP system** using high-silicon cast iron anodes (utilisation factor 0.75) of unit mass 50 kg each, M(Fe) = 55.85 g/mol, n = 2. Compute: (i) Total protective current required. (ii) Number of anodes if each anode can deliver a maximum of 5 A. (iii) Total anode mass for a 25-year design life. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **components and working of an Impressed Current Cathodic Protection system** for a marine bridge pier. Mention the role of the reference electrode, rectifier, and anode bed. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Q.A(a):** **(i) Total Current Required** External surface area of the pipe (neglecting ends): $$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.6 \times 12\,000 = 22\,619.5\ \mathrm{m^2}$$ Total current: $$I_{total} = j_{design} \times A = 0.020 \times 22\,619.5 = 452.4\ \mathrm{A}$$ **(ii) Number of Anodes** $$N = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{452.4}{5} = 90.48 \Rightarrow \textbf{91 anodes (round up)}$$ **(iii) Anode Mass for 25 Years** Using Faraday's law with utilisation factor: $$m_{a} = \dfrac{I_{total} \cdot M \cdot \eta \cdot 3.1536\times10^{7}}{n \cdot F \cdot u}$$ Numerator: $452.4 \times 55.85 \times 25 \times 3.1536\times10^{7} = 1.992\times10^{13}$ Denominator: $2 \times 96\,485 \times 0.75 = 144\,727.5$ $$m_{a} = \dfrac{1.992\times10^{13}}{1.4473\times10^{5}} = 1.376\times10^{8}\ \mathrm{g} = \textbf{137\,600\ kg \ (i.e., 137.6 tonnes)}$$ Mass per anode = $137\,600 / 91 \approx$ **1512 kg each** — indicating the need for either larger anodes or higher per-anode current rating in a real design. --- **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Components of ICCP for a marine pier:** 1. **DC Rectifier Unit (5–50 V):** Takes AC mains and delivers a regulated DC output to the anode bed. Includes surge protection and an automatic potential controller. 2. **Anode Bed (submerged in seawater):** Typically **MMO/Ti tubular anodes** mounted on the seabed or suspended from the pier cap. Connected to the **positive** terminal of the rectifier. 3. **Reference Electrodes:** Two **Ag/AgCl** electrodes permanently installed in the splash and submerged zones to monitor "on" and "off" potentials. 4. **Negative Return Cable:** A heavy cross-section HDPE-insulated copper cable from the pier reinforcement cage to the **negative** terminal of the rectifier — completing the circuit. 5. **Junction Boxes, Cabling, and Data Logger:** For periodic recording of potentials and rectifier output. **Working principle:** The rectifier impresses DC current from the inert anode through the seawater (electrolyte) to the steel reinforcement, forcing the rebar to behave as a cathode. The reference electrode senses the rebar potential; the controller adjusts the rectifier output to maintain the **−850 mV vs CSE** (or equivalent Ag/AgCl) criterion continuously. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]* **(a)** Explain **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection** with a schematic. Compare the **electrochemical characteristics** of zinc, magnesium, and aluminium anodes in terms of: (i) Open-circuit potential vs CSE. (ii) Practical current capacity (Ah/kg). (iii) Typical application environments. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A reinforced-concrete jetty uses **discrete zinc galvanic anodes** (unit mass 1.2 kg, utilisation factor 0.85, M = 65.38 g/mol, n = 2) embedded in patch repair. Each anode delivers a steady current of **0.08 A**. Calculate the **service life** of a single anode. Comment on the suitability of this life for a 20-year design life and suggest **two design modifications** if insufficient. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to Q.B(a):** **SACP Schematic:** (See SECTION 4.2 above for the Mermaid block; in exam, draw a labelled sketch of a zinc anode connected to a buried pipe via a cable, with soil as electrolyte, and a portable CSE reference electrode for measurement.) **Comparison of Sacrificial Anode Materials:** | Property | Magnesium (Mg) | Zinc (Zn) | Aluminium-Zinc-Indium (Al) | |---|---|---|---| | Open-circuit potential vs CSE | **−1.55 to −1.75 V** (most active) | **−1.10 V** | **−1.05 to −1.15 V** | | Theoretical capacity (Ah/kg) | **2200** | **820** | **2700–2900** | | Practical current capacity (Ah/kg) | **1100–1300** | **780** | **2000–2500** | | Driving voltage | Highest | Moderate | Moderate (passive film risk) | | Electrolyte resistivity (Ω·cm) | Up to **10 000** | Up to **2000** | Up to **1500** (seawater excellent) | | Typical use | High-resistivity soils, freshwater | Marine, low-resistivity soil | Seawater, offshore | > **Conclusion:** **Mg** is chosen for high-resistivity soils; **Zn** for marine/coastal low-resistivity conditions; **Al** for long-life marine structures (e.g., ship hulls, offshore jackets) provided a small amount of indium is alloyed to break the passive Al₂O₃ film. --- **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Service life calculation:** Using the formula: $$\eta = \dfrac{u \cdot m_{a} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I \cdot 3.1536\times10^{7}}$$ Substitute: u = 0.85, ma = 1200 g, n = 2, F = 96 485 C/mol, M = 65.38 g/mol, I = 0.08 A. Numerator: $0.85 \times 1200 \times 2 \times 96\,485 = 1.9670\times10^{8}$ Denominator: $65.38 \times 0.08 \times 3.1536\times10^{7} = 1.6493\times10^{8}$ $$\eta = \dfrac{1.9670\times10^{8}}{1.6493\times10^{8}} = 1.193\ \mathrm{years} \approx \textbf{14.3\ months}$$ **Suitability Comment:** A service life of only **~14 months** is **grossly inadequate** for a 20-year design life. Each anode would require replacement ~16 times during the structure's life — economically and logistically unacceptable. **Two Design Modifications:** 1. **Increase Anode Mass:** Either raise the discrete anode mass (e.g., 5 kg Zn) or use **continuous zinc ribbon anodes** along the patch perimeter to provide a larger zinc reservoir. 2. **Switch to Impressed Current (ICCP):** For the entire jetty, install a rectifier-driven MMO/Ti mesh anode in the concrete cover, with embedded Ag/AgCl reference electrodes for automatic potential control — this provides 20+ year life with negligible maintenance. 3. *(Alternative)* Use **hybrid discrete anodes** of higher capacity Al-Zn-In alloy instead of pure zinc — capacity rises to ~2000 Ah/kg, giving a 4–5 year life per 1.2-kg unit. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on CP questions:** > 1. **Forgetting IR-drop correction:** A potential measured *with* the CP system ON includes a voltage drop through the soil. Always state whether your quoted potential is "on" or "instantaneous-off" (IR-free). > 2. **Wrong Faraday constant:** Use **F = 96 485 C/mol** — not 96 000, and never omit it. > 3. **Mixing up anode/cathode roles:** In CP, the *protected structure* is the **cathode** and the auxiliary metal is the **anode**. Many students invert this. > 4. **Forgetting the utilisation factor (u):** In real designs, only **50–85%** of the anode mass is consumed before it becomes mechanically/electrically ineffective. Always include u. > 5. **Ignoring hydrogen embrittlement in PSC:** Citing only the −850 mV criterion without warning about over-protection in prestressed steel costs full marks. > 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Current density *area* must yield total current in amperes; surface area in m²; time in seconds inside Faraday's law. Show unit cancellation in the model answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Cathodic Protection =** making the structure the *cathode* of an electrochemical cell, suppressing anodic metal loss. - **Two types:** **SACP** (Zn, Mg, Al — self-driven) and **ICCP** (rectifier + inert anode — externally driven). - **Protection criteria (EN 12954 / NACE):** Eoff ≤ −0.85 V vs CSE, OR ΔE ≥ 100 mV decay, OR E vs CSE < −0.95 V (with caution in PSC). - **Faraday's Law (master equation):** $$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ with F = **96 485 C/mol**. - **Anode life formula:** $$\eta = \dfrac{u \cdot m_a \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot 3.1536\times10^7}\ \text{[years]}$$ - **Design current densities:** 5–200 mA/m² depending on environment (highest in marine splash zone). - **Reference electrodes:** CSE (soil/water), Ag/AgCl (marine/concrete), Zn (submerged), SCE (lab). - **Common anode materials:** HSCI, MMO/Ti, graphite, Pb-Ag (ICCP); Zn, Mg, Al-Zn-In (SACP). - **Backup protection criterion:** 100 mV polarisation decay over 4 h. - **Pitfall to remember:** Hydrogen embrittlement in **prestressed** steel if potential < −0.95 V vs CSE. - **Concrete repair strategy:** Patch repair + galvanic discrete anodes (Zn) + coating — the most-tested KTU hybrid system. - **Verification procedure:** Measure *on* potential → switch off → wait 1–4 h → measure *off* potential → confirm criterion. - **Stainless-steel note:** CP on stainless can produce **H₂S corrosion** in sour service and **alkaline cracking**; special care required (a KTU 14-mark favourite). - **Standards to memorise:** EN 12954, NACE SP0169, ISO 12696 (concrete), BS EN 12473 (design). - **Unit conversion:** 1 year = 3.1536 × 10⁷ s; always use SI units in the formula.

topics.title: Jacketing - Column Jacketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Jacketing - Column Jacketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Column Jacketing** is a structural rehabilitation and strengthening technique in which an existing reinforced concrete (RC) column is encased by an additional layer of material — typically **Reinforced Concrete (RC)**, **Steel Plates/Sections**, or **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) wraps** — to restore or enhance its axial load capacity, flexural strength, shear capacity, and ductility. The new jacket acts compositely (or partially compositely) with the original section through proper **shear transfer mechanisms** (adhesion, mechanical interlock, and dowel action).

According to the **IS 15988:2013** and ACI 562 guidelines adopted in Indian repair practice, jacketing is classified as a *Section Enlargement* or *Member Strengthening* strategy wherein the cross-sectional dimensions are increased, additional longitudinal and transverse reinforcement is provided, and the original member is monolithically integrated with the new section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):**
> Jacketing of columns is studied under three major variants — **Concrete Jacketing, Steel Jacketing, and FRP Jacketing** — covering material selection, design philosophy, surface preparation, load transfer detailing, and evaluation of strengthened capacity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **old, crumbling wooden table leg** that has begun to wobble. You cannot easily replace it because the table is heavy and structurally tied to the tabletop. So instead, you **wrap and clamp a fresh, sturdy steel sleeve** around the weak leg, then pour some adhesive grout inside. The sleeve now carries a portion of the load, the wobble disappears, and the table can hold more weight than before.

A damaged RC column behaves exactly the same way:
- The **old concrete** = the worn-out table leg (it has lost strength due to corrosion, fire, or overloading).
- The **jacket** = the steel sleeve or new concrete shell.
- The **shear connectors / dowels / bonding agent** = the adhesive that makes them work as one unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:**
> Effective cross-sectional enlargement in concrete jacketing typically ranges from **75 mm to 100 mm** on each side, providing a minimum **15% to 40%** enhancement in axial capacity depending on the original damage state.

## 1.3 Why Is Column Jacketing Necessary?

A column is the **primary vertical load-carrying element** of any framed structure. Deterioration can occur due to:

| Cause of Deterioration | Effect on Column |
|------------------------|------------------|
| **Corrosion of reinforcement** | Loss of steel area, spalling of cover concrete |
| **Fire exposure** | Loss of concrete strength, spalling |
| **Earthquake/seismic event** | Cracking, plastic hinge formation |
| **Overloading / change of use** | Exceedance of original design capacity |
| **Carbonation / chloride attack** | Loss of passive protection to steel |
| **Construction defects** (honeycombing, low cover) | Reduced effective cross-section |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Reference:**
> **IS 15988:2013 (Code of Practice for Repair and Seismic Strengthening of Buildings)** classifies column jacketing as a *“major structural intervention”* and mandates detailed engineering assessment before execution.

---
