# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

Column jacketing, irrespective of material type, follows a universal **five-stage operational logic**:

1. **Condition Assessment & Diagnosis**
   - Non-destructive testing (rebound hammer, UPV, GPR, cover meter).
   - Estimation of residual strength, carbonation depth, chloride content.
   - Identification of load path and structural role.

2. **Surface Preparation**
   - Removal of loose, delaminated, or contaminated concrete.
   - Chipping to expose sound substrate (typically minimum **12 mm to 25 mm** behind reinforcement).
   - Roughening of bonding interface to **amplitude ≥ 6 mm** for mechanical interlock.

3. **Provision of Shear Transfer Mechanism**
   - Drilling and grouting of **dowel bars** (typically 12 mm dia. at 300 mm c/c, 300 mm embedment length).
   - Application of **bonding agents** — epoxy resins, polymer-modified cementitious slurries, or latex bonding compounds.

4. **Installation of Additional Reinforcement / Jacket Material**
   - Placement of additional longitudinal bars (≥ 4 nos. at corners + intermediate).
   - Provision of additional lateral ties (≥ 8 mm dia. at 100–150 mm c/c).
   - Or installation of steel plates, FRP wraps, or CFRP laminates.

5. **Casting / Wrapping and Curing**
   - Application of micro-concrete, grout, or polymer mortar.
   - Curing for **minimum 7 days** to develop composite action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:**
> The strengthened section is designed assuming **full composite action** between old and new concrete, i.e., strain compatibility across the interface. However, in seismic retrofits, the design often considers **partial composite action** with a reduction factor of **0.8 to 0.9** to account for bond slip.

## 2.2 Types of Column Jacketing

### 2.2.1 Reinforced Concrete (RC) Jacketing

Most common and economical. The original column is enlarged by adding a new layer of RC around its perimeter.

- **Minimum jacket thickness:** 75 mm (one side), as per IS 15988.
- **Additional longitudinal steel:** ≥ 4 bars of 12 mm dia., corner + intermediate.
- **Lateral ties:** ≤ 150 mm spacing, often closely spaced (100 mm) in plastic hinge zones.
- **Concrete grade:** minimum **M25** for new jacket concrete.

**Advantages:** Economical, increases stiffness, fire-resistant, compatible with original RC.
**Disadvantages:** Increases member size, additional dead load, curing time.

### 2.2.2 Steel Jacketing

Steel angles and plates are welded/bonded around the column to form an external cage.

- **Typical arrangement:** Four corner steel angles (e.g., 75 × 75 × 6 mm) with horizontal steel straps (e.g., 50 × 6 mm flat) welded at 300–500 mm c/c.
- **Grouting:** Gap between steel and concrete filled with non-shrink grout or epoxy.

**Advantages:** High strength-to-volume ratio, fast execution, minimal cross-section increase.
**Disadvantages:** Susceptible to corrosion, requires fire protection, costly.

### 2.2.3 FRP / CFRP Jacketing

**Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP)** wraps are bonded externally using epoxy resin.

- **Wraps:** Carbon (CFRP), Glass (GFRP), or Aramid (AFRP).
- **Orientation:** Hoop (transverse) wraps for confinement; longitudinal for flexure.
- **Thickness:** 1.0 mm to 3.0 mm per ply.

**Advantages:** Lightweight, corrosion-resistant, minimal size increase.
**Disadvantages:** High cost, brittle failure, fire sensitivity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **most frequently tested** in KTU university examinations on this topic:

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Axial Capacity of RC Jacketed Column (IS 456) | $P_{u,j} = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_{c,eff} + 0.67 \cdot f_{y} \cdot A_{sc,eff}$ | $f_{ck}$ in N/mm², $A$ in mm², $P$ in N | Combined axial capacity of old + new section |
| 2 | Effective Cross-Sectional Area | $A_{c,eff} = A_{g,old} + A_{g,new}$ | Gross area, mm² | Total area considered monolithic |
| 3 | Effective Steel Area | $A_{sc,eff} = A_{sc,old} + A_{sc,new}$ | mm² | Sum of old + added longitudinal steel |
| 4 | Mander's Confinement Model (Concrete Core) | $f_{cc}^{'} = f_{co}^{'} \cdot \left[ 2.254 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{7.94 \cdot f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}}} - \dfrac{2 \cdot f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} - 1.254 \right]$ | $f_{cc}^{'}$ confined strength, $f_{co}^{'}$ unconfined strength, $f_{l}^{'}$ effective lateral pressure, N/mm² | Confinement effectiveness of jacket |
| 5 | Lateral Confinement Pressure (Circular Jacket) | $f_{l}^{'} = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_{jacket} \cdot t_{j}}{D}$ | $f_{jacket}$ jacket tensile strength, $t_{j}$ thickness, $D$ diameter, all in N/mm² and mm | For circular columns with continuous wrap |
| 6 | Lateral Confinement Pressure (Rectangular Section) | $f_{lx}^{'} = k_{e} \cdot \rho_{x} \cdot f_{jacket}$  ;  $f_{ly}^{'} = k_{e} \cdot \rho_{y} \cdot f_{jacket}$ | $k_{e}$ effectiveness factor (≤ 1), $\rho$ volumetric ratio of jacket | FRP/Steel jacketed rectangular columns |
| 7 | Confinement Effectiveness Factor | $k_{e} = \dfrac{A_{e}}{A_{c}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{b}{h} \right)^{2}$ | $A_{e}$ effectively confined area, $b/h$ aspect ratio | Accounts for arching action in rectangular sections |
| 8 | Volumetric Ratio of FRP | $\rho_{j} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_{j} \cdot (b + h)}{b \cdot h}$ | $n$ number of plies, $t_{j}$ ply thickness, $b, h$ section dimensions, mm | Quantity of FRP required |
| 9 | Bond Strength Requirement (Interface) | $\tau_{bond} \geq \dfrac{V_{u}}{b_{j} \cdot L_{j}}$ | $\tau$ shear stress, $V_u$ factored shear, $b_j L_j$ interface area | Dowel/grout design at old-new interface |
| 10 | Strength Enhancement Factor (Confinement) | $K = \dfrac{f_{cc}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}}$ | Dimensionless | Direct measure of confinement gain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:**
> For **rectangular columns**, the confinement effectiveness factor $k_e$ is typically in the range **0.30 to 0.65**, reflecting that confinement is far less efficient than in circular sections. For elliptical/round columns, $k_e \approx 0.95$ to $1.0$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Column jacketing is extensively applied in:
- **Seismic retrofit of old buildings** (pre-IS 13920 structures in seismic zones III, IV, V of Kerala).
- **Bridge pier rehabilitation** after scour, corrosion, or impact damage.
- **Industrial structures** subjected to chemical attack.
- **Heritage buildings** requiring strength upgrade without complete demolition.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Axial Load Capacity of a Jacketed Composite Column

**Problem Setup:**
A square RC column of original size **300 mm × 300 mm** is jacketed with a **75 mm thick** RC layer on all four sides. The original column has **4 bars of 16 mm dia.** with concrete grade **M20** and steel grade **Fe415**. The new jacket has **4 corner bars of 12 mm dia.** plus **4 intermediate bars of 12 mm dia.** with concrete grade **M25**. Compute the ultimate axial load capacity assuming full composite action (IS 456:2000 short column formula).

### Step 1 — Define Original Section Geometry
$$A_{g,old} = 300 \times 300 = 90000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{sc,old} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (16)^{2} = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Define New (Jacket) Section Geometry
The new overall dimension becomes:
$$B_{new} = 300 + 2 \times 75 = 450 \text{ mm}$$

The added area is the area of the L-shaped jacket, but for **full composite action**, we simply add the gross areas:
$$A_{g,new,total} = 450 \times 450 = 202500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

The **effective added area** is the jacket alone (not double-counting the original):
$$A_{g,new} = A_{g,new,total} - A_{g,old} = 202500 - 90000 = 112500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Effective Areas for Design
For the IS 456 short-column formula applied to the composite section:
$$A_{c,eff} = A_{g,old} + A_{g,new} = 90000 + 112500 = 202500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Note:** In practice, since the original section uses M20 and the jacket uses M25, we cannot simply use a single $f_{ck}$. A weighted (transformed-section) approach is required:

$$f_{ck,eff} = \frac{f_{ck,old} \cdot A_{g,old} + f_{ck,new} \cdot A_{g,new}}{A_{c,eff}}$$

$$f_{ck,eff} = \frac{20 \times 90000 + 25 \times 112500}{202500}$$

$$f_{ck,eff} = \frac{1800000 + 2812500}{202500} = \frac{4612500}{202500} = 22.78 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Steel Area in Jacketed Section
$$A_{sc,new} = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (12)^{2} = 8 \times 113.10 = 904.78 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{sc,eff} = A_{sc,old} + A_{sc,new} = 804.25 + 904.78 = 1709.03 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Axial Load Capacity
Using IS 456:2000, Clause 39.3 (axially loaded short column):
$$P_{u} = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck,eff} \cdot A_{c,eff} + 0.67 \cdot f_{y} \cdot A_{sc,eff}$$

$$P_{u} = 0.4 \times 22.78 \times 202500 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1709.03$$

$$P_{u} = 1844916 + 475042.6$$

$$P_{u} = 2319958.6 \text{ N} \approx 2320 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Original Capacity
$$P_{u,old} = 0.4 \times 20 \times 90000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 804.25$$

$$P_{u,old} = 720000 + 223742 = 943742 \text{ N} \approx 944 \text{ kN}$$

**Strength Enhancement:**
$$\text{Enhancement} = \frac{2320 - 944}{944} \times 100\% = 145.8\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> The 75 mm RC jacket **nearly 2.5× the axial capacity** of the original column, demonstrating the effectiveness of section enlargement as a strengthening strategy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Confinement Pressure in FRP-Jacketed Circular Column

**Problem Setup:**
A circular RC column of diameter **D = 400 mm** is wrapped with **2 plies of CFRP** of thickness **t_j = 1.2 mm** each, with ultimate tensile strength **f_jacket = 2400 MPa**. Determine the lateral confinement pressure and the confined concrete strength using Mander's model, given unconfined strength **f_co' = 25 MPa**.

### Step 1 — Total FRP Thickness
$$t_{j,total} = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Lateral Confinement Pressure (Circular Section)
For a fully wrapped circular column, the jacket exerts uniform radial pressure:
$$f_{l}^{'} = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_{jacket} \cdot t_{j,total}}{D}$$

$$f_{l}^{'} = \dfrac{2 \times 2400 \times 2.4}{400} = 28.8 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Confinement Ratio
$$\dfrac{f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} = \dfrac{28.8}{25} = 1.152$$

### Step 4 — Mander's Confined Strength
$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_{co}^{'} \cdot \left[ 2.254 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{7.94 \cdot f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}}} - \dfrac{2 \cdot f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} - 1.254 \right]$$

**Compute inner square-root term:**
$$1 + \dfrac{7.94 \times 28.8}{25} = 1 + 9.147 = 10.147$$

$$\sqrt{10.147} = 3.185$$

$$2.254 \times 3.185 = 7.179$$

**Compute the linear term:**
$$\dfrac{2 \times 28.8}{25} = 2.304$$

**Combine:**
$$f_{cc}^{'} = 25 \times [7.179 - 2.304 - 1.254] = 25 \times 3.621$$

$$f_{cc}^{'} = 90.53 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Strength Enhancement Factor
$$K = \dfrac{f_{cc}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} = \dfrac{90.53}{25} = 3.62$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:**
> The CFRP wrap increases concrete compressive strength by **3.62×** — a dramatic enhancement due to triaxial confinement. In practice, this translates to substantially higher axial capacity, ductility, and energy absorption under seismic loading.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Ratio and Confinement in Rectangular FRP Column

For a rectangular section of **b × h = 300 × 450 mm**, wrapped with **n = 3 plies** of GFRP of thickness **t_j = 1.0 mm** each, $f_{jacket} = 1500$ MPa.

### Step 1 — Volumetric Ratio
$$\rho_{j} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_{j} \cdot (b + h)}{b \cdot h}$$

$$\rho_{j} = \dfrac{2 \times 3 \times 1.0 \times (300 + 450)}{300 \times 450} = \dfrac{4500}{135000} = 0.0333$$

### Step 2 — Effective Confinement Pressure (with k_e = 0.5)
$$f_{lx}^{'} = k_{e} \cdot \rho_{x} \cdot f_{jacket} = 0.5 \times 0.0333 \times 1500 = 25.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$f_{ly}^{'} = 0.5 \times 0.0333 \times 1500 = 25.0 \text{ MPa}$$

(Assuming equal volumetric ratios in both directions for simplicity.)

This is then substituted into Mander's model to obtain the confined strength.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> F

    F1 --> G1[Chipping Surface Prep]

    F3 --> G3[Surface Grinding Epoxy Application]

    G1 --> H1[Dowel Bar Installation]

    G3 --> H3[Wrap Application Hoop Orientation]

    H1 --> I1[Add Longitudinal Bars Ties]

    H3 --> I3[Multiple Plies Resin Curing]

    I1 --> J1[Micro Concrete Casting]

    J3 --> K

    K --> L[Quality Control Testing]
    L --> M[Service Life Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Forces and Load Transfer in Jacketed Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Applied Axial Load P] --> OC[Original Column Core]
    L1 --> JC[Jacket Section]

    OC --> OC1[Original Concrete M20]
    OC --> OC2[Original Steel Fe415]
    OC --> OC3[Residual Strength Index R]

    JC --> JC1[Jacket Concrete M25 or Higher]
    JC --> JC2[Added Longitudinal Bars]
    JC --> JC3[Added Lateral Ties or FRP]

    OC -.->|Interface Shear| JC
    JC -.->|Confining Pressure f_l| OC

    OC --> RES[Composite Capacity P_u]
    JC --> RES

    RES --> OUT[Enhanced Axial Strength Ductility Stiffness]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define column jacketing. List any three types of jacketing used for RC columns.**

**Model Answer:**
Column jacketing is a structural strengthening technique wherein an additional layer of material (concrete, steel, or FRP) is provided around an existing RC column to restore or enhance its load-carrying capacity, stiffness, and ductility.

**Three Types:**
1. Reinforced Concrete (RC) Jacketing
2. Steel Jacketing (angles + straps)
3. FRP/CFRP Jacketing (external wrapping)

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always list types **with brief context** (e.g., "FRP – lightweight, used for seismic retrofit") to score beyond the minimum.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of dowel bars in column jacketing?**

**Model Answer:**
Dowel bars are short reinforcement bars (typically 12 mm dia., 300 mm embedment) grouted into holes drilled into the original column. They serve to:
1. **Transfer shear** between the old concrete and the new jacket, ensuring composite action.
2. Resist **interface delamination** under axial load and seismic forces.
3. Provide **mechanical interlock** to supplement chemical adhesion from bonding agents.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — **Part B Option 1**

**(a) Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of RC column jacketing. Discuss the design considerations and detailing requirements as per IS 15988:2013. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Condition Assessment:** Conduct NDT (rebound hammer, UPV), estimate residual strength, identify reinforcement layout using cover meter, and assess carbonation depth.

2. **Load Shedding/Transfer:** Props are placed to relieve or transfer the existing dead load from the column during jacketing.

3. **Surface Preparation:** Loose, spalled, or contaminated concrete is chipped off to expose sound substrate (minimum 12–25 mm behind rebar). Surface is roughened to amplitude ≥ 6 mm.

4. **Treatment of Reinforcement:** Corroded rebars are cleaned, supplemented if loss > 20% of original area, and coated with anti-corrosion primer.

5. **Dowel Bar Installation:** 12 mm dia. bars at 300 mm c/c are drilled and grouted into the original column with epoxy.

6. **Placement of Additional Reinforcement:** Longitudinal bars (≥ 4 nos. of 12 mm dia., one at each corner) and lateral ties (≤ 150 mm c/c) are installed around the column.

7. **Formwork and Casting:** Micro-concrete or polymer-modified mortar is poured, compacted, and cured for ≥ 7 days.

8. **Quality Control:** Load testing and rebound hammer testing validate the strengthened member.

**Design Considerations (IS 15988:2013):**
- Minimum jacket thickness: **75 mm on each side**.
- Minimum additional longitudinal steel: **0.8% of added gross area**.
- Lateral tie spacing in plastic hinge zone: **100 mm c/c**.
- Bonding agent: polymer-modified cementitious slurry.
- Reduction factor: **0.8–0.9** for partial composite action.

---

**(b) An RC column of size 400 mm × 400 mm with 6 bars of 20 mm dia., M20 concrete, and Fe415 steel is jacketed with 100 mm thick M25 concrete. The jacket has 8 bars of 16 mm dia. with 8 mm ties at 150 mm c/c. Compute the axial load capacity of the jacketed column. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Areas:**
$$A_{g,old} = 400 \times 400 = 160000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{sc,old} = 6 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^{2} = 6 \times 314.16 = 1884.96 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — New Section:**
$$B_{new} = 400 + 2 \times 100 = 600 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_{g,new,total} = 600 \times 600 = 360000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{g,new} = 360000 - 160000 = 200000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Concrete Strength (Weighted):**
$$f_{ck,eff} = \frac{20 \times 160000 + 25 \times 200000}{360000} = \frac{3200000 + 5000000}{360000} = 22.78 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Steel Area:**
$$A_{sc,new} = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (16)^{2} = 8 \times 201.06 = 1608.50 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A_{sc,eff} = 1884.96 + 1608.50 = 3493.46 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Axial Capacity (IS 456):**
$$P_{u} = 0.4 \times f_{ck,eff} \times A_{c,eff} + 0.67 \times f_{y} \times A_{sc,eff}$$

$$P_{u} = 0.4 \times 22.78 \times 360000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 3493.46$$

$$P_{u} = 3280320 + 970817.6 = 4251137.6 \text{ N} \approx 4251 \text{ kN}$$

**Original Capacity:**
$$P_{u,old} = 0.4 \times 20 \times 160000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1884.96$$
$$P_{u,old} = 1280000 + 523766 = 1803766 \text{ N} \approx 1804 \text{ kN}$$

**Enhancement:**
$$\frac{4251 - 1804}{1804} \times 100\% \approx 135.6\%$$

**Final Answer:** $P_u = 4251 \text{ kN}$ (about 2.36× the original).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — **Part B Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a) Describe the construction procedure and design philosophy of steel jacketing for RC columns. Compare its merits and demerits with concrete jacketing. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction Procedure:**
1. Surface preparation of column by chipping, sandblasting, and cleaning.
2. Four corner steel angles (e.g., 75 × 75 × 6 mm) are placed around the column and anchored using expansion bolts or dowels.
3. Horizontal steel straps (e.g., 50 × 6 mm flats) are welded at 300–500 mm c/c between angles.
4. Gap between steel and concrete surface is pressure-injected with non-shrink cementitious or epoxy grout.
5. Anti-corrosion coating and fire-protection lining are applied.

**Design Philosophy:**
Steel jacketing relies on **passive confinement**: as the original concrete expands laterally under axial load, the steel cage resists the expansion, creating triaxial stress state. Design uses Mander's model with effective lateral pressure:
$$f_{l}^{'} = \frac{2 \cdot F_y \cdot t_{strap}}{s \cdot D}$$
where $s$ is the strap spacing.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | RC Jacketing | Steel Jacketing |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Cost | Low | Moderate to High |
| Size increase | Significant (75–100 mm/side) | Minimal |
| Speed of execution | Slow (curing) | Fast |
| Fire resistance | Excellent | Poor (needs protection) |
| Corrosion resistance | Good | Susceptible |
| Ductility gain | Moderate | High |
| Weight addition | High | Low |

---

**(b) A circular RC column of 500 mm diameter is wrapped with 3 plies of CFRP, each of 1.0 mm thickness and ultimate tensile strength 2800 MPa. The unconfined concrete strength is 30 MPa. Estimate the confined compressive strength using Mander's model. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Jacket Thickness:**
$$t_{j,total} = 3 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral Confinement Pressure:**
$$f_{l}^{'} = \frac{2 \cdot f_{jacket} \cdot t_{j,total}}{D} = \frac{2 \times 2800 \times 3.0}{500} = 33.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Confinement Ratio:**
$$\frac{f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} = \frac{33.6}{30} = 1.12$$

**Step 4 — Mander's Model:**
$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_{co}^{'} \cdot \left[ 2.254 \sqrt{1 + \frac{7.94 \cdot f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}}} - \frac{2 f_{l}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} - 1.254 \right]$$

**Inner term:**
$$1 + 7.94 \times 1.12 = 1 + 8.8928 = 9.8928$$
$$\sqrt{9.8928} = 3.145$$
$$2.254 \times 3.145 = 7.090$$

**Linear term:** $2 \times 1.12 = 2.24$

**Combine:**
$$f_{cc}^{'} = 30 \times [7.090 - 2.24 - 1.254] = 30 \times 3.596 = 107.88 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Enhancement:**
$$K = \frac{f_{cc}^{'}}{f_{co}^{'}} = \frac{107.88}{30} = 3.60$$

**Final Answer:** $f_{cc}^{'} \approx 107.9 \text{ MPa}$, indicating a **3.6-fold strength gain** in confined concrete.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to transform $f_{ck}$** when old and new concretes have different grades — students often apply $f_{ck,new}$ to the entire section, leading to inflated capacity (deduct 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Neglecting $k_e$** in rectangular FRP columns — assuming circular-section confinement equations on rectangular sections is a major error (deduct 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Misapplying tie spacing rules** — Seismic code (IS 13920) requires ≤ 100 mm c/c in plastic hinge regions of jacketed columns, not just 150 mm.
> 4. **Ignoring dowel bar design** — Examiners expect explicit detailing of dowels (dia, spacing, embedment) for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing "Strength Enhancement Factor"** with **"Confinement Factor"** — these are related but not identical; state the relationship explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick-Reference Bullet Checklist

- **Column Jacketing** = section enlargement + new reinforcement + composite action through shear transfer.
- **Five Stages:** Assessment → Surface Prep → Dowels/Bonding → Reinforcement/Jacket Installation → Casting/Curing.
- **Minimum RC jacket thickness:** **75 mm** (IS 15988).
- **Minimum additional longitudinal steel:** **4 corner bars of 12 mm dia.** (often 8 bars for full section).
- **Lateral ties in seismic zones:** **8 mm dia. at ≤ 100 mm c/c** in plastic hinge regions.
- **Bonding agent types:** Epoxy resin, polymer-modified cementitious slurry, SBR latex.
- **Mander's Confinement Model:** Most commonly tested formula — memorize $f_{cc}^{'}$ expression.
- **Circular vs Rectangular Confinement:** $k_e = 1.0$ for circular, $k_e = 0.3$–$0.65$ for rectangular.
- **CFRP enhancement** in circular sections: typically **2.5× to 4×** strength gain.
- **Steel jacketing formula:** $f_l = \dfrac{2 F_y t}{sD}$ for strap-based confinement.
- **Dowel bar spacing:** 300 mm c/c, 300 mm embedment, 12 mm dia. typical.
- **Composite action factor:** Assume **0.8–0.9** for design safety unless bond is experimentally verified.
- **Order of typical enhancements (same original column):**
  - RC Jacketing → **1.5× to 2.5×** capacity
  - Steel Jacketing → **1.8× to 3.0×** capacity
  - FRP Jacketing (circular) → **2.5× to 4.0×** confined strength

### 📌 Codes to Remember

- **IS 15988:2013** – Seismic Evaluation & Strengthening of Existing Buildings.
- **IS 456:2000** – Plain and Reinforced Concrete (axially loaded column formula).
- **IS 13920:2016** – Ductile Detailing of RC Structures (relevant for seismic jacketing).
- **ACI 562** – Code Requirements for Assessment, Repair, and Rehabilitation of Existing Concrete Structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word for KTU Aspirants:**
> Numerical problems on column jacketing almost always involve the **IS 456 short-column formula with composite transformation of $f_{ck}$**. Practice at least 3–4 such problems before the exam. For theory, memorize the **step-by-step procedure, types, and design detailing rules** — these are guaranteed 7- to 14-mark questions in every KTU ESE cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam Jacketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beam Jacketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beam Jacketing** is a structural rehabilitation and strengthening technique in which an additional layer (or "jacket") of high-performance material — typically **Reinforced Cement Concrete (R.C.C.)**, **structural steel (steel plates/angles)**, or **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites** — is bonded/anchored onto the external surfaces (soffit and/or sides) of an existing damaged or under-strength concrete beam. The composite action between the parent (old) beam and the new jacket restores or upgrades the **flexural capacity (Mu)**, **shear capacity (Vu)**, **stiffness (EI)**, and **ductility (μ)** to meet the current design demand as per **IS 456:2000 / IS 15988:2013 / ACI 562-19** codal provisions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, beam jacketing is broadly classified under **"Section Enlargement / Member Enlargement"** techniques of retrofitting, and is treated as a sub-category of **"Conventional Retrofit Methodology"** alongside column jacketing and slab jacketing.

| Jacketing Type | Material Used | Primary Failure Mode Addressed | Typical Strength Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concrete Jacketing** | Micro-concrete / Polymer-modified mortar + Rebar | Flexure + Shear + Ductility | 25–80 % |
| **Steel Jacketing** | Rolled steel plates / angles / channels | Shear + Flexure | 30–60 % |
| **FRP Jacketing** | Carbon (CFRP) / Glass (GFRP) / Aramid (AFRP) | Flexure (soffit) / Shear (U-wrap) | 20–100 % |
| **Hybrid Jacketing** | Steel + FRP overlay | Combined flexure-shear | Up to 120 % |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Casting a Second Skeleton" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine an elderly person who has lost bone density (osteoporosis).** Doctors do not replace the skeleton — they surgically wrap a supporting brace, splint, or even add external metal plates *around* the weakened bone so that the bone and the brace share the load. The original bone is preserved; the new "jacket" carries part of the stress and prevents further crack propagation.
>
> **A damaged/under-reinforced concrete beam is treated in exactly the same way.** The original beam is the "weakened bone," and the new jacket (concrete, steel, or FRP wrap) is the "external brace." Once the jacket hardens/bonds, the old beam and the new jacket deform *together* as a single, deeper, stronger monolithic section. This composite behaviour is the heart of beam jacketing.

### Real-World Applications
- **Seismic Retrofitting** of pre-code (pre-1970s) buildings in Kerala's seismic Zone III.
- **Load Upgrade** of beams when a floor is converted from residential (2 kN/m²) to library/IT server (8–10 kN/m²) loadings.
- **Fire-Damaged Beam Restoration** in industrial RCC framed structures.
- **Corrosion-Damaged Beam Rehabilitation** in coastal Kerala buildings (chloride attack on stirrups).

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Minimum jacket thickness (concrete):** **50 mm** on each face (IS 15988:2013, Cl. 6.4).
- **Minimum jacket thickness (FRP):** **1.0 mm** per ply (typical 1.2–1.4 mm for CFRP).
- **Target design bond strength:** **τbd ≥ 1.5 MPa** at the old-new concrete interface.
- **Characteristic compressive strength of jacketing micro-concrete:** **M30 to M60** (commonly **M40**).
- **Design ultimate temperature for epoxy cure:** **≥ 23 °C** (cold-weather epoxy requires ≥ 10 °C).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Jacketing

A beam fails in service when the **demand (Mu,d / Vu,d)** exceeds the **capacity (Mu,c / Vu,c)**. Beam jacketing is a *passive* strengthening strategy that **increases the capacity side** of the inequality:

$$M_{u,d} \leq M_{u,c}^{\,\text{enlarged section}}$$

$$V_{u,d} \leq V_{u,c}^{\,\text{enlarged section}}$$

The technique rests on **three cardinal engineering assumptions**:

1. **Composite Action (Strain Compatibility):** The interface between the old concrete and the new jacket is assumed to transfer shear perfectly — i.e., plane sections remain plane across the enlarged section. *Reality:* This is achieved via **shear keys / dowels / epoxy bond**, never relied upon as 100 % perfect.

2. **Differential Shrinkage Compensation:** The new concrete jacket shrinks as it cures. If shrinkage is unaccounted for, the jacket can *peel off* from the parent beam. *Mitigation:* Use **shrinkage-compensating cement (IS 8042)** or **polymer-modified mortar (SBR/latex)** with **wet curing ≥ 7 days**.

3. **Anchorage of New Reinforcement:** The new longitudinal bars in the jacket require development length *beyond* the critical section. *This is achieved by:* **lapping with existing bars**, **anchorage into adjacent columns/joints**, or **mechanical anchors (Hilti HIT-HY 200)**.

## 2.2 Three Primary Failure Modes of a Jacketed Beam

| # | Failure Mode | Root Cause | Preventive Design Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | **Debonding of jacket at the interface** | Poor surface prep / smooth substrate | Sand-blast, roughen to ≥ 3 mm amplitude, epoxy bonding agent |
| **F-2** | **Flexural failure at the new critical section** | Under-reinforced jacket | Check Mu at the **column face** (not span centre) |
| **F-3** | **Shear failure at the jacket termination** | Jacket cut off at low-moment region | Extend jacket by **full development length (Ld) + d/2** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board valuation, the **interface shear transfer (τbd)** and the **anchorage of new longitudinal bars (Ld)** are the two most-frequently tested concepts in Part B questions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula you are likely to need** in a 14-mark Part B question on Beam Jacketing. Commit these to memory.

| # | Parameter | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Required additional moment capacity | $\Delta M_u = M_{u,\text{new}} - M_{u,\text{old}}$ | Demand − Old capacity | kN·m |
| 2 | Additional steel area (flexural jacket) | $A_{st,\text{add}} = \dfrac{\Delta M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, d_{\text{eff}}}$ | New effective depth | mm² |
| 3 | Effective depth of enlarged section | $d_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{old}} + t_j - c_c$ | Jacket thickness + cover | mm |
| 4 | Interface shear demand | $V_{u,\text{int}} = \dfrac{V_u \, S \, y}{I_b}$ | Where S = first moment, y = lever, I = moment of inertia of composite section | kN |
| 5 | Allowable interface shear (concrete-concrete) | $\tau_{bd,\text{allow}} = 0.6 \, \beta \, \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | β = roughness factor (0.6 rough, 1.0 monolithic) | MPa |
| 6 | Development length (new bars) | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \, f_y}{4 \, \tau_{bd}}$ | φ = bar diameter | mm |
| 7 | Lap length (with existing rebar) | $L_{lap} = 1.5 \, L_d$ (tension) or $1.0 \, L_d$ (compression) | As per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1 | mm |
| 8 | Shear capacity of enlarged RC section | $V_{u,c} = \tau_c \, b_w \, d + \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{sv} \, d}{s_v}$ | τc from IS 456 Table 19 | kN |
| 9 | Shear capacity of FRP U-wrap (ACI 440.2R) | $V_{f} = \dfrac{2 \, n \, t_f \, w_f \, \varepsilon_{fe} \, E_f \, d_f}{s_f}$ | n = plies, tf = ply thickness, wf = width | kN |
| 10 | Effective FRP strain (debonding-controlled) | $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004 \leq 0.75 \, \varepsilon_{fu}$ | εfu = ultimate FRP strain | — |
| 11 | Section enlargement factor (depth) | $k_d = \dfrac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{old}}}$ | Ratio of new to old overall depth | — |
| 12 | Capacity enhancement factor (flexure, concrete jacket) | $\eta_M = k_d^{\,\alpha}$ where $\alpha \approx 1.8$–$2.1$ | Empirical from tests | — |

> **Engineering Utility:** Equations 1–3 are used for **flexural jacketing design**; Equations 4–7 govern the **interface integrity and anchorage** (the most-overlooked check in practice); Equation 8 is the standard IS 456 shear check applied to the enlarged section; Equations 9–10 govern **FRP U-wrap shear strengthening**, which is the most common KTU Part B question on FRP jacketing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Flexural Capacity of a Concrete-Jacketed Beam (Doubly-Symmetric Enlargement)

### Problem Setup (KTU-style)
A simply-supported RCC beam of overall depth **D = 500 mm**, width **b = 250 mm**, effective depth **d = 450 mm**, with **3 bars of 16 mm φ at bottom (As = 603 mm²)** and **2 bars of 12 mm φ at top (As' = 226 mm²)**, using **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**, has a design moment demand of **Mu = 220 kN·m**. The original moment capacity is only **Mu,old = 150 kN·m**. The beam is to be **section-enlarged by 100 mm on the soffit and 50 mm on each side** with **M40 micro-concrete jacket** and **additional 3 bars of 16 mm φ at the new bottom layer**. Verify whether the jacketed section is safe in flexure.

### Step 1 — Quantify the Capacity Deficit

$$
\Delta M_u = M_{u,\text{new}} - M_{u,\text{old}} = 220 - 150 = 70 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> **Logic:** The jacket must "make up" this 70 kN·m shortfall. Any design that delivers ≥ 70 kN·m of additional flexural capacity is acceptable.

### Step 2 — Establish the New Effective Depth

$$
D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} + t_{j,\text{soffit}} = 500 + 100 = 600 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
b_{\text{new}} = b_{\text{old}} + 2 \, t_{j,\text{side}} = 250 + 2(50) = 350 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
c_c = 50 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{clear cover of new bars})
$$

$$
d_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{new}} - c_c = 600 - 50 = 550 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the New Tension Steel Area

The new layer of 3–16 mm φ bars is added *below* the existing 3–16 mm φ bars. The centroid of the new bar group is at 50 mm (cover) + 8 mm (half bar diameter) = **58 mm from the new soffit**.

The combined centroid of **old (3–16 φ) + new (3–16 φ)** bars from the new soffit:

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{3 \times (100 + 8) + 3 \times (50 + 8)}{3 + 3} = \dfrac{3(108) + 3(58)}{6} = \dfrac{324 + 174}{6} = 83 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the effective depth of the **combined tension steel** from the **new top fibre**:

$$
d_{\text{eff,combined}} = 600 - 83 = 517 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Logic:** Because the new bars are added *below* the old bars, the lever arm of the combined tension steel is slightly *reduced* (from 550 to 517 mm) — a subtle point that examiners love to test.

### Step 4 — Total Tension Steel Area in Jacketed Section

$$
A_{st,\text{total}} = A_{st,\text{old}} + A_{st,\text{new}} = 603 + 603 = 1206 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Depth of Neutral Axis (Limit-State Method, IS 456 Cl. 38.1)

Equating compression and tension forces at ultimate state (assume rectangular stress block for fck = 40 MPa):

$$
0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b_{\text{new}} \, x_u + 0.446 \, f_{ck} \, (b_{\text{new}} - b_w) \, (x_u - D_f) = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st,\text{total}}
$$

Since this is a doubly-symmetric enlargement (bw = 250 mm flange, 350 mm full width), we iterate. Assume **xu = 200 mm** (less than flange thickness Df = 500 + 50 = 550 mm; hence the **section behaves as a rectangular beam of width 350 mm**).

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 350 \times 200 = 1{,}008{,}000 \text{ N} = 1008 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1206 = 435{,}927 \text{ N} \approx 435.93 \text{ kN}
$$

Since **C > T**, xu < 200 mm. Recompute with xu = 175 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 350 \times 175 = 882{,}000 \text{ N} = 882 \text{ kN}
$$

Still C > T. Try xu = 150 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 350 \times 150 = 756{,}000 \text{ N} = 756 \text{ kN}
$$

Closer. Try xu = 140 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 350 \times 140 = 705{,}600 \text{ N} \approx 705.6 \text{ kN}
$$

Try xu = 145 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 350 \times 145 = 730{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 730.8 \text{ kN}
$$

Interpolating for T = 435.93 kN:

$$
x_u \approx 145 - 5 \times \dfrac{730.8 - 435.93}{730.8 - 705.6} = 145 - 5 \times \dfrac{294.87}{25.2} \approx 145 - 58.5 \approx 86.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Section is Under-Reinforced (IS 456 Cl. 38.1, Xu,lim)

For **Fe415** and **M40**:

$$
x_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.48 \, d_{\text{eff,combined}} = 0.48 \times 517 = 248.16 \text{ mm}
$$

Since **xu = 86.5 mm < 248.16 mm**, the section is **under-reinforced (ductile)** — *safe per IS 456*.

### Step 7 — Compute the Moment of Resistance of the Jacketed Section

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st,\text{total}} \left( d_{\text{eff,combined}} - \dfrac{x_u}{2} \right)
$$

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1206 \times \left( 517 - \dfrac{86.5}{2} \right) \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 435{,}927 \times (517 - 43.25) = 435{,}927 \times 473.75
$$

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 2.065 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 206.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 8 — Decision

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 206.5 \text{ kN·m} \;\; < \;\; M_{u,\text{demand}} = 220 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The proposed jacketing is **UNSAFE** by 13.5 kN·m (≈ 6.1 % deficit). Two remedies exist:
> 1. **Increase jacket depth** to 150 mm (i.e., Dnew = 650 mm) → re-derive, OR
> 2. **Add one more 16 mm φ bar** in the new layer (i.e., 4–16 φ new, total At = 1407 mm²) → re-derive.
>
> Either remedy will push Mujacketed above 220 kN·m. **This kind of "design does not converge on the first iteration" is the most common KTU 14-mark question pattern — students are expected to *comment on the inadequacy and propose a corrective redesign*.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — FRP U-Wrap Shear Strengthening (ACI 440.2R-17)

### Problem Setup
The same beam (bw = 250 mm, d = 450 mm) is in a region where the factored shear demand is **Vu = 280 kN**, while the existing shear capacity is **Vc + Vs = 180 kN**. We will use **CFRP U-wraps** with the following properties:
- Width per wrap: **wf = 200 mm**
- Centre-to-centre spacing: **sf = 150 mm**
- Ply thickness: **tf = 1.2 mm**
- Number of plies: **n = 2**
- Elastic modulus: **Ef = 165 GPa**
- Ultimate strain: **εfu = 0.017**
- FRP effective depth: **df = 0.9 × d = 405 mm**

### Step 1 — Effective FRP Strain (Debonding-Controlled)

$$
\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004 \leq 0.75 \, \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.75 \times 0.017 = 0.01275
$$

Governing value: **εfe = 0.004** (the debonding limit governs — typical for side-bonded U-wraps).

### Step 2 — FRP Contribution to Shear Capacity (ACI 440.2R Eq. 10-4)

$$
V_f = \dfrac{2 \, n \, t_f \, w_f \, \varepsilon_{fe} \, E_f \, d_f}{s_f}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 1.2 \times 200 \times 0.004 \times 165{,}000 \times 405}{150}
$$

Compute the numerator step-by-step:

$$
2 \times 2 \times 1.2 \times 200 = 960
$$

$$
960 \times 0.004 = 3.84
$$

$$
3.84 \times 165{,}000 = 633{,}600
$$

$$
633{,}600 \times 405 = 2.566 \times 10^8
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{2.566 \times 10^8}{150} = 1{,}710{,}672 \text{ N} \approx 1710.7 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 3 — Check Against ACI 440.2R Upper Limit (Vƒ ≤ 0.66 Vn limit is implicit; here check FRP contribution ratio)

The shear-strengthening contribution should not exceed the concrete + steel capacity:

$$
V_f \leq V_{c} + V_{s}
$$

Here Vƒ = 1710.7 kN is **drastically** higher than the demand of 100 kN — meaning **even a single-ply U-wrap at 300 mm spacing** would suffice. This is the conservative, design-side reality.

> **Practical Design:** Use **n = 1, sf = 300 mm** → Vƒ ≈ 855 kN, which is more than adequate. The derivation above proves that FRP is *enormously efficient* for shear strengthening — a typical 2-ply CFRP U-wrap on a 250 mm wide beam can deliver **> 1000 kN** of shear capacity, often 5–8× the demand.

### Step 4 — Final Check — Total Shear Capacity

$$
V_n = V_c + V_s + V_f = 180 + 1710.7 = 1890.7 \text{ kN} \;\; \gg \;\; V_u = 280 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The proposed U-wrap is *grossly conservative*. In a real design, one would either reduce plies or increase spacing — but the **calculation method is what KTU examiners test**, not the optimisation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Jacketing Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Damaged or Under-Strength Beam Identified] --> B{What is the Governing Deficiency?}
    B -- Flexural Mu deficient --> C{What is the Headroom and Access?}
    C -- Headroom > 100 mm and Access on 3 sides --> D[Concrete Jacketing on Soffit + 2 Sides]
    C -- Minimal headroom and soffit-only access --> E[Steel Plate Bonded on Soffit with Epoxy + Bolts]
    C -- No headroom and strict weight limit --> F[NSM Near Surface Mounted CFRP Strips]
    B -- Shear Vu deficient --> G{Beam Geometry?}
    G -- Rectangular, all 4 sides accessible --> H[CFRP U-Wrap Continuous or Discontinuous]
    G -- T-Beam with flange --> I[CFRP U-Wrap Sides Only, No Top]
    G -- Deep beams, aD ratio less than 1 --> J[Steel Plate Stirrups Welded to Soffit Plate]
    B -- Both Mu and Vu deficient --> K[Hybrid Jacket - Steel Plates on Soffit and CFRP U-Wrap on Sides]
    D --> L[Check Interface Bonding and Anchorage Ld]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Verification Passes Mu and Vu Demands?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Issue Construction Drawings - IS 15988]
    M -- No --> O[Iterate - Increase Jacket Thickness or Rebar Area]
    O --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Composite Action Mechanism in a Jacketed Beam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OldBeam[Existing Parent Beam - Pre 1970s Concrete]
        OB1[Original Top Reinforcement]
        OB2[Original Bottom Reinforcement]
        OB3[Original Concrete M15 to M20]
        OB4[Original Stirrups - Often Corroded]
    end
    subgraph Interface[Interface Zone - Critical for Composite Action]
        IZ1[Sandblasted Substrate Amplitude over 3 mm]
        IZ2[Epoxy Bonding Agent or SBR Latex]
        IZ3[Shear Keys / Dowels 12 mm phi at 300 mm c/c]
    end
    subgraph NewJacket[New Strengthening Jacket]
        NJ1[Additional Longitudinal Bars 16 to 25 mm phi]
        NJ2[New Stirrups 8 to 12 mm phi at 100 to 150 mm c/c]
        NJ3[Micro-Concrete or Polymer Mortar M40 to M60]
        NJ4[Optional CFRP Overlay 1.2 mm for Flexure]
    end
    OB3 --- IZ1
    IZ1 --- IZ2
    IZ2 --- IZ3
    IZ3 --- NJ3
    OB1 --> NJ1
    OB2 --> NJ1
    OB4 --> NJ2
    NJ1 --> NJ3
    NJ2 --> NJ3
    NJ3 --> NJ4
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Cross-Section of a Jacketed Beam (for sketch-paper reproduction in KTU exams)

```
                    ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Original top
                    │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │     deck
        Original    │  ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ──   │  ← Old top bars
        Beam        │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
        500 mm      │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
        deep        │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
                    │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
                    │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │  ← New top bars
        ────────────┤ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │──────────  ← Interface zone
        New jacket  │ ████████████████████████████████████ │   50 mm  ← Side jacket (each side)
        (soffit     │  ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ── ──   │  ← New bottom bars
        100 mm)     │  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱  ╲╱    │  ← New stirrups
                    └────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      ↑                              ↑
                      250 mm                         350 mm
                      Original width                 New total width
```

**Legend:** ░ Old concrete (M20)  █ New jacket (M40 micro-concrete)  ▓ Interface (epoxy + roughened)  ── Bars  ╲╱ Stirrups

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET634]
**Differentiate between "Concrete Jacketing" and "FRP Jacketing" of beams, citing two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Concrete Jacketing | FRP Jacketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Micro-concrete (M40–M60) + rebar | CFRP/GFRP fabric or laminates |
| **Thickness required** | ≥ 50 mm on each face | 1.0–1.4 mm per ply |
| **Self-weight added** | Significant (≈ 1.2–1.5 kN/m per face) | Negligible (≈ 0.02 kN/m²) |
| **Construction time** | 7–14 days (formwork, curing) | 1–3 days (no formwork) |
| **Durability** | Excellent (50+ years) | Good (20–30 years, UV-sensitive) |
| **Fire resistance** | Excellent | Poor (epoxy softens at 65 °C; needs fire-protection) |

**Advantage of Concrete:** High ductility, can be designed to any shape, low cost. **Disadvantage:** Adds dead load, requires curing, increases member size. **Advantage of FRP:** No size increase, rapid installation, corrosion-proof. **Disadvantage:** Brittle failure (no warning), fire-sensitive, expensive.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET634]
**List any three critical "interface preparation" requirements that govern the success of beam jacketing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Substrate Roughening:** The existing concrete surface must be mechanically roughened (chipping, sandblasting, needle-scaling) to expose aggregate and achieve an amplitude of **at least 3 mm** (IS 15988:2013). Smooth substrates cause debonding.

2. **Cleaning and Priming:** Loose particles, laitance, oil, and dust must be removed via high-pressure water jet or compressed air. An **epoxy bonding agent (e.g., Sikadur 32 or Fosroc Nitobond EP)** must be applied while tacky.

3. **Dowel Anchors:** **12 mm φ dowels** must be grouted at **300 mm c/c** into the parent beam to transfer shear across the interface. Dowels must be embedded to a depth of **≥ 100 mm** with chemical anchors (Hilti HY-200 or equivalent).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — Design of Concrete Beam Jacketing

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks, CO4 / Bloom Level: Apply–Analyse]**
>
> An existing simply-supported RCC beam of **clear span 6 m**, width **b = 300 mm**, overall depth **D = 550 mm**, effective depth **d = 500 mm**, is reinforced with **4 bars of 20 mm φ at the bottom (As = 1256 mm²)** and **2 bars of 16 mm φ at the top (As' = 402 mm²)** in **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. The live load on the beam has increased such that the new factored moment demand is **Mu = 320 kN·m**, while the original moment capacity of the beam is **Mu,old = 180 kN·m**.
>
> **Design a concrete jacket** of **M40 grade** with additional **Fe415 reinforcement** to restore the required flexural capacity. The jacket is to be **75 mm thick on the soffit** and **40 mm on each side**. Use **IS 456:2000** limit-state method. Verify the new section is safe in flexure.

#### (a) Step-by-Step Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute new geometry of the jacketed section**

$$
D_{\text{new}} = 550 + 75 = 625 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
b_{\text{new}} = 300 + 2(40) = 380 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Position of combined tension steel (assume new 2–20 mm φ bars are added below the existing 4–20 mm φ bars)**

The centroid of **old** 4–20 mm φ bars from the old soffit is at 50 mm (cover) + 10 mm (half bar) = **60 mm**.
The centroid of **new** 2–20 mm φ bars from the new soffit is at 50 mm (cover) + 10 mm = **60 mm**.
The centroid of new bars from the **old soffit** is at 75 mm (jacket thickness) + 60 mm = **135 mm**.

Combined centroid of tension steel from the **old soffit**:

$$
\bar{y}_{\text{from old soffit}} = \dfrac{4 \times 60 + 2 \times 135}{6} = \dfrac{240 + 270}{6} = 85 \text{ mm}
$$

Effective depth of combined tension steel from the **new top fibre**:

$$
d_{\text{eff}} = 625 - 85 = 540 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tension steel area**

$$
A_{st,\text{total}} = 1256 + 628 = 1884 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Depth of neutral axis**

Assume xu = 200 mm (rectangular behaviour since xu < Df):

$$
C = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b_{\text{new}} \, x_u = 0.36 \times 40 \times 380 \times 200 = 1{,}094{,}400 \text{ N} = 1094.4 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1884 = 680{,}536 \text{ N} \approx 680.5 \text{ kN}
$$

Since C > T, xu < 200 mm. Recompute for xu = 130 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 380 \times 130 = 711{,}360 \text{ N} \approx 711.4 \text{ kN}
$$

Closer. Try xu = 124 mm:

$$
C = 0.36 \times 40 \times 380 \times 124 = 678{,}528 \text{ N} \approx 678.5 \text{ kN} \;\; (\approx T)
$$

**Therefore xu ≈ 124 mm.**

**Step 5 — Verify under-reinforced (xu < xu,lim)**

For Fe415: xu,lim = 0.48 × d = 0.48 × 540 = **259.2 mm**. Since 124 < 259.2, section is ductile — **safe per IS 456**.

**Step 6 — Moment of resistance of the jacketed section**

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = T \left( d_{\text{eff}} - \dfrac{x_u}{2} \right)
$$

$$
M_{u,\text{jacketed}} = 680.5 \times \left( 540 - 62 \right) = 680.5 \times 478 = 325{,}279 \text{ kN·mm} = 325.3 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Decision:** Mujacketed = 325.3 kN·m > Mudemand = 320 kN·m. **Section is SAFE in flexure by 5.3 kN·m (1.7 % margin).**

#### (b) Interface Shear and Anchorage Check [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the section properties of the transformed (composite) section**

For simplicity, assume the original and new concrete both transform with modular ratio m = 280/(3 × σcbc) — using the simplified IS 456 approach, treat the composite section as monolithic since both concretes are similarly aged.

**Step 2 — Compute interface shear stress at the column face**

The maximum interface shear occurs at the **column face (highest Vu)**. Assuming the beam is loaded with a UDL giving Vu,max = 200 kN at the face:

The first moment of area of the **jacket (below the interface)** about the neutral axis of the composite section:

- Area of jacket in tension zone below interface: $A = 380 \times 75 = 28{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$
- Centroid of this area from the new top fibre: $y_{bar} = 625 - 75/2 = 587.5 \text{ mm}$
- Distance from neutral axis: $\bar{y} = 587.5 - (625 - 124) = 587.5 - 501 = 86.5 \text{ mm}$

Moment of inertia of composite section (approximate, using the new section's bw = 380 mm):

$$
I_b \approx \dfrac{b_{\text{new}} \, d_{\text{eff}}^3}{12} + \text{steel correction} \approx \dfrac{380 \times 540^3}{12} = 4.99 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Interface shear stress (ACI 318 / IS 15988 approach):

$$
\tau_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{V_u \, S}{I \, b_w}
$$

$$
\tau_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{200{,}000 \times (28{,}500 \times 86.5)}{4.99 \times 10^9 \times 380} = \dfrac{200{,}000 \times 2{,}465{,}250}{1.896 \times 10^{12}} = 0.26 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with allowable interface shear**

For roughened interface (β = 0.6) with fck = 40 MPa (new) and fck,old = 20 MPa, use the lesser:

$$
\tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.6 \times 0.6 \times \sqrt{20} = 0.36 \times 4.47 = 1.61 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Since τint = 0.26 MPa < τallow = 1.61 MPa, the interface is SAFE.**

**Step 4 — Anchorage of new 20 mm φ bars (development length)**

For Fe415 in M40, τbd = 1.9 MPa (IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.1):

$$
L_d = \dfrac{\phi \, f_y}{4 \, \tau_{bd}} = \dfrac{20 \times 415}{4 \times 1.9} = 1092 \text{ mm} \approx 1100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Lap with existing bars**

Provide lap length of **1.5 × Ld = 1.5 × 1100 = 1650 mm** between the new 20 mm φ bars and the **continuation bars extending from the column** (since the original beam bars may not extend into the jacket). Use **Hilti HIT-HY 200 chemical anchors** at the column face if lap is not feasible.

**Final Decision:** Jacketing design is **safe in flexure, interface shear, and anchorage**. Construction specifications: 75 mm soffit jacket with 2–20 mm φ Fe415 + 8 mm φ stirrups at 125 mm c/c, dowel anchors 12 mm φ at 300 mm c/c, M40 micro-concrete with 7-day wet curing, epoxy bonding agent at interface.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — FRP Shear Strengthening of Beam

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks, CO4 / Bloom Level: Apply–Analyse]**
>
> An existing RC beam of **bw = 250 mm, d = 500 mm, fck = 25 MPa, fy = 415 MPa** has a factored shear demand of **Vu = 220 kN** at a critical section near the support. The existing shear reinforcement consists of **2-legged 8 mm φ stirrups at 200 mm c/c** (Asv = 100 mm², sv = 200 mm).
>
> **Design a CFRP U-wrap shear strengthening** using the ACI 440.2R-17 method. Use CFRP with the following properties: **tf = 1.0 mm per ply, Ef = 165 GPa, εfu = 0.015, wf = 150 mm, n = 2 plies, sf = 200 mm c/c (centre-to-centre of strips)**. Verify the strengthened section.

#### (a) Compute Existing Shear Capacity [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Concrete contribution (IS 456 Table 19):**

For fck = 25 MPa and pt = (100 × As / (b × d)) = (100 × 1000) / (250 × 500) = **0.8 %** (assuming minimum As = 1000 mm² for illustration; if actual As is given, substitute that value).

From IS 456 Table 19, τc,max = 3.1 MPa for pt = 0.8 % and M25:

$$
V_c = \tau_c \, b \, d = 0.62 \times 250 \times 500 = 77{,}500 \text{ N} = 77.5 \text{ kN}
$$

(Note: For Pt < 0.5, τc = 0.54 MPa is conservative; for Pt = 0.75, τc = 0.59; for Pt = 1.0, τc = 0.64. We use τc = 0.62 MPa for Pt = 0.8 %.)

**Step 2 — Steel stirrup contribution:**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{sv} \, d}{s_v} = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100 \times 500}{200} = 90{,}262 \text{ N} \approx 90.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Total existing shear capacity:**

$$
V_{n,\text{old}} = V_c + V_s = 77.5 + 90.3 = 167.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Shear deficit:**

$$
\Delta V_u = V_u - V_{n,\text{old}} = 220 - 167.8 = 52.2 \text{ kN}
$$

#### (b) Design of CFRP U-Wrap and Verification [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Effective FRP strain (debonding-controlled):**

$$
\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.004 \leq 0.75 \, \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.75 \times 0.015 = 0.01125
$$

Governing: **εfe = 0.004**

**Step 2 — FRP contribution to shear (ACI 440.2R Eq. 10-4):**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{2 \, n \, t_f \, w_f \, \varepsilon_{fe} \, E_f \, d_f}{s_f}
$$

With df = 0.9 × d = 0.9 × 500 = 450 mm:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 1.0 \times 150 \times 0.004 \times 165{,}000 \times 450}{200}
$$

Compute numerator step-by-step:

$$
2 \times 2 \times 1.0 \times 150 = 600
$$

$$
600 \times 0.004 = 2.4
$$

$$
2.4 \times 165{,}000 = 396{,}000
$$

$$
396{,}000 \times 450 = 1.782 \times 10^8
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{1.782 \times 10^8}{200} = 891{,}000 \text{ N} = 891 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Total shear capacity of strengthened section:**

$$
V_n = V_c + V_s + V_f = 77.5 + 90.3 + 891 = 1058.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
V_n = 1058.8 \text{ kN} \;\; \gg \;\; V_u = 220 \text{ kN}
$$

**Section is grossly over-strengthened** — practical design would reduce plies to 1 and increase spacing to 300 mm. *However, the calculation methodology is what is being tested.*

**Step 5 — Practical design recommendation:**

Use **n = 1 ply, sf = 300 mm c/c** → Vf ≈ 594 kN, still > 220 kN. Recommended final design: **Single-ply 150 mm wide CFRP U-wrap strips at 300 mm c/c**, bonded with **Sikadur 330 epoxy**, with **50 mm overlap at the top of the U-wrap** and **fibre orientation 90° to the beam axis** (i.e., perpendicular to the longitudinal axis). Provide a **fire-protection intumescent coating (e.g., Nullifire S607, 1.5 mm DFT)** to address the 65 °C glass-transition issue of the epoxy.

**Final Decision:** Strengthening design is **safe and validated per ACI 440.2R-17**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the under-reinforced check (xu < 0.48d for Fe415):** This is a **1–2 mark deduction** if omitted. Always verify the section is ductile per IS 456 Cl. 38.1.
> 2. **Using old fck in the new section calculation:** The jacket concrete (M40) has a *different* strength from the original (M20). The IS 456 design uses the **lower of the two strengths** for the composite section in the conservative approach (some IS provisions use the average). Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring interface shear:** The 14-mark question often has a 4-mark "interface shear check" sub-part that students skip. *This is the single largest mark-loss area in beam-jacketing questions.*
> 4. **Confusing "L" (development length) with "Lap length":** Lap length = **1.5 Ld in tension** for deformed bars per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.1. Using Ld instead of 1.5 Ld costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the fire-protection note for FRP:** KTU examiners test this. A 1-line note on intumescent coating or sacrificial gypsum board earns 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing units in the final answer:** Always end with **"kN·m"** for moment and **"kN"** for shear — examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Beam Jacketing = Section Enlargement** to upgrade flexural, shear, and ductility capacity.
- [x] **Three main types:** Concrete, Steel, FRP — each with distinct advantages and limitations.
- [x] **Concrete Jacket minimum thickness = 50 mm** (IS 15988:2013).
- [x] **FRP effective strain is debonding-controlled (εfe = 0.004)** for side-bonded U-wraps (ACI 440.2R-17).
- [x] **Interface shear** must be checked: τint = V·S / (I·bw) ≤ τallow = 0.6·β·√fck.
- [x] **Development length of new bars** Ld = φfy / (4τbd) — use 1.5 Ld for tension laps.
- [x] **Composite action** is the *core assumption*; achieved via roughening, epoxy bonding, and dowel anchors.
- [x] **Differential shrinkage** must be compensated using **shrinkage-compensating cement (IS 8042)** or **polymer-modified mortar**.
- [x] **Xu,lim = 0.48 d** for Fe415 (IS 456 Cl. 38.1) — verify the jacketed section remains **under-reinforced**.
- [x] **FRP wrapping fails at 65 °C** (epoxy Tg) — fire protection is mandatory.
- [x] **Dowel anchors: 12 mm φ at 300 mm c/c**, embedded 100 mm with chemical grout.
- [x] **Wet curing ≥ 7 days** for the new concrete jacket (IS 456 Cl. 13.5).
- [x] **Sand-blast / chip the substrate** to a roughness amplitude of **≥ 3 mm** before applying epoxy.
- [x] **Always extend the jacket by Ld + d/2 beyond the critical section** to prevent termination shear failure.
- [x] **KTU's most-asked concept:** interface shear and development length — *not* the moment capacity calculation alone.
- [x] **Codes to cite:** IS 456:2000, IS 15988:2013, ACI 440.2R-17, ACI 562-19, IS 8042 (shrinkage-compensating cement).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam-Column Joint Jacketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beam-Column Joint Jacketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Beam-Column Joint Jacketing** is a localized seismic retrofitting and rehabilitation technique in which the existing reinforced concrete (RC) beam-column joint region—often the most vulnerable zone in a moment-resisting frame—is encased with an additional layer of reinforced concrete (and optionally supplementary steel plates, CFRP wraps, or headed studs) to enhance its **shear strength, flexural capacity, ductility, confinement, and anchorage performance** under reversed cyclic (seismic) loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET634, Module 4):**
> *Beam-column joint jacketing is the process of adding a reinforced concrete overlay or encasement around an existing joint core to restore or upgrade its load-carrying capacity, prevent brittle shear failure, and improve energy dissipation during seismic events.*

The **joint core** is defined as the volume of concrete within the depth of the deepest beam framing into the column. Jacketing is mandated whenever:

- The existing joint lacks transverse reinforcement (pre-1970s construction).
- The joint exhibits diagonal cracking, spalling, or bar slippage.
- Capacity-based design demands a moment or shear upgrade greater than **20 %** of the original.
- A change of occupancy or addition of floors increases the seismic demand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Cast"

Imagine a human bone that has suffered a hairline fracture near the joint of the knee. The orthopaedic surgeon does not replace the entire leg—he wraps the affected region in a **rigid, reinforced cast** that holds the fragments together, redistributes the load, and prevents the crack from spreading.

A beam-column joint behaves identically:

- The **beams** are the "femur," the **columns** are the "tibia," and the **joint** is the "knee."
- After an earthquake, the joint core develops **diagonal shear cracks** (the "hairline fracture").
- **Jacketing** is the "surgical cast"—an external RC sleeve that confines the joint, supplements missing stirrups, and re-establishes a continuous load path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why target the joint specifically?**
> Modern seismic philosophy (Capacity-Based Design) intentionally allows plastic hinges to form in **beams**, never in **columns**, and absolutely **never in joints**. If a joint fails before the beam yields, the entire collapse mechanism becomes non-ductile and catastrophic. Jacketing "reprograms" the structure to enforce this hierarchy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum jacket thickness:** **75 mm** (per IS 15988:2013 and ACI 369.1)
- **Typical jacket thickness in practice:** **75 mm – 100 mm**
- **Concrete grade for jacket:** minimum **M25** (often M30 to M40 for seismic zones IV/V)
- **Minimum clear cover in jacket:** **25 mm** for bars ≤ 12 mm, **40 mm** for larger bars
- **Effective joint width factor (k):** typically **1.0 – 1.4** depending on eccentricity
- **Coefficient of friction (μ) between old and new concrete:** assumed **0.7 – 1.0** (IS 456-based shear-friction) or **1.4λ** (ACI 318 shear-friction model)
- **Standard joint shear coefficient (γ):** **1.7** (interior joint, ACI 318-19)
- **Joint shear coefficient (exterior/corner joint):** **1.2**
- **Unit weight of jacketing concrete:** **24 kN/m³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Joint Failure — Why Jacketing Works

### 2.1.1 Force Transfer Inside a Joint

When seismic moments act on the framing beams framing into a joint, three force streams must be safely transferred:

1. **Tension (T_b)** from the top longitudinal bars of the beam on one face.
2. **Compression (C_b)** from the bottom longitudinal bars (or vice versa under reversed loading) on the opposite face.
3. **Column shear (V_c)** that equilibrates the beam moments through the joint core.

These forces generate a **principal diagonal tension** inside the joint, which—if not resisted by adequate transverse hoops—produces the classic **"X-shaped" diagonal shear cracks**.

### 2.1.2 Two Principal Failure Modes Targeted by Jacketing

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Jacketing Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint Shear Failure (Mode B)** | Diagonal tension cracks in core; concrete crushes | Add external hoops/cross-ties to carry diagonal tension |
| **Bar Anchorage / Bond Slip (Mode C)** | Beam bars pull out of joint due to insufficient embedment | Increase effective depth by extending jacket, add anchorage plates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Concept:** The hierarchy is **Beam yielding → Beam hinging → Joint shear** capacity must exceed the **overstrength flexural demand** of the beam. Jacketing is used precisely to push the joint shear capacity above this demand.

## 2.2 Types of Beam-Column Joint Jacketing

### (a) Concrete (RC) Jacketing — *Most Common in KTU Context*
- A reinforced concrete overlay is cast around the joint using dowel/anchor bars epoxied into the parent concrete.
- Provides monolithic confinement, ductility, and added flexural capacity.

### (b) Steel Jacketing
- Welded steel plates or angles encase the column and joint region.
- Used when headroom is critical; faster to install.

### (c) FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Polymer) Jacketing
- Carbon or glass fibre wraps bonded with epoxy resin.
- Lightweight, corrosion-free, but limited by fire rating.

### (d) Hybrid Jacketing
- Combines RC jacket with CFRP U-wraps or steel collars at corners.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Design Philosophy (Capacity-Based)

1. **Compute the flexural overstrength of the beam** at the joint face: $M_{b,o} = 1.25 \cdot M_y$ (using probable steel yield $f_y = 1.25 f_{yd}$).
2. **Compute the joint shear demand** from the beam and column moment couples.
3. **Compare with existing joint shear capacity** (often governed by old, deficient transverse reinforcement).
4. **Design the jacket** such that the upgraded capacity exceeds the demand by a margin of **≥ 1.4** (importance factor).
5. **Detailing:** Minimum longitudinal dowels, U-shaped cross-ties at **≤ 100 mm** spacing, anchorage length ≥ **$l_{dt}$**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-board standard; memorize them in this exact form for the 14-mark derivations.

### 2.4.1 Joint Shear Demand

For an interior joint under seismic moments $M_{b1}$ (left beam) and $M_{b2}$ (right beam):

$$V_{jh} = \frac{M_{b1} + M_{b2}}{h_b} - V_{col}$$

For an exterior joint (single beam framing):

$$V_{jh} = \frac{T_b + C_c}{1} \cdot \frac{h_c}{h_b \cdot h_c} \quad \text{or simplified as} \quad V_{jh} = \frac{M_b}{h_b}$$

where
- $M_b$ = beam moment at the column face (kN·m)
- $h_b$ = beam overall depth (mm)
- $V_{col}$ = column shear above the joint

### 2.4.2 Nominal Joint Shear Capacity (ACI 318-19 Simplified)

For an interior joint with effective joint area $A_j = b_j \cdot h$:

$$V_n = \gamma \cdot \sqrt{f_c'} \cdot A_j$$

- $\gamma = 1.7$ for interior joints confined on all four faces
- $\gamma = 1.2$ for exterior/corner joints
- $f_c'$ in MPa, $A_j$ in mm², $V_n$ in N

### 2.4.3 Effective Joint Width (ACI 318-19)

$$b_j = \min\begin{Bmatrix} b_c + h \\ b_c + 2x \\ b_b + h \end{Bmatrix}$$

where $x$ is the smaller of the beam depth minus column depth or the column depth perpendicular to the beam.

### 2.4.4 Concrete Jacket Thickness Recommendation (IS 15988)

$$t_j \geq 75 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_j \geq \frac{d_{b,\text{new}}}{2} + c_{clear} + d_{b,\text{dowel}}$$

### 2.4.5 Anchorage Length for Dowel Bars

$$L_{dt} = \frac{0.25 \cdot f_y \cdot d_b}{\sqrt{f_c'}} \quad \text{(for hooked bars, ACI 318)}$$

### 2.4.6 Shear-Friction Capacity at Old–New Interface

$$V_{nf} = \mu \cdot A_{vf} \cdot f_y$$

with $\mu = 1.4\lambda$ for concrete placed monolithically and $\mu = 1.0\lambda$ for hardened concrete with roughened surface.

### 2.4.7 Summary Table — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint shear demand | $V_{jh} = (M_{b1} + M_{b2})/h_b - V_{col}$ | ACI 318, IS 13920 |
| 2 | Nominal joint shear capacity | $V_n = \gamma \sqrt{f_c'} A_j$ | ACI 318-19 §18.8 |
| 3 | Effective joint width | $b_j = \min(b_c + h,\ b_c + 2x,\ b_b + h)$ | ACI 318-19 |
| 4 | Jacket thickness | $t_j \geq 75$ mm | IS 15988:2013 |
| 5 | Anchorage length | $L_{dt} = 0.25 f_y d_b / \sqrt{f_c'}$ | ACI 318-19 |
| 6 | Shear friction | $V_{nf} = \mu A_{vf} f_y$ | ACI 318-19 §22.9 |
| 7 | Overstrength factor | $\Omega_0 = 1.25$ (beams), $1.5$ (columns) | FEMA 547 |
| 8 | Importance factor (UBC/IBC) | $I = 1.0$ (std), $1.25$ (essential), $1.5$ (hazardous) | IS 1893, IBC |
| 9 | Concrete grade (jacket) | $\geq M25$ | IS 15988 |
| 10 | Cover in jacket | $c \geq 25$ mm (≤ 12 mm bar) | IS 456:2000 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beam-column joint jacketing is the **backbone of seismic retrofit programs** in India, Japan, Turkey, and the United States. Iconic applications include:

- **Post-2001 Bhuj earthquake** rehabilitation of school and hospital buildings in Gujarat.
- **Post-1999 Kocaeli (Turkey) earthquake** mass retrofit of ~80,000 damaged apartments.
- **California Hospital Retrofit Program** following the 1994 Northridge earthquake.
- Modern **metro rail pier retrofits** in Delhi and Bengaluru for proximity-induced vibration loading.

The technique is also used in **non-seismic strengthening** when buildings undergo vertical extensions or functional changes that overload the original joint shear capacity.
---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Joint Shear Demand for an Interior Joint

### Statement
Determine the horizontal joint shear $V_{jh}$ transferred through an interior beam-column joint when beams of equal span deliver moments $M_{b1}$ and $M_{b2}$ at the column face, and the column carries axial load $P$ with shears $V_{c1}$ (above) and $V_{c2}$ (below).

### Free-Body Diagram Concept
- The horizontal force couple from the two beams acts on the top of the joint.
- The horizontal forces from column flexure act on the bottom.
- The **net horizontal shear** through the joint core is the imbalance of these two couples divided by the beam depth.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Sum the horizontal beam forces at the top of the joint.**

From beam bending at the column face, the top bars are in tension on one side and compression on the other. The resultant couple on the top face of the joint is:

$$T_{top} + C_{top} = \frac{M_{b1} + M_{b2}}{h_b}$$

This is because each beam moment $M_b$ is internally resisted by a tension–compression couple separated by the lever arm $\approx h_b$ (approximately equal to the beam overall depth).

**Step 2 — Compute the column shear contributions.**

Equilibrium of the column segment above and below the joint gives:

$$V_{c1} = \frac{M_{c,\text{top}}}{H_1}, \qquad V_{c2} = \frac{M_{c,\text{bot}}}{H_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium of the joint panel.**

The horizontal shear through the joint core equals the sum of beam-induced horizontal forces minus the column shear components acting on the joint boundary:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{jh} &= T_{top} + C_{top} - V_{c1} - V_{c2} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{M_{b1} + M_{b2}}{h_b} - V_{col,\text{net}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification using capacity-based design.**

When the beam reaches its overstrength $M_{b,o} = 1.25 M_y$ on both sides (worst-case plastic mechanism):

$$V_{jh,\text{demand}} = \frac{2 \, M_{b,o}}{h_b}$$

The column shear is conservatively neglected because column yielding is **not permitted** (strong-column/weak-beam hierarchy).

**Step 5 — Numerical check example.**

Given: $M_{b,o} = 250$ kN·m, $h_b = 500$ mm.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{jh} &= \frac{2 \times 250}{0.500} \\[4pt]
       &= 1000 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

This **1000 kN** must be resisted by the joint core + jacket system.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nominal Shear Capacity of a Jacketed Joint

### Statement
Compute the nominal joint shear capacity $V_n$ for a jacketed interior joint with:
- Column size = **400 × 400 mm**
- Beam depth = **500 mm** (overall)
- Beam width = **300 mm**
- Jacket concrete grade **M30** ($f_c' = 30$ MPa)
- Interior joint classification.

### Step 1 — Determine the effective joint area.

Joint height (panel height) $h = 500$ mm (beam depth).
Joint effective width $b_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_j &= \min(b_c + h,\ b_c + 2x,\ b_b + h) \\[4pt]
    &= \min(400 + 500,\ 400 + 2(200),\ 300 + 500) \\[4pt]
    &= \min(900,\ 800,\ 800) = 800 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the joint area:

$$A_j = b_j \cdot h = 800 \times 500 = 400{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ACI 318-19 simplified joint shear equation.

For an interior joint, $\gamma = 1.7$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_n &= \gamma \cdot \sqrt{f_c'} \cdot A_j \\[4pt]
    &= 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{30} \cdot 400{,}000 \\[4pt]
    &= 1.7 \cdot 5.477 \cdot 400{,}000 \\[4pt]
    &= 3{,}724{,}480 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
    &\approx 3724 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with demand and check the Design Strength.

The design strength (with $\phi = 0.85$ for shear):

$$\phi V_n = 0.85 \times 3724 \approx 3165 \text{ kN}$$

Since $\phi V_n = 3165 \text{ kN} > V_{jh,\text{demand}} = 1000 \text{ kN}$, the jacketed joint is **safe**, with a **demand-to-capacity ratio (DCR) of just 0.32**, indicating a robust upgrade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always present the final ratio DCR = $V_{jh}/(\phi V_n)$ and conclude "SAFE" or "UNSAFE." Examiners expect this final verdict line.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Jacket Thickness for Anchorage Compliance

### Statement
Determine the required jacket thickness $t_j$ around a **300 × 300 mm** column where 16 mm diameter longitudinal dowels must be embedded with **25 mm clear cover** and a 12 mm transverse tie must be seated inside the jacket.

### Step

$$\begin{aligned}
t_j &= c_{clear} + d_{b,\text{transverse}} + d_{b,\text{dowel}}/2 + \text{lapping allowance} \\[4pt]
    &= 25 + 12 + 16/2 + 5 \\[4pt]
    &= 25 + 12 + 8 + 5 = 50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Since IS 15988 mandates a **minimum of 75 mm**, adopt $t_j = 75 \text{ mm}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation illustrates why **75 mm** is the practical floor—smaller thicknesses cannot simultaneously accommodate cover, ties, and dowel geometry without bar congestion.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Anchorage Length for Dowel Bars (with Worked Numbers)

Given: $f_y = 500$ MPa (Fe500), $d_b = 12$ mm, $f_c' = 30$ MPa (M30 jacket), ACI 318-19 standard hook.

### Step 1 — Compute $L_{dt}$ for a standard 90° hook:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{dt} &= \frac{0.25 \cdot f_y \cdot d_b}{\sqrt{f_c'}} \cdot \psi_e \cdot \psi_c \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{0.25 \times 500 \times 12}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{1500}{5.477} = 273.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the code minimum:

$$L_{dt} \geq 8 d_b = 8 \times 12 = 96 \text{ mm} \quad \text{and} \quad \geq 150 \text{ mm (absolute min)}$$

So adopt $L_{dt} = 274$ mm ≈ **275 mm** in design.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Joint Jacketing

| Observation in Field Survey | Diagnosis | Recommended Jacketing Type |
|---|---|---|
| Visible X-cracks, no stirrups in core | Brittle joint shear failure | Full RC jacket with cross-ties @ ≤ 100 mm |
| Beam bars pulled out of joint | Bar anchorage failure | RC jacket + U-shaped anchor plates |
| Concrete spalling at corner | Local compression failure | RC jacket + corner L-angles |
| Joint depth < 1/20 of beam span | Stiffness discontinuity | Concrete haunch + jacket |
| Corrosion of column longitudinals | Durability failure | RC jacket with corrosion-inhibiting admixture |
| Fire damage, surface charring | Heat-induced strength loss | RC jacket (minimum 75 mm) |

---
