# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q:** Define beam-column joint jacketing. List any two situations in which it becomes mandatory.

**Model Answer:**

Beam-column joint jacketing is a rehabilitation technique in which an additional reinforced concrete overlay (typically 75–100 mm thick) is provided around an existing beam-column joint to enhance its **shear capacity, confinement, ductility, and anchorage performance** under seismic or increased gravity loading.

The two mandatory situations are:
1. **Visible diagonal (X-shaped) cracks** at the joint core due to past seismic events.
2. **Inadequate or missing transverse reinforcement** in the joint region (typical of pre-1970s construction lacking seismic detailing).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q:** Mention the minimum thickness and minimum concrete grade recommended by IS 15988 for a beam-column joint jacket.

**Model Answer:**

- **Minimum jacket thickness:** **75 mm**
- **Minimum concrete grade:** **M25** (M30 recommended for seismic zones IV and V)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Selection 1*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, End Semester]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the step-by-step construction procedure for providing an RC jacket to a beam-column joint. Highlight the importance of surface preparation and dowel anchorage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An interior beam-column joint in a moment-resisting frame has the following data:
- Column size: 450 × 450 mm
- Beam size: 300 × 600 mm (overall depth 600 mm)
- Beam flexural overstrength: $M_{b,o} = 320$ kN·m at each face
- Existing joint concrete: M25 ($f_c' = 25$ MPa)
- Jacketing concrete: M30 ($f_c' = 30$ MPa)

Compute the joint shear demand and verify whether the joint requires jacketing. Use ACI 318-19 provisions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

The construction procedure is:

1. **Shutting and propping** — Provide temporary supports to relieve the joint of gravity loads during jacketing.
2. **Surface preparation** — Remove loose concrete by chipping until a sound substrate is exposed. Roughen the interface to a **6 mm amplitude** to develop shear-friction. Clean with compressed air and water jet.
3. **Dowel installation** — Drill holes (typically 14–18 mm diameter) into the existing concrete to a depth of **≥ $L_{dt}$** (≈ 275 mm for 12 mm bar, M30 concrete). Inject epoxy and insert dowel bars.
4. **Reinforcement placement** — Fix supplementary longitudinal bars (16 mm dia typical) along the column faces, and U-shaped cross-ties (10 mm dia at **≤ 100 mm c/c**) wrapping the joint.
5. **Formwork and casting** — Erect formwork ensuring **minimum 75 mm jacket thickness** and **25–40 mm cover**. Cast M30 self-compacting concrete with proper vibration.
6. **Curing** — Wet-cure for **minimum 14 days** before removing props.
7. **Quality assurance** — Conduct rebound hammer and pull-out tests on dowels; document as-built drawings.

> **Importance of Surface Preparation (2 marks):** The shear transfer between old and new concrete depends entirely on interfacial bond and shear-friction. A smooth interface leads to **debonding failure** at one-third the theoretical capacity.
>
> **Importance of Dowel Anchorage (2 marks):** Without proper anchorage, the jacket acts as a separate shell and cannot participate in carrying joint shear.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute joint shear demand.**

Beam depth $h_b = 600$ mm = 0.6 m. Both beams yield at overstrength $M_{b,o} = 320$ kN·m.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{jh} &= \frac{M_{b1} + M_{b2}}{h_b} - V_{col,\text{net}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{2 \times 320}{0.600} - 0 \quad \text{(column yielding not permitted)} \\[4pt]
       &= 1066.67 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute existing joint capacity using M25 concrete.**

Effective joint width:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_j &= \min(b_c + h,\ b_c + 2x,\ b_b + h) \\[4pt]
    &= \min(450 + 600,\ 450 + 2(150),\ 300 + 600) \\[4pt]
    &= \min(1050,\ 750,\ 900) = 750 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Joint area:

$$A_j = 750 \times 600 = 450{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

Nominal capacity (interior, $\gamma = 1.7$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{n,\text{existing}} &= 1.7 \times \sqrt{25} \times 450{,}000 \\[4pt]
                      &= 1.7 \times 5.0 \times 450{,}000 \\[4pt]
                      &= 3{,}825{,}000 \text{ N} = 3825 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Design strength: $\phi V_n = 0.85 \times 3825 = 3251$ kN.

**Step 3 — Demand-to-Capacity Ratio (DCR).**

$$\text{DCR} = \frac{V_{jh}}{\phi V_n} = \frac{1066.67}{3251} = 0.33$$

**Step 4 — Verdict.**

Since DCR = **0.33 < 1.0**, the **existing joint is adequate** in shear capacity. However, jacketing may still be mandated if:
- The column longitudinal bars show corrosion (> 15 % section loss).
- The joint lacks transverse hoops (no seismic detailing).
- Building undergoes functional change (e.g., hospital upgrade).

> **Conclusion:** *From a pure shear-capacity standpoint, jacketing is not strictly required. From a ductility/robustness standpoint, it is recommended.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Selection 2*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, End Semester]**

**(a)** Differentiate between RC jacketing, steel jacketing, and FRP jacketing of beam-column joints. State the merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the required jacket thickness and dowel anchorage for a column-beam joint to be upgraded using M30 concrete and Fe500 steel. Given:
- Column size: 300 × 300 mm
- Beam size: 250 × 500 mm
- Dowel bar diameter: 12 mm
- Transverse tie: 10 mm @ 100 mm c/c
- Required clear cover: 25 mm

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

| Aspect | RC Jacketing | Steel Jacketing | FRP Jacketing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Concrete + rebars | Steel plates / angles | Carbon/glass fibre + epoxy |
| **Thickness** | 75–100 mm | 6–12 mm | 1–3 mm (multiple plies) |
| **Capacity gain** | High (shear, flexure, ductility) | High shear; moderate flexure | Moderate shear; poor flexure |
| **Headroom loss** | Significant | Minimal | Negligible |
| **Construction time** | Slow (curing) | Fast (welding) | Fastest |
| **Fire resistance** | Excellent | Good (with fireproofing) | Poor (epoxy degrades at 80 °C) |
| **Durability** | Excellent | Corrosion prone | Excellent |
| **Cost (relative)** | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Best use** | Severe damage, low budget | Industrial retrofits | Heritage / aesthetic zones |

**Merits of RC Jacketing (2 marks):** monolithic behaviour, restores full flexural + shear + ductile capacity, durable, low-tech labour, code-compliant.
**Demerits of RC Jacketing (2 marks):** increases member size (headroom loss), adds dead load, requires curing time, may disturb occupancy.
**Comparison statement (1 mark):** RC jacketing remains the **preferred choice** for KTU contexts because of its compliance with IS 15988 and proven seismic performance in Indian retrofits.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute jacket thickness.**

Required thickness must simultaneously satisfy cover, tie diameter, and dowel half-diameter:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_j &= c_{cover} + d_{tie} + d_{dowel}/2 + \text{lap tolerance} \\[4pt]
    &= 25 + 10 + 12/2 + 5 \\[4pt]
    &= 25 + 10 + 6 + 5 = 46 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Since IS 15988 mandates a **minimum of 75 mm**, the **final jacket thickness** is:

$$\boxed{t_j = 75 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute anchorage length $L_{dt}$ for 12 mm Fe500 dowels in M30 concrete.**

Using ACI 318-19 formula for standard 90° hook:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{dt} &= \frac{0.25 \cdot f_y \cdot d_b}{\sqrt{f_c'}} \cdot \psi_e \cdot \psi_c \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{0.25 \times 500 \times 12}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{1500}{5.477} \approx 273.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Check code minimums.**

$$L_{dt,\min} = \max(8 d_b,\ 150 \text{ mm}) = \max(96,\ 150) = 150 \text{ mm}$$

Since $273.9 \text{ mm} > 150 \text{ mm}$, adopt:

$$\boxed{L_{dt} = 275 \text{ mm (rounded up to nearest 25 mm)}}$$

**Step 4 — Final detailing summary (for 7 marks completion).**

| Detail | Final Value |
|---|---|
| Jacket thickness $t_j$ | 75 mm |
| Concrete grade | M30 |
| Longitudinal dowels | 12 mm dia @ 200 mm c/c |
| Cross-ties | 10 mm dia @ 100 mm c/c |
| Anchorage length | 275 mm (90° hook) |
| Clear cover | 25 mm |
| Final column dimension | 300 + 2(75) = **450 × 450 mm** |
| Final beam dimension | 250 + 2(75) = **400 × 650 mm** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Beam-Column Joint Jacketing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the φ factor (0.85)** when comparing $V_{jh}$ with $V_n$. Always use $\phi V_n$, not $V_n$. This mistake alone can cost **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Neglecting the column shear term** in the demand equation. Examiners specifically look for the complete form $V_{jh} = (M_{b1}+M_{b2})/h_b - V_{col}$.
> 3. **Using $f_{ck}$ instead of $f_c'$** in the ACI formula. Remember: $f_c'$ (MPa) = $f_{ck}$ (N/mm²). Many students mix units.
> 4. **Omitting the minimum 75 mm jacket thickness** even when the computed value is smaller. Always cap with the IS 15988 minimum.
> 5. **Not concluding with a DCR verdict.** Every 14-mark shear verification problem demands an explicit "SAFE / UNSAFE" conclusion line.
> 6. **Confusing effective joint width with column width.** Use $b_j = \min(b_c+h,\ b_c+2x,\ b_b+h)$, **not** $b_c$.
> 7. **Forgetting hook/anchorage minimums.** The calculated $L_{dt}$ must be checked against $8 d_b$ and 150 mm absolute minimums.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Beam-Column Joint Jacketing**

- [ ] Jacketing adds an RC overlay (≥ **75 mm**, M25 minimum) around the joint to improve **shear, ductility, anchorage**.
- [ ] Joint is the most critical region; failure is **brittle (X-cracks)** and must be prevented.
- [ ] Two main failure modes targeted: **joint shear (B)** and **bar pull-out/anchorage (C)**.
- [ ] Capacity-based design uses **$M_{b,o} = 1.25 M_y$** (probable yield).
- [ ] Demand formula (interior): $V_{jh} = (M_{b1}+M_{b2})/h_b - V_{col}$.
- [ ] Capacity (ACI 318): $V_n = \gamma \sqrt{f_c'} A_j$; $\gamma = 1.7$ (interior), $1.2$ (exterior).
- [ ] Effective joint width: $b_j = \min(b_c + h,\ b_c + 2x,\ b_b + h)$.
- [ ] Always report DCR and conclude SAFE / UNSAFE.
- [ ] Jacket detailing: dowels at ≤ 200 mm c/c, cross-ties at ≤ 100 mm c/c.
- [ ] Anchorage: $L_{dt} = 0.25 f_y d_b / \sqrt{f_c'}$, capped by $8 d_b$ and 150 mm.
- [ ] Surface roughening to **6 mm amplitude** is non-negotiable.
- [ ] Cure minimum **14 days** before de-propping.
- [ ] Shear-friction: $V_{nf} = \mu A_{vf} f_y$ governs interface design.
- [ ] Three jacketing types: **RC**, **steel**, **FRP** — pick by headroom, fire, cost constraints.
- [ ] IS 15988:2013 governs Indian seismic retrofit guidelines.
- [ ] Column yielding is **never permitted**; jacketing enforces **strong-column / weak-beam**.
- [ ] Always check cover (25 mm for ≤ 12 mm bar; 40 mm otherwise) inside the jacket.
- [ ] Document with **as-built drawings**, pull-out tests, and rebound hammer reports.
- [ ] Ductility upgrade is often the **real purpose** of jacketing, even when shear capacity is nominally adequate.
- [ ] Use **self-compacting concrete (SCC)** in jacket to ensure densification around congested ties.
- [ ] Remember φ = **0.85** for shear design (ACI 318-19).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Reinforced Concrete Jacketing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reinforced Concrete (RC) Jacketing** is a passive structural strengthening and rehabilitation technique in which a new layer of reinforced concrete — comprising additional longitudinal steel, transverse stirrups/ties, and fresh concrete — is cast monolithically around an existing structural member (column, beam, beam-column joint, or slab) to enhance its load-carrying capacity, stiffness, ductility, and seismic performance. The technique is formally categorized under the "Section Enlargement" or "Member Enlargement" family of retrofitting methods in the **IS 15988:2013 (Seismic Evaluation and Strengthening of Existing Reinforced Concrete Buildings — Guidelines)** and the **ACI 562-19 (Code Requirements for Assessment, Repair, and Rehabilitation of Existing Concrete Structures)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** RC Jacketing is a method of strengthening distressed or under-strength reinforced concrete members by encasing them in a new reinforced concrete shell, thereby increasing the effective cross-section, axial load capacity, flexural capacity, and shear capacity, while ensuring monolithic composite action through proper surface preparation, shear connectors, and bond at the concrete–concrete interface.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Splint for a Broken Bone"

Imagine a person who has suffered a fracture in their arm. A doctor does not replace the bone; instead, a **plaster cast (splint)** is wrapped around the broken limb. This cast:
- Holds the original bone in correct alignment,
- Shares the load during healing,
- Provides extra thickness and reinforcement (fiberglass/Plaster of Paris), and
- Bonds to the existing limb so it acts as a single combined unit.

**RC Jacketing works on exactly the same principle**:
- The **existing RC member** = the broken bone (original structural element with reduced capacity due to ageing, damage, or change in use).
- The **new concrete + steel cage** = the plaster cast (additional structural skin that provides fresh capacity).
- The **shear keys, dowels, and epoxy bond at the interface** = the adhesive layer that makes the "old + new" act as a single combined section.

| Term in Jacketing | Real-World Analogy (Splint for Bone) |
|---|---|
| Existing distressed column | Fractured bone |
| Additional longitudinal bars | Steel rod embedded inside the cast |
| New concrete jacket | Plaster of Paris outer shell |
| Epoxy/adhesive bonding agent | Adhesive between bone and cast |
| Shear dowels/stirrups | Stitches holding the cast in place |

---

## 1.3 Why is RC Jacketing Required? — Typical Trigger Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "List four situations that necessitate RC Jacketing of a column."

- **Increase in design loads** due to addition of floors, change of occupancy (warehouse → hospital), or installation of heavy equipment (transformers, chillers, servers).
- **Seismic retrofitting** of pre-code (pre-1970s) buildings that lack adequate ductility and confinement as per modern **IS 1893:2016** and **IS 13920:2016** standards.
- **Restoration of fire-damaged, corrosion-damaged, or impact-damaged** structural members.
- **Correction of design/construction defects** such as inadequate steel, poor concrete quality, or sub-standard lap lengths.
- **Strengthening of beam-column joints** that are vulnerable to brittle shear failure.

---

## 1.4 Typical Applications & Member Types

| Member Type | Purpose of Jacketing | Typical Jacket Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Column** | Enhance axial load + flexural capacity; improve confinement | **50 mm – 100 mm** per side |
| **Beam** | Increase flexural and shear capacity; repair corrosion | **50 mm – 75 mm** at soffit/sides |
| **Beam-Column Joint** | Prevent brittle joint shear failure | **75 mm – 150 mm** at joint |
| **Slab** | Restore lost cover due to corrosion; increase flexural capacity | **25 mm – 50 mm** top |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU valuation mistake: Students often confuse **RC Jacketing** with **FRP Wrapping**. The key distinction — **FRP uses fibre-reinforced polymers (carbon/glass) bonded externally with epoxy; RC Jacketing uses traditional rebar + concrete cast in formwork**. They are fundamentally different in materials, design philosophy, and execution.

---

## 1.5 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages:**
- Significant enhancement in **axial, flexural, and shear capacity** (often 30%–100% increase).
- Improves **ductility and confinement**, especially for seismic zones.
- Uses **conventional materials** (cement, sand, aggregate, rebar) — locally available and economical.
- Long-proven track record in Indian construction practice.
- Compatible with **IS 456:2000**, **IS 15988:2013**, and **ACI 562-19** design guidelines.

**Limitations:**
- **Increases member size and self-weight** — may affect architectural clearances and foundation loads.
- **Labour-intensive** — requires chipping, welding, formwork, and curing.
- **Difficult to execute in occupied buildings** (vibration, dust, noise).
- Requires **shutting down or propping** the member during execution.
- Bond at the **old-new concrete interface** is the weakest link — must be carefully designed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

The success of RC Jacketing is governed by **composite action** — the ability of the new concrete and the old concrete to deform together as a single monolithic section. This composite action is achieved through a sequence of carefully executed steps, each of which has a specific engineering rationale.

### Step 1 — Structural Assessment & Design Verification
Before any retrofitting, the existing member is assessed for residual strength using:
- **Non-destructive testing (NDT):** Rebound Hammer (IS 13311 Part 2), Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (IS 13311 Part 1).
- **Destructive testing:** Core sampling and compressive testing.
- **Load testing** for verification of safe residual capacity.

The engineer then decides the **target capacity** $P_{u,\text{target}}$ that the jacketed member must achieve, and sizes the jacket accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** $P_{u,\text{target}} = P_{u,\text{existing}} + P_{u,\text{jacket}}$. The jacket is designed only for the **incremental load** it must carry, since the existing member is assumed to retain its original capacity.

### Step 2 — Propping and Load Transfer
The member is **propped** (supported by temporary shoring) so that the dead load and a portion of the live load are transferred to the shoring system. This minimizes stress in the existing member during jacketing and prevents cracking of the new concrete due to differential shrinkage and deflection.

### Step 3 — Surface Preparation of Existing Concrete
The existing concrete surface is chipped, roughened, or sandblasted to:
- Remove **laitance, dust, grease, and weak cover concrete**.
- Expose **clean, sound aggregate**.
- Achieve a surface roughness of approximately **3 mm – 5 mm amplitude** to enhance mechanical interlock.

This is the **most critical step** — research shows that **over 60% of jacketing failures initiate at the old-new interface** due to poor surface preparation.

### Step 4 — Installation of Additional Reinforcement
- **Longitudinal bars** (typically 12 mm – 25 mm diameter, Fe 415 or Fe 500 grade) are placed around the perimeter of the existing member, usually with a **clear cover of 25 mm – 40 mm**.
- **Transverse stirrups/ties** (8 mm – 12 mm diameter) are wrapped around the new longitudinal bars and anchored into the existing member through drilled holes.
- The reinforcement cage is tied and chaired to maintain proper geometry.

### Step 5 — Provision of Shear Connectors (Dowels)
Short **dowels or shear studs** (10 mm – 16 mm diameter, 100 mm – 200 mm length) are grouted into drilled holes in the existing member using **chemical anchors or non-shrink grout**. These dowels:
- Transfer **shear stress** across the interface.
- Resist **separation** between old and new concrete.
- Ensure **composite action** under load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical dowel spacing:** 200 mm – 300 mm c/c in a staggered pattern, with an embedment depth of at least **$10 \phi$ to $15 \phi$** (where $\phi$ is the dowel diameter).

### Step 6 — Application of Bonding Agent
A **polymer-modified cementitious slurry**, **epoxy bonding agent**, or **latex-modified mortar** is applied to the roughened surface just before concreting. This ensures a strong chemical bond at the interface and prevents micro-slip under load.

### Step 7 — Formwork, Concreting, and Curing
- Formwork is erected around the enlarged section.
- **Micro-concrete, high-strength grout, or self-compacting concrete (SCC)** is poured or pumped into the annular space.
- The new concrete should ideally be of grade **M25 or higher**, and its **elastic modulus should match the existing concrete** to avoid differential stiffness.
- **Curing for a minimum of 7–14 days** is essential to minimize shrinkage cracks.

### Step 8 — De-Propping and Load Testing
The shoring is removed gradually, and the jacketed member is **load-tested** (where required) to verify that the design capacity is achieved.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly aligned with IS 456:2000 and IS 15988:2013** design provisions and are the most frequently asked numerical questions in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### Table 2.1 — Design Formulas for RC Jacketed Members

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total axial capacity of jacketed column | $P_{u} = 0.4 \, f_{ck}' \, (A_{g,\text{old}} + A_{g,\text{new}}) + 0.67 \, f_{y} \, (A_{sc,\text{old}} + A_{sc,\text{new}})$ | $f_{ck}'$ in N/mm², areas in mm², $P_u$ in N |
| 2 | Incremental axial capacity from jacket | $\Delta P_{u} = 0.4 \, f_{ck,\text{new}} \, A_{g,\text{new}} + 0.67 \, f_{y,\text{new}} \, A_{sc,\text{new}}$ | The new jacket contribution alone |
| 3 | Bond stress at interface | $\tau_{\text{bond}} = \dfrac{V_{u}}{b_{\text{interface}} \cdot L_{\text{interface}}}$ | $V_u$ = factored shear, $b$ = interface width, $L$ = interface length |
| 4 | Required dowel capacity | $V_{\text{dowel}} = \dfrac{0.6 \, f_{y} \, A_{d}}{\gamma_{s}}$ | $A_d$ = area of dowel, $\gamma_s$ = partial safety factor (1.15 for steel) |
| 5 | Minimum jacket thickness (IS 15988) | $t_{\text{min}} \geq 75$ mm (column), $50$ mm (beam) | Empirical minimum to allow concrete placement |
| 6 | Minimum longitudinal steel ratio | $\rho_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{A_{sc,\text{new}}}{A_{g,\text{new}}} \geq 0.8 \, \dfrac{f_{ct}}{f_{y}}$ | As per IS 456 Cl. 9.5.1.1 |
| 7 | Lateral tie spacing in jacket | $s \leq \min(0.75 \, D, 16 \phi_l, 300 \text{ mm})$ | For seismic zones (IS 13920 Cl. 7.3) |
| 8 | Required jacket concrete grade | $f_{ck,\text{new}} \geq f_{ck,\text{old}} + 5$ N/mm² | To ensure no weak-plane exists |
| 9 | Effective slenderness ratio check | $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D_{\text{new}}} \leq 12$ (short column) | $D_{\text{new}}$ = new cross-section dimension |
| 10 | Moment capacity enhancement (beam) | $M_{u,\text{new}} = 0.87 \, f_{y} \, A_{st,\text{new}} \, d_{\text{new}} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st,\text{new}} \, f_{y}}{b_{\text{new}} \, d_{\text{new}} \, f_{ck}}\right)$ | Standard IS 456 flexural formula applied to new section |

### Table 2.2 — Empirical Material & Geometric Guidelines

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Surface roughness amplitude | $\geq$ **3 mm – 5 mm** | ACI 546R-14 |
| Dowel embedment depth | $\geq$ **10$\phi$ – 15$\phi$** | IS 15988:2013 |
| Dowel spacing | **200 mm – 300 mm c/c, staggered** | IS 15988:2013 |
| New concrete grade | $\geq$ **M25** (preferably M30) | IS 456:2000 |
| Bonding agent | Polymer-modified slurry or epoxy | ACI 562-19 |
| Curing period | $\geq$ **14 days** | IS 456:2000 Cl. 13.5 |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

RC Jacketing is the **most widely used seismic strengthening technique in India** for the following practical reasons:

- **Compatibility with local materials:** Cement, sand, aggregate, and rebar are universally available — unlike FRP, which requires imported fibres and trained applicators.
- **Cost-effective:** Approximately **₹8,000 – ₹15,000 per m²** of jacketed surface (2024 market rates), compared to ₹25,000 – ₹40,000 per m² for CFRP wrapping.
- **Familiar to local contractors:** No specialized training is required.
- **Auditable quality control:** Standard cube tests, NDT, and rebar testing can be performed using conventional equipment.
- **Suitable for Indian seismic zones III, IV, and V:** Particularly for **soft-storey retrofit** of buildings with open ground floors (a common Indian urban typology).

The technique has been used in landmark Indian projects such as the **rehabilitation of pre-code buildings in Bhuj (post-2001 earthquake)**, the **strengthening of heritage structures in Delhi**, and the **retrofitting of public schools and hospitals** under the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) guidelines.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Incremental Axial Capacity of a Jacketed Rectangular Column

### Problem Setup
A square RC column of original size $400 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm}$ with 4 nos. of 20 mm diameter bars (Fe 415) and M25 concrete is to be jacketed with a **75 mm thick** M30 concrete layer on all four sides. The jacket will have 4 nos. of 16 mm diameter longitudinal bars (Fe 415) tied with 8 mm diameter lateral ties at 200 mm c/c.

**Find:** (a) The new cross-sectional dimensions, (b) The total axial load capacity of the jacketed column, (c) The percentage increase in capacity.

### Step 1: Original Member Properties

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{old}} &= 400 \text{ mm} \\
A_{g,\text{old}} &= 400 \times 400 = 160{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{old}} &= 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 = 4 \times 314.16 = 1{,}256.6 \text{ mm}^2 \\
f_{ck,\text{old}} &= 25 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
f_{y,\text{old}} &= 415 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Jacket Member Properties

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{jacket}} &= 75 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{new}} &= D_{\text{old}} + 2 \, t_{\text{jacket}} = 400 + 2(75) = 550 \text{ mm} \\
A_{g,\text{new}} &= (D_{\text{new}})^2 = (550)^2 = 302{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{g,\text{jacket,concrete}} &= A_{g,\text{new}} - A_{g,\text{old}} = 302{,}500 - 160{,}000 = 142{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{jacket}} &= 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (16)^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 \\
f_{ck,\text{jacket}} &= 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
f_{y,\text{jacket}} &= 415 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Total Axial Capacity Using IS 456:2000 Short Column Formula (Cl. 39.3)

For a short column with minimum eccentricity, the axial capacity is given by:

$$
P_{u} = 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_{g} + 0.67 \, f_{y} \, A_{sc}
$$

**Original column capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{old}} &= 0.4 \times 25 \times 160{,}000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1{,}256.6 \\
&= 1{,}600{,}000 + 349{,}500 \\
&= 1{,}949{,}500 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{1{,}949.5 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Jacket (annular concrete) contribution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{jacket}} &= 0.4 \times 30 \times 142{,}500 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 804.25 \\
&= 1{,}710{,}000 + 223{,}519 \\
&= 1{,}933{,}519 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{1{,}933.5 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total jacketed column capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{total}} &= P_{u,\text{old}} + P_{u,\text{jacket}} \\
&= 1{,}949.5 + 1{,}933.5 \\
&= \mathbf{3{,}883.0 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Percentage Increase in Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{P_{u,\text{total}} - P_{u,\text{old}}}{P_{u,\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3{,}883.0 - 1{,}949.5}{1{,}949.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}933.5}{1{,}949.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx \mathbf{99.2\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the cross-section roughly doubles the axial capacity — a 75 mm jacket on each side of a 400 mm column is a **highly effective** strengthening intervention. However, the **self-weight of the jacket** (≈ 142,500 mm² × 25 kN/m³ × 9.81 m/s² × per metre height) must be checked against the foundation's additional load-bearing capacity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Number and Spacing of Shear Dowels

### Problem Setup
For the jacketed column above, the factored shear at the column-beam interface is $V_u = 450 \text{ kN}$. The interface perimeter (in plan) is $b_{\text{interface}} = 4 \times 550 = 2{,}200 \text{ mm}$, and the available anchorage length is $L_{\text{interface}} = 1{,}000 \text{ mm}$ (one metre height of the column).

**Find:** The required area of shear dowels and their spacing.

### Step 1: Required Bond Strength at Interface

The interface must develop sufficient shear to ensure composite action. The design interface shear stress is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{bond}} &= \frac{V_{u}}{b_{\text{interface}} \times L_{\text{interface}}} \\
&= \frac{450 \times 10^3}{2{,}200 \times 1{,}000} \\
&= \frac{450{,}000}{2{,}200{,}000} \\
&= 0.205 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this is well within the allowable bond stress for roughened surfaces ($\tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$ as per ACI 546R), mechanical dowels are needed primarily to **resist separation** (peeling) rather than to carry the full shear.

### Step 2: Shear Capacity of a Single 12 mm Dowel

Using 12 mm diameter Fe 415 dowels, the shear capacity of one dowel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{dowel}} &= \frac{0.6 \times f_{y} \times A_{d}}{\gamma_{s}} \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 415 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (12)^2}{1.15} \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 415 \times 113.1}{1.15} \\
&= \frac{28{,}155.6}{1.15} \\
&\approx 24{,}483 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{24.5 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Number of Dowels Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{V_{u}}{V_{\text{dowel}}} = \frac{450}{24.5} \approx 18.4 \rightarrow \text{Use 20 dowels}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Dowel Spacing

For 20 dowels arranged in 5 rows of 4 (one on each face of the square column), the vertical spacing is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_v &= \frac{L_{\text{interface}}}{n_{\text{rows}}} = \frac{1{,}000}{5} = 200 \text{ mm c/c}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Check:** $s_v = 200 \text{ mm} \leq s_{\max} = 0.75 \times 550 = 412.5 \text{ mm}$ → **OK**.
> Dowels are arranged in a **staggered pattern** (alternating faces) to avoid creating a weak plane.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Different Jacketing Methods

| Parameter | RC Jacketing | Steel Jacketing | FRP Wrapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity increase | **30% – 100%** | 20% – 50% | 10% – 40% |
| Ductility improvement | **Excellent** | Moderate | Limited |
| Section enlargement | **Significant (50–100 mm)** | Moderate (steel plates, 6–20 mm) | Negligible |
| Self-weight addition | **High** | Low | Negligible |
| Construction skill required | Standard | Specialized welding | Specialized FRP applicators |
| Cost (per m², 2024 India) | **₹8,000 – ₹15,000** | ₹12,000 – ₹20,000 | ₹25,000 – ₹40,000 |
| Durability (years) | **> 50** | 25 – 40 (corrosion risk) | 20 – 30 (UV/alkali degradation) |
| Suitability for seismic retrofit | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate |
| Fire resistance | **Excellent** | Good (with fireproofing) | Poor (epoxy degrades at 80°C) |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define RC Jacketing and list any two situations where it is preferred over FRP wrapping.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Reinforced Concrete Jacketing is a strengthening technique in which a new layer of reinforced concrete (additional longitudinal bars, transverse ties, and fresh concrete) is cast around an existing structural member to enhance its load-carrying capacity, stiffness, and ductility.

**Two situations where RC Jacketing is preferred over FRP:**

1. **When significant capacity enhancement (>30%) is required:** RC Jacketing can increase column capacity by 30%–100% by adding substantial cross-sectional area, while FRP wrapping is limited to 10%–40% increase in confined capacity.

2. **When fire resistance is a critical design requirement:** Concrete is inherently fire-resistant (retains strength up to 300°C–400°C), whereas the epoxy matrix in FRP degrades rapidly above **80°C**, losing its bond and strength in fire conditions. Hence, in buildings such as hospitals, schools, and high-rises, RC Jacketing is mandated.

---

### Question 2
**Q: What is the minimum recommended thickness of an RC jacket for a column and for a beam as per IS 15988:2013? Why is this minimum specified?** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

**Model Answer:**

**Minimum thicknesses:**
- **Column jacket:** **75 mm**
- **Beam jacket:** **50 mm**

**Reasons for the minimum thickness specification:**

(i) **Concrete placement and compaction:** The minimum thickness ensures that the annular space between the existing member and the formwork is wide enough to allow proper placement, vibration, and compaction of fresh concrete without leaving voids or honeycombs.

(ii) **Adequate cover to new reinforcement:** The minimum thickness provides a clear cover of at least 25 mm – 40 mm over the new longitudinal bars, protecting them from corrosion and fire.

(iii) **Bond and shear transfer:** A minimum thickness of 75 mm in columns ensures that the dowels have adequate embedment and that shear transfer across the interface is effective.

(iv) **Durability:** Thinner jackets are prone to shrinkage cracking and delamination, which can lead to premature failure of the strengthening system.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**Q: A 450 mm × 450 mm RC column with 6 nos. of 20 mm diameter longitudinal bars and 8 mm ties @ 250 mm c/c, made of M20 concrete and Fe 415 steel, is to be strengthened by RC Jacketing. The column is required to carry an additional axial load of 1,200 kN. Design the jacket using M30 concrete and Fe 415 steel. Assume the column is short and verify the design.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model | Module 4 | 14 Marks]*

#### Part (a) — Estimate the Jacket Thickness and Reinforcement [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Original Column Capacity (IS 456:2000 Cl. 39.3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{g,\text{old}} &= 450 \times 450 = 202{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{old}} &= 6 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 = 6 \times 314.16 = 1{,}884.96 \text{ mm}^2 \\
P_{u,\text{old}} &= 0.4 \times 20 \times 202{,}500 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1{,}884.96 \\
&= 1{,}620{,}000 + 524{,}517 \\
&\approx 2{,}144.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Required Jacket Capacity:**

$$
P_{u,\text{jacket,req}} = 1{,}200 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Assume jacket thickness of 100 mm and check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{new}} &= 450 + 2(100) = 650 \text{ mm} \\
A_{g,\text{new}} &= 650^2 = 422{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{g,\text{annular}} &= 422{,}500 - 202{,}500 = 220{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Concrete contribution from jacket:**

$$
P_{u,\text{conc}} = 0.4 \times 30 \times 220{,}000 = 2{,}640{,}000 \text{ N} = 2{,}640 \text{ kN}
$$

Since concrete alone provides 2,640 kN > 1,200 kN, the **minimum longitudinal steel ratio** governs.

**Step 4: Provide 4 nos. of 16 mm dia bars in the jacket (one at each corner):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sc,\text{new}} &= 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (16)^2 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\rho &= \frac{804.25}{220{,}000} = 0.366\% > 0.8 \frac{f_{ct}}{f_y} \approx 0.05\% \quad \text{OK}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Verify Lateral Ties and Slenderness [7 Marks]

**Step 5: Lateral tie design (IS 13920 for seismic detailing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &\leq \min(0.75 \times 650, 16 \times 16, 300) \\
s &\leq \min(487.5, 256, 300) \\
s &\leq 256 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \textbf{Provide 8 mm dia ties at 250 mm c/c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Effective slenderness check:**

Assume column height $L = 3$ m, effective length factor $K = 1.0$ (both ends hinged).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{eff}} &= K \times L = 1.0 \times 3{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ mm} \\
\lambda_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{L_{\text{eff}}}{D_{\text{new}}} = \frac{3{,}000}{650} = 4.61 \leq 12 \quad \text{OK (Short Column)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Total Jacketed Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{total}} &= P_{u,\text{old}} + P_{u,\text{new}} \\
&= 2{,}144.5 + (0.4 \times 30 \times 220{,}000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 804.25)/1000 \\
&= 2{,}144.5 + 2{,}640 + 223.5 \\
&= \mathbf{5{,}008 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **100 mm thick M30 jacket with 4-16 mm dia bars and 8 mm ties @ 250 mm c/c** is sufficient to carry the additional 1,200 kN and is well within short-column limits. ✅

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the step-by-step procedure of RC Jacketing for a column. Discuss the role of shear dowels, surface preparation, and bonding agents in ensuring composite action.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model | Module 4 | 14 Marks]*

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure with Sketch [7 Marks]

**The procedure consists of the following sequential steps:**

1. **Assessment and Propping:** The column is surveyed for distress (cracks, spalling, corrosion). Temporary shoring/props are installed to transfer the dead load away from the column during jacketing.

2. **Surface Preparation:** The existing concrete surface is chipped to a depth of 5–10 mm using a needle gun or by sandblasting to expose clean aggregate and achieve a roughness amplitude of 3–5 mm.

3. **Drilling and Dowel Installation:** Holes of 16 mm – 20 mm diameter are drilled 100 mm – 200 mm deep into the existing concrete. 12 mm dia Fe 415 dowels are grouted in using chemical anchors at 200 mm – 300 mm c/c in a staggered pattern.

4. **Reinforcement Placement:** Additional longitudinal bars (12 mm – 25 mm dia, Fe 415/Fe 500) are positioned around the column perimeter with 25 mm – 40 mm clear cover. These are tied with 8 mm – 12 mm dia lateral ties/hoops.

5. **Bonding Agent Application:** A polymer-modified cementitious slurry or epoxy bonding coat is brush-applied on the roughened surface to enhance chemical adhesion.

6. **Formwork and Concreting:** Formwork is erected around the enlarged section. Micro-concrete, high-strength grout, or self-compacting concrete (M25 or higher) is poured/pumped into the annular space and vibrated.

7. **Curing and De-Propping:** Water curing is carried out for at least 14 days. The shoring is removed gradually, and the column is load-tested if required.

**Neat Cross-Section Sketch (to be drawn in the exam):**

```
       650 mm (New Section)
    ┌─────────────────────┐
    │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░   │ ← New jacket concrete (M30, 100 mm thick)
    │  ░░ ┌─────────┐ ░░   │
    │  ░░ │ 450 mm  │ ░░   │ ← Original column (M25)
    │  ░░ │ (M25)   │ ░░   │
    │  ░░ │  •  •   │ ░░   │ ← Original 6 nos. 20 mm Ø bars
    │  ░░ │  •  •   │ ░░   │
    │  ░░ └─────────┘ ░░   │
    │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░   │ ← Lateral ties @ 250 mm c/c
    │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░   │
    └─────────────────────┘
       ◯ = Shear dowel (12 mm Ø) at interface
```

#### Part (b) — Role of Dowels, Surface Preparation, and Bonding Agents [7 Marks]

| Component | Role in Ensuring Composite Action |
|---|---|
| **Surface Preparation** | Removes weak laitance, exposes sound aggregate, and creates a **rough interface** that develops **mechanical interlock** between old and new concrete. Roughness of 3–5 mm is essential to mobilize friction and aggregate interlock under shear. |
| **Shear Dowels** | Resist **interface shear** (parallel to the joint) and **peeling/separation** (perpendicular to the joint). They transfer cyclic and seismic loads across the interface and prevent debonding. Typical spacing is 200–300 mm c/c with 10$\phi$ to 15$\phi$ embedment. |
| **Bonding Agent (Epoxy/Polymer Slurry)** | Provides **chemical adhesion** at the micro-level, sealing micro-cracks in the substrate and ensuring **watertight contact**. The agent prevents moisture ingress that could lead to corrosion of new reinforcement. It is applied just before concreting (typically within 15–30 minutes of mixing). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the propping step** — Examiners award 1 mark specifically for mentioning load transfer to shoring. Many students omit this.
> 2. **Confusing RC Jacketing with Guniting / Shotcreting** — Guniting is a *surface repair* technique, not a strengthening technique. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Failing to mention curing duration** — Curing for **minimum 14 days** is a mandatory requirement; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of bonding agent** — Just writing "apply epoxy" is not enough; students must explain *why* (chemical adhesion, moisture seal, corrosion protection).
> 5. **Incorrect dowel spacing formula** — Dowels are spaced based on **shear demand**, not arbitrarily at 100 mm or 500 mm c/c.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4: RC Jacketing**

- **Definition:** Section enlargement technique using new concrete + new rebars around an existing member.
- **Minimum jacket thickness:** **75 mm for columns**, **50 mm for beams** (IS 15988:2013).
- **Minimum new concrete grade:** **M25**, and must be **≥ f_ck,old + 5 N/mm²**.
- **Surface preparation:** Roughness amplitude **3 mm – 5 mm**; remove all laitance and weak cover.
- **Bonding agent:** Polymer-modified slurry or epoxy, applied within 15–30 minutes of mixing.
- **Dowel embedment depth:** **10$\phi$ – 15$\phi$**; spacing **200 mm – 300 mm c/c, staggered**.
- **Lateral tie spacing (seismic):** $s \leq \min(0.75 D, 16 \phi_l, 300 \text{ mm})$ per **IS 13920**.
- **Slenderness limit for short column:** $\lambda_{\text{eff}} \leq 12$.
- **Axial capacity formula (IS 456 Cl. 39.3):** $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_g + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$.
- **Typical capacity enhancement:** **30% – 100%** increase over original capacity.
- **Self-weight of jacket** must be checked against foundation's additional load capacity.
- **Composite action** is the key design objective — ensured by surface prep + dowels + bonding agent.
- **RC Jacketing vs FRP Wrapping:** RC Jacketing uses conventional materials, gives larger capacity gain, and has better fire resistance; FRP is thinner, faster, but limited in fire/UV durability.
- **Common applications:** Seismic retrofitting of soft-storey buildings, strengthening of columns for additional floor loads, restoration of fire/corrosion-damaged members.
- **Indian standards to remember:** **IS 15988:2013** (seismic strengthening), **IS 456:2000** (design), **IS 13920:2016** (ductile detailing), **IS 13311** (NDT).
- **International standards:** **ACI 562-19** (US), **ACI 546R-14** (repair guidance), **fib Bulletin 17** (European).
- **Examiner's tip:** Always **draw a labelled cross-section** in numerical/design questions — it is worth **2–3 marks** in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steel Jacketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Steel Jacketing** is a passive strengthening and rehabilitation technique in which external steel elements — typically steel plates, rolled steel angles, channels, or prefabricated steel cages — are mechanically or adhesively bonded to the surface of an existing reinforced concrete (RC) or steel structural member to restore or enhance its load-carrying capacity, stiffness, ductility, and energy dissipation characteristics.

As per **IS 15988:2013** (*Code of Practice for Repair and Rehabilitation of Concrete Structures*) and **ACI 562-19** (*Code Requirements for Assessment, Repair, and Rehabilitation of Existing Concrete Structures*), steel jacketing is classified as a **passive confinement retrofit** system, where the added steel section does not participate in initial service loads but mobilises progressively as the original member undergoes deformation under subsequent loading or seismic events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET634 - Module 4):**
> Steel jacketing is studied under "Strengthening Techniques" and covers column jacketing, beam jacketing, and beam-column joint jacketing for flexural, shear, and axial strengthening — frequently appearing in KTU university examinations with **14-mark analytical problems**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "External Skeleton" Principle

Imagine a tree trunk that has developed a deep internal cavity due to rot. Rather than replacing the tree, a horticulturist wraps the trunk with a steel wire mesh and bolts a steel band around it. The internal wood continues to bear load, but the steel band now **shares the stress**, **prevents buckling of the outer fibres**, and **confines the damaged core** from spalling outward.

Steel jacketing operates on exactly this principle:

- The **original RC member** = the inner (weakened/damaged) core.
- The **steel plates/angles** = the external skeletal cage.
- The **bolts/welds/epoxy** = the load-transfer interface that makes the two act as a **composite section**.

When lateral or axial loads are applied, the original concrete tends to expand outward (Poisson's effect). The steel jacket **restrains this lateral expansion**, generating a triaxial confining stress state that dramatically enhances the compressive strength and ductility of the encased concrete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Confinement Mechanism:**
> Concrete confined by a steel jacket can achieve compressive strength enhancements of **30% to 200%** and ultimate strain enhancements of **5 to 10 times** the unconfined value, depending on jacket thickness, spacing, and configuration.

## 1.3 When is Steel Jacketing Specified?

Steel jacketing is the **preferred retrofitting strategy** when:

- A structural member has suffered **section loss** due to corrosion of reinforcement or concrete spalling.
- The axial load demand on a column has **increased** due to a change in building use (e.g., residential to commercial).
- The seismic capacity of a column or beam-column joint is **inadequate** under revised seismic zonation (e.g., revisions following the 2001 Bhuj earthquake led to widespread adoption in India).
- The **lap-splice zone** at the column base has insufficient development length.
- A quick, **non-disruptive** in-situ strengthening is required without major demolition.

## 1.4 Classification of Steel Jacketing Systems

Steel jacketing is broadly classified into **four categories** based on geometric configuration:

| Classification | Geometry | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Box Jacketing** (Cage) | Four angle sections at corners + horizontal batten plates | Square/Rectangular Columns |
| **Circular Jacketing** | Continuous rolled steel plates welded circumferentially | Circular Columns |
| **U-Channel / L-Angle Jacketing** | Two angles or channels on opposite faces | Beams (flexural/shear) |
| **Wing-Plate Jacketing** | Steel plates welded to angle brackets at top and bottom | Beam-Column Joints |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Examiners most frequently test **column jacketing (box type)** because it allows the cleanest demonstration of confinement equations, axial load enhancement, and IS code design provisions. Always draw a clear cross-section in your answer scripts to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constituent Components of a Steel Jacketing System

A complete steel jacketing assembly comprises the following engineering components, each with a specific structural function:

1. **Steel Plates / Angles (Primary Jacket):**
   - Form the main confining shell around the original member.
   - Common materials: **Mild Steel (Fe 410)**, **High Yield Strength Deformed Steel (Fe 500 / Fe 550)**, or **Corten Steel** for corrosion-prone zones.
   - Typical thickness: **6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm** for plates; **50 × 50 × 6 mm, 75 × 75 × 8 mm** for angles.

2. **Batten / Strap Plates (Lateral Connectors):**
   - Horizontally welded/bolted steel strips that interconnect the vertical corner angles.
   - Maintain the geometry of the jacket and ensure uniform stress transfer.
   - Typical spacing: **150 mm to 300 mm c/c** (centre-to-centre).

3. **Shear Connectors (Studs / Anchor Bolts):**
   - Chemically anchored or expansion-type bolts that transmit shear between the jacket and the original concrete surface.
   - Typical diameter: **M12, M16, M20**; grade **8.8 or 10.9** high-tensile bolts.
   - Spacing: typically **200 mm to 300 mm** in a staggered pattern.

4. **Grout / Epoxy Interface:**
   - Non-shrink cementitious grout (minimum thickness **20 mm to 50 mm**) or structural epoxy resin fills the gap between the existing concrete surface and the steel jacket.
   - Ensures uniform load transfer and prevents corrosion at the interface.
   - Typical grout strength: **M60 grade** or higher for column jacketing.

5. **Weld Metal (Fillet / Butt Welds):**
   - E60XX or E90XX electrodes for joining plates to angles.
   - Weld size typically equal to the thinner of the two joined plates.
   - All welds must conform to **IS 816** and **IS 9595**.

## 2.2 Mechanics of Composite Action — Why Steel Jacketing Works

The fundamental engineering principle underlying steel jacketing is the **transformation of an unconfined concrete section into a confined concrete section** through external lateral pressure.

When an axially loaded concrete column is encased in a steel jacket, the following sequence of stress mobilisation occurs:

- **Stage 1 — Service Loads:** Both the original concrete and the steel jacket share the axial compression in proportion to their axial stiffness (EA).
- **Stage 2 — Cracking Onset:** As the column approaches its unconfined ultimate load, micro-cracks develop and concrete begins to dilate (Poisson's ratio of cracked concrete ≈ 0.5).
- **Stage 3 — Confinement Activation:** The steel jacket resists this lateral dilation, generating a **radial confining pressure** $\sigma_r$ on the concrete core.
- **Stage 4 — Strength Enhancement:** The confined concrete attains a triaxial stress state, with the axial compressive strength $f_{cc}^{'}$ given by a confinement model (discussed in Section 3).

This mechanism is mathematically captured by the **classical Richart's Confinement Model (1928)** for concrete under triaxial compression.

## 2.3 Failure Modes of Steel-Jacketed Members

A designer **must** anticipate the following failure modes; KTU examiners often test the student's ability to identify them:

| Failure Mode | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Buckling of Jacket** | Steel plates buckle outward between batten straps at high axial strains. | Reduce batten spacing, increase plate thickness. |
| **Weld Fracture** | Fillet welds at plate-to-angle junctions fail in shear due to cyclic loading. | Use full-penetration butt welds; provide weld access holes. |
| **Slip at Steel-Concrete Interface** | Loss of composite action due to inadequate shear connectors. | Increase bolt density; roughen concrete surface; use epoxy bond. |
| **Crushing of Grout Layer** | Grout fails in compression under axial load concentration. | Use high-strength non-shrink grout (M60+). |
| **Yielding of Angle Corners** | Stress concentration at corner angles leads to premature yielding. | Use larger angle sections; provide gusset plates. |
| **Lap-Splice Debonding** | Inadequate column splice zone fails before jacket mobilises. | Extend jacket full height of lap-splice zone. |

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on Steel Jacketing. Memorise the **form, units, and applicability** of each.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Governing Standard | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + k_1 \cdot \sigma_r$ | $f_{cc}^{'}$ = confined strength, $f_c^{'}$ = unconfined strength, $\sigma_r$ = confining pressure, $k_1$ = confinement coefficient (**4.1** for circular, varies for square) | Richart's Model | Confined concrete strength |
| 2 | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{D}$ | $t_j$ = jacket thickness, $f_{yj}$ = jacket yield strength, $D$ = column diameter | Mander & Priestley | Circular jacket hoop stress |
| 3 | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}}$ | $b, h$ = column cross-section dimensions | Mander Model | Rectangular jacket confining pressure |
| 4 | $P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc} + 0.67 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot A_{sj}$ | $A_c$ = concrete area, $A_{sc}$ = main rebar area, $A_{sj}$ = steel jacket longitudinal area | IS 456 / IS 15988 | Axial capacity of jacketed column |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{cu,conf} = \varepsilon_{co} \cdot \left[1 + k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_r}{f_c^{'}}\right]$ | $\varepsilon_{co}$ = unconfined strain (0.002), $k_2$ = strain coefficient (**20.5** for circular) | Mander Model | Confined ultimate strain |
| 6 | $\dfrac{s}{d} \le \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_j}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E_j}{3 \cdot f_{yj}}}$ | $s$ = batten spacing, $d$ = section dimension, $E_j$ = jacket elastic modulus | Priestley & Seible | Anti-buckling batten spacing |
| 7 | $V_{ns} = \dfrac{0.4}{f_y} \cdot (A_v \cdot f_y + A_{vj} \cdot f_{yj}) \cdot d$ | $A_v$ = existing stirrup area, $A_{vj}$ = jacket contribution as shear reinforcement | ACI 318-19 | Shear capacity enhancement |
| 8 | $f_y = 250 \text{ MPa (Fe 410)}$ | Default yield strength | IS 2062 | Material property |
| 9 | $f_y = 415 \text{ MPa to 550 MPa}$ | High-yield steel grades | IS 1786 | Rebar yield strength |
| 10 | $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$ | Elastic modulus of steel | Universal | Stiffness calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, the examiner expects you to use **IS code values** ($f_{ck}$, $f_y$, $\gamma_m$ = **1.5 for steel**, **1.5 for concrete**). Mixing ACI and IS provisions without justification leads to mark deductions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

Steel jacketing is extensively deployed in:

- **Indian Railway Bridge Piers** — Strengthening against corrosion-induced section loss and increased live load demands from heavier locomotives.
- **Highway Overpass Columns** — Seismic retrofitting after the **2001 Bhuj (Gujarat)** and **2015 Nepal** earthquakes.
- **Industrial RCC Chimneys** — Strengthening of hollow circular shafts.
- **Heritage Building Restoration** — Where minimal cross-section increase is desired (steel jackets typically add only **50 mm to 100 mm** to each face).
- **Offshore Platform Columns** — Marine corrosion rehabilitation combined with strength enhancement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Confined Compressive Strength of Circular Steel-Jacketed Columns

We derive the relationship between the confining pressure $\sigma_r$ generated by a circular steel jacket and the resulting confined compressive strength $f_{cc}^{'}$ of the encased concrete.

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram of a Half-Circumference of the Jacket

Consider a circular concrete column of diameter $D$ encased in a steel jacket of thickness $t_j$ and yield strength $f_{yj}$. Cut the jacket along a vertical diametral plane. The internal concrete exerts a uniform radial pressure $\sigma_r$ on the inner surface of the jacket.

By symmetry, the hoop (circumferential) tensile force in the jacket balances the radial pressure resultant.

### Step 2: Equilibrium in the Hoop Direction

The radial pressure resultant over half the circumference equals:

$$F_{radial} = \sigma_r \cdot D \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the length of the column segment under consideration (set to unit length, $L = 1$, for stress analysis).

The hoop tensile force in the steel jacket at the diametral cut is:

$$F_{hoop} = 2 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot t_j$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the two halves of the jacket resisting the radial pressure at the diametral cut.

### Step 3: Setting Up the Equilibrium Equation

For equilibrium of the half-jacket in the radial direction:

$$\sigma_r \cdot D = 2 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot t_j$$

### Step 4: Solving for Confining Pressure $\sigma_r$

Rearranging the equilibrium equation for $\sigma_r$:

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{D}$$

> This is the **Mander-Priestley equation** for circular confinement, valid at the jacket's first yield (i.e., when the jacket transitions from elastic to plastic behaviour).

### Step 5: Applying Richart's Confinement Model (1908 — Empirical)

Based on extensive triaxial compression tests, **Richart, Brandtzaeg, and Brown (1928)** proposed that the confined compressive strength $f_{cc}^{'}$ is related to the unconfined strength $f_c^{'}$ and confining pressure $\sigma_r$ by:

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + k_1 \cdot \sigma_r$$

For circular confinement (Richart's original tests on spirally-confined cylinders), $k_1 = 4.1$. Therefore:

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + 4.1 \cdot \sigma_r$$

Substituting the expression for $\sigma_r$:

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + \dfrac{8.2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{D}$$

### Step 6: Generalised Derivation for Rectangular Jackets (Mander Model)

For a rectangular column of dimensions $b \times h$ jacketed with corner angles of width $b_a$, the confining pressure is non-uniform. The **Mander, Priestley & Park (1988)** model approximates the effective confining pressure at the column centre as:

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}}$$

The diagonal distance $\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}$ represents the "effective confinement arc length" along the column cross-section.

Substituting into Richart's equation with $k_1 = 4.1$:

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + \dfrac{8.2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For rectangular columns, the actual confinement is **non-uniform** — maximum at the corners and minimum at the mid-span of each face. Mander's model uses an "effectively confined core" concept with a parabolic arching zone. For KTU board exams, the simplified rectangular formula above is sufficient and is the **expected answer**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Axial Load Capacity of a Steel-Jacketed Column (IS 15988)

The ultimate axial load capacity of a steel-jacketed column is derived as the sum of contributions from: (i) confined concrete, (ii) existing longitudinal reinforcement, and (iii) longitudinal steel jacket material.

### Step 1: Identification of Load-Resisting Components

For a square column of dimension $b \times h$ with longitudinal bars of area $A_{sc}$, encased in a steel cage (4 angles + batten plates), the load paths are:

- **Confined Concrete Core:** Carries axial compression with enhanced strength $f_{cc}^{'}$.
- **Original Longitudinal Rebar:** Carries axial compression up to yield.
- **Steel Jacket Longitudinal Elements (Corner Angles):** Carry axial compression.
- **Batten Plates:** Do **not** contribute directly to axial load capacity but provide confinement.

### Step 2: Total Axial Capacity (Unfactored)

Summing the three axial contributions:

$$P_u = P_{concrete} + P_{rebar} + P_{jacket}$$

$$\boxed{P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc} + 0.67 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot A_{sj}}$$

where:
- $A_c = (b \cdot h) - A_{sc} - A_{sj}$ is the net concrete area
- $A_{sj}$ is the cross-sectional area of all four corner angles
- $0.4 f_c$ is the IS 456 design compressive stress for concrete (incorporating $\gamma_m = 1.5$)
- $0.67 f_y$ is the IS 456 design stress for steel reinforcement

### Step 3: Factored (Design) Axial Capacity

Applying the load factor $\gamma_f = 1.5$ from IS 456 for safety:

$$P_{design} = \dfrac{P_u}{1.5}$$

> The required capacity $P_{required}$ (from structural analysis under factored loads) must satisfy:
> $$P_{design} \geq P_{required}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slenderness Ratio Modification for Slender Jacketed Columns

A common **KTU exam question** asks how steel jacketing affects the slenderness ratio and buckling capacity of a slender column.

### Step 1: Transformed Section Concept

When computing the slenderness ratio $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r}$ for a steel-jacketed column, the **transformed (homogenised) section** approach is used.

The moment of inertia of the composite section is:

$$I_{eff} = I_c + \dfrac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot I_s + (n-1) \cdot I_{rebar}$$

where:
- $I_c$ = moment of inertia of gross concrete section (ignoring reinforcement)
- $I_s$ = moment of inertia of the steel jacket (4 angles + batten plates treated as a cage)
- $I_{rebar}$ = moment of inertia of longitudinal rebar about the section centroid
- $n = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c}$ = modular ratio (typically **10** for $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$)

### Step 2: Effective Radius of Gyration

$$r_{eff} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{eff}}{A_{eff}}}$$

where $A_{eff} = A_c + n \cdot A_{rebar} + n \cdot A_{sj}$ is the transformed area.

### Step 3: Modified Slenderness Ratio

$$\lambda_{modified} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{eff}}$$

For **short columns** ($\lambda \le 12$): Jacketing primarily enhances compressive strength; no buckling reduction needed.

For **slender columns** ($\lambda > 12$): Apply IS 456 reduction factor $C_r = 1.25 - \dfrac{\lambda}{48}$ (for $\lambda$ between 12 and 60) and multiply $P_u$ by $C_r$:

$$P_{u,slender} = C_r \cdot P_u$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **recompute** the slenderness ratio **after** jacketing. The added steel makes the section stiffer, lowering $\lambda$, but adds weight that increases axial demand. Always verify the post-jacketing $\lambda$ falls within IS 456 limits.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style — 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A square RC column of size **300 mm × 300 mm** is reinforced with **4 bars of 20 mm diameter (Fe 415)** and **8 mm lateral ties at 200 mm c/c**. The concrete grade is **M25**, and the column is required to support an axial factored load of **2400 kN**. The column is to be strengthened by **steel jacketing** using **4 equal angles of 50 × 50 × 6 mm (Fe 410)** placed at the corners with **6 mm thick batten plates** welded at **200 mm c/c**. Verify the adequacy of the jacketed section. *(Use Mander's equation and IS 456 design stresses.)*

### Solution:

#### Step 1: Material Properties

Given data:
- Concrete: $f_{ck} = 25 \text{ MPa}$
- Rebar: $f_y = 415 \text{ MPa}$
- Jacket Steel: $f_{yj} = 250 \text{ MPa}$
- Modular ratio: $n = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{5000 \sqrt{25}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{25000} = 8$

#### Step 2: Cross-Sectional Areas

- Concrete (gross): $A_g = 300 \times 300 = 90000 \text{ mm}^2$
- Rebar area: $A_{sc} = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2$
- Angle area (each): $A_{angle} = (50 + 50 - 6) \times 6 = 564 \text{ mm}^2$
- Total jacket area: $A_{sj} = 4 \times 564 = 2256 \text{ mm}^2$

#### Step 3: Net Concrete Area

$$A_c = A_g - A_{sc} - A_{sj} = 90000 - 1256.64 - 2256 = 86487.36 \text{ mm}^2$$

#### Step 4: Confining Pressure from Rectangular Jacket (Mander Model)

For a square column, $b = h = 300$ mm, so $\sqrt{b^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{2} \times 300 = 424.26$ mm.

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 6 \times 250}{424.26}$$

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{3000}{424.26} = 7.07 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Step 5: Confined Concrete Strength (Richart-Mander)

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + k_1 \cdot \sigma_r = 25 + 4.1 \times 7.07 = 25 + 28.99 = 53.99 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Step 6: Total Ultimate Axial Capacity

$$P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc} + 0.67 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot A_{sj}$$

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 53.99 \times 86487.36 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1256.64 + 0.67 \times 250 \times 2256$$

Component-wise evaluation:

$$P_{concrete} = 21.596 \times 86487.36 = 1867771 \text{ N} \approx 1867.77 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{rebar} = 278.05 \times 1256.64 = 349356 \text{ N} \approx 349.36 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{jacket} = 167.5 \times 2256 = 377880 \text{ N} \approx 377.88 \text{ kN}$$

Summing:

$$P_u = 1867.77 + 349.36 + 377.88 = 2595.01 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 7: Design Axial Capacity (Factored)

$$P_{design} = \dfrac{P_u}{1.5} = \dfrac{2595.01}{1.5} = 1730.01 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 8: Verification

The required factored load is **2400 kN**. The design capacity is only **1730 kN**.

$$P_{design} = 1730 \text{ kN} < P_{required} = 2400 \text{ kN} \quad \Rightarrow \text{NOT ADEQUATE}$$

#### Step 9: Redesign Recommendation

Increase jacket thickness to **10 mm** and use **75 × 75 × 8 mm** angles.

Recompute with $t_j = 10$ mm and $A_{sj} = 4 \times (75 + 75 - 8) \times 8 = 4 \times 1136 = 4544 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times 250}{424.26} = 11.79 \text{ MPa}$$

$$f_{cc}^{'} = 25 + 4.1 \times 11.79 = 73.34 \text{ MPa}$$

$$A_c = 90000 - 1256.64 - 4544 = 84199.36 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 73.34 \times 84199.36 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1256.64 + 0.67 \times 250 \times 4544$$

$$P_u = 2469410 + 349356 + 761120 = 3579886 \text{ N} \approx 3580 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{design} = \dfrac{3580}{1.5} = 2386.7 \text{ kN} \geq 2400 \text{ kN}$$

> Now, $P_{design} = 2386.7 \text{ kN} < 2400 \text{ kN}$ is still marginally short — increase angle section to **100 × 100 × 10 mm** or increase plate thickness to **12 mm** to achieve the required capacity.

This example illustrates the **complete design workflow** expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Jacketing Techniques (Common KTU 14-Mark Question)

| Property | Steel Jacketing | Concrete Jacketing | FRP Jacketing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strength Enhancement** | High (1.5x – 3x) | Moderate (1.3x – 1.8x) | High (1.5x – 2.5x) |
| **Cross-Section Increase** | Moderate (50–100 mm) | Large (75–150 mm) | Minimal (5–20 mm) |
| **Self-Weight Addition** | Moderate | Significant | Negligible |
| **Construction Speed** | Fast (in-situ welding) | Slow (formwork + curing) | Fast (in-situ wrapping) |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor (needs anti-rust coating) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Ductility Enhancement** | Excellent | Moderate | Good |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Skilled Labour Required** | Welding + Bolting | Carpentry + Concreting | Specialised FRP workers |
| **Fire Resistance** | Poor (loses strength above 600°C) | Excellent | Poor (epoxy degrades at 80°C) |
| **Code Reference** | IS 15988, ACI 562 | IS 15988, IS 456 | ACI 440.2R-17, IS 15988 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Steel Jacketing** with **FRP Wrapping** in answer sheets. The fundamental difference is that steel jacketing is a **rigid, bolted/welded mechanical system**, whereas FRP wrapping is a **flexible, adhesively-bonded composite system**. The confinement equations, design philosophy, and failure modes are entirely different.
