# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define steel jacketing. Mention any four situations where steel jacketing is preferred over other rehabilitation techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

Steel jacketing is a passive strengthening technique in which external steel elements such as angles, plates, or cages are mechanically and/or adhesively bonded to the surface of an existing structural member to enhance its load-carrying capacity, stiffness, and ductility through confinement of the parent concrete section.

Steel jacketing is preferred over other techniques in the following situations:

1. **Section Loss Rehabilitation:** When concrete has spalled or rebar has corroded, leading to a substantial reduction in the effective cross-sectional area of the column or beam.
2. **Increased Load Demand:** When the structure is repurposed (e.g., residential to commercial) and the existing members are inadequate for the new factored loads.
3. **Seismic Retrofit:** When the seismic ductility and shear capacity of columns or beam-column joints are insufficient under revised seismic zonation (e.g., after the 2001 Bhuj earthquake retrofitting programmes in Gujarat).
4. **Rapid In-Situ Strengthening:** When time is a constraint and minimal disruption to building occupancy is required, as steel jacketing avoids formwork and curing time.
5. **Lap-Splice Strengthening:** When lap splices at the base of columns have inadequate development length due to construction defects.
6. **Limited Headroom:** When only a moderate increase in member cross-section is acceptable (typically 50–100 mm per face).

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Explain the role of batten plates in a steel jacketing system. State the IS code spacing requirement for batten plates.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Batten Plates:**

Batten plates are horizontal steel strips welded or bolted between the vertical corner angles of a box-type steel jacket. Their primary structural roles are:

1. **Geometric Stability:** They maintain the rectangular geometry of the jacket cage, preventing the corner angles from bowing outward under axial or lateral loads.
2. **Local Buckling Prevention:** They provide lateral support to the corner angles, reducing their effective unsupported length and preventing local buckling between supports.
3. **Confinement Enhancement:** They transmit hoop (circumferential) tensile forces around the jacket, ensuring that the confining pressure $\sigma_r$ on the concrete core is uniformly distributed.
4. **Shear Transfer:** They transfer horizontal shear forces between opposite corner angles under lateral (seismic) loading.

**IS Code Spacing Requirement:**

As per **IS 15988:2013** and **Priestley & Seible (1991)** guidelines, the spacing of batten plates is governed by the anti-buckling criterion:

$$\dfrac{s}{d} \le \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_j}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E_j}{3 \cdot f_{yj}}}$$

For typical jacket plates ($t_j = 6$ mm, $E_j = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $f_{yj} = 250$ MPa) on a 300 mm column, this yields a maximum batten spacing of approximately **150 mm to 300 mm c/c**. A practical field recommendation is **200 mm c/c**, which is widely adopted by Indian contractors and is the **IS code-recommended default for M25–M40 grade columns**.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A square RC column of size 400 mm × 400 mm, reinforced with 8 bars of 16 mm diameter (Fe 415) and 8 mm lateral ties at 250 mm c/c, is required to carry an additional axial load of 1500 kN due to a change in building occupancy. The concrete grade is M30. Design a steel jacket using Fe 410 grade steel angles and 6 mm batten plates. Verify the adequacy of the design using the Mander-Richart confinement model.**

#### Solution:

##### Part (a): Design of Steel Jacket (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Column size: $400 \times 400$ mm
- Concrete grade: M30 → $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa
- Main rebars: 8 bars of 16 mm dia Fe415
- Lateral ties: 8 mm @ 250 mm c/c
- Existing axial load capacity (from structural analysis): $P_{existing} = 3000$ kN
- Additional axial load: $\Delta P = 1500$ kN
- Required total capacity: $P_{required} = 4500$ kN
- Jacket material: Fe410, $f_{yj} = 250$ MPa
- Batten plate thickness: $t_j = 6$ mm (assumed initial trial)

**Step 1: Estimate Required Confined Strength**

Trial assumption: $A_{sc} = 8 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 1608.5$ mm$^2$, $A_{sj}$ is to be designed.

Assume $A_{sj} \approx 2000$ mm$^2$ (trial); then $A_c \approx 400^2 - 1608.5 - 2000 = 156391.5$ mm$^2$.

For IS 456 design stresses, the unfactored capacity is:

$$P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc} + 0.67 \cdot f_{yj} \cdot A_{sj}$$

Factored capacity = $\dfrac{P_u}{1.5}$ must equal $4500$ kN, so $P_u = 6750$ kN = $6750 \times 10^3$ N.

Plugging in:

$$6750 \times 10^3 = 0.4 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \cdot 156391.5 + 0.67 \cdot 415 \cdot 1608.5 + 0.67 \cdot 250 \cdot 2000$$

$$6750 \times 10^3 = 62556.6 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} + 446761 + 335000$$

$$6750 \times 10^3 - 446761 - 335000 = 62556.6 \cdot f_{cc}^{'}$$

$$5968239 = 62556.6 \cdot f_{cc}^{'} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{cc}^{'} = 95.4 \text{ MPa}$$

##### Step 2: Required Confining Pressure

$$f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + k_1 \cdot \sigma_r \quad \Rightarrow \quad 95.4 = 30 + 4.1 \cdot \sigma_r$$

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{65.4}{4.1} = 15.95 \text{ MPa}$$

##### Step 3: Required Jacket Thickness

Using Mander model for rectangular column:

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}}$$

For 400 mm × 400 mm column, $\sqrt{b^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = 565.69$ mm.

$$15.95 = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot 250}{565.69} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_j = \dfrac{15.95 \times 565.69}{500} = 18.04 \text{ mm}$$

> Provide 20 mm thick plates (next standard size up) for safety.

##### Step 4: Selection of Corner Angles

Provide 4 angles of $100 \times 100 \times 10$ mm at corners:
- Each angle area: $A_{angle} = (100 + 100 - 10) \times 10 = 1900$ mm$^2$
- Total jacket area: $A_{sj} = 4 \times 1900 = 7600$ mm$^2$

##### Part (b): Verification of Design (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Recompute Confined Strength with Designed Parameters**

$$A_c = 400 \times 400 - 1608.5 - 7600 = 151791.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \times 20 \times 250}{565.69} = 17.68 \text{ MPa}$$

$$f_{cc}^{'} = 30 + 4.1 \times 17.68 = 30 + 72.49 = 102.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Recompute Ultimate Capacity**

$$P_{concrete} = 0.4 \times 102.49 \times 151791.5 = 6223.6 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 6223.6 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{rebar} = 0.67 \times 415 \times 1608.5 = 446.8 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{jacket} = 0.67 \times 250 \times 7600 = 1273.0 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_u = 6223.6 + 446.8 + 1273.0 = 7943.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Factored Capacity**

$$P_{design} = \dfrac{7943.4}{1.5} = 5295.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8: Verification Check**

$$P_{design} = 5295.6 \text{ kN} > P_{required} = 4500 \text{ kN} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ DESIGN IS ADEQUATE}$$

**Margin of Safety:** $\dfrac{5295.6 - 4500}{4500} \times 100\% = 17.7\%$ — acceptable per IS 15988 recommendations (typically 10–20%).

**Step 9: Batten Plate Spacing Check**

Apply Priestley-Seible formula:

$$\dfrac{s}{d} \le \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_j}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E_j}{3 \cdot f_{yj}}}$$

For $t_j = 20$ mm, $D = 400$ mm, $E_j = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $f_{yj} = 250$ MPa:

$$\dfrac{s}{400} \le 0.5 \times \dfrac{20}{400} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{750}}$$

$$\dfrac{s}{400} \le 0.025 \times \sqrt{266.67} = 0.025 \times 16.33 = 0.408$$

$$s \le 163 \text{ mm}$$

Provide batten plates at **150 mm c/c** (next standard spacing below 163 mm for safety).

> **[End of Question A Solution]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Describe the step-by-step construction procedure for steel jacketing of an existing RC column. Explain the role of (i) corner angles, (ii) batten plates, and (iii) non-shrink grout in the system. Also, state any four advantages and two limitations of steel jacketing.**

#### Solution:

##### Part (a): Step-by-Step Construction Procedure (7 Marks)

The construction of a steel-jacketed column follows a **strict sequence of operations** to ensure composite action between the existing concrete and the new steel cage.

**Step 1 — Initial Survey and Structural Assessment:**
- Conduct a detailed condition assessment of the existing column using non-destructive testing (rebound hammer, ultrasonic pulse velocity, half-cell potential for rebar corrosion).
- Identify and mark all delaminated/spalled concrete zones and corroded rebars.

**Step 2 — Shoring and Load Transfer:**
- Install temporary props or needle beams to transfer the existing floor loads away from the column being jacketed.
- This step is **critical** as the column must be relieved of all dead loads during jacketing to allow the new cage to participate in future load-sharing.

**Step 3 — Concrete Surface Preparation:**
- Remove all loose, spalled, and carbonated concrete using chipping hammers or hydro-jetting.
- Expose corroded rebars to at least 25 mm beyond the corroded zone.
- Clean the exposed rebar surface using wire brushes or sandblasting to a near-white metal finish (Sa 2.5 as per **IS 9954**).

**Step 4 — Corrosion Protection of Existing Rebar:**
- Apply zinc-rich primer or epoxy-based anti-corrosion coating on the exposed rebars.
- Bond a new rebar (splice with existing) if the original rebar has lost more than **20% cross-section**.

**Step 5 — Repair Mortar Application:**
- Apply polymer-modified repair mortar (e.g., **Mapei Mapegrout T60** or equivalent) to restore the column profile to its original dimensions.
- Allow curing for at least 7 days before proceeding.

**Step 6 — Fabrication and Placement of Steel Cage:**
- Cut four corner angles to the required length (typically column height plus **100 mm overlap** at top and bottom for lap with beams/slabs).
- Position the angles at the four corners of the column, maintaining a **minimum 25 mm gap** between angle face and existing concrete surface (for grout placement).
- Weld horizontal batten plates at the designed spacing (150–300 mm c/c).
- All welds must conform to **IS 816** and **IS 9595**.

**Step 7 — Anchor Stud Installation:**
- Drill holes into the existing concrete at 200–300 mm c/c in a staggered pattern.
- Insert **M12 or M16 grade 8.8** anchor studs using chemical anchor adhesive (e.g., Hilti HIT-RE 500).

**Step 8 — Non-Shrink Grout Injection:**
- Seal the perimeter of the cage with quick-setting mortar.
- Inject high-strength non-shrink grout (M60 grade) into the gap through pre-fixed grout ports.
- Ensure complete filling without voids (use ultrasonic testing to verify).

**Step 9 — Final Welding and Finishing:**
- Complete all final welds, including the top and bottom laps with adjacent structural members.
- Apply anti-corrosion paint system (zinc-rich primer + epoxy + polyurethane topcoat) as per **IS 9197**.

**Step 10 — Load Restoration and Monitoring:**
- Gradually release the temporary shores, transferring the floor load back to the strengthened column.
- Monitor the column for at least 72 hours for any distress.

##### Part (b): Roles, Advantages, and Limitations (7 Marks)

**Role of (i) Corner Angles:**
Corner angles are the **primary load-bearing elements** of the steel cage. They:
- Carry axial compression in parallel with the existing concrete.
- Provide vertical continuity of the jacket from top to bottom.
- Anchor the batten plates and serve as the main load-transfer path between the cage and the existing structure.
- Resist bending stresses under eccentric or lateral loads.

**Role of (ii) Batten Plates:**
Batten plates are the **secondary confining elements** that:
- Provide lateral support to the corner angles, preventing them from buckling outward under high axial strain.
- Transfer circumferential (hoop) tensile stresses around the jacket, ensuring uniform confining pressure on the concrete core.
- Maintain the rectangular geometry of the cage during grout injection and service life.

**Role of (iii) Non-Shrink Grout:**
Non-shrink grout is the **interfacial load-transfer medium** that:
- Distributes axial load from the steel cage to the existing concrete uniformly, preventing stress concentrations.
- Bonds the anchor studs to the existing concrete, mobilising the bolts as shear connectors.
- Provides a dense, impermeable barrier against moisture and chlorides, protecting both the existing rebar and the steel jacket from corrosion.

**Four Advantages of Steel Jacketing:**
1. High enhancement of axial, shear, and flexural capacity (typically 1.5x to 3x).
2. Rapid construction with no curing time required (after grout curing).
3. Excellent seismic ductility enhancement through active confinement.
4. Well-established design methodology backed by extensive experimental data and IS code provisions.

**Two Limitations of Steel Jacketing:**
1. **Corrosion Susceptibility:** Requires elaborate anti-corrosion coating systems and periodic maintenance, especially in coastal or industrial environments.
2. **Fire Performance Degradation:** Steel loses significant strength above **600°C**, requiring fire-protection cladding (e.g., cementitious spray or intumescent coatings) which adds cost.

> **[End of Question B Solution]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Steel Jacketing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Steel Jacketing with FRP Wrapping:** Many students write confinement equations for FRP (e.g., ACI 440.2R formulas) when the question is on steel jacketing (IS 15988/Mander). This is an automatic **3–4 mark penalty**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Jacket Area from Concrete Area:** When computing $A_c$, students often ignore $A_{sj}$ entirely. This overestimates the capacity by 5–10% and indicates conceptual weakness — **2 mark penalty**.
>
> 3. **Using Unfactored Capacity Directly:** Comparing $P_u$ (unfactored) with factored load demand without dividing by 1.5 — **2 mark penalty**.
>
> 4. **Drawing Poor Cross-Sections:** A sketch showing the steel cage configuration is worth **2–3 marks** in most KTU board valuations. Omitting it loses easy marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the 0.67 Factor in Steel Design Stress:** Writing $P_{steel} = f_y \cdot A_s$ instead of $0.67 f_y A_s$ — a hallmark of an unprepared student.
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning IS Code Provisions:** Any design question without an IS code citation (IS 456:2000, IS 15988:2013) is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Batten Plate Spacing Check:** This is a frequently tested design verification step. Omitting it loses **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — READ BEFORE THE EXAM**

- **Definition Recap:** Steel jacketing is the passive confinement of an RC member using external steel plates/angles welded/bolted to form a cage.
- **Primary Mechanism:** Generation of radial confining pressure $\sigma_r$ that places the concrete in a triaxial stress state.
- **Key Formula 1:** $\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{D}$ (circular jacket).
- **Key Formula 2:** $\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_j \cdot f_{yj}}{\sqrt{b^2 + h^2}}$ (rectangular jacket — Mander).
- **Key Formula 3:** $f_{cc}^{'} = f_c^{'} + 4.1 \cdot \sigma_r$ (Richart confinement).
- **Key Formula 4:** $P_u = 0.4 f_{cc}^{'} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc} + 0.67 f_{yj} A_{sj}$.
- **Confinement Coefficient:** $k_1 = 4.1$ for circular, ~3.0 to 3.5 for rectangular (use 4.1 conservatively as per Mander).
- **Design Material:** Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa) for jacket; Fe 415 / Fe 500 for rebars; M60+ non-shrink grout.
- **Plate Thickness Range:** 6 mm to 20 mm for typical columns (300–600 mm).
- **Batten Spacing:** 150–300 mm c/c; verified by Priestley-Seible anti-buckling criterion.
- **Corner Angles:** Typically 50×50×6 mm to 100×100×10 mm; chosen based on axial load demand.
- **Anchor Studs:** M12 to M20 grade 8.8/10.9 bolts at 200–300 mm c/c staggered.
- **Grout Gap:** Minimum **25 mm** between existing concrete and jacket face.
- **Welding Standards:** IS 816, IS 9595; electrodes E60XX for Fe410, E90XX for Fe500.
- **Anti-Corrosion Coating:** Zinc-rich primer (50–75 µm) + epoxy (100–150 µm) + polyurethane topcoat (50 µm).
- **Fire Protection:** Required for fire ratings exceeding 60 minutes; cementitious spray or intumescent paint.
- **Code References:** IS 15988:2013, IS 456:2000, IS 800:2007, IS 816, IS 9595, ACI 562-19, ACI 318-19.
- **Failure Modes to Memorise:** Local buckling, weld fracture, interface slip, grout crushing, corner yielding, lap-splice debonding.
- **Comparison Pointers:** Steel jacketing is heavier and less corrosion-resistant than FRP but offers superior ductility, fire performance over FRP, and is more cost-effective than carbon-FRP wrapping.
- **Inspection & Testing:** Rebound hammer, UPV, half-cell potential (for rebar corrosion), load testing before and after jacketing.
- **Design Workflow:** Assessment → Jacketing Selection → Material Choice → Geometry Design → Confinement Calculation → Capacity Verification → Detailing (battens, welds, studs, grout).
- **Typical Application Zones in India:** Seismic Zones III, IV, and V as per IS 1893; particularly relevant in Gujarat, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, and the Northeast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FRP Jacketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** FRP Jacketing # FRP Jacketing: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition — FRP Jacketing** > **Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Jacketing** is a structural strengthening and rehabilitation technique in which continuous high-strength fibers (carbon, glass, or aramid) embedded in a polymer resin matrix are externally bonded to the surface of a structural member (typically concrete columns, beams, slabs, or walls) to enhance its axial load capacity, flexural strength, shear capacity, ductility, and confinement. The composite acts as an external "skin" that restrains lateral expansion, provides passive confinement, and supplements the tensile capacity of the parent structural element. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECET634 syllabus** and recognized codes such as **ACI 440.2R-17**, **ACI 440.1R-15**, and **IS 15911 (2018)** — *Strengthening of Reinforced Concrete Structures using Externally Bonded Fibre Reinforced Polymer Composites* — FRP jacketing is formally classified under **Externally Bonded Reinforcement (EBR)** systems. ### Constituent Materials | Component | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | **Fibers** | Carbon (CFRP), Glass (GFRP), Aramid (AFRP) | Carry tensile stress | | **Matrix / Resin** | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester | Transfer stress, protect fibers | | **Primer** | Epoxy-based | Seal substrate pores | | **Saturant** | Resin | Wet out fibers | | **Adhesive** | Epoxy paste | Bond FRP to concrete | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** FRP jacketing is one of the most preferred non-corrosive, lightweight, high-strength-strengthening methods covered in **Module 4** for seismic retrofitting and confinement of reinforced concrete (RC) columns. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a soft, hollow cardboard tube (the **concrete column**). If you squeeze it vertically, it bulges outward and buckles — a classic compression failure. Now, wrap that same tube tightly with a strong, thin, inextensible plastic tape (the **FRP jacket**). When you try to compress it again, the tape *resists the outward bulging*, keeping the tube in a tri-axial state of compression. The concrete inside becomes **confined**, dramatically increasing its strength and ductility. > This is the **"Confined Concrete"** principle that powers FRP jacketing. **In simple terms:** - **Concrete** = strong in compression, weak in tension. - **FRP jacket** = strong in tension, wraps the column like a corset. - Together, they create a system that is far stronger, more ductile, and more seismic-resistant than the original bare concrete element. > [!TIP] > Think of FRP jacketing as **"putting a reinforced armor"** around a damaged or deficient concrete member — without adding significant dead weight or altering the geometry drastically. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of FRP Jacketing FRP jacketing works primarily through **passive confinement**. The steps are: 1. **Surface Preparation:** The concrete substrate is cleaned, ground, and made sound. All laitance, dust, grease, and loose material are removed. Surface must be dry and roughened. 2. **Primer Application:** A low-viscosity epoxy primer is applied to penetrate the concrete pores and seal micro-cracks. 3. **Resin Putty Application:** A thixotropic epoxy putty is applied to fill surface defects (honeycombs, bug holes). 4. **Fiber Installation:** - **Wet Layup System:** Fibers are impregnated with resin on-site and wrapped around the member. - **Prepreg System:** Factory-impregnated fibers are cured on-site. - **Precured Laminates:** Pre-fabricated FRP strips are bonded with adhesive. 5. **Curing:** Resin cures at ambient or elevated temperature, forming a monolithic composite. ## 2.2 Types of FRP Based on Fiber Material | Type | Fiber | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elastic Modulus (GPa) | Cost | Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **CFRP** | Carbon | **3500 – 4900** | **200 – 235** | High | Columns, beams (premium) | | **GFRP** | Glass (E-glass) | **1900 – 3000** | **70 – 90** | Low | General confinement | | **AFRP** | Aramid (Kevlar) | **2900 – 3600** | **70 – 130** | Medium | Impact-resistant retrofit | > [!IMPORTANT] > For **seismic retrofitting** in India, **GFRP and CFRP** are the most widely used systems. CFRP is preferred for column confinement due to its high modulus and negligible creep. ## 2.3 Modes of Strengthening - **Confinement of Columns:** Wraps around circular/rectangular columns to enhance axial capacity and ductility. - **Flexural Strengthening:** FRP laminates bonded to the tension face of beams/slabs. - **Shear Strengthening:** FRP strips/U-wraps bonded to the sides of beams. - **Axial Strengthening:** For columns, piers, piles. - **Torsional Strengthening:** Closed wraps on beams. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are key for KTU End Semester Examinations under Module 4 of PECET634. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $f_{cc}' = f_{c}' + k_1 \cdot f_l$ | Confined concrete strength (Lam & Teng model) | | 2 | $f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_f \cdot E_{fe}}{D}$ | Lateral confining pressure (circular column) | | 3 | $E_{fe} = \dfrac{E_f}{2}$ (or $0.55 E_f$ ACI) | Effective FRP modulus (strain efficiency) | | 4 | $\varepsilon_{cu} = \varepsilon_{c}' \left(1.5 + 12 \cdot \dfrac{f_l}{f_c'} \right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{E_f}{E_c + \dfrac{2 \cdot t_f \cdot E_{fe}}{D}}\right)$ | Ultimate confined strain | | 5 | $f_{l,\max} = 0.43 \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \cdot E_{fe}$ | Max confining pressure (Lam & Teng) | | 6 | $P_n = 0.85 \cdot f_{cc}' \cdot (A_g - A_{st}) + f_y \cdot A_{st}$ | Axial capacity of confined column | | 7 | $\rho_{frp} = \dfrac{4 \cdot t_f}{D}$ | FRP volumetric ratio (circular section) | | 8 | $\varepsilon_{fe} = 0.586 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$ (ACI 440.2R) | Effective FRP strain | | 9 | $\phi \cdot P_n \geq P_u$ | Strength design check (ACI 318) | | 10 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_{fv} \cdot f_{fe} \cdot d}{s_f}$ | Shear contribution of FRP (U-wrap/full wrap) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The constant **k₁** in equation (1) is **3.3** for circular sections and **1.0–1.5** for rectangular sections (confinement effectiveness drops sharply for non-circular shapes). ## 2.5 Engineering Utility FRP jacketing is used in: - **Seismic retrofitting** of older RC frame buildings (pre-IS 13920 era). - **Bridge pier rehabilitation** after corrosion or impact damage. - **Industrial chimneys, silos, and water tanks** for axial capacity enhancement. - **Heritage structures** where mass and aesthetic changes must be minimized. - **Marine and coastal structures** due to corrosion resistance. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Confined Concrete Strength (Lam & Teng Model — 2003) The **Lam & Teng model** is the most widely accepted analytical model for FRP-confined concrete in circular sections and is regularly referenced in KTU examinations. ### Step 1: Define Lateral Confining Pressure Consider a circular column of diameter **D** wrapped with **n** layers of FRP, each of thickness **t_f** and elastic modulus **E_f**. The FRP shell exerts a uniform radial pressure **f_l** on the concrete core, analogous to internal hydrostatic pressure in a pressure vessel. By **force equilibrium** on a diametral plane: $$ 2 \cdot f_l \cdot D = 2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot f_{fe} $$ Where $f_{fe}$ is the effective stress in the FRP at ultimate state. Therefore: $$ f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot t_f \cdot f_{fe}}{D} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_{f,\text{total}} \cdot f_{fe}}{D} $$ > Here $t_{f,\text{total}} = n \cdot t_f$. ### Step 2: Effective FRP Stress (Strain Efficiency) Due to the multi-axial state of stress and premature failure, the FRP never reaches its ultimate tensile strength $f_{fu}$ at rupture. ACI 440.2R limits the effective strain: $$ \varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_\varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} $$ Where the strain reduction factor $\kappa_\varepsilon \approx 0.586$ for FRP-wrapped columns. So: $$ f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} = 0.586 \cdot E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} $$ ### Step 3: Confined Concrete Compressive Strength Based on extensive experimental data, Lam & Teng proposed: $$ f_{cc}' = f_{c}' + k_1 \cdot f_l $$ For circular sections, $k_1 = 3.3$, giving: $$ f_{cc}' = f_{c}' + 3.3 \cdot f_l $$ ### Step 4: Ultimate Axial Strain Enhancement The ultimate axial strain of confined concrete is enhanced from the unconfined value $\varepsilon_{c}'$ (taken as 0.002) as: $$ \varepsilon_{cu} = \varepsilon_{c}' \cdot \left( 1.5 + 12 \cdot \dfrac{f_l}{f_{c}'} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{30 \cdot \varepsilon_{fe}}{0.30} \right)^{0.5} $$ > A more commonly used simplified form (Teng et al.): $$ \varepsilon_{cu} = \varepsilon_{c}' \left( 1.5 + 12 \cdot \dfrac{f_l}{f_{c}'} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{E_f}{E_c + \dfrac{2 \cdot t_f \cdot E_{fe}}{D}} \right) $$ ### Step 5: Axial Load Capacity The factored axial capacity of the FRP-confined column is: $$ P_n = 0.85 \cdot f_{cc}' \cdot (A_g - A_{st}) + f_y \cdot A_{st} $$ For design: $$ \phi P_n \geq P_u \quad (\phi = 0.65 \text{ for tied columns, 0.70 for spiral, ACI 318-19}) $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style 14-mark problem) > **Problem:** > A circular RC column has **D = 400 mm**, gross area $A_g = 125,664 \text{ mm}^2$, longitudinal steel area $A_{st} = 1800 \text{ mm}^2$, $f_y = 415$ MPa, $f_c' = 25$ MPa, $E_c = 25,000$ MPa. It is wrapped with **2 layers of GFRP**, each of thickness **t_f = 1.2 mm**, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.025$. > > Determine the **confined compressive strength** and **ultimate axial capacity**. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Total FRP thickness: $$ t_{f,\text{total}} = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2:** Effective FRP strain and stress: $$ \varepsilon_{fe} = 0.586 \times 0.025 = 0.01465 $$ $$ f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} = 70{,}000 \times 0.01465 = 1025.5 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3:** Lateral confining pressure: $$ f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_{f,\text{total}} \cdot f_{fe}}{D} = \dfrac{2 \times 2.4 \times 1025.5}{400} = 12.31 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4:** Confined concrete strength: $$ f_{cc}' = f_c' + 3.3 \cdot f_l = 25 + 3.3 \times 12.31 = 25 + 40.62 = 65.62 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 5:** Axial capacity (gross): $$ P_n = 0.85 \times 65.62 \times (125{,}664 - 1800) + 415 \times 1800 $$ $$ P_n = 55.78 \times 123{,}864 + 747{,}000 $$ $$ P_n = 6{,}909{,}534 + 747{,}000 = 7{,}656{,}534 \text{ N} \approx 7656.5 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 6:** Design capacity ($\phi = 0.65$): $$ \phi P_n = 0.65 \times 7656.5 = 4976.7 \text{ kN} $$ > **Result:** The confined column capacity (~4977 kN) is approximately **2.0–2.2 times** the unconfined capacity, demonstrating the dramatic strengthening effect of FRP jacketing. --- ## 3.3 Failure Modes of FRP Jacketed Members (Analytical Matrix) | Failure Mode | Description | Governing Equation / Limit | |---|---|---| | **FRP Rupture** | Tensile rupture of fibers | $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_{fu}$ | | **FRP Debonding** | Adhesive failure at concrete–FRP interface | $\tau_{bond} \leq 1.5$ MPa (typical) | | **Concrete Crushing** | Triaxial compression failure | $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{cu}$ | | **Lap Splice Failure** | Insufficient overlap of FRP wraps | $L_{lap} \geq 150$ mm (ACI) | | **Peeling** | Local debonding near cracks | $s_f \leq d/4$ (anchor spacing) | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of FRP Jacketing System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Damaged Concrete Member] --> B[Surface Preparation
Grinding, Cleaning, Drying] B --> C[Primer Application
Low Viscosity Epoxy] C --> D[Resin Putty Application
Surface Defect Filling] D --> E[Fiber Wrapping
Wet Layup / Prepreg] E --> F[Fiber Impregnation
Resin Saturation] F --> G[Orientation Control
Hoop / Longitudinal] G --> H[Curing Stage
Ambient / Elevated Temp] H --> I[Quality Inspection
Tap Test, Pull-off Test] I --> J[Strengthened Member
Confinement Active] K[Carbon Fibers] --> E L[Glass Fibers] --> E M[Aramid Fibers] --> E N[Epoxy Resin] --> F O[Vinyl Ester Resin] --> F style A fill:#ffe0e0 style J fill:#d0f0d0 style E fill:#fff4b0 style F fill:#b0e0ff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Confinement Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Axial Load P] --> B[Concrete Core] B --> C{Lateral Expansion
Poissons Effect} C --> D[FRP Jacket
Tension Activated] D --> E[Confining Pressure fl] E --> F[Triaxial Stress State
Increased Strength] F --> G[Enhanced Axial Capacity
Pn = 0.85 fcc Ag] D --> H[Improved Ductility
Increased Strain] H --> I[Seismic Energy Dissipation] style A fill:#ffcccc style G fill:#ccffcc style I fill:#cce5ff style E fill:#fff2cc ``` ## 4.3 FRP Wrapping Configuration Layout ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Column Cross Section] --> B[Rectangular Section] A --> C[Circular Section] A --> D[Elliptical Section] B --> B1[Continuous Wrap
4 Sides] B --> B2[Partial Wrap
2 Sides] B --> B3[Corner Radius
R greater than 20 mm] C --> C1[Full Continuous Wrap
Maximum Confinement] C --> C2[Hoop Orientation
90 degrees to Axis] D --> D1[Variable Pressure
Less Effective] style C1 fill:#90ee90 style B1 fill:#ffe4b5 style D1 fill:#ffcccb ``` ## 4.4 Process-Parameter Relationship Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Fiber Type] --> R1[Tensile Capacity] P2[Number of Layers] --> R1 P3[Wrap Orientation] --> R2[Confinement Efficiency] P1 --> R2 P4[Resin Type] --> R3[Stress Transfer] P5[Surface Prep] --> R3 P6[Corner Radius] --> R2 P7[Curing Conditions] --> R4[Long Term Performance] P8[Environmental Exposure] --> R4 R1 --> OUT1[Strength Gain] R2 --> OUT2[Ductility Gain] R3 --> OUT3[Bond Integrity] R4 --> OUT4[Durability] style OUT1 fill:#90ee90 style OUT2 fill:#90ee90 style OUT3 fill:#ffe4b5 style OUT4 fill:#ffe4b5 ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define FRP jacketing. List any four advantages of FRP over conventional steel jacketing for column strengthening.** **Model Answer:** **FRP Jacketing** is a structural strengthening technique in which high-strength fibers (carbon, glass, or aramid) embedded in a polymer matrix are externally bonded to a structural member to enhance its load capacity, ductility, and confinement. **Advantages over steel jacketing:** 1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — negligible increase in dead load. 2. **Corrosion resistance** — no rusting, ideal for coastal/marine environments. 3. **Tailorability** — fiber type, orientation, and thickness can be selected for specific demand. 4. **Minimal geometric change** — preserves aesthetics and floor space. 5. Faster installation with **no welding, no heavy equipment.** 6. **Excellent fatigue and chemical resistance.** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **What is passive confinement? How does FRP jacketing achieve it in RC columns?** **Model Answer:** **Passive confinement** is the lateral pressure generated on a concrete core *automatically* when the core attempts to expand under axial load, resisted by an external wrapping. **Mechanism in FRP jacketing:** - Under axial load, the concrete core expands laterally (Poisson effect, $\nu \approx 0.15$–$0.20$). - The FRP wrap, bonded externally, resists this expansion. - The FRP develops **tensile hoop stresses**, which translate into a **radial confining pressure** $f_l$ on the concrete. - This puts the core in a **tri-axial compressive state**, increasing $f_c'$ and $\varepsilon_{cu}$. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]** > > **(a)** Explain in detail the constituent materials of an FRP system used in column jacketing. List the typical mechanical properties of CFRP and GFRP. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the step-by-step procedure of FRP installation using the **wet layup system**, including surface preparation, primer, putty, and curing. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The FRP composite system consists of **three key constituents:** **(i) Fibers (Reinforcement):** - Provide tensile strength and stiffness. - Available as **unidirectional sheets, woven fabrics, or rovings**. - **CFRP (Carbon):** $f_{fu} \approx 3500$–$4900$ MPa; $E_f \approx 200$–$235$ GPa; density $\approx 1.6$ g/cc. - **GFRP (E-Glass):** $f_{fu} \approx 1900$–$3000$ MPa; $E_f \approx 70$–$90$ GPa; density $\approx 2.5$ g/cc. **(ii) Matrix (Resin):** - Transfers stress from concrete to fibers; protects fibers from environmental damage. - Types: **Epoxy** (most common, 7–14 day cure, $f_t \approx 30$–$80$ MPa), **Polyester** (cheaper, lower performance), **Vinyl ester** (intermediate). **(iii) Adhesives and Primers:** - **Primer** seals surface pores. - **Putty** fills minor surface defects. - **Saturant** wets out fiber sheets. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Wet Layup Installation Procedure:** 1. **Substrate Preparation:** - Remove all loose, deteriorated concrete (chipping, sandblasting). - Round off all corners to a radius **R ≥ 20 mm** (to prevent stress concentration in FRP). - Achieve surface roughness equivalent to sandpaper grit 60. - Surface must be **clean, dry, and sound** (minimum tensile pull-off strength ≥ 1.7 MPa). 2. **Primer Application:** - Mix two-part epoxy primer as per manufacturer ratio. - Apply with brush/roller to a **wet film thickness of 100–200 μm**. - Allow tack-free curing (typically 24 hours). 3. **Putty / Resin Paste Application:** - Fill all bug holes, honeycombs, and surface irregularities. - Level the surface for uniform fiber contact. 4. **Fiber Sheet Impregnation:** - Cut fabric to required dimensions. - Apply saturating resin to the primed surface. - Place the fabric; press and roll to remove air pockets and ensure full wetting. - Apply additional resin coat on top (wet-on-wet or after gel). 5. **Multi-Layer Application:** - Repeat for additional layers, allowing each to cure. - Maintain **lap length ≥ 150 mm** for hoop wraps. 6. **Curing:** - Maintain ambient temperature **15–32°C** for at least **24–72 hours**. - Avoid mechanical loading during curing. 7. **Quality Control:** - Visual inspection, tap test, pull-off test (≥ 1.7 MPa bond strength). --- ### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]** > > **(a)** Derive the expression for the **confined compressive strength** of a circular RC column wrapped with FRP using the Lam & Teng model. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A circular RC column of **D = 500 mm**, $f_c' = 30$ MPa, $A_{st} = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$, $f_y = 415$ MPa, is wrapped with **3 layers of CFRP** (each 0.165 mm thick, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.015$). Compute the **confined concrete strength** and **ultimate axial capacity** using ACI 440.2R provisions. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Derivation of Confined Strength — Lam & Teng Model:** For a circular column of diameter **D** wrapped with **n** layers of FRP (each of thickness $t_f$): **Step 1:** Total FRP thickness: $$ t_{f,tot} = n \cdot t_f $$ **Step 2:** Effective FRP strain (per ACI 440.2R-17): $$ \varepsilon_{fe} = \kappa_\varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} = 0.586 \cdot \varepsilon_{fu} $$ **Step 3:** Effective FRP stress: $$ f_{fe} = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} $$ **Step 4:** Lateral confining pressure (force equilibrium of half FRP shell): $$ 2 \cdot f_l \cdot D = 2 \cdot t_{f,tot} \cdot f_{fe} $$ $$ \Rightarrow f_l = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_{f,tot} \cdot f_{fe}}{D} $$ **Step 5:** Confined compressive strength: $$ f_{cc}' = f_c' + k_1 \cdot f_l \quad \text{with} \quad k_1 = 3.3 $$ $$ \boxed{f_{cc}' = f_c' + \dfrac{6.6 \cdot t_{f,tot} \cdot f_{fe}}{D}} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $D = 500$ mm, $f_c' = 30$ MPa, $A_{st} = 2500$ mm², $f_y = 415$ MPa, $n = 3$, $t_f = 0.165$ mm, $E_f = 230{,}000$ MPa, $\varepsilon_{fu} = 0.015$. **Step 1:** Total FRP thickness: $$ t_{f,tot} = 3 \times 0.165 = 0.495 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2:** Effective FRP strain: $$ \varepsilon_{fe} = 0.586 \times 0.015 = 0.00879 $$ **Step 3:** Effective FRP stress: $$ f_{fe} = 230{,}000 \times 0.00879 = 2021.7 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4:** Confining pressure: $$ f_l = \dfrac{2 \times 0.495 \times 2021.7}{500} = \dfrac{2001.5}{500} = 4.00 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 5:** Confined concrete strength: $$ f_{cc}' = 30 + 3.3 \times 4.00 = 30 + 13.2 = 43.2 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 6:** Gross area: $$ A_g = \dfrac{\pi \times 500^2}{4} = 196{,}349.5 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 7:** Nominal axial capacity: $$ P_n = 0.85 \cdot f_{cc}' \cdot (A_g - A_{st}) + f_y \cdot A_{st} $$ $$ P_n = 0.85 \times 43.2 \times (196{,}349.5 - 2500) + 415 \times 2500 $$ $$ P_n = 36.72 \times 193{,}849.5 + 1{,}037{,}500 $$ $$ P_n = 7{,}118{,}555 + 1{,}037{,}500 = 8{,}156{,}055 \text{ N} \approx 8156 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 8:** Design capacity ($\phi = 0.65$): $$ \phi P_n = 0.65 \times 8156 = 5301.4 \text{ kN} $$ > **Answer:** $f_{cc}' = 43.2$ MPa, $\phi P_n = 5301.4$ kN. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes (Mark-Deductions Identified by KTU Valuators):** > > 1. **Forgetting the strain efficiency factor ($\kappa_\varepsilon = 0.586$)** — using $\varepsilon_{fu}$ directly is the **#1 mistake**. Loss: **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Using $k_1 = 3.3$ for rectangular columns** — it is invalid; rectangular sections use a **shape factor** and effectiveness coefficient. Loss: **2 marks**. > 3. **Not rounding corner radius (R < 20 mm)** — premature FRP rupture will occur, question is invalid. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Unit errors** — D in mm, $E_f$ in MPa, result in MPa; do not mix mm with m. Loss: **1 mark**. > 5. **Ignoring lap splice length (≥ 150 mm)** in multi-layer wraps — practical implementation marks lost. Loss: **1 mark**. > 6. **Writing $E_{fe} = E_f$** — should be $0.55 E_f$ (ACI 440). Loss: **1 mark**. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **FRP** = Fibers + Resin (composite). Three fiber types: **C, G, A** = Carbon, Glass, Aramid. - **CFRP** has the highest modulus; **GFRP** is the cheapest and most commonly used in India. - **FRP jacketing = passive confinement** — works via Poisson's effect. - **Key formula:** $f_{cc}' = f_c' + 3.3 \cdot f_l$ (circular section, Lam & Teng). - **Lateral pressure:** $f_l = 2 t_{f,tot} f_{fe} / D$. - **Effective strain factor:** $\kappa_\varepsilon = 0.586$ (ACI 440.2R). - **Corner radius** must be **≥ 20 mm** for rectangular sections. - **Lap splice** of FRP wraps must be **≥ 150 mm**. - **Surface pull-off strength** must be **≥ 1.7 MPa** before installation. - **Codes to cite:** ACI 440.2R-17, IS 15911:2018, fib Bulletin 14. - **Failure modes:** FRP rupture, debonding, lap splice failure, concrete crushing. - **Advantages:** light, corrosion-free, high strength, easy install. - **Limitations:** poor fire resistance, brittle failure, degrades under UV without coating, expensive for CFRP. - **Wet layup** = on-site impregnation; **prepreg** = factory-pre-impregnated; **NSM** = Near-Surface Mounted (different technique). - **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always show the **5-step derivation** + numerical with units. - **For 3-mark questions:** Definition + any 3–4 advantages/limitations, or mechanism diagram. - **Typical exam trap:** asking for shear contribution of FRP — remember $V_f = A_{fv} f_{fe} d / s_f$. - **Triaxial stress state** is the *core physics* behind FRP confinement — remember it.

topics.title: Strengthening of Structural Elements Due to Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strengthening of Structural Elements Due to Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Damage Rehabilitation** is defined as the systematic process of assessing, repairing, and restoring the load-carrying capacity, serviceability, and durability of structural elements (concrete, steel, masonry) that have undergone physical, chemical, and mechanical degradation due to exposure to elevated temperatures (typically **300°C to 1100°C**) during a fire incident.

When concrete, steel, or composite structural elements are exposed to high temperatures during a fire, they undergo irreversible changes in their micro-structural composition, leading to:
- Loss of compressive strength in concrete
- Loss of yield strength in steel reinforcement
- Spalling of concrete cover
- Loss of bond between concrete and steel
- Deformation and deflection of structural members

The strengthening of these fire-damaged elements involves **damage assessment**, **removal of unsound material**, and application of suitable **repair/strengthening techniques** to restore or enhance the original structural capacity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Burnt Toast" Analogy**: Imagine leaving a slice of bread in a toaster too long. The bread turns black, becomes brittle, loses its moisture, and structurally it can no longer hold toppings without crumbling. If you try to "repair" it by spreading butter, it breaks. But if you **scrape off the burnt layer** and then **add a fresh layer of reinforcement** (like a supportive base), the toast can be functional again.

Similarly, when a structural element is exposed to fire:
- The outer layer (concrete cover) becomes like the burnt toast — cracked, spalled, and weak.
- The inner core (concrete + steel) may still have some residual strength.
- The repair strategy is to **remove the damaged layer** (like scraping burnt toast) and **restore it with new material** (new concrete, FRP wrapping, jacketing, etc.).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following thermal thresholds are critical to fire damage assessment:
> - **Critical Temperature for Steel**: **550°C** (above this, steel loses 50% of its yield strength)
> - **Critical Temperature for Concrete**: **300°C** (significant strength loss begins)
> - **Calcite Disintegration Temperature**: **573°C** (quartz transformation in aggregate)
> - **Lime Dehydration Temperature**: **700°C–900°C** (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂)
> - **Standard Fire Exposure Curve**: **ISO 834** or **ASTM E119** (time-temperature relationship)
> - **Spalling Initiation Temperature**: **250°C–300°C** (for high-strength concrete)

## 1.4 Types of Fire Damage

| Damage Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Surface Damage** | Discoloration, soot deposition, minor surface cracking |
| **Spalling** | Detachment of concrete cover due to pore pressure |
| **Explosive Spalling** | Sudden violent detachment during fire exposure |
| **Strength Loss** | Reduction in compressive and tensile strength |
| **Deformation** | Deflection, buckling, sagging of members |
| **Bond Loss** | Reduction in bond between concrete and rebar |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effects of Fire on Structural Materials

### 2.1.1 Effect of Fire on Concrete

Concrete undergoes progressive degradation when subjected to elevated temperatures:

**Stage 1 (100°C – 200°C):**
- Free water begins to evaporate
- Initial expansion of aggregate
- Minor strength reduction (~5–10%)

**Stage 2 (200°C – 300°C):**
- CSH (Calcium Silicate Hydrate) gel begins to dehydrate
- C-S-H bond breakdown initiates
- Strength reduction: 15–25%
- *Pink coloration* may appear due to iron compounds

**Stage 3 (300°C – 500°C):**
- Portlandite (Ca(OH)₂) decomposes into CaO + H₂O
- Significant strength loss (25–40%)
- Visible cracking begins
- *Whitish-gray color*

**Stage 4 (500°C – 700°C):**
- Limestone aggregate calcination (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂)
- Quartz transformation at **573°C** (volume expansion ~5%)
- Severe strength loss (40–60%)
- *Buff/pink color*

**Stage 5 (700°C – 1000°C):**
- Complete destruction of cement paste matrix
- Concrete becomes chalky, friable
- Strength loss: 70–90%
- *Whitish color, granular texture*

### 2.1.2 Effect of Fire on Steel Reinforcement

Steel exhibits a more predictable degradation pattern:

| Temperature (°C) | Residual Yield Strength (%) | Color Change |
|---|---|---|
| **20** | **100** | Original (grey) |
| **300** | **80** | Slight darkening |
| **400** | **70** | Dark blue/black |
| **500** | **60** | Blue-black |
| **600** | **40** | Dark brown |
| **700** | **20** | Light brown |
| **800+** | **< 10** | Grey/white |

### 2.1.3 Effect of Fire on Structural Steel Members

- **Buckling** due to loss of stiffness
- **Lateral-torsional buckling** of beams
- **Connection failures** at welded/bolted joints
- **Distortion** of cross-sections

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure for Fire Damage Rehabilitation

The rehabilitation process follows a structured methodology:

1. **Preliminary Visual Assessment** — Identify areas of spalling, cracking, and deformation
2. **Documentation of Damage** — Photographic records, sketches, temperature mapping
3. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — Rebound hammer, ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV)
4. **Destructive Testing** — Core extraction, petrographic analysis
5. **Structural Analysis** — Recalculate load capacity using residual strength
6. **Decision Making** — Repair vs. Demolish
7. **Execution of Repair Strategy** — Material removal, surface preparation, application of repair system
8. **Post-Repair Testing** — Load testing, monitoring

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on fire damage repair.

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $$f_c'(T) = f_c' \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{800}\right]$$ | Residual compressive strength of concrete at temperature T (°C) | Estimating residual capacity |
| $$f_y(T) = f_y \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{900}\right]$$ | Residual yield strength of steel at temperature T (°C) | Steel strength assessment |
| $$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$ | Thermal strain in concrete/steel | Thermal expansion analysis |
| $$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$$ | Free thermal expansion | Deformation calculation |
| $$f_{c,residual} = k_c \cdot f_c'$$ | Residual strength (where k_c is residual strength factor) | NDT correlation |
| $$R = \frac{\rho_{damaged}}{\rho_{original}}$$ | Residual strength ratio (sonic/ultrasonic) | UPV-based assessment |
| $$T_{max} = 20 + 800(1 - f_c'/f_{c,peak})$$ | Maximum temperature experienced | Back-calculating fire severity |

Where:
- $f_c'(T)$ = compressive strength at temperature T
- $f_c'$ = original compressive strength at 20°C
- $f_y(T)$ = yield strength of steel at temperature T
- $f_y$ = original yield strength at 20°C
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (concrete: $\alpha \approx 10 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C; steel: $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C)
- $\rho$ = ultrasonic pulse velocity through concrete
- $R$ = velocity ratio
- $k_c$ = residual strength factor (typically **0.3 – 1.0**)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fire damage rehabilitation is critical in:
> - **High-rise buildings** (post-fire floor restoration)
> - **Industrial structures** (refineries, power plants, chemical facilities)
> - **Tunnels and underground structures** (where evacuation is difficult)
> - **Heritage structures** (where demolition is not an option)
> - **Bridges and transportation infrastructure** (after tanker fires, accidents)
> - **Nuclear power plants** (post-fire containment integrity)

## 2.5 Strengthening Techniques for Fire-Damaged Elements

### 2.5.1 Concrete Elements (Columns, Beams, Slabs)

1. **Concrete Jacketing**
   - Removal of damaged concrete cover
   - Application of new reinforcement (longitudinal + lateral ties)
   - Casting of new concrete (micro-concrete or polymer-modified mortar)
   - Typical thickness: **50–100 mm**

2. **FRP (Fiber Reinforced Polymer) Wrapping**
   - Carbon Fiber (CFRP) or Glass Fiber (GFRP) wrapping
   - Provides confinement and shear strengthening
   - Used when column capacity enhancement is required
   - Adhesion using epoxy resin

3. **Steel Plate Jacketing**
   - Encasing the element with steel plates
   - Welding or bolting the plates
   - Grouting the gap between steel and existing concrete

4. **Shotcrete / Gunite Application**
   - Sprayed concrete at high velocity
   - Effective for overhead and vertical surfaces
   - Pneumatically applied

5. **Polymer-Modified Mortar (PMM)**
   - Used for patch repairs of small damaged areas
   - Contains polymer additives for improved bond

### 2.5.2 Steel Elements

1. **Heat Straightening**
   - Controlled heating to induce plastic deformation
   - Used to restore deformed steel members

2. **Welding of New Plates**
   - Adding additional steel to restore cross-section
   - Requires post-weld heat treatment

3. **Replacement of Members**
   - In cases of severe damage, complete member replacement

### 2.5.3 Masonry Elements

1. **Re-pointing and Crack Injection**
   - Epoxy or polyurethane injection for cracks
   - Repointing of mortar joints

2. **Wall Jacketing**
   - Ferrocement or reinforced plaster overlays
   - Welded wire mesh + cementitious coating

## 2.6 Materials Used for Post-Fire Repair

| Material | Application | Properties |
|---|---|---|
| **Micro-concrete** | Jacketing, large volume repairs | High strength, low shrinkage, flowable |
| **Polymer-modified mortar (PMM)** | Patch repairs | Improved bond, reduced permeability |
| **Epoxy resins** | Crack injection, bonding agent | High bond strength, chemical resistance |
| **FRP composites (CFRP/GFRP)** | External strengthening | High tensile strength, lightweight |
| **Geopolymer concrete** | Sustainable repair | Fire-resistant, low CO₂ footprint |
| **Refractory concrete** | High-temperature areas | Withstands up to **1600°C** |
| **Shotcrete** | Large area repairs | Rapid application, good bond |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Residual Strength of Concrete After Fire

The empirical relationship for the residual compressive strength of concrete after exposure to elevated temperatures is derived from experimental data fitted to a linear approximation (Eurocode-based simplification):

**Step 1: Define the problem**
We need to find an equation that gives the residual strength $f_c'(T)$ as a function of the original strength $f_c'$ and the maximum temperature $T$ experienced by the concrete.

**Step 2: Assume linear degradation**
Based on experimental observations (Abrams, 1971; Malhotra, 1956), the relationship between residual strength and temperature can be approximated linearly between **20°C and 800°C** (beyond which concrete is considered structurally unusable):

$$\frac{f_c'(T)}{f_c'} = 1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{800}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to solve for $f_c'(T)$**

$$f_c'(T) = f_c' \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{800}\right]$$

**Step 4: Verify boundary conditions**
- At $T = 20°C$: $f_c'(T) = f_c' [1 - 0/800] = f_c' \cdot 1 = f_c'$ ✓
- At $T = 820°C$: $f_c'(T) = f_c' [1 - 800/800] = f_c' \cdot 0 = 0$ ✓ (Concrete has no strength)

**Step 5: Refine for higher accuracy (Eurocode 2 Part 1-2 form)**

The Eurocode 2 gives a more refined relationship using a tabular approach:

$$k_c(T) = \frac{f_c'(T)}{f_c'}$$

For siliceous aggregate concrete:
- $T \leq 100°C$: $k_c = 1.00$
- $T = 200°C$: $k_c = 0.95$
- $T = 300°C$: $k_c = 0.85$
- $T = 400°C$: $k_c = 0.75$
- $T = 500°C$: $k_c = 0.60$
- $T = 600°C$: $k_c = 0.45$
- $T = 700°C$: $k_c = 0.30$
- $T = 800°C$: $k_c = 0.15$

For calcareous aggregate concrete, the values are slightly higher (better fire resistance).

## 3.2 Derivation: Residual Strength of Steel Reinforcement

**Step 1: Establish boundary conditions**
- At $T = 20°C$: $f_y(T) = f_y$ (100% retention)
- At $T = 800°C$: $f_y(T) \approx 0.1 f_y$ (negligible strength)
- Linear interpolation gives:

$$\frac{f_y(T)}{f_y} = 1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{900}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $f_y(T)$**

$$f_y(T) = f_y \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{900}\right]$$

**Step 3: Verify**
- At $T = 20°C$: $f_y(T) = f_y [1 - 0] = f_y$ ✓
- At $T = 920°C$: $f_y(T) = 0$ ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A reinforced concrete column (300 mm × 300 mm) was exposed to a fire. Original characteristic strength of concrete is **f_c' = 30 MPa** and steel yield strength is **f_y = 500 MPa**. The fire damage assessment using petrographic analysis indicates that the maximum temperature experienced by the column was **500°C**. Determine:
> (a) Residual compressive strength of concrete
> (b) Residual yield strength of steel
> (c) Whether repair is feasible or if replacement is required

### Solution:

**Part (a): Residual compressive strength of concrete**

$$f_c'(T) = f_c' \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{800}\right]$$

$$f_c'(500) = 30 \left[1 - \frac{(500 - 20)}{800}\right]$$

$$f_c'(500) = 30 \left[1 - \frac{480}{800}\right]$$

$$f_c'(500) = 30 \left[1 - 0.6\right]$$

$$f_c'(500) = 30 \times 0.4 = 12 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b): Residual yield strength of steel**

$$f_y(T) = f_y \left[1 - \frac{(T - 20)}{900}\right]$$

$$f_y(500) = 500 \left[1 - \frac{(500 - 20)}{900}\right]$$

$$f_y(500) = 500 \left[1 - \frac{480}{900}\right]$$

$$f_y(500) = 500 \left[1 - 0.5333\right]$$

$$f_y(500) = 500 \times 0.4667 = 233.35 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (c): Feasibility of Repair**

| Parameter | Original | Residual | Strength Loss (%) | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concrete | **30 MPa** | **12 MPa** | **60%** | Significant loss — repair needed |
| Steel | **500 MPa** | **233.35 MPa** | **53.3%** | Severe loss — replacement of bars may be needed |

**Conclusion:** Since both materials have lost more than **50%** of their original strength, the column requires **extensive jacketing** (concrete jacketing or FRP wrapping) to restore capacity. **Complete replacement** is recommended if the residual strength is less than **40%** of the original.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Repair Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix helps in selecting appropriate repair techniques based on damage severity:

| Damage Severity | Temperature Range | Strength Loss | Recommended Repair |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light** | < 300°C | < 25% | Surface cleaning, polymer-modified mortar patch |
| **Moderate** | 300°C – 600°C | 25% – 60% | Concrete jacketing, FRP wrapping |
| **Severe** | 600°C – 800°C | 60% – 90% | Concrete jacketing + additional reinforcement |
| **Critical** | > 800°C | > 90% | Demolition and reconstruction |

## 3.5 Detailed Steps of Concrete Jacketing (Most Common Method)

**Step 1:** Prop and shore the structural member to relieve loads
**Step 2:** Remove all damaged concrete cover using chipping hammers or hydro-jetting
**Step 3:** Clean the exposed reinforcement; remove scale, soot, and debris
**Step 4:** Cut back damaged rebars to sound material and weld new bars of same diameter
**Step 5:** Provide additional longitudinal reinforcement if required
**Step 6:** Install additional lateral ties/stirrups with closer spacing
**Step 7:** Apply bonding agent (epoxy slurry) on the prepared surface
**Step 8:** Place formwork and cast micro-concrete or polymer-modified concrete
**Step 9:** Cure the new concrete for at least **7 to 14 days**
**Step 10:** Remove props gradually and apply protective coating

## 3.6 Step-by-Step FRP Wrapping Procedure

**Step 1:** Surface preparation — sandblasting, grinding to expose sound concrete
**Step 2:** Surface cleaning — remove dust and contaminants
**Step 3:** Primer application — to seal the substrate
**Step 4:** Putty/filler application — to level surface irregularities
**Step 5:** CFRP/GFRP fabric cutting to required dimensions
**Step 6:** Epoxy resin application on the surface
**Step 7:** FRP fabric placement and rolling to remove air bubbles
**Step 8:** Additional resin coating for saturation
**Step 9:** Curing for at least **24 hours** at room temperature
**Step 10:** Application of fire-resistant topcoat (since FRP loses strength at ~80°C)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> List any **three** important visual indicators used to assess the severity of fire damage in a reinforced concrete structural element.

### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> The three important visual indicators of fire damage severity in a reinforced concrete element are:
>
> 1. **Color Change of Concrete Surface**: The color of concrete changes with temperature exposure. Pink/red color indicates temperatures of **300°C – 600°C**, while whitish-gray color indicates temperatures above **800°C**.
>
> 2. **Spalling Pattern**: The presence, depth, and pattern of concrete spalling indicates the severity. *Explosive spalling* with deep cover loss suggests temperatures exceeding **500°C**.
>
> 3. **Crack Pattern and Deformation**: Network cracking (crazing) and visible deflection of members indicate thermal stress and loss of stiffness. Deflection exceeding **span/250** indicates severe damage.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**

> What is the **critical temperature** at which steel reinforcement loses **50%** of its yield strength, and why is this value significant in fire damage assessment?

### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical temperature for steel reinforcement** is **550°C**. At this temperature, steel loses approximately **50%** of its yield strength.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. This temperature serves as a **threshold limit** for determining the usability of fire-damaged reinforcement.
> 2. If petrographic analysis or metallurgical examination indicates that the steel was exposed to temperatures above **550°C**, the bars must be **replaced** rather than reused.
> 3. It is used in **post-fire structural assessment** to estimate the residual load-carrying capacity of the structural member.
> 4. This value is codified in IS 456, ACI 216, and Eurocode 2 for fire-resistant design.
