## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

> (a) **Explain in detail** the effect of fire on the mechanical properties of concrete. Discuss the various stages of degradation with reference to temperature ranges and corresponding strength losses. (**7 Marks**)
>
> (b) **Describe** the step-by-step procedure of concrete jacketing as a strengthening technique for fire-damaged reinforced concrete columns, including the materials used and quality control measures. (**7 Marks**)

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) Solution (7 Marks)

The effect of fire on concrete is temperature-dependent and follows a progressive degradation pattern:

**Stage 1 — Loss of Free Water (100°C – 200°C):**
At this temperature range, the free water in the capillary pores begins to evaporate. There is a slight expansion of the aggregate. The compressive strength reduction is minimal, around **5% – 10%**. No visible damage occurs.

**Stage 2 — CSH Dehydration (200°C – 300°C):**
The C-S-H (Calcium Silicate Hydrate) gel, which is the primary binder in concrete, begins to dehydrate. This causes a noticeable reduction in compressive strength (**15% – 25%**). Micro-cracks begin to form, and the concrete develops a pinkish tint due to iron compound transformation.

**Stage 3 — Portlandite Decomposition (300°C – 500°C):**
The Ca(OH)₂ (portlandite) in the cement matrix decomposes into CaO and water vapor. The strength reduction reaches **25% – 40%**. Visible cracks appear on the surface, and the concrete becomes more porous.

**Stage 4 — Aggregate Transformation (500°C – 700°C):**
At **573°C**, quartz (a common mineral in siliceous aggregates) undergoes a phase transformation with a **5% volume expansion**, causing internal stresses and severe cracking. Limestone aggregates calcine, releasing CO₂. Strength loss ranges from **40% – 60%**.

**Stage 5 — Complete Degradation (700°C – 1000°C):**
The cement paste matrix is completely destroyed. The concrete becomes chalky, friable, and granular. Strength loss exceeds **70%**, and the concrete is structurally unusable. The color becomes whitish-gray.

| Stage | Temperature | Strength Loss | Visual Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100–200°C | 5%–10% | None |
| 2 | 200–300°C | 15%–25% | Pink color, micro-cracks |
| 3 | 300–500°C | 25%–40% | Surface cracks, spalling |
| 4 | 500–700°C | 40%–60% | Deep cracks, color change |
| 5 | 700–1000°C | > 70% | Chalky, friable |

#### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks)

**Concrete Jacketing Procedure for Fire-Damaged Columns:**

**Step 1: Load Transfer and Propping**
Before commencing any repair, the column must be properly propped and shored to transfer the construction loads to adjacent members. Hydraulic jacks or needle beams may be used to relieve the existing load.

**Step 2: Removal of Damaged Concrete**
All spalled, cracked, and heat-damaged concrete cover is removed using pneumatic chipping hammers or hydro-demolition. The depth of removal should extend until sound, undamaged concrete is exposed (typically determined by sounding or NDT).

**Step 3: Cleaning and Surface Preparation**
The exposed reinforcement is cleaned using wire brushes, sandblasting, or needle scalers to remove soot, scale, and rust. The substrate concrete surface is roughened to achieve an adequate bond profile.

**Step 4: Replacement of Damaged Reinforcement**
Heavily damaged rebars (those exposed to > 550°C) are cut back to sound material. New reinforcement bars of the same diameter and grade are welded or lapped to the existing bars. Sufficient lap length (typically **50 times the bar diameter** for tension) must be provided.

**Step 5: Installation of Additional Reinforcement**
If the original column capacity needs enhancement, additional longitudinal bars are added, and closer-spaced lateral ties (stirrups) are provided. The ties are typically spaced at **100 mm – 150 mm c/c** to ensure confinement.

**Step 6: Application of Bonding Agent**
An epoxy-based bonding agent or a polymer-modified cementitious slurry is applied to the prepared concrete surface to ensure monolithic behavior between the old and new concrete.

**Step 7: Casting of New Concrete**
Formwork is erected, leaving a minimum gap of **50 mm** for jacket thickness. Micro-concrete (with maximum aggregate size of **6–8 mm**), polymer-modified concrete, or self-compacting concrete (SCC) is poured or pumped into the formwork. The high flowability of micro-concrete ensures proper filling around the dense reinforcement cage.

**Step 8: Curing**
The newly cast concrete must be cured for a minimum of **7 to 14 days** using wet gunny bags, curing compounds, or water sprinkling to prevent shrinkage cracks.

**Step 9: Quality Control**
- Slump test for workability
- Cube testing for compressive strength (**at 7 and 28 days**)
- Rebound hammer testing on the hardened jacket
- UPV testing for homogeneity
- Pull-off test for bond strength

**Step 10: Protective Coating**
A final protective coating (anti-carbonation, anti-chloride) is applied to enhance the durability of the repaired column.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

> (a) **Discuss** the various **non-destructive testing (NDT) methods** used for assessment of fire-damaged concrete structures. Compare their effectiveness and limitations. (**7 Marks**)
>
> (b) **Explain** the application of **Fibre Reinforced Polymer (FRP) wrapping** for strengthening fire-damaged columns. Include design considerations, material properties, and the step-by-step installation procedure. (**7 Marks**)

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) Solution (7 Marks)

**Non-Destructive Testing Methods for Fire-Damaged Concrete:**

**1. Rebound Hammer Test (Schmidt Hammer):**
This test measures the surface hardness of concrete, which correlates with compressive strength. A spring-loaded mass impacts the concrete surface, and the rebound distance is recorded as the *rebound number*.
- *Advantages*: Quick, simple, inexpensive
- *Limitations*: Only tests the surface (top **20–30 mm**), affected by surface carbonation, moisture, and texture. Not reliable for fire-damaged concrete where surface layer is compromised.

**2. Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test:**
This test measures the velocity of ultrasonic pulses passing through concrete. The velocity depends on the elastic modulus and density of concrete.
- *Velocity > 4.5 km/s*: Excellent quality
- *Velocity 3.5–4.5 km/s*: Good
- *Velocity 3.0–3.5 km/s*: Doubtful
- *Velocity < 3.0 km/s*: Poor
- *Advantages*: Detects internal cracks, voids, and delamination
- *Limitations*: Requires access to both sides of the member, sensitive to reinforcement and moisture content

**3. Infrared Thermography:**
This method uses infrared cameras to detect surface temperature variations, which indicate subsurface defects like delamination, voids, and moisture ingress.
- *Advantages*: Rapid scanning of large areas, non-contact
- *Limitations*: Affected by ambient temperature, surface emissivity, and solar radiation

**4. Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR):**
GPR uses electromagnetic waves to detect the location of reinforcement, voids, and delamination within concrete.
- *Advantages*: Detects rebar location and cover depth, identifies internal defects
- *Limitations*: Limited penetration depth, requires skilled interpretation

**5. Half-Cell Potential Test:**
Used to assess the corrosion potential of reinforcement by measuring the electrical potential difference between the rebar and a reference electrode.
- *Advantages*: Detects active corrosion
- *Limitations*: Indicates likelihood, not actual extent of corrosion

**6. Core Sampling and Petrographic Analysis:**
This involves extracting concrete cores and analyzing them in the laboratory for compressive strength, aggregate condition, cement paste, and signs of fire exposure.
- *Advantages*: Most accurate assessment
- *Limitations*: Destructive, expensive, limited sample size

| Method | Surface | Internal | Speed | Cost | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rebound Hammer | ✓ | ✗ | Fast | Low | Moderate |
| UPV | Limited | ✓ | Fast | Moderate | Good |
| IR Thermography | ✓ | Limited | Very Fast | High | Moderate |
| GPR | ✓ | ✓ | Fast | High | Good |
| Core Test | ✓ | ✓ | Slow | Very High | Excellent |

#### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks)

**FRP Wrapping for Fire-Damaged Columns:**

**Background:**
FRP (Fibre Reinforced Polymer) wrapping is a popular external strengthening technique where carbon, glass, or aramid fibres embedded in a polymer matrix are bonded to the surface of a structural element using epoxy resin. For columns, FRP wrapping provides **passive confinement**, enhancing axial load capacity and ductility.

**Design Considerations:**

1. **Type of FRP**:
   - **CFRP (Carbon FRP)**: Highest tensile strength (**> 2500 MPa**), low weight, high stiffness
   - **GFRP (Glass FRP)**: Lower cost, moderate strength, good ductility
   - **AFRP (Aramid FRP)**: Good impact resistance

2. **Number of Layers**: Typically 1–4 layers depending on the required strength enhancement
3. **Overlap Length**: Minimum **100 mm** for columns
4. **Column Geometry**: Effective for circular columns; for rectangular columns, the corners must be rounded with a minimum radius of **25 mm** to prevent stress concentration
5. **Fire Protection**: FRP materials lose significant strength at temperatures above **80°C–100°C**. Hence, a fire-resistant topcoat or intumescent coating must be applied.

**Material Properties of FRP Composites:**

| Property | CFRP | GFRP | AFRP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | **2500–4000** | **1500–2500** | **2000–3000** |
| Elastic Modulus (GPa) | **150–250** | **40–70** | **70–120** |
| Density (kg/m³) | **1600–1900** | **1800–2200** | **1400–1600** |
| Failure Strain (%) | **1.0–1.5** | **2.5–3.5** | **2.0–3.0** |

**Step-by-Step Installation Procedure:**

1. **Surface Preparation**: The concrete surface is ground, sandblasted, or hydro-blasted to remove weak laitance, dust, and contaminants. The surface should be dry and sound.
2. **Surface Levelling**: Any irregularities are filled with epoxy putty to provide a smooth, level surface.
3. **Primer Application**: A low-viscosity epoxy primer is applied to seal the substrate and improve adhesion.
4. **Resin Application**: The epoxy resin (saturant) is mixed in the correct ratio and applied uniformly to the prepared surface.
5. **FRP Fabric Placement**: The pre-cut FRP fabric is carefully placed onto the wet resin, ensuring proper alignment and orientation (typically perpendicular to the column axis for confinement).
6. **Rolling and De-bulking**: A ribbed roller is used to remove entrapped air bubbles and to ensure complete saturation of the fabric with resin.
7. **Multi-Layer Application**: For additional layers, the process is repeated after the previous layer has partially cured.
8. **Final Resin Coat**: A top coat of epoxy resin is applied to fully encapsulate the FRP system.
9. **Curing**: The system is allowed to cure for at least **24 hours** at ambient temperature (or accelerated curing at elevated temperatures).
10. **Fire Protection Coating**: A fire-resistant intumescent coating or cementitious fireproofing is applied as a topcoat to protect the FRP from elevated temperatures.

**Advantages of FRP Wrapping:**
- High strength-to-weight ratio
- Corrosion-resistant
- Rapid installation
- Minimal change in member dimensions
- Enhanced ductility and energy absorption

**Limitations:**
- High initial cost
- Brittle failure mode
- Reduced effectiveness at elevated temperatures (above **80°C**)
- Requires skilled labor and quality control
- Cannot be applied on wet surfaces

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing chemical and physical changes** in concrete during fire exposure: Students often forget the CSH dehydration, portlandite decomposition, and quartz transformation. These are **high-value points** worth 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Omitting curing period and quality control tests** in jacketing procedures: Examiners specifically look for *micro-concrete*, *curing for 7–14 days*, and *NDT verification*.
> 3. **Forgetting fire protection of FRP**: Many students describe FRP application but miss the critical point that FRP loses strength above **80°C**, requiring intumescent or cementitious fireproofing. This is a **favourite KTU question**.
> 4. **Not mentioning residual strength formulas**: Numerical problems often require the formulas $f_c'(T) = f_c'[1 - (T-20)/800]$ and $f_y(T) = f_y[1 - (T-20)/900]$. Students who skip derivations lose marks.
> 5. **Confusing epoxy and polyester resins**: KTU expects students to know that *epoxy* is the correct choice for structural FRP applications, not polyester.
> 6. **Ignoring environmental and health hazards** during repair work (silica dust, fumes from resins, etc.) — this is a frequently tested safety aspect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strengthening of Structural Elements Due to Fire**

**Key Temperatures to Memorize:**
- Concrete strength loss begins at **300°C**
- Critical temperature for steel reinforcement: **550°C** (50% strength loss)
- Quartz transformation in aggregate: **573°C**
- Portlandite decomposition: **400°C–500°C**
- Lime dehydration: **700°C–900°C**
- FRP loses strength above **80°C**
- Concrete becomes unusable above **800°C**

**Standard Fire Curves:**
- **ISO 834** standard fire curve
- **ASTM E119** standard fire curve
- **Hydrocarbon fire curve** (HCC) for petrochemical industries

**Color Indicators of Fire Damage:**
- **100°C–300°C**: No change
- **300°C–600°C**: Pink/Red
- **600°C–900°C**: Whitish-gray
- **> 900°C**: Buff or dark brown

**Key Formulas:**
- $f_c'(T) = f_c'[1 - (T-20)/800]$
- $f_y(T) = f_y[1 - (T-20)/900]$
- $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$

**NDT Methods:**
- Rebound Hammer, UPV, IR Thermography, GPR, Half-Cell Potential, Core Test

**Strengthening Techniques:**
- **For columns**: Concrete jacketing, FRP wrapping, Steel jacketing
- **For beams**: External FRP plates, Steel plate bonding, Section enlargement
- **For slabs**: Overlay, FRP strips, Additional reinforcement
- **For steel members**: Heat straightening, Welding, Replacement

**Materials for Repair:**
- Micro-concrete, PMM, Epoxy resins, FRP, Geopolymer, Shotcrete, Refractory concrete

**Selection Criteria:**
- Damage < 25%: Surface repair (PMM patching)
- Damage 25%–60%: Jacketing or FRP wrapping
- Damage 60%–90%: Major repair with additional reinforcement
- Damage > 90%: Demolition and reconstruction

**Quality Control Tests After Repair:**
- Cube test, Rebound hammer, UPV, Pull-off test, Load test

**Standards & Codes:**
- **IS 456:2000** (Plain and Reinforced Concrete)
- **IS 13311** (NDT methods)
- **ACI 216.1** (Fire Resistance of Concrete)
- **ACI 562** (Repair Code)
- **Eurocode 2 Part 1-2** (Fire Design)
- **ACI 440.2R** (FRP Strengthening)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leakage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Leakage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Leakage

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leakage in Structures** is defined as the unwanted ingress or movement of water (and associated aggressive agents such as chlorides, sulphates, and dissolved gases) through the building envelope, including roofs, walls, basement retaining systems, expansion joints, water-retaining structures, and service penetrations, leading to deterioration of the host material, corrosion of reinforcement, loss of serviceability, and reduction in the design life of the structure.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET634 – Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures)**, leakage is treated as a **pathology (distress)** rather than a mere aesthetic defect. It is the most prevalent and economically significant serviceability failure in both new and aged buildings across Kerala, owing to the region's heavy annual rainfall (**~3000 mm in many districts**), high humidity, and coastal salinity.

**Key Terminology (Aligned to KTU Syllabus Terminology):**

| Term | Technical Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dampness** | The general presence of moisture within building elements, often a precursor or symptom of leakage. |
| **Seepage** | Slow, continuous movement of water through a porous medium (e.g., brick masonry, concrete). |
| **Infiltration** | Driven flow of water through discontinuities such as cracks, joints, or honeycombs. |
| **Rising Damp** | Upward capillary migration of groundwater through porous masonry in contact with soil. |
| **Hydrostatic Pressure** | The pressure exerted by static water head, the primary driving force for basement leakage. |
| **Vapour Transmission** | Migration of moisture in gaseous (vapour) form, leading to condensation-related dampness. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, leakage is studied as a *symptom-driven distress* — the emphasis is on **diagnosis (identifying root cause)** before **prescription (selecting a repair system)**. Examiners reward students who distinguish between *cause* and *symptom*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Sponge-Bucket" Analogy:** Imagine a concrete structure as a **bucket made of sponge**. When the bucket is brand new, the sponge pores are tight, and water slips off. But as the sponge ages, dries, and micro-cracks, it begins to *suck* and *weep* water. Leakage in a building works the same way: a healthy, well-cured, dense concrete resists water by virtue of its tight capillary network. Once the capillary network is breached — by cracks, carbonation, poor compaction, or shrinkage — the structure behaves like a sponge, drawing water in through any pathway. The repair engineer's job is essentially to **"re-seal the sponge"** by either *filling the pores* (crystallisation, pore-blocking) or *coating the surface* (membranes, coatings).

A more vivid analogy is **the leaky boat**: a boat has many planks (concrete/masonry), many rivets (joints), and one bilge (drainage). Water finds the *weakest* plank or rivet — never the strongest. Similarly, water in a building finds the path of **least hydraulic resistance**: a crack, a poorly sealed joint, a honeycombed patch, or an unsealed service penetration.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

The following metrics are routinely tested in the **Engineering Science Examination (ESE)** and **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)**:

- **Water-Cement Ratio (w/c):** The single most influential mix-design parameter; a w/c > **0.45** is universally associated with high permeability.
- **Permeability Coefficient (k):** Measured in **cm/sec** or **m/s**; for good concrete, k ≤ **10⁻¹² m/s**.
- **Water Absorption (% by mass):** Acceptance limit for good concrete ≤ **3%** (IS 516); for AAC blocks, **≤ 10%** after 24h immersion.
- **Cover to Reinforcement:** Minimum cover in moderate exposure = **30 mm**; in severe/marine exposure = **50 mm** (IS 456:2000).
- **Hydrostatic Head (h):** Measured in **metres of water column (mWC)**; basement leakage is critical at h > **3 m**.
- **Damp-Proof Course (DPC) thickness:** Conventionally **40 mm to 50 mm** in residential construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **University Examination Pointers (KTU 2024):** When asked to "discuss the causes of leakage," always start with the *water source* (rain, plumbing, groundwater, condensation) and follow with the *pathway* (crack, joint, porous matrix, capillary). This cause-pathway-symptom triad is the **mark-winning structure** prescribed by the KTU board's valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why and How" — A Structured Causal Analysis

Leakage in a structure is a **three-element phenomenon**, often called the *Leakage Triangle* in forensic civil engineering:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Leakage Triangle**
> 1. **Water Source (Driver):** Where the water originates (rain, plumbing failure, groundwater, condensation).
> 2. **Pathway (Medium):** The route water uses to enter (crack, joint, honeycombed concrete, porous masonry, capillary pore structure).
> 3. **Vulnerability (Receiver):** The susceptible element (reinforcement, finishes, occupant, equipment).
>
> *Eliminate any one element and leakage stops. Most repair strategies focus on eliminating the **pathway** because the source and the receiver are often fixed by usage.*

### 2.1.1 Classification of Leakage Based on Source

1. **Rainwater Leakage (Climatic Source)**
   - *Roof leakage:* Failed waterproofing membrane, ponding, cracked RCC slab, improperly detailed rain-water down-take pipes.
   - *Wall leakage:* Cracks in plaster, honeycombing in RCC beams/columns, failed window-to-wall junctions, missing sill projections.
   - *Parapet leakage:* The interface between parapet wall and roof slab is the **single most common defect** in Kerala buildings.

2. **Plumbing Leakage (Service Source)**
   - Concealed pipe joints, corroded GI/CI lines, leaking water tanks, failed gaskets in WCs.
   - Often appears as **localized damp patches** on soffits or walls, typically **directly below** a wet area.

3. **Groundwater Leakage (Capillary / Hydrostatic Source)**
   - **Rising Damp:** Capillary rise through foundation masonry in the absence of a damp-proof course (DPC).
   - **Basement Leakage:** Hydrostatic pressure pushes water through construction joints, tie-rod holes, and shrinkage cracks in retaining walls.
   - Most severe in **Kerala's coastal alluvial and lateritic belts** where water tables are shallow.

4. **Condensation Leakage (Vapour Source)**
   - Warm, humid indoor air condenses on cooler surfaces (e.g., un-insulated air-conditioning ducts, north-facing walls).
   - Often misdiagnosed as roof leakage; the **salt rings / tide marks** are the diagnostic giveaway.

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Pathway Mechanism

| Pathway | Mechanism | Diagnostic Clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary Pore Suction** | Surface tension draws water into pores of diameter < 1 mm. | Uniform damp band, tide mark, efflorescence line. |
| **Crack Infiltration** | Hydrostatic / wind-driven pressure pushes water through discrete cracks > 0.1 mm. | Localized dripping, staining along crack line. |
| **Joint Failure** | Sealant aging, debonding, or compression-set failure. | Leakage follows joint geometry. |
| **Honeycomb Channel** | Water flows through interconnected voids left by poor compaction. | Random damp patches, slow seepage even after rain stops. |
| **Permeation** | Bulk flow through the porous matrix under pressure differential. | Generalized dampness, more uniform. |
| **Vapour Diffusion** | Molecular diffusion of water vapour through materials. | Condensation droplets, no dripping. |

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **exam-essential mathematical toolkit** for Module 4. Memorize the variable definitions, the units, and the *physical meaning* of each formula. Numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE typically test combinations of these equations.

### 2.2.1 Darcy's Law (For Permeation Flow)

$$Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of water (m³/sec)
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability (m/sec)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $\Delta h$ = hydraulic head difference across the element (m of water)
- $L$ = thickness / flow path length (m)

> **Physical Meaning:** Doubles the head, doubles the leakage rate. Doubles the thickness, halves the leakage. This is the **foundation equation** of all basement waterproofing design.

### 2.2.2 Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

$$h_{c} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{\rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot r}$$

Where:
- $h_{c}$ = equilibrium capillary rise (m)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of water (≈ **0.0728 N/m** at 20°C)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between water and pore wall (≈ **0°** for hydrophilic cement paste, ≈ **90°** for hydrophobic surfaces)
- $\rho_{w}$ = density of water (**1000 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $r$ = effective pore radius (m)

> **Physical Meaning:** Finer pores produce **higher** capillary rise. This is why DPCs must be **impermeable** (no fine pores), not just dense.

### 2.2.3 Water Absorption (IS 516 Test)

$$W_{a} = \dfrac{M_{sat} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 \, \%$$

Where:
- $W_{a}$ = percentage water absorption (%)
- $M_{sat}$ = mass of saturated specimen (kg)
- $M_{dry}$ = mass of oven-dry specimen (kg)

> **Acceptance limits (IS 516):** Good concrete $W_{a} \leq 3\%$; Poor concrete $W_{a} > 5\%$.

### 2.2.4 Sorptivity (Rate of Capillary Absorption)

$$i = S \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

Where:
- $i$ = cumulative volume of water absorbed per unit area (mm)
- $S$ = sorptivity coefficient (mm/min^0.5)
- $t$ = elapsed time (min)

> **Physical Meaning:** Sorptivity quantifies the *speed* of capillary suction. Lower $S$ indicates a denser, more durable cover concrete.

### 2.2.5 Hydrostatic Pressure on a Sub-surface Element

$$P = \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P$ = hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ (Pa or N/m²)
- $h$ = depth of water table below the structure (m)

> **Conversion:** 1 mWC ≈ **9.81 kPa**. This number is the **design driver** for basement waterproofing membranes.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in practice:** In a coastal KTU-affiliated engineering consultancy, almost 60% of all "waterproofing complaints" trace back to one of three issues: (1) missing or failed DPC in old masonry, (2) roof slab-to-parapet joint failure, and (3) plumbing sleeves left unsealed during construction. The formulas above let a **Repair and Rehabilitation Engineer** *quantify* the rate of water ingress, *size* the drainage/relief system, and *specify* a membrane thickness based on the calculated hydrostatic head. For instance, a 5 m deep basement in Kochi's high water-table conditions experiences a hydrostatic pressure of **49.05 kPa**, and the selected waterproofing membrane must be rated for at least this pressure plus a **safety factor of 2.0** (i.e., ≥ 100 kPa service pressure).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capillary Rise in a Building Wall (Jurin's Law)

**Context:** A residential building in Kerala has a 200 mm thick brick masonry wall in contact with soil. The brick has an effective pore radius of 0.05 mm. Estimate the capillary rise if the contact angle is **30°**. Use $\gamma = 0.0728$ N/m.

### Step 1 — Identify the Physical Principle

Water rises in a thin capillary tube due to the balance between **upward surface tension force** and **downward gravitational force** on the rising column.

### Step 2 — Force Balance on the Water Column

The upward force is the vertical component of surface tension acting along the circumference of the meniscus:

$$F_{up} = 2 \pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta$$

The downward force is the weight of the cylindrical water column of height $h_{c}$:

$$F_{down} = \pi r^{2} \cdot h_{c} \cdot \rho_{w} \cdot g$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Forces at Equilibrium

At equilibrium, $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$2 \pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta = \pi r^{2} \cdot h_{c} \cdot \rho_{w} \cdot g$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Capillary Rise $h_{c}$

Cancel one factor of $\pi r$ from both sides and rearrange:

$$h_{c} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho_{w} g r}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{c} &= \dfrac{2 \times 0.0728 \times \cos 30^{\circ}}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 0.866}{0.4905} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.1261}{0.4905} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.257 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
&\approx 257 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> **Result:** In a wall with 0.05 mm pores, water can rise approximately **257 mm** purely by capillary action. This is why a DPC placed **at least 150 mm above the highest external ground level** is mandated by IS 2212 — the capillary rise can be defeated only if the DPC is installed **above** the natural capillary fringe. If the DPC is *below* the fringe (e.g., laid at plinth level without a clear vertical separation), it is bypassed and rising damp persists.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rate of Seepage Through a Basement Wall (Darcy's Law)

**Context:** A 300 mm thick RCC basement wall in Thrissur is subjected to a groundwater head difference of 2.5 m across the wall. The wall is of grade M25 with an estimated permeability coefficient $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-12}$ m/s. Determine the **seepage rate per square metre of wall area** and the **total daily seepage** if the wetted wall area is 60 m².

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation

For laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, Darcy's Law applies:

$$Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$$

### Step 2 — List the Known Quantities

- $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-12}$ m/s
- $A = 60$ m²
- $\Delta h = 2.5$ m
- $L = 0.3$ m

### Step 3 — Calculate the Hydraulic Gradient

$$i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.3} \approx 8.33$$

> **Physical Meaning:** A gradient of 8.33 means each metre of wall thickness corresponds to 8.33 m of head drop.

### Step 4 — Calculate the Volumetric Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= k \cdot A \cdot i \\[6pt]
&= 1.2 \times 10^{-12} \times 60 \times 8.33 \\[6pt]
&\approx 6.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Litres per Day for Practical Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{day} &= 6.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} \times 86400 \text{ s/day} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^{3} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.052 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> **Result:** The seepage is **0.052 L/day** — a tiny *invisible* trickle. However, this is for *intact* M25 concrete. A **single 0.3 mm wide crack** across the wall can carry water at a rate **1000 to 10,000 times higher** because crack flow obeys a cubic law (analogous to Hagen-Poiseuille flow), not Darcy's law. This numerical contrast is the **single most important conceptual point** in Module 4: *cracks amplify leakage far more than permeability does*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Repair Strategy

The matrix below is the **KTU board examiner's standard answer template** for the question *"Suggest a suitable repair strategy for leakage in [context]."*

| Distress Context | Primary Cause | Preferred Repair System | Materials |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rising damp in old masonry** | Capillary rise, missing DPC | Chemical DPC injection; re-plaster with waterproofing admixture | Silicone-based water repellents, polymer-modified mortar |
| **Roof slab cracks** | Shrinkage, thermal, structural | Polyurethane injection; crystalline waterproofing coating | PU resin, crystalline admixtures, acrylic coatings |
| **Basement wall seepage** | Hydrostatic pressure, construction joint failure | Crystalline coating + HDPE membrane; PU grout injection at joints | Crystalline slurry, HDPE/PVC membranes, PU grout |
| **Bathroom/WC leakage** | Plumbing joint failure, missing puddle flange | Epoxy grouting; replace puddle flange; apply elastomeric membrane | Epoxy, APP/SBS membrane, cementitious waterproof coat |
| **Expansion joint leakage** | Sealant aging, joint movement | Remove old sealant, install backer rod + PU/polysulphide sealant | PU, polysulphide, silicone sealants |
| **Honeycombed RCC** | Poor compaction, segregation | Polymer-modified mortar repair; crystalline slurry on inner face | Latex-modified mortar, crystalline slurry |
| **Condensation on walls** | Vapour diffusion, thermal bridging | Improve ventilation; apply vapour barrier; insulate cold surfaces | Aluminium-foil vapour barrier, XPS insulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When the question asks *"Why is repair X suitable here?"*, your answer should mention: (1) compatibility with the substrate, (2) ability to resist the *driving force* (hydrostatic, capillary, or vapour), and (3) durability under the *exposure condition* (UV, chemical, biological). A bare list of materials without these three justifications loses 4–5 marks out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Leakage Triangle (Causal Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram maps the **three-element causal architecture** of leakage. Every repair strategy targets one of the three nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source] --> B[Pathway]
    B --> C[Vulnerability]
    C --> D[Leakage Manifestation]
    D --> E[Repair Action]

    A1[Rainwater] --> A
    A2[Groundwater] --> A
    A3[Plumbing] --> A
    A4[Condensation] --> A

    B1[Crack] --> B
    B2[Construction Joint] --> B
    B3[Porous Matrix] --> B
    B4[Honeycomb] --> B
    B5[Failed Sealant] --> B

    C1[Reinforcement Corrosion] --> C
    C2[Finish Deterioration] --> C
    C3[Occupant Discomfort] --> C

    D --> D1[Efflorescence]
    D --> D2[Damp Patches]
    D --> D3[Peeling Paint]
    D --> D4[Mould Growth]
    D --> D5[Spalling]

    E --> E1[Source Elimination]
    E --> E2[Pathway Sealing]
    E --> E3[Vulnerability Protection]

    E1 --> E1a[Re-route Plumbing]
    E1 --> E1b[Improve Drainage]

    E2 --> E2a[Injection Grouting]
    E2 --> E2b[Membrane Application]
    E2 --> E2c[Sealant Replacement]

    E3 --> E3a[Anti-corrosion Coating]
    E3 --> E3b[Vapour Barrier]

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef vuln fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000

    class A,A1,A2,A3,A4 source
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 path
    class C,C1,C2,C3 vuln
    class E,E1,E2,E3,E1a,E1b,E2a,E2b,E2c,E3a,E3b action
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **left branch (Source)** is rarely attackable in occupied buildings — we cannot stop the rain. The **centre branch (Pathway)** is the most economical and effective repair target. The **right branch (Vulnerability)** is protected by coatings and barriers.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Diagnostic Decision Tree for Leakage

This block diagram represents the **standard diagnostic algorithm** a repair consultant follows before specifying a repair.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Leakage Complaint Received]) --> S1{Where is the dampness?}
    S1 -->|Ceiling / Roof| S2{Does it appear after rain only?}
    S1 -->|Wall, low height band| S3{Is there a tide mark or efflorescence line?}
    S1 -->|Basement| S4{Is the water table above the floor level?}
    S1 -->|Below sanitary fittings| S5{Is the dampness localized to one room?}

    S2 -->|Yes| S6[Roof Membrane or Parapet Joint Failure]
    S2 -->|No| S7[Check Condensation - Measure Humidity]

    S3 -->|Yes, below 1.0 m| S8[Rising Damp - Check DPC]
    S3 -->|No| S9[Check Wall Cracks and Plumbing]

    S4 -->|Yes| S10[Hydrostatic Leakage - Test Construction Joints]
    S4 -->|No| S11[Lateral Seepage - Check Retaining Wall Drainage]

    S5 -->|Yes| S12[Plumbing Leakage - Pressure Test Pipes]
    S5 -->|No| S13[Capillary Action from Adjacent Wet Area]

    S6 --> R1[Specify PU Injection or Membrane Overlay]
    S7 --> R2[Install Vapour Barrier or Improve Ventilation]
    S8 --> R3[Inject Chemical DPC, Re-plaster]
    S9 --> R4[Crack Injection, Plumbing Repair]
    S10 --> R5[Crystalline Slurry plus HDPE Membrane]
    S11 --> R6[Install Drainage Board and Weep Holes]
    S12 --> R7[Replace Pipe, Seal Sleeves]
    S13 --> R8[Apply Cementitious Tanking]

    classDef diag fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef cause fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef repair fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 diag
    class S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11,S12,S13 cause
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5,R6,R7,R8 repair
```

> **Reading Guide:** The decision tree starts with a *location* question, then narrows by *temporal pattern*, and finally routes to a *cause class* and a *prescription*. This is the **gold-standard structure** expected in a 14-mark ESE answer.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Material Architecture for a Waterproofed Roof Slab

A cross-sectional block schematic of a typical **inverted roof waterproofing system** used in coastal Kerala.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Top Layer - Protection]
        L1A[Tile or Paver on Pedestals]
    end

    subgraph L2[Insulation Layer]
        L2A[Extruded Polystyrene XPS 50 mm]
    end

    subgraph L3[Waterproofing Membrane]
        L3A[APP or SBS Modified Bitumen 4 mm]
        L3B[Geotextile Separator 200 gsm]
    end

    subgraph L4[Slope and Drainage]
        L4A[Cement Screed 1 to 80 Slope]
    end

    subgraph L5[Structural Slab]
        L5A[RCC Slab M30 150 mm with Waterproofing Admixture]
    end

    subgraph L6[Ceiling Finish]
        L6A[POP or Gypsum Board]
    end

    L1A --> L2A
    L2A --> L3A
    L3A --> L3B
    L3B --> L4A
    L4A --> L5A
    L5A --> L6A

    classDef protect fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    classDef insul fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef memb fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    classDef slope fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016
    classDef struct fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    classDef ceil fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000

    class L1A protect
    class L2A insul
    class L3A,L3B memb
    class L4A slope
    class L5A struct
    class L6A ceil
```

> **Reading Guide:** Water enters the assembly from the **top** and migrates **downward** through the joints between pavers. The **L3 membrane** is the *single barrier* that must remain intact. The **L2 insulation** sits *above* the membrane (inverted roof) so that the membrane is shielded from UV and thermal cycling — extending its service life from ~7 years (conventional) to ~20 years (inverted).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Distinguish between "rising damp" and "lateral seepage" in a building. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rising Damp | Lateral Seepage |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of water movement | Upward, vertical | Horizontal, lateral |
| Driving force | Capillary suction in pores | Hydrostatic pressure from adjacent soil / rain |
| Typical location | Lower portions of ground-floor walls | Basement retaining walls, below-grade walls |
| Diagnostic marker | Horizontal tide-mark, efflorescence, plaster decay | Continuous damp patches, water droplet trails |

> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark each for direction, driving force, and diagnostic marker. A clean comparison table is the **valuation-key expectation**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. List any three causes of leakage in flat RCC roofs. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Any three of the following):**

1. **Failure of waterproofing membrane** due to ageing, UV exposure, or poor adhesion at overlaps.
2. **Ponding of water** caused by inadequate slope (slope < 1 in 80) or blocked rain-water down-take pipes.
3. **Cracks in the RCC slab** due to thermal movement, shrinkage, or structural overloading.
4. **Poor detailing at the parapet–roof junction** allowing water to enter along the chase.
5. **Honeycombing** of the roof slab concrete due to inadequate vibration during casting.

> **Marking Note:** Each valid cause carries 1 mark. A bare one-line answer with no *engineering reason* may be penalised 0.5 marks under the KTU marking rubric.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 carries a mandatory internal choice in the ESE. The 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of "rising damp" in old masonry buildings. With neat sketches, describe the construction of a chemical damp-proof course (DPC). **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 250 mm thick brick wall has an effective pore radius of 0.04 mm. Using Jurin's Law, calculate the capillary rise, given the contact angle is 25° and the surface tension of water is 0.072 N/m. Comment on the height at which the DPC should be placed. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Phenomenon (3 marks):** Rising damp is the **upward capillary migration** of groundwater through the fine pore structure of masonry in contact with soil. It occurs when there is **no effective damp-proof course (DPC)** at the plinth level, or when the existing DPC has been **bypassed** by debris, mortar droppings, or by the construction of a raised internal floor that buries the original DPC.

The water rises by **capillary suction** (governed by Jurin's Law), carrying dissolved salts (sulphates, chlorides) that crystallise near the evaporation front, producing the characteristic **tide-mark** and **efflorescence** at a height of typically 0.3 to 1.0 m above the floor.

**Step 2 — Chemical DPC Construction (4 marks):** The chemical DPC is installed by **drilling a series of holes** (typically 10–12 mm diameter) into the mortar course at the chosen DPC level, spaced at **100–150 mm centre-to-centre** along the wall length. A **siliconate-based or silane-siloxane cream** is injected into these holes under low pressure (or by gravity feed). The chemical diffuses through the pore network and **lines the pore walls with a hydrophobic film**, breaking the capillary chain.

**Layout (text-based schematic):**

```
Floor Level (Plinth)
|
|---Hole Ø12mm @ 100 mm c/c -->|--> Chemical Cream Injected
|                                |
|--- Brick Wall ------------------|
|                                |
|---Hole Ø12mm @ 100 mm c/c -->|--> Chemical Cream Injected
|
External Ground Level
```

> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark for a correct definition, 1 mark for the cause (missing/bypassed DPC), 1 mark for the tide-mark explanation, and 3 marks for the construction procedure (hole size, spacing, chemical type, mechanism).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Wall thickness $t = 250$ mm (not directly used)
- Pore radius $r = 0.04$ mm = $0.04 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Contact angle $\theta = 25°$
- Surface tension $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m
- Density of water $\rho_{w} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Apply Jurin's Law (2 marks):**

$$h_{c} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho_{w} g r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{c} &= \dfrac{2 \times 0.072 \times \cos 25^{\circ}}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.144 \times 0.9063}{0.3924} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.1305}{0.3924} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.333 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
&\approx 333 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Recommendation (2 marks):**

> The capillary rise is **333 mm**. Therefore, the DPC should be placed at **a minimum of 150 mm above the highest external ground level** (per IS 2212), but in practice, **at least 400–500 mm above the ground** to provide a safety margin and to be above the calculated capillary rise. A lower DPC would be defeated by the capillary fringe and rising damp would persist.

> **Marking Note:** Award 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for correct substitution and numerical result, 2 marks for the IS-code-based recommendation with units. **Penalty:** A common error is forgetting to convert mm to m for the pore radius — this leads to an answer 1000× too small and costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Discuss the various methods of diagnosing the source of leakage in a building. What non-destructive tests are commonly used? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the procedure of **polyurethane (PU) injection grouting** for sealing a live water-bearing crack in a basement wall. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Diagnostic Methods (4 marks):**

1. **Visual Inspection:** Look for tide-marks, efflorescence patterns, paint blistering, and the **geometry** of the damp patch. A patch that mirrors the ceiling shape is roof leakage; a horizontal band on a wall is rising damp.
2. **Temporality Test:** Does the dampness appear *only after rain* (suggesting roof/wall leakage) or is it *constant* (suggesting plumbing or groundwater)? Does it *increase at night* when temperature drops? (suggests condensation).
3. **Moisture Meter Survey:** A **pin-type** or **capacitance-type** moisture meter gives a quantitative reading (% moisture content) at various depths and locations. Mapping the readings reveals the **moisture profile** and the *source direction*.
4. **Plumbing Pressure Test:** All suspect water lines are isolated and pressurised to **1.5× working pressure** for 30 minutes. A pressure drop indicates a leak.
5. **Infrared Thermography (NDT):** A thermal imaging camera detects **temperature anomalies** caused by evaporative cooling on wet surfaces. It is invaluable for locating **concealed plumbing leaks** behind tiles.
6. **Tracer Gas Test:** Hydrogen-nitrogen mixture is introduced into a suspect pipe run, and a **sensitive gas detector** locates the leak by the rising tracer gas.
7. **Nuclear Moisture Gauge (NDT):** A non-destructive surface gauge that uses neutron back-scatter to measure moisture content at depth.

**Step 2 — NDT Summary (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of NDT Leak Diagnosis (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Moisture Mapping** — pin / capacitance meters.
> 2. **Thermal Mapping** — infrared thermography.
> 3. **Tracer Mapping** — hydrogen tracer, smoke pencils, dye tests.

> **Marking Note:** Each correctly explained NDT method carries 0.5–1 mark. The KTU board expects **at least three named NDT methods** with their working principle.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Material and Applicability (2 marks):**

**Polyurethane (PU) resin** is a **hydrophobic, flexible, foaming grout** that reacts on contact with water to form a **closed-cell elastomeric foam**. It is the *preferred* material for **live water-bearing cracks** (active leaks) in basements, tunnels, water tanks, and sewer systems because it **does not wash out** during injection and **expands to fill the crack cavity completely**.

**Step 2 — Procedure (5 marks):**

```
[STEP 1] Surface Preparation
   - Chisel out a V-groove 20 mm deep along the crack line
   - Clean with compressed air and water

[STEP 2] Installation of Injection Packers
   - Drill injection ports (packers) at 45° into the crack
   - Spacing: 150 to 200 mm centre-to-centre, alternating sides

[STEP 3] Surface Sealing
   - Seal the crack surface between packers with quick-setting epoxy putty
   - Allow 30 minutes cure

[STEP 4] Flushing
   - Inject clean water to verify packer continuity and to wet the crack walls

[STEP 5] PU Resin Injection
   - Use a single-component PU injection pump
   - Start from the LOWEST packer
   - Pump until resin appears at the next packer
   - Move sequentially upward

[STEP 6] Curing and Finishing
   - Cure for 24 hours
   - Remove packers
   - Grind flush and seal with epoxy mortar
```

**Step 3 — Mechanism (Free, 0 marks but a key concept):**

> When PU resin contacts water, it **reacts with the available moisture**, producing **CO₂ gas** that causes the resin to **foam and expand up to 10–20 times** its liquid volume. The foam **fills the crack**, **expels the water**, and **cures to a flexible, watertight seal** that can accommodate small future movements of the structure.

> **Marking Note:** A clean flow diagram of the procedure (4 steps minimum) gets 3 marks; the material justification (1 mark); the mechanism of action (1 mark); and the safety/quality checks (1 mark). **Penalty:** Stating only *what* PU is without the *why* (i.e., why PU and not cementitious grout) loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Module 4 Answers (Leakage Topic):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "cause" with "symptom":** Many students write *"damp patches"* as a cause. Damp patch is a **symptom**; the cause is the *pathway + driver*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Recommending a material without justification:** Writing *"apply epoxy"* without stating *why* epoxy is appropriate for that substrate, exposure, and movement condition is treated as a half-answer.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Capillary rise in **mm vs m**, permeability in **cm/s vs m/s**. Missing or wrong units cost 1 mark per error.
> 4. **Mixing repair systems:** Stating *"use PU grout on rising damp"* is a mismatch — PU is for *active water flow*; rising damp is a *static capillary* problem best solved by *chemical DPC injection*. Examiners flag this as a *lack of diagnostic skill*.
> 5. **No mention of safety factor or IS code:** The KTU 2024 board rewards answers that cite the relevant **IS code (IS 2212, IS 3067, IS 2645)**. A purely descriptive answer without code reference loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Overlooking the diagnostic phase:** Jumping directly to "apply X material" without a diagnostic step (visual, moisture meter, thermography) is a **structural deficiency in the answer** — the KTU valuation key explicitly states *"diagnosis precedes prescription."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Leakage" — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- [ ] **Leakage Triangle:** Source → Pathway → Vulnerability. Repair targets the pathway.
- [ ] **Darcy's Law:** $Q = kAi$ — the foundation of all seepage and waterproofing calculations.
- [ ] **Jurin's Law:** $h_{c} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho_{w} g r)$ — defines capillary rise; finer pores → higher rise.
- [ ] **Sorptivity:** $i = S\sqrt{t}$ — rate of capillary absorption; low $S$ = durable concrete.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic Pressure:** 1 mWC ≈ 9.81 kPa. Design membrane for **2× service head** as a safety factor.
- [ ] **DPC Placement:** Minimum **150 mm above the highest external ground level** (IS 2212).
- [ ] **Concrete Cover:** **30 mm** in moderate exposure, **50 mm** in severe/marine (IS 456).
- [ ] **Water Absorption Limit:** ≤ **3%** for good concrete (IS 516).
- [ ] **PU Resin:** For *active* water-bearing cracks; foams on contact with water.
- [ ] **Crystalline Slurry:** For *basement* waterproofing; reacts with free lime to seal pores.
- [ ] **Chemical DPC:** Silicone/silane injection for *rising damp* in old masonry.
- [ ] **NDT Methods:** Moisture meter, Infrared Thermography, Tracer gas — name at least three.
- [ ] **Efflorescence vs. Cryptoflorescence:** Surface salt (cosmetic) vs. sub-surface salt crystallisation (destructive).
- [ ] **Inverted Roof:** Membrane *below* insulation → service life ↑ from 7 to 20 years.
- [ ] **Kerala-Specific Failure Pattern:** **Parapet–roof slab joint** is the #1 site of leakage complaints in coastal KTU-region buildings.
- [ ] **Ventilation First:** Many "leakage" complaints in air-conditioned buildings are actually **condensation** — diagnose before you prescribe.
- [ ] **Drainage Board:** A dimpled HDPE sheet that creates an air gap between the wall and the backfill, relieving hydrostatic pressure.
- [ ] **IS Codes to Remember:** IS 2212 (DPC), IS 3067 (General waterproofing), IS 2645 (Integral waterproofing admixtures), IS 13182 (Waterproofing membranes).

> **Closing Note:** The KTU 2024 examiner rewards **structured, code-aware, diagnosis-first answers** over rote material lists. When in doubt, follow the triad: **(1) Identify the source, (2) Identify the pathway, (3) Match the repair to the pathway.** This single discipline will earn 10+ marks on any 14-mark question in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthquake - Epoxy Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Earthquake - Epoxy Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Earthquake Damage in Concrete Structures

Earthquakes induce **inertial forces** in structures that are proportional to the mass and ground acceleration. When these forces exceed the design capacity, structures develop damage in the form of **cracks, spalling, crushing, and in severe cases, partial or total collapse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> Earthquake damage in reinforced concrete structures typically manifests as **diagonal shear cracks (X-pattern) in columns and beam-column joints**, **flexural cracks at the ends of beams**, **shear cracks in beams, walls, and slabs**, and **bond failure** between concrete and reinforcement. Damage is classified based on severity as **Minor, Moderate, Severe, or Collapse-level**.

### 1.2 Epoxy Injection — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Epoxy Injection** is a permanent, structural-grade repair technique in which a **low-viscosity, two-part epoxy resin system** is injected under pressure into clean, dry, dormant cracks in concrete to **restore tensile strength, monolithicity, and structural integrity** across the crack plane. It conforms to the guidelines of **ACI 503R** and **IS 14330 / IS 9197** for epoxy-based repairs.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Stitch" Approach

Imagine a deep wound on your skin. A doctor first **cleans the wound (surface preparation)**, then places **small entry points (injection ports)** along the cut, and finally **injects a healing adhesive under pressure** that flows into the crack, hardens, and bonds the two sides back together as if they were never separated.

**Epoxy injection works exactly the same way on concrete:**

| Human Body Analogy | Concrete Repair Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The wound | The crack in concrete |
| Cleaning the cut | Surface preparation (wire brushing, air blasting) |
| Injection needles | Surface-mounted injection ports (T-ports) |
| Medical adhesive | Low-viscosity epoxy resin |
| Hardening / setting | Curing of epoxy (typically **24 hours at 25°C**) |
| Healed skin | Restored monolithic concrete section |

> [!TIP]
> The minimum crack width for effective epoxy injection is generally **0.05 mm (50 microns)**. Cracks narrower than this are too tight to allow resin penetration under practical pressures.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The effectiveness of epoxy injection is judged by:

- **Tensile bond strength:** typically **> 10 MPa (1450 psi)** to concrete
- **Compressive strength of cured epoxy:** **> 55 MPa (8000 psi)**
- **Viscosity of mixed resin:** **< 500 centipoise (cP)** for crack-penetration grades
- **Pot life (working time):** **20 to 60 minutes** at 25°C
- **Full cure time:** **7 days at 25°C** for design strength

These values are taken from standard manufacturer data sheets for **crack-injection grade epoxies** and are consistent with ACI 503R-93 specifications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Earthquake Damage (Why Epoxy Injection Is Needed)

When seismic forces act on a structure, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Inertial Force Generation** — Every mass $m$ in the structure experiences a horizontal force $F = m \cdot a$, where $a$ is the ground acceleration.
2. **Stress Concentration at Joints** — Beam-column joints, being the intersection of two flexural members, attract the **highest shear stresses** in the entire frame.
3. **Crack Initiation** — When the principal tensile stress $\sigma_t$ exceeds the tensile strength of concrete $f_t$, cracks form. For concrete, $f_t \approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ where $f_{ck}$ is the characteristic compressive strength in MPa.
4. **Crack Propagation** — During cyclic seismic loading, cracks open and close repeatedly (cyclic crack breathing), degrading bond between concrete and rebar.
5. **Need for Restoration** — Epoxy injection restores the **tensile load-transfer mechanism** across the crack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Epoxy injection is suitable **only for dormant (non-moving) cracks**. If the crack is **live (active)** — meaning it continues to open and close seasonally or under live load — flexible sealants like polyurethane must be used instead.

### 2.2 Chemistry of Epoxy Resin (The 'Why' Behind Performance)

Epoxy is a **thermosetting polymer** formed by the reaction between:

- **Part A — Epoxy Resin:** A diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA) molecule containing reactive **epoxide (oxirane) rings**.
- **Part B — Hardener / Curing Agent:** Typically a polyamine (e.g., triethylenetetramine — TETA) or polyamide.

When mixed in stoichiometric ratio, the amine hydrogen reacts with the epoxide ring, forming a **cross-linked 3D molecular network** that is:

- **Rigid** (high modulus, ~ 2 to 4 GPa)
- **Strong in tension** (> 30 MPa)
- **Chemically resistant** (resists water, salts, mild acids)
- **Low shrinkage** (< 2 % on cure)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inertial seismic force | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | Force on a mass $m$ under acceleration $a$ |
| 2 | Tensile strength of concrete | $f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | MPa | Estimate of $f_t$ from $f_{ck}$ (IS 456) |
| 3 | Modulus of rupture (flexural) | $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | MPa | First-crack strength under bending |
| 4 | Elastic modulus of concrete | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | MPa | IS 456 short-term modulus |
| 5 | Pressure for injection | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{2\tau \cdot L}{d}$ | MPa | Where $\tau$ = shear at wall, $L$ = length, $d$ = port spacing |
| 6 | Volume of epoxy required | $V_e = w \cdot t \cdot L_c$ | mm³ or cm³ | $w$ = crack width, $t$ = wall thickness, $L_c$ = crack length |
| 7 | Bond strength target | $\tau_{bond} \geq 1.7$ | MPa | ACI 503R minimum for structural repair |
| 8 | Pot life at 25°C | $t_{pot} = 20$ to $60$ | minutes | Working time before resin gels |
| 9 | Viscosity (crack grade) | $\mu \leq 500$ | cP | For penetration into $\geq 0.05$ mm cracks |
| 10 | Compressive strength (cured) | $f_{c,epoxy} \geq 55$ | MPa | Confirms structural-grade epoxy |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, always state the **units** explicitly and use the **IS 456 relationship** $f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for any crack-strength argument.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Epoxy injection is widely used for repairing:

- **Cracked bridge piers and girders** after seismic events
- **Beam-column joints** in RC frames that develop X-cracks
- **Cracked shear walls** in high-rise buildings
- **Damaged concrete silos and chimneys**
- **Heritage stone masonry walls** (with non-shrink epoxy variants)
- **Industrial floor cracks** subject to heavy forklift loading

### 2.5 Selection Criteria — When to Use Epoxy Injection

| Condition | Epoxy Injection Suitable? |
|---|---|
| Dormant (non-moving) crack | ✅ Yes |
| Live / active crack | ❌ No (use polyurethane or flexible sealant) |
| Crack width $\geq 0.05$ mm | ✅ Yes |
| Crack width $< 0.05$ mm | ⚠️ Limited (capillary penetration needed) |
| Structural crack (load path) | ✅ Yes (structural-grade epoxy) |
| Water-bearing / leaking crack | ⚠️ Use hydrophobic epoxy or polyurethane first |
| Wet / submerged surface | ❌ No (resin cannot bond to wet surface) |
| Aesthetics-critical surface | ✅ Yes (color-matched, polished finish) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Volume of Epoxy Resin Required

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
A reinforced concrete beam-column joint in a 4-storey hospital building developed an X-shaped crack of total length $L_c = 1.8$ m during the 2024 earthquake. The beam width is $b = 300$ mm and the crack penetrates the full width. The average crack width measured on site is $w = 0.5$ mm. Estimate the **volume of epoxy resin** required for full injection, assuming 25 % over-packing for wastage.

**Given:**
- Crack length: $L_c = 1.8$ m $= 1800$ mm
- Crack width: $w = 0.5$ mm
- Wall (thickness) penetrated: $t = 300$ mm
- Wastage factor: $f_w = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry of the crack volume.**

We model the crack as a **rectangular parallelepiped** of dimensions $L_c \times t \times w$. The volume of fluid resin needed to fill this space is:

$$
V_{geometric} = L_c \times t \times w
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

We must ensure all units are consistent (use mm for length dimensions):

$$
V_{geometric} = 1800 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm} \times 0.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
V_{geometric} = 1800 \times 300 \times 0.5
$$

$$
V_{geometric} = 540{,}000 \times 0.5 = 270{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to convenient units (cm³ and litres).**

Since $1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3$:

$$
V_{geometric} = \frac{270{,}000}{1000} = 270 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Since $1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$:

$$
V_{geometric} = \frac{270}{1000} = 0.270 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the wastage factor.**

In actual field practice, resin escapes through micro-voids, seals leak, and ports are over-filled during finishing. A standard over-packing factor of **1.20 to 1.30** is applied:

$$
V_{actual} = V_{geometric} \times f_w
$$

$$
V_{actual} = 270 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.25
$$

$$
V_{actual} = 337.5 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 0.338 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{V_{actual} \approx 0.34 \text{ L (or } 338 \text{ cm}^3\text{) of mixed epoxy resin}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often award full marks only when the **units and wastage factor** are both mentioned. A 0.27 L answer without the wastage factor is considered incomplete.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Crack-Width Suitability Check for Injection

**Problem Statement:**
A shear wall has developed diagonal cracks of width $w = 0.08$ mm after a seismic event. The characteristic compressive strength of concrete is $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa. Determine whether the crack is **injectable** using a low-viscosity (300 cP) epoxy system, and compute the **expected tensile load that can be transferred** across the crack after repair over a length of $L_{inj} = 1$ m at the wall surface.

**Step 1 — Compute the tensile strength of concrete.**

Using the IS 456 relation:

$$
f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}
$$

$$
f_t = 0.7\sqrt{25}
$$

$$
f_t = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the minimum crack width for injectability.**

For low-viscosity epoxy (300 cP) injected under practical pressures of **0.2 to 1.0 MPa**, the minimum crack width is approximately:

$$
w_{min} = 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with actual crack width.**

$$
w = 0.08 \text{ mm} > w_{min} = 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion: The crack IS injectable.** ✓

**Step 5 — Compute the load that can be transferred across the crack after repair.**

The repaired cross-section transfers tension through the **epoxy-concrete interface** over a bonded length. Assuming a conservative bond strength of $\tau_{bond} = 1.7$ MPa (ACI 503R minimum) acting over the **wall surface of area $A = L_{inj} \times t$**:

For simplicity, consider the **per unit width** approach. The tensile force per metre width of wall that the repair can transmit is:

$$
T_{repaired} = \tau_{bond} \times L_{inj}
$$

$$
T_{repaired} = 1.7 \text{ MPa} \times 1000 \text{ mm} = 1700 \text{ N/mm per metre depth}
$$

Expressing the same per metre depth, the line-load is:

$$
T_{repaired} = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m} \cdot 1 \text{ m} = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1.7 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{T_{repaired} = 1.7 \text{ MN per metre of repaired length (assuming } \tau_{bond} = 1.7 \text{ MPa)}}
$$

The crack width **0.08 mm is injectable**, and the repair can sustain a tensile line-load of **1.7 MN/m**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Epoxy vs Other Repair Methods

| Criteria | Epoxy Injection | Polyurethane Injection | Cementitious Grouting | Routing & Sealing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crack type | Structural, dormant | Live, leaking | Wide, dormant | Surface, non-structural |
| Min crack width | 0.05 mm | 0.10 mm | 1.0 mm | Any |
| Tensile strength | **Very high (30+ MPa)** | Low (flexible) | Moderate | Nil |
| Flexibility | Rigid | Flexible | Rigid | Flexible sealant |
| Waterproofing | Good (if dry) | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Low | Very low |
| Skilled labour required | **Yes** | Yes | Moderate | No |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** ⭐ | Medium | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Epoxy Injection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Damage Survey and Crack Mapping] --> B[Step 2: Clean Crack Surface with Wire Brush and Compressed Air]
    B --> C[Step 3: Drill and Mount Surface Ports T-ports at 200 to 300 mm Spacing]
    C --> D[Step 4: Seal Surface Between Ports with Epoxy Paste]
    D --> E[Step 5: Mix Two Part Epoxy Resin and Hardener as per TDS Ratio]
    E --> F[Step 6: Inject from Lowest Port First using Hand or Pneumatic Gun]
    F --> G{Q: Does Resin Flow Out of Adjacent Port?}
    G -->|No| H[Increase Pressure Slowly up to 1.0 MPa]
    H --> G
    G -->|Yes| I[Cap the Outlet Port and Move Injection Point Upward]
    I --> J[Continue Injection Up the Crack]
    J --> K[Step 7: Allow 24 to 48 hours Cure at Ambient Temperature]
    K --> L[Step 8: Remove Surface Ports and Grind Flush with Concrete Surface]
    L --> M[Step 9: Quality Check by Core Drilling and Pull Off Test]
    M --> N{Result: Tensile Bond Strength > 1.7 MPa?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Repair Accepted]
    N -->|No| P[Re Inject or Use Alternative Method]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Materials and Equipment Used in Epoxy Injection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RESIN_SYSTEM[Resin System]
        A1[Part A: DGEBA Epoxy Resin] 
        A2[Part B: Polyamine Hardener]
    end
    
    subgraph ANCILLARY[Ancillary Materials]
        B1[Surface Seal Epoxy Paste]
        B2[T Port Injection Nozzles]
        B3[Solvent for Cleaning]
    end
    
    subgraph EQUIPMENT[Equipment]
        C1[Hand Operated Injection Gun]
        C2[Pneumatic Injection Pump]
        C3[Pressure Gauge 0 to 2 MPa]
        C4[Mixing Container and Stirrer]
    end
    
    subgraph PPE[Safety and PPE]
        D1[Chemical Resistant Gloves]
        D2[Goggles and Respirator]
        D3[Ventilation Blower]
    end
    
    A1 --> E[Mixing Station]
    A2 --> E
    B1 --> F[Surface Preparation]
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> G[Injection Operation]
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> F
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Typical Earthquake Damage Patterns and Repair Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earthquake Damage Assessment] --> B{Type of Damage}
    B -->|Hairline Cracks less than 0.3 mm| C[Surface Sealing with Epoxy Putty]
    B -->|Structural Cracks 0.3 to 6 mm| D[Epoxy Injection]
    B -->|Wide Cracks greater than 6 mm| E[Routing and Sealing with Mortar]
    B -->|Spalled Concrete with Exposed Rebar| F[Chipping and Recasting with Polymer Mortar]
    B -->|Buckled or Fractured Rebar| G[Supplemental Jacketing with FRP or RC]
    B -->|Joint Shear Failure| H[Column Jacketing and Joint Retrofitting]
    D --> I[Restore Monolithic Action]
    I --> J[Final Quality Acceptance Test]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Epoxy Injection Pressure Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Low Pressure 0.1 MPa] --> B[Resin Reaches Next Port]
    B --> C[Increase to 0.3 MPa Slowly]
    C --> D[Hold for 5 Minutes]
    D --> E{Port Above Sees Resin}
    E -->|No| F[Increase to 0.5 MPa Maximum 1.0 MPa]
    F --> E
    E -->|Yes| G[Cap Port and Move Up]
    G --> H[Continue Until Crack Top is Reached]
    H --> I[Cap All Ports and Cure 24 to 48 hours]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **golden rule of injection pressure**: never exceed the **clamping pressure of the surface seal**, or the seal will blow out. Begin at **0.1 MPa**, ramp to **0.3 to 0.5 MPa**, and only exceed **1.0 MPa** for thick sections with deep cracks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*List any three typical earthquake-induced damage patterns observed in reinforced concrete buildings. State whether epoxy injection is suitable for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Diagonal shear cracks in columns** — appears as X-pattern in beam-column joints due to bi-directional seismic forces. **Epoxy injection IS suitable** because these are structural dormant cracks.
2. **Flexural cracks at the ends of beams** — vertical cracks near the supports caused by reversing bending moments. **Epoxy injection IS suitable** after confirming the crack is dormant.
3. **Spalling of cover concrete with exposed reinforcement** — concrete cover breaks off due to bond failure. **Epoxy injection alone is NOT sufficient**; chipping and recasting with polymer-modified mortar is required, followed by possible jacketing.

*(Award full 3 marks only when all three are listed with the correct suitability decision.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
*State the minimum crack width suitable for epoxy injection. Name the governing code.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **minimum crack width** for effective low-viscosity epoxy injection is approximately **0.05 mm (50 microns)**. Cracks finer than this do not allow resin penetration under practical pressures of 0.2 to 1.0 MPa.
- The governing code is **ACI 503R-93** *"Use of Epoxy Compounds with Concrete"* (American Concrete Institute).
- The **Indian Standard** equivalent guidance is from **IS 9197** (epoxy resins for concrete repair) and **IS 14330** (polymer-modified mortars).

*(Full marks require both width value, code, and at least one IS reference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — From KTU University Exam — Dec 2023**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure of **pressure injection of epoxy resin** for repairing dormant cracks in a reinforced concrete beam-column joint damaged during an earthquake. Include surface preparation, port installation, sealing, injection, and curing stages in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reinforced concrete column of cross-section **300 mm × 300 mm** has developed a vertical earthquake crack running along its full height of **3.0 m**, with an average crack width of **1.2 mm**. Calculate the **volume of epoxy resin** required for full injection, assuming a **30% wastage factor**. State one assumption.

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The procedure for pressure injection of epoxy is as follows:

1. **Damage survey and marking (1 mark)** — Identify the extent, width, and depth of cracks using a crack-width comparator and marking pencil. Distinguish dormant cracks (stable) from live cracks (moving).

2. **Surface preparation (1 mark)** — Clean the concrete surface along the crack for at least **25 mm on each side** using a wire brush, grinder, or compressed air to remove dust, laitance, oil, and loose material. The surface must be **dry** because epoxy does not bond to wet substrates.

3. **Port installation (1 mark)** — Surface ports (T-ports or pneumatic nipples) are mounted on the crack at intervals of **200 to 300 mm** along its length. Ports are bonded using a fast-setting epoxy paste.

4. **Surface sealing (1 mark)** — The crack between the ports is sealed with epoxy paste to confine the injected resin. Allow the seal to cure for at least **12 to 24 hours** before injection.

5. **Resin mixing and injection (2 marks)** — Mix Parts A and B in the **manufacturer-specified ratio** (typically 1:1 to 4:1 by volume) for 3 minutes. Inject from the **lowest port first** at low pressure (**0.1 MPa**), ramping to **0.3 to 0.5 MPa**. When resin appears at the next port, cap the lower port and continue upward.

6. **Curing and finishing (1 mark)** — Allow **24 to 48 hours** for cure at ambient temperature. Then remove ports, grind flush, and apply a surface seal coat if needed.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**

- Crack length: $L_c = 3.0$ m $= 3000$ mm
- Crack width: $w = 1.2$ mm
- Wall / depth dimension: $t = 300$ mm (column dimension penetrated)
- Wastage factor: $f_w = 1.30$

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric crack volume.**

$$
V_{geom} = L_c \times t \times w
$$

$$
V_{geom} = 3000 \times 300 \times 1.2
$$

$$
V_{geom} = 900{,}000 \times 1.2 = 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to litres.**

$$
V_{geom} = \frac{1{,}080{,}000}{1000 \times 1000} = 1.08 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the wastage factor.**

$$
V_{actual} = 1.08 \times 1.30 = 1.404 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{V_{actual} \approx 1.40 \text{ L of mixed epoxy resin}}
$$

**Assumption (1 mark):** The crack is modeled as a **uniform rectangular parallelepiped** of constant width and depth. In practice, crack width varies, and resin also fills micro-voids adjacent to the crack.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **epoxy injection** and **cementitious grouting** as repair methods for earthquake-induced cracks. State the situations where each is preferred.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **chemistry of epoxy resin curing** and explain how the **cross-linked network** gives cured epoxy its high strength and chemical resistance. Mention the role of pot life.

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Parameter | Epoxy Injection | Cementitious Grouting |
|---|---|---|
| Binder type | Epoxy resin (polymer) | Portland cement + water |
| Min crack width | **0.05 mm** | 1.0 mm or more |
| Bond strength to concrete | **1.7 to 3.5 MPa** | 0.5 to 1.5 MPa |
| Strength gain time | 24 to 48 hours | 7 to 28 days |
| Tensile strength of binder | 30+ MPa | 2 to 4 MPa |
| Water resistance | Excellent | Moderate |
| Shrinkage on cure | < 2% | 5 to 10% |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Skilled labour | Essential | Moderate |
| **Preferred for** | Fine dormant structural cracks in beams, columns, joints, walls | Wide cracks, foundation grouting, mass voids |

**Epoxy injection is preferred (2 marks):** when the crack is **fine (0.05 to 3 mm)**, **structural (load-bearing)**, and **dry**. Examples: beam-column joint X-cracks, shear wall diagonal cracks, girder flexural cracks.

**Cementitious grouting is preferred (2 marks):** when the crack is **wide (> 3 mm)**, when the structure is **mass concrete** (dams, foundations), or when **cost is a constraint** and structural-grade tensile strength is not required.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

Epoxy is a **two-part thermosetting system** consisting of:

- **Part A — Epoxy Resin:** Most commonly **diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA)**, which contains reactive **epoxide (oxirane) rings** at both ends of the molecule.
- **Part B — Hardener:** A **polyamine** (e.g., TETA — triethylenetetramine) or polyamide that contains active hydrogen atoms.

**Curing reaction (3 marks):** When the two parts are mixed in stoichiometric ratio, the **amine hydrogen attacks the epoxide ring**, opening it and forming a **covalent C-N bond**. The reaction:

$$
\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Epoxide Ring} \longrightarrow \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'}
$$

Because each amine has **multiple active hydrogens** and each resin molecule has **two epoxide groups**, the reaction produces a **3-dimensional cross-linked polymer network**.

**Properties from cross-linking (2 marks):**
- The dense network **restricts molecular motion**, giving **high rigidity and strength** (tensile strength > 30 MPa, modulus 2 to 4 GPa).
- Covalent bonds are **chemically stable**, resisting water, salts, mild acids, and alkalis.
- The cross-linked network has **low free volume**, resulting in **low shrinkage** (< 2%) during cure.

**Role of pot life (2 marks):** *Pot life* is the working time available between mixing and the point at which the resin begins to gel due to the onset of cross-linking. A typical crack-injection epoxy has a **pot life of 20 to 60 minutes at 25°C**. The pot life **decreases with higher temperature** and **increases in cold weather**. Operators must mix only as much resin as can be injected within the pot life, otherwise the material will harden in the gun and be wasted.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wastage factor** in volume calculations — examiners typically deduct **1 to 2 marks** when only the geometric volume is stated.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions** — mixing mm with m without conversion is a frequent error. Always convert all dimensions to a common unit (mm or m) **before** multiplying.
> 3. **Confusing epoxy with polyurethane** — students often claim "epoxy is flexible" or "polyurethane is for structural repair". The truth: **epoxy is rigid and structural**; **polyurethane is flexible and waterproofing**.
> 4. **Not mentioning surface preparation** — in 14-mark procedure questions, surface cleaning and drying is worth **at least 1 to 2 marks**. Skipping it is a major valuation penalty.
> 5. **Ignoring pot life and ambient temperature** — a 14-mark answer that does not mention pot life, mix ratio, or temperature sensitivity of cure is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Using $f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$** without citing **IS 456** — examiners expect the code reference. Always state the source of the formula.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Earthquake cracks** in RC structures are typically **X-pattern in joints**, **diagonal in columns/walls**, and **flexural at beam ends**.
- **Epoxy injection** is a **structural, permanent repair** for **dormant cracks** wider than **0.05 mm**.
- The two parts are **DGEBA resin (A)** and **polyamine hardener (B)**. Their reaction creates a **3D cross-linked thermoset**.
- Key performance targets: **bond strength $\geq 1.7$ MPa**, **tensile strength $\geq 30$ MPa**, **compressive strength $\geq 55$ MPa**.
- **Pot life = 20 to 60 minutes** at 25°C; **full cure = 7 days**; **handling strength at 24 to 48 hours**.
- **Volume formula:** $V = L_c \times t \times w \times f_w$, where $f_w = 1.20$ to $1.30$.
- **Procedure sequence:** Survey → Clean → Port install → Seal → Mix → Inject → Cure → Finish → Test.
- **Injection pressure:** start at **0.1 MPa**, ramp to **0.3 to 0.5 MPa**, maximum **1.0 MPa**.
- **Governing codes:** **ACI 503R**, **IS 9197**, **IS 14330**, and **IS 456** (for $f_t$ formula).
- **Epoxy is NOT for:** live cracks, submerged surfaces, or cracks < 0.05 mm. Use **polyurethane or flexible sealants** for these cases.
- **Quality check** is done by **core drilling and pull-off test** to confirm bond strength $\geq 1.7$ MPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Pointers for Last-Minute Revision:**
> 1. Always **state the IS code** when using $f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
> 2. Always **include the wastage factor** in any resin-volume calculation.
> 3. Always **mention pot life and full cure time** in procedure answers.
