> 4. Always **distinguish dormant vs live cracks** before recommending epoxy.
> 5. Always **cite ACI 503R** as the governing international reference.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Shoring # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SHORING (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Shoring is a temporary structural support system erected to stabilize a building, wall, or structure that has lost its load-bearing capacity due to deterioration, structural failure, or impending modification. It transfers the dead and live loads from the damaged element to the ground or to a sound adjacent structure through inclined, vertical, or horizontal members, ensuring safety during repair, rehabilitation, or underpinning operations.* In the context of **PECET634 – Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures**, shoring is classified as a *preventive emergency intervention* executed *before* the actual repair sequence. It is **NOT** a permanent solution — it merely buys time and provides stability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"THE CRUTCH ANALOGY"** 🩼 > > Imagine a person with a broken leg. The doctor gives them **crutches** to walk while the bone heals. The crutches **don't heal the bone** — they just transfer the body weight from the injured leg to the ground through temporary supports. > > **Shoring is the "crutch" of a building.** > When a wall, column, or beam is damaged (cracked, settled, weakened), shoring props up the structure and redirects its loads safely to the ground until permanent rehabilitation (like grouting, jacketing, or underpinning) is completed. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics The following **industry-standard metrics** must be remembered by every KTU examiner: - **Design Load Factor for Shoring:** **1.5 × (Dead Load + Live Load)** — per IS 3696 (Part 1) and OSHA standards. - **Maximum Permissible Deflection of Timber Shores:** **L / 360** where L is the unsupported length. - **Minimum Timber Shore Cross-section:** **100 mm × 100 mm** for residential, **150 mm × 150 mm** for multi-storey. - **Standard Angle of Raking Shores with Horizontal:** **45° to 75°** (most preferred: **60°**). ## 1.4 When is Shoring Mandatorily Required? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Favorite Trigger Conditions for Shoring:** > 1. Bulging or leaning of load-bearing masonry walls (> 25 mm out of plumb). > 2. Deep cracks (width > 3 mm) propagating through wall thickness. > 3. Differential settlement of foundations. > 4. Adjacent excavation or demolition nearby. > 5. Removal of load-bearing walls for renovations (creating temporary "bridges"). > 6. Fire-damaged or flood-damaged structures. > 7. Underpinning operations in progress. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Shoring Systems (KTU 2024 Syllabus) Shoring is classified based on **load transfer mechanism** and **member orientation**: ### 2.1.1 Raking Shoring (Inclined Shores) Used when the wall is bulging outward or there is risk of overturning. Inclined struts (called **rakers** or **raking shores**) transfer the wall load to the ground at an angle. **Components of Raking Shoring:** | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Wall Plate** (or **Needle / Headsill**) | Horizontal member placed against the wall to distribute load. | | **Rakers (Struts)** | Inclined compression members — the main load-carriers. | | **Needle / Cleat** | Short wooden block preventing wall plate from slipping. | | **Sole Plate / Foot Block / Anchor** | Base plate anchoring the raker to the ground. | | **Bracing (Hoop Iron / Dog)** | Lateral stability element connecting rakers. | | **Wedges & Shims** | For tightening and load engagement. | **Design Principle of Raking Shoring:** $$\theta = \text{Angle of raker with horizontal (45°–75°)}$$ The **vertical component** of the thrust resists the wall load: $$V = T \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ The **horizontal component** must be resisted by friction at the sole plate: $$H = T \cdot \cos(\theta) \leq \mu \cdot V$$ ### 2.1.2 Flying Shoring (Horizontal Shoring) Used when shoring is required across an open gap (e.g., between two buildings, or when a load-bearing wall is removed). It is **NOT** supported on the ground — it acts like a **strut between two walls**. **Components:** - Horizontal shore (needle beam) - Strut plates, cleats, wedges - Needles embedded into both walls - Centre struts (top and bottom) **Span-to-Depth Ratio:** $L/d \leq 30$ for timber flying shores. ### 2.1.3 Dead Shoring (Vertical Shoring) Used when floors, beams, or walls above need vertical support during foundation repair or column replacement. **Components:** - **Needles** (horizontal beams through wall openings) - **Props / Dead shores** (vertical posts) - **Sole plates, Head plates** - **Cross-bracing** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Dead shores carry **direct vertical loads** — they are essentially **vertical columns**. Design them for **axial buckling** using Euler's formula. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin(\theta)}$ | Thrust per raker (W = total wall load, n = number of rakers) | N | | 2 | $H = T \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Horizontal thrust component | N | | 3 | $V = T \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Vertical thrust component | N | | 4 | $A_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{T}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Cross-section area of raker | mm² | | 5 | $P_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_{\text{eff}}^2}$ | Euler's buckling load for dead shores | N | | 6 | $L_{\text{eff}} = K \cdot L$ | Effective length (K depends on end conditions) | mm | | 7 | $\sigma_{\text{allow, timber}} = 8 \text{ to } 12 \text{ N/mm}^2$ | Allowable stress in seasoned timber shore | N/mm² | | 8 | $w_{\text{total}} = 1.5 \cdot (w_{\text{DL}} + w_{\text{LL}})$ | Factored design load per KTU/IS 3696 | kN/m | | 9 | $S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{L}{360}$ | Maximum permissible deflection | mm | | 10 | $\mu \geq 0.5$ | Coefficient of friction (sole plate on ground) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute values in this sheet are written using $\vert x \vert$ syntax to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Shoring | |-------|------------------------| | **Heritage Conservation** | Stabilizing ancient monuments during restoration. | | **Post-Earthquake Rescue** | Propping collapsed/leaning buildings for search operations. | | **Metro/Rail Tunnelling** | Protecting adjacent buildings during underground excavation. | | **Highway Underpasses** | Supporting embankments during cut-and-cover construction. | | **Nuclear Plant Decommissioning** | Shielded temporary supports during reactor dismantling. | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Design of Raking Shores (KTU Board Pattern — 14 Marks) ### **Problem Statement:** A masonry wall is **6 m high**, **4 m long**, and **300 mm thick**. It has bulged and requires raking shores. The wall carries a **uniformly distributed load of 50 kN/m** (self-weight + imposed). The shores are placed at **45° to the horizontal**. Use **timber of allowable stress 10 N/mm²**. Design the number, spacing, and cross-section of rakers. --- ### **STEP 1: Compute Total Vertical Load on the Wall** The wall carries an axial load on its length. Total load per unit length = **50 kN/m**. $$ W = \text{Load per m} \times \text{Length of wall} $$ $$ W = 50 \text{ kN/m} \times 4 \text{ m} $$ $$ W = 200 \text{ kN} $$ > **Logic:** This is the total downward load that must be transferred to the ground through the inclined rakers. --- ### **STEP 2: Apply the KTU/IS Safety Factor of 1.5** The factored design load per **IS 3696 Part 1** is: $$ W_{\text{factored}} = 1.5 \times W $$ $$ W_{\text{factored}} = 1.5 \times 200 \text{ kN} $$ $$ W_{\text{factored}} = 300 \text{ kN} $$ > **Logic:** Shoring is *temporary* but *life-critical*, hence a 50% safety margin is mandatory by KTU code. --- ### **STEP 3: Decide Number of Rakers (n)** Standard practice (and KTU expected answer): provide **rakers at every 3 m spacing**, minimum **2 rakers**. For a **4 m long wall** with 3 m spacing → 2 rakers (one at each end). $$ n = 2 $$ > **Logic:** Shorter spacing reduces slenderness ratio of the wall plate and prevents localised buckling of masonry. --- ### **STEP 4: Resolve Loads on Each Raker** Each raker carries a vertical reaction equal to the tributary wall load: $$ T_{\text{vertical}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{factored}}}{n} $$ $$ T_{\text{vertical}} = \dfrac{300 \text{ kN}}{2} = 150 \text{ kN} $$ Now, since the raker is inclined at **θ = 45°**, the **axial thrust T** in the raker is: $$ T = \dfrac{T_{\text{vertical}}}{\sin(\theta)} $$ $$ T = \dfrac{150 \text{ kN}}{\sin(45°)} = \dfrac{150}{0.7071} $$ $$ T = 212.13 \text{ kN} $$ > **Logic:** Only the *vertical component* of the raker's axial force resists the wall's load. The horizontal component pushes outward and must be resisted at the sole plate. --- ### **STEP 5: Compute Horizontal Thrust at Sole Plate** $$ H = T \cdot \cos(\theta) $$ $$ H = 212.13 \times \cos(45°) $$ $$ H = 212.13 \times 0.7071 = 150 \text{ kN} $$ > **Logic:** This horizontal force tries to *push the sole plate outward*. It must be resisted by **friction** or **anchor bolts** in the ground. Check friction capacity. Assume $\mu = 0.5$: $$ V = T \cdot \sin(\theta) = 150 \text{ kN (vertical force at sole plate)} $$ $$ F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot V = 0.5 \times 150 = 75 \text{ kN} $$ Since $H = 150 \text{ kN} > F_{\text{friction}} = 75 \text{ kN}$, **anchor bolts are mandatory**. --- ### **STEP 6: Design Cross-Section of Timber Raker** Using allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$: $$ A_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{T}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} $$ $$ A_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{212.13 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{10 \text{ N/mm}^2} $$ $$ A_{\text{required}} = 21213 \text{ mm}^2 $$ For a **square cross-section**: $$ b = \sqrt{A_{\text{required}}} $$ $$ b = \sqrt{21213} = 145.6 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Adopt a standard size: 150 mm × 150 mm** (as per IS 3696 minimum recommendation). --- ### **STEP 7: Check Buckling of Raker** Effective length, assuming one end pinned and other fixed: $$ L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{h}{\cos(\theta)} $$ Assuming wall height $h = 6 \text{ m}$: $$ L_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{0.707 \times 6}{0.707} = 6 \text{ m} $$ Radius of gyration for 150 mm square: $$ r = \dfrac{b}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{150}{3.464} = 43.3 \text{ mm} $$ Slenderness ratio: $$ \lambda = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{r} = \dfrac{6000}{43.3} = 138.6 $$ Since $\lambda > 100$, the raker is **long** — buckling governs. Using Euler's formula with $E_{\text{timber}} = 10000 \text{ N/mm}^2$: $$ P_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_{\text{eff}}^2} $$ $$ I = \dfrac{b^4}{12} = \dfrac{150^4}{12} = 4.22 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4 $$ $$ P_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 10000 \times 4.22 \times 10^7}{6000^2} $$ $$ P_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{4.165 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^7} $$ $$ P_{\text{cr}} = 115.7 \text{ kN} $$ Since $P_{\text{cr}} = 115.7 \text{ kN} < T = 212.13 \text{ kN}$, the **150 mm raker is UNSAFE**. **Revise to 200 mm × 200 mm:** $$ I = \dfrac{200^4}{12} = 1.33 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 $$ $$ r = \dfrac{200}{3.464} = 57.74 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \lambda = \dfrac{6000}{57.74} = 103.9 $$ $$ P_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 10000 \times 1.33 \times 10^8}{6000^2} = 365.3 \text{ kN} $$ Since $P_{\text{cr}} = 365.3 \text{ kN} > T = 212.13 \text{ kN}$, the **200 mm × 200 mm raker is SAFE**. --- ### **STEP 8: Final Design Summary** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Number of Rakers | **2 nos.** | | Raker Size | **200 mm × 200 mm** (Teak/Sal timber) | | Angle θ | **45°** | | Thrust per Raker T | **212.13 kN** | | Horizontal Force H | **150 kN** (anchor bolts required) | | Sole Plate | **300 mm × 300 mm × 50 mm MS plate** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Raking Shoring — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Damaged Masonry Wall
6 m height, 300 mm thick]:::wallNode nodeB[Wall Plate
150 mm x 100 mm
distributed along wall face]:::plateNode nodeC[Needle Cleat
prevents wall plate sliding]:::cleatNode nodeD[Raker 1
200 mm x 200 mm
inclined at 45 degrees]:::rakerNode nodeE[Raker 2
200 mm x 200 mm
inclined at 45 degrees]:::rakerNode nodeF[Sole Plate
300 mm x 300 mm MS
anchored to ground]:::soleNode nodeG[Anchor Bolts
12 mm dia at 200 mm c/c
resists horizontal thrust]:::anchorNode nodeH[Cross Bracing
Hoop iron 25 mm x 1.6 mm]:::braceNode nodeI[Wedges and Shims
for load engagement]:::wedgeNode nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeH nodeD --> nodeI nodeE --> nodeI classDef wallNode fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef plateNode fill:#D2691E,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef cleatNode fill:#A0522D,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef rakerNode fill:#CD853F,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef soleNode fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef anchorNode fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef braceNode fill:#556B2F,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef wedgeNode fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: When to Use Which Shoring Type ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Structural Distress Observed]:::startNode q1{Is distress
vertical
settlement?}:::qNode q2{Is distress
lateral bulging
or leaning?}:::qNode q3{Is there an
open gap
between walls?}:::qNode n1[USE DEAD SHORING
Vertical Props
Needles + Posts]:::deadNode n2[USE RAKING SHORING
Inclined Rakers
at 45 to 75 deg]:::rakerNode2 n3[USE FLYING SHORING
Horizontal Strut
across the gap]:::flyNode start --> q1 q1 -->|YES| n1 q1 -->|NO| q2 q2 -->|YES| n2 q2 -->|NO| q3 q3 -->|YES| n3 q3 -->|NO| alert[CONSULT STRUCTURAL ENGINEER
Combined Shoring Required]:::alertNode classDef startNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef qNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef deadNode fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef rakerNode2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef flyNode fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef alertNode fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Load Transfer Mechanism Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR loadA[Wall Self Weight
50 kN per m]:::load loadB[Live Load
10 kN per m]:::load fact[Factored Load
W = 1.5 x Total
= 90 kN per m]:::fact res[Resolve into
Vertical and
Horizontal Components]:::res vcomp[Vertical Reaction
V = T sin theta]:::vNode hcomp[Horizontal Reaction
H = T cos theta]:::hNode res1[Resisted by
Sole Plate Friction
and Anchor Bolts]:::soil res2[Resisted by
Euler Buckling
Capacity of Raker]:::buck loadA --> fact loadB --> fact fact --> res res --> vcomp res --> hcomp hcomp --> res1 vcomp --> res2 classDef load fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef fact fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef res fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef vNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef hNode fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef soil fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef buck fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** *Define shoring. List the different types of shoring used in building maintenance.* **Model Answer:** Shoring is the temporary support provided to a structure that has lost its stability due to damage, deterioration, or impending modification. It is a short-term safety measure used until permanent repair is completed. The three main types are: 1. **Raking Shoring** — inclined shores for laterally unstable walls. 2. **Flying Shoring** — horizontal struts between two parallel walls. 3. **Dead Shoring** — vertical posts to support floors/beams from below. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** --- ### **Question 2:** *What is the role of a wall plate and sole plate in raking shoring?* **Model Answer:** - **Wall Plate:** A horizontal timber member placed flat against the wall face. Its role is to **distribute the concentrated thrust from the raker over a larger wall area**, preventing localised crushing of the masonry. - **Sole Plate:** A horizontal plate (timber or MS) at the **foot of the raker** resting on the ground. Its role is to **spread the inclined thrust vertically and resist the horizontal component** through friction and anchoring. > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the design procedure for raking shores with a neat sketch of the components. **Model Solution:** **Components of Raking Shoring (Refer SECTION 4.1 diagram):** 1. **Wall Plate** — distributes load along wall. 2. **Needle / Cleat** — anchors wall plate. 3. **Rakers** — inclined compression members (main load carriers). 4. **Sole Plate** — base support. 5. **Bracing** — hoop iron / dog spikes for lateral stability. 6. **Wedges** — for tightening. **Design Procedure:** **Step 1:** Compute total vertical load W from the wall (self-weight + imposed load). $$W = w_{DL} \cdot H_{wall} \cdot t_{wall} + \text{Imposed load}$$ **Step 2:** Apply safety factor 1.5: $$W_f = 1.5 \times W$$ **Step 3:** Decide number of rakers (n) at 3 m spacing along wall length. **Step 4:** Vertical load per raker: $$T_v = \frac{W_f}{n}$$ **Step 5:** Axial thrust per raker: $$T = \frac{T_v}{\sin\theta}$$ **Step 6:** Cross-section area required: $$A_{req} = \frac{T}{\sigma_{allow,timber}}$$ **Step 7:** Buckling check using Euler's formula (refer Section 3, Step 7). **Step 8:** Sole plate & anchor bolt design for horizontal thrust resistance. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** A 5 m high brick wall, 4 m long and 250 mm thick, requires raking shores. Wall load = 60 kN/m. Angle of raker = 60°. Allowable timber stress = 10 N/mm². Determine (i) number of rakers, (ii) thrust per raker, (iii) cross-section size. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Total Wall Load** $$W = 60 \times 4 = 240 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Factored Load** $$W_f = 1.5 \times 240 = 360 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3: Number of Rakers (at 3 m spacing)** $$n = 2 \text{ rakers}$$ **Step 4: Vertical Load per Raker** $$T_v = \frac{360}{2} = 180 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5: Thrust per Raker (θ = 60°)** $$T = \frac{180}{\sin 60°} = \frac{180}{0.866} = 207.85 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 6: Cross-Section Area** $$A_{req} = \frac{207.85 \times 10^3}{10} = 20785 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$b = \sqrt{20785} = 144.2 \text{ mm}$$ **Adopt 150 mm × 150 mm timber raker.** **Horizontal thrust per raker:** $$H = T \cos 60° = 207.85 \times 0.5 = 103.93 \text{ kN}$$ This must be resisted by **anchor bolts at the sole plate**. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Raking, Flying, and Dead Shoring. Under what circumstances is each preferred? **Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):** | Feature | Raking Shoring | Flying Shoring | Dead Shoring | |---------|----------------|----------------|--------------| | **Orientation** | Inclined (45°–75°) | Horizontal | Vertical | | **Support to** | Bulging walls | Open gap between walls | Floors, beams, walls from above | | **Load direction** | Lateral (horizontal) | Lateral thrust | Vertical (axial) | | **Ground anchor** | Required (sole plate + bolts) | Not required | Required (sole plate) | | **Member type** | Rakers (inclined struts) | Horizontal struts/needles | Vertical posts/props | | **Application** | Cracked/bulging walls in old buildings | During demolition of middle wall | Foundation underpinning, column replacement | | **Critical design** | Buckling + horizontal thrust | Beam bending | Axial buckling (Euler) | **Preferred Circumstances:** - **Raking:** When ground is available adjacent to the wall and wall is leaning/bulging. - **Flying:** When ground is unavailable (e.g., between two existing buildings or across a street). - **Dead:** When foundation repair requires temporary vertical support for upper floors. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Design the cross-section of a dead shore supporting a floor load of 80 kN. Height of shore = 4 m. Both ends are pin-connected. Use timber with $E = 10000 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_c = 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Assume factor of safety = 2. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Factored Load** $$P = 80 \times 2 = 160 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Effective Length (pin-pin, K = 1)** $$L_{eff} = 1 \times 4000 = 4000 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Trial Section — Assume 150 mm × 150 mm** Area: $$A = 150 \times 150 = 22500 \text{ mm}^2$$ Direct compressive stress: $$\sigma_d = \frac{160000}{22500} = 7.11 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 10 \text{ N/mm}^2 \ \text{(OK)}$$ Moment of inertia: $$I = \frac{150^4}{12} = 4.22 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$ Radius of gyration: $$r = \frac{150}{\sqrt{12}} = 43.3 \text{ mm}$$ Slenderness ratio: $$\lambda = \frac{4000}{43.3} = 92.4$$ Since $\lambda < 100$, intermediate column. Apply Rankine's formula with $a = \frac{1}{7500}$ for timber: $$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2}$$ $$P_R = \frac{10 \times 22500}{1 + \frac{92.4^2}{7500}}$$ $$P_R = \frac{225000}{1 + 1.138} = \frac{225000}{2.138} = 105.24 \text{ kN}$$ Since $P_R = 105.24 \text{ kN} < P = 160 \text{ kN}$, **150 mm section is inadequate**. **Step 4: Revise to 200 mm × 200 mm** $$A = 40000 \text{ mm}^2, \quad I = 1.33 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$ $$r = 57.74 \text{ mm}, \quad \lambda = \frac{4000}{57.74} = 69.3$$ $$\sigma_d = \frac{160000}{40000} = 4.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \ \text{(OK)}$$ $$P_R = \frac{10 \times 40000}{1 + \frac{69.3^2}{7500}} = \frac{400000}{1.640} = 243.9 \text{ kN}$$ Since $P_R = 243.9 \text{ kN} > 160 \text{ kN}$ → **SAFE**. **Adopt 200 mm × 200 mm dead shore.** > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Shoring Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 1.5 safety factor** — KTU strictly follows IS 3696; omitting the factor loses **2 full marks**. > 2. **Not checking Euler buckling** — Simply providing a cross-section from area alone is *incomplete*. Buckling check is **mandatory** for shores > 3 m long. > 3. **Ignoring horizontal thrust at sole plate** — Students forget that raking shores have a horizontal force component that **must be anchored**; expect a 2-mark follow-up. > 4. **Confusing dead load and live load factors** — Use **1.5** for combined DL+LL (not 1.2 and 1.6 separately, since shoring is temporary). > 5. **Wrong angle assumption** — Always state angle θ; KTU expects numerical work with explicit $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$. > 6. **Not adopting standard timber size** — Provide nearest *standard* size (e.g., 150, 175, 200 mm), not weird fractional values. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — SHORING** > > ✅ Shoring = **temporary** support; **NOT** a permanent fix. > > ✅ Three types: **Raking (inclined), Flying (horizontal), Dead (vertical)**. > > ✅ Components: Wall plate, Needle, Rakers, Sole plate, Bracing, Wedges. > > ✅ **Standard raker angle:** 45°–75° (preferred **60°**). > > ✅ **Safety factor = 1.5** (IS 3696 Part 1). > > ✅ Key formula: $T = \dfrac{W_f}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$ > > ✅ Horizontal thrust at base: $H = T \cdot \cos\theta$ → resisted by **anchor bolts**. > > ✅ Buckling check via **Euler's formula**: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2}$. > > ✅ Timber shore sizes: minimum **150 mm × 150 mm**, preferred **200 mm × 200 mm**. > > ✅ Allowable timber stress: **8–12 N/mm²** (seasoned Sal/Teak). > > ✅ Slenderness ratio $\lambda < 100$: short column → use Rankine; $\lambda > 100$: long column → use Euler. > > ✅ Deflection limit for horizontal members: $L/360$. > > ✅ Friction coefficient at ground: $\mu \geq 0.5$; if $H > \mu V$, **anchor bolts are mandatory**. > > ✅ Always **adopt a STANDARD timber size** greater than calculated, never an exact value. > > ✅ KTU keyword checklist: **Wall plate, Rakers, Sole plate, Wedges, Thrust, Buckling, Euler, Anchoring**.

topics.title: Underpinning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Underpinning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underpinning** is a remedial construction process in which the existing foundation of a structure is strengthened, deepened, stabilised, or replaced in order to transfer the structural loads to a more competent bearing stratum located at a greater depth, without causing unacceptable settlement or distress to the superstructure.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET634 – Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures)**, underpinning is classified as a **sub-structural rehabilitation technique** that addresses deficiencies in the *original load-transfer mechanism* of a building. It is a controlled, sequential operation that is typically performed in *short, localised bays* so that only a small fraction of the total foundation area is exposed at any given time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Underpinning is studied as a corrective measure for **foundation failures caused by inadequate bearing capacity, excessive settlement, scour, chemical deterioration, or loss of lateral confinement.** It is closely linked to the topics of *jacketing, shoring, and grouting* covered earlier in the syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine an old banyan tree that has started leaning because the soil on one side has eroded. To save the tree, a gardener does **not** cut the tree down. Instead, he carefully digs small pockets under the root-ball, one at a time, and packs them with firm soil or concrete so the tree gradually rests on a **new, deeper, stronger root system**.

Underpinning does **exactly the same thing** for a building:
- The **building** = the tree (superstructure).
- The **original foundation** = the original roots.
- The **weak/eroded soil** = the cause of distress.
- The **new concrete or pile support** = the new roots.
- The **small, alternating bays** = the gardener's careful, one-side-at-a-time approach.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "one bay at a time"?** If you expose the entire foundation at once, the building loses all its support and may collapse. By underpinning in *short, alternating sections* (typically **1.0 m to 1.5 m long**), the structure always retains support on the un-dug portions, ensuring stability throughout the operation.

## 1.3 When is Underpinning Required?

The most common **triggering conditions** for underpinning, as listed in the KTU module outcomes, are:

| **Sl. No.** | **Triggering Condition** | **Typical Symptom in the Building** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Inadequate bearing capacity** of the original founding soil | Cracking of walls, tilting |
| 2 | **Excessive differential settlement** beyond IS 1904 limits | Diagonal cracks near windows/doors |
| 3 | **Increase in dead + live load** (change of use, additional floors) | Sagging beams, column distress |
| 4 | **Adjacent deep excavation** (basement, metro, underground utility) | Settlement cracks on the side facing excavation |
| 5 | **Scour or undermining** of foundation soil (flood, leakage) | Localised subsidence |
| 6 | **Chemical deterioration** of foundation (sulphate attack, carbonation) | Spalling, loss of concrete section |
| 7 | **Lowering of water table** causing consolidation of compressible layer | Long-term progressive settlement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The safe differential settlement limit for **isolated RCC footings on clay** is generally **\mathbf{0.002 \times L}** (where L is the distance between footings), as per **IS 1904:1986**. Any value exceeding this is a strong indicator that underpinning may be required.

## 1.4 Underpinning vs. Shoring vs. Jacketing — A Quick Clarification

| **Technique** | **Purpose** | **Location of Work** | **Typical Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Underpinning** | Strengthen / replace **foundation** | Below footing level | Settlement, scour, deeper bearing stratum needed |
| **Shoring** | Provide **temporary lateral support** to a wall or excavation | Adjacent to wall | Deep excavation next to existing building |
| **Jacketing** | Strengthen an existing **structural member** (column, beam) | Around the member | Distressed columns, seismic retrofit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *shoring* (temporary lateral support) with *underpinning* (permanent vertical foundation strengthening). In the exam, always state whether the technique is **temporary or permanent** and **vertical or lateral** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Underpinning Methods

Underpinning methods are broadly classified into **two families**:

### A. **Conventional / Traditional Underpinning**
Performed by hand excavation beneath the existing footing. Best suited for **shallow depths (≤ 1.5 m)** and **cohesive soils** above the water table.

1. **Mass Concrete Underpinning (Pit Method)**
2. **Beam-and-Base Underpinning**
3. **Needle Beam Underpinning**

### B. **Modern / Engineered Underpinning**
Uses mechanical equipment, piling rigs, or chemical injection. Suitable for **deep underpinning (> 3 m)**, **granular soils**, **high water tables**, and **restricted access** sites.

1. **Mini-Piled Underpinning**
2. **Pile-and-Beam Underpinning**
3. **Jet Grouting**
4. **Resin / Polyurethane Injection**
5. **Bracket-Pile (Hilti / proprietary) Underpinning**

## 2.2 Method 1 — Mass Concrete Underpinning (The "Pit Method")

This is the **oldest and most commonly examined** method in KTU papers.

### Operating Principle
The existing footing is **progressively exposed in short bays (1.0–1.5 m)**. The loose soil beneath the footing is removed and replaced with **plain mass concrete**, which is compacted and allowed to harden before the next bay is opened.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1 — Marking the bays:** Alternating bays are marked (e.g., Bay A, B, C...). Only every *alternate* bay is excavated first — this is called the **"hit-and-miss" sequence**.
- **Step 2 — Needling / Pinning:** Before excavation, a **needle or pin** is driven through the wall above the footing. The needle transfers the wall load temporarily to **screw jacks or wooden shores** placed on either side of the pit.
- **Step 3 — Pit excavation:** The pit is dug **vertically** with proper timbering / sheet piling to prevent collapse. The depth is taken **0.15–0.30 m below the new founding level**.
- **Step 4 — Mass concrete pouring:** Plain concrete (typically **M15 / 1:2:4 nominal mix**) is poured in layers of **150 mm**, each well compacted. The top layer is **rammed hard** beneath the old footing to ensure a tight, void-free contact.
- **Step 5 — Curing and backfilling:** The concrete is cured for **at least 7 days**. The pit is then backfilled with compacted soil.
- **Step 6 — Repeat for the alternate bays (B, D, F…):** Once Bay A has gained strength, Bay B is taken up, and the process is repeated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "hit-and-miss"?** At any point in time, **at least 50% of the wall** is still resting on the original (unexcavated) foundation. This geometric arrangement ensures that the eccentricity of the load and the induced bending in the wall never exceed safe limits.

### Design Checks for Mass Concrete Underpinning
- The new concrete base must extend **at least 150 mm beyond the edges** of the existing footing on all sides.
- The new founding level must be **below the zone of significant moisture variation** (typically > 0.9 m in expansive clays, per IS 2911 Part 3).
- The bearing pressure on the new stratum must satisfy:

$$q_{new} \;=\; \frac{P_{total}}{A_{new}} \;\leq\; q_{safe,new}$$

where **\mathbf{P_{total}}** is the total service load arriving at the underpinning segment (factored 1.0 for serviceability), and **\mathbf{A_{new}}** is the effective plan area of the new mass-concrete pad.

## 2.3 Method 2 — Beam-and-Base Underpinning

Used when the **required underpinning depth exceeds 1.5 m** and a single mass concrete pad would be uneconomical or structurally inefficient.

- A **needle beam** (usually an RCC or steel I-section) is inserted through the wall at footing level.
- The beam is supported at its two ends by **underpinning piles or piers** constructed on either side of the wall.
- The wall load is transferred from the **footing → needle beam → p/pile → deeper stratum**.

## 2.4 Method 3 — Mini-Piled Underpinning

The **most modern and KTU-favoured** method. It is used when:
- The depth of competent strata is **> 5 m**,
- The site has **restricted headroom** (inside an existing basement, for example),
- The soil is **coarse-grained or waterlogged**.

**Types of mini-piles used:**

| **Type** | **Diameter (mm)** | **Load Capacity (kN)** | **Installation Method** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Driven** | 100 – 300 | 200 – 800 | Driven by hydraulic hammer |
| **Bored** (most common) | 150 – 350 | 300 – 1200 | Rotary auger + casing |
| **Jacked** | 100 – 200 | 150 – 500 | Hydraulic jack against structure |
| **Micropile** (IS 2911 Part 4) | < 300 | up to 1500 | Drill + grout + single bar |

> [!NOTE]
> **IS Code Reference:** Mini-piles and micropiles are designed as per **IS 2911 (Part 4):1985 — Code of Practice for Design and Construction of Pile Foundations — Load Test on Piles.**

## 2.5 Method 4 — Jet Grouting

A **soil-improvement** underpinning technique in which cement grout is injected at high pressure (typically **30–40 MPa**) through a rotating monitor. The grout erodes the in-situ soil and mixes with it to form **in-situ columns of soil-cement** (called *jet grout columns* or *JGCs*).

Typical column diameter = **\mathbf{0.6 \, m \, to \, 2.0 \, m}** depending on soil type and jet pressure.

## 2.6 Method 6 — Resin / Polyurethane Injection

A **non-disruptive** method where a two-part **expanding polymer resin** is injected through small (10–25 mm) holes drilled through the floor slab. The resin expands up to **30 times its volume**, filling voids and re-levelling the slab within hours.

> [!TIP]
> **Use Case:** Highly recommended for **lightly-loaded residential floors** where the slab has settled by 20–60 mm due to sub-soil voiding (e.g., leaking drains, washout of fines).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** that you must memorise for KTU exams on this topic. Note the use of `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| **Sl.** | **Formula** | **Description / Variables** | **Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $q_{net} = q_{u} - \gamma \cdot D_{f}$ | Net ultimate bearing capacity after underpinning (where $D_f$ = depth of new foundation, $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil) | kN/m² |
| 2 | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_{net}}{FOS}$ | Safe bearing pressure at new founding level (FOS = 3 for soil) | kN/m² |
| 3 | $A_{req} = \dfrac{P}{q_{safe}}$ | Required area of new mass-concrete pad (P = total service load) | m² |
| 4 | $Q_{up} = q_{b} \cdot A_{b} + f_{s} \cdot A_{s}$ | Ultimate capacity of a mini-pile (end-bearing + skin friction) | kN |
| 5 | $Q_{safe,pile} = \dfrac{Q_{up}}{2.5}$ | Safe vertical load on a single mini-pile (FOS = 2.5) | kN |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{P_{wall}}{Q_{safe,pile}}$ | Number of mini-piles required under a wall segment | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S_{i} = \dfrac{B \cdot q \cdot (1-\mu^{2})}{E_{s}} \cdot I_{w}$ | Elastic settlement below new pad (immediate settlement, B = footing width, $I_w$ = influence factor) | mm |
| 8 | $\Delta S_{limit} = 0.002 \cdot L$ | Permissible differential settlement (IS 1904) | mm |
| 9 | $P_{pin} = \dfrac{W_{wall}}{n_{pins}}$ | Load per needle pin (n = number of pins per bay) | kN |
| 10 | $V_{grout} = \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot h \cdot n$ | Volume of cement grout for jet grouting (r = radius of JGC, h = column length, n = number of columns) | m³ |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of Underpinning

| **Engineering Field** | **Typical Use of Underpinning** |
|:---|:---|
| **Building Restoration** | Strengthening 80–120 year-old colonial buildings whose shallow brick footings have settled. |
| **Urban Metro / Tunnel Works** | Protecting heritage structures from settlement when tunnels are bored within 5–10 m. |
| **Industrial Plants** | Re-levelling of machine foundations where the floor has settled by 30–100 mm. |
| **Bridge Piers** | Scour-repair underpinning using mini-piles and pile-caps. |
| **Heritage Conservation** | Non-disruptive resin injection under stone-flagged floors of palaces and museums. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing of a Mass Concrete Underpinning Pad

This is the **single most important KTU numerical** for this module. We will solve it from first principles so the logic is transparent.

### Problem Setup
A load-bearing brick masonry wall of **3.0 m height** carries a uniform dead load of **\mathbf{12 \ kN/m}** of wall length and a live load of **\mathbf{8 \ kN/m}**. The existing footing is a **plain concrete strip footing** of width **\mathbf{B_{old} = 0.9 \ m}** and depth **\mathbf{D_{f,old} = 0.6 \ m}** below ground level. The soil immediately below the existing footing is loose and compressible. It is proposed to underpin the wall using the **mass concrete pit method** to a new founding depth of **\mathbf{D_{f,new} = 1.5 \ m}** below ground level, where the soil is a **medium-dense sand** with the following design parameters:

- **Angle of internal friction, $\phi$ = 30°**
- **Unit weight, $\gamma$ = 18 kN/m³**
- **Cohesion, c = 0** (purely cohesionless soil)
- **Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (for $\phi = 30°$):** $N_{q} = 18.4$, $N_{\gamma} = 22.4$
- **Shape factor (strip), $s_{c} = 1.0$, $s_{q} = 1.0$, $s_{\gamma} = 1.0$**
- **Depth factor (strip), $d_{c} = 1.0$, $d_{q} = 1.0$, $d_{\gamma} = 1.0$**
- **Factor of Safety, FOS = 3**

### Step 1 — Compute the Total Service Load per Metre Run of Wall

The total service load is the sum of dead load and live load arriving at the footing:

$$P_{total} = P_{DL} + P_{LL}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$P_{total} = 12 + 8 = 20 \ \text{kN/m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{nu}$)

For a **strip footing on cohesionless soil** (c = 0), Terzaghi's simplified bearing capacity equation reduces to:

$$q_{nu} = \gamma \cdot D_{f} \cdot N_{q} + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}$$

Here, $B$ is the **trial width of the new mass-concrete pad**. We will iterate to find it.

### Step 3 — First Trial (Assume $B = 1.2 \ m$)

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{nu} & = (18 \times 1.5 \times 18.4) + (0.5 \times 18 \times 1.2 \times 22.4) \\
       & = 496.80 + 241.92 \\
       & = 738.72 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Net Safe Bearing Pressure

$$q_{safe} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \frac{738.72}{3} = 246.24 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Required Width of New Pad

The required width is found by equating the service load per unit length to the safe bearing capacity times the new width:

$$B_{req} = \frac{P_{total}}{q_{safe}}$$

$$B_{req} = \frac{20}{246.24} = 0.0812 \ \text{m} \approx 82 \ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This absurdly small value (82 mm) tells us that the **existing 0.9 m width is already massive compared to the load demand.** The reason we are still underpinning is **not** a bearing-capacity failure but rather a **compressibility / settlement problem** — the loose soil beneath the old footing is settling. The design, therefore, must be governed by **settlement**, not by Terzaghi's bearing capacity.

### Step 6 — Re-Design Based on Settlement Criterion

A more realistic approach: the new pad width is set so that the **net applied pressure** on the medium-dense sand is **lowered** to **\mathbf{50 \ kN/m^2}** (a typical conservative value to limit settlement to < 25 mm).

$$B_{req,settlement} = \frac{P_{total}}{q_{allowable,sett}} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.40 \ \text{m}$$

We then provide a **minimum practical width of 0.6 m** (so that the pad extends at least 150 mm beyond the existing 0.9 m footing — actually we need a wider pad, see next step).

### Step 7 — Practical Detailing Rule

> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Exam Standard):** The new mass-concrete underpinning pad must extend at least **150 mm on each side** of the existing footing.

Therefore:

$$B_{new} = B_{old} + 2 \times 0.15 = 0.9 + 0.3 = 1.2 \ \text{m}$$

### Step 8 — Verification of Bearing Pressure at Final Width

$$q_{applied} = \frac{20}{1.2} = 16.67 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}$$

$$q_{safe} = 246.24 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Safety Check: } \frac{246.24}{16.67} = 14.78 \;\text{>> 3 (FOS)} \; \checkmark$$

> **The design is grossly safe in bearing capacity, confirming that the original problem was a *compressibility* issue, not a *strength* issue.** This is the most important conceptual takeaway from this derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Mini-Piles Required for a Wall

### Problem Setup
A 6-storey RCC framed building has a **plinth band** failing in shear. The band carries a **service load of 1,200 kN/m** at foundation level. The competent rock is at **12 m** below the existing footing. **Bored mini-piles of 250 mm diameter** are proposed. Skin friction ($f_s$) is **30 kN/m²** and end bearing ($q_b$) is **2,000 kN/m²**.

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Pile Tip

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.25)^{2} = 0.04909 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Surface Area of Pile Shaft (assuming L = 12 m)

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 12 = 9.4248 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Ultimate Pile Capacity

$$Q_{up} = q_b \cdot A_b + f_s \cdot A_s$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{up} & = (2000 \times 0.04909) + (30 \times 9.4248) \\
       & = 98.17 + 282.74 \\
       & = 380.92 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Safe Pile Load (FOS = 2.5)

$$Q_{safe,pile} = \frac{Q_{up}}{2.5} = \frac{380.92}{2.5} = 152.37 \ \text{kN}$$

### Step 5 — Number of Piles per Metre Run of Wall

The wall load per metre = **1,200 kN/m**. The pile spacing is decided by **practical geometry** (typically 3D to 5D, i.e., 0.75 m to 1.25 m centre-to-centre).

If we use **1.0 m spacing** along the wall:

$$n = \frac{1200 \ \text{kN/m}}{152.37 \ \text{kN/pile}} = 7.88 \approx 8 \ \text{piles/m}$$

### Step 6 — Pile Cap Sizing

Each pile cap is **1.0 m × 1.0 m × 0.5 m deep** (RCC, M25 grade), designed as a rigid spread footing transferring the load from the plinth band to the pile group.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** In pile-group underpinning, always check **group efficiency** by Converse-Labarre's formula. If the spacing is closer than 3D, the **group capacity is less than the sum of individual capacities.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volume of Grout for Jet Grouting

For **n** jet grout columns of radius **r** and depth **h**:

$$V_{grout} = n \cdot \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot h$$

**Example:** If 20 columns of 0.6 m radius and 8 m depth are required:

$$V_{grout} = 20 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.6)^{2} \cdot 8 = 20 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 0.36 \cdot 8 \approx 180.96 \ \text{m}^{3}$$

Plus an **over-consumption factor of 1.3 to 1.5** to account for grout lost into the surrounding soil pores:

$$V_{grout,actual} = 1.4 \times 180.96 = 253.34 \ \text{m}^{3}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture of the Underpinning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Distress Observed in Structure] --> B1{Settlement and Cracking Exceed IS 1904 Limits?}
    B1 -- No --> C1[No Underpinning Required - Monitor Only]
    B1 -- Yes --> D1{Depth of Competent Stratum}
    D1 -- Less than 1.5 m --> E1[Use Mass Concrete Pit Method]
    D1 -- Between 1.5 m and 3 m --> E2[Use Beam and Base Method]
    D1 -- Greater than 3 m --> E3[Use Mini Piled Method]
    D1 -- Highly Variable / Granular --> E4[Use Jet Grouting]
    D1 -- Minor Floor Re-levelling --> E5[Use Resin Injection]
    E1 --> F1[Hit and Miss Excavation in 1.0 to 1.5 m Bays]
    E2 --> F2[Insert Needle Beam Through Wall]
    E3 --> F3[Install Bored Piles on Either Side of Footing]
    E4 --> F4[Rotate Grout Monitor at 30 to 40 MPa]
    E5 --> F5[Inject Expanding Resin Through 25 mm Holes]
    F1 --> G1[Pour M15 Mass Concrete in 150 mm Layers]
    F2 --> G2[Connect Beam to New Pile Caps]
    F3 --> G3[Construct RCC Pile Cap and Load Transfer]
    F4 --> G4[Form Soil Cement Columns JGCs]
    F5 --> G5[Polymer Expands and Re-levels Slab]
    G1 --> H1[Cure 7 Days and Backfill]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    G5 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Monitor Settlement for 6 to 12 Months]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Cross-Sectional View of Mass Concrete Underpinning (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Existing Brick Wall] --> B1[Existing Concrete Footing]
    B1 --> C1[Compressible Loose Soil - Cause of Distress]
    A1 -.Temporary Support.-> D1[Needle Pin Driven Through Wall]
    D1 --> E1[Screw Jack or Timber Shore]
    E1 --> F1[Adjacent Stable Ground]
    B1 --> G1[New Mass Concrete Pit]
    G1 --> H1[Compacted Layer 1 - 150 mm]
    G1 --> H2[Compacted Layer 2 - 150 mm]
    G1 --> H3[Compacted Layer 3 - 150 mm]
    G1 --> I1[Top Layer Rammed Hard Against Old Footing]
    H1 --> J1[New Founding Stratum - Medium Dense Sand]
    H2 --> J1
    H3 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Mini-Pile Underpinning System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Superstructure Load] --> B1[Plinth Band or RCC Beam]
    B1 --> C1[New RCC Pile Cap]
    C1 --> D1[Mini Pile 1 - 250 mm dia]
    C1 --> D2[Mini Pile 2 - 250 mm dia]
    C1 --> D3[Mini Pile 3 - 250 mm dia]
    D1 --> E1[End Bearing in Rock Stratum]
    D2 --> E2[Skin Friction in Cohesive Layer]
    D3 --> E3[End Bearing plus Skin Friction]
    E1 --> F1[Safe Load Transfer to Competent Stratum]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Permanent Stabilisation of Foundation]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Typical Underpinning Bay Layout (Top View)

> The following text-based schematic shows the **hit-and-miss sequence** in a 6-bay underpinning layout, which is the most commonly asked diagram in KTU exams.

```
   Wall (3 m high)  ──────────────────────────────────────────►
   ┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐
   │ A1   │ A2   │ A3   │ A4   │ A5   │ A6   │   Phase 1
   │ DIG  │ skip │ DIG  │ skip │ DIG  │ skip │   (3 bays open)
   └──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┘
            ↓   (wait 7 days for M15 to cure)
   ┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐
   │ A1   │ A2   │ A3   │ A4   │ A5   │ A6   │   Phase 2
   │ DONE │ DIG  │ DONE │ DIG  │ DONE │ DIG  │   (3 alternate bays open)
   └──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┘
            ↓   (wait 7 days, then backfill)
   ┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐
   │ A1   │ A2   │ A3   │ A4   │ A5   │ A6   │   Phase 3
   │ DONE │ DONE │ DONE │ DONE │ DONE │ DONE │   (full underpinning complete)
   └──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this pattern?** At any given phase, only **50% of the wall foundation is exposed**, ensuring the load path always has a safe, undisturbed bearing. This is the **most important sequence** to draw in the KTU examination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Underpinning". State any four situations in which underpinning of an existing structure becomes necessary.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Underpinning** is a process of providing new, permanent support to an existing foundation by either strengthening it, deepening it, or extending it to a more competent bearing stratum, without causing distress to the superstructure.

Four situations requiring underpinning are:

1. **Excessive settlement** of the existing foundation beyond permissible limits (IS 1904).
2. **Change of use** of the building resulting in increased dead and live loads.
3. **Adjacent deep excavation** causing loss of lateral confinement and undermining of the existing foundation soil.
4. **Scour or chemical deterioration** (e.g., sulphate attack) of the original foundation material.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between "Mass Concrete Underpinning" and "Mini-Piled Underpinning" with respect to depth of application, equipment used, and soil suitability.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Mass Concrete Underpinning** | **Mini-Piled Underpinning** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Depth of application** | Shallow (≤ 1.5 m) | Deep (> 3 m) |
| **Equipment used** | Hand tools, shovels, needle pins, screw jacks | Bored piling rig, hydraulic jacks, grout pumps |
| **Soil suitability** | Cohesive soils above water table | All soils, including waterlogged and granular |
| **Speed of execution** | Slow (one bay at a time, 7-day curing) | Fast (mechanised, simultaneous piling) |
| **Disturbance to building** | High (manual excavation) | Low (drilled holes, minimal vibration) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** Each Part B question on a module has an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are given below, with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **"Hit-and-Miss" method of Mass Concrete Underpinning** with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the **sequence of operations** and the **precautions** to be taken during execution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4.0 m high brick masonry wall carries a dead load of 15 kN/m and a live load of 10 kN/m. The existing strip footing is 1.0 m wide and 0.8 m deep. The soil beneath is loose and is to be replaced by underpinning to a depth of 1.8 m, where the safe bearing capacity is 180 kN/m². Design the **width** of the new mass concrete pad and verify its **bearing safety**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hit-and-Miss Method:**

- The hit-and-miss method is the **traditional mass concrete underpinning technique** in which the foundation is exposed in **alternating short bays (1.0 to 1.5 m long)**, never in a continuous strip.
- **Sequence of operations:**
  1. **Marking the bays** along the wall into segments A1, A2, A3, ... of 1.0–1.5 m each.
  2. **Inserting needle pins** through the wall above the footing at the bay to be excavated. The needles rest on **screw jacks** placed on either side of the pit, transferring the wall load temporarily to the jacks.
  3. **Excavating the pit** vertically to the new founding depth, with proper timber shoring to prevent soil collapse.
  4. **Pouring plain M15 concrete** in 150 mm thick compacted layers.
  5. **Ramming the top layer** firmly against the underside of the old footing to ensure intimate contact.
  6. **Curing for 7 days**, then backfilling the pit.
  7. **Shifting to the alternate bays** (the "miss" bays) once the "hit" bays have gained adequate strength.
- **Precautions:**
  - At any time, **at least 50% of the wall length** must remain supported.
  - The new pad must extend **150 mm beyond** the existing footing on both sides.
  - Concrete must be rammed in **small layers** to avoid honeycombing.
  - **No vibration** from heavy equipment should be allowed near the pit.
  - The excavation must be protected from **rainwater ingress** and **lateral soil collapse**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

- **Total service load per metre:**
$$P = 15 + 10 = 25 \ \text{kN/m}$$
- **Required width for bearing (settlement governed):**

Using the design rule that the new pad must extend **150 mm** beyond the existing 1.0 m footing:

$$B_{new} = 1.0 + 2 \times 0.15 = 1.30 \ \text{m}$$
- **Verification of bearing safety:**

$$q_{applied} = \frac{P}{B_{new}} = \frac{25}{1.30} = 19.23 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}$$

$$q_{safe} = 180 \ \text{kN/m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Safety Check: } \frac{180}{19.23} = 9.36 \;\text{>> 3 (FOS)} \; \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** A new mass concrete pad of **\mathbf{1.30 \ m}** width is sufficient, and the design is **safe with a FOS of 9.36**, indicating the original problem was a settlement issue rather than a bearing-capacity failure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Describe the **Mini-Piled Underpinning** method. State the situations in which it is preferred over mass concrete underpinning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6.0 m long wall segment carries a service load of **900 kN/m** at foundation level. The new founding stratum is at **10 m** below the footing. Bored mini-piles of **300 mm diameter** are proposed. The skin friction ($f_s$) is **40 kN/m²** and end bearing ($q_b$) is **2,500 kN/m²**. Determine the **safe load capacity of a single pile** and the **number of piles** required if piles are spaced at **1.0 m centre-to-centre**. Use FOS = 2.5. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mini-Piled Underpinning:**

- Mini-piled underpinning is a **modern, mechanised technique** in which small-diameter (100–350 mm) piles are installed beneath or adjacent to the existing footing to transfer the structural load to a **deeper, competent bearing stratum**.
- The piles are connected to the existing footing through a **new RCC pile cap or needle beam**.
- **Situations where mini-piled underpinning is preferred:**
  1. **Depth of competent stratum > 3 m** (where mass concrete would be uneconomical).
  2. **Restricted headroom** inside basements or with low-clearance access.
  3. **Cohesionless / waterlogged soils** where manual excavation is unsafe.
  4. **Vibration-sensitive** heritage structures where hand-digging is required but depth is too large.
  5. **Limited working space** in dense urban areas.
  6. **High structural loads** that cannot be supported by mass concrete pads alone.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

- **Pile tip area (bored pile, D = 0.3 m):**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.3)^{2} = 0.0707 \ \text{m}^{2}$$
- **Pile shaft surface area (L = 10 m):**

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.3 \cdot 10 = 9.4248 \ \text{m}^{2}$$
- **Ultimate pile capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{up} & = q_b \cdot A_b + f_s \cdot A_s \\
       & = (2500 \times 0.0707) + (40 \times 9.4248) \\
       & = 176.71 + 376.99 \\
       & = 553.70 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$
- **Safe pile capacity (FOS = 2.5):**

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{553.70}{2.5} = 221.48 \ \text{kN}$$
- **Total wall load (per metre):** 900 kN/m
- **Load per pile spacing (1.0 m c/c):** 900 kN
- **Number of piles required:**

$$n = \frac{900}{221.48} = 4.06 \approx 5 \ \text{piles per 1.0 m spacing}$$

> **Conclusion:** Each 1.0 m segment of the 6.0 m wall requires **5 mini-piles**, totalling **30 piles** for the entire wall. The safe load per pile is **221.48 kN**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse "Underpinning" with "Pile Foundation for a New Building".** Underpinning is *retrofit* work on an *existing* structure, and the load transfer mechanism, design philosophy, and execution sequence are fundamentally different.
> - **Always mention the "Hit-and-Miss" sequence** in mass concrete underpinning questions — students who draw a continuous strip lose **2–3 marks** for the sketch.
> - **For mini-pile problems, students frequently forget to apply the FOS to the *total* capacity (end-bearing + friction) and then add the FOS to each term separately.** This gives the wrong answer. Apply FOS to the *final* $Q_{up}$, not to the individual terms.
> - **Mention IS codes** wherever possible: **IS 1904:1986** (settlement), **IS 2911 Part 4:1985** (mini-piles), **IS 2974 Part 1** (machine foundations). Even a single IS code reference adds **1–2 marks** in valuation.
> - **In sketches, always show:** (i) the old footing, (ii) the new pad/pile, (iii) the temporary support (needle/shore), and (iv) the new founding stratum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Underpinning (Module 4) — 12-Point Cheat Sheet:**

- **1. Definition:** Underpinning is the *rehabilitation of an existing foundation* by strengthening, deepening, or extending it to a more competent stratum.
- **2. Two Major Families:** (a) **Conventional** — Mass Concrete, Beam-and-Base, Needle Beam. (b) **Modern** — Mini-Piled, Jet Grouting, Resin Injection.
- **3. Pit Method Rule:** Maximum bay length = **1.0–1.5 m**; at any time, **≥ 50% of the wall** must remain supported.
- **4. Concrete Grade:** Mass concrete underpinning uses **M15 (1:2:4)** nominal mix.
- **5. Layer Thickness:** Each compacted layer of concrete = **150 mm**.
- **6. Curing Period:** Minimum **7 days** before opening the alternate bay.
- **7. Practical Detailing:** New pad must extend **≥ 150 mm** on each side of the existing footing.
- **8. Pile Design FOS:** **2.5** for end-bearing + skin friction combined; **3.0** for end-bearing alone.
- **9. Settlement Limit:** Differential settlement ≤ **0.002 L** (IS 1904:1986).
- **10. Jet Grout Columns:** Diameter **0.6 m – 2.0 m**; grout pressure **30–40 MPa**; volume over-consumption factor **1.3–1.5**.
- **11. Mini-Pile Codes:** **IS 2911 (Part 4):1985** for micropiles; **IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 2):1979** for bored piles.
- **12. Key Differences — Underpinning vs. Shoring vs. Jacketing:** Underpinning is **permanent + vertical** (foundation); Shoring is **temporary + lateral** (wall); Jacketing is **member-level** (column/beam).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demolition Techniques - Non-explosive and Explosive Demolition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Demolition Techniques - Non-explosive and Explosive Demolition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Demolition** is the deliberate, engineered process of dismantling or destroying a structure—either partially or completely—once it has reached the end of its service life, becomes structurally unsafe, or needs to be replaced. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PECET634), demolition is treated as the *inverse of construction*: a controlled reverse engineering procedure governed by safety, sequence, and structural mechanics.

In the context of **Repair and Rehabilitation of Structures**, demolition is the *terminal stage* of the structural life cycle, executed when retrofitting, strengthening, or repair is technically infeasible or economically unviable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> Demolition is the systematic dismantling of a building or structural component using controlled methods—manual, mechanical, chemical, thermal, or explosive—so as to ensure **zero uncontrolled collapse**, **minimum collateral damage**, and **maximum recovery of reusable materials**.

### 1.2 Broad Classification

Demolition techniques are classified into **two principal families**:

| Family | Tag | Core Philosophy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Explosive Demolition | **NED** | Apply force gradually, locally, or through chemical expansion | Urban, sensitive, low-rise to mid-rise |
| Explosive Demolition | **ED** | Apply a sudden, calculated energy release to overcome structural resistance | High-rise chimneys, towers, bridges, mass concrete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-demolition Survey (Clause 4.1 of IS 4130:1991):** Before *any* technique is selected, a **structural audit** must be conducted to map load paths, material strengths, and adjacent property risks. This governs the choice between NED and ED.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **disassembling a Lego tower**:
- **Non-explosive demolition** is like pulling out the bricks *one by one* (manual) or *knocking the bottom rows* so the upper rows gently settle (mechanical pusher). You control *where* the energy goes.
- **Explosive demolition** is like setting a small firecracker at the base—the energy release is sudden, the structure "fails" along a pre-designed collapse path, and gravity does the rest.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's heuristic):**
> If the structure's **Height $\ge 3 \times$ Base Width**, mechanical breakers become inefficient → *consider implosion*.
> If the structure is **within 15 m of an operational facility**, explosive methods are *typically prohibited* → *use NED*.

---

### 1.4 Selection Criteria (Why a particular technique?)

The choice of demolition technique is governed by the following decision variables, taught in Module 4 of PECET634:

1. **Height ($H$) and footprint ($B$)** of the structure.
2. **Structural system** — RCC framed, load-bearing masonry, steel truss, composite, or mass concrete.
3. **Adjacency** — proximity to roads, live utilities, occupied buildings.
4. **Material recovery target** — percentage of reusable steel, concrete aggregate, brick.
5. **Time window** — project schedule vs. allowed shutdown.
6. **Noise / dust / vibration limits** — municipal or environmental norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability Note (KTU 2024 NEP emphasis):**
> Modern demolition aims for **$\geq 70\%$ diversion of debris from landfill**, achieved through selective NED. ED methods produce mixed, often contaminated rubble—less aligned with circular-economy goals.

---

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (as used by KTU Board Examiners)

- **Concrete compressive strength (assumed for design)**: $f_c = 20\text{–}30\ \text{MPa}$ for old RCC, **$25\ \text{MPa}$** as default design value.
- **Reinforcement yield strength (mild steel)**: $f_y = 250\ \text{MPa}$.
- **Density of structural concrete**: $\rho_c = \mathbf{25\ kN/m^3}$.
- **Density of masonry**: $\rho_m = \mathbf{18\text{–}20\ kN/m^3}$.
- **Density of steel**: $\rho_s = \mathbf{78.5\ kN/m^3}$.
- **Permissible peak particle velocity (PPV) at adjacent structure**: $v_{ppv} \le \mathbf{5\ mm/s}$ (DIN 4150, commonly cited in KTU answers).
- **Charge factor (explosive)**: $C_f = \mathbf{0.2\text{–}0.5\ kg/m^3}$ of concrete volume to be removed.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Non-Explosive Demolition (NED) — Detailed Mechanisms

Non-explosive demolition encompasses all methods that **do not rely on detonation** of a chemical explosive. They are the *default* in Indian urban practice and constitute roughly **80% of demolition work** executed under KTU-relevant projects.

#### 2.1.1 Manual Demolition
- **Principle:** Hand tools (sledgehammers, chisel hammers, oxy-acetylene torches, concrete saws).
- **Step-wise logic:**
  1. Disconnect all utilities (electrical, water, gas).
  2. Remove non-structural elements (doors, partitions, flooring).
  3. Cut beams and slabs into manageable panels.
  4. Lower debris via chutes — *never drop from height*.
- **Why this works:** Eliminates the risk of uncontrolled collapse in **load-bearing masonry** structures where the wall itself is the load path.
- **Limitation:** Labor-intensive; **production rate $\approx 0.5\text{–}1.0\ \text{m}^3$/hour per worker**.

#### 2.1.2 Mechanical Demolition
- **Equipment deployed:**
  - **Hydraulic excavator** with breaker attachment (jackhammer).
  - **Crushing jaws / pulverizers** (concrete crackers).
  - **High-reach demolition arm** (long-boom excavator, reach up to $40\ \text{m}$).
  - **Wrecking ball** (legacy, $1\text{–}5$ tonne steel sphere on a cable).
- **Operational logic:**
  1. Position machine on stable, level ground (verify soil bearing capacity $\ge \mathbf{100\ kN/m^2}$).
  2. Begin at top; work downward in reverse-construction sequence.
  3. Always pre-weaken the structure at the **collapse line** by removing columns/slabs in a designed pattern.
- **Why this works:** The excavator delivers **impact energy** $E_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ where $m$ is the hammer mass and $v$ is the impact velocity, easily exceeding the **fracture energy** of concrete.

#### 2.1.3 Chemical / Expansive Demolition
- **Principle:** Bore holes into the concrete, fill with **non-explosive demolition agent (NEDA)** — a calcium-silicate based powder that expands up to **$12{,}000\ \text{kN/m}^2$** of expansive pressure when mixed with water.
- **Step-wise logic:**
  1. Drill holes of diameter $d = 30\text{–}50\ \text{mm}$ at spacing $s = (5\text{–}10) \cdot d$.
  2. Mix NEDA with water (water-to-powder ratio $\approx 0.30$).
  3. Pour slurry into holes; tamp lightly.
  4. Wait **12–24 hours** for full hydration expansion.
  5. Concrete cracks along the designed hole pattern.
- **Why this works:** Internal tensile stress $\sigma_t$ generated exceeds the **modulus of rupture** $f_{cr}$ of concrete, typically $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- **Use case:** Vibration-sensitive zones (hospitals, heritage buildings, computer rooms).

#### 2.1.4 Thermal Demolition
- **Principle:** Use an **oxy-fuel lance** or **thermic lance** burning iron in pure oxygen, reaching temperatures $> \mathbf{3{,}000\ ^\circ C}$, which **melts and oxidizes** reinforcement and disintegrates concrete matrix.
- **Why this works:** Differential thermal expansion between aggregate and cement paste creates micro-cracking; subsequent melting of steel removes the tensile reinforcement.
- **Use case:** Cutting through heavily reinforced sections (pile caps, thick raft foundations).

#### 2.1.5 Water-Jet / Hydraulic Bursting
- **Principle:** High-pressure water jet ($> 250\ \text{MPa}$) or hydraulic splitters (a wedge driven between pre-drilled holes).
- **Use case:** Precision cutting of concrete, especially where dust and vibration are forbidden.

---

### 2.2 Explosive Demolition (ED) — Detailed Mechanisms

Explosive demolition, often misleadingly called *implosion*, is a **scientifically orchestrated** release of energy to overcome the **gravitational and structural resistance** of a building.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implosion vs. Explosion (KTU 2024 distinction):**
> - **Explosion** → debris flies *outward* (uncontrolled, undesirable in demolition).
> - **Implosion** → debris collapses *inward* into the building's own footprint (controlled, desirable in urban demolition).

#### 2.2.1 Pre-Weakening (The "Collapse Path")
Before charges are placed, the structure is **surgically weakened** along a designed vertical *collapse line*:
- Columns on selected floors are cut to expose only the longitudinal reinforcement.
- Shear walls are pre-cut.
- Slab-beam connections may be partially severed.
- This converts the structure from a *rigid frame* into a *kinematically admissible collapse mechanism*.

#### 2.2.2 Charge Placement and Detonation Sequence
- Charges (typically **RDX/TNT/PETN-based shaped boosters**) are placed against pre-weakened columns.
- **Detonation delay sequence** (millisecond delay) is critical:
  - **First delay** ($\approx 0$ ms): Central columns — structure begins to sag.
  - **Second delay** ($\approx 250\text{–}500$ ms): Perimeter columns — structure folds inward.
  - **Third delay** (if needed): Outer columns — final "pancake" stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Millisecond Delay Logic:**
> Sequential detonation transforms a *static structure* into a *dynamic mechanism*. Each delay allows debris from the previous blast to clear the path for the next, ensuring debris falls *inside* the footprint.

#### 2.2.3 Charge Calculation (KTU High-Yield)
The total explosive charge $W$ for a column of cross-section $A$ and height $h$ is empirically given by:

$$W = k \cdot A \cdot h$$

where $k$ is the **charge factor** (kg of explosive per $\text{m}^3$ of concrete), typically $\mathbf{0.2\text{–}0.5\ kg/m^3}$.

For multi-column blasts, total charge scales with volume of concrete to be pulverized.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table note:** Vertical bars (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown structure.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress from temperature rise $\Delta T$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 2 | $P_{exp} \ge 4 \cdot f_t$ | Expansive pressure must exceed $4 \times$ tensile strength | $\text{kN/m}^2$ |
| 3 | $W = k \cdot A \cdot h$ | Charge mass for a column | $\text{kg}$ |
| 4 | $C_f = W / V$ | Charge factor (explosive mass per concrete volume) | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 5 | $E_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Impact energy of mechanical breaker | $\text{Joules}$ |
| 6 | $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Modulus of rupture of concrete | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 7 | $v_{ppv} \le 5\ \text{mm/s}$ | Safe peak particle velocity at adjacent building | $\text{mm/s}$ |
| 8 | $H/B \ge 3$ | Threshold for considering implosion | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\rho_c = 25\ \text{kN/m}^3$ | Unit weight of RCC (design value) | $\text{kN/m}^3$ |
| 10 | $d = (1/5 \text{ to } 1/10) \cdot s$ | NEDA hole diameter vs. spacing rule | $\text{mm}$ |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NED methods** dominate urban Indian demolition practice: high-rise apartment dismantling in Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, and Kerala's coastal towns where adjacent buildings are within 3–5 m.
- **ED methods** are reserved for **smokestacks** (thermal power stations), **abandoned factories** with isolated footprints, and **bridge demolition** (e.g., controlled deck drop onto prepared sand bed).
- **Hybrid methods** — partial NED pre-weakening followed by ED collapse — are increasingly common and frequently appear in KTU Part B questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's heuristic for selecting NED vs. ED in exam answers:**
> 1. Adjacent property within 15 m → **NED**.
> 2. Structure height $> 30$ m and isolated footprint → **ED**.
> 3. Reinforcement recovery target $\ge 80\%$ → **NED (mechanical pulverizer)**.
> 4. Time window $< 7$ days for a 10-storey building → **ED (implosion)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — NED vs. ED Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is structured exactly as examiners expect in a 14-mark descriptive answer. Memorize the comparative logic, not the wording.

| Parameter | Non-Explosive Demolition (NED) | Explosive Demolition (ED) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Mechanical, thermal, chemical | Detonation of chemical explosive |
| **Rate of energy release** | Slow, controllable | Sudden ($\mu$s timescale) |
| **Noise level** | Moderate (75–95 dB) | Very high (120–180 dB) |
| **Vibration / PPV** | Low ($\le 2\ \text{mm/s}$ typical) | High (requires monitoring $\le 5\ \text{mm/s}$ at boundary) |
| **Dust generation** | Moderate, can be suppressed with water spray | High; pre-wetting mandatory |
| **Adjacency tolerance** | Tight (as low as 0.5 m) | Generous (minimum 15–30 m clear zone) |
| **Material recovery** | High (selective dismantling, $70\text{–}90\%$) | Low (mixed rubble, $20\text{–}40\%$) |
| **Skill requirement** | Standard trades + machine operators | Licensed blaster + structural engineer |
| **Permit complexity** | Low–moderate | High (PESO, district administration, police) |
| **Cost** (per m³, indicative) | ₹ 800–1,500 | ₹ 1,200–2,500 (excluding permits) |
| **Collapse pattern** | Controlled, top-down | Designed inward fold ("implosion") |
| **Pre-demolition survey** | Detailed, structural audit | Critical — drives charge calculation |
| **Best suited for** | Urban, sensitive, mid-rise, adaptive reuse | Isolated, tall, mass concrete, time-critical |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Charge Factor for a Column

**Problem statement (typical KTU-style 7-mark):**
A $300\ \text{mm} \times 300\ \text{mm}$ RCC column of height $3.0\ \text{m}$ is to be demolished by explosive method. Given the charge factor $k = 0.35\ \text{kg/m}^3$, determine the **total charge required** and verify whether it falls within the safe range for a single column charge.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 0.300 \times 0.300 = 0.09\ \text{m}^2$$
Height of column to be shattered:
$$h = 3.0\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the concrete volume of the column.**
$$V = A \cdot h = 0.09 \times 3.0 = 0.27\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the charge-mass equation.**
$$W = k \cdot A \cdot h = 0.35 \cdot 0.27 = 0.0945\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to grams for practical placement.**
$$W = 0.0945 \times 1000 = 94.5\ \text{g} \approx 95\ \text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Distribute the charge along the column (KTU standard practice).**
For a $3.0\ \text{m}$ column, place charges at **three levels** (top, middle, base):
$$W_{\text{each}} = \frac{95}{3} \approx 31.7\ \text{g per level}$$

**Step 6 — Verify safety constraint.**
A typical safe single-column charge limit is $\le 0.2\ \text{kg}$ (200 g) to avoid fly-rock beyond the safety perimeter. Our computed $W = 0.0945\ \text{kg} < 0.2\ \text{kg}$ → **SAFE**.

**Final answer:**
> Total charge required $= \mathbf{0.0945\ kg\ (94.5\ g)}$; distributed as $\approx 32\ \text{g}$ at each of three detonation levels. The charge is within safe limits for a single column.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — NEDA Hole Pattern

**Problem statement (typical KTU-style 7-mark):**
A $1.5\ \text{m}$ thick RCC mass concrete pier is to be split using a non-explosive expansive agent. Hole diameter chosen is $d = 40\ \text{mm}$. Determine (a) the recommended hole spacing, and (b) the number of holes required per square metre of exposed face.

**Step 1 — Recall the spacing rule.**
$$s = (5 \text{ to } 10) \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Choose the lower-bound spacing (more conservative, better crack control).**
For mass concrete where cracks must be tight and clean, $s = 5d$ is preferred:
$$s = 5 \times 40 = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.20\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute number of holes per square metre.**
Grid layout — holes at every $0.20\ \text{m}$:
$$N = \frac{1}{s^2} = \frac{1}{(0.20)^2} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25\ \text{holes/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using the upper-bound alternative.**
If $s = 10d = 400\ \text{mm}$ is used (less aggressive, for lightly reinforced sections):
$$N = \frac{1}{0.16} \approx 6\ \text{holes/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Engineering judgement.**
Since the pier is mass concrete (low reinforcement, low tensile capacity), the **upper bound** is acceptable. Recommended: $s = 400\ \text{mm}$, giving $\approx \mathbf{6\ holes/m^2}$.

> **Final answer:** Recommended spacing $s = 0.40\ \text{m}$ → **$\mathbf{6\ \text{holes per square metre}}$** of exposed face.

---

### 3.4 Decision-Flow Derivation — When to use ED over NED

**Engineering derivation of the 15 m clearance rule:**

The blast-induced pressure wave attenuates with distance as:
$$P(r) = \frac{P_0}{(r/r_0)^{1.5}}$$

For a typical charge $W = 0.1\ \text{kg}$, scaled distance:
$$Z = \frac{r}{W^{1/3}} = \frac{r}{(0.1)^{1/3}} = \frac{r}{0.464}$$

The safe scaled distance (no glass breakage, no structural damage) corresponds to $Z \ge 30\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$. Solving:
$$r = 0.464 \times 30 \approx 13.9\ \text{m} \approx \mathbf{15\ m}$$

Hence, the **15 m clearance rule** is not arbitrary — it is derived from the inverse-cube-root blast scaling law (Hopkinson's cube law).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define demolition. List any four non-explosive methods of demolition with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Demolition:** Demolition is the engineered, controlled process of dismantling or destroying a structure (or part of it) once it has reached the end of its service life or is declared unsafe, using methods that ensure safety of personnel, adjacent property, and maximize material recovery.

**Four non-explosive methods:**

1. **Manual Demolition** – Hand tools such as sledgehammers, chisel hammers, and oxy-acetylene torches are used to dismantle the structure element-by-element from top to bottom.
2. **Mechanical Demolition** – Hydraulic excavators fitted with breakers, pulverizers, or high-reach arms apply impact energy to fracture concrete.
3. **Chemical / Expansive Demolition (NEDA)** – Calcium-silicate based expansive grout is poured into pre-drilled holes; hydration generates pressures $\ge 12{,}000\ \text{kN/m}^2$, cracking the concrete silently.
4. **Thermal Demolition** – An oxy-fuel / thermic lance burns iron in pure oxygen ($\sim 3{,}000\ ^\circ C$), melting reinforcement and disintegrating concrete.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the difference between *implosion* and *explosion* in the context of structural demolition?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Explosion | Implosion |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of debris | Outward (radial) | Inward (vertical) |
| Energy release | Uncontrolled, rapid | Controlled, sequenced |
| Use in demolition | **Not desirable** (causes fly-rock, collateral damage) | **Standard practice** for tall isolated structures |
| Mechanism | Sudden failure of the entire load path | Millisecond-delay, sequential failure of pre-weakened columns allowing gravity-driven inward fold |

> In modern KTU-aligned demolition practice, the term *"implosion"* specifically refers to the engineered *inward* collapse of a structure onto its own footprint, achieved by sequencing the detonation of charges on pre-weakened columns.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B answers on demolition, students **consistently lose 3–4 marks** because they:
> 1. Forget to mention the **Pre-Demolition Structural Audit** (often asked as a sub-part).
> 2. Confuse *charge factor* with *total charge* — always show units and the formula separately.
> 3. Skip the **safety perimeter / PPV** check in explosive demolition answers.
> 4. Write only *advantages* of NED/ED without a **comparative table** — examiners explicitly reward structured tables.
> 5. Forget to cite the **standard code reference** (IS 4130:1991 for safety, IS 14692:1998 for blasting, where applicable).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of demolition techniques** with suitable sketches/flow diagrams. Highlight the **selection criteria** for choosing between non-explosive and explosive methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RCC building of plan $20\ \text{m} \times 15\ \text{m}$ and height $45\ \text{m}$ is to be demolished. The adjacent building is at $8\ \text{m}$ from the boundary. Recommend a suitable demolition method with **full justification**, and outline the **step-by-step procedure** to be followed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Selection Criteria:**

Demolition techniques are broadly classified as:

- **Non-Explosive Demolition (NED):** Manual, Mechanical (excavator-breaker, pulverizer, high-reach arm, wrecking ball), Chemical (Non-Explosive Demolition Agent), Thermal (oxy-fuel lance), Hydraulic bursting, Water-jet cutting.
