```mermaid
    Q1{Is the site constrained with adjacent structures?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is excavation depth more than 8 m?}
    Q1 -->|No| C1[Use Conventional Bottom-Up Method]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is the soil soft clay or loose sand?}
    Q2 -->|No| C1
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Are there noise vibration or settlement restrictions?}
    Q3 -->|No| C2[Consider Anchored Diaphragm Wall with Bottom-Up]
    Q4 -->|Yes| R1[USE TOP-DOWN METHOD]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is project timeline critical?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| R1
    Q5 -->|No| C2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** When drawing the Top-Down sequence in KTU answer sheets, use a vertical timeline (left to right) with downward excavation shown as descending arrows and superstructure construction as ascending arrows. Label each slab B0, B1, B2, B3, and clearly mark the **open shaft** through which mucking occurs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Top-Down Method of construction. List any four situations in which it is preferred over the conventional bottom-up method.**

**Model Answer:**

The Top-Down Method is a construction technique in which the permanent structural elements of a building (basement walls, columns, and floor slabs) are constructed simultaneously from the top of the excavation downward, while excavation progresses downward. The permanent floor slabs themselves act as lateral struts to support the perimeter retaining walls.

**Situations where it is preferred:**
1. Deep excavations (more than 8–10 m) in dense urban areas with adjacent structures.
2. Sites with strict restrictions on noise, vibration, and ground settlement.
3. Projects requiring fast track construction (superstructure and substructure can proceed in parallel).
4. Repair and rehabilitation of existing buildings requiring new deep basements or foundation underpinning.
5. Construction of underground metro stations and transit hubs in operational city centers.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition – 1 mark | Situations – 2 marks (0.5 each for 4 correct points)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a king post in Top-Down construction? Explain its function.**

**Model Answer:**

A **king post** is a vertical structural member (typically a steel H-section or a reinforced concrete barrette) installed in the ground before excavation begins. It is founded on a load-bearing stratum (rock or hard clay) below the final excavation level.

**Functions of a King Post:**
1. **Load Transfer:** It carries the vertical loads of the superstructure and the basement floors down to the deep foundation stratum, bypassing the soil that will be excavated.
2. **Lateral Stability:** It acts as a vertical prop, restraining the inward movement of the D-wall during staged excavation.
3. **Construction Sequencing:** It allows the superstructure to be built upward in parallel with basement excavation going downward.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition – 1 mark | Functions – 2 marks

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step construction sequence of the Top-Down Method with the help of a neat sketch. Highlight the role of the diaphragm wall, king post, and floor slab struts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Top-Down construction is planned for a building with a 4-level basement in soft clay. The soil has $\phi_u = 0°$, $c_u = 60$ kN/m², and $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. The excavation depth is **15 m**. Calculate the maximum bending moment per meter run in the D-wall during the cantilever stage (i.e., before the first basement slab is cast). Take $H_{eq} = 1.2 H_e$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction Sequence and Roles (7 Marks):**

The Top-Down Method proceeds in the following sequence:

1. **Construction of Perimeter Diaphragm Wall:** A reinforced concrete diaphragm wall, typically 600–1000 mm thick, is constructed using the slurry trench technique. The wall is taken down to a depth of 1.2 to 1.5 times the final excavation depth to ensure adequate embedment and fixity. **Role:** It acts as the permanent basement wall and provides lateral earth retention throughout construction.

2. **Installation of King Posts:** Steel H-piles or reinforced concrete barrettes are drilled and socketed into the rock/bearing stratum at the future column locations. **Role:** They carry the entire vertical load of the superstructure and basement floors down to the deep foundation, allowing construction to proceed in any direction.

3. **Casting of the Ground Floor Slab (B0):** The B0 slab is cast at the existing ground level, monolithically connected to the D-wall and king posts. **Role:** It serves as the first permanent strut and provides a working platform for superstructure construction.

4. **Commencement of Superstructure and Excavation:** Superstructure columns are extended upward from B0, while excavation begins below B0 through pre-planned **open shafts**. Soil is removed by clamshell grabs lowered through the shafts.

5. **Casting of B1 Slab:** When excavation reaches the B1 level, the B1 floor slab is cast and tied to the D-wall. **Role:** It becomes the second permanent strut, redistributing earth pressure.

6. **Repetition:** Steps 4–5 are repeated for B2, B3, and so on, until the formation level is reached.

7. **Casting of Base Slab/Raft:** The base slab seals the structure and provides the final reaction against uplift and lateral pressure.

8. **Superstructure Completion:** Construction continues upward until the top-down and bottom-up works meet at the upper floors.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** A cross-section showing the D-wall on both sides, king posts in the center, B0 at top, B1, B2, B3 below, with descending excavation arrows and ascending superstructure arrows. An open shaft should be shown penetrating all slabs for mucking access.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\phi_u = 0°$ (undrained condition for soft clay)
- $c_u = 60$ kN/m²
- $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³
- $H_e = 15$ m
- $H_{eq} = 1.2 \times 15 = 18$ m

**Step 1: Active Earth Pressure Coefficient for $\phi = 0°$**

$$
K_a = \tan^2\left(45^\circ - \frac{0}{2}\right) = \tan^2(45^\circ) = 1.0
$$

**Step 2: Active Pressure at Depth z**

For $\phi = 0°$ soil, Rankine's equation is:

$$
\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = 1.0 \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2 \cdot c_u \cdot 1.0
$$

$$
\sigma_a = 17z - 120 \quad \text{(in kN/m²)}
$$

**Step 3: Depth of Zero Net Pressure (Tension Crack Depth)**

Setting $\sigma_a = 0$:

$$
17z_0 = 120 \implies z_0 = 7.06 \text{ m}
$$

For $\phi = 0°$ soil, the actual negative pressures (tension zone) are neglected in design. Therefore, we consider active pressure to act only below $z_0 = 7.06$ m. The effective height for the cantilever is:

$$
H_{eff} = H_{eq} - z_0 = 18 - 7.06 = 10.94 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Moment at the Cantilever Base**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \gamma \cdot K_a \cdot H_{eff}^3
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{1}{6} \times 17 \times 1.0 \times (10.94)^3
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{17 \times 1309.4}{6} = \frac{22259.8}{6} \approx 3710 \text{ kN·m per m run}
$$

**Step 5: Practical Design Value**

A more conservative value (often used in KTU board answers) is obtained by ignoring the cohesion contribution and using the full cantilever height:

$$
M_{max,conservative} = \frac{K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H_{eq}^3}{6} = \frac{1.0 \times 17 \times 18^3}{6} = \frac{17 \times 5832}{6} = 16524 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

> **Recommended Answer for KTU:** $M_{max} \approx 16524$ kN·m/m (if using the full cantilever height conservatively) OR $\approx 3710$ kN·m/m (if using the tension-crack-corrected effective height). **Always state your assumption.**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the Top-Down Method with the conventional Bottom-Up Method of construction. Discuss at least six points of comparison and state two situations where Top-Down is indispensable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Top-Down project, a floor slab at a depth of **6 m** acts as a strut between two D-walls spaced **20 m apart**. The soil parameters are $\phi = 32°$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Estimate the axial compression developed in the slab per meter width, assuming it is the only strut supporting the D-wall. Neglect surcharge. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table and Indispensable Situations (7 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Bottom-Up Method | Top-Down Method |
|---------|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Construction Direction | Upward (base → top) | Both directions (top-down and bottom-up simultaneously) |
| 2 | Lateral Support | Temporary steel struts and walers | Permanent floor slabs (used as struts) |
| 3 | Construction Time | Longer (sequential) | Shorter (30–40% time savings) |
| 4 | Cost of Temporary Works | High | Low (struts are part of permanent structure) |
| 5 | Deflection Control | Moderate; can be large | Excellent; multiple slab levels limit movement |
| 6 | Suitability for Urban Sites | Limited | Excellent |
| 7 | Complexity | Low | High (M&E coordination through king posts) |
| 8 | Weather Impact | High at open excavation | Low; top slab provides cover |

**Two Indispensable Situations:**
1. **Deep Basements in Dense Urban Areas:** When adjacent heritage or sensitive buildings cannot tolerate settlement.
2. **Metro/Underground Transit Construction:** Where surface disruption is unacceptable and fast project delivery is essential.

**Marks:** 1 mark per comparison point × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for indispensable situations.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Depth of strut from ground level: $z = 6$ m
- Span between D-walls: $L = 20$ m
- Soil friction angle: $\phi = 32°$
- Soil unit weight: $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³
- Surcharge: $q = 0$
- Single strut system (cantilever below strut + simply supported above)

**Step 1: Active Earth Pressure Coefficient**

$$
K_a = \tan^2\left(45^\circ - \frac{32°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(29°) = (0.5543)^2 = 0.3073
$$

**Step 2: Total Active Thrust on the D-Wall (per meter run)**

For a cantilever of height $z = 6$ m (since the slab is the first and only support, the wall above the slab acts as a cantilever):

$$
P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z^2
$$

$$
P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3073 \times 19 \times 6^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3073 \times 19 \times 36
$$

$$
P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 210.19 = 105.1 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 3: Reaction Force at the Strut**

The strut must provide a horizontal reaction equal to the active thrust on the D-wall above it. Per meter run of wall:

$$
R = P_a = 105.1 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4: Axial Compression in the Slab**

The slab spans horizontally between the two D-walls. The axial compression per meter of slab length (along the wall) is:

$$
C = R \times L = 105.1 \times 20 = 2102 \text{ kN}
$$

This is the **total axial compression in the slab strip across the full 20 m span**.

**Step 5: Compression per meter width of slab**

If the slab is, say, 250 mm thick and 1 m wide strip, its cross-sectional area is $A = 0.25 \times 1 = 0.25$ m². The compressive stress in the slab is:

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{C}{A} = \frac{2102}{0.25} = 8408 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 8.41 \text{ MPa}
$$

For M30 concrete, $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa, so the compressive stress is well within limits. However, the slab must also be checked for buckling using Euler's formula.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often calculate $P_a$ but forget to multiply by the span $L$ to get the axial compression. The reaction force $R$ is per meter, and the slab compression is $R \times L$. **Loss of 2 marks** if missed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $H_{eq}$ with $H_e$ in cantilever moment problems. Always state your assumed factor.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the negative sign correction in the Rankine equation for $\phi = 0°$ soils. The cohesion term $-2c\sqrt{K_a}$ reduces active pressure significantly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not mentioning the role of king posts and the need for open shafts. These are **expected parts of any KTU Top-Down answer**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Top-Down Method**

- **Core Concept:** Top-Down = Build permanent structure downward while digging downward. Floor slabs act as permanent struts.

- **Key Components:** Diaphragm Wall (D-Wall), King Posts/Barrettes, Floor Slab Struts, Open Shafts, Base Slab/Raft.

- **Standard D-Wall Thickness:** **600 mm to 1200 mm**; typical depth up to **40–60 m**.

- **Active Pressure Formula:** $\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$

- **Cantilever Moment Formula:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H_{eq}^3}{6}$ with $H_{eq} = (1.0 \text{ to } 1.5) \times H_e$

- **Strut Reaction at Level $i$:** $R_i = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot (z_i^2 - z_{i-1}^2)$

- **Time Savings:** 30–40% faster than conventional bottom-up method.

- **Best Suited For:** Deep basements (more than 8 m), urban constrained sites, heritage rehabilitation, metro stations, and seismic zones with liquefaction concerns.

- **Critical Design Checks:** (1) Wall bending moment, (2) Strut axial capacity, (3) King post buckling, (4) Base slab uplift, (5) Wall deflection ($\leq 0.002H$).

- **Sequence Mnemonic (for exams):** "**D-Wall → King Post → Top Slab → Excavate → Cast Next Slab → Repeat → Base Slab → Superstructure Up.**"

- **Key Difference from Bottom-Up:** No temporary struts are required; the permanent structure does the work of shoring.

- **Numerical Tip:** Always include the surcharge term $K_a \cdot q$ in the active pressure equation, even if $q = 0$, to show the examiner you know the full form.

- **Real-World Examples:** Chennai Metro, Mumbai High-Rise Basements, Delhi Metro Phase 4, Bengaluru Namma Metro stations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Crusher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Crusher

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydraulic Crusher** is a mechanized demolition device that converts hydrostatic fluid pressure into a controlled, high-impact linear or rotary crushing force, used for the localized fracturing, splitting, or removal of deteriorated concrete, masonry, and reinforced steel components during the structural rehabilitation process. It operates on **Pascal's Law**, which states that pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET634 - Module 4):**
> A hydraulic crusher is a demolition attachment/tool that uses pressurized hydraulic fluid (typically mineral oil at operating pressures of **100–300 bar**) to generate a powerful crushing or shearing action, enabling selective dismantling of defective structural members without causing excessive vibration, dust, or noise to adjacent sound portions of the structure.

## 1.2 Key Classification of Hydraulic Crushers

| Type | Application | Typical Capacity |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Concrete Pulverizer / Cracker** | Primary crushing of concrete beams, columns, walls | 30–80 ton crushing force |
| **Hydraulic Breaker (Hammer)** | Impact breaking of rock, asphalt, reinforced concrete | 500–8,000 J blow energy |
| **Hydraulic Shear** | Cutting steel reinforcement, plates, beams | Up to 600 mm bar diameter |
| **Multi-Processor (Combo Crusher)** | Combined crushing + cutting + demolition | 40–100 ton |
| **Concrete Splitter (Silent Crusher)** | Static splitting of oversized concrete blocks | 200–500 ton splitting force |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Industrial Nutcracker" Analogy:**
> Imagine trying to crack a very hard walnut. Using a regular hammer creates messy splinters and shakes the whole table. But if you place the walnut in a *nutcracker* and slowly squeeze — the shell splits cleanly along a controlled line, and the table doesn't move.
>
> A **Hydraulic Crusher works on the exact same principle**. Instead of violent impact (like a hammer), it uses the *gentle but irresistible* force of pressurized oil to slowly squeeze concrete apart along its weakest plane. The "hand" of the nutcracker is the hydraulic cylinder piston, and the "muscle" is the oil pump.

## 1.4 Why Hydraulic Crushers Are Used in Repair & Rehabilitation

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction from New Construction:**
> In *new construction*, you BUILD UP. In *rehabilitation*, you often must take things APART carefully. Traditional demolition (sledgehammers, wrecking balls) transmits shockwaves through the structure, cracking the **sound concrete you want to keep**. A hydraulic crusher is the only tool that allows **selective, low-vibration, surgical removal** of defective zones.

The main engineering reasons hydraulic crushers are mandated by modern rehabilitation codes:

- **Vibration Control:** Limits peak particle velocity (PPV) to **< 5 mm/s** to prevent damage to adjacent sound concrete (per IS 6922 / DIN 4150 standards).
- **Dust Suppression:** Water-spray compatible, critical for urban repair projects.
- **Noise Compliance:** Operates at **< 75 dB(A)** at 7 m, suitable for hospitals and occupied buildings.
- **Precision:** Can crush a single beam without damaging the column-beam joint.
- **Steel Recovery:** Shears cut rebar cleanly for recycling.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in Hydraulic Crusher Design (must be memorized for KTU):**
>
> - **Operating Pressure (P):** **100 – 300 bar** (1 bar = 0.1 MPa = 10⁵ Pa)
> - **Crushing Force (F):** **30 – 100 ton-force** (1 tonf ≈ 9.81 kN)
> - **Hydraulic Fluid:** Mineral oil (ISO VG 46), kinematic viscosity **46 cSt @ 40°C**
> - **Flow Rate (Q):** **50 – 250 L/min**
> - **Cycle Time:** **8 – 15 seconds** (open-close)
> - **Power Pack:** Diesel engine **50 – 200 kW** or electric motor
> - **Jaw Opening:** **400 – 1,200 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Pascal's Law Applied

The hydraulic crusher operates purely on **Pascal's Law** (1653):

> *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible static fluid is transmitted with equal magnitude to all parts of the fluid and the walls of the container."*

This is mathematically expressed as:

$$P_1 = P_2 \implies \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = Pressure on small piston (pump side) = **100 – 300 bar**
- $P_2$ = Pressure transmitted to large piston (crusher side)
- $F_1$ = Input force from pump (kN)
- $F_2$ = Output crushing force (kN)
- $A_1$ = Area of pump cylinder (mm²)
- $A_2$ = Area of crusher cylinder (mm²)

Since the pressure is equal, the **force is amplified** in the ratio of the piston areas. A small force on a small piston generates an enormous force on a large piston. This is the **hydraulic mechanical advantage**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The crusher operates in a closed hydraulic circuit, with the following sequential steps:

1. **Fluid Reservoir:** Hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46) stored in a tank, capacity **200 – 600 L**.
2. **Prime Mover:** Diesel engine or electric motor drives the pump.
3. **High-Pressure Pump:** Axial piston pump generates **280 – 320 bar** system pressure.
4. **Directional Control Valve (DCV):** 4/3 solenoid-operated valve directs oil to the cap-end or rod-end of the crusher cylinder.
5. **Actuation Cycle:**
   - **Cap-end pressurized** → Piston extends → Jaw closes → Crushing force applied.
   - **Rod-end pressurized** → Piston retracts → Jaw opens → Concrete fragment released.
6. **Relief Valve:** Prevents over-pressure at **> 320 bar**, protecting seals and hoses.
7. **Regeneration Circuit:** Oil from rod-end is fed back to cap-end during rapid jaw opening, doubling speed (used for cycle-time optimization).

## 2.3 Theoretical Crushing Mechanics

When a hydraulic crusher jaw closes on a concrete element, the **crushing force F** must overcome the **compressive strength of concrete** $f_{ck}$ over the contact area. The basic equilibrium is:

$$F_{crusher} \geq f_{ck} \times A_{contact} \times n_{safety}$$

Where:
- $F_{crusher}$ = Required crushing force (kN)
- $f_{ck}$ = Characteristic compressive strength of concrete (MPa) = **20 – 60 MPa** for typical RCC
- $A_{contact}$ = Contact area between jaw tooth and concrete (m²)
- $n_{safety}$ = Factor of safety (typically **1.5 – 2.0**)

For reinforced concrete, the force must also sever the **longitudinal reinforcement** at the cut section. The force to shear a rebar of diameter $d$ made of Fe415 steel is:

$$F_{rebar} = 2 \times 0.45 \times f_{y} \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 0.225 \times \pi \times f_{y} \times d^2$$

Where $f_y$ = yield strength of rebar = **415 MPa** for Fe415, **500 MPa** for Fe500.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Hydraulic Crusher (Must remember for KTU 2024 ESE)**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Piston area | Pump sizing |
| 2 | $F_2 = F_1 \times \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Force amplification via Pascal's law | Crushing force calculation |
| 3 | $F_c = f_{ck} \times A_{contact} \times n$ | Required crushing force | Crusher selection |
| 4 | $F_{rebar} = 0.225 \pi f_y d^2$ | Rebar shear force | Steel-cutting crusher sizing |
| 5 | $Q = A_p \times v_p$ | Flow = Area × Piston velocity | Pump flow rate selection |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F_{crusher}}{A_{piston}}$ | Hydraulic pressure for desired F | Pressure setting |
| 7 | $t_{cycle} = \dfrac{2L}{v_{avg}}$ | Cycle time, L = stroke length | Productivity estimation |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{hyd}$ | Combined efficiency ≈ 0.80 | Power pack sizing |
| 9 | $E_{impact} = P \times V_{displ}$ | Breaker blow energy (J) | Hammer selection |
| 10 | $PPV_{limit} < 5$ mm/s | Peak particle velocity | Vibration control check |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the above table, all absolute value / division operators use `$\times$` and `$\div$` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Hydraulic crushers are **standard equipment** in:

- **Bridge Rehabilitation:** Removing damaged pier caps while keeping the foundation intact.
- **Highway Overpass Strengthening:** Cutting and removing portions of deck slab for retrofitting carbon-fiber strips.
- **Heritage Restoration:** Carefully removing added layers from heritage buildings (e.g., lime plaster over historic stone) without vibration damage.
- **Nuclear Power Plant Decontamination:** Remote-controlled crushers dismantle contaminated concrete with no human exposure.
- **Seismic Retrofitting:** Removing weak non-ductile beam-column joints to be replaced with modern ductile detailing (as per IS 15988 and IS 13920).
- **Underwater Repairs:** Submersible hydraulic crusher-shear units used for dam, pier, and offshore platform repair.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crushing Force from Pascal's Law

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic crusher uses a pump cylinder of diameter $D_1 = 50$ mm to pressurize oil to **250 bar**. This pressure is transmitted to the crusher cylinder of diameter $D_2 = 250$ mm. Calculate the theoretical crushing force developed at the jaw, and the effective mechanical advantage.

**Given:**
- Pump pressure $P = 250$ bar $= 250 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 2.5 \times 10^7$ N/m²
- Pump piston diameter $D_1 = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- Crusher piston diameter $D_2 = 250$ mm $= 0.250$ m

**Find:** Crushing force $F_2$ and mechanical advantage MA.

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional areas of the two pistons:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4}$$

$$\boxed{A_1 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.250)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0625}{4}$$

$$\boxed{A_2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law to find the force on the small piston:**

The pressure acts on the small piston from the pump, generating input force:

$$F_1 = P \times A_1 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_1 = 49,087.5 \text{ N} \approx 49.09 \text{ kN}$$

This is the force the pump must deliver — easily achievable.

**Step 3 — Apply Pascal's Law at the crusher piston:**

Since pressure is transmitted undiminished, the pressure on the crusher piston is also **250 bar**. Force on crusher piston:

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 4.9087 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$F_2 = 1,227,184 \text{ N} \approx 1227.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ton-force for engineering interpretation:**

$$F_2 = \frac{1227.2}{9.81} \approx 125.1 \text{ tonf}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Mechanical Advantage (MA):**

$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{250}{50}\right)^2 = 5^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{MA} = 25}$$

**Step 6 — Final Verification using force-ratio identity:**

$$F_2 = F_1 \times \text{MA} = 49.09 \times 25 = 1227.2 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A modest 5-ton pump force becomes a **125-ton crushing force** at the jaw — this 25× multiplication is what makes hydraulic crushers the most powerful hand-held demolition tool in civil engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Required to Crush a Concrete Beam

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic crusher has a piston stroke of **L = 600 mm**. During the closing stroke, oil flows at $Q = 150$ L/min into the cap-end of the crusher cylinder (effective area $A_2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-2}$ m²). Calculate the jaw closing time, the average piston velocity, and the cycle time (open + close).

**Given:**
- Stroke length $L = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m
- Flow rate $Q = 150$ L/min $= 150 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/min $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- Piston area $A_2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-2}$ m²

**Step 1 — Calculate closing time $t_{close}$:**

Volume of oil needed to displace piston through full stroke:

$$V_{oil} = A_2 \times L = 4.9087 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.6 = 2.9452 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_{close} = \frac{V_{oil}}{Q} = \frac{2.9452 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.78 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate average piston velocity during closing:**

$$v_{avg,close} = \frac{L}{t_{close}} = \frac{0.6}{11.78} = 0.0509 \text{ m/s} \approx 50.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate opening time using rod area:**

The piston rod has diameter $d_{rod} = 100$ mm, so the rod-side annular area is:

$$A_{rod,annular} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_2^2 - d_{rod}^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25^2 - 0.10^2)$$

$$A_{rod,annular} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0625 - 0.0100) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0525)$$

$$A_{rod,annular} = 4.1233 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

Oil flow into rod-end during opening:

$$V_{rod} = A_{rod,annular} \times L = 4.1233 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.6 = 2.474 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_{open} = \frac{V_{rod}}{Q} = \frac{2.474 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.90 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Total cycle time:**

$$t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{open} = 11.78 + 9.90 = 21.68 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Productivity estimation (bites per hour):**

$$N_{bites} = \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{21.68} \approx 166 \text{ bites/hour}$$

This is the basis for site planning and equipment selection in rehabilitation projects.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to select the right crusher for a given repair scenario — a frequently tested KTU concept.**

| Repair Scenario | Recommended Crusher | Crushing Force (ton) | Reason |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------------|--------|
| Removing spalled cover concrete from beam soffit | **Hand-held Hydraulic Breaker** | 0.5 – 1.0 | Light, localized, low vibration |
| Demolishing damaged column jacketing | **Skid-Steer Concrete Pulverizer** | 30 – 50 | Mid-size, precise |
| Cutting rebar during column removal | **Hydraulic Shear** | 40 – 60 | Clean steel cut |
| Removing large bridge deck section | **Excavator-Mounted Pulverizer** | 60 – 100 | High capacity, mobile |
| Splitting oversized concrete chunks at site | **Static Hydraulic Splitter** | 200 – 500 | Silent, no vibration |
| Heritage building — non-structural plaster | **Needle Scaler / Hand Breaker** | 0.1 – 0.3 | Ultra-fine, minimal impact |
| Underwater pier repair | **Submersible Hydraulic Crusher** | 50 – 80 | Sealed, water-resistant |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydraulic Crusher System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Engine 150 kW] --> B[Axial Piston Pump]
    B --> C[High Pressure Oil 250 bar]
    C --> D[Directional Control Valve 4/3]
    D --> E[Cap End of Crusher Cylinder]
    D --> F[Rod End of Crusher Cylinder]
    E --> G[Crusher Piston D 250 mm]
    G --> H[Fixed Jaw Tooth]
    G --> I[Moving Jaw Tooth]
    I --> J[Concrete Element to be Crushed]
    H --> J
    F --> K[Return Line to Tank]
    C --> L[Pressure Relief Valve 320 bar]
    L --> M[Oil Reservoir 400 L]
    B --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Inline Filter 10 micron]
    N --> B
    J --> O[Crushed Concrete Debris]
    C --> P[Pressure Gauge]
    P --> Q[Operator Control Panel]
    Q --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Operator Activates Pump] --> S2{Pressure Check 250 bar}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Alarm and Shutdown]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Open Directional Valve to Cap End]
    S4 --> S5[Piston Extends, Jaw Closes]
    S5 --> S6[Crushing Force Applied on Concrete]
    S6 --> S7{Concrete Fractured}
    S7 -->|No| S8[Increase Pressure 5 bar]
    S8 --> S6
    S7 -->|Yes| S9[Reverse Valve to Rod End]
    S9 --> S10[Piston Retracts, Jaw Opens]
    S10 --> S11[Debris Drops Under Gravity]
    S11 --> S12[Cycle Reset]
    S12 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Component Roles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Generation Block]
        P1[Diesel Engine] --> P2[Coupling]
        P2 --> P3[Axial Piston Pump]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Control Block]
        C1[Operator Lever] --> C2[Solenoid DCV]
        C2 --> C3[Pressure Compensator]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION[Hydraulic Actuation Block]
        A1[Crusher Cylinder] --> A2[Piston and Rod]
        A2 --> A3[Jaw Mechanism]
        A3 --> A4[Tooth Tip Engagement]
    end
    subgraph SAFETY[Safety Block]
        Q1[Relief Valve] --> Q2[Accumulator 10 L]
        Q2 --> Q3[Load Hold Valve]
    end
    P3 --> C2
    C2 --> A1
    A1 --> Q1
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Jaw Geometry and Force Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Hydraulic Cylinder Piston] --> N[Linkage Arm 1]
    N --> O[Fixed Jaw Body]
    O --> P[Tooth Tip Point A]
    P --> Q[Concrete Element Width B mm]
    Q --> R[Tooth Tip Point C]
    R --> S[Moving Jaw Body]
    S --> T[Linkage Arm 2]
    T --> U[Housing Anchor]
    M -.applies F_c on.-> O
    S -.reacts with F_c.-> T
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** The hydraulic piston (M) pushes linkage arm (N), which rotates the moving jaw (S) about anchor (U). The moving jaw tooth (C) presses against the concrete (Q), with the fixed jaw tooth (A) acting as the reaction surface. The concrete is **squeezed** between A and C — analogous to the nutcracker jaw.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a hydraulic crusher. State any two advantages of using a hydraulic crusher over conventional impact-type demolition tools during structural rehabilitation.**

**Model Answer:**

A hydraulic crusher is a demolition device that uses pressurized hydraulic fluid (typically 100–300 bar) to generate a controlled, high crushing or shearing force for breaking concrete and cutting steel reinforcement during repair works.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Low vibration output** — Peak particle velocity is < 5 mm/s, preventing damage to sound concrete adjacent to the repair zone (critical for selective demolition).
2. **Low noise and dust** — Operates below 75 dB(A) at 7 m and is water-spray compatible, making it suitable for occupied buildings and urban sites.

*(Any other valid advantage — precision, steel recovery, operator safety — also acceptable for full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: State Pascal's Law. How is it used as the working principle of a hydraulic crusher?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law (1653):** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible static fluid is transmitted with equal magnitude to all parts of the fluid and the walls of the container."*

**Application in a hydraulic crusher:**
- A high-pressure pump pressurizes hydraulic oil (typically **250 bar**) in a small-diameter pump cylinder.
- This pressure is transmitted undiminished through the oil to a much larger-diameter crusher cylinder.
- Since Force = Pressure × Area, the larger piston area (e.g., 25× larger) produces a crushing force magnified by the same factor (e.g., 25×).
- A pump force of just **5 ton** can thus deliver a **125 ton** crushing force at the jaw — enabling demolition of high-strength concrete.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are given below — students answer ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: A hydraulic crusher is to be used for demolishing a 300 mm × 450 mm reinforced concrete beam of grade M30 during a rehabilitation project. The crusher has a pump cylinder diameter of 60 mm, a crusher cylinder diameter of 240 mm, and operates at 280 bar pressure.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate (i) the theoretical crushing force developed at the jaw in kN, and (ii) the mechanical advantage of the system.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** The beam contains 4 nos. of 20 mm dia. Fe500 longitudinal bars at the cut section. Determine (i) the total force required to shear these bars, and (ii) comment on whether the crusher can simultaneously crush the concrete and shear the rebar in a single bite.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Crushing Force and Mechanical Advantage**

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to SI units:
$$P = 280 \text{ bar} = 280 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.8 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute crusher cylinder area $A_2$:
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.240)^2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.0576}{4}$$
$$A_2 = 4.524 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute crushing force $F_2$:
$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 2.8 \times 10^7 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$\boxed{F_2 = 1.267 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1267 \text{ kN} \approx 129.2 \text{ tonf}}$$

**Step 4:** Mechanical advantage:
$$\text{MA} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{240}{60}\right)^2 = 4^2 = \mathbf{16}$$

**Part (b) — Rebar Shear Force and Feasibility Check**

**Step 1:** Force to shear one 20 mm dia. Fe500 bar:
$$F_{1\,bar} = 0.225 \times \pi \times f_y \times d^2 = 0.225 \times \pi \times 500 \times (20)^2$$
$$F_{1\,bar} = 0.225 \times 3.1416 \times 500 \times 400$$
$$F_{1\,bar} = 141,372 \text{ N} \approx 141.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Total force for 4 bars:
$$F_{4\,bars} = 4 \times 141.4 = 565.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3:** Force required to crush M30 concrete over the contact area (assume jaw contact covers 300 mm × 100 mm = 0.03 m², $f_{ck}$ = 30 MPa):
$$F_{concrete} = f_{ck} \times A_{contact} \times n_{safety} = 30 \times 10^3 \times 0.03 \times 1.5$$
$$F_{concrete} = 1350 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** Total force required for combined concrete crushing + rebar shear:
$$F_{total} = 1350 + 565.5 = 1915.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with available crushing force:
$$F_{available} = 1267 \text{ kN} < F_{required} = 1915.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Conclusion:** The crusher **cannot** crush the concrete and shear the rebar in a single bite. **Two-stage demolition is required:** first use the crusher to crush the concrete cover, then use a separate hydraulic shear to cut the exposed rebar. This is standard rehabilitation practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students forget to **include the factor of safety** (1.5×) on the concrete crushing force, and forget to **sum the concrete and rebar forces** before comparing. This causes a 3-mark loss. Always present the comparison as a final boxed conclusion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: A rehabilitation contractor is using a skid-steer mounted hydraulic concrete pulverizer for selective demolition of a damaged column.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the hydraulic crusher system, identifying the main components of the power pack, control system, and actuation system.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Discuss the selection criteria for choosing an appropriate hydraulic crusher attachment for (i) bridge pier rehabilitation, (ii) underwater concrete removal, and (iii) heritage masonry restoration. State the specific force and vibration limits applicable in each case.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working**

*(Examiner expects a labeled block diagram showing: Power Pack → Pump → DCV → Crusher Cylinder → Jaw. Use the Section 4.1 diagram as reference.)*

**Step-by-step working:**

1. **Power Pack:** Diesel engine (~75 kW) drives a variable-displacement axial piston pump through a coupling. The pump draws oil from the reservoir (400 L capacity) and pressurizes it to **250–280 bar**.

2. **Control System:** A 4/3 solenoid-operated directional control valve (DCV), actuated by the operator's lever, directs pressurized oil to either the cap-end (jaw close) or rod-end (jaw open) of the crusher cylinder. A pressure-compensator maintains constant flow despite load variation.

3. **Safety System:** A relief valve set at **320 bar** protects against overload. Load-hold valves prevent accidental jaw opening if a hose bursts. A 10-L bladder accumulator absorbs pressure spikes.

4. **Actuation System:** Pressurized oil enters the cap-end of the double-acting cylinder (bore 250 mm, stroke 600 mm). The piston extends, rotating the moving jaw through a 4-bar linkage. The moving jaw tooth presses against the concrete against the reaction of the fixed jaw, generating **80–120 ton** of crushing force.

5. **Return Circuit:** Oil from the rod-end returns to the tank through a return-line filter (10 µm), completing the closed loop. A heat exchanger keeps oil below **60°C**.

**Part (b) — Selection Criteria**

| Application | Recommended Crusher | Crushing Force | Vibration Limit (PPV) | Special Feature |
|-------------|---------------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **(i) Bridge Pier Rehabilitation** | Excavator-mounted Pulverizer (20–25 ton class) | 60–80 ton | < 10 mm/s (DIN 4150-3 for industrial structures) | Long reach boom; can work from bank |
| **(ii) Underwater Concrete Removal** | Submersible Hydraulic Crusher-Shear | 50–80 ton | < 5 mm/s (water dampens, but must protect marine life) | Sealed hydraulic motor; ROV-controlled; hot-dip galvanized body |
| **(iii) Heritage Masonry Restoration** | Hand-held Needle Scaler + Static Splitter | 0.1–0.5 ton (needle) / 200 ton (splitter) | < 3 mm/s (strict heritage limit) | Zero-impact static splitting; hand tool to preserve historic fabric |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> In part (b), students often give **generic answers** like "use a crusher". You must specify the **crusher type, force rating, vibration limit (PPV in mm/s), and a justification**. Memorize PPV thresholds: **Heritage < 3 mm/s, Residential < 5 mm/s, Industrial < 10 mm/s, Robust structures < 20 mm/s** (per DIN 4150-3 and IS 6922).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Hydraulic Crusher (KTU 2024)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Hydraulic crusher is a **demolition tool**, not a construction tool — used in repair and rehabilitation.
- ☐ Working principle is **Pascal's Law** (1653): $P_1 = P_2$, leading to $F_2 = F_1 \times (A_2/A_1)$.
- ☐ Typical operating pressure: **100–300 bar** (memorize).
- ☐ Typical crushing force: **30–100 tonf** (memorize).
- ☐ Typical mechanical advantage: **15–30×** (ratio of piston area squares).

**Must-Remember Numbers:**
- ☐ Force amplification: $\text{MA} = (D_2/D_1)^2$.
- ☐ Rebar shear force: $F = 0.225 \pi f_y d^2$ (use this exact formula).
- ☐ Concrete crushing: $F = f_{ck} \times A \times n$, with $n = 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- ☐ Vibration limit for sound concrete: **PPV < 5 mm/s**.
- ☐ Noise limit at 7 m: **< 75 dB(A)**.
- ☐ Cycle time: **20–25 seconds** (open + close).

**Equipment Selection Triggers:**
- ☐ **Selective demolition** → use crusher (not breaker).
- ☐ **Silent operation required** → use static splitter (not breaker).
- ☐ **Steel cutting** → use hydraulic shear.
- ☐ **Underwater** → use sealed submersible crusher.
- ☐ **Heritage** → use needle scaler / static splitter (lowest PPV).

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ☐ Don't confuse **crushing force** (steady, sustained) with **impact energy** (single blow, for breakers).
- ☐ Don't apply Pascal's Law to the **rod-end** without subtracting the rod area.
- ☐ Don't forget the **factor of safety** ($n = 1.5$–$2.0$) on concrete crushing force.
- ☐ Don't recommend a breaker for selective demolition — examiners will deduct marks.

**Past KTU Recurrence Probability:**
- ☐ **Very High** (≥ 80%): Pascal's Law derivation, crushing force calculation, advantages over impact tools.
- ☐ **High** (≥ 60%): Selection criteria, vibration/noise limits, equipment comparison table.
- ☐ **Moderate** (≥ 40%): Rebar shear force calculation, cycle-time / productivity estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implosion by Delayed Detonation Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET634 - REPAIR AND REHABILITATION OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Implosion by Delayed Detonation Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# IMPLOSION BY DELAYED DETONATION TECHNIQUE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Implosion Demolition** is a controlled, engineered collapse methodology in which a structure is made to fall *inward* onto its own footprint, primarily through the strategic removal of vertical load-bearing elements (columns and shear walls) using precisely timed explosive charges. The **Delayed Detonation Technique** is the governing sub-method that introduces *millisecond-to-second timing offsets* between successive explosive events so that gravity and progressive unbracing, rather than outward blast pressures, become the principal collapse mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Implosion by delayed detonation is a *non-explosive-mass-driven* collapse. The detonation simply eliminates the resisting skeleton; gravity and inertial forces of the falling mass complete the demolition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **stack of wooden blocks** standing on a table. If you pull out the bottom block *first* and then the next, and so on in quick succession, the entire stack collapses *vertically downward* — straight onto the table — without scattering sideways. The "delay" simply controls *which block is pulled, and when*.

In an actual building, the "blocks" are reinforced concrete columns, and the "table" is the building's own foundation pad. By firing charges on the **ground-floor columns first**, then the **first-floor columns** a fraction of a second later, the upper structure is given no time to topple laterally — it merely *sinks* into the basement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Misconception Cleared:** Despite the word "explosion," only a few kilograms of explosives are typically used. The energy source for the actual collapse is **gravitational potential energy** of the structure itself. The blast merely *removes the props*.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Charge per column (RC) | **0.5 to 1.5 kg** of RDX/TNT equivalent | $W_c$ |
| Inter-row delay | **0.25 s to 0.75 s** | $\Delta t$ |
| Stemming length | Equal to burden (≈ 0.5 m) | $T$ |
| Charge density in column | 0.3 to 1.0 kg/m³ | $\rho_{ch}$ |
| Total charge per building | **5 to 250 kg** (case-specific) | $W_{total}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The technique relies on **four sequential engineering decisions**:

1. **Identification of Critical Load Path:** Locate the columns that carry the highest axial load (typically ground-floor interior columns).
2. **Pre-Weakening (or Pre-cutting):** Reduce a column's load capacity by 60–80% through concrete saw-cuts, thermocuts, or *partial explosive charge*, so the *delayed main charge* finishes the job.
3. **Charge Placement with Delay Sequencing:** Place shaped charges on the columns in a *bottom-up* arrangement, with each successive row fired after a calculated delay.
4. **Controlled Collapse Geometry:** Confirm that the building's centre of gravity remains inside the planned *debris footprint* during every instant of the fall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bottom-Up?** Firing the bottom first allows the upper portion to develop vertical momentum before lateral instability grows. This maximizes the *vertical displacement component* and minimizes lateral scatter.

### 2.2 The Four Theoretical Mechanisms Triggered by Delayed Detonation

1. **Loss of Vertical Support** — Axial load transfer path is broken sequentially.
2. **Progressive Plastic Hinge Formation** — Beams and slabs above the removed column undergo large rotations, converting strain energy into kinetic energy.
3. **Dynamic Load Redistribution** — Adjacent columns experience momentary overloads (impact factor 1.5–2.0) which they are not designed to carry, causing cascade failure.
4. **Debris Compaction (Debris Bulking)** — Falling mass impacts the slab below; the rubble fills the basement volume.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the *high-yield* equations from the KTU board pattern. Memorize the variables, units, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = K \cdot B^{3}$ | Charge weight (Swedish/Indian Blasters' formula) | kg |
| 2 | $B = \frac{H}{3} \text{ to } \frac{2H}{3}$ | Burden for concrete column (H = least dimension) | m |
| 3 | $S = 1.5\,B$ | Spacing between boreholes/columns | m |
| 4 | $SD = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt[3]{W}}$ | Scaled distance for vibration safety | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 5 | $V = K_v \left(\dfrac{D}{\sqrt{W}}\right)^{-n}$ | Peak Particle Velocity (USBM) | mm/s |
| 6 | $\Delta t_{min} = \dfrac{2B}{v_{fall}}$ | Minimum safe inter-row delay | s |
| 7 | $T = B$ | Stemming length rule | m |
| 8 | $h_{fall} = \dfrac{1}{2} g\,t^{2}$ | Drop height for debris (kinematic) | m |
| 9 | $E_{collapse} = m g h$ | Gravitational energy driving collapse | J |
| 10 | $IF = 1 + \dfrac{\sqrt{2H/\Delta_s}}{1 + h/B}$ | Impact Factor (for cascade) | – |

### 2.4 Variables Glossary

- $W$ = Explosive charge weight (kg)
- $K$ = Material constant; for concrete $K \approx 0.5$, for reinforced concrete $K \approx 0.7$, for steel-cut $K \approx 1.5$
- $B$ = Burden = shortest distance from charge to free face (m)
- $H$ = Least lateral dimension of column (m)
- $S$ = Spacing between adjacent charges (m)
- $D$ = Distance from blast to point of interest (m)
- $SD$ = Scaled distance (m/kg$^{1/3}$)
- $V$ = Peak Particle Velocity (mm/s)
- $K_v, n$ = Site transmission constants ($K_v \approx 1140$, $n \approx 1.6$ for typical soil)
- $v_{fall}$ = Fall velocity of collapsing slab (m/s)
- $IF$ = Impact factor (dimensionless, typically 1.5–2.5)
- $h$ = Story height (m)
- $\Delta_s$ = Static deflection under impact (m)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Demolition** — Used in dense city centres where lateral throw is unacceptable (e.g., Kingsbridge Armory, NY; Marina Square, Singapore).
- **Industrial Decommissioning** — Chimneys, cooling towers, blast furnaces, refinery towers.
- **Disaster Response** — Controlled collapse of earthquake-damaged structures.
- **Bridge Demolition** — Slab-by-slab deck removal.

> [!NOTE]
> The same physics governs *planned structural failure analysis* in earthquake engineering — buildings designed to fail in a ductile, progressive manner rather than catastrophically.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge Weight for a Reinforced Concrete Column

**Problem Setup:** A 600 mm × 600 mm RC column is to be demolished by implosion. Determine the required charge weight and verify vibration safety for a sensitive structure at 50 m.

**Step 1 — Determine the Burden $B$**

For an RC column, the least dimension governs. Here $H = 0.6$ m. Using the recommended range:

$$B = \frac{H}{2.5} = \frac{0.6}{2.5} = 0.24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Select the Material Constant $K$**

For reinforced concrete with mild steel reinforcement (≤ 1% steel by volume), the Indian Blasters' Handbook recommends:

$$K_{RC} = 0.7 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cube Formula**

$$W = K \cdot B^{3}$$

Substituting:

$$W = 0.7 \times (0.24)^{3}$$

Compute $B^3$:

$$(0.24)^{3} = 0.24 \times 0.24 \times 0.24$$

$$0.24 \times 0.24 = 0.0576$$

$$0.0576 \times 0.24 = 0.013824 \text{ m}^{3}$$

Therefore:

$$W = 0.7 \times 0.013824$$

$$\boxed{W = 0.009677 \text{ kg} \approx 9.68 \text{ g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a small charge confirms that **only grams of explosive** are needed per column when the geometry is engineered correctly. The collapse is driven by the *building's own mass*.

**Step 4 — Verification with a More Conservative Formula**

For heavily reinforced columns, use the Langefors formula:

$$W = 0.07 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{F \cdot c}$$

where $d$ = hole depth (m), $F$ = fixation coefficient (1.0 for one free face, 1.5 for two, 2.0 for three), $c$ = consumption constant.

For our column with $d = 0.5$ m, $F = 1.5$ (two free faces), $c = 0.4$ (concrete):

$$W = 0.07 \times 0.5 \times \sqrt{1.5 \times 0.4}$$

$$W = 0.035 \times \sqrt{0.6}$$

$$W = 0.035 \times 0.7746$$

$$\boxed{W = 0.02711 \text{ kg} \approx 27.1 \text{ g}}$$

The design charge is taken as the **larger** of the two, i.e., **~30 g per column** (with safety factor 1.2).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inter-Row Delay Calculation

**Goal:** Compute the minimum safe delay between row 1 (ground floor) and row 2 (first floor) such that the upper slab has gained sufficient velocity but not yet hit the lower one.

**Step 1 — Estimate Fall Velocity of Upper Slab after Row 1 Detonation**

For a 4 m story height and a 1 m vertical drop before engagement of next column:

$$v_{fall} = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.0}$$

$$v_{fall} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Travel Time for the Fall**

$$t_{fall} = \frac{2h}{v_{fall}} = \frac{2 \times 1.0}{4.43} = 0.452 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Minimum Delay Criterion**

The delay must be long enough for the slab to develop velocity but short enough that the structure has not become laterally unstable:

$$\Delta t_{min} = 0.5 \cdot t_{fall} = 0.5 \times 0.452 = 0.226 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Maximum Delay Criterion (lateral stability)**

For a building of height $H_b = 12$ m, the natural period is:

$$T_n = 0.1 \cdot N = 0.1 \times 4 = 0.4 \text{ s}$$

For stability, $\Delta t \leq T_n / 4$:

$$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{0.4}{4} = 0.10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the Engineering Value**

Since $\Delta t_{min} > \Delta t_{max}$, the slab will fall too fast. We must **pre-weaken** the first-floor columns so they don't resist. In practice:

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{designed} = 0.25 \text{ s to 0.50 s (typical design range)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peak Particle Velocity at a Neighbouring Structure

Using the USBM formula:

$$V = K_v \left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{W}}\right)^{-n}$$

Given: $D = 50$ m, $W = 0.03$ kg, $K_v = 1140$, $n = 1.6$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Scaled Distance**

$$SD = \frac{D}{\sqrt{W}} = \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.03}} = \frac{50}{0.1732} = 288.7 \text{ m/kg}^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the USBM Equation**

$$V = 1140 \times (288.7)^{-1.6}$$

Compute $(288.7)^{1.6}$:

$$\ln(288.7) = 5.665$$

$$1.6 \times 5.665 = 9.064$$

$$e^{9.064} = 8{,}679$$

Therefore:

$$V = \frac{1140}{8{,}679} = 0.1313 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Safe Limit**

For residential buildings (IS 6922: Indian Standard on blasting vibrations):

$$V_{safe} = 5 \text{ mm/s at 50 m}$$

Since $0.1313 \ll 5$, the operation is **safe by a factor of ~38**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Full Building Sizing

A 4-storey, 12 m tall building has 16 columns per floor. Design the full implosion charge:

**Step 1 — Charge per column (from §3.1)**

$$W_c = 30 \text{ g} = 0.030 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total number of charges**

We only blow columns on the **ground and first floors** (8 columns × 2 floors = 16 charges):

$$N = 16$$

**Step 3 — Total explosive**

$$W_{total} = N \times W_c = 16 \times 0.030 = 0.48 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Less than **half a kilogram** of explosive will bring down a 4-storey building. The remaining 99.9% of the collapse energy is the **gravitational potential energy**:

$$E_{collapse} = m g h = (2000 \text{ tonnes}) \times 9.81 \times 6 \text{ m (half-height)}$$

$$E_{collapse} = 117.7 \text{ MJ}$$

Equivalent to **28 kg of TNT** of gravitational energy — confirming the building *demolishes itself*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of Implosion by Delayed Detonation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Survey and Structural Audit] --> B[Identify Load Bearing Columns]
    B --> C[Pre Weaken Columns Saw Cut or Thermic Lance]
    C --> D[Place Shaped Charges on Selected Columns]
    D --> E[Connect Detonator Network Bottom Row First]
    E --> F[Evacuate Exclusion Zone 200m Radius]
    F --> G[Initiate Detonation Sequence]
    G --> H[Row 1 Ground Floor Fires]
    H --> I[Delay 0.25 to 0.50s]
    I --> J[Row 2 First Floor Fires]
    J --> K[Delay 0.25 to 0.50s]
    K --> L[Row 3 Second Floor Fires]
    L --> M[Building Sinks Vertically into Footprint]
    M --> N[Vibration and Dust Monitoring]
    N --> O[Site Clearance and Debris Audit]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Charge Sequencing Logic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GroundFloor[Ground Floor Charges]
        G1[Column 1 Charge]
        G2[Column 2 Charge]
        G3[Column 3 Charge]
        G4[Column 4 Charge]
    end
    subgraph FirstFloor[First Floor Charges]
        F1[Column 1 Charge]
        F2[Column 2 Charge]
        F3[Column 3 Charge]
        F4[Column 4 Charge]
    end
    subgraph SecondFloor[Second Floor Charges]
        S1[Column 1 Charge]
        S2[Column 2 Charge]
        S3[Column 3 Charge]
        S4[Column 4 Charge]
    end
    Master[Master Detonator and Timer] -->|t equals 0.00s| G1
    Master -->|t equals 0.00s| G2
    Master -->|t equals 0.00s| G3
    Master -->|t equals 0.00s| G4
    Master -->|t equals 0.40s| F1
    Master -->|t equals 0.40s| F2
    Master -->|t equals 0.40s| F3
    Master -->|t equals 0.40s| F4
    Master -->|t equals 0.80s| S1
    Master -->|t equals 0.80s| S2
    Master -->|t equals 0.80s| S3
    Master -->|t equals 0.80s| S4
```

## 4.3 Delay Detonator Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primary Detonator] -->|electric pulse| S1[Secondary 0ms]
    S1 --> D1[Delay Element 250ms]
    S1 --> D2[Delay Element 500ms]
    S1 --> D3[Delay Element 750ms]
    D1 --> R1[Row 1 Charges Fire]
    D2 --> R2[Row 2 Charges Fire]
    D3 --> R3[Row 3 Charges Fire]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Detonator Types used in KTU Industry Practice:**
> - **Electric Delay Detonators:** Copper-wire based, 25 ms increments, susceptible to RF interference.
> - **Electronic Detonators:** Programmable 1 ms resolution; preferred for urban sites.
> - **NONEL (Non-Electric) Detonators:** Shock-tube based, immune to stray currents, very common in Indian practice.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Q: Define implosion demolition. How does it differ fundamentally from a conventional explosion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Implosion demolition** is a controlled collapse technique in which a structure is made to fall *inward onto its own footprint* by sequentially removing vertical load-bearing elements using precisely timed small explosive charges.

The fundamental differences from a conventional explosion are:

1. **Energy Source:** In a conventional explosion, the chemical energy of the explosive is the *primary* destructive agent. In implosion, the *gravitational potential energy* of the structure itself is the primary agent — explosives only remove the props.
2. **Direction of Force:** A conventional explosion generates an *outward* blast pressure wave. Implosion generates a *downward* gravitational collapse by removing the support skeleton.
3. **Charge Magnitude:** Conventional blasting may use tonnes of explosive; implosion uses only **kilograms** of charge, sometimes less than 1 kg total.
4. **Outcome Geometry:** Explosion scatters debris radially; implosion confines debris to a predictable vertical footprint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write "implosion = inward explosion." That is *not* the engineering definition. Implosion is a *gravity-driven collapse* enabled by the removal of supports.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List any three types of delay detonators used in implosion demolition and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Detonator Type | One Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electric Delay Detonator** | Precise timing from milliseconds to several seconds, well established. |
| 2 | **Electronic Detonator** | Programmable 1 ms resolution, two-way verification, immune to RF noise. |
| 3 | **NONEL (Non-Electric) Shock-Tube Detonator** | Completely immune to stray electrical currents and radio frequency, safer near power lines. |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a):** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for implosion by delayed detonation technique** for a multi-storey reinforced concrete building. Include the role of pre-weakening, charge placement, and timing sequence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The implosion by delayed detonation technique follows a sequence of eight engineering steps:

1. **Detailed Structural Survey and Audit:**
   - Obtain as-built drawings, identify all load-bearing columns and shear walls.
   - Conduct non-destructive testing (rebound hammer, UPV) to estimate concrete strength.
   - Plot column grid and identify critical load path columns (interior columns of central bay).

2. **Computational Modelling:**
   - Build a 3D finite element model (ETABS, SAP2000).
   - Run non-linear static (pushover) and dynamic analyses to predict collapse mechanism.
   - Simulate column removal scenarios to identify which columns, if removed, cause total progressive collapse.

3. **Selection of Columns to be Charged:**
   - Typically, columns on the **ground floor and first floor** are selected.
   - Heavily loaded interior columns are prioritised.
   - Corner columns may be charged last to control lateral spread.

4. **Pre-Weakening of Columns:**
   - The targeted columns are *partially weakened* to reduce the explosive charge required.
   - Methods include: concrete saw-cuts at top and bottom of column, thermic lance cutting of steel rebars, hydrodemolition of cover concrete.
   - Pre-weakening achieves 50–70% reduction in column capacity.

5. **Charge Calculation and Placement:**
   - Compute charge weight $W = K \cdot B^{3}$ where $B$ is the burden (half the column width).
   - Place shaped charges on the **mid-height** of the column, oriented horizontally.
   - Each column receives 2–4 charges stacked vertically for uniform failure.

6. **Detonator Network and Delay Sequencing:**
   - Wire charges in series-parallel for redundancy.
   - Programme the master timer: **Row 1 (Ground) at 0 ms; Row 2 (First Floor) at 400 ms; Row 3 (Second Floor) at 800 ms**.
   - Use two independent initiation paths for safety.

7. **Safety Evacuation and Pre-Detonation Checks:**
   - Evacuate a **200 m exclusion zone** (or as per IS 4081).
   - Conduct continuity tests on each detonator circuit.
   - Deploy vibration sensors (seismographs) and dust monitors at sensitive neighbours.

8. **Detonation, Monitoring, and Clearance:**
   - Sound the final siren, fire the sequence.
   - Wait 30 minutes for dust settling.
   - Conduct post-blast survey, then clear debris.

**Q (b):** An RC column of size 400 mm × 400 mm is to be removed by implosion. Calculate **(i)** the required charge weight using $W = K \cdot B^{3}$ with $K = 0.7$ for concrete, **(ii)** the scaled distance for a sensitive instrument at 30 m, and **(iii)** comment on whether the resulting Peak Particle Velocity (using USBM formula with $K_v = 1140$, $n = 1.6$) is within safe limits (limit = 5 mm/s). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Column size: $H = 0.4$ m
- $K = 0.7$
- $D = 30$ m
- $K_v = 1140$, $n = 1.6$
- $V_{safe} = 5$ mm/s

**Step 1 — Calculate Burden $B$**

For a square column with explosive placed on one face, the burden is half the column width:

$$B = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Charge Weight $W$**

$$W = K \cdot B^{3} = 0.7 \times (0.2)^{3}$$

$$(0.2)^{3} = 0.008 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$W = 0.7 \times 0.008 = 0.0056 \text{ kg} = 5.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Scaled Distance $SD$**

$$SD = \frac{D}{\sqrt{W}} = \frac{30}{\sqrt{0.0056}} = \frac{30}{0.07483} = 400.9 \text{ m/kg}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Peak Particle Velocity**

$$V = K_v \left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{W}}\right)^{-n} = 1140 \times (400.9)^{-1.6}$$

Compute $(400.9)^{1.6}$:

$$\ln(400.9) = 5.994$$

$$1.6 \times 5.994 = 9.590$$

$$e^{9.590} = 14{,}590$$

$$V = \frac{1140}{14{,}590} = 0.0781 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Safe Limit**

$$V_{computed} = 0.0781 \text{ mm/s} \ll V_{safe} = 5 \text{ mm/s}$$

The operation is **safe by a factor of ~64**, well within the permissible limit. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cube $B$:** Many students write $W = K \cdot B$ — this is **wrong**. The cube relationship is *non-negotiable* per the Swedish/Indian formula.
> 2. **Using the wrong SD formula:** KTU boards expect $SD = D / \sqrt{W}$ (square root) for vibration, **not** the cube-root form (which is used in some American texts). State which one you are using.
> 3. **Not including the unit conversion:** 5.6 g is 0.0056 kg — writing "5.6" without unit loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the safety comparison:** A complete answer *must* compare $V$ with $V_{safe}$ and state "safe" or "unsafe" explicitly.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a):** Explain the **concept of delay timing and the factors that govern the choice of delay interval** between successive rows in an implosion demolition. Derive the expression for the minimum safe delay in terms of the fall velocity and the free fall distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The delay interval is the time gap between the firing of one row of charges and the next. It is a *critical design variable* that determines whether the building collapses vertically (success) or topples laterally (failure).

**Factors Governing Delay Interval:**

1. **Story height and fall velocity:** Taller stories require more delay to let the slab gain velocity.
2. **Mass and stiffness of structure:** Stiffer structures need shorter delays to prevent them from "freezing" in a deformed shape.
3. **Desired debris pattern:** A larger delay produces a more compact debris pile; a shorter delay produces a more "explosive" fragmentation.
4. **Stability of adjacent structures:** Larger charges per delay = higher vibration; spreading charge over more delays reduces instantaneous peak vibration.
5. **Natural period of structure:** Delay must be less than $T_n/4$ to prevent resonance amplification.
6. **Detonator availability:** Practical detonators are available in 25 ms or 250 ms increments.

**Derivation of Minimum Safe Delay:**

Consider a slab of mass $m$ that has just lost its support at row 1. The slab begins to fall under gravity through a distance $h$ before it would normally impact a lower slab or column. The average velocity during this fall is:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot v_{fall} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{\frac{gh}{2}}$$

The time taken for the fall is:

$$t_{fall} = \frac{2h}{v_{fall}}$$

For the next row of charges to fire *after* the slab has begun moving but *before* it impacts the lower structure, the delay must be:

$$\Delta t_{min} = \frac{2B}{v_{fall}}$$

where $B$ is the burden (the half-width of the column, since the slab has to drop by this much to fully disengage).

Substituting $v_{fall} = \sqrt{2gh}$:

$$\Delta t_{min} = \frac{2B}{\sqrt{2gh}} = \frac{B\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{gh}} = \sqrt{\frac{2B^{2}}{gh}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{min} = B \sqrt{\frac{2}{gh}}}$$

For typical values $B = 0.3$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 1$ m:

$$\Delta t_{min} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{9.81 \times 1}} = 0.3 \times 0.4515 = 0.135 \text{ s}$$

Hence a delay of **250 ms to 500 ms** is the typical design range.

**Q (b):** A 6-storey RC frame building is to be demolished by implosion. Each ground-floor column is 500 mm × 500 mm in cross-section. The building has 24 columns on the ground floor. Using $K = 0.7$ and a safety factor of 1.5 on charge weight, calculate: **(i)** the charge per column, **(ii)** the total charge for the building, **(iii)** the gravitational energy released in the collapse (assume building mass = 3000 tonnes, average drop height = 9 m). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Column size: 500 mm × 500 mm
- $N_{cols} = 24$ (ground floor only)
- $K = 0.7$
- Safety Factor (SF) = 1.5
- Mass of building $m = 3000$ tonnes = $3 \times 10^{6}$ kg
- Average drop height $h = 9$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate Burden $B$**

$$B = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Charge per Column (without safety factor)**

$$W_c = K \cdot B^{3} = 0.7 \times (0.25)^{3}$$

$$(0.25)^{3} = 0.015625 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$W_c = 0.7 \times 0.015625 = 0.01094 \text{ kg} = 10.94 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Safety Factor**

$$W_{c,designed} = SF \times W_c = 1.5 \times 10.94 = 16.4 \text{ g per column}$$

**Step 4 — Total Charge for 24 Columns**

$$W_{total} = 24 \times 16.4 \text{ g} = 393.6 \text{ g} = 0.394 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice: less than **400 grams** of explosive will bring down a 3000-tonne building.

**Step 5 — Calculate Gravitational Energy Released**

$$E_{collapse} = m g h = 3 \times 10^{6} \times 9.81 \times 9$$

$$E_{collapse} = 2.649 \times 10^{8} \text{ J} = 264.9 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 6 — Express in TNT Equivalent for Comparison**

Energy of 1 kg TNT = 4.184 MJ

$$E_{TNT} = \frac{264.9}{4.184} = 63.3 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **gravitational energy (63.3 kg TNT equivalent) is ~160 times the chemical energy of the explosive (0.394 kg)**. This single numerical comparison is a *favourite KTU 14-mark question ending* and proves the point that the building demolishes itself.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Safety Factor** in (i) — KTU boards explicitly test whether you apply SF; missing it loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit conversion** in (iii): 3000 tonnes = $3 \times 10^6$ kg. Many students write "3000" and lose the mark because the unit "kg" is not used in the formula.
> 3. **Final comparison sentence** in (iii) — KTU examiners want an explicit statement such as *"the building's gravitational energy is 160 times the explosive energy, confirming gravity-driven collapse."* Do not skip this interpretive step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Implosion by Delayed Detonation Technique**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Definition:** Gravity-driven vertical collapse, *not* outward explosion. Explosive only removes supports.
- [ ] **Charge per column:** Typically **5–30 grams** of RDX/TNT for an RC column of 400–600 mm.
- [ ] **Total charge per building:** **< 1 kg to ~few hundred kg** depending on size.
- [ ] **Inter-row delay:** **0.25 s to 0.75 s**, bottom-up sequence.
- [ ] **Detonator hierarchy:** Master → Secondary → Delay Elements → Row Charges.

### Key Formulas
- [ ] $W = K \cdot B^{3}$ — Swedish/Indian Blasters' formula (charge weight)
- [ ] $B = H/2$ for square columns, or $H/3$ to $2H/3$ in general
- [ ] $S = 1.5\,B$ — spacing
- [ ] $SD = D/\sqrt{W}$ — scaled distance
- [ ] $V = 1140 \cdot (D/\sqrt{W})^{-1.6}$ — USBM PPV equation
- [ ] $\Delta t_{min} = B\sqrt{2/(gh)}$ — minimum inter-row delay
- [ ] $E_{collapse} = mgh$ — gravitational energy driving the fall

### Engineering Decisions
- [ ] Pre-weakening reduces explosive charge by 50–70%.
- [ ] Always place charges on the **mid-height** of the column.
- [ ] Sequence: **ground floor first**, then upward.
- [ ] Exclusion zone: minimum **200 m radius**.
- [ ] Monitor **PPV ≤ 5 mm/s** at residential neighbours, **≤ 2 mm/s** at sensitive instruments.

### Common Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern)
- [ ] Don't write "implosion = inward explosion."
- [ ] Don't confuse cube-root SD vs. square-root SD.
- [ ] Always state safety factor explicitly.
- [ ] Always end numerical answers with a **safety interpretation sentence**.

### Indian Standard References
- [ ] **IS 4081** — Safety code for blasting and related operations.
- [ ] **IS 6922** — Criteria for safety and design of structures subjected to underground blasts.
- [ ] **DGMS (India) Regulations** — For any commercial blasting operation.

### One-Line Board Answer
> *"Implosion by delayed detonation is a controlled, gravity-driven collapse method in which timed explosive charges (grams per column, bottom-up, 0.25–0.75 s delay) remove the vertical load path, allowing the building's own mass to bring it down into its own footprint."*
